#1 Post-ACL Reconstruction Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (post-operative knee pain/swelling fits many conditions), each clue adds new detail (surgical history → specific movement deficits → imaging confirmation), and progression is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 24-year-old athlete. 4 weeks ago had knee surgery for a ligament injury. Reports pain and swelling around the surgical site. Struggling to regain normal movement and function.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform sport-specific movements. Favours right leg, walks with limp. Struggles particularly with loaded knee bending (stairs, squats, kneeling).
C3 History: Knee ligament tear from skiing injury. Reconstruction with autograft hamstring. Early post-operative phase.
C4 Examination: Knee flexion 80 degrees, extension lag 5-10 degrees. Quadriceps strength 3+/5. Hamstring strength weak 3/5.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative X-rays show graft positioning adequate. Tunnel placement appropriate. MRI shows intact graft.
#2 Bell's Palsy Cranial Nerves
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 3 essentially names the diagnosis with tick bite + erythema migrans + positive serology, leaving minimal diagnostic challenge; the puzzle collapses the Lyme story too early and makes guessing a formality.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young adult with acute facial asymmetry and inability to move one side of face.
C2 Activity: Loss of facial symmetry, difficulty with oral hygiene. Water falls from affected side during drinking.
C3 History: Recent tick bite and erythema migrans rash. Positive Lyme serology. Facial paralysis developed 2 weeks after rash.
C4 Examination: Unilateral lower facial weakness greater than upper, incomplete eye closure. Tear dysfunction, hyperacusis possible.
C5 Imaging: EMG shows acute denervation pattern. CSF and brain imaging unremarkable. Nerve biopsy identifies Borrelia spirochete.
#3 Femoroacetabular Impingement Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (recreational athlete with hip pain fits multiple diagnoses), Clues 2–4 progressively narrow via movement patterns and findings, and Clue 5 clinches with imaging; fair difficulty and good learning progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged woman with intermittent hip pain after returning to recreational dancing.
C2 Activity: Pain during hip adduction and internal rotation; worse with repetitive flexion movements.
C3 History: Lifelong hip tightness; no prior hip injury. Recently increased dance frequency and intensity over past 3 months before symptom onset.
C4 Examination: Hip flexion passive range of motion 100 degrees with pain; positive impingement sign.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows labral fraying without full-thickness tear; acetabular version abnormality.
#4 Temporomandibular Joint Disorder Jaw
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (ear pain, jaw stiffness, headaches fit multiple conditions including cervical pathology), clues progressively add functional and examination detail, and imaging confirmation is logical; good diagnostic progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports ear pain, jaw stiffness, and headaches; symptoms worse in morning.
C2 Activity: Pain provoked by talking and chewing; morning symptoms suggest nocturnal grinding.
C3 History: History of psychological stress and parafunctional habits; associated neck muscle tension and frequent tension-type headaches; no prior jaw surgery or significant dental work.
C4 Examination: Jaw deviation on opening, severe masticatory muscle tenderness, tooth wear visible, limited mouth opening.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows normal disc position with early degenerative joint surface changes; EMG demonstrates sustained nocturnal masseter and temporalis hyperactivity consistent with parafunctional grinding activity.
#5 Overtraining Syndrome Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (persistent fatigue and recurrent injuries fit overuse, nutritional issues, or systemic illness), subsequent clues build a coherent clinical picture (training history, vital signs, menstrual dysfunction, lab abnormalities), and Clue 5 confirms with metabolic/endocrine findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young female athlete reports persistent fatigue, mood changes, and recurrent lower limb injuries over past 6 months despite continued training.
C2 Activity: Unable to maintain usual training intensity; reports disproportionate fatigue on exertion and slow recovery between sessions despite high motivation.
C3 History: Training volume increased 40% over 3 months; coincided with deliberate caloric restriction for competition. Multiple stress fractures in past 4 months. No previous injury history.
C4 Examination: HR and BP lower than expected for age; slow resting heart rate. Decreased muscle bulk in legs. Generalized weakness on manual muscle testing (4/5). Menarche at age 14, normal cycle until 8 months ago.
C5 Imaging: DEXA scan: T-score −2.5 spine, −2.1 femoral neck (osteopenia range). Serum 25-OH vitamin D 18 ng/mL, iron studies low-normal, TSH elevated at 5.2 mIU/L.
#6 Heart Failure Heart
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 5 duplicates Clue 4 examination findings verbatim and adds no new diagnostic information; the puzzle lacks progression and clinical coherence (cardiac pathology should show clearer exam or echo findings by Clue 5).
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 55-year-old with prior chemotherapy presents with exertional dyspnea and reduced exercise tolerance discovered during routine follow-up.
C2 Activity: Unable to exercise at previously tolerated intensities. Mild dyspnea with brisk walking. Asymptomatic at rest.
C3 History: Breast cancer treated 18 months ago with anthracycline-based chemotherapy. No prior cardiac history. Excellent overall cancer prognosis.
C4 Examination: Well-appearing, vital signs stable. No edema, no JVD, no crackles. Heart sounds normal.
C5 Imaging: Resting heart rate 88, BP 128/82. Cardiac auscultation: S1 and S2 normal, no murmurs or gallops. Lungs clear bilaterally. No lower limb edema or JVD.
#7 Femoral Stress Fracture Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (groin/hip pain in runners fits multiple diagnoses), clues progressively add running context and examination specificity, and imaging definitively confirms; fair difficulty for physio student level.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Distance runner reports groin or anterior hip pain that worsens during long runs.
C2 Activity: Unable to run without severe limp; pain worse with impact activities. Antalgic gait restricts hip extension and loading.
C3 History: Significant training volume increase, female athlete with energy deficiency. Menarche delayed or amenorrhea.
C4 Examination: Groin pain with passive hip flexion, adduction, and internal rotation (FADI). Anterior hip pain with hip extension and axial loading. Hip abductor weakness noted. Positive hop test reproduction of groin pain.
C5 Imaging: X-rays may appear normal initially, CT or MRI shows stress fracture on femoral neck. Compression-side fractures seen.
#8 Vertebral Compression Fracture Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (progressive back pain and height loss fit multiple spinal pathologies), clues build systematically through postural changes and examination, and imaging with DEXA confirms osteoporotic fracture; logical and educational progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Older adult with insidious progressive back pain and progressive height loss.
C2 Activity: Unable to maintain upright posture for prolonged periods; stooping and kyphotic positioning for comfort; difficulty walking distances due to postural fatigue.
C3 History: Multiple previous compression fractures, chronic condition, and associated with corticosteroid use.
C4 Examination: Increased thoracic kyphosis with fixed deformity; acute midline tenderness over multiple thoracic vertebrae; no neurological deficit; pain reproduced on axial loading.
C5 Imaging: Imaging shows multiple level compression fractures, significant kyphotic deformity, and dEXA scan confirms osteoporosis.
#9 Cervical Spinal Cord Injury Cervical Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 names 'cervical fracture-dislocation' and 'severe quadriplegia' directly, eliminating diagnostic challenge; Clues 2–5 are unnecessarily verbose and mix complaint/activity/exam/imaging poorly; the puzzle is too explicit from the start.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Elderly patient with cervical fracture-dislocation from fall causing severe quadriplegia.
C2 Activity: Elderly patient with osteoporosis experienced a fall from standing. Onset was immediate with severe weakness in all limbs.
C3 History: Patient unable to perform any self-care activities. Requires assist for all activities of daily living and continuous monitoring for respiratory status.
C4 Examination: Fall occurred without significant trauma protection. Patient is known to have long-standing osteoporosis. No prior spinal pathology or neurological history.
C5 Imaging: Motor: absent movement in all four limbs bilaterally. Sensory: pinprick/touch loss below C5 dermatome with preserved sensation above. Reflexes: absent throughout lower extremities; biceps reflexes present but hyperreflexic. Respiratory: shallow breathing, unable to generate adequate tidal volume without mechanical support.
#10 Post-ACL Reconstruction Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonable, but Clue 4 (soft endpoint on Lachman, positive anterior drawer) suggests graft failure or insufficient healing—creating clinical ambiguity that conflicts with Clue 5 (intact graft with good incorporation); clarify exam findings or graft status.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 24-year-old athlete with acute knee pain and swelling. Onset 4 weeks ago after injury during skiing. Unable to tolerate walking and light activities without discomfort.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with dynamic control: cutting and pivoting movements cause sense of instability or 'giving way'. Single-leg stance poor.
C3 History: Injury mechanism: hyperextension and sudden deceleration during skiing. Treated surgically with graft reconstruction 4 weeks ago. No prior knee surgery or ligament injury history.
C4 Examination: Knee flexion 125 degrees with soft-end feel, full extension achieved. Quadriceps 4+/5, hamstring 4/5. Anterior drawer test 1+ with firm endpoint. Lachman test positive with soft endpoint. No effusion on palpation.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows intact patellar tendon graft with good incorporation. Knee shows normal alignment. No meniscal involvement.
#11 Fibromyalgia Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Complaint is appropriately broad (widespread pain fits multiple conditions), each clue adds genuine new information, and the progression logically narrows toward fibromyalgia by Clue 5.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged person with widespread musculoskeletal pain that comes and goes across the body.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with stress, cold weather, and poor sleep; improves with gentle movement and warmth.
C3 History: Gradual onset over weeks following major life stressor; concurrent non-musculoskeletal symptoms including unexplained fatigue, poor concentration, and sleep disturbance. No prior significant medical history.
C4 Examination: Tender points at characteristic sites (bilateral occiput, trapezius, supraspinatus, lateral epicondyle, gluteus, greater trochanter, knee). Normal power 5/5 throughout. Full active and passive ROM all joints. No focal neurological signs. Widespread allodynia to light palpation.
C5 Imaging: All standard imaging normal; diagnosis based on clinical criteria including widespread pain index.
#12 Lower Back Pain - Work-Related Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue structure is confused—C2 repeats C1 information (work-related + onset timing), C3 mixes examination-level findings with activity restrictions, and C4/C5 overlap significantly with duplicated exam findings; the clinical narrative is incoherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Manual worker reports low back pain triggered by occupational activities.
C2 Activity: Manual worker reports acute-on-chronic low back discomfort. Symptoms began 6 months ago, initially mild, now interfering with work capacity.
C3 History: Unable to sustain lifting >15kg. Symptoms escalate during 2+ hour desk work. Pain resolves substantially with 30 min rest in supine.
C4 Examination: Limited spinal flexion, weak core muscles, positive straight leg raise bilaterally, muscle guarding.
C5 Imaging: Lumbar flexion limited to 40°, pain at 20°. Hip flexors tight bilaterally. Core strength 3/5. Palpable paraspinal tenderness left > right. Negative neural tension tests, normal lower limb reflexes and sensation.
#13 Multiple Sclerosis CNS
✓ READY
Verdict: Complaint is reasonably broad (progressive neuro symptoms fit several conditions), and each clue progressively narrows the differential with new clinical data; Clue 5 is definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 55-year-old man with progressive difficulty walking and cognitive slowing over 5 years.
C2 Activity: Walks very slowly with walker, fatigue significantly limits activity, problems with attention.
C3 History: Gradual onset without clear relapses. History of single demyelinating event 6 years ago. Progressive worsening since.
C4 Examination: Spastic paraparesis (MRC 3/5), hyperreflexia and extensor plantars bilaterally, positive Romberg test, marked slowing of thought processing.
C5 Imaging: Spinal MRI shows cervical cord atrophy; brain MRI shows extensive white matter changes and gadolinium-enhancing lesions.
#14 Congestive Heart Failure Cardiopulmonary
✓ READY
Verdict: Complaint is broad enough for a cardiac rehabilitation referral, each clue adds distinct clinical information, and the progression from symptoms → functional limitation → history → exam → imaging is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old male is referred to cardiac rehabilitation with breathlessness, exercise intolerance, and bilateral ankle swelling that worsens throughout the day.
C2 Activity: Becomes breathless walking from the bedroom to the kitchen (NYHA Class III). Reports needing 3 pillows to sleep (orthopnoea) and waking at night gasping for air (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea).
C3 History: History of myocardial infarction 5 years ago. Hypertension and type 2 diabetes. On multiple cardiac medications including ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, and diuretic.
C4 Examination: Elevated JVP. Bilateral pitting oedema to mid-shins. Bibasal fine crackles on auscultation.
C5 Imaging: Echocardiogram shows significantly reduced ejection fraction (severely depressed LV systolic function). BNP markedly elevated. Chest X-ray shows enlarged heart silhouette, upper lobe pulmonary venous redistribution, and bilateral pleural effusions.
#15 Legg-Calvé-Perthes Disease Hip
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is quite specific (hip pain + limp + referred knee pain in a 6-year-old) and may limit differential to only a few conditions; otherwise progression is sound but the opening clue could be slightly broader.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient Boy age 6 has hip pain and limp; pain referred to thigh and knee.
C2 Activity: Antalgic gait, limitation of hip abduction and internal rotation. Weight-bearing difficult.
C3 History: Insidious onset, no trauma. Otherwise healthy, similar condition in maternal uncle.
C4 Examination: Positive Trendelenburg sign, hip flexion contracture. Limited internal rotation, pain on log roll test.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows femoral head deformity with widened physis and early sclerosis; MRI reveals signal loss in femoral epiphysis consistent with bone ischemia.
#16 Chemotherapy-Induced Peripheral Neuropathy Peripheral Nerves
✓ READY
Verdict: Complaint is broad (peripheral neuropathy has many etiologies), each clue adds causally relevant information, and Clue 3's chemotherapy history is the key pivot that makes Clue 5 definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Cancer survivor with gradually progressive numbness and burning pain in the feet, starting during treatment and continuing for 2 years after.
C2 Activity: Unable to walk barefoot on uneven ground, frequent falls, difficulty with fine motor tasks like fastening buttons, sleep disrupted by foot pain.
C3 History: Single-agent platinum exposure for gynecological malignancy completed 2 years ago. Neuropathy onset coincided with treatment cycles, has not improved despite stopping chemotherapy. No prior peripheral neuropathy or diabetes.
C4 Examination: Marked sensory loss distally, weakness of foot intrinsics, absent reflexes, protective gait, hyperesthesia to touch.
C5 Imaging: EMG/NCS shows distal lower-limb axonal sensorimotor neuropathy with reduced amplitudes and normal conduction velocities, consistent with length-dependent pattern. No active denervation.
#17 Achilles Tendon Rupture Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Complaint is characteristic but fits acute injury broadly, and the clues progressively confirm acute rupture; Thompson test and imaging findings clinch diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Sudden severe pain in the back of the lower leg during a pushing or jumping movement. Patient heard or felt a pop.
C2 Activity: Cannot walk normally. Refuses to put weight on the leg and drags foot when attempting to move. Cannot plantarflex the ankle actively.
C3 History: Patient was pushing off to jump or sprint when injury occurred. No prior ankle or calf problems. Age 45.
C4 Examination: Positive Thompson test, palpable gap in tendon, complete loss of plantarflexion strength and severe swelling with ecchymosis.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound or MRI confirms tendon discontinuity and imaging shows acute rupture with gap between tendon ends. Hemorrhage visible.
#18 Adhesive Capsulitis of the Ankle Ankle & Foot
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Adhesive capsulitis of the ankle is extremely rare and not a standard diagnosis in physiotherapy practice; the puzzle would confuse students and is clinically implausible as a core teaching condition.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports progressive ankle stiffness and discomfort that worsened gradually over several months, limiting ability to move the ankle in multiple directions.
C2 Activity: Cannot walk normally or wear regular shoes; ankle feels "locked" and resists movement in all directions, worse with weight-bearing activities.
C3 History: Develops months after initial injury and unresponsive to standard rehabilitation.
C4 Examination: Capsular restriction pattern, all ankle motions reduced, and talocrural hypomobility.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows synovial thickening, capsular changes, and X-rays may be normal.
#19 Anterior Cruciate Ligament Tear Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is somewhat vague (chronic knee pain with giving way fits multiple conditions), but the progression is logical; Clue 4 placement is awkward (reads like history rather than exam findings) and should be reordered.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Older patient with chronic knee pain and recurrent episodes of giving way during daily activities.
C2 Activity: Stairs, uneven terrain provoke instability, patient has adapted with activity avoidance, and no recent acute injury recalled.
C3 History: Remote injury from years prior, progressive compensatory movement patterns, and concurrent symptoms of mechanical catching.
C4 Examination: Sustained knee injury several years ago during pivoting movement; progressive onset of symptoms with recent acceleration in frequency. Patient reports brief episodes of knee 'locking' or catching sensation.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows degenerative appearance of ACL remnant with bone marrow edema and secondary osteoarthritis changes.
#20 Hypermobility Syndrome Whole Body
✓ READY
Verdict: Complaint is appropriately broad (joint pain + fatigue in a child), family history and Beighton score narrow progressively, and Clue 5 confidently suggests heritable connective tissue disorder; educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 9-year-old girl presents with recurring joint pain affecting multiple joints and fatigue. Her parents report she bruises easily and has always seemed more flexible than her peers.
C2 Activity: Ability to perform unusual contortions, frequent joint dislocations that worsen with movement.
C3 History: Lifelong excessive flexibility noted in childhood; multiple family members with similar presentation and skin fragility. Prior treatments have been mainly supportive.
C4 Examination: Beighton score 7/9; marked passive range of motion in fingers, thumbs, and trunk flexion. Skin appears thin and translucent over joints with visible venous pattern. Multiple small scars and residual bruising on forearms and shins.
C5 Imaging: Imaging shows no structural joint damage or ligamentous injury despite recurrent dislocations. Genetic testing unavailable in routine practice; diagnosis based on clinical criteria and family history consistent with heritable connective tissue disorder.
#21 Myofascial Pain Syndrome Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (covers fibromyalgia, chronic pain syndromes, etc.), each subsequent clue adds genuine information, and Clue 5 clinches diagnosis through exclusion; fair and educational progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports persistent muscle pain in multiple body areas; symptoms don't follow a consistent nerve or dermatomal pattern.
C2 Activity: Unable to maintain sustained posture or repetitive activity; symptoms worsen with prolonged muscle engagement but improve with rest and movement variation.
C3 History: Symptoms developed insidiously over months to years; patient reports significant psychological stress and poor sleep quality; multiple specialists consulted with no structural diagnosis identified.
C4 Examination: Widespread taut bands, multiple trigger points in different muscles, variable referred pain patterns.
C5 Imaging: Normal nerve conduction studies and imaging; symptoms don't follow dermatome distribution.
#22 Pressure Injury Stage 2-3 Skin
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too specific (localised breakdown over bony prominence essentially names pressure injury) and Clue 4/5 jump to advanced stages (necrotic tissue, bone exposure, infection) making earlier clues inconsistent with severity described.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Elderly patient with localised area of skin breakdown over a bony prominence. Reports discomfort over past 2–3 weeks.
C2 Activity: Cannot reposition or weight-bear independently. Confined to bed or chair for extended periods without pressure relief.
C3 History: Injury developed insidiously over weeks with no clear trauma. Multiple comorbidities: diabetes, poor nutrition, incontinence. Previous dressing changes and repositioning attempts ineffective.
C4 Examination: Large open wound with necrotic tissue, significant drainage. Surrounding erythema.
C5 Imaging: Imaging shows extensive tissue involvement, possible bone exposure. Infection confirmed.
#23 Muscular Dystrophy Muscle
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 3 strongly hints diagnosis through family history and progression pattern; consider generalizing family history or moving it later, but overall progression is sound and playable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 16-year-old boy with progressive difficulty climbing stairs and rising from chairs over the past 2 years.
C2 Activity: Recently started using wheelchair for community mobility; reports shortness of breath on minimal exertion and occasional chest discomfort with activity.
C3 History: Onset age 12–13 with insidious progression. No trauma or viral illness preceding symptoms. Parents note similar pattern in maternal uncle (deceased at age 20). Physiotherapy and bracing have slowed but not halted decline.
C4 Examination: Severe proximal and generalized weakness, minimal movement in lower extremities, cardiac murmur, respiratory muscle weakness.
C5 Imaging: Echocardiogram shows dilated cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction and emg shows myopathic pattern. Elevated ck >40,000.
#24 Lateral Ankle Sprain Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (ankle pain + swelling from twisting fits many ankle conditions), each clue adds specificity, and Clue 5 confirms diagnosis; solid clinical-to-imaging progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged patient with ankle pain and swelling after a twisting injury during walking.
C2 Activity: Cannot tolerate uneven ground or lateral ankle weight-bearing; ankle becomes stiff and swollen with activity despite normal ROM in plantarflexion and dorsiflexion.
C3 History: Injury occurred 3 days ago. No prior ankle injuries or instability. No systemic disease or inflammatory history. No current medications affecting healing.
C4 Examination: Lateral ankle tenderness,, anterior drawer test positive, limited range of motion with inversion, and plantarflexion.
C5 Imaging: X-rays rule out fracture and ultrasound shows ligament. MRI demonstrates partial ligament tear with surrounding inflammation.
#25 Posterior Cruciate Ligament Tear Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is sufficiently vague (posterior knee pain + heaviness fits multiple conditions), each clue progressively narrows through mechanism and exam findings, and Clue 5 is definitive; good diagnostic flow.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient presents with posterior knee pain and vague 'heaviness' in the lower leg during prolonged sitting.
C2 Activity: Symptoms exacerbated by activities requiring sustained knee flexion and minimal acute instability but functional impairment noted.
C3 History: Remote or poorly recalled mechanism; patient notes gradual onset of calf swelling and tightness over weeks to months. Minimal initial trauma recall.
C4 Examination: Posterior tibial sag at 90° knee flexion; posterior drawer test positive but less pronounced than expected for acute injury. Quadriceps atrophy and weakness with positive lag sign. Hip external rotators weak.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows PCL fibre disruption or avulsion from tibial insertion; secondary posterolateral knee OA and posterior capsular thickening. No ACL or meniscal involvement.
#26 Cauda Equina Syndrome Lumbar Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 3 mention of metastatic cancer and radiation therapy is highly suggestive of the diagnosis before exam findings; consider generalizing to 'recent spinal pathology' or moving cancer context later.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Progressive lower back pain with bilateral leg symptoms and functional decline over recent weeks.
C2 Activity: Worsening despite rest, night prominent, and progressive functional decline.
C3 History: Known metastatic cancer. Recent lumbar radiation therapy. Constitutional symptoms (weight loss, fever). Acute worsening over past 5 days despite analgesia.
C4 Examination: Lower limb neurological exam: grade 4/5 bilateral hip flexors and ankle dorsiflexors. Saddle anaesthesia present. Diminished anal tone. Lower limb reflexes variable. Straight leg raise bilaterally limited by pain at 45 degrees.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows metastatic disease with epidural spinal cord compression.
#27 Osgood-Schlatter Disease Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (anterior knee pain in adolescent athletes fits multiple conditions), each clue adds specificity, and Clue 5 with apophyseal fragmentation is definitive; engaging clinical story.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Adolescent athlete with anterior knee pain and localized swelling below the kneecap, worse with jumping and sprinting during a period of rapid growth.
C2 Activity: Unable to play sport comfortably; pain worsens with running, jumping, and ascending stairs. Symptoms plateau during periods of slower growth but flare with accelerated growth spurts.
C3 History: Involved in basketball and volleyball for 2+ years; recently increased training intensity. Reports bilateral quadriceps and hamstring tightness. No prior knee injury or surgery.
C4 Examination: Prominent tibial tuberosity with localized tenderness, knee extension against resistance reproduces, and palpable apophyseal prominence.
C5 Imaging: X-rays reveal irregular fragmentation and calcification at the tibial tuberosity apophysis. MRI shows bone marrow edema and soft tissue inflammation localized to the apophyseal insertion without intra-articular pathology.
#28 Hallux Valgus Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is suitably broad (great toe pain with footwear fits other forefoot conditions), clues progress logically through activity limitations to deformity to imaging confirmation; straightforward and playable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports pain at the base of the great toe, worse with prolonged walking and footwear pressure.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens significantly with walking on hard surfaces and prolonged standing; patient avoids certain footwear due to discomfort at medial foot.
C3 History: Gradual development of deformity and family history common. Often begins in adolescence or early adulthood may worsen with pregnancy.
C4 Examination: Visible medial deviation of great toe and bony prominence at first metatarsal head. Bursa inflammation may be present possible second toe crowding.
C5 Imaging: X-rays show increased intermetatarsal angle, hallux valgus angle, joint degenerative changes possible, and assess first metatarsal position.
#29 Tibial Plateau Fracture Knee
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 4 describes ACL tear findings (anterior drawer/Lachman positive) but Clue 5 specifies tibial plateau fracture; exam findings don't match imaging diagnosis, creating incoherence.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Chronic knee pain and recurrent instability episodes following a traumatic knee injury several months ago.
C2 Activity: Unable to navigate stairs confidently; episodes of sudden knee buckling during weight-bearing, particularly on uneven surfaces or with pivoting movements.
C3 History: She has a history of osteoporosis and was previously treated for a distal radius fracture two years ago. She takes calcium supplementation but is not on bisphosphonate therapy.
C4 Examination: Positive anterior drawer test and Lachman test; mild joint effusion present. Pain with valgus stress testing in full extension.
C5 Imaging: CT imaging demonstrates partial healing of depressed lateral tibial plateau with residual articular step-off; marginal osteophytes and early post-traumatic osteoarthritis noted.
#30 Scaphoid Fracture Wrist & Hand
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 3 'six months ago' with Clue 4 'scaphoid compression test positive' is somewhat redundant; consider varying the timeline or removing the direct mechanism reference from history.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old man presents with radial-sided wrist pain and functional loss following a fall six months ago.
C2 Activity: Patient reports pain and weakness with gripping and twisting activities; avoids weight-bearing through the wrist.
C3 History: He sustained a fall from height six months ago with residual wrist stiffness. He has no history of inflammatory arthritis or prior hand surgery.
C4 Examination: Marked tenderness in the radial wrist anatomical space, restricted radial wrist deviation, and significantly diminished grip strength. Scaphoid compression test positive.
C5 Imaging: CT imaging demonstrates scaphoid non-union with sclerotic fracture margins. MRI reveals avascular necrosis of the proximal pole.
#31 Computer Vision Syndrome Eye
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (eye strain, headache fit many conditions), progression is logical, and Clue 5 clinches with tear testing; fair for a physio student guessing game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Office worker reports eye strain, dry eyes, and headache after prolonged screen time.
C2 Activity: Symptoms worsen with prolonged computer use; improve with reduced screen time and frequent breaks.
C3 History: Unable to maintain near vision comfort for extended periods; symptoms persist despite distance breaks, but resolve with overnight rest.
C4 Examination: Reduced tear break-up time, conjunctival injection, accommodation lag, dry eye findings.
C5 Imaging: Normal ophthalmologic imaging; dry eye testing confirms reduced tear production or quality.
#32 Atrial Fibrillation Heart
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Atrial fibrillation is a cardiac arrhythmia outside core physiotherapy scope; while treatable by physios in cardiac rehab, it's primarily a medical diagnosis better suited to medicine-focused games rather than Physiodle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 67-year-old presents with palpitations, dyspnea, and chest discomfort that developed acutely during sleep.
C2 Activity: Symptoms episodic; patient reports palpitations and breathlessness on exertion or at rest unpredictably. No clear postural or positional pattern.
C3 History: Known hypertension and chronic kidney disease. No prior arrhythmia. No recent chest pain history.
C4 Examination: Tachycardic at 115 bpm; pulse regular at rest but patient reports palpitation episodes. Blood pressure 158/92. Heart sounds normal. Lung bases clear.
C5 Imaging: 12-lead ECG shows absent P waves, irregular ventricular rhythm, and fibrillatory baseline confirming atrial fibrillation. Troponin normal. Echo shows normal EF with LA enlargement.
#33 Sever's Disease Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple heel pain conditions, each clue adds genuine information (age, activity pattern, examination findings, imaging), and progression is smooth and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Child presenting with heel pain that worsens with sports activity, particularly running and jumping. Pain localised to posterior heel.
C2 Activity: Unable to tolerate sustained running or jumping. Symptoms improve with activity reduction but return within days of resuming sport. No night pain or resting symptoms.
C3 History: Occurs during growth spurts, typically 8-14 years old. High activity level, tight gastrocnemius-soleus complex.
C4 Examination: Tenderness at calcaneal insertion of Achilles, pain with passive dorsiflexion. Tight calf muscles, pain with squeezing heel.
C5 Imaging: X-rays may show fragmented apophysis, MRI shows apophyseal inflammation. Self-limited condition resolving with growth plate closure.
#34 Labyrinthitis Inner Ear
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 reasonably broad (post-viral dizziness fits vestibular dysfunction, BPPV, etc.), subsequent clues progressively narrow via hearing loss and imaging, and Clue 5 clinches with labyrinthine changes.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports persistent dizziness and imbalance 6 weeks after an acute viral illness. Initial severe spinning sensation has largely resolved, but positional symptoms and difficulty with spatial orientation remain.
C2 Activity: Patient reports feeling unsteady in crowded or visually complex environments. Gait on uneven surfaces is slow and cautious. Recovers well with rest and visual fixation.
C3 History: Acute viral illness 6 weeks ago with initial severe vertigo and nausea. Hearing decline noted; audiometry confirms high-frequency sensorineural loss. No recent head trauma or recurrent episodes.
C4 Examination: Dix-Hallpike now negative, Spontaneous nystagmus absent, Romberg test positive but improved from acute phase, Gait shows cautious pattern but not severely restricted, Dynamic gait testing shows deficits with head turns.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows mild residual labyrinthine enhancement with overall resolving inflammation. No central lesions or structural abnormality. Audiometry confirms high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss consistent with inner ear injury.
#35 Glenohumeral Instability Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 and 2 are redundant (both describe recurrent dislocation with minimal trauma), and History clue should appear earlier; reorganize so Activity adds mechanical context (position-specific triggers) and History provides timeline before Examination detail.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 31-year-old with history of shoulder dislocation reports repeated episodes with minimal trauma.
C2 Activity: A 31-year-old reports recurrent episodes of shoulder slipping out of place, often with minimal provocation. Recently happening more frequently.
C3 History: Unable to throw or lift arm overhead without fear of dislocation. Certain arm positions trigger immediate apprehension and guarding.
C4 Examination: First dislocation occurred 5 years ago. Initially required manual reduction at ED; now dislocates several times monthly with trivial movements. No significant trauma history.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows labral damage with Hill-Sachs lesion, glenoid bone loss. Chronic instability changes.
#36 Cyclist's Palsy Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (hand numbness fits multiple compressive/neuropathic conditions), each clue adds layer (activity reproduction, progressive timeline, nerve testing), and Clue 5 is definitive with NCS findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Road cyclist reports hand numbness affecting ring and little fingers after long rides.
C2 Activity: Symptoms reproducible with sustained grip on drop bars; relief occurs with wrist extension or lateral deviation, and with padded gloves. Cannot sustain long rides without symptom escalation.
C3 History: Progressive over 6 months with increased training volume and hours on bike. No trauma or prior hand/wrist injury. No systemic symptoms or neck pain. Symptoms worse with more aggressive riding positions.
C4 Examination: Positive Tinel's test over ulnar nerve at wrist, weakness in intrinsic hand muscles, sensory loss in ulnar distribution.
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction studies show ulnar nerve slowing at wrist; ultrasound shows compression site.
#37 Sacroiliac Joint Dysfunction Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple pelvic/buttock pain conditions, postpartum history and SIJ provocation tests progressively refine diagnosis, and Clue 5 (bone marrow oedema without erosion) clinches; educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 30-year-old female presents with unilateral left-sided buttock pain that she points to with one finger over the posterior superior iliac spine.
C2 Activity: is worse with prolonged standing, walking uphill, and rolling over in bed. Relieved by sitting. Reports when stepping up onto a curb with the left leg.
C3 History: Symptoms began 4 months postpartum. Vaginal delivery with prolonged second stage of labour. Reports her pelvis felt 'unstable' since delivery.
C4 Examination: Three or more SIJ provocation tests positive: compression test, thigh thrust (posterior shear), Gaenslen's test, and sacral thrust. Positive active straight leg raise (ASLR) test bilaterally. Lumbar spine examination unremarkable.
C5 Imaging: X-rays are normal. MRI shows mild bone marrow oedema at the left SIJ without erosions or sclerosis, consistent with inflammatory changes rather than structural pathology.
#38 Infrapatellar Bursitis Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (anterior knee pain/swelling fit multiple conditions), kneeling-specific activity pattern and localized bursal findings narrow focus, and ultrasound confirms; fair progression for physio students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old male tradesperson presents with anterior knee pain and swelling of two weeks duration. Symptoms began without a specific injury incident.
C2 Activity: Pain with kneeling on knees; cannot kneel for extended periods without significant discomfort.
C3 History: Symptoms triggered by work activities involving sustained kneeling. No acute injury recalled; symptoms developed insidiously over several weeks.
C4 Examination: Palpable swelling anterior to proximal tibia; tenderness localized deep to patellar tendon. No effusion in knee joint; passive knee flexion reproduces anterior pain.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates bursal enlargement with fluid collection.
#39 Diabetic Foot Ulcer Foot
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too specific—purulent drainage, malodor, fever, and cellulitis together essentially name a diabetic foot ulcer, leaving minimal diagnostic room; also contains duplicated examination detail ('involvement involvement').
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports progressive foot ulceration with signs of infection 2 months duration. Purulent drainage, malodor, and surrounding cellulitis present. Fever reported.
C2 Activity: Patient reports progressive foot pain and drainage from lower leg wound over 2 months. Fever and general malaise present.
C3 History: Unable to bear weight on affected foot. Difficulty with ambulation and lower limb self-care activities.
C4 Examination: Large ulcer with exposed subcutaneous tissue and bone involvement involvement, Surrounding cellulitis is significant, Systemic signs of infection present. Fever, tachycardia, No protective sensation, Pulses absent in dorsum.
C5 Imaging: Examination reveals large deep ulcer with granulation tissue and exposed structures. Complete loss of protective sensation in foot. Dorsalis pedis pulses absent bilaterally. No blanching erythema beyond ulcer margins.
#40 Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (joint pain, fatigue, dizziness fit hypermobility, chronic fatigue, autonomic dysfunction), each clue adds distinct layers (activity intolerance, lifelong sprains, hypermobility score, genetic confirmation), and progression is logical and engaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 34-year-old woman presents with widespread joint pain and fatigue. Reports dizziness when standing up quickly and has noticed her skin is unusually stretchy.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with sustained standing and aerobic activities. Fatigue out of proportion to exertion level. Symptoms worsen significantly with physical activity despite attempting graded exercise.
C3 History: Lifelong history of joint sprains and dislocations. Multiple hospitalizations in childhood for unexplained bleeding. Family history of hypermobility and connective tissue problems. No recent trauma.
C4 Examination: Beighton score 7/9. Skin velvety and translucent with visible veining. Tachycardia (104 bpm) at rest, blood pressure drops 18 mmHg on standing. Joint palpation reveals generalized laxity without inflammation.
C5 Imaging: Genetic testing confirms EDS; imaging shows degenerative changes despite young age.
#41 Long COVID Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (post-viral fatigue syndrome, deconditioning, pulmonary fibrosis, myalgic encephalomyelitis all fit), progression is logical, and Clue 5 clinches with specific imaging findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old with severe initial COVID requiring ICU admission reports ongoing dyspnea and reduced exercise tolerance 6 months later.
C2 Activity: Cannot return to work due to dyspnea with stairs. Exercise intolerance marked. Persistent cough.
C3 History: Severe COVID with pneumonia and 10 days mechanical ventilation. Post-discharge: progressive fatigue worsened by minimal exertion, night sweats, and brain fog. Gradual decline over 6 months despite apparent medical recovery. No prior cardiopulmonary disease.
C4 Examination: Decreased respiratory muscle strength. Able to walk 200 meters before dyspnea onset. Oxygen saturation drops to 92% with exertion.
C5 Imaging: Chest CT shows resolved pulmonary edema but persistent ground-glass opacities suggesting ongoing inflammation. PFTs show reduced DLCO and exercise-induced desaturation.
#42 Proximal Hamstring Tendinopathy Thigh
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple causes of ischial pain, each clue adds distinct information (activity pattern, training history, specific clinical tests, imaging confirmation), and the diagnosis emerges naturally.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Cyclist with chronic pain at ischium; activity-dependent discomfort.
C2 Activity: Worse during intervals and climbs; improved with cadence reduction and seat height adjustment.
C3 History: She has a background of recurrent sacroiliac joint pain and recently increased her cycling training volume by 40% over eight weeks. No prior hamstring injuries.
C4 Examination: Localized ischial tenderness, positive Puranen-Orava test. Pain with resisted hip flexion.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic swelling and vascularity within proximal hamstring tendon at ischial insertion; dynamic imaging reproduces pain with hip flexion against resistance.
#43 Psoriatic Arthritis Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 mentions both skin changes and nail symptoms alongside joint pain, which is somewhat specific; consider removing or softening one skin descriptor in C1 to broaden initial possibilities.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 42-year-old male with progressive pain in hands and feet. Associated with skin changes and nail symptoms.
C2 Activity: Morning stiffness lasting over 1 hour. Pain primarily in DIP joints of hands and forefoot. Symptoms improve with activity.
C3 History: Skin lesions preceded joint symptoms by approximately 1 year. No prior joint disease. Exacerbations correlate with high stress periods. Father has inflammatory arthritis.
C4 Examination: Psoriatic skin plaques on extensor surfaces. Nail pitting and onycholysis visible. Ray-like swelling of fingers (sausage digits).
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows pencil-in-cup deformities and osteolysis. Asymmetric joint involvement. MRI shows inflammation and bone erosions.
#44 Calcaneal Fracture Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple heel injuries, Clue 3 establishes mechanism and acuity, Clue 4 adds physical signs including red flag (compartment syndrome), and Clue 5 confirms with characteristic imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient with severe heel pain following a fall from standing height.
C2 Activity: Severe pain with heel weight-bearing, massive swelling. Unable to ambulate.
C3 History: High-energy mechanism (3-metre fall onto hard surface). Immediate swelling and inability to weight-bear from time of injury. No significant comorbidities or prior heel pathology.
C4 Examination: Severe diffuse heel swelling with ecchymosis. Loss of normal heel contour. Positive bulge sign. Severe pain on passive ankle dorsiflexion and palpation of heel. Calf tenderness present; compartment syndrome suspected clinically.
C5 Imaging: CT scan shows intra-articular comminution, loss of Bohler's angle. Subtalar involvement.
#45 Ulnar Nerve Palsy Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (multiple nerve/root lesions present with finger numbness), occupation provides useful context without naming the condition, and progression logically culminates in specific EMG/imaging findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient with hand, numbness in ring, and little fingers progressing over months.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform pinch grip or manipulate small objects. Difficulty with finger spreading and coordinated finger movements.
C3 History: Insidious onset over several months. Occupational: prolonged computer work with elbow in flexion. No acute trauma or significant injury history.
C4 Examination: Interosseous atrophy, weakness of finger abduction and adduction, sensory loss in ulnar distribution, Froment sign positive.
C5 Imaging: EMG shows slowing across elbow, ultrasound shows nerve thickening at cubital tunnel, and mRI shows nerve compression.
#46 Tarsal Coalition Foot
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 mentions 'Child' (grammatically awkward) and 'growth spurt' which together fairly narrow focus; consider rewording to 'A young patient develops foot pain and limited ankle motion during adolescence' to soften specificity.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient Child develops foot pain and limited ankle motion during growth spurt.
C2 Activity: During walking and sports, patient cannot invert or evert the foot; ankle feels stiff and rigid rather than mobile.
C3 History: Insidious onset age 10-14, family history of foot problems, and progressive symptoms.
C4 Examination: Special tests reveal severely restricted subtalar motion in isolation; ankle dorsi/plantarflexion preserved. Foot appears persistently flat with rigid midfoot. Heel varus present on weight-bearing.
C5 Imaging: CT scan shows talocalcaneal or talonavicular coalition and osseous or fibrocartilaginous bridge.
#47 Idiopathic Adolescent Scoliosis Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (mild back discomfort + asymmetry fits multiple spinal and postural conditions), Clue 2 distinguishes from inflammatory causes, and serial imaging in Clue 5 clinches progressive idiopathic scoliosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Teenager reports mild back discomfort and notices uneven clothing fit.
C2 Activity: Patient continues to participate fully in sports and physical education without restrictions or symptom exacerbation during activity.
C3 History: Curve detected at age 10, slowly progressive over 3 years, and still growing.
C4 Examination: Left lumbar scoliosis with rib hump, mild pain with spinal palpation, and flexible curve.
C5 Imaging: Serial X-rays show Cobb angle progression from 22 to 35 degrees and immature skeletal age.
#48 Spondylodiscitis Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 naming 'fever' and 'systemic symptoms' together with 'severe back pain' is somewhat directive toward infection; consider moving fever mention to Clue 3 or softening language to broaden initial differential.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient with severe back pain, systemic symptoms, and fever present.
C2 Activity: Unable to maintain upright posture; symptoms not relieved by position changes or rest; night pain present.
C3 History: Risk factors present: immunosuppression, recent infection, IV drug use, spinal procedure.
C4 Examination: Midline vertebral tenderness over affected segment; fever (if not already documented); possible lower extremity neurological deficit or myelopathic signs if cord compression present.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows disc space narrowing, vertebral body involvement, and inflammatory signal changes.
#49 Wrist Sprain Wrist & Hand
✗ REWORK
Verdict: The clinical presentation (persistent worsening after 2 years, clicking, clunk test positive, partial ligamentous damage) strongly suggests scaphoid nonunion, midcarpal instability, or SLAC wrist rather than simple 'wrist sprain'; either reframe as a specific carpal ligament injury or clarify the diagnosis intent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old office worker presents with persistent wrist discomfort that has been gradually worsening over the past two years.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with sustained gripping and heavy object manipulation. Clicking sensation with radial and ulnar deviation. Pain exacerbated during typing.
C3 History: Suffered a fall onto an outstretched hand two years ago that was initially treated conservatively. Symptoms never fully resolved and have progressively worsened despite splinting.
C4 Examination: Positive clunk test with radial deviation. Marked loss of grip strength on affected side. Palpable clicking over mid-carpal region with passive wrist motion. Pain with axial loading through thumb.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows normal alignment without fracture or significant gap widening. MRI reveals partial ligamentous damage to dorsal wrist stabilizers with mild inflammatory changes but preserved carpal architecture.
#50 Occupational Asthma Respiratory System
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (workplace respiratory exposure fits multiple occupational lung diseases), work-off/work-on symptom pattern is classic and diagnostic, and Clue 5 with peak flow variability and specific IgE confirms occupational asthma.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Healthcare or animal facility worker reports progressive respiratory symptoms at work.
C2 Activity: Symptoms resolve on days off and weekends, recur upon return to workplace.
C3 History: Progressive sensitization over months to years and initial symptoms mild but worsening.
C4 Examination: Baseline forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1) reduced; significant reversibility or fluctuation noted across shift timings; wheezing audible during work-shift samples.
C5 Imaging: Peak flow diary shows ≥20% variability between work days and non-work days; specific IgE elevated to workplace-relevant allergen; baseline spirometry shows obstructive pattern consistent with chronic exposure effect.
#51 Radial Nerve Palsy Upper Limb
✓ READY
Verdict: Good progression from acute wrist drop and weakness (C1-C2) through compressed sleep position history (C3) to focal sensory loss and motor deficit localization (C4), with EMG/ultrasound confirmation (C5); fair difficulty for a physio student.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient woke unable to lift hand at wrist. Arm was in unusual compressed position during sleep. No preceding trauma.
C2 Activity: Cannot extend wrist or fingers. Wrist held in flexion (droops downward). Has numbness over back of hand and thumb.
C3 History: Acute onset after sleeping on arm in compressed position across armchair. No trauma, no prior episodes. No sensory or motor symptoms before sleep.
C4 Examination: Wrist drop present. Finger extension severely weak (grades 1-2). Elbow extension normal strength. Sensory loss confined to first web space and dorsal thumb. No other neurological deficits distally.
C5 Imaging: EMG shows conduction block at mid-humerus and ultrasound shows nerve compression. X-ray normal.
#52 Intercostal Muscle Strain Thorax
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C5 (Imaging) is redundant—it repeats information already covered in C4 (focal tenderness, reproduction with movement) and adds little new diagnostic value beyond confirming no serious pathology.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports chest wall pain, described as sharp or aching. Chronic onset over weeks to months.
C2 Activity: Movements involving trunk rotation and deep breathing exacerbate pain; difficulty with overhead reaching and lifting.
C3 History: Gradual onset following repeated heavy lifting 3 months ago. No acute trauma. Previous similar episodes resolved with rest. No systemic illness or referred symptoms from viscera.
C4 Examination: Focal tenderness over lateral chest wall, between ribs 4–6. Pain reproduces with palpation and resisted trunk rotation. Normal neurological examination. Costochondral junctions non-tender.
C5 Imaging: CT chest shows no fracture, effusion, or pneumothorax. MRI demonstrates muscle oedema and minor scarring in external intercostal muscles at symptomatic level; no nerve root compression.
#53 Metatarsalgia Ankle & Foot

Verdict: C5 is not imaging but rather duplicates examination findings (tenderness, weak intrinsics, calluses), breaking the intended progression from complaint through imaging; imaging clue is missing.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient with forefoot pain affecting second, third metatarsal heads, pain with walking, and standing activities.
C2 Activity: Gradual-onset forefoot pain worsened by walking and prolonged standing. Patient notes pain under the ball of the foot.
C3 History: Cannot tolerate prolonged weight-bearing. Symptoms improve with wider toe-box footwear and activity reduction but return with running or long periods on feet.
C4 Examination: Localized metatarsal head tenderness and with great toe dorsiflexion. Intrinsic muscle weakness possible plantar callus formation.
C5 Imaging: Tenderness over 2nd and 3rd metatarsal heads. Positive first metatarsophalangeal joint dorsiflexion test. Weak foot intrinsics on manual testing. Plantar calluses under forefoot.
#54 Clubfoot Foot

Verdict: C1 is too specific (names congenital foot deformity plus age/prior treatment), C3 and C4 repeat the same treatment history information, and the clinical story lacks coherence for a guessing puzzle rather than a case presentation.

C2 Activity: 18-month-old with persistent foot deformity and gait dysfunction.
C3 History: Treated with Ponseti casting from birth, casting discontinued prematurely, and no maintenance splinting.
C4 Examination: Underwent casting for congenital foot deformity starting at birth; parents discontinued treatment before completion and did not use maintenance orthosis.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows incomplete correction with persistent talus deviation and calcaneal positioning abnormal.
#55 Costochondritis Thoracic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from acute chest wall pain and breathing aggravation (C1-C2) through acute inflammatory onset (C3) to reproducible costochondral tenderness and visible inflammation (C4) and imaging exclusion of serious pathology (C5); educational and playable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young person with acute chest wall pain, worse with movement and breathing.
C2 Activity: Pain is aggravated by deep breathing, coughing, and direct pressure to the chest wall. She avoids lying on her left side and finds relief only with complete rest and analgesia.
C3 History: Acute onset over days without preceding trauma. Multiple investigations performed to rule out cardiac and pulmonary causes.
C4 Examination: Visible swelling and erythema at the costochondral junctions. Reproducible tenderness on direct palpation of affected cartilage. No cardiac or respiratory findings.
C5 Imaging: Imaging shows no rib fractures, malignancy, or cardiac pathology. Costochondral changes consistent with benign inflammatory swelling.
#56 Sinus Tarsi Syndrome Ankle/Foot
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 is quite detailed and somewhat specific (inversion injury + persistent + uneven ground = sinus tarsi is fairly narrowed); clue could be broader as a chief complaint (e.g., 'persistent lateral ankle pain after inversion injury'), though the puzzle remains playable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old recreational runner presents with persistent lateral ankle pain and a feeling of ankle instability following an inversion sprain three months ago. Symptoms are reproduced by walking on uneven ground.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform lateral ankle proprioceptive tasks; difficulty with stairs and single-leg stance; gives way on direction changes.
C3 History: Follows ankle inversion injury and persistent despite ankle rehabilitation.
C4 Examination: Lateral hindfoot tenderness with deep palpation between talus and calcaneus; positive talar tilt and anterior drawer; pain with inversion stress; subtalar joint mobility reduced.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates oedema and inflammatory changes localized to subtalar coalition region with associated synovitis; weightbearing CT reveals subtle talocalcaneal rotation and reduced posterior facet alignment.
#57 Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Systemic
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 explicitly names 'malar (butterfly) rash'—a near-pathognomonic feature of SLE—making this too specific for a guessing game; C2 largely repeats C1; puzzle does not allow fair diagnostic reasoning progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old female with polyarticular joint pain affecting hands, wrists, and knees. Associated malar (butterfly) rash across cheeks. Nose.
C2 Activity: A 32-year-old female with insidious onset polyarticular pain and swelling in hands, wrists, and knees. Reports associated facial rash and constitutional symptoms including fatigue and low-grade fever.
C3 History: Significant morning stiffness lasting 1–2 hours. Difficulty with gripping and fine motor tasks. Symptoms fluctuate with activity; some joints improve with rest, others remain persistently swollen.
C4 Examination: Six-month insidious progression. Associated weight loss (3–4 kg), night sweats, and recurrent low-grade fevers. No prior joint trauma or infection trigger. Family history notable for autoimmune disease. Currently taking no disease-modifying therapies.
C5 Imaging: Symmetric swelling of MCP and PIP joints without bony deformity or erosions on plain film. Positive rheumatoid factor absent. Reduced grip strength (15 mmHg bilaterally). Oral ulceration on hard palate. Photosensitivity elicited on direct questioning.
#58 Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Good progression from lateral hip pain on activity (C1) through movement aggravation (C2) to recent overuse and weakness history (C3), exquisite trochanteric tenderness and weakness (C4), and MRI-confirmed tendinopathy and bursitis (C5); concise and clinically sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged female with sharp lateral hip pain after increasing walking distance.
C2 Activity: Pain reproducible with side-lying; aggravated by crossing legs while standing.
C3 History: Recent increase in running or gym activity; gluteal weakness noted.
C4 Examination: Exquisite tenderness at trochanter, positive lateral hip pain test. Weak hip abduction.
C5 Imaging: MRI reveals gluteal medius tendinopathy with associated bursal inflammation.
#59 Piriformis Syndrome Hip/Buttock
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear stepping progression from buttock pain with thigh radiation despite normal lumbar MRI (C1) through sitting/movement aggravation (C2) to 4-month history and increased running (C3), positive FAIR and Pace signs (C4), and MRI/MR neurography confirmation (C5); fair difficulty and good clinical coherence.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old female office worker presents with pain in her left buttock that radiates down the back of her thigh. She has been treated for sciatica but her lumbar MRI was normal.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting, crossing legs, and climbing stairs. Driving is uncomfortable. Pain eases with standing and walking.
C3 History: Symptoms present for 4 months. No low back pain. No trauma but increased running recently.
C4 Examination: Tenderness on deep palpation reproducing buttock and leg symptoms. Positive FAIR test (flexion, adduction, internal rotation). Positive Pace sign.
C5 Imaging: MRI pelvis shows mild hypertrophy and oedema of the piriformis muscle with close relationship to the sciatic nerve. MR neurography suggests nerve irritation at that level.
#60 Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome Hip
~ MINOR FIX

Show clues
C1 Complaint: Older individual with persistent lateral hip discomfort limiting activity.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with prolonged standing, walking on inclines, and lying on affected side. No significant morning stiffness.
C3 History: Chronic presentation; history of previous ankle or knee injuries altering gait.
C4 Examination: Positive Trendelenburg sign; palpable tenderness over greater trochanter; pain with resisted hip abduction and external rotation.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows thickened bursa with fluid; gluteus medius tendon appears hypoechoic with areas of tendinopathy but no full-thickness tear.
#61 Hamstring Strain Thigh
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong progression from acute posterior thigh pain to inability to bear weight to visible bruising/gap to imaging confirmation; clues are distinct and build appropriately toward high-grade muscle strain.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Athlete with acute posterior thigh pain during sprinting or kicking.
C2 Activity: Unable to bear weight through affected leg; limping or favoring limb immediately after injury and unable to resume activity.
C3 History: No significant prior symptoms and high-speed activity or overstretching mechanism.
C4 Examination: Visible bruising in posterior thigh and palpable gap or indentation in muscle belly. Positive straight leg raise and reproduction with hamstring stretch.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound or MRI confirms muscle tear with retraction and fluid collection.
#62 Baker's Cyst Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is incomplete ('discovered during screening or.' has a hanging conjunction) and the presentation of asymptomatic/incidental finding is quite specific for Baker's cyst; minor text cleanup needed.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient with incidental finding of posterior knee mass and minimal or no symptoms. Discovered during screening or.
C2 Activity: Mass may feel tense or fuller with knee extension, may fluctuate with activity level, or patient notes transient swelling that resolves with rest.
C3 History: Cyst present for extended period without change and no acute episodes. Underlying knee pathology may be minimal often found on ordered for other reasons.
C4 Examination: Palpable cyst without significant tenderness, primary knee examination largely normal, and size stable neurovascular intact.
C5 Imaging: Imaging confirms simple cyst and no complex features. Primary knee joint may be normal or show minimal degenerative changes.
#63 Nerve Root Adhesion Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 (post-operative persistent radicular pain) is too specific and essentially names the diagnosis; additionally, nerve root adhesion is a rare/niche diagnosis that physio students may not recognize even with full clues, making it poorly suited for a guessing game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Post-operative patient with persistent radicular pain despite initial improvement.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform certain movements without leg symptoms; pain worsens with sustained postures or repeated movements in the affected direction.
C3 History: Surgery performed 6-24 months ago with initial relief; symptoms gradually or acutely worsened months later. No prior episodes of radiculopathy on the operative side.
C4 Examination: Asymmetric ROM restriction in spinal flexion/extension; lower limb neuromusculoskeletal testing shows variable motor/sensory findings inconsistent with single root level; reproduction of radicular pain does not match dermatomal pattern.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows scar tissue, nerve root adherent to surrounding structures, and may not explain all symptoms.
#64 Plantar Fasciitis Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from heel pain worse after rest to activity limitation to palpation findings to ultrasound confirmation; clues are appropriately broad at start and converge well toward plantar fasciitis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports heel pain that worsens after periods of rest, particularly noticeable with initial weight-bearing.
C2 Activity: Unable to tolerate prolonged standing or running; symptoms improve transiently with calf stretching but recur with activity resumption.
C3 History: Gradual onset with increased running volume, associated with tight calves, and high-impact activities.
C4 Examination: Maximal tenderness along medial plantar fascia at heel insertion; pain reproduced with passive ankle plantarflexion and toe extension. Ankle dorsiflexion ROM limited to 5 degrees.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates plantar fascia thickness >4 mm at heel insertion with hypoechoic appearance consistent with inflammation.
#65 Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome Knee

C1 Complaint: A 19-year-old female university student reports diffuse anterior knee pain that has gradually worsened over 2 months since starting running.
C2 Activity: is aggravated by going up and down stairs, squatting,. Prolonged sitting with knees bent ('movie '). Running downhill is worse than uphill.
C3 History: No locking, giving way, or significant swelling. Increased her running volume by 40% over 3 weeks. Wears old running shoes.
C4 Examination: Tenderness on patellar facets with compression. Positive Clarke's test (patella grind). Increased dynamic valgus on single-leg squat.
C5 Imaging: X-ray unremarkable. MRI shows no structural pathology — no cartilage defects, intact menisci and ligaments. Mild patellar lateral tilt on axial views.
#66 Acromioclavicular Joint Sprain Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Good narrative flow from direct blow to shoulder tip to activity-specific pain (cross-body adduction) to AC joint tenderness to X-ray findings; clues progressively narrow the differential effectively.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old presents with pain and swelling at the shoulder tip after direct blow.
C2 Activity: Pain with adduction across body (cross-body adduction); pain at top of shoulder.
C3 History: Mechanism was sudden collision; patient reports immediate onset. No prior shoulder injury or surgery. Unremarkable general health.
C4 Examination: Tenderness at AC joint, positive AC joint compression test. Crossover test positive, assess grade of injury.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows AC joint displacement, assess for grade 1-3 injury. Comparison views helpful.
#67 Elbow Dislocation Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent clinical storytelling with severe deformity and swelling following FOOSH, loss of motion, neurovascular preservation check, and definitive imaging showing posterior ulnar displacement; clues are coherent and diagnostic confidence increases appropriately.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old with sudden severe elbow pain and visible deformity after a fall onto an outstretched arm during sport.
C2 Activity: Cannot flex or extend elbow; extreme difficulty locating elbow joint contour due to gross swelling and visible deformity.
C3 History: Immediate swelling within minutes. No prior elbow injury or instability. High-energy impact; patient did not attempt to relocate the elbow.
C4 Examination: Elbow held in 45° flexion, resistant to passive movement. Posterolateral bony prominence palpable. Radial and ulnar pulses present; sensory intact in radial, ulnar, and median distributions. No crepitus.
C5 Imaging: Anteroposterior and lateral X-rays show posterior displacement of ulna and radius relative to humerus. Coronoid process and radial head intact. No associated fractures.
#68 Groin Strain Groin

C2 Activity: Difficulty with resisted leg crossing (adduction) and rapid acceleration; symptoms ease with rest but recur with return to sport.
C3 History: Acute non-contact injury during acceleration or lateral movement; pain radiates along medial thigh.
C4 Examination: Adductor tenderness, positive adductor squeeze test, pain with hip adduction against resistance.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows muscle strain with hypoechoic area; MRI shows muscle fiber disruption and edema.
#69 Boutonniere Deformity Wrist & Hand
