C5 Imaging: X-ray shows RA changes with joint destruction; MRI shows synovitis and tendon changes.
#70 Post-Mastectomy Rehabilitation Chest
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 5 contains examination/palpation findings rather than imaging (cord-like band, skin erythema, ROM measurements belong in C4), C4 and C5 substantially overlap, and the puzzle conflates multiple post-surgical complications (cording, radiation changes, early lymphedema) without clear diagnostic focus—too scattered for a single-answer game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports persistent shoulder dysfunction and radiation-related skin changes 12 weeks post-operatively. New complaint of cording sensation along inner arm.
C2 Activity: Woman reports shoulder tightness and a pulling sensation along the inner arm, 12 weeks after surgery. Also noticing skin irritation in the surgical area.
C3 History: Unable to raise arm overhead or reach across body without discomfort. Reports heaviness and swelling in the upper arm that worsens throughout the day.
C4 Examination: Significant axillary web syndrome (cording) palpable extending from axilla toward medial upper arm, Erythematous skin changes from radiation, Shoulder internal rotation particularly restricted, Beginning lymphedema in upper arm.
C5 Imaging: Palpable cord-like band from axilla to medial arm. Shoulder internal rotation markedly limited (40°). Visible erythema over chest and arm. Upper arm circumference increased 2 cm compared to contralateral side.
#71 Thoracic Outlet Syndrome Neck/Upper Limb
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (diffuse arm pain/numbness fits many conditions), clues build logically from complaint through overhead provocation to specific tests and imaging that clinch TOS.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old female violinist presents with diffuse arm pain, numbness in two of her fingers, and a sensation of heaviness in her right arm.
C2 Activity: Symptoms are provoked by prolonged overhead activities and playing violin for more than 20 minutes. Reports occasional colour changes in her hand during cold weather.
C3 History: Lean build with postural forward head and rounded shoulders. History of whiplash 2 years ago. Symptoms worsening with increased rehearsal schedule over 12 months.
C4 Examination: Positive Roos test (elevated arm stress test) at 90 seconds — reproduces symptoms and hand pallor. Positive Adson's test with diminished radial pulse. Tender anterior and middle scalenes.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray shows a rudimentary cervical rib on the right. Doppler ultrasound demonstrates subclavian artery compression with arm abduction. Nerve conduction studies are normal.
#72 Long COVID Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 3 is slightly redundant with Clue 2 (both emphasize post-exertional malaise and lack of acute recovery); move the 4-month timeline emphasis earlier or restructure for better progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 36-year-old reports persistent fatigue, dyspnea, and cognitive difficulties 4 months after mild initial COVID infection.
C2 Activity: Profound fatigue worsening with minimal exertion and lingering for days (post-exertional malaise). Dyspnea on walking. Difficulty concentrating at work.
C3 History: Confirmed COVID-19 infection 4 months prior; acute phase was mild but patient has not recovered baseline function. No return of symptoms after initial recovery—decline occurred during apparent convalescence. Previously very active; now significantly limited.
C4 Examination: Vital signs and resting SpO2 normal. Formal cognitive testing shows measurable impairment (not subjective complaint). Heart rate response to minimal exertion assessed—check for abnormal chronotropic response.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray shows no acute consolidation or effusion. High-resolution CT of the chest demonstrates mild ground-glass opacities in the lower lobes bilaterally. Pulmonary function tests show a mild restrictive and diffusion-capacity defect.
#73 Cardiotoxicity Post-Cancer Treatment Heart
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 4 and C5 contradict the setup—imaging (C5) shows myocarditis findings, but C4 places the cancer history where it should clinch the diagnosis; the clue ordering is fundamentally disordered and C2–C3 redundantly describe the same acute presentation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old 2 months post-completion of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy for melanoma presents with chest pain and dyspnea.
C2 Activity: A 52-year-old presents with acute onset chest pain and shortness of breath beginning 6 weeks ago. Symptoms are progressively worsening.
C3 History: Symptoms triggered by minimal exertion. Unable to climb stairs or walk >50 metres without significant dyspnea. Reports sensation of irregular heartbeats.
C4 Examination: Completed 6 months of dual immunotherapy 2 months ago for melanoma. Experienced inflammatory symptoms during treatment course. No prior cardiac history. Symptoms began shortly after treatment completion.
C5 Imaging: ECG shows diffuse ST elevation and PR depression consistent with myocarditis. Troponin markedly elevated at 4.2 ng/mL. Echo shows global hypokinesis and reduced EF 25%.
#74 Lateral Ankle Sprain Ankle
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too broad and generic; clues 2–5 describe acute-on-chronic instability but lack meaningful progression (all focus on ligament laxity/peroneal findings without introducing new clinical insight).
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Person reports ankle pain and significant swelling following fall or awkward step.
C2 Activity: Unable to bear weight on affected ankle. Unable to walk or weight-shift normally.
C3 History: Recurrent ankle sprains, episodes of giving way. Chronic ankle instability pattern.
C4 Examination: Positive anterior talofibular ligament stress test with significant laxity. Positive anterior drawer sign. Peroneal tendon tenderness on palpation.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows multiple ligament involvement (ATFL, CFL), chronic changes, possible peroneal tendon pathology.
#75 Antenatal Pelvic Girdle Pain Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 establishes pregnancy and severe pelvic pain (fits many obstetric conditions), subsequent clues add weight-bearing pattern, localisation, pelvic floor dysfunction, and finally imaging confirmation—logical and fair progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Pregnant patient at 35 weeks gestation reports severe, debilitating pelvic pain affecting all mobility. Pain radiates to lower extremities. Significant impact on function and quality of life.
C2 Activity: Ambulation severely limited; walking with severe antalgic gait. Unable to perform any household activities. Sleeping nearly impossible despite multiple positional changes.
C3 History: Pain worsens significantly with weight-bearing and asymmetric leg movements (stairs, rolling in bed). Single-leg stance nearly impossible. Pain localises to pubic symphysis and sacroiliac joints.
C4 Examination: Significant pain with any pelvic motion. Marked diastasis recti with functional weakness. Severe pelvic floor dysfunction with spasm.
C5 Imaging: Obstetric imaging confirms normal pregnancy progression. No fetal compromise. Pelvic imaging shows pubic diastasis of 3cm.
#76 Hallux Rigidus Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately general (forefoot pain/stiffness fits multiple conditions), clues progressively narrow via push-off loss, insidious degenerative pattern, and dorsiflexion restriction to classic hallux limitus and osteoarthritic imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient with progressive pain and stiffness in the great toe, affecting walking. Pain worsens with activity.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform push-off phase of gait or sustain propulsive activities. Pain with weight-bearing through medial forefoot.
C3 History: Onset insidious over years. Patient reports gradual progression despite activity modification. No recent trauma or acute event.
C4 Examination: Severe dorsiflexion limitation, pain and stiffness predominate. First MTP joint swelling present, gait adapted for pain avoidance.
C5 Imaging: X-rays show advanced joint degenerative changes, significant osteophytes. Marked joint space loss, bone-on-bone appearance possible.
#77 Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (groin/hip pain without trauma fits several hip pathologies), clues build through progressive weight-bearing pain, risk factors, and characteristic rotation limitation to definitive bone marrow edema and collapse on imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged patient with insidious-onset groin pain and progressive hip discomfort; no recent trauma.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens progressively with weight-bearing and standing; night pain prominent and sleep-disrupting; functional decline over weeks to months.
C3 History: History of steroid use, alcohol abuse, previous femoral neck fracture, or radiation.
C4 Examination: Antalgic gait, hip internal rotation severely limited and painful; positive FABER and FADIR tests; hip flexion contracture present; pain reproduced by combined hip flexion-adduction-internal rotation.
C5 Imaging: Early MRI shows bone marrow edema and later stages show segmental collapse on X-ray.
#78 Axillary Nerve Palsy Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Strong puzzle overall, but Clue 3 (history) should emphasize post-surgical timing more clearly upfront; clue 2's mention of 'mild pain' is slightly incongruent with the severity of motor loss emphasized in C4.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient with shoulder pain and progressive arm weakness 6 weeks after shoulder surgery.
C2 Activity: Cannot initiate shoulder abduction; arm hangs limply at side despite pain being mild.
C3 History: Iatrogenic nerve injury suspected; injury mechanism during surgery unknown. No prior shoulder pathology. No improvement with conservative management over 6 weeks.
C4 Examination: Severe deltoid weakness (grade 1/5), notable deltoid atrophy, sensory loss over lateral shoulder, normal rotator cuff strength.
C5 Imaging: EMG shows complete denervation of deltoid and ultrasound shows deltoid atrophy. MRI shows nerve compression at quadrangular space from post-surgical changes.
#79 Falls in the Elderly Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad for a multifactorial fall syndrome, but Clue 3 introduces a specific medication trigger (antihypertensive) that could prematurely narrow guesses; otherwise solid progression through gait/balance deficits to imaging exclusions.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 78-year-old woman is referred after multiple falls over the past three months. She reports that the falls occur both during the day and at night, often without a clear trip or slip.
C2 Activity: Unable to walk without support, requires assistance with stairs, and reports difficulty adapting to changes in floor level or lighting.
C3 History: Started antihypertensive medication two months ago. Multiple comorbidities including diabetes and hypertension. Wears bifocal glasses but admits to not wearing them consistently indoors.
C4 Examination: Romberg test positive with eyes closed. Tandem stance unable to hold >3 seconds. Gait shows reduced arm swing and shortened stride. Ankle dorsiflexion strength 4/5 bilaterally. Cognitive screen (MoCA) 24/30 with slowed processing.
C5 Imaging: MRI brain shows age-appropriate mild cortical atrophy and small vessel ischemic changes. No acute stroke, no subdural collection. Cervical spine imaging normal.
#80 Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (joint swelling + stiffness in a child fits infection, trauma, and other arthritides), clues progress through functional loss, insidious afebrile onset, bilateral warm effusions, and inflammatory imaging—fair and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient Child age 3 has swollen knee joints and morning stiffness lasting over an hour.
C2 Activity: Difficulty walking after waking, refuses to run, play, and refuses to bear weight on legs.
C3 History: Insidious onset 2 months ago, no fever or trauma, and otherwise healthy development.
C4 Examination: Bilateral warm knee effusions with restricted flexion/extension, positive antinuclear antibodies, and no systemic signs of infection.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows symmetric synovial hypertrophy with inflammatory enhancement in both knees; no bone erosion or cartilage damage. Findings consistent with early inflammatory arthropathy.
#81 Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome - Paediatric Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (knee pain in children), progression is logical from activity limitations through growth factors to imaging confirmation, and final clue clinches the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient Child age 10 has pain around the kneecap during activity.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform jumping and running without knee pain; stairs and deep squatting particularly provocative.
C3 History: No acute trauma, insidious onset, and active in sports rapid growth.
C4 Examination: Positive j-sign with knee flexion, weak hip abductors bilaterally, tight rectus femoris, mild effusion on palpation.
C5 Imaging: X-rays normal, MRI shows no cartilage damage or meniscal pathology, slight lateral patellar tracking on imaging.
#82 Traumatic Brain Injury Brain
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is fundamentally outside physio scope (neurology/medical emergency domain) and Clue 1 essentially names the diagnosis by specifying 'struck in head during game'; not suitable for a physio guessing game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 20-year-old college athlete struck in head during game, reports seeing stars.
C2 Activity: Returned to play initially but later developed inability to focus on coursework, persistent headache with activity, and unsteadiness when turning head quickly.
C3 History: Previous subconcussive impact 4 weeks earlier. Current symptoms evolved gradually over 6–8 hours post-collision rather than immediately. Reports ongoing sensitivity to light and noise.
C4 Examination: GCS 15, pupils equal and reactive. Balance impairment on single-leg stance (left > right). Positive King-Devick test (delayed number-naming). No focal weakness or speech disturbance. Resting heart rate elevated at 95 bpm.
C5 Imaging: MRI brain shows no structural lesion, contusion, or microhemorrhage. Neuropsychological testing 2 weeks post-injury reveals slowed processing speed and impaired executive function relative to pre-season baseline. Symptoms persist despite physical and cognitive rest.
#83 Hip Osteoarthritis Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple hip conditions, progression from activity limitations to structural findings is smooth, and imaging clearly confirms OA; straightforward and fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Elderly gentleman with hip discomfort in both groin and lateral regions.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with stairs and getting up from chair; better with rest.
C3 History: Progressive worsening over several years. Remote hip injury in his 20s with persistent symptoms since then.
C4 Examination: Antalgic gait, significant hip flexion contracture. Pain at end-range movements.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows cartilage loss, bone marrow lesions, and joint space reduction.
#84 Suprascapular Neuropathy Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (posterior shoulder pain from multiple causes), each clue adds distinct information (activity patterns → atrophy/EMG → compression imaging), and progression is logical.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old with progressive, insidious shoulder pain over several months. Pain is diffuse around the posterior shoulder.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform overhead reaching or throwing. Activities aggravate pain, particularly cross-body adduction. Progressive functional decline over months.
C3 History: No prior trauma or injury. No history of shoulder instability. Pain developed insidiously without clear precipitant. Patient denies neck pain or radicular symptoms.
C4 Examination: Marked infraspinatus weakness (4/5) on manual testing. Supraspinatus strength intact. Positive infraspinatus atrophy noted on inspection. EMG shows denervation pattern in infraspinatus; supraspinatus EMG normal.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows suprascapular nerve compression and associated paralabral ganglion cyst visible.
#85 Post-Cardiac Surgery Rehabilitation Heart
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is primarily a cardiology/post-operative medical management issue, not a physio diagnosis puzzle; the complexity and medical focus make it unsuitable for the game format.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 75-year-old presents with exertional breathlessness and fatigue that began 2 months ago. Reports swelling in one leg and feeling anxious about physical activity.
C2 Activity: Limited walking tolerance, getting winded easily. Swelling in saphenous vein harvest leg still present. Anxiety about returning to normal activities.
C3 History: Aortic stenosis corrected with mechanical valve, on warfarin. Concurrent CABG performed. Hypertension, diabetes type 2.
C4 Examination: Sternal scar well-healed but hypertrophic. Left lower leg shows pitting edema, skin pigmentation changes, and reduced skin turgor. Heart sounds reveal an audible mechanical valve click in systole.
C5 Imaging: Echocardiogram: normal mechanical valve gradients, no regurgitation, ejection fraction 50%. Chest X-ray: clear lung fields, normal cardiac silhouette, no pleural effusion.
#86 Behçet's Disease Whole Body
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 naming 'recurrent painful mouth sores' combined with 'skin rashes' and 'eye discomfort' is highly specific to Behçet's and leaves few alternatives; also outside typical physio scope.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports recurrent painful mouth sores and skin rashes. Recent eye discomfort with some visual changes.
C2 Activity: Difficulty eating and speaking due to oral sores. Pain with sitting and friction on lower body.
C3 History: Recurrent ulceration pattern for over 10 years, with progressive involvement. Genital ulcers preceded eye symptoms by several years.
C4 Examination: Shallow oral ulcers with erythematous halo. Scarring and induration at genital sites. Pustular lesions and nodules on lower extremities.
C5 Imaging: Ophthalmologic findings: anterior and posterior uveitis with retinal vasculitis. Inflammatory response consistent with systemic vasculitic disease.
#87 Post-Herpetic Neuralgia Variable
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is solid (many causes of band-like trunk/limb pain), but Clue 5 undermines the game by stating 'diagnosis is clinical; imaging not needed'—contradicting the game format where imaging should clinch it.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Older adult with severe burning pain in a band-like region of the trunk or limb that began weeks after a shingles rash resolved.
C2 Activity: Patient avoids wearing clothing or touching affected skin due to severe pain; activities requiring contact with the affected area are impossible.
C3 History: Pain persists months after acute varicella zoster infection; severe in elderly or immunocompromised.
C4 Examination: Decreased sensation to pinprick and light touch in dermatomal distribution; hyperalgesia to pin-prick; residual erythema or post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation in the affected dermatome.
C5 Imaging: Diagnosis is clinical; imaging and serology not routinely needed. Consider MRI if atypical presentation to exclude other compressive neuropathies in the same dermatome.
#88 Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (joint pain in young patients), each clue progressively narrows toward hypermobility disorder, and final genetic/imaging confirmation fits the game structure well.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young patient with widespread joint pain and chronic pain complaints.
C2 Activity: Repeated joint dislocations with minimal trauma; difficulty with high-demand activities due to instability and fatigue.
C3 History: Lifelong hypermobility since childhood; family history of joint laxity or connective tissue disorder; multiple joint dislocations across different sites over years.
C4 Examination: Generalized hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, and tissue fragility poor wound healing.
C5 Imaging: Imaging normal; structural degenerative changes absent despite clinical hypermobility; genetic testing available to confirm specific EDS subtype.
#89 Antenatal Pelvic Girdle Pain Pelvis
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C2 is an exact repeat of C1 (same complaint reworded), C4 is history rather than examination findings, and progression is broken; significant structural revision needed.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 26-year-old female at 8 weeks gestation with early onset pelvic pain. Anterior pubic pain and lateral hip pain bilaterally.
C2 Activity: 26-year-old female at 8 weeks gestation presenting with bilateral pelvic and hip pain.
C3 History: First pregnancy with very early PGP onset. History of sacroiliac joint dysfunction pre-pregnancy. Pelvic hypermobility syndrome.
C4 Examination: History of sacroiliac joint dysfunction and generalized ligamentous laxity prior to pregnancy. Unusually early symptom onset at 8 weeks gestation, despite first pregnancy.
C5 Imaging: Positive SIJ provocation tests bilaterally. Palpable asymmetry of pelvic landmarks. Generalized ligamentous laxity evident on physical examination.
#90 Vulvodynia Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple pelvic pain conditions, progression from activity triggers through negative findings to histology is logical, and final clue is appropriately definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Female patient reports chronic vulvar pain described as burning, rawness, and irritation without visible pathology. Pain constant but variable in intensity. Dyspareunia severe; sexual activity avoided.
C2 Activity: Sitting intolerance, particularly with pressure on perineum. Symptom triggered by tight clothing. Standing and walking preferred over prolonged sitting.
C3 History: Progressive vulvar pain over 3 years with no identified cause despite extensive gynecologic evaluation. Multiple treatments attempted without sustained relief. Pain affecting relationship and psychological well-being.
C4 Examination: Normal vulvar skin appearance; no obvious dermatologic pathology. Pain reproduction with light touch to vulva (allodynia). No visible erythema, ulceration, or lesion.
C5 Imaging: Vulvoscopy unremarkable; biopsy if performed shows only scattered perivascular inflammation with no infection, malignancy, or lichenoid changes. No neuromas or abnormal innervation on histology.
#91 Cerebral Palsy CNS

Show clues
C1 Complaint: Parent notices child walks with stiffness and one-sided weakness.
C2 Activity: Right hand function limited; child prefers left hand despite being right-handed family member.
C3 History: Child cannot climb stairs or run smoothly; fatigues easily during play compared to peers.
C4 Examination: Right-sided hemiparesis with increased tone; right foot in plantarflexion with tiptoe gait.
C5 Imaging: Hyperreflexia in right lower limb; positive Babinski sign bilaterally; scissoring gait pattern observed; minimal right hand dexterity with fisted posture at rest.
#92 Postural Syndrome - Work-Related Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (office back pain fits many conditions), each clue adds meaningful detail (activity pattern → history → exam findings → imaging), and progression logically narrows to postural dysfunction.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Office worker reports low back or mid-back pain that develops during work hours.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting and poor posture; improves with movement breaks and stretching.
C3 History: Symptoms began 6-12 months ago; patient reports no trauma or prior back history. Does not improve with rest days or weekends off.
C4 Examination: Limited thoracic rotation and extension; pain reproduces with sustained slouching posture held for 5 minutes. Abdominal strength graded 4/5; lumbopelvic stability impaired.
C5 Imaging: MRI lumbar spine normal; no disc herniation, spondylosis, or instability. Postural analysis via photography shows loss of normal lumbar lordosis and anterior pelvic tilt pattern.
#93 Bronchiectasis Lungs
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong clinical progression from recurrent pneumonia and cough → activity limitation and morning symptoms → neonatal risk factors and clubbing → HRCT confirmation; appropriately specific at each stage.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 6-year-old with recurrent pneumonia and chronic cough dating back to infancy presents for evaluation.
C2 Activity: Cannot keep up with peers during play. Morning cough productive. Recurrent ear infections and sinusitis.
C3 History: Neonatal intensive care admission for prematurity and respiratory distress. Three episodes of pneumonia by age 5. Immotile cilia suspect.
C4 Examination: Clubbing noted in fingers. Bilateral fine end-inspiratory crackles at lung bases. Hyperinflation evident on auscultation.
C5 Imaging: HRCT chest shows dilated bronchi with bronchus-to-artery ratio exceeding 1 in bilateral lower lobes, bronchial wall thickening, and bronchiolectasis. Bronchiectasis pattern is cylindrical.
#94 Femoroacetabular Impingement Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 (anterior hip pain in athletes) is reasonably broad, each clue adds specific mechanical and imaging detail, and the final diagnosis is well-supported without being given away early.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young athletic male with anterior hip pain during high-demand activities.
C2 Activity: Pain with deep hip flexion and internal rotation; mechanical catching sensation.
C3 History: Recurrent episodes over several years with periods of relative relief. Training modification sometimes helps briefly, but symptoms return with intensity. No prior hip surgery or significant trauma.
C4 Examination: Positive FADIR test, limited internal rotation in flexion. Posterior hip tightness.
C5 Imaging: Imaging confirms cam morphology with alpha angle >60 degrees; acetabular coverage normal. Labral pathology evident on high-resolution sequences.
#95 Rotator Cuff Tear Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent progression from shoulder pain with night symptoms → functional limitations and sudden event → occupational history and progressive weakness → positive tests → definitive MRI findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old male retired painter presents with difficulty lifting his arm and a deep ache on the outside of his upper arm that worsens at night.
C2 Activity: Cannot reach overhead to change a light bulb. Reports a 'sudden worsening' event 3 months ago when lifting a heavy bucket, though he's had on-and-off problems for years.
C3 History: Decades of overhead work as a painter. Progressive weakness in the affected arm. Difficulty with hair washing and dressing.
C4 Examination: Positive drop arm test. Weakness in external rotation and abduction. Positive empty can test.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows a full-thickness tear of the supraspinatus tendon (2cm retraction) with early fatty infiltration of the muscle belly (Goutallier grade 2). Associated superior labral fraying.
#96 Vibration White Finger Hand
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C4 contains both vascular symptoms (blanching pattern) and neuropathic signs (sensory loss, muscle wasting) that feel slightly misaligned with typical examination progression; slightly redundant with C5.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Chronic construction worker reports progressive finger pain, numbness, and color changes.
C2 Activity: Cannot grip or manipulate objects when hands are cold; symptoms worsen significantly in winter months.
C3 History: Persistent symptoms despite ergonomic modifications and tool changes; progressive despite reducing vibration exposure in past 3 years.
C4 Examination: Significant thenar and hypothenar muscle wasting; two-point discrimination >10mm in digits 2-5; blanching follows typical asymmetric pattern (index/middle > ring/little).
C5 Imaging: Doppler ultrasound shows intermittent digital artery occlusion on cold challenge; EMG/NCS demonstrates demyelinating pattern consistent with vibration-induced neuropathy.
#97 Dupuytren's Contracture Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from functional loss → activity limitations → risk factors (age, descent, comorbidities) → pathognomonic examination findings → imaging confirmation; engaging and fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old male presents with progressive inability to fully straighten his right ring and little fingers. He cannot lay his hand flat on a table.
C2 Activity: Difficulty putting his hand in his pocket, pulling on gloves, and shaking hands. The contracture has gradually worsened over 2 years. No pain associated with the condition.
C3 History: Northern European descent. History of heavy alcohol use (now sober 5 years). Type 2 diabetes.
C4 Examination: Palpable firm nodule and cord in the palmar fascia extending to the ring finger. Fixed flexion contracture at the MCP joint (40 degrees) and PIP joint (25 degrees) of the ring finger. Positive Hueston tabletop test.
C5 Imaging: Clinical diagnosis — imaging not typically required. Ultrasound shows hypoechoic nodule in the palmar aponeurosis with fibrous cord extension. No imaging features of tenosynovitis.
#98 Knee Arthroscopy Rehabilitation Knee
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 describes post-procedural inflammation too generically (fits many knee conditions); the puzzle essentially gives away 'recent knee procedure' in C3, which collapses the diagnostic space too early and makes guessing mechanical rather than clinical.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports knee pain and stiffness that worsened acutely 1 week ago. Swelling present. Protective guarding limiting active movement.
C2 Activity: Unable to achieve terminal knee extension or full flexion. Limp noted during gait. Difficulty with stairs and rising from sitting.
C3 History: Recent invasive knee procedure performed for investigation of chronic knee pain. No structural damage identified intra-operatively. Expected recovery window 4-6 weeks.
C4 Examination: Knee flexion 85°, extension lag 10°. Soft end-feel to flexion. Moderate suprapatellar effusion palpable. Quadriceps strength 4/5.
C5 Imaging: Arthroscopic visualization confirmed intact menisci and cruciate ligaments. Patellofemoral joint showed focal cartilage softening. No loose bodies or synovitis noted.
#99 Cancer-Related Fatigue General
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 is very broad (fits chronic fatigue, depression, metabolic illness, cardiopulmonary disease); C5 relies heavily on normal structural imaging to exclude mimics rather than confirm cancer-fatigue itself, making the endpoint feel like 'ruling out' rather than 'ruling in'.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports persistent fatigue and malaise that has worsened over recent months despite adequate rest. General sense of low energy affecting daily function.
C2 Activity: Unable to sustain work shifts or household activities; becomes exhausted after minimal exertion; struggles with cognitive tasks and concentration throughout day.
C3 History: Recent history of cancer diagnosis and completion of chemotherapy/radiation approximately 3–6 months ago. Fatigue onset coincided with treatment and has persisted through recovery phase.
C4 Examination: Six-minute walk test shows reduced distance covered compared to pre-morbid baseline. Grip strength reduced bilaterally. Heart rate rises excessively with minimal exertion. Mood screening indicates depression/anxiety symptoms.
C5 Imaging: Cardiac imaging normal; metabolic panels show iron deficiency and low vitamin D. Inflammatory markers (CRP, cytokines) elevated. Thyroid function normal. All findings consistent with post-treatment recovery state rather than primary cardiac or metabolic disease.
#100 Parkinson's Disease Brain
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 (resting tremor) fits multiple tremor disorders but is a fair starting point, each clue adds cardinal features (bradykinesia, rigidity, gait), and DaTscan is appropriately definitive without being obvious from earlier clues.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 65-year-old man reports tremor in right hand that is worse at rest and improves with activity.
C2 Activity: Slow movements when picking up objects, difficulty with fine motor tasks, stooped posture when standing.
C3 History: Tremor began 2 years ago and is slowly progressive. Family member noticed loss of facial expression. Writing becoming smaller.
C4 Examination: Resting tremor (pill-rolling), cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia, masked facies, shuffling gait with reduced arm swing.
C5 Imaging: DaTscan shows significantly reduced dopamine uptake in bilateral striatum, confirming dopaminergic denervation. MRI brain normal, ruling out structural or secondary causes.
#101 Cardiotoxicity Post-Cancer Treatment Heart
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (dyspnea + fatigue post-treatment fits multiple cardiopulmonary conditions), progression is logical (orthopnea/edema → cumulative dose history → displaced apex → diagnostic echo), and Clue 5 clinches diagnosis; fair and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old breast cancer survivor presents with progressive dyspnea and fatigue 18 months after completing anthracycline-based chemotherapy.
C2 Activity: Orthopnea and lower limb edema noted. Symptoms worse when lying flat; legs swell by evening after minimal activity.
C3 History: Cumulative anthracycline dose 450 mg/m². No prior cardiac history, hypertension, or diabetes. No recent infection or fever. Symptoms began insidiously 12 months post-treatment completion.
C4 Examination: Mild tachycardia, normal blood pressure. Baseline lung exam clear. Displaced apex beat.
C5 Imaging: Echocardiogram shows EF 38% (baseline 60% at end of treatment). Global hypokinesis without regional wall motion abnormality. Diastolic dysfunction present.
#102 Fibromyalgia Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (diffuse pain from minor injury fits many conditions), each clue adds new information (trigger → deconditioning → tender points → normal imaging + CNS findings), and the progression is coherent and clinically realistic.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young athletic patient reports diffuse body pain triggered by minor injuries that don't resolve.
C2 Activity: Unable to return to baseline training; minimal exertion produces pain lasting days, leading to progressive deconditioning and fear-avoidance.
C3 History: Onset following concussion or viral illness; accompanied by cognitive difficulties and persistent fatigue.
C4 Examination: Tender points elicited across all body quadrants with minimal pressure; normal strength and reflexes; no focal neurological deficits or signs of structural damage.
C5 Imaging: Normal structural imaging; functional neuroimaging shows abnormal pain processing in central nervous system.
#103 Plica Syndrome Knee
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 5 explicitly names 'plica' and describes arthroscopic confirmation, essentially giving the answer away; Clue 4's unreliable plica-specific tests contradict diagnosis confidence; overall structure is too specific early and diagnostic certainty is artificially inflated.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient with anterior knee pain that is difficult to localize, pain quality variable. Intermittent mechanical symptoms.
C2 Activity: Pain triggered unpredictably by knee flexion activities (stairs, squatting, prolonged sitting). Swelling or catching sensation may accompany episodes but not always present.
C3 History: Chronic intermittent symptoms over months to years. No clear traumatic onset. Prior physiotherapy and anti-inflammatory interventions provided temporary relief only. Imaging workup negative for meniscal tear, ACL injury, or patellofemoral osteoarthritis.
C4 Examination: Tenderness along medial joint line and suprapatellar region. Knee range of motion normal. Lachman, anterior drawer, McMurray tests negative. No effusion. Plica-specific tests (flexion-extension with medial palpation) elicit pain inconsistently; reproduction not reliable.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates thickened, fibrotic plica impinging on patellofemoral joint space. Arthroscopic visualization confirms plica with synovial irritation and patellofemoral cartilage reaction. Plica resection resolves symptoms.
#104 De Quervain's Tenosynovitis Wrist/Hand
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 2 is garbled/incomplete ('is worst when...' appears twice, sentence fragments), Clue 4 repeats activity history already covered in C2–C3 with no new examination findings, and the clue ordering is confused (history/activity mixed).
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old new mother reports wrist pain that started 6 weeks after her baby was born. She finds it increasingly difficult to grip things.
C2 Activity: is worst when lifting her baby with an open-hand grip and when wringing out washcloths. Gripping. Twisting movements are particularly.
C3 History: Symptoms are worst when lifting her baby with thumb abducted and wrist extended, and when wringing out wet cloths. Fine motor tasks like fastening buttons also provoke pain.
C4 Examination: The pain developed insidiously after hospital discharge. She has been performing frequent repetitive hand activities (baby care, bottle preparation, laundry). No prior wrist injuries. No systemic inflammatory disease history.
C5 Imaging: Localized tenderness and mild swelling over the radial styloid process. Finkelstein test strongly positive. Crepitus palpable over the first dorsal compartment during thumb abduction and wrist extension.
#105 Ménière's Disease Vestibular
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 4's Weber test description (conductive pattern) contradicts sensorineural Ménière's pathology, and Clue 5 describes bilateral sensorineural loss yet the disease is typically unilateral; clinical coherence is compromised.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 42-year-old female with recurrent episodes of severe spinning sensation lasting 20 minutes to several hours, accompanied by nausea and vomiting.
C2 Activity: During attacks: completely unable to move or work; nausea and vomiting severe. Between episodes: fully functional, though reports progressive difficulty hearing conversation.
C3 History: Recurrent episodes over 6 months. Progressive unilateral hearing loss, particularly low frequencies. Fluctuating tinnitus (roaring quality).
C4 Examination: During acute attack: vertical and rotatory nystagmus present. Weber test shows conductive-pattern hearing loss on affected side. Between episodes: nystagmus resolves, Weber normalizes.
C5 Imaging: Audiometry shows sensorineural hearing loss at low frequencies bilaterally, worse on affected side. Caloric testing reveals reduced caloric response on affected side. MRI normal — rules out acoustic neuroma.
#106 High Ankle Sprain Ankle & Foot
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 4 is incomplete/garbled ('with dorsiflexion-external rotation, positive crossover test. Weight-bearing.' lacks clear syntax), but the clinical narrative and clue progression are otherwise sound and specific enough by Clue 5; needs text cleanup.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient with ankle pain following inversion injury; symptoms persist beyond typical resolution timeframe.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform cutting/pivoting movements; pain and instability with activities requiring ankle rotation. Difficulty with weight-bearing on uneven surfaces.
C3 History: Initial injury diagnosis may have been missed, slow recovery. Previous ankle sprain complications, recurrent episodes.
C4 Examination: Syndesmotic tenderness present, positive squeeze test. with dorsiflexion-external rotation, positive crossover test. Weight-bearing.
C5 Imaging: MRI confirms chronic syndesmotic inflammation and ligamentous thickening. Stress radiographs demonstrate persistent tibiofibular widening compared to contralateral side.
#107 Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease Peripheral Nerves
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (progressive lower limb weakness in young adult fits multiple neuropathies), progression is logical (sensory loss → family history → stocking-glove pattern + areflexia → genetic/EMG confirmation), and the clinical story is coherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young adult (25 years old) with progressive lower limb weakness causing frequent falls and difficulty with stairs.
C2 Activity: Marked sensory loss in hands and feet affecting proprioception and fine motor tasks. Difficulty with balance in darkness.
C3 History: Noticed foot deformities as teenager. Father has similar condition. Slowly progressive.
C4 Examination: Stocking-glove sensory loss (vibration and proprioception worse distally). Widespread areflexia. Peroneal nerve thickening palpable in legs.
C5 Imaging: EMG shows demyelinating neuropathy with slowed conduction velocities and genetic testing shows PMP22 duplication.
#108 Patellar Tendinopathy Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is quite specific ('below the kneecap' + 'jumping/sprinting' essentially screams patellar tendinopathy); consider broadening to 'anterior knee pain in athlete' to allow more plausible wrong guesses and maintain engagement.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Master's level athlete with anterior knee pain localised below the kneecap, persistent despite training load reduction, affecting jumping and sprinting performance.
C2 Activity: Explosive movements like jumping, bounding, and rapid deceleration are highly provocative; symptoms worsen with repeated plyometric activity and running on hard surfaces.
C3 History: Symptoms have persisted for over 18 months despite physiotherapy, corticosteroid injections, and multiple rest periods. No significant improvement with conservative management. Patient reports occasional swelling and stiffness on mornings after high-load training.
C4 Examination: Focal nodularity palpable in tendon, significant with resisted knee extension, and eccentric quadriceps loading highly provocative.
C5 Imaging: Imaging shows chronic tendinopathy with significant structural disorganization and possible tears or partial rupture. Considerable degeneration.
#109 Vision Impairment - Age-Related Eye
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Vision-related conditions fall outside the scope of physiotherapy practice and would not be appropriate for a Physiodle game designed for physio students; this puzzle is out of domain.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Elderly patient reports gradual blurring of central vision over months, affecting ability to perform fine tasks.
C2 Activity: Cannot read small print or watch television comfortably. Difficulty navigating in low light and bumping into objects.
C3 History: Age 78. Long-standing smoker. Mother had late-onset vision problems requiring low-vision aids.
C4 Examination: Reduced central vision, scotoma in central field, and drusen present on macula.
C5 Imaging: OCT shows macular drusen and atrophy consistent with age-related macular degeneration.
#110 Pes Anserine Bursitis Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 2 is incomplete ('with stairs descending, running,' lacks grammatical structure) and Clue 3 overlaps too much with Clue 4 (both emphasize hip stiffness/adductor tightness); text cleanup and clue separation would improve flow.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports medial-sided knee pain that began insidiously 2-3 weeks ago, no history of trauma or acute injury.
C2 Activity: with stairs descending, running, symptoms worse with hip adduction, internal rotation, and morning stiffness present.
C3 History: Recent history of repetitive running or gym activity, prior knee or hip surgery, or persistent hip/hamstring stiffness despite stretching.
C4 Examination: Tenderness maximal over proximal medial tibia, pain with resisted hip adduction and internal rotation; hip flexibility significantly limited (adductor and hamstring tightness); knee effusion absent.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound or MRI shows bursal fluid, tendon changes, bursa may be thickened, and associated muscle strain visible.
#111 Tietze Syndrome Chest
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (chest wall swelling), progression is logical from activity/history to exam to imaging confirmation, and the clinical story is coherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old male presents with acute onset of localised chest wall swelling and pain at the second costochondral junction on the left side. The swelling appeared suddenly several days ago and has been progressively enlarging.
C2 Activity: A 28-year-old male presents with acute onset of localised chest wall pain and visible swelling. Symptoms began several days ago and have been progressively worsening.
C3 History: He had a mild upper respiratory tract infection with a productive cough two weeks prior. He denies any chest trauma or heavy manual work. Of note, there is no fever, weight loss, or systemic symptoms that would suggest a sinister aetiology.
C4 Examination: A visible, firm, fusiform swelling is palpable at the second costochondral junction measuring approximately 3cm. The swelling is warm and tender to direct palpation. Respiratory examination is otherwise clear, and cardiovascular examination is unremarkable.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray is normal with no cardiopulmonary pathology. CT of the chest wall confirms focal non-suppurative inflammatory swelling of the costal cartilage at the second costochondral junction, with no features of malignancy or infection. These findings, in combination with the characteristic clinical presentation, confirm the diagnosis.
#112 Snapping Hip Syndrome Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (clicking/pain in running athlete), each clue adds specific detail (activity limitation, temporal onset, palpable snap, ultrasound confirmation), and progression is smooth.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged athlete noticing a clicking sensation in the hip during running and jumping; pain gradually worsening over weeks.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform running drills or high-intensity hip movements without triggering the clicking sensation; limiting participation in sport.
C3 History: Started 6 weeks ago after ramping up training volume; initially just a painless click, now sharp pain with certain hip movements.
C4 Examination: Palpable snapping sensation directly over greater trochanter on passive hip flexion/extension with external rotation; no hip ROM restriction.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows tensor fasciae latae snapping over trochanter; may see underlying bursal inflammation.
#113 Breast Cancer Lymphedema Upper Limb
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is quite specific (post-breast surgery with recurrent infections) and essentially narrows to lymphedema immediately, reducing the guessing challenge slightly; could broaden C1 to 'progressive arm swelling' without the surgery mention.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports progressive arm swelling and heaviness over the past 2 years. Symptoms began following left breast surgery. Recurrent skin infections have become increasingly problematic.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to perform overhead activities, carrying, or prolonged use of affected arm. Significant functional loss in activities of daily living despite conservative treatment attempts.
C3 History: Left-sided mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection 2 years ago. Patient completed adjuvant radiation therapy to the chest wall. Attempted early compression therapy and exercise without sustained improvement.
C4 Examination: Pitting edema in proximal arm, non-pitting in forearm and hand. Skin thickened and fibrotic. Stemmer sign positive. Circumferential measurements show 4-5 cm difference compared to contralateral limb. Evidence of old cellulitis scars.
C5 Imaging: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates delayed tracer clearance and asymmetric drainage pattern. Absence of axillary lymph node activity on affected side. Ultrasound confirms diffuse subcutaneous fibrosis consistent with chronic lymphostasis.
#114 Congenital Torticollis Neck
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (newborn head tilt), history of difficult delivery adds context, exam findings are specific, and ultrasound confirms muscle pathology appropriately.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Newborn with persistent head tilt to one side; parents report asymmetric head position since birth.
C2 Activity: Difficulty turning head past midline in one direction; resists passive rotation away from affected side.
C3 History: Difficult breech extraction at delivery; birth weight 3.8 kg with large frame.
C4 Examination: Firm, non-tender mass palpable along sternocleidomastoid; passive rotation to opposite side blocked; no apparent neurological deficit.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows sternocleidomastoid muscle echogenicity increased with muscle thickening; fibrosis present.
#115 Occupational Overuse Syndrome Limbs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 4 describes specific nerve compression signs (wrist/forearm, thenar atrophy) that point directly to CTS/radial nerve compression rather than generic 'overuse,' and the clinical story conflates CTS with broader overuse which muddies the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Active individual with gradual onset of pain affecting work performance over several months.
C2 Activity: Unable to sustain repetitive gripping and fine motor tasks; symptoms worsen throughout work shifts.
C3 History: Decade of assembly-line work with poor desk ergonomics; symptoms started in dominant hand and have progressively spread proximally over 18 months despite rest periods.
C4 Examination: Positive nerve compression signs at wrist and forearm; weakness in finger extension and grip; tenderness along flexor-pronator mass; sensory changes in radial distribution.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows nerve swelling at multiple compression points (carpal tunnel, pronator teres region); selective atrophy of thenar and first dorsal interosseous muscles.
#116 Rheumatoid Arthritis Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (polyarticular pain, morning stiffness, symmetry), progression logically builds from activity impact to exam findings to serology, fitting a fair guessing game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 52-year-old woman with pain in multiple joints, particularly hands and feet. Symptoms worse in the morning, with stiffness that takes 2 hours to improve. Symmetric distribution.
C2 Activity: Unable to grip or perform fine motor tasks. Symptoms are worst in the mornings before activity, then gradually improve throughout the day.
C3 History: Insidious onset 6 months ago. Progressive functional decline. Smoking history.
C4 Examination: Palpable warmth and soft tissue swelling at MCPs and PIPs bilaterally. Morning stiffness reproduced on examination. No nodules or deformity palpated.
C5 Imaging: Hand X-rays demonstrate uniform joint space narrowing across multiple MCPs and PIPs with periarticular osteopenia. Serology positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies.
#117 Hip Osteoarthritis Hip
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonable but the groin-specific pain mention and activity-worsening pattern slightly narrow the field early; could be made slightly broader for better challenge, but still playable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Older adult with groin or hip pain, pain worse with activity and improving with rest. Morning stiffness present.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with loaded hip flexion (stairs, car transfers, bending) and sustained walking. Pain particularly felt in groin, worse later in day with fatigue. Can walk short distances without difficulty.
C3 History: Gradual onset of pain, possible previous hip injury or surgery. BMI elevated, pain progressively worsening.
C4 Examination: Limited hip internal rotation and flexion, pain at end-range hip movements. Trendelenburg gait present, pain with log-roll test.
C5 Imaging: X-rays show joint space narrowing, osteophytes, and sclerosis characteristic of osteoarthritis.
#118 Pulmonary Embolism Lungs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is nearly diagnostic (sudden severe dyspnea, syncope, immobility, calf swelling) leaving minimal room for guessing, and PE is a medical emergency beyond typical physiotherapy scope, making it inappropriate for an educational physio game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old with a significant smoking history presents with sudden onset severe shortness of breath and loss of consciousness. Onset was abrupt during a routine hospital visit.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to walk more than 10 meters without severe breathlessness. Cannot lie flat. One syncopal episode occurred with minimal exertion.
C3 History: Recent immobilization for chemotherapy over past 3 weeks. No recent surgery or fracture. No family history of clotting disorders. Patient noted calf swelling two days ago but dismissed it.
C4 Examination: Severely hypoxemic (O2 sat 78%), hypotensive, tachycardic. Distended neck veins. Right heart strain signs.
C5 Imaging: CT chest with contrast shows filling defects within bilateral pulmonary arteries. Echocardiogram reveals acute RV dilation with reduced function. Elevated troponin and D-dimer markedly elevated.
#119 Medial Tibial Stress Syndrome Lower Leg
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is very broad (bilateral shin pain) but C4 exam findings are somewhat redundant with the complaint (medial tibial tenderness restates the diagnosis); could strengthen C4 with additional findings like muscle weakness or gait pattern changes.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Athlete reports bilateral shin pain limiting training capacity.
C2 Activity: Unable to tolerate impact loading; pain worsens with increases in training volume or intensity, improving with rest.
C3 History: Chronic symptoms from high-volume training; poor recovery between sessions.
C4 Examination: Bilateral tenderness along medial tibial border; pain reproducible on palpation of posteromedial tibia. No focal bony step-off or point tenderness. Calf strength intact; tibialis posterior and soleus demonstrate mild weakness.
C5 Imaging: Bilateral periosteal changes on imaging, no fracture line; significant training-related stress evident.
#120 Total Knee Replacement Rehabilitation Knee
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is a post-operative rehabilitation stage, not a diagnosable condition amenable to guessing; clue 1 explicitly states 'day 1 post-op' which is procedural management rather than diagnosis, fundamentally misaligned with the Wordle game format.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 70-year-old female presents day 1 post-operatively with significant right knee pain and marked loss of bending ability. She reports difficulty with basic mobility tasks.
C2 Activity: Unable to independently bed-to-chair transfer. Marked quadriceps inhibition with significant extensor lag noted. Active knee flexion approximately 70 degrees.
C3 History: Four-year history of progressive, treatment-resistant tricompartmental knee osteoarthritis. Pre-operative ROM 5–95 degrees. BMI 33, no significant comorbidities affecting healing.
C4 Examination: Wound clean and dry, staples intact. Moderate swelling present. Palpable soft-tissue resistance at end-range; pain limits further flexion testing.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative X-ray shows well-aligned cemented total knee prosthesis with no evidence of periprosthetic fracture. Component alignment within acceptable parameters on AP and lateral views.
#121 Spring Ligament Injury Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately broad (medial foot pain, gradual onset fits multiple conditions), progression is logical (activity limitation → structural findings → imaging confirmation), and Clue 5 is definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old runner reports medial foot pain that has developed gradually over four months, worse with activity.
C2 Activity: Unable to run; pain limits prolonged standing. Experiences foot instability during walking, particularly on uneven surfaces.
C3 History: No recalled specific injury; pain developed without trauma. Patient reports family history of flat feet and hypermobility.
C4 Examination: Single-leg stance reveals marked arch collapse. Navicular drop test ≥8 mm. Dorsiflexion and eversion weakness noted. Pain on spring ligament palpation between medial malleolus and navicular.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows ligament disruption; ultrasound demonstrates gap in ligament.
#122 Pelvic Organ Prolapse Pelvis
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is highly specific (heaviness, dragging, bowel emptying difficulty essentially names the condition); a physio student would narrow diagnoses immediately rather than requiring the full progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old female reports sensation of heaviness and dragging in the lower pelvis, worse by end of day. Reports difficulty with bowel emptying.
C2 Activity: Unable to tolerate prolonged standing or walking without worsening symptoms. Finds relief only when horizontal. Manages light activities but avoids leaving home due to concern about symptom progression during day.
C3 History: Three vaginal deliveries with difficult labor. Chronic constipation and straining over 40 years. Post-menopausal for 20 years.
C4 Examination: Grade 3 cystocele with anterior wall prolapsing beyond introitus. Significant rectocele with posterior wall prolapse. Weak perineal body.
C5 Imaging: Pelvic MRI with straining shows extensive pelvic floor descent. Defecography demonstrates rectocele. Cystocele extends beyond introitus.
#123 Baker's Cyst Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 (posterior knee swelling with calf pain) is reasonably broad, each clue adds distinct information (activity impact → chronicity → palpable mass and underlying joint changes), and Clue 5 confirms with imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports posterior knee swelling with associated calf pain and tightness.
C2 Activity: Unable to fully extend knee; symptoms worse with prolonged standing and walking. Occasional acute exacerbations with sudden increase in calf swelling and pain.
C3 History: History of gradual onset swelling over months to years. Patient reports episodic knee pain exacerbated by activity. Recent increase in symptoms following increased activity level.
C4 Examination: Palpable mass in popliteal fossa, tender on deep palpation. Knee flexion limited to 90 degrees. Primary knee joint crepitus and mild effusion present. Calf soft tissue swelling present but neurovascular exam normal, capillary refill brisk.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows large cyst with possible rupture, fluid extravasation into calf. Underlying knee joint changes associated with OA or meniscal.
#124 Brachial Plexus Injury Shoulder & Neck
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is somewhat specific (sudden burning pain down arm after tackle in young athlete narrows field), but the progression is sound; consider making Clue 1 more generic (e.g., 'sudden arm pain and weakness after contact injury').
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 22-year-old football player experiences sudden burning pain down arm after tackle.
C2 Activity: Cannot lift arm overhead or abduct shoulder; grip strength markedly reduced on affected side.
C3 History: Acute impact during contact sport and neck distraction or shoulder compression mechanism.
C4 Examination: Deltoid and rotator cuff weakness; sensory loss in C5-C6 distribution; intact motor branches distally; no Horner's sign.
C5 Imaging: MRI may show nerve edema, often normal in neurapraxia, and assess for avulsion in severe cases.
#125 Lisfranc Injury Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 (midfoot pain post-ankle injury) is appropriately broad, each clue meaningfully adds specificity (activity limitation → chronic timeline → distinctive examination findings → imaging confirmation), and progression is logical.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient with midfoot pain following an ankle injury months ago. Pain worsening despite initial treatment.
C2 Activity: Cannot tolerate single-leg stance or push off during walking. Pain significantly limits running and jumping activities.
C3 History: Acute inversion ankle injury 12 months ago. Initially treated conservatively for ankle sprain, then discharged. Midfoot pain began 2 months post-injury and has not resolved despite rest and physio.
C4 Examination: Positive midfoot abduction stress test (forefoot abducted passively with hindfoot fixed). Increased midfoot hypermobility compared to contralateral side. No tenderness over syndesmosis or anterior ankle. Subtle increase in height of medial midfoot arch bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Weight-bearing X-rays show increased gap between first and second cuneiforms (>5mm). CT demonstrates articular surface irregularity at the tarsometatarsal joints with early post-traumatic OA. No acute fracture; findings consistent with chronic ligamentous disruption.
#126 Delirium - Rehabilitation Phase General
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This puzzle conflates multiple domains (neuro-psychiatric condition vs. physiotherapy rehabilitation outcome) and is too vague; Clue 1 could describe ICU-acquired weakness, post-viral syndrome, or many other conditions, making it unsuitable for a physio-focused diagnostic game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Elderly patient presenting with marked functional decline and persistent cognitive slowing weeks after hospitalization.
C2 Activity: Unable to walk without assistance, requires supervision for ADLs, forgets recent instructions despite appearing alert.
C3 History: Recent hospitalization with sepsis and delirium resolved but recovery slow.
C4 Examination: Proximal weakness (hip flexors 3/5), gait slow and unsteady, attention span <2 minutes, unable to follow 3-step commands reliably.
C5 Imaging: Imaging shows no structural cause and cognitive testing shows impairment.
#127 Thoracic Spine Dysfunction Thoracic Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: The diagnosis is too broad and non-specific (mechanical restriction and normal imaging could apply to dozens of thoracic conditions); Clue 1 alone is nearly generic, and subsequent clues don't narrow meaningfully or add progressive diagnostic clarity.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Active person with mid-back pain that worsens with certain movements during training.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform overhead or rotational movements required for sport; restrictions noted in morning, ease during activity.
C3 History: Activity-related, training demands exceed mobility, and responsive to intervention.
C4 Examination: Segmental restriction in mid-thoracic extension and rotation; rib cage symmetry asymmetrical on one side. Neurological examination and strength normal.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows normal alignment and no fracture. CT or MRI unnecessary; clinical presentation and movement pattern restriction confirm functional origin.
#128 Acquired Torticollis Neck
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 3 (fixed head position, inability to rotate) and Clue 4 (post-viral/post-vaccination history with acute spasm) essentially name the condition before imaging; clue progression is illogical with examination details appearing out of diagnostic sequence.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Child with previous normal neck development now has head tilt.
C2 Activity: Recently limited neck rotation, prefers head in one position, and possible dystonia.
C3 History: Cannot rotate neck fully to one side; head held in fixed position despite attempts to correct.
C4 Examination: Acute onset following viral illness or fever; recent history of upper respiratory infection or vaccination; no prior neck trauma.
C5 Imaging: Unilateral sternocleidomastoid or trapezius muscle spasm/hypertonia; head tilt away from affected muscle; neck extension and ipsilateral rotation limited; pain on palpation of affected muscle; no clear trigger point or mechanical restriction.
#129 Osteochondritis Dissecans Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 (intermittent knee pain with locking in teenage athlete) is broad enough for multiple knee pathologies, each clue adds distinct information (activity triggers → physical findings with positive Wilson's test → imaging confirmation), and progression is clinically sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 16-year-old male athlete presents with intermittent knee pain that has been worsening over the past three months. He plays competitive basketball and notices the knee sometimes catches or locks during games.
C2 Activity: Symptoms are aggravated by running, jumping, and pivoting movements. He reports occasional episodes where the knee feels like it locks momentarily before releasing. There is mild swelling after intense activity.
C3 History: No specific traumatic injury recalled. The symptoms began insidiously and have gradually progressed. He has increased his training load significantly over the past six months in preparation for the competitive season.
C4 Examination: There is tenderness on palpation of the medial femoral condyle. Wilson's test is positive, with pain on internal rotation of the tibia during extension that resolves with external rotation. A small effusion is present.
C5 Imaging: MRI reveals a well-defined osteochondral lesion of the medial femoral condyle with evidence of partial fragment separation. The overlying cartilage shows signs of instability with fluid signal tracking beneath the fragment.
#130 Post-ACL Reconstruction Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 (swelling and pain 6 weeks post-reconstruction) is somewhat specific given the surgical context; consider broadening to 'recent knee surgery with ongoing swelling and weakness' to give multiple conditions a fair chance before Clue 2 provides timeline.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports knee swelling and pain 6 weeks after reconstructive surgery of the knee. Quadriceps weakness noted with difficulty walking. Minimal sensation of instability.
C2 Activity: Walking on flat surfaces improving but stairs remain challenging. Difficulty with pivoting and cutting movements. Unable to bear weight on affected leg for dynamic activities.
C3 History: ACL tear sustained 6 weeks ago during soccer with pivot-shift mechanism. Concomitant meniscal tear. Treated surgically. No prior knee surgery or significant past medical history.
C4 Examination: Effusion present, Lachman test shows improved stability compared to acute injury. Anterior drawer test moderately positive. Quadriceps lag evident strength approximately 4-/5 and range of motion limited in extension.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows appropriate graft positioning and early integration into bone tunnels. No graft rupture or retear. Associated meniscal repair healing appropriately.
#131 Hip Fracture Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (sudden severe hip pain post-fall in elderly), clues progress logically through movement patterns → history → specific exam finding (log roll test) → definitive imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An 82-year-old woman with known osteoporosis presents with sudden severe pain in the right hip and groin after a fall from standing at home. She cannot stand or walk.
C2 Activity: Unable to internally rotate or adduct the hip. Pain is significantly worse with any attempt to move the leg into these ranges compared to other movements.
C3 History: Osteoporosis, postmenopausal. Fall from standing, multiple prior fractures.
C4 Examination: Hip flexed, abducted and externally rotated at rest. Severe pain on internal rotation and adduction. Log roll test produces severe groin pain. No crepitus. Neurological exam intact.
C5 Imaging: X-ray demonstrates a displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture with varus angulation and shortening.
#132 Scheuermann's Disease Thoracic Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple thoracic conditions, subsequent clues narrow progressively through rigidity, postural progression, and characteristic radiographic findings that clinch the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Older adolescent with chronic thoracic pain and postural concerns.
C2 Activity: Difficulty maintaining upright posture; pain worsens with prolonged sitting or activities requiring trunk extension; forward-curved posture develops over time.
C3 History: Progressive kyphosis noticed by parents, symptomatic since teen years, and adult residual symptoms.
C4 Examination: Rigid thoracic kyphosis with loss of extension ROM; vertebral segments appear stiff rather than mobile; posterior chain tightness; pain reproducible at apical kyphotic level on palpation.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs show anterior vertebral body wedging (≥5° across multiple consecutive thoracic vertebrae), irregular endplates, and loss of disc space height—consistent with resolved Schmorl's nodes and mature structural change.
#133 Medial Epicondylitis Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is generic enough for multiple elbow conditions, each subsequent clue adds specific detail (activity pattern, repetitive history, resisted wrist flexion, imaging confirmation) without premature naming.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old recreational golfer presents with gradual onset inner arm pain.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with grasping, throwing, or flexing the wrist and relief with rest and immobilization.
C3 History: Repetitive gripping activities at work over the past 3 years, with pain onset following a weekend golf tournament. Previous left shoulder impingement treated conservatively 2 years ago, now resolved.
C4 Examination: Tenderness on palpation of inner bony prominence and pain with resisted wrist flexion and mild weakness.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates tendon inflammation, thickening, and X-rays show no fracture.
#134 Post-Total Knee Replacement Knee

Verdict: Clue 1 explicitly names 'recent major surgery' which essentially narrows to joint replacement; would be stronger if phrased as acute post-operative stiffness/pain without surgical context until Clue 2-3.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 68-year-old female presenting with pain and swelling at the knee, with difficulty regaining movement after recent major surgery.
C2 Activity: Difficulty achieving heel-to-buttock flexion and full knee extension. Struggles with stairs and sit-to-stand transfers.
C3 History: Severe osteoarthritis resistant to 6+ months of physiotherapy and injections. Open surgical approach used. Patient reports good pre-operative functional level relative to disease severity.
C4 Examination: Significant effusion present. Knee flexion 60 degrees, extension lag 10 degrees. Quadriceps inhibition evident.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative X-rays show appropriate prosthetic alignment. Component position good. No instability.
#135 Pressure Injury Stage 2-3 Skin
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is not a physiotherapy diagnosis—pressure injury management is nursing/wound care; including it in a physio game dilutes educational value and scope of practice.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Older patient reports a painful area of skin that has become red, swollen, and tender over the past week.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to reposition or change position independently; spending 12+ hours daily in same position on a single surface.
C3 History: Prolonged immobility, inadequate nutrition. Diabetes, infection risk.
C4 Examination: Open wound with partial or full thickness loss, exposed dermis or deeper tissue. Purulent drainage.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound or MRI shows tissue depth and any undermining; wound culture positive if infected.
