Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old office worker presents with persistent mid-thoracic stiffness and aching that has worsened over six months. She describes a constant dull ache between the shoulder blades that worsens after prolonged sitting.
C2 Activity: Unable to maintain posture for extended periods; pain increases with reaching forward or overhead activities; some relief with manual therapy.
C3 History: No specific traumatic event; symptoms coincided with new desk setup and remote work three months ago; no previous spinal issues; denies trauma or falls.
C4 Examination: Reduced thoracic rotation, tenderness over mid-thoracic segments, and no neurological signs.
C5 Imaging: Imaging usually normal and functional restriction without structural pathology.
#137 Subtrochanteric Fracture Hip
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 3 explicitly states 'fracture fixed 12 weeks ago' which immediately identifies this as a post-fracture complication rather than requiring diagnosis; should reframe as post-operative complication without naming the initial injury.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Elderly with persistent hip pain after surgery for fracture.
C2 Activity: Unable to weight-bear or ambulate without pain and instability; functionally immobile.
C3 History: Proximal femoral fracture fixed 12 weeks ago with internal fixation. Recent onset of increasing hip pain and giving way despite apparent initial healing.
C4 Examination: Marked pain with hip flexion/abduction; positive Trendelenburg sign; audible clunk on hip rotation under load; no neurovascular compromise.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows lateral cortical perforation of proximal femur; loss of fracture line reduction; severe osteoporosis limits revision options.
#138 Myasthenia Gravis Neuromuscular Junction
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 4's 'Tensilon test' is somewhat dated clinically; overall progression is solid but this puzzle sits at the edge of neurology scope for physiotherapy education—still playable if intended for advanced students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged patient with progressive leg weakness and difficulty with breathing that worsens toward evening.
C2 Activity: Cannot climb stairs, requiring mechanical ventilation at night, and weakness improves after rest.
C3 History: Started with eye drooping 1 year ago, progressed to generalized weakness over months. Recently hospitalized for acute respiratory decline requiring intubation.
C4 Examination: Ptosis and ophthalmoplegia bilaterally. Weakness of facial and bulbar muscles (nasal speech, difficulty with chewing). Strength deficits worse after sustained muscle contraction. Tensilon test shows transient improvement in strength.
C5 Imaging: Repetitive nerve stimulation demonstrates >10% decremental response in proximal muscles. Anti-acetylcholine receptor and anti-MuSK antibodies negative.
#139 Autism Spectrum Disorder Whole Body
✗ REWORK
Verdict: ASD is a neurodevelopmental psychiatric diagnosis, not a primary physiotherapy condition; while physios support motor/sensory components, framing ASD as a 'diagnosis puzzle' conflates developmental neurology with musculoskeletal/movement disorders.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: School-age child reports feeling clumsy and uncoordinated. Struggles with balance and dislikes certain clothing textures, bright lights, and loud noises.
C2 Activity: Cannot participate in organised team sports due to coordination difficulties. Prefers solitary or structured activities. Trips frequently and seems to lack spatial awareness.
C3 History: History of developmental delay identified at age 3. Currently receiving occupational therapy and speech pathology support. No specific injury or recent change in function reported.
C4 Examination: Gait pattern irregular and energy-inefficient. Difficulty coordinating bilateral limbs during crossing midline activities. Hyper-responsive to light touch; defensive reactions to textures. Proprioceptive testing shows reduced body awareness.
C5 Imaging: Functional MRI demonstrates altered connectivity in motor coordination and sensory integration networks. Standardised motor development scales confirm delays in gross and fine motor milestones.
#140 Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo Vestibular
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 4 contains unnecessary psychosocial content (anxiety, social withdrawal) that dilutes the clinical focus; Clue 5 is strong but the Dix-Hallpike nystagmus is nearly definitive, making earlier clues feel padded.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 64-year-old female with recurrent episodes of room-spinning vertigo lasting seconds to minutes. Triggered by lying down or rolling in bed.
C2 Activity: Vertigo occurs with specific positional changes: head tilted back, rolling in bed, looking up. Can predict and avoid triggering positions. Between episodes, completely normal.
C3 History: Patient avoids head movements and certain positions to prevent vertigo. Between episodes, able to walk, stand, and perform ADLs normally. Hesitant to lie flat or turn in bed independently due to fear of triggering attack.
C4 Examination: Vertigo onset insidious, 3 weeks ago. No recent head trauma or significant illness. No prior balance problems or vestibular disorders. Lives alone; symptoms causing anxiety about falling and social withdrawal.
C5 Imaging: Hallmark feature: brief (< 60 seconds) rotatory nystagmus with Dix-Hallpike maneuver. No imaging needed typically. Otoconia from utricle confirmed by characteristic nystagmus.
#141 Femoroacetabular Impingement Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Good progression from groin pain (many causes) through movement patterns to specific ROM limitations and imaging findings; fair guessing difficulty.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged woman with groin and anterior hip pain, worse with prolonged sitting and certain movements.
C2 Activity: Pain with hip flexion and adduction; limited external rotation in flexion.
C3 History: Insidious onset over months; no clear traumatic event; symptoms gradually worsened with running and hip-heavy activities; no prior hip surgery.
C4 Examination: FABER test reproduces anterior hip pain; hip external rotation in flexion is limited to 15°; adductor tightness; pain with log-roll test.
C5 Imaging: Imaging shows over-coverage of acetabulum; coxa profunda or acetabular retroversion.
#142 Dementia-Related Mobility Decline Systemic
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too broad (could be any age-related decline), Clues 2-3 are vague about the actual diagnosis (cognitive decline mentioned but not specific), and C4 describes Parkinson-like features that contradict pure dementia, making the clinical story incoherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An 82-year-old woman in residential aged care presents with progressive unsteadiness and increasing reluctance to mobilise over the past several months.
C2 Activity: She has become increasingly dependent for transfers and walking. Staff report she frequently forgets to use her walking frame and becomes confused about where she is going mid-task.
C3 History: Diagnosed with a progressive neurodegenerative condition three years ago. Gradual cognitive decline with recent acceleration. Multiple falls over the past six months, now requiring two-person assist for mobilisation.
C4 Examination: Marked postural instability with backward tendency on perturbation. Parkinsonian features: rigid trunk, reduced arm swing, shuffling steps with freezing tendency. Minimal arm swing despite instruction to swing arms. Difficulty turning; requires multiple steps to redirect.
C5 Imaging: CT brain shows marked generalised cerebral atrophy with disproportionate frontosubcortical involvement and substantial ventricular dilatation. Basal ganglia appear hypodense. No acute findings. DaTscan shows reduced striatal dopamine uptake bilaterally.
#143 Lumbar Spinal Cord Injury Lumbar Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 essentially names the diagnosis ('sudden onset paraplegia' is nearly pathognomonic for SCI) leaving minimal differential and making the puzzle trivial.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient experienced sudden onset paraplegia following a crush injury to the lower back at work.
C2 Activity: Unable to walk or stand; complete loss of lower limb motor control bilaterally.
C3 History: Crushed between heavy machinery at work. Mechanism suggests high-energy axial or compressive force to the lower spine.
C4 Examination: Severe bilateral leg weakness (grade 1/5), saddle anesthesia, absent anal wink, absent bulbocavernosus reflex, and flaccid paralysis.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows conus medullaris compression with hemorrhagic infarction and significant edema.
#144 Huntington's Disease Brain
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Not appropriate for Physiodle as Huntington's is primarily a genetic neurodegenerative disease better suited to a neurology game; presentation heavily relies on family history and genetic testing rather than physical examination findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged patient with progressive involuntary movements and behavioral deterioration over the past 2 years.
C2 Activity: Tremor and uncoordinated limb movements worsening with intentional tasks; difficulty with fine motor control and balance.
C3 History: Mother had similar symptoms and died at age 60. Behavioral issues began 2 years ago, followed by choreiform movements 1 year ago.
C4 Examination: Generalized dance-like involuntary movements, difficulty with smooth eye tracking, mild slowing of thought and speech, preserved strength.
C5 Imaging: Genetic testing shows CAG repeat >39 in HTT gene. Brain MRI demonstrates caudate atrophy.
#145 Long Head of Biceps Rupture Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from acute onset pain through weakness pattern to visible clinical sign and confirmatory imaging; playable with fair difficulty.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 55-year-old male with sudden onset severe upper arm pain and visible lump or swelling in the biceps region.
C2 Activity: Weakness with elbow flexion and supination reduces arm strength.
C3 History: He felt a sudden pop in the upper arm while lifting a heavy box overhead at work three days ago. No prior shoulder surgery or rotator cuff history.
C4 Examination: Marked weakness with elbow flexion and supination strength testing (grade 2/5). Visible bulge or absence of normal biceps contour in upper arm on inspection.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows absent tendon at shoulder. MRI confirms rupture with retracted muscle.
#146 Post-Total Knee Replacement Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 3 explicitly mentions 'recent surgical intervention' which essentially narrows diagnosis to post-operative conditions; could be less specific (e.g., 'history of knee pathology requiring intervention').
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports significant pain in the knee with variable symptoms depending on activity level and time of day.
C2 Activity: Walking improving with single-point cane support. Knee flexion progressing but extension lag persists; sit-to-stand transfers improving.
C3 History: History of severe knee osteoarthritis refractory to conservative management. Recent surgical intervention within the past month.
C4 Examination: Well-healed incision with mild effusion. Active knee flexion approximately 90 degrees with 15-degree extension lag. Quadriceps weakness evident.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs show proper prosthesis positioning with neutral limb alignment. No evidence of loosening.
#147 Pneumonia Lungs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Pneumonia is outside typical Physiodle scope (it's a medical diagnosis requiring pharmacological treatment, not primarily a movement/musculoskeletal condition) and Clue 1 is overly specific for a pediatric respiratory infection.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 3-year-old child with high fever, persistent cough, and rapid breathing for one week. No improvement with rest.
C2 Activity: Child is breathless even at rest and cannot walk short distances without increased respiratory distress.
C3 History: Born at term with no perinatal complications. No prior hospital admissions. Currently unwell for four days.
C4 Examination: On examination, respiratory rate is 62 breaths per minute with subcostal and intercostal recession. Oxygen saturation is 91% on room air. Auscultation reveals coarse crepitations and reduced air entry in the left lower lobe.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray shows left lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms. Nasopharyngeal swab positive for respiratory syncytial virus.
#148 Wound Dehiscence Post-Surgical General
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 nearly names the condition ('visible gap opening at wound edges'), which limits differential too early; could be more vague (e.g., 'increasing drainage and separation at incision site').
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Increasing discharge from surgical site with visible gap opening at wound edges, beginning second post-operative week.
C2 Activity: Unable to bear weight or perform upper limb loading activities; prone positioning contraindicated due to wound location.
C3 History: Major surgical procedure 10 days prior with initially unremarkable course. Risk factors include obesity, diabetes, poor nutrition, and immunosuppression.
C4 Examination: Partial wound separation with serosanguineous drainage. Surrounding tissue induration with minimal erythema; patient systemically stable.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows separation of fascial layers with fluid collection. Early abscess formation possible.
#149 Concussion Brain
✓ READY
Verdict: Good progression from acute head impact through cognitive symptoms to normal imaging but abnormal neurocognitive testing; fits Physiodle well and requires integration of multiple clues to diagnose.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young soccer player reports 'seeing stars' and brief confusion after heading the ball forcefully.
C2 Activity: Sat out game, reports feeling 'foggy' and having trouble remembering play sequences.
C3 History: Second head impact to same region 3 weeks prior; reported worsening of fogginess and concentration issues since first incident despite return to light training.
C4 Examination: GCS 15, normal neurological exam, normal pupil response, mild headache, no focal deficits.
C5 Imaging: CT head normal. ImPACT baseline neurocognitive testing shows decline from pre-season scores in processing speed and reaction time; score consistent with concussive injury.
#150 Femoroacetabular Impingement Hip
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 'persistent groin discomfort' is reasonable but Clue 2 jumping immediately to 'compound movements like squats and lunges' is quite specific; could broaden to general activity limitations first.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young professional with persistent groin discomfort interfering with gym workouts.
C2 Activity: Pain triggered by compound movements like squats and lunges; relieved with rest.
C3 History: Insidious onset over 6 months; no prior hip injury or surgery. Symptoms began after increasing deadlift volume at the gym.
C4 Examination: Positive anterior hip impingement test; pain on flexion-internal rotation combined movement. Hip flexion ROM limited to 100° and internal rotation to 10° in flexion.
C5 Imaging: 3D CT imaging demonstrates combined cam and pincer morphology.
#151 Asthma Lungs
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (occupational chest tightness + cough fits multiple conditions), each clue adds distinct information, and Clue 5 clinches with spirometry and FeNO—fair progression for a physio student.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient 34-year-old occupational worker develops chest tightness. A 34-year-old occupational worker develops chest tightness, cough, and wheezing specifically during work shifts and weekends off symptom-free.
C2 Activity: Patient is unable to perform sustained physical exertion; symptoms improve markedly with rest and are worse in the evening after activity.
C3 History: Patient has a childhood history of atopy and seasonal allergies. No prior respiratory diagnoses. Symptoms began 6 weeks ago and have progressively worsened. No recent infections or surgery.
C4 Examination: On examination, diffuse expiratory wheeze is audible on auscultation bilaterally. Peak expiratory flow rate is 52% of predicted. Accessory muscle use is present with mild tachycardia.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms and no consolidation. Spirometry demonstrates FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.62 with 18% bronchodilator reversibility. Fractional exhaled nitric oxide is elevated at 42 ppb.
#152 Lumbar Disc Herniation Lumbar Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too specific (lower back pain + shooting leg pain is nearly pathognomonic for disc herniation), leaving no real guessing space.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Adult office worker with lower back pain and shooting pain down the leg.
C2 Activity: Worse with prolonged sitting and forward bending; improves with lying down. Unable to bend to pick up light objects.
C3 History: Pain started after lifting a heavy object; positive lower limb radiation below the knee.
C4 Examination: Positive straight leg raise test, diminished ankle reflex. Weakness in foot dorsiflexion.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows posterolateral disc protrusion at L5-S1 level with nerve root compression.
#153 Chronic Fatigue Syndrome Systemic
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is overly specific (profound fatigue unrelieved by rest + muscle pain + cognitive impairment is too narrow), and Clue 2 essentially confirms CFS with post-exertional malaise, leaving minimal diagnostic uncertainty.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports profound fatigue unrelieved by rest, muscle pain, and impaired cognitive function.
C2 Activity: Significant post-exertional malaise with disproportionate symptom worsening after minor activities.
C3 History: Symptoms began gradually following a mild viral illness 14 months ago and have progressively worsened. She has no history of prior chronic fatigue, fibromyalgia, or mood disorder.
C4 Examination: Orthostatic intolerance on tilt-table testing, reduced cardiac output with minimal exertion, cervical lymphadenopathy, and absence of motor weakness or focal neurological signs.
C5 Imaging: Low natural killer cell counts, elevated inflammatory markers, abnormal cytokine levels, brain imaging changes.
#154 Lumbar Disc Herniation Lumbar Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad but Clues 2-4 are heavily overlapping (all focus on position/movement-related pain and nerve root signs), making progression feel repetitive rather than revelatory.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 34-year-old warehouse worker with sudden leg pain following heavy lifting.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with sitting, forward bending, and coughing. Partially relieved by lying on side with knees bent.
C3 History: Two previous back pain episodes over 5 years without leg symptoms. Smoker with sedentary lifestyle outside work.
C4 Examination: Positive straight leg raise at 35 degrees reproducing leg pain. Reduced ankle jerk with weak great toe extension (L5 myotome) and sensory changes in L5 dermatome.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows posterolateral disc herniation at L4/L5 compressing the left L5 nerve root.
#155 Posterior Cruciate Ligament Tear Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple knee injuries (acute pain after flexion/blow), Clues 2-4 progressively narrow toward PCL (posterior sag and drawer are PCL-specific), and Clue 5 confirms with MRI—solid educational progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Adult patient with acute knee pain after forced flexion or direct anterior tibial blow; minimal to moderate swelling over initial hours.
C2 Activity: Pain with kneeling and deep squatting, symptoms worsen with stairs. Less instability sensation compared to ACL injury.
C3 History: Injury from high-energy mechanism; patient often unaware of complete injury details. May report feeling or hearing a pop at time of injury, with rapid effusion development.
C4 Examination: Positive posterior sag sign at rest, posterior drawer test positive with knee at 90 degrees. Quadriceps strength assessment important.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates PCL fiber disruption with increased T2 signal throughout ligament; proximal or distal avulsion may be present. Associated posterolateral corner injury or medial meniscal tear common.
#156 Vision Impairment - Age-Related Eye
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 (difficulty focusing on near objects) is essentially the diagnosis name (presbyopia), leaving no genuine guessing challenge for a physio student.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Older patient reports gradual-onset difficulty focusing on near objects.
C2 Activity: Patient reports difficulty maintaining clear focus during sustained reading; symptoms worsen with dim lighting and small print sizes.
C3 History: Onset coincided with patient's mid-50s; she has no prior eye disease, trauma, or systemic comorbidities. Reading glasses were never needed before this.
C4 Examination: Near-point accommodation reduced to 33 cm (normal for age ~25 cm). Distance visual acuity normal. Pupillary reflexes intact; no visual field defect.
C5 Imaging: Cycloplegic refraction shows hyperopic shift relative to patient's prior prescription from 5 years ago; lens power insufficient for near-distance focusing.
#157 Ménière's Disease Vestibular
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (recurrent vertigo + tinnitus + ear fullness fits multiple vestibular conditions), each clue adds distinct info (activity triggers, examination findings, imaging confirmation), and progression is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 55-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of acute vertigo lasting 2-3 hours, accompanied by unilateral ear fullness and roaring tinnitus. The episodes began 18 months ago and have progressively worsened. Between attacks, she reports fluctuating low-frequency hearing loss and mild persistent dizziness.
C2 Activity: During acute episodes, she experiences severe nausea with vomiting and is unable to walk safely. She has noticed that exposure to loud noise and crowded environments tends to trigger or worsen symptom clusters. Her symptoms are unpredictable in timing.
C3 History: Hypertension is managed on medication. She reports that high salt intake and periods of stress seem to cluster her symptom episodes. She denies recent head injury or upper respiratory infection. No known family history of similar vestibular or hearing disorders.
C4 Examination: On examination, during an acute episode, horizontal nystagmus is present with characteristic rotatory component. Romberg test shows significant instability. Dix-Hallpike maneuver does not reproduce vertigo. Caloric testing demonstrates reduced vestibular response on the affected side. Audiometry shows sensorineural hearing loss pattern in low frequencies.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the temporal bones with high-resolution sequences shows asymmetric enlargement of the membranous labyrinth (endolymphatic hydrops) on the affected side. No mass at the cerebellopontine angle. No demyelinating lesions or other structural abnormalities.
#158 Paget's Disease of Bone Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (progressive back/hip pain fits many conditions), Clues 2-4 build with history (family history, increasing shoe size) and examination (localized warmth, bone deformity), and Clue 5 confirms with characteristic imaging—fair challenge.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive low back and hip pain over 2 years, which has significantly impacted his mobility and independence. He reports increasing bone pain in the lumbar spine and proximal femur, particularly with weight-bearing activities.
C2 Activity: He demonstrates a slightly widened gait and reports pain with prolonged standing. Walking distances have diminished from 2 miles to 200 meters. He experiences pain relief with rest but finds the pain reoccurs with minimal activity, restricting his previously active lifestyle.
C3 History: He has a family history of Paget's disease (father was affected). No recent trauma, but he notes gradual increase in shoe size and hat size over the past decade. Laboratory work shows markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase at 285 U/L (normal 30-120) and elevated urinary hydroxyproline.
C4 Examination: On palpation, the lumbar spine and proximal femurs demonstrate localized warmth indicating increased bone vascularity. Direct percussion over affected areas elicits pain. No neurological deficits currently present, though mild lower extremity weakness (4+/5) noted in hip flexors. Bone deformity palpable in lumbar region with mild anterior bowing of left femur.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs of the lumbar spine and pelvis show characteristic mixed lytic and sclerotic bone lesions with coarse trabecular pattern. The femoral necks and lumbar vertebrae demonstrate cortical thickening and increased bone density. Radionuclide bone scan reveals significantly increased uptake in multiple sites including lumbar spine, pelvis, and proximal femurs.
#159 Computer Vision Syndrome Eye
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (eye discomfort + blurred vision from multiple causes), Clue 2 adds work-correlation context, Clues 3-4 narrow to strain/tear film dysfunction, and Clue 5 confirms with meibomian gland findings—solid logical progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old office worker presents with eye discomfort and occasional blurred vision that has developed gradually over 3 months. She reports the symptoms worsen as her workday progresses and describes mild frontal headaches in the afternoons. Symptoms are worse on working days than weekends.
C2 Activity: Her symptoms are tightly correlated with continuous near-work activity, improving rapidly with breaks and distance viewing. She also reports significantly fewer symptoms during periods away from work, such as weekends and vacation. This pattern has been consistent and reproducible.
C3 History: Transition to full-time remote work 3 months ago coincided with symptom onset. She has noticed reduced blinking when concentrating on her screens. Recent optometric assessment was normal, and she denies any systemic autoimmune or rheumatological history.
C4 Examination: On examination, visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally at distance but becomes 20/40 at near distance (approximately 40 cm). Tear film break-up time is reduced at 4 seconds (normal >5 seconds), indicating aqueous tear deficiency. Ocular surface staining with lissamine green shows scattered punctate epithelial erosions. Accommodation amplitude measured at 10 diopters (expected 12-14 at age 32).
C5 Imaging: Anterior segment optical coherence tomography demonstrates decreased tear meniscus height and thinning of the tear film layer. Meibography reveals mild gland dropout in the lower lid consistent with meibomian gland dysfunction from reduced blinking. No retinal pathology identified on dilated fundoscopy or OCT of optic disc and macula.
#160 Postural Syndrome - Work-Related Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is sufficiently broad (chronic back/neck pain from desk work fits multiple diagnoses), Clues 2-4 progressively exclude serious pathology and narrow to postural/mechanical dysfunction, and Clue 5 confirms with normal imaging—educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old office manager presents with chronic lower back pain and neck stiffness that has developed over 8 months of desk work. She describes gradual onset of central low back discomfort and progressive cervical tightness without any acute injury or trauma.
C2 Activity: Pain intensifies significantly after prolonged sitting (>2 hours) and improves markedly with movement, stretching, and postural correction. She reports morning stiffness that resolves within 30 minutes of activity. Standing and walking provide symptomatic relief. Pain does not radiate distally and is not aggravated by specific movements or neural tension tests.
C3 History: Patient works 8-10 hours daily at a desk with minimal breaks. Recent ergonomic assessment identified workplace risk factors contributing to sustained postural deviation. No history of significant spinal trauma, prior disc pathology, or neurological symptoms. General health unremarkable.
C4 Examination: On examination, postural assessment reveals prominent forward head posture (approximately 3 cm anterior to plumb line) with thoracic kyphosis exaggeration and anterior pelvic tilt. Cervical and lumbar range of motion are full but slightly limited by muscle tightness. Lumbar flexion shows reversal of normal curve but no radicular pain. Straight leg raise 85 degrees bilaterally. Slump test negative. Palpation reveals sustained muscle tension in cervical paraspinals and lumbar erector spinae.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs show preserved spinal alignment and disc heights without degenerative changes. MRI demonstrates normal disc signal, intact facet joints, normal canal diameter, and no neural compression. Imaging is structurally normal.
#161 Ulnar Collateral Ligament Injury Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (overhead throwing athlete with medial elbow pain fits multiple conditions), each subsequent clue adds specific clinical findings with good progression, and Clue 5 clinches the diagnosis with MRI confirmation of partial UCL tear.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 27-year-old overhead throwing athlete (baseball pitcher) presents with progressive medial elbow pain over 6 weeks. The pain is localized to the medial aspect of the elbow and has progressively worsened with continued pitching activity despite attempting rest periods.
C2 Activity: Pain is specifically triggered by throwing, particularly during the late cocking phase and deceleration phase of the pitch. He reports ability to perform other upper extremity activities without pain. Throwing velocity has decreased noticeably, and he cannot maintain control of pitches. Non-throwing activities cause minimal discomfort.
C3 History: He increased his pitching volume significantly 2 months ago without adequate progression in training load. He reports no specific acute traumatic event but rather insidious onset with progressive symptoms. He played through mild pain initially, thinking it would resolve. No previous elbow surgery or significant injury to this extremity.
C4 Examination: On examination, point tenderness is elicited directly over the medial collateral ligament origin. The moving valgus stress test reproduces pain (also called dynamic valgus stress test). Milking maneuver is positive, reproducing sharp pain over the ligament. Manual valgus stress at 90 degrees elbow flexion demonstrates increased laxity compared to contralateral side with a soft end-feel. Grip strength is reduced on affected side. No nerve involvement signs on upper limb neurological testing.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the elbow reveals partial thickness tear of the anterior bundle of the ulnar collateral ligament with associated edema at the ligament origin. High signal intensity within the ligament substance indicates ligamentous disruption. Small joint effusion present. Flexor-pronator origin shows mild edema consistent with associated overuse strain. No osteochondral lesion identified.
#162 Post-Total Hip Replacement Hip
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 3 essentially names the diagnosis ('underwent hip surgery 8 weeks ago'), making the puzzle solvable immediately; the clinical story of weakness post-THR without imaging explanation is also potentially contradictory or suggests underexplored complications.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old woman reports persistent hip region pain and difficulty with basic mobility tasks including walking and stairs. Onset was gradual over recent weeks.
C2 Activity: Patient uses a cane and demonstrates significant weakness with hip abduction tasks. She struggles particularly with activities requiring hip flexion beyond 90 degrees or weight-bearing through the affected leg.
C3 History: Patient underwent hip surgery via anterolateral approach 8 weeks ago for osteoarthritis. Completed standard rehabilitation protocol. No episodes of catching, locking, or sudden instability.
C4 Examination: On examination, she demonstrates significant hip flexion weakness (3/5) and hip abduction weakness (2+/5). Hip flexion range is 85 degrees limited by pain and stiffness. Hip abduction range is 25 degrees with weakness noted. Hip extension appears functional at 15 degrees. No apparent signs of hip instability with log-roll test or flexion-adduction test. Mild swelling present around anterior surgical site. Sensation intact to light touch throughout lower extremity.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs show appropriate component positioning with no signs of aseptic loosening, malalignment, or early failure. Imaging does not explain the ongoing weakness and functional loss.
#163 Rotator Cuff Tear Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately encompasses multiple shoulder pathologies (chronic overuse converting to acute tear is clinically sound), each clue adds meaningful information about weakness and mechanism, and Clue 5 definitively confirms full-thickness supraspinatus tear with staging details.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old man with a history of recurrent shoulder pain presents with acute worsening of his chronic shoulder discomfort following a fall onto his right shoulder 2 weeks ago. He reports constant aching pain in the lateral shoulder and difficulty with overhead activities.
C2 Activity: He has marked weakness with attempts to raise his arm overhead, which significantly limits reaching activities and self-care. Sleeping on the affected shoulder is impossible. Passive motion causes less pain than active motion. Pain is worse with lifting, pulling, and throwing activities. He avoids these activities due to weakness and sharp pain.
C3 History: He has a 2-year history of intermittent right shoulder pain from overuse related to his work as a carpenter. He had not sought formal treatment, managing with over-the-counter analgesics. The acute trauma 2 weeks ago appears to have converted his chronic tendinopathy into a symptomatic tear. He reports no prior shoulder surgery or significant trauma to this shoulder.
C4 Examination: On examination, abduction is weak and painful at 3/5. External rotation strength is reduced compared to internal rotation (which is relatively preserved). Drop arm test cannot be completed due to weakness. Supraspinatus tenderness is present on palpation. Apprehension test is negative.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the right shoulder reveals a full-thickness rotator cuff tear of the supraspinatus measuring 1.2 cm in anteroposterior dimension with retraction. Fatty infiltration is present in the supraspinatus muscle at stage 2. The infraspinatus is intact. Acromion is hypertrophic (curved type) contributing to subacromial space narrowing. Acromioclavicular joint osteoarthritis is mild.
#164 Peripheral Neuropathy Peripheral Nerves
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is sufficiently broad (bilateral lower limb pain and sensory loss in a diabetic patient could reflect many conditions), progressive clues narrow toward distal symmetric pattern, and Clue 5 with NCS/EMG findings clinches diabetic peripheral neuropathy diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 64-year-old man with a 12-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with progressive bilateral lower limb pain and sensory loss over 18 months. He describes a burning sensation in his feet and difficulty with fine sensory perception.
C2 Activity: Walking distances are limited due to burning pain in his feet, and he reports difficulty with balance and proprioception, particularly in darkness. He has had episodes of stumbling. Nighttime pain significantly disrupts sleep quality. He avoids walking barefoot due to numbness and fear of injury. Standing tolerance is reduced to 15-20 minutes.
C3 History: Poor medication adherence has resulted in suboptimal glycemic control (HbA1c 9.2%) throughout his diabetes course. Associated comorbidities include hypertension and hyperlipidaemia. Notably, B12 level is normal and there is no history of chemotherapy, malignancy, or significant trauma.
C4 Examination: On examination, ankle reflexes are bilaterally diminished (1+/4). Vibratory sense is markedly reduced at both ankles (perceives only 2/8 seconds of tuning fork). Proprioception is impaired in toes (unable to detect direction of passive toe movement). Two-point discrimination is diminished in feet (distance >1 cm). Monofilament testing shows decreased sensation to 5.07 monofilament in distal lower extremities bilaterally. Motor strength is 4+/5 in foot dorsiflexors with distal lower extremity weakness.
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction studies demonstrate reduced conduction velocities and decreased amplitudes in both peroneal and tibial nerves consistent with demyelination. Distal latencies are prolonged. F-waves are abnormal or absent. Electromyography shows denervation potentials (fibrillations and positive sharp waves) in intrinsic foot muscles, confirming axonal degeneration. MRI of lumbar spine is normal without disc herniation or stenosis contributing to symptoms.
#165 Chronic Fatigue Syndrome Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad but the specific mention of 'disproportionate exhaustion lasting days' after mild activity (post-exertional malaise) is somewhat diagnostic; post-viral onset in Clue 3 also narrows quickly, though overall progression is sound and Clue 5 supports the diagnosis through exclusion.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 44-year-old woman reports persistent fatigue lasting 4 years that significantly impairs her ability to work and engage in daily activities. She notes that physical exertion, even mild, triggers worsening of symptoms that can last for days.
C2 Activity: She is now largely housebound and unable to sustain any consistent activity level. Even brief periods of light activity (walking to the mailbox, preparing a meal) result in disproportionate exhaustion lasting 24-48 hours, forcing her to rest significantly afterward.
C3 History: Her symptoms began insidiously approximately 2 months after a presumed viral illness. Prior to illness, she was an active professional with full work capacity. She reports new-onset difficulties with concentration and short-term memory. She denies depression, major life stressors, or psychiatric illness. Comprehensive metabolic panel, thyroid function, and vitamin levels are normal.
C4 Examination: On examination, mental status is clear and oriented, though she demonstrates slowed processing and occasional difficulty with recall. No signs of joint inflammation or objective muscle weakness (5/5 strength throughout). Neurological examination is otherwise normal. Vital signs show no significant orthostatic hypotension. Cognitive testing reveals mild impairment in attention and processing speed.
C5 Imaging: MRI brain is normal with no white matter abnormalities or structural lesions. Metabolic panel, CBC, thyroid, and autoimmune markers (ANA, RF, anti-CCP) are all normal. Sleep study demonstrates non-restorative sleep architecture without obstructive apnea.
#166 Hip Labral Tear Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 (insidious groin pain in recreational athlete) is broad enough to include femoroacetabular impingement, labral pathology, and other hip conditions; subsequent clues progressively narrow with FADIR positivity and imaging confirmation of anterior-superior labral tear.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old recreational soccer player presents with insidious-onset deep groin pain that began 3 months ago without any specific traumatic event. He describes sharp anterior hip pain with occasional clicking sensation within the hip joint.
C2 Activity: Symptoms are aggravated by prolonged sitting, particularly with hip flexion greater than 90 degrees, and by cutting and pivoting movements in soccer. He experiences pain with hip flexion-adduction activities. Walking and straight-line running are tolerable but aggravated by direction changes. Pain improves with rest and anti-inflammatory medication.
C3 History: He has no prior hip injuries or surgery. Training volume increased markedly 6 months ago with shorter recovery between matches. Denies any family history of joint disease. Generally fit with no other musculoskeletal complaints. No previous groin or hip symptoms.
C4 Examination: On examination, the anterior impingement test (FADIR - flexion, adduction, internal rotation) reproduces his groin pain. Hip flexion adduction test is positive. Log-roll test is negative. McCarthy test demonstrates reproduction of clicking and pain. Hip range of motion shows mild limitation in internal rotation (25 degrees compared to 35 degrees on contralateral side). Pain does not radiate down the leg. Neurological examination is intact.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the hip with intra-articular gadolinium demonstrates an anterior-superior labral tear (ASLAP-type tear) at the 1-2 o'clock position. Labral fraying and signal intensity changes indicate labral fiber disruption. No hip joint effusion. The femoral and acetabular cartilage appears intact. No femoroacetabular impingement morphology. No occult fractures or osteonecrosis identified.
#167 Functional Neurological Disorder CNS
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 presents with progressive neurological-sounding symptoms (weakness, tremor) following viral infection, fitting multiple organic conditions; Clues 2-4 reveal inconsistency and non-anatomical patterns; Clue 5 definitively excludes organic pathology, leaving FND as diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old woman reports progressive weakness and clumsiness affecting her left arm and leg over the past 6 months. Onset followed a minor respiratory infection. She describes episodes of tremor and difficulty with fine motor control. She is significantly distressed about her symptoms and reports high levels of anxiety.
C2 Activity: She reports inability to use her left hand for fine motor tasks but demonstrates inconsistent functional use of the limb in natural settings. Her gait is slow and cautious, but the pattern is variable and non-organic. She reports improvement with distraction and inconsistency between reported symptoms and observed function during casual observation.
C3 History: She experienced significant life stressors approximately 6 months prior to symptom onset, including relationship dissolution and work-related stress. She reports high emotional distress and anxiety. No family history of neurological disease. She is a high-achiever with perfectionist traits. She has seen multiple specialists without definitive organic diagnosis.
C4 Examination: On examination, she demonstrates weakness that does not follow anatomical nerve or dermatome distribution, described as 'give-way' weakness that gives impression of effort limitation rather than true paralysis. Hoover sign is positive (contralateral hip extension weakness when attempting to lift affected limb). Gait is inconsistent and varies with attention. Romberg test shows excessive sway that normalizes with distraction. Strength grades vary between examinations.
C5 Imaging: Brain MRI including diffusion weighted imaging is completely normal with no white matter lesions, acute infarcts, or structural pathology. No evidence of demyelinating disease. Cervical and thoracic spine MRI show normal cord signal and normal structural anatomy. Comprehensive neurophysiological testing including EMG and NCS is entirely normal. No evidence of organic neurological disease on imaging or electrophysiological studies.
#168 Morton's Neuroma Foot
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 4 is entirely redundant—it repeats history information (hairdresser, prolonged standing, no trauma) rather than providing new examination findings; this breaks the intended clue progression and suggests incomplete puzzle design.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old woman presents with progressive pain in the forefoot, specifically between the third and fourth toes, over the past 8 months. She describes sharp, burning pain and paresthesias that radiate into the toes, particularly when wearing tight footwear.
C2 Activity: Pain is exacerbated by walking in heels or tight shoes and improves significantly with removal of footwear and massage. She reports difficulty with prolonged standing or walking more than 30 minutes. Pain is absent during rest with feet elevated. She has modified her shoe choices to loose-fitting, flat footwear to minimize symptoms.
C3 History: She has no prior history of foot trauma or surgery. She worked as a hairdresser for 25 years requiring prolonged standing, which may have contributed to the condition. She reports increasing pain over the past 8 months despite footwear modifications. No systemic disease or diabetes. No history of inflammatory arthropathy.
C4 Examination: Worked as hairdresser for 25 years with prolonged standing. No prior foot trauma, surgery, or orthopedic intervention. No diabetes, inflammatory arthropathy, or peripheral neuropathy. Pain progressive despite conservative footwear modifications over 8 months, suggesting mechanical compression not responding to load reduction alone.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound of the forefoot demonstrates a hypoechoic nodular mass measuring 8 mm in the third intermetatarsal space, consistent with Morton's neuroma. The lesion is located plantarly between the metatarsal heads with compression of adjacent tissues. MRI shows a T2-hyperintense fusiform mass at the third intermetatarsal space with perineural fibrosis. No bony abnormalities or stress fractures identified.
#169 Piriformis Syndrome Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 (buttock pain radiating posterolateral in sedentary worker) is broad enough to include radiculopathy and other hip pathologies; FAIR test and muscle hypertrophy on imaging progressively narrow toward piriformis syndrome diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old sedentary office worker presents with gradually worsening unilateral hip and buttock pain over 4 months, radiating down the posterolateral thigh. The pain is constant and has significantly impaired their ability to sit for prolonged periods at work.
C2 Activity: The patient reports pain is aggravated by prolonged sitting, hip internal rotation movements, and driving; it eases with standing, walking, and hip external rotation. They must change positions frequently throughout the day and have started standing at their desk.
C3 History: No recent trauma or falls reported. The patient has a sedentary lifestyle with minimal physical activity and denies regular stretching or conditioning. No bowel/bladder dysfunction or weight loss reported.
C4 Examination: On examination, pain is reproduced with the FAIR test (flexion-adduction-internal rotation) and Freiberg's sign (internal rotation of flexed hip). Hip external rotators are weak at 4/5, and palpation over the greater sciatic notch elicits tenderness. The SLR and Kernig's tests are negative.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the hip and pelvis shows no structural pathology, disc herniation, or facet arthrosis. However, hypertrophy of the piriformis muscle is noted with signal changes suggesting muscle strain, and mild compression of the sciatic nerve is visualized.
#170 Lumbar Spinal Stenosis Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 (progressive bilateral lower back and leg pain with walking limitation) fits multiple spinal pathologies; claudication pattern, relief with flexion, and multilevel degenerative changes on MRI with severe central stenosis provide clear diagnostic progression and confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old retired tradesperson presents with progressive bilateral lower back and buttock pain over 18 months, with radiation into both lower extremities when walking. Symptoms are reproducible and significantly limit walking distance and tolerance for standing activities.
C2 Activity: Walking more than 100 meters triggers pain and numbness in both legs, forcing the patient to sit and rest; symptoms resolve quickly with sitting or forward bending. The patient has adopted a flexed posture at the waist and can walk longer distances leaning on a shopping trolley.
C3 History: Longstanding history of lower back pain since 1990s following manual work. No acute traumatic event precipitated this decline. Patient reports no bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or lower limb weakness. Sleep quality is disrupted due to nighttime pain.
C4 Examination: On examination, bilateral hip and ankle reflexes are diminished (1+). Sensation to light touch is reduced in a bilateral L5 and S1 distribution. Straight leg raise is full and painless. The patient demonstrates exaggerated lumbar lordosis with mild kyphotic correction when bending forward.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the lumbar spine reveals multilevel degenerative changes with significant central stenosis at L4-L5, characterized by ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, disc bulging, and facet joint arthrosis. The spinal canal diameter is severely narrowed, particularly at the level of maximum stenosis.
#171 Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome Pelvis
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 and C2 are nearly identical (both emphasize constant pain worsening with menstrual cycle and quality-of-life impact), creating redundancy; C2 should focus on functional activities instead.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old woman presents with persistent pelvic pain and deep perineal discomfort over 3 years that has significantly impacted her quality of life, sexual function, and work productivity. Pain is constant but fluctuates in intensity throughout the day and menstrual cycle.
C2 Activity: A 48-year-old woman with persistent pelvic pain over 3 years. Pain is constant but variable in intensity, worsening with her menstrual cycle. Significant impact on daily activities and quality of life.
C3 History: Pain is worse with prolonged sitting and sexual intercourse. Patient improves with position changes, lying down, and heat. Has significantly modified activities to avoid triggers.
C4 Examination: On examination, palpation of pelvic floor muscles reveals significant guarding and tenderness, particularly on the left side. Pain is reproduced with pelvic floor pressure and vaginal palpation of obturator internus. Straight leg raise and hip range of motion are unremarkable, but internal hip rotation is limited by discomfort.
C5 Imaging: Pelvic ultrasound and MRI imaging show no ovarian cysts, fibroids, or endometrial pathology. No structural abnormalities of the bladder, uterus, or bowel are identified. Imaging is essentially normal, ruling out other organic pelvic pathology as the primary driver of symptoms.
#172 Cervical Facet Joint Syndrome Cervical Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C4 and C5 are duplicated almost verbatim (both describe rotation restriction, lateral flexion pain, facet tenderness, and intact neurology), eliminating the progression that should come with imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 56-year-old office worker presents with unilateral right-sided neck pain and occipital headaches over 6 months, worsening with prolonged computer work. Pain is not radiating and has caused significant functional limitation in work capacity and neck mobility.
C2 Activity: Pain is aggravated by prolonged sitting, sustained neck extension, and specific head movements (particularly right rotation and right side bending). The patient reports relief with frequent position changes, gentle stretching, and heat application. Symptoms fluctuate throughout the day with worse pain in the afternoon.
C3 History: No acute trauma or motor vehicle accident; symptoms developed gradually over months related to ergonomic factors. Patient reports no previous neck injury, no radiation into the arms, and no hand tingling or weakness. Sleep is disrupted by neck pain when rolling onto the right side.
C4 Examination: On examination, right cervical rotation is restricted to 60 degrees compared to left side. Right lateral flexion provokes localized neck pain at the C4-C5 level. Palpation over the right cervical facet joints elicits marked tenderness. Upper extremity strength and reflexes are intact, and sensation is normal in all dermatomes.
C5 Imaging: On examination: right cervical rotation restricted to 60 degrees (left side 80+ degrees). Right lateral flexion reproduces localized neck pain at the C4-C5 level. Palpation directly over the right cervical facet joints at C4-C5 elicits marked tenderness. Upper extremity strength, reflexes, and sensation are intact in all dermatomes; no findings of nerve root compression.
#173 Lower Crossed Syndrome Lower Body
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is suitably broad (lower back pain), progression is logical from activity through postural findings to imaging ruling out serious pathology, and a physio student would find it fair and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old office worker presents with progressive lower back pain over 2 years. Pain is localized to the lower lumbar and sacroiliac region, worsened by prolonged sitting.
C2 Activity: Patient reports pain with sitting for more than 30 minutes and difficulty rising from a chair. Pain is relieved by lying down and walking. Forward bending is limited and uncomfortable.
C3 History: No history of acute trauma or injury. Patient reports morning stiffness and tightness in hip flexors and lower back. Minimal exercise activity. No radicular symptoms or systemic features.
C4 Examination: Thomas test markedly positive. Hip abductor strength 4/5 bilaterally. Core stability poor: cannot maintain neutral spine in prone bridge. Lumbar extension painful and limited. Palpable anterior pelvic tilt with increased lumbar lordosis.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the lumbar spine shows no disc herniation, stenosis, or neural compromise. Mild degenerative changes are present but not sufficient to explain symptoms. Imaging does not reveal significant structural pathology; findings are consistent with muscular and postural dysfunction rather than structural disease.
#174 Long Head of Biceps Rupture Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from acute anterior shoulder pain and audible pop → loss of function → MRI confirmation of complete rupture; clinical story is coherent and imaging is definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old recreational weightlifter presents with acute onset sharp anterior shoulder pain and an audible 'pop' during bench press. He reports immediate arm weakness and functional loss. Pain persists at rest and with movement.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot lift overhead or carry loads. All shoulder movement, particularly reaching across the body, reproduces anterior pain. Symptoms present even at rest but substantially worse with active use.
C3 History: Long history of heavy weightlifting with progressive training intensity over recent years. No prior shoulder pathology. Injury occurred during a heavy loaded lift. No neck involvement or constitutional symptoms.
C4 Examination: On palpation, anterior shoulder is tender with visible ecchymosis along proximal arm. Shoulder flexion strength is 3+/5. Speed's test and O'Brien's test reproduce anterior shoulder pain. Rotator cuff strength testing is intact.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the shoulder demonstrates complete rupture of the long head of biceps tendon at the intertubercular groove with significant proximal retraction of approximately 5 cm. There is associated hemorrhage and edema in the bicipital groove. The rotator cuff tendons are intact without tear or signal abnormality.
#175 Chemotherapy-Induced Peripheral Neuropathy Peripheral Nerves
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately broad (bilateral distal neuropathy), each clue adds new information (activity limitations, temporal history, examination findings, EDX confirmation), and final clue is near-definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 54-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of progressive bilateral symmetrical distal lower extremity pain, numbness, and burning sensation starting in her toes. Symptoms began approximately 4 weeks into a chemotherapy regimen and have steadily worsened.
C2 Activity: The patient reports significant functional limitation with walking and balance, particularly in low-light environments. She experiences increased symptoms with prolonged standing or walking and finds relief with sitting and lower extremity elevation. She reports difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning clothes and writing.
C3 History: Patient reports no diabetes, significant alcohol use, or family history of hereditary neuropathy. She denies recent infections or medication changes aside from chemotherapy. No prior chemotherapy exposure.
C4 Examination: On examination, bilateral lower extremity sensation is diminished distally in a stocking distribution. Vibration sense is reduced to the ankle bilaterally. Achilles reflexes are absent bilaterally. Motor strength in lower extremities remains intact at 5/5.
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction studies and electromyography demonstrate a sensory-predominant axonal polyneuropathy with reduced sensory nerve action potentials bilaterally in the sural and superficial peroneal nerves. No demyelinating features are present. MRI of the lumbar spine is normal, ruling out structural causes.
#176 Rotator Cuff Tear Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent progression from painless weakness → inability to abduct against gravity → marked muscle atrophy → MRI showing full-thickness tear with fatty infiltration; clinical reasoning is sound throughout.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 71-year-old retired teacher presents with painless progressive weakness of the right shoulder over 12 months, with inability to actively raise her arm overhead. She initially attributed symptoms to aging but sought evaluation when function significantly deteriorated.
C2 Activity: She cannot perform overhead tasks such as reaching into cabinets or combing her hair. Attempting to abduct her shoulder against gravity results in immediate collapse. She can perform passive range of motion comfortably but cannot actively lift her arm from her lap. Non-rotator cuff activities are relatively preserved with good hand grip strength.
C3 History: She denies any acute shoulder injury or trauma. She did have a remote history of shoulder impingement symptoms 8 years ago that improved with conservative treatment. She has not undergone any shoulder surgery. She reports long-standing use of corticosteroid inhalers for COPD (15 years). She attributes her weakness to aging and declining activity level.
C4 Examination: On examination, she demonstrates severe weakness of shoulder abduction (1/5) and external rotation (0/5). The drop arm test is dramatically positive with inability to maintain any arm elevation. Infraspinatus atrophy is marked and visible bilaterally with greater atrophy on right (affected) side. Supraspinatus atrophy is also present. Strength testing demonstrates preserved hand grip (5/5) and elbow strength (5/5). Passive shoulder motion is pain-free and full.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the right shoulder reveals a large full-thickness rotator cuff tear involving both supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons measuring 2.8 cm x 1.5 cm with significant retraction. Severe fatty infiltration of both muscles indicating chronic degenerative change (Goutallier grade 3-4). Supraspinatus muscle atrophy is advanced. No significant arthritic changes. Acromion is a hooked morphology.
#177 Hip Fracture Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately general (elderly patient with fall and hip pain), subsequent clues add functional limitation, medical history, physical exam findings, and imaging confirmation; logical and fair progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 78-year-old woman with known osteoporosis fell at home and was unable to get up. She has severe pain in the hip and groin region with immediate onset. She was found on the floor 2 hours later.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot tolerate any weight on the left leg. Pain is constant and severe; even passive hip movements (flexion, rotation) are acutely painful. She is bed-bound and requires full assistance.
C3 History: Patient has had osteoporosis for 8 years but reports poor adherence to therapy. She lives independently and ambulated with a walker at baseline. No head injury, confusion, or loss of consciousness reported.
C4 Examination: On examination, the left lower extremity is shortened and externally rotated compared to the right. Left hip is markedly tender, and any attempt at movement (flexion, internal/external rotation) reproduces severe pain. Hip flexors and extensors are 0/5 due to pain inhibition. Sensation and pulses are intact distally.
C5 Imaging: X-rays of the left hip reveal a displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture. CT imaging confirms a subcapital fracture with approximately 90-degree angulation. There is significant osteopenia evident on imaging. Associated soft tissue swelling is noted in the hip region.
#178 Idiopathic Adolescent Scoliosis Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C2 and C3 largely overlap (both emphasize lack of pain/symptoms and truncal asymmetry); C3 should focus more on progression/timeline rather than repeating functional status.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 14-year-old girl is referred to physiotherapy following screening detection of spinal curvature at school. She reports no pain and has no functional complaints, but her mother notes she appears to have unequal shoulder heights and breast heights when viewed from behind.
C2 Activity: The patient reports no activity limitations, pain, or breathing difficulty with normal daily activities, sports, or school. She has no difficulty with physical education and participates in volleyball without symptoms. Posture appears asymmetrical with shoulder drop on one side and truncal asymmetry evident.
C3 History: Patient denies any activity limitations, pain, or breathing difficulty even with volleyball. Truncal asymmetry is visually evident; patient and parents have noticed progressive postural changes over the past 6–12 months.
C4 Examination: On examination, Adam's forward bend test reveals asymmetrical rib prominence on the right side with thoracic rib hump measuring approximately 1.5 cm. Shoulder heights are unequal with right shoulder higher than left. Skin examination is normal without hairy patches, cafe-au-lait spots, or dimpling.
C5 Imaging: Spinal X-rays demonstrate a right thoracic spinal curvature measuring approximately 35 degrees Cobb angle. The curve is primarily thoracic with apex at T7-T8. Vertebral heights are normal, and there is no kyphosis, vertebral rotation, or bony abnormalities suggesting secondary causes.
#179 Stress Urinary Incontinence Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (involuntary urine leakage), each clue adds meaningful information (triggers, response to pelvic floor training, muscle strength findings, urethral hypermobility), and imaging confirms mechanism.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old woman presents with a 5-year history of involuntary urine leakage with coughing, sneezing, and laughing that has progressively worsened and is now affecting her social and professional life. She reports light-to-moderate urine loss during these exertional events, particularly when activities are unexpected.
C2 Activity: Incontinence is reliably triggered by physical exertion including coughing, sneezing, jumping, and exercise activity. Walking long distances may precipitate leakage if the bladder is full. The patient reports relief with pelvic floor muscle contraction before coughing and with regular pelvic floor muscle training exercises.
C3 History: Patient reports she can manage symptoms by contracting pelvic floor muscles proactively (before coughing/sneezing) and has noticed improvement with dedicated pelvic floor muscle training over the past 6 months. She has not sought other treatments (pessary, medications, or injections) to date.
C4 Examination: On examination, pelvic floor muscle strength is graded 3/5 using Oxford scale, with reduced endurance and poor voluntary contraction quality. Cough test with full bladder reproduces leakage. Abdominal examination is soft without masses. Neurological examination is normal. Urinalysis is negative for infection.
C5 Imaging: Pelvic ultrasound during Valsalva maneuver demonstrates urethral hypermobility with funneling of the bladder neck and descent of the proximal urethra of approximately 1.5 cm. Post-void residual is normal at 15 mL. No pelvic organ prolapse or other structural abnormalities are identified.
#180 Lumbar Radiculopathy Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from acute lifting injury with radicular pain → activity triggers and relief pattern → neurological findings (weakness, sensory loss, diminished reflex) → MRI showing nerve root compression; standard and fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old manual laborer presents with acute onset lower back and leg pain following a lifting injury at work 2 weeks ago. Pain radiates down the right leg with associated numbness and tingling.
C2 Activity: Pain is significantly worse with forward bending and prolonged sitting. Walking and extension-based movements provide relief. Patient has stopped heavy lifting and adopted a lumbar support belt.
C3 History: Patient reports a 3-week history of progressive worsening symptoms after the acute lifting incident. He has a prior history of lower back pain 5 years ago that resolved with conservative care. He denies bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or progressive neurological decline. He reports sleeping poorly due to leg pain.
C4 Examination: On examination, straight leg raise on the right is limited to 40 degrees due to leg pain. Right ankle dorsiflexion strength is 4/5 with normal hip flexor strength. Sensation to light touch is reduced in the right L5 distribution (lateral leg and dorsum of foot). Right knee reflex is normal but ankle reflex is diminished at 1+.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the lumbar spine demonstrates a right-sided disc herniation at L5-S1 level with extrusion of nuclear material. There is compression of the right L5 nerve root with associated nerve root edema. The herniated material is in the axillary zone, just lateral to the spinal canal. No cauda equina syndrome is evident.
#181 Medial Tibial Stress Syndrome Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple lower leg conditions; progression is logical from activity patterns to specific posteromedial location to imaging confirmation of stress reaction without fracture.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 26-year-old recreational runner presents with progressive lower leg pain over 6 weeks that has forced her to reduce running frequency and distance. She reports morning stiffness and tightness in the lower leg that improves with activity, and localized swelling.
C2 Activity: Pain is aggravated by running, particularly on hard or uneven surfaces, and by impact activities. The patient reports pain during the first 10-15 minutes of running that improves during the run, then worsens again after activity. Walking causes mild discomfort, and the patient has modified her training to non-impact activities.
C3 History: Patient recently increased her running volume by 50% over the past 8 weeks as she trained for a half-marathon. She reports a change to a new running shoe 3 months ago. No acute trauma, no previous lower leg injuries. She denies systemic symptoms, fevers, or weight loss. She runs 4-5 days per week.
C4 Examination: On examination, localized tenderness is present along the posteromedial tibia in the middle third of the leg, spanning approximately 5 cm. There is mild swelling and erythema over the tender area. Palpation reproduces pain. Ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion are full and painless. Lower extremity alignment shows mild pronation at the foot.
C5 Imaging: MRI reveals cortical stress reaction at the posteromedial tibia in the middle third with periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema. Tibialis posterior and soleus fascia show inflammation. No fracture line is visible, consistent with early-stage stress injury rather than frank fracture or other pathology.
#182 Cystic Fibrosis Lungs

Verdict: Clue 3 and C4 are nearly identical with redundant information, C3 already names 'genetic pulmonary condition' which is too specific, and the progression lacks meaningful new information across clues.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 16-year-old with chronic productive cough and dyspnea on exertion. Progressive respiratory symptoms with frequent infections. Functional capacity has declined over the past year.
C2 Activity: A 16-year-old with a one-year history of progressive dyspnea on exertion and chronic productive cough. Recently experiencing fatigue and recurrent infections requiring treatment.
C3 History: Diagnosed with genetic pulmonary condition in early childhood. History of recurrent respiratory tract infections treated with multiple antibiotic courses. Pancreatic insufficiency managed with enzyme supplementation.
C4 Examination: Genetic pulmonary condition first diagnosed in early childhood. Multiple courses of antibiotics for recurrent respiratory infections. No pancreatic or gastrointestinal issues reported.
C5 Imaging: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates hyperinflation with bronchial wall thickening and bronchiectatic changes predominantly in upper lobes. Evidence of bronchial secretion impaction with localized air trapping.
#183 Spina Bifida Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too specific (lower extremity weakness with sensory loss below mid-back plus wheelchair use essentially defines spina bifida), leaving insufficient diagnostic challenge across the 5 clues.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An 8-year-old with lower extremity weakness and loss of sensation below the mid-back. Patient uses wheelchair for community mobility but has limitations with transfers and positional changes.
C2 Activity: 8-year-old with chronic lower limb weakness and reduced sensation. Uses wheelchair for distance but family reports increasing difficulty with positional transfers and balance activities.
C3 History: Patient manages self-care with assistance but has poor trunk control in sitting. Cannot participate in playground activities; bladder and bowel management requires scheduled catheterization and bowel routine.
C4 Examination: Detected prenatally; emergency spinal closure within 48 hours of birth. Subsequent surgeries for progressive deformity. VP shunt placed for hydrocephalus. Multiple surgical episodes across childhood.
C5 Imaging: MRI of spine demonstrates segmental spinal cord abnormality with partial agenesis at thoracic level. Progressive spinal deformity with 45-degree scoliotic curve noted on X-ray. Lateral recess stenosis from ossified spinal canal.
#184 Lymphoedema Limbs
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately broad (post-cancer swelling could be many things); each clue adds specificity through activity limitations, surgical history, examination findings, and imaging confirmation of lymphatic pathology.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 47-year-old woman with progressive swelling of the right upper extremity following cancer treatment. Notes heaviness and tightness in arm and hand with clothing becoming too tight over weeks.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with fine motor tasks including gripping and keyboard work. Unable to wear rings or watch on affected arm. Experiences increased swelling after prolonged use of arm or in warm weather. Sleep disrupted due to uncomfortable arm positioning.
C3 History: Right breast cancer treated with mastectomy and axillary lymph node dissection 18 months ago. Completed adjuvant chemotherapy and radiation to chest wall. No known family history of lymphatic disorders. Recent cellulitis episode of affected arm.
C4 Examination: Right arm circumference exceeds left by 3.5 cm at forearm; 2.8 cm at wrist. Pitting edema present in dorsum of hand and forearm. Skin thickened with loss of normal contours; palpable nodularity in subcutaneous tissue. Stemmer sign positive.
C5 Imaging: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates absent lymph uptake in right axilla with delayed transit in upper extremity. Ultrasound confirms honeycomb pattern of subcutaneous edema with echogenic dermal thickening. Absent or hypoplastic deep lymph nodes on imaging.
#185 Serratus Anterior Injury Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple shoulder conditions; clear progression through mechanism, scapular winging on exam, and EMG/MRI confirmation of long thoracic nerve injury makes for a fair and educational puzzle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old competitive athlete with 3 weeks of progressive shoulder blade pain. Notices the shoulder blade on one side looks different when training, and overhead activities feel uncomfortable.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform push-ups, bench press, or throwing activities without exacerbation of scapular pain. Shoulder flexion and horizontal adduction movements reproduce symptoms. Fatigue and heaviness in shoulder girdle after minimal activity.
C3 History: Acute onset during intense strength training involving heavy bench pressing 3 weeks ago. No prior shoulder injuries. Progressive weakness noted with each training session. Denied neurological symptoms or neck pain referral.
C4 Examination: Marked scapular winging with medial border prominence; winging increases with wall push-up. Weakness of scapular protraction on manual testing. Inability to maintain scapular position during forward arm raise.
C5 Imaging: EMG demonstrates denervation pattern in serratus anterior with positive sharp waves and fibrillations. Serratus anterior muscle shows altered signal intensity on MRI. Nerve conduction studies of long thoracic nerve show evidence of axonal injury.
#186 Shoulder Arthroscopy Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C3 and C4 overlap significantly (both describe post-operative status and ROM findings); Clue 5 adequately rules out structural failure but the puzzle would benefit from more distinct separation between clues 3-4.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 35-year-old with shoulder pain and stiffness for 6 weeks following a sports-related overhead injury. Pain worsens with movement and at night. Currently attending physiotherapy with minimal improvement.
C2 Activity: Unable to reach overhead or out to side beyond 45 degrees. Sleeping on affected shoulder not tolerable. Avoids pushing/pulling activities. Pain with dressing and grooming tasks.
C3 History: Injury occurred during overhead throwing sport. Underwent surgical repair 6 weeks ago. Patient reports good adherence to post-operative restrictions and early physiotherapy. No prior shoulder pathology or surgery.
C4 Examination: Significant discrepancy between passive (110° flexion) and active ROM (60° flexion, 45° abduction). Marked muscle guarding and apprehension. Pain-limited movement. Deltoid strength difficult to isolate due to guarding.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative ultrasound demonstrates successful repair integrity with no evidence of re-tear, gap formation, or fluid collection. Findings exclude major structural failure and support mechanistic cause of persistent stiffness as adhesions or inadequate post-operative mobilization rather than surgical complication.

Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple headache types; good progression through triggers/activity relationship, postural/muscular findings, and imaging exclusion of structural pathology.
