C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old office worker with frequent bilateral headaches described as pressing/squeezing around the head. Headaches occur most days, lasting hours to full day, without complete resolution between episodes.
C2 Activity: Symptoms worsen with sustained computer work and poor posture; improve with brief breaks and neck stretching. Stress and fatigue are clear triggers. No nausea or light sensitivity, but neck stiffness accompanies headache onset.
C3 History: Headache pattern started approximately 2 years ago coinciding with job change requiring prolonged desk work. Denies head trauma, neurological symptoms, or medication overuse. Previous episodes responded well to rest and over-the-counter analgesics.
C4 Examination: Palpation reveals significant tenderness in bilateral upper trapezius and suboccipital muscles. Forward head posture noted with chin jutting during cervical flexion. Cervical range of motion preserved but reproduction of symptoms with sustained flexion. No focal neurological deficits.
C5 Imaging: Cervical spine X-rays demonstrate loss of normal cervical lordosis consistent with postural dysfunction. MRI excludes structural pathology, mass, or intracranial abnormality. No evidence of cervical spondylosis or foraminal stenosis.
#188 Boxer's Fracture Hand
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 and C2 are nearly identical (both describe acute hand injury from boxing 2 weeks ago), C3 and C4 repeat overlapping information, and there is insufficient progression to warrant 5 distinct clues.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 24-year-old male fighter with acute hand injury sustained during boxing training 2 weeks ago. Localized pain and swelling at knuckle level with visible deformity of hand.
C2 Activity: A 24-year-old male with acute hand pain and swelling sustained during contact sport training 2 weeks ago. Localized symptoms at knuckle level with visible hand deformity.
C3 History: Direct punch impact to knuckle during sparring session. Initial swelling managed at home with ice and elevation. No prior hand injuries or fractures. Training halted immediately post-injury; minimal physiotherapy initiated.
C4 Examination: No prior hand fractures or injuries. Initial management with ice and elevation only; no imaging obtained acutely. Patient remains concerned about hand alignment and residual prominence at knuckle.
C5 Imaging: X-ray hand demonstrates fifth metacarpal neck fracture with angulation of 35 degrees and minimal displacement. Metacarpal head intact without intra-articular extension. No associated carpal bone involvement or soft tissue ossification.
#189 Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome Pelvis
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: The clinical picture is complex and partially contradictory (5-year history but S2-S4 sensory loss suggests neuropathy rather than pure CPPS); Clue 1 could be more specific and the imaging clue somewhat contradicts the severity of symptoms.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old woman presents with a 5-year history of chronic pain in the pelvic region. She reports dyspareunia and urinary frequency but denies fever, weight loss, or systemic symptoms.
C2 Activity: She avoids prolonged standing and sitting due to symptom exacerbation. She reports difficulty with normal bowel and bladder function. Symptoms ease with rest in a supine position with hips flexed.
C3 History: The patient reports a history of hysterectomy 8 years ago without resolution of pain. She has had multiple consultations with gynecologists and urologists with no clear diagnosis. She denies fever, weight loss, or malignancy risk factors. She reports significant psychological distress related to chronic pain.
C4 Examination: Pelvic floor muscles are hypertonic and tender on palpation. Hip abduction and internal rotation produce discomfort at end-range. Lower limb reflexes and motor strength are intact, but sensory testing reveals decreased light touch sensation in a dermatomal pattern corresponding to S2-S4.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the pelvis demonstrates no visible masses, adhesions, or structural abnormalities. The bladder, uterus (absent post-hysterectomy), and rectum appear normal. Imaging findings are reassuring and do not identify any obvious pathology explaining the patient's chronic pain.
#190 Traumatic Brain Injury Brain
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple post-concussive conditions; logical progression through vestibular/balance findings on exam to imaging confirmation of contusions and microhemorrhages consistent with traumatic mechanism.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 19-year-old student with persistent headaches, dizziness, and significant difficulty concentrating. Reports fatigue that interferes with daily tasks. Symptoms began 4 weeks ago following a motor vehicle collision.
C2 Activity: Unable to tolerate prolonged visual tasks or bright environments. Vertigo triggered specifically by rapid head movements. Gait unsteady, particularly on uneven surfaces or when turning.
C3 History: Loss of consciousness for approximately 3 minutes at scene. Admitted to hospital and required ICU-level monitoring in acute phase. No mechanical ventilation needed. Previously no significant medical or neurological history.
C4 Examination: Dynamic gait assessment reveals unsteady balance with increased arm swing for compensation. Positive Romberg test with 20-second stance instability. Dix-Hallpike maneuver triggers vertigo; Fukuda test shows significant deviation. Cranial nerves II-XII intact; no focal neurological deficits.
C5 Imaging: Follow-up MRI demonstrates resolving contusions in frontal lobes with minimal residual subdural fluid. Microhemorrhages visible on susceptibility-weighted imaging in white matter tracts. Cerebrospinal fluid spaces normal; no mass effect or midline shift.
#191 Prepatellar Bursitis Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (anterior knee swelling fits multiple conditions), each clue adds specific details (localization, chronicity, mechanism, exam findings, imaging confirmation), and the progression is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old with acute-onset knee swelling localized to the front of the kneecap. Visible fullness anterior to patellar tendon with noticeable increase in size over several days.
C2 Activity: 45-year-old with acute-onset knee swelling over the past week. Reports visible fullness in front of the knee that has progressively worsened. Denies significant trauma or mechanical symptoms.
C3 History: New job requiring frequent kneeling positions on hard flooring for 8-10 hours daily. Symptoms began after three weeks of work. Denies recent fall or direct knee trauma. No previous knee problems or inflammatory arthritis history.
C4 Examination: Palpable, fluctuant swelling anterior to patella measuring approximately 4 cm in transverse diameter. Skin erythema and slight warmth over bursa; no fever. Pain with deep palpation of bursa. Knee joint motion unrestricted; meniscal and ligamentous tests normal.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates simple fluid collection anterior to patella without debris or septations. Bursa measures 1.2 cm at greatest depth. Patellar tendon and underlying joint space sonographically normal. No bursal wall enhancement on post-contrast imaging.
#192 Kinesiophobia General
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (persistent back pain post-injury fits many conditions), clues progressively reveal psychological overlay and fear-avoidance patterns, and imaging inconsistency with disability level clinches the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old reports persistent low back pain 8 months after a work-related strain injury. Pain level has remained high despite gradual functional improvement and medical clearance to gradually resume normal activities.
C2 Activity: Avoids bending, lifting, and sustained sitting despite medical clearance for light activity. Catastrophizes about pain with minimal provocation. Self-imposed restrictions on activities far exceed physiological limitations. Delayed recovery despite gradual symptom improvement.
C3 History: Initial lumbar strain from work injury 8 months ago. Received passive therapies exclusively; no progressive exercise rehabilitation. Multiple provider visits with varying diagnoses heightened anxiety. Family members reinforce avoidance behaviors; overprotective support system present.
C4 Examination: Objective findings minimal and inconsistent: straight leg raise limited by guarding but improves markedly when patient is distracted during conversation. Able to flex lumbar spine 60 degrees when attention diverted, but reports severe pain with intentional forward bending. Fear-avoidance beliefs questionnaire indicates elevated fear-related cognitions with score in severe range (67/96).
C5 Imaging: MRI shows minimal degenerative changes with small central disc protrusion at L4-L5 without cord compression; findings inconsistent with reported disability level. Prior imaging reviewed; changes have remained stable. No imaging evidence of progressive structural pathology.
#193 Achilles Tendinopathy Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately general (Achilles region pain with activity pattern fits several ankle/foot conditions), clues logically progress through activity limitations, training history, clinical findings, and imaging confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old recreational runner with progressive pain in the Achilles tendon region. Reports morning stiffness and pain that improves with activity, then worsens toward evening and after intense running.
C2 Activity: Running distances limited to less than 3 km before pain becomes limiting. Uphill running and speed work significantly aggravate symptoms. Walking on stairs, particularly downstairs, causes focal Achilles pain. Stiffness pronounced after sedentary periods.
C3 History: Recent increase in running volume over past 8 weeks; added hills and tempo sessions without gradual progression. No prior significant Achilles problems. Previous left ankle sprain 5 years ago. Tight calves noted since increased training. Age-appropriate general health; no inflammatory conditions.
C4 Examination: Tenderness on palpation of Achilles tendon at 3-4 cm proximal to insertion, with palpable nodularity. Calf strength 5/5 bilaterally but pain with resisted plantarflexion against resistance. Single leg calf raise limited to 8 repetitions; pain increases progressively.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates thickening of Achilles tendon at midportion with hypoechoic regions suggesting intratendinous degeneration. Doppler flow reveals increased neovascularity within the tendon. Retrocalcaneal bursitis noted on imaging with fluid accumulation.
#194 Lateral Epicondylitis Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is sufficiently broad (lateral elbow pain from repetitive activity could suggest multiple upper extremity conditions), each clue adds meaningful detail (gripping mechanism, specific tests, imaging findings), and progression is smooth.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old with localized lateral elbow pain following repetitive gripping activities. Pain is sharp and well-localized over the lateral elbow prominence with progressive worsening over 6 weeks.
C2 Activity: Gripping, lifting, and twisting activities reproduce pain. Difficulty carrying objects with the affected arm; weak grip strength noted. Symptoms aggravated by pronation of forearm under load. Pain often radiates down lateral forearm toward wrist.
C3 History: Began after increased repetitive work involving tool use and forceful gripping. No prior elbow injuries; no rheumatological history. Works as tradesperson requiring repetitive wrist and forearm activity. Symptoms gradually worsened despite continued activity on affected arm.
C4 Examination: Point tenderness over lateral epicondyle with pain reproduction on palpation. Mill's test and Cozen's test both positive eliciting lateral elbow pain. Grip strength reduced 15% compared to contralateral side. Wrist extension against resistance reproduces symptoms; no neurological deficits present.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound reveals hypoechoic thickening of common extensor tendon origin at lateral epicondyle with heterogeneous echotexture. Mild surrounding soft tissue edema noted. Power Doppler demonstrates neovascularity within the tendon. No calcifications or cortical irregularities.
#195 Stress Fracture - Metatarsal Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately vague (foot overuse pain fits many midfoot/forefoot conditions), clues progressively narrow through mileage history, female athlete with metabolic risk factors, localized tenderness with load, and definitive imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old recreational runner presents with localized foot pain of gradual onset over the past 3 weeks. The pain is activity-dependent and worsens with prolonged weight-bearing. This presentation could suggest several overuse injuries or acute traumatic conditions affecting the midfoot.
C2 Activity: Pain is reproducibly triggered by running and walking activities, particularly on hard surfaces. The patient reports relief with complete rest and ice application. Progressive functional decline with each week of attempted training.
C3 History: Recent increase in running mileage by 40% over four weeks without adequate recovery. No acute traumatic event recalled. Patient is female with history of irregular menstrual cycles and reports low dietary calcium intake.
C4 Examination: On palpation, focal point tenderness at the second or third metatarsal shaft with reproducible pain on applied load-bearing. Single-leg stance and hopping produce immediate pain. Swelling is minimal and localized to the affected metatarsal.
C5 Imaging: X-rays demonstrate a linear fracture line through the metatarsal shaft with early periosteal reaction at the margins. MRI shows bone marrow edema surrounding the fracture site with characteristic stress reaction pattern in the trabecular bone.
#196 Radial Head Fracture Elbow
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: The FOOSH mechanism in Clue 1 is quite specific and immediately suggests radial head fracture to experienced clinicians; consider making Clue 1 more general (e.g., 'acute-onset lateral elbow pain and swelling after falling on an outstretched hand') without emphasizing the lateral localization.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old presents with acute onset elbow pain and swelling after a fall on an outstretched hand while rollerblading. Pain is laterally localized and worsens with movement. The mechanism involved falling from standing height onto an extended arm.
C2 Activity: Patient reports inability to supinate the forearm without marked pain and functional loss. Pronation is relatively preserved but limited. Weight-bearing through the upper extremity in standing push position is not tolerated.
C3 History: No prior elbow injuries or instability. No inflammatory history. Impact occurred at the lateral aspect of the elbow during the fall. Immediate swelling and inability to continue activity.
C4 Examination: On examination, palpable point tenderness over the radial head with reactive swelling. Pain is elicited with supination against resistance. Varus and valgus stress tests are negative; however, rotational movements of the forearm produce significant discomfort.
C5 Imaging: CT imaging reveals a fracture through the radial head with minimal displacement of the fracture fragment. The fracture line extends through the articular surface of the radiocapitellar joint. Associated soft tissue swelling is present without evidence of significant comminution.
#197 Cancer-Related Fatigue General
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 3 essentially names the diagnosis by stating 'chemotherapy 4 months ago' and 'systemic illness,' leaving no real mystery; restructure to obscure the cancer history earlier or reformulate as a broader fatigue presentation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old patient reports a 6-month history of persistent, overwhelming tiredness that is disproportionate to physical activity level and not relieved by rest. Daily activities that were previously manageable now require significant effort.
C2 Activity: Work capacity has declined from full-time employment to part-time, with frequent required rest days. Even light household tasks such as cooking and laundry result in exhaustion lasting several hours. Social and recreational activities have been substantially curtailed.
C3 History: Patient underwent chemotherapy 4 months ago for a newly diagnosed systemic illness. Although fatigue was expected during treatment, it has persisted beyond the typical recovery window. Sleep quality remains disrupted with early morning awakening.
C4 Examination: On assessment, resting heart rate is elevated at 88 bpm. No objective weakness is found on manual muscle testing, which is normal throughout. Cardiovascular and respiratory responses to light exertion are disproportionately elevated compared to normal activity levels.
C5 Imaging: Laboratory analysis shows persistent anemia (hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL) and elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR). Thyroid function studies are normal. Imaging surveillance shows no evidence of disease recurrence.
#198 Shoulder Osteoarthritis Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (progressive shoulder pain and stiffness fits rotator cuff pathology, impingement, or OA), clues methodically add activity restrictions, remote trauma history, exam findings showing crepitus/loss of motion, and imaging confirmation of degenerative changes.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 64-year-old with several years of progressive shoulder pain and stiffness. Pain worsens with overhead activities and is notably stiff in the mornings. Gradual onset over many years.
C2 Activity: Overhead reaching for items on shelves is significantly limited and painful. Swimming, a formerly enjoyed activity, now produces pain during both backstroke and freestyle. Dressing activities, particularly putting on a shirt, are increasingly difficult.
C3 History: Remote shoulder dislocation treated conservatively 15 years prior. Multiple episodes of subacromial impingement over the past decade. Thirty years of occupational overhead work.
C4 Examination: On examination, active external rotation is limited to 45 degrees with pain at end-range. Painful arc is present between 70-120 degrees of abduction. Crepitus is palpable within the glenohumeral joint during movement. Supraspinatus and infraspinatus strength testing shows weakness secondary to pain.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate joint space narrowing at the glenohumeral joint with osteophyte formation along the humeral head margins. MRI confirms hyaline cartilage thinning and small focal chondral defects. Bone marrow edema is present at the articular surfaces.
#199 Lateral Ankle Sprain Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately general (acute ankle pain after cutting movement fits multiple ankle injuries), clues logically progress through inability to bear weight, previous injury history, specific ligamentous tests, and MRI grading/associated injuries.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 22-year-old basketball player presents acutely with ankle pain and swelling after a sudden cutting movement during a game. The injury occurred approximately 2 hours ago.
C2 Activity: Patient reports inability to bear weight on the affected side. Attempted ambulation produces severe pain. Running, jumping, and any lateral weight shift are impossible.
C3 History: This ankle was previously injured 8 months ago with similar mechanism. That prior sprain was treated with rest and taping but did not receive formal physiotherapy rehabilitation. Patient reports ankle 'giving way' episodes twice in the intervening months.
C4 Examination: On examination, the anterior drawer test is positive with anterior displacement of the talus. Inversion stress testing reproduces pain and excessive ankle motion. Palpable tenderness is present over the anterior talofibular ligament. Proprioceptive deficits are apparent on single-leg stance testing.
C5 Imaging: MRI reveals grade II tear of the anterior talofibular ligament with partial disruption of the calcaneofibular ligament. Edema surrounds both ligaments. Small syndesmotic ligament inflammation is visible at the tibiofibular joint.
#200 Golfer's Hip Hip
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 uses the term 'Golfer's Hip' colloquially which may be too leading; consider rephrasing as 'insidious-onset groin pain in an avid golfer' to be more neutral, though the puzzle is otherwise solid and playable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old avid golfer presents with insidious-onset groin pain that has progressively worsened over 6 months. Pain is localized to the medial hip and inner thigh region.
C2 Activity: The golf swing, particularly the follow-through phase, reproduces pain consistently. Walking for distances exceeding 15 minutes triggers discomfort. Internally rotating the hip, such as crossing the leg while sitting, immediately provokes sharp groin pain.
C3 History: 30-year golf history with chronic poor swing mechanics emphasizing deep hip rotation. Recently completed extended tournament schedule over 4 weeks with minimal rest. No acute traumatic event. Progressive activity-related presentation.
C4 Examination: The FABER test reproduces pain in the ipsilateral groin. Internal hip rotation in flexion is painful and limited to 20 degrees. Hip adductor strength testing shows pain-limited weakness at 4/5. Palpation of the adductor longus origin is tender.
C5 Imaging: MRI reveals adductor longus tendinopathic changes with mild partial tearing at the pubic symphysis insertion. Concurrent mild hip joint cartilage thinning and edema at pubic symphysis noted.
#201 Post-Meniscectomy Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad for acute post-operative knee pain, progression is logical with imaging clinching surgical status, and a physio student would find this fair and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old with a recent knee injury presents with persistent joint pain and swelling that has not improved with conservative management over the past 3 weeks. Pain is localized to the knee and worsens with activity. Some loss of motion is noted.
C2 Activity: Walking distances greater than 10 minutes increases pain and swelling. Stairs, particularly descending, are difficult and require rail support. Sitting with the knee flexed beyond 90 degrees produces discomfort.
C3 History: Patient sustained a medial meniscal injury requiring surgical intervention 3 weeks ago. Prior to injury, patient was training at a competitive level with high-impact demands. Recovery is slower than anticipated at this stage.
C4 Examination: On examination, reactive joint effusion is palpable in the suprapatellar pouch. Knee flexion is limited to 110 degrees secondary to pain and swelling. Quadriceps strength is 4/5 with visible atrophy. McMurray's test is now negative; the medial joint line tenderness has resolved.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative imaging demonstrates successful surgical resection with evidence of reactive inflammatory changes within the joint. No evidence of iatrogenic ligament injury or articular cartilage involvement. Structural integrity of remaining knee anatomy is preserved.
#202 Plantar Fasciitis Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Classic presentation with characteristic morning pain pattern in Clue 1, activity triggers and examination findings align well, and imaging provides definitive confirmation via fascia thickening measurement.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old presents with heel pain that is worst upon rising in the morning and taking the first steps, then gradually improves with activity before returning at end of day.
C2 Activity: Activities requiring prolonged standing or walking on hard surfaces significantly exacerbate pain. Patient notices barefoot walking and light footwear worsen symptoms; pain is minimal when wearing supportive shoes with cushioning.
C3 History: Pain began insidiously 4 months ago without specific precipitating injury. Patient recently increased exercise volume and has BMI of 32. Works a job requiring standing for 6-8 hours daily. Wears predominantly flat casual shoes without arch support.
C4 Examination: On palpation, discrete tenderness is elicited at the medial calcaneal tubercle along the plantar fascia insertion. Pain is reproduced with passive dorsiflexion of the foot combined with toe extension. Calf tightness limits ankle dorsiflexion to 5 degrees.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound imaging demonstrates fascia thickening to 4.2 mm (normal <3 mm) at the medial insertion. Hypoechoic appearance indicates inflammatory change. No heel spur is visible on radiographs, ruling out the osseous pathology.
#203 Burns Rehabilitation Various
✗ REWORK
Verdict: The puzzle conflates acute burn wound management with physiotherapy rehabilitation; Clue 1 is too vague and could fit many post-injury conditions, and the focus on wound healing rather than movement dysfunction makes it poorly suited for a physio diagnosis game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old manual laborer presents 4 weeks after a thermal injury to the hand and forearm. Pain and functional loss are significant. Wound care is ongoing.
C2 Activity: Unable to grip or perform fine motor tasks. Self-care activities including bathing and dressing are severely limited. Sleep is disrupted by pain and frequent dressing protocol requirements.
C3 History: Injury occurred 4 weeks ago from hot liquid exposure while cooking. Lives alone and relies on manual work. No surgical intervention (grafting) has been performed. Treated conservatively with dressings and topical care.
C4 Examination: Wound bed shows granulation tissue with epithelialization beginning at margins. Moderate persistent edema in hand and forearm. Early finger flexion contractures evident on passive range of motion. Grip strength severely limited by pain on testing.
C5 Imaging: Thermal imaging demonstrates heterogeneous temperature distribution across the wound bed indicating variable perfusion and healing rates. Ultrasound shows hypoechoic edema within subcutaneous tissues. No imaging evidence of deep structure involvement such as tendon damage.
#204 Medial Collateral Ligament Sprain Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately covers acute knee ligament injuries broadly, valgus stress test in Clue 4 adds specific physical examination information, and MRI grading in Clue 5 clinches the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 29-year-old female soccer player reports acute onset medial knee pain and swelling sustained during a game. Injury occurred with non-contact mechanism during dynamic movement. Pain is localized around the knee joint.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform cutting or rapid directional changes. Straight-line running is tolerated. Activities requiring knee stability with rotation are significantly limited.
C3 History: No prior knee injuries. Patient has generalized ligamentous hypermobility affecting multiple joints. Competitive training history without prior significant lower limb pathology.
C4 Examination: On examination, valgus stress testing at 30 degrees knee flexion produces pain and increased lateral opening compared to the contralateral side. The Lachman test is negative. Palpable tenderness is present over the medial collateral ligament at the tibial attachment. Swelling is localized medially without significant effusion.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates a grade I-II strain of the medial collateral ligament with partial tearing of the fibular and tibial components. Associated edema surrounds the ligament. Anterior cruciate ligament and menisci are structurally intact without additional injury.
#205 Exercise-Induced Mood Improvement General
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This puzzle describes a psychosocial outcome rather than a clinical diagnosis, making it unsuitable for Physiodle's format; Clue 1 is too descriptive of a treatment response rather than a complaint that fits multiple pathologies.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 36-year-old reports gradual improvement in mood and anxiety over the past 6 weeks. Symptoms were previously stable despite pharmacological intervention. Patient attributes change to lifestyle modification.
C2 Activity: Patient has resumed cardiovascular exercise at moderate intensity (45 min, 5x/week). Secondary gains include restored ability to engage in previously abandoned recreational activities and normalized sleep architecture.
C3 History: One-year history of depression partially refractory to SSRI monotherapy. Exercise program initiated independently following health promotion event. Timeline of mood improvement correlates precisely with sustained physical activity adherence.
C4 Examination: Observed affect is euthymic with appropriate emotional responsiveness. Cardiovascular parameters show improvement: resting heart rate reduced by approximately 8-10 bpm, systolic blood pressure decreased 10-12 mmHg from baseline. Exercise capacity on treadmill testing increased 15-20%.
C5 Imaging: Functional neuroimaging reveals altered activation in regions associated with reward processing and emotional regulation. Biomarker panel demonstrates elevation in neurotrophic factors consistent with neuroplastic adaptation. Structural imaging unremarkable.
#206 Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough for progressive hip pain in adolescents, history of childhood treatment adds useful context without naming the diagnosis, and imaging measurements (acetabular index, center-edge angle) provide definitive confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 14-year-old adolescent female presents with progressive hip pain over the past 6 months. She reports a limp that her parents have noticed worsening gradually. No history of trauma or acute incident. Pain is felt deep in the hip joint.
C2 Activity: Running activities, particularly sprinting, consistently provoke sharp hip pain. Prolonged sitting with hip flexion causes progressive discomfort. Crawling motions and activities requiring hip abduction are significantly limited.
C3 History: Family history is positive for hip dysplasia in a maternal aunt. Patient was treated with an Pavlik harness as an infant for suspected developmental hip dysplasia but was deemed resolved. No specific documentation of hip stability confirmation at skeletal maturity has been completed.
C4 Examination: On examination, hip abductor strength testing shows weakness at 4/5 with pain reproduction. The FABER test produces lateral hip and buttock discomfort. Internal hip rotation is limited and painful. A subtle positive Trendelenburg sign is evident during single-leg stance.
C5 Imaging: Plain film radiographs demonstrate increased acetabular index measuring 28 degrees (normal <20 degrees). The center-edge angle is diminished at 18 degrees. MRI shows early labral chondral damage at the acetabular margin with adjacent cartilage irregularity.
#207 Coccydynia Pelvis
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clues 1 and 2 are nearly identical and redundant (both describe localized pain worsened by sitting); condensing or restructuring these would improve progression without changing the overall playability.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged adult presents with localized pain in the lower sacral region that has been present for several weeks. The pain is worse with sitting and standing, and the patient reports difficulty with activities of daily living without clear antecedent trauma.
C2 Activity: A middle-aged adult presents with localized pain in the lower back and buttock region that has worsened over recent weeks. Pain is aggravated by prolonged sitting and standing, with no clear injury recalled.
C3 History: The patient reports that sitting exacerbates pain dramatically, particularly on hard surfaces, whereas lying flat provides significant relief. Forward bending movements also provoke discomfort.
C4 Examination: On examination, direct palpation of the sacrococcygeal region reproduces the patient's symptoms. Passive flexion of the coccyx causes sharp pain, while hip rotation is pain-free. Neurological examination of the lower limbs is normal with intact reflexes.
C5 Imaging: X-ray imaging of the sacrococcygeal region shows a mildly anteriorly deviated coccyx with increased angulation at the coccygeal articulation. Magnetic resonance imaging reveals mild degeneration of the intervertebral disc at S5 but no neural compression.
#208 Myocardial Infarction Heart
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is a medical emergency outside physiotherapy scope of practice; while a physio student should recognize it, including cardiac pathology puzzles undermines the educational focus on conditions within physiotherapy assessment and management.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old male with a history of hypertension presents with acute-onset chest discomfort described as pressure and heaviness. Associated symptoms include sweating and shortness of breath. Pain began during exertion.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot tolerate any physical exertion without symptom escalation. Rest and positional changes provide no relief. Symptoms persist at rest and are progressive.
C3 History: Former smoker with 10-year history of hypertension and high cholesterol. Family history of early coronary disease in father (age 58). Symptoms began 3 days after major workplace stress.
C4 Examination: On examination, the patient is diaphoretic with elevated heart rate (110 bpm) and blood pressure (150/95 mmHg). Auscultation reveals a fourth heart sound and mild signs of pulmonary congestion. Peripheral perfusion is reduced with cool extremities.
C5 Imaging: Electrocardiography demonstrates ST segment elevation in the anterolateral leads with reciprocal changes. Cardiac troponin levels are markedly elevated, and coronary angiography reveals a thrombotic occlusion of the left anterior descending coronary artery.
#209 Proximal Hamstring Tendinopathy Thigh
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately describes deep buttock pain fitting multiple conditions, activity triggers and examination findings progress logically, and ultrasound/MRI tendon pathology imaging clinches the specific diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old recreational runner presents with gradual-onset pain in the deep buttock region that has persisted for six weeks. The pain is felt primarily on the affected side during sitting and is described as deep ache rather than sharp sensation.
C2 Activity: The condition worsened with running and sprinting activities, particularly with high-intensity interval training. Prolonged sitting exacerbates pain significantly, whereas gentle walking and swimming provide partial relief. Hip flexion with knee extension reproduces discomfort.
C3 History: The patient recently increased running volume without adequate progression, coinciding with symptom onset. Prior physiotherapy for hip flexor tightness was ineffective, and stretching exercises have not improved symptoms. The patient reports a sedentary desk job as a secondary risk factor.
C4 Examination: On examination, resisted hip extension and adduction of the hip reproduce symptoms reproducibly. A positive slump test with hip flexion exacerbates pain, while straight leg raise is mildly restricted. Direct palpation of the ischial tuberosity region elicits tenderness.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound imaging demonstrates tendon thickening at the musculotendinous junction with hypoechoic areas consistent with partial tendon disruption. Magnetic resonance imaging confirms proximal hamstring tendon pathology with peritendinous edema but no complete rupture.
#210 Whiplash Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough for post-traumatic neck pain syndromes, psychosocial factors are appropriately mentioned early, and imaging findings with neurological examination intact provide realistic clinical context.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old reports neck pain with headache and jaw discomfort that began immediately after a motor vehicle collision. Symptoms vary throughout the day and are worsened by stress.
C2 Activity: Turning the head, particularly toward the affected side, significantly increases pain. Sustained neck postures and overhead reaching aggravate symptoms, while supported supine resting provides temporary relief. The patient avoids driving due to fear of symptom provocation.
C3 History: The collision occurred ten days ago. Patient had no prior cervical complaints. Symptoms fluctuate with emotional stress and activity levels, raising consideration of central sensitization or psychosocial factors.
C4 Examination: On examination, cervical range of motion is significantly reduced in all directions with pain reproduction on combined extension and rotation. Palpation reveals muscle guarding and tenderness in the upper trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles. Upper extremity neurological testing is normal.
C5 Imaging: MRI reveals edema in posterior cervical musculature and mild disc bulging at C5-C6 without neural involvement. X-ray shows loss of cervical lordosis but no fracture.
#211 Myasthenia Gravis Neuromuscular Junction
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (fatiguing weakness could fit multiple neuromuscular conditions), each subsequent clue progressively narrows to the diagnosis, and Clue 5 is definitive; progression is logical and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old female reports progressive weakness and drooping of the eyelid that worsens as the day progresses. She also notices her face feels tired when talking or smiling for sustained periods.
C2 Activity: She has particular difficulty with activities requiring sustained muscle contraction—prolonged reading, extended conversations, and chewing become progressively harder as the day goes on. Symptoms improve markedly after sleeping.
C3 History: Symptoms began insidiously three months ago and have progressively worsened. The patient has no significant past medical history apart from recent viral illness. Recent stress has coincided with exacerbation of symptoms, and sleep quality has been poor.
C4 Examination: On examination, sustained upward gaze reproduces progressive ptosis bilaterally within 30 seconds. Repetitive facial muscle contraction demonstrates fatiguing response, with decreased strength after five contractions. Edrophonium testing produces rapid improvement in ocular weakness.
C5 Imaging: Computed tomography of the chest reveals a mediastinal mass consistent with a thymoma. Antibody testing demonstrates significantly elevated acetylcholine receptor antibodies, and electromyography shows decremental response in ocular muscles on repetitive stimulation.
#212 Cervical Radiculopathy Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits 10+ conditions (radiculopathies, referred pain syndromes), clues progress logically from complaint through activity triggers and dermatome specificity, and Clue 5 clinches with imaging; fair and engaging for physio students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old office worker presents with sharp, radiating pain from the neck extending into the right shoulder, arm, and hand. The pain is described as burning with associated numbness and tingling in the fourth and fifth fingers, affecting daily function significantly.
C2 Activity: Prolonged sitting and repetitive reaching overhead significantly aggravate symptoms. Cervical extension and ipsilateral rotation worsen the radiating pain acutely. Relief is obtained with gentle neck flexion and use of a supportive cervical pillow during sleep.
C3 History: Symptoms began gradually two months ago without clear injury. Patient works in finance with heavy keyboard use and poor ergonomic setup. Previous neck pain episodes responded to conservative care. Current exacerbation followed a prolonged period of working from home with a makeshift desk setup.
C4 Examination: On examination, the right upper limb tension test reproduces arm pain and dysesthesia in the fourth and fifth fingers. Cervical extension and right rotation are restricted and painful. Grip strength is reduced on the right. Sensation to light touch is diminished over the medial forearm and small finger (C8 dermatome).
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging of the cervical spine demonstrates a left-sided herniated disc at C6-C7 with lateral recess stenosis compressing the C8 nerve root. The nerve root shows signal changes consistent with irritation, and moderate foraminal narrowing is present.
#213 Osteitis Pubis Hip & Groin
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 5 is duplicative of examination findings (hip adduction weakness, resisted adduction pain) rather than providing imaging data, breaking the intended 5-clue progression and leaving the puzzle clinically incomplete.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old elite soccer player presents with insidious-onset pain at the pubic symphysis that has progressed over three weeks. The pain is felt in the midline lower abdomen and groin, radiating toward the medial thigh on both sides with variable intensity.
C2 Activity: The condition worsened significantly with high-impact activities, twisting movements, and kicking, forcing the athlete to reduce training volume by 50 percent. Walking with adduction against resistance reproduces sharp pain, whereas relative rest and gentle stretching provide temporary relief.
C3 History: The patient recently increased training frequency and intensity without adequate progression. A previous history of similar symptoms resolved with conservative management six months ago. Current symptoms emerged after a five-day intensive training camp with emphasis on explosive movements.
C4 Examination: On examination, palpation of the pubic symphysis directly elicits tenderness and reproduces the patient's complaints. The adductor squeeze test performed at 45 degrees hip flexion reproduces pain, and crossed adductor palpation reveals muscle tenderness bilaterally. Hip abduction strength is reduced against resistance.
C5 Imaging: On examination, hip adduction strength is markedly reduced bilaterally, and pain is reproduced with resisted hip adduction in standing. Single-leg stance on the affected side produces groin discomfort. No neurological deficits detected; lower limb strength otherwise intact except adductors.
#214 Ulnar Collateral Ligament Injury Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (throwing-related elbow pain fits multiple pathologies), clues progress logically from complaint through activity specificity and palpation to ligament-specific tests, and Clue 5 is definitively diagnostic; playable and educationally robust.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 31-year-old athlete presents with acute onset sharp pain on the inside of the elbow that started 3 weeks ago during a throwing activity. The pain is severe and has progressively worsened, preventing him from returning to his sport.
C2 Activity: Pain is strictly activity-dependent and occurs with throwing at any velocity. Non-throwing upper extremity activities—strength training, daily tasks, overhead reaching—are completely pain-free. The pattern is consistent and reproducible with every throwing attempt.
C3 History: No acute traumatic event or fall preceded the pain—it emerged during routine training in an athlete with decades of throwing experience. No prior significant elbow pathology. Interestingly, the onset was insidious despite normal pre-season conditioning, suggesting a cumulative overuse mechanism rather than acute injury.
C4 Examination: On examination, point tenderness is localized directly over the medial collateral ligament. The moving valgus stress test is positive, reproducing sharp pain in the medial elbow with valgus stress applied at 90 degrees of elbow flexion during external rotation of the shoulder. Manual valgus stress reveals increased ligamentous laxity with a soft end-feel. Milking maneuver is positive. Upper extremity neurological examination is intact.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the elbow demonstrates a complete full-thickness tear of the anterior bundle of the ulnar collateral ligament with discontinuity of the ligament fibers and surrounding edema. The posterior bundle is intact. Significant joint effusion is present. No associated osteochondral lesions identified. No flexor-pronator tendinopathy noted. The tear is located at the proximal origin of the ligament.
#215 Hill-Sachs Lesion Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 3 mentions immobilization post-reduction, which strongly suggests prior dislocation and narrows diagnosis prematurely; moving dislocation context to Clue 4 or later would improve the progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 31-year-old recreational basketball player presents with posterior shoulder pain and apprehension triggered by abduction combined with external rotation. Onset was acute two weeks ago during competition, requiring emergency room evaluation.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform overhead throwing or defensive positioning maneuvers required in basketball. Symptoms are reproducible and episodic rather than constant; pain-free periods occur with activity modification.
C3 History: No prior significant shoulder pathology. Patient was immobilized briefly post-reduction and returned to sport within two weeks. Post-reduction imaging was deferred at the time.
C4 Examination: On examination, positive apprehension and relocation tests (Crank and Jobe) demonstrate anteroposterior instability. The posterior shoulder demonstrates loss of bone contour on palpation compared to the contralateral side. Resisted external rotation at 90 degrees abduction reproduces posterior shoulder apprehension.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging of the shoulder joint reveals a compression fracture of the posterolateral humeral head consistent with a Hill-Sachs lesion. The lesion measures approximately 20 percent of the humeral head articular surface, and associated soft tissue damage to the anterior labrum is noted.
#216 Rectocele Pelvis
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 2 is nearly identical to Clue 1 (same complaint rephrased), and Clue 5 duplicates examination findings rather than providing imaging, resulting in minimal information progression across the 5 clues.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old female reports a sensation of heaviness or bulging in the posterior vaginal wall. The patient notes difficulty with bowel evacuation and describes manual splinting of the perineum during defecation to facilitate adequate emptying, a symptom that has worsened over two years.
C2 Activity: A 58-year-old female reports progressive sensations of heaviness and fullness in the perineal region, accompanied by difficulty emptying her bowels. She has begun using manual pressure around the perineum during defecation to facilitate evacuation, a compensatory strategy that has become increasingly necessary over two years.
C3 History: Symptoms worsen significantly with prolonged standing and physical exertion; they resolve completely when lying flat. The patient reports incomplete evacuation despite 15+ minutes of straining, necessitating repeated evacuation attempts or manual assistance.
C4 Examination: On vaginal examination, a bulge of the posterior vaginal wall is observed with Valsalva maneuver, extending approximately 3 centimeters beyond the hymenal ring. Pelvic floor muscle strength is graded as 3/5 on digital assessment, and perineal descent measures 4 centimeters during straining.
C5 Imaging: Vaginal examination reveals a smooth, mobile bulge of the posterior wall that protrudes visibly with Valsalva, measuring approximately 3 centimeters distal to the hymenal ring. Pelvic floor muscles are weak (3/5 on digital contraction). Perineal descent during straining measures 4 centimeters. No palpable masses or tenderness.
#217 Central Sensitisation Syndrome Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is somewhat broad but could fit fibromyalgia or other chronic pain syndromes more specifically; the puzzle is otherwise sound, though clarifying whether this is meant to be fibromyalgia specifically would sharpen the clinical picture.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old female presents with widespread, non-specific pain affecting multiple body regions including neck, shoulders, lower back, and bilateral knees. The pain is variable in location and intensity, with associated fatigue, poor sleep quality, and cognitive difficulties that have persisted for over two years.
C2 Activity: The patient demonstrates inconsistent responses to physical activity, with some days tolerating light exercise while other days minimal activity provokes exacerbation. Pain appears disproportionate to tissue damage and can be triggered by minor physical stressors or emotional stress. Improvement plateaus despite escalating intervention intensity.
C3 History: Symptoms began insidiously following a workplace injury with subsequent multiple investigations revealing minimal structural pathology. The patient has undergone numerous treatments with limited sustained benefit, leading to increasing disability. Significant stress at work preceded symptom onset by approximately six months.
C4 Examination: Widespread allodynia is demonstrated with pain to light touch across multiple body regions. Tender point count meets diagnostic criteria with significant pain provocation. Notably, strength, reflexes, and motor coordination are fully intact; no focal neurological deficits. Two-point discrimination and proprioception are normal.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging of the affected joints reveals minimal degenerative change inconsistent with symptom severity. Functional magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates altered central pain processing with amplified responses in pain-sensitive brain regions. Laboratory investigations exclude systemic inflammatory or rheumatological conditions.
#218 Olecranon Bursitis Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute elbow swelling fits multiple conditions), clues progress logically through occupational context and fluid characteristics, and Clue 5 definitively confirms bursal fluid; playable and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old male presents with acute swelling and redness at the back of the elbow that developed over two days. He reports mild to moderate discomfort and denies significant functional loss. His occupation involves repetitive contact of the elbows with work surfaces.
C2 Activity: Leaning or applying direct pressure on the elbow during work significantly exacerbates swelling. Resting the arm in extension and applying ice provide temporary relief. Most other arm activities are minimally affected.
C3 History: The patient's occupation involves frequent contact of the elbows with work surfaces throughout the day. Prior episodes of minor elbow inflammation occurred but resolved spontaneously with relative rest. Recent increased work demands have coincided with the current acute exacerbation.
C4 Examination: On examination, significant swelling is palpable over the olecranon with localized warmth and mild erythema. The fluid collection is non-fluctuant at this stage, suggesting early serous accumulation. Elbow flexion range of motion is mildly restricted by swelling, but passive and active extension is pain-free.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound imaging demonstrates fluid accumulation in the olecranon bursa measuring approximately 1.5 centimeters in anteroposterior dimension with echogenic particles suggestive of inflammatory material. Magnetic resonance imaging confirms serous bursitis without evidence of bursal infection, and surrounding soft tissue edema is minimal.
#219 Carpal Tunnel Syndrome Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (hand paresthesias fit multiple neuropathies), clues progressively narrow through activity triggers, neurological signs, and finally electrodiagnostic confirmation; clear progression and fair for guessing.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 47-year-old office worker presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, middle, and medial aspect of the ring finger. The symptoms are worst at night and early morning, often waking the patient from sleep, and are occasionally accompanied by mild pain in the forearm.
C2 Activity: Keyboard and mouse work throughout the day significantly aggravates symptoms, with numbness intensifying as the workday progresses. Driving and gripping activities exacerbate paresthesias acutely. Symptoms improve markedly with wrist splinting at night and frequent breaks with wrist stretching during the day.
C3 History: The patient has worked as a typist for 18 years with increasing repetitive strain on both wrists. Symptoms began three months ago and have progressively worsened despite ergonomic modifications. Recent weight gain and onset of hypothyroidism have coincided with symptom escalation.
C4 Examination: On examination, positive Phalen test sustained for 30 seconds reproduces paresthesias in the median nerve distribution. Percussion of the median nerve at the wrist (Tinel sign) provokes tingling in the fingers. Grip strength is preserved, but two-point discrimination is impaired in the index finger.
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction studies demonstrate significantly prolonged distal latency of the median nerve motor response (5.8 milliseconds) and reduced median sensory nerve conduction velocity. Ultrasound imaging reveals cross-sectional swelling of the median nerve with a cross-sectional area of 12 mm squared at the carpal tunnel inlet.
#220 Post-Total Shoulder Replacement Shoulder
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clues 1-2 essentially name the diagnosis (post-surgical pain 6 weeks after replacement for OA), leaving minimal room for guessing; the puzzle lacks diagnostic mystery and clinical reasoning engagement required for Physiodle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old retired teacher presents with shoulder pain and stiffness in the glenohumeral region. Pain is described as dull ache at rest and sharp with movement attempts. Functional limitations include difficulty with overhead activities and hand-to-mouth movements.
C2 Activity: A 68-year-old retired teacher presents with shoulder pain and stiffness. Pain is dull at rest but sharp with movement attempts. Reports difficulty with overhead activities and hand-to-mouth movements. Onset was acute, following a surgical procedure 6 weeks ago.
C3 History: Patient cannot don upper clothing or reach behind head. Pain significantly limits external rotation and abduction. Passive motion is notably better tolerated than active attempts.
C4 Examination: Recent surgical procedure 6 weeks ago for end-stage joint degeneration unresponsive to conservative management. Pre-operative imaging showed bone-on-bone articulation and severe cartilage loss. Medical history includes polyarticular osteoarthritis and controlled hypertension. No prior shoulder surgery.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative radiographs show appropriate prosthetic component positioning with good bony support. No signs of loosening, subluxation, or periprosthetic fracture. CT scan confirms stable component fixation and adequate cement mantle distribution.
#221 Hallux Rigidus Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong progression from vague forefoot pain to specific first MTP joint arthritis with characteristic imaging; each clue adds new information and a physio student would find this fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old tradesperson presents with progressive pain localized to the base of the great toe. Pain is worse during weight-bearing activities and pushing off during walking. Patient reports noticeable stiffness in this area that worsens with activity throughout the day.
C2 Activity: The patient describes difficulty with propulsion activities including running, walking on inclines, and climbing stairs. Pain and loss of motion occur with plantarflexion and extension of the great toe. Flat shoes cause increased discomfort compared to those with a rocker design.
C3 History: The patient reports a 2-year progressive course with intermittent swelling noticed after work shifts. No significant trauma, though patient recalls landing awkwardly on the foot during activity 3 years ago. Family history notable for osteoarthritis affecting other joints.
C4 Examination: On examination, dorsiflexion of the great toe measures only 15 degrees with firm end-feel; plantarflexion is preserved at 45 degrees. Palpation reveals osteophyte formation at the medial aspect of the first metatarsophalangeal joint. Passive motion elicits pain at end-range, and crepitus is noted during movement.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate marginal osteophytes at the first metatarsophalangeal joint with joint space narrowing. The articular surface shows characteristic flattening, and subchondral sclerosis is evident on both sides of the articulation.
#222 Antenatal Pelvic Girdle Pain Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from unilateral lower back/buttock pain in pregnancy to SIJ-specific tests to exclusion of structural pathology; well-constructed for the clinical context.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old woman in her third trimester presents with unilateral pain in the lower back and buttock region. Pain onset occurred approximately 8 weeks ago and has progressively worsened with advancing pregnancy. Patient describes the discomfort as sharp and aching, affecting activities and sleep quality.
C2 Activity: The patient reports pain with walking, climbing stairs, and rolling over in bed. Certain positions such as standing on one leg or crossing legs exacerbate symptoms significantly. Pain is notably worse at the end of the day and after prolonged standing or walking activities.
C3 History: This is the patient's second pregnancy; she had a complication-free first pregnancy 6 years prior. Current pregnancy is progressing normally without complications. Hormonal factors and weight gain associated with pregnancy are contributing factors. No prior history of low back or pelvic pain.
C4 Examination: On examination, the long dorsal sacroiliac ligament test reproduces pain on the affected side. Patrick's test and FABER positioning elicit discomfort in the sacroiliac region. Palpation over the sacroiliac joint produces tenderness; midline sacral tenderness is absent. Hip abductor strength is reduced due to pain inhibition.
C5 Imaging: Pelvic ultrasound shows normal fetal development with no structural abnormalities. No signs of sacroiliac joint hypermobility or inflammatory changes on imaging. Diagnosis confirmed by clinical presentation and exclusion of alternative structural pathology.
#223 Phantom Limb Pain Limbs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C2 repeats C1 nearly verbatim, C3 and C4 both contain identical amputation history information, and C5 imaging findings contradict phantom pain diagnosis (neuromas are actually expected); severe structural redundancy and incoherence.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old manual laborer who underwent amputation 8 months ago presents with pain sensation in the absent limb. The patient describes burning, cramping, and shooting sensations in the amputated region. Pain occurs spontaneously and is not influenced by pressure on the residual limb.
C2 Activity: A 45-year-old manual laborer presents with pain in a region of his body that no longer exists. He describes burning, cramping, and shooting sensations that arise spontaneously without clear external trigger. The pain is not relieved by pressing or manipulating the area where he feels it.
C3 History: Amputation was secondary to traumatic crush injury sustained in occupational accident with subsequent infection. Initial post-operative pain was well-controlled, but phantom pain emerged gradually over weeks following healing. No prior peripheral nerve injuries or chronic pain conditions.
C4 Examination: Amputation followed a crush injury with subsequent infection. Initial post-operative pain resolved within weeks, but then a different pain syndrome emerged and has persisted. No prior history of peripheral nerve injury or chronic pain conditions.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the residual limb shows well-healed amputation site with normal bone formation and scar tissue maturation. No evidence of neuroma formation on high-resolution ultrasound or MRI. Stump shows normal vascularization without arterial insufficiency on vascular imaging.
#224 Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome Chest
✗ REWORK
Verdict: ARDS is an ICU/critical care condition outside typical physiotherapy scope and requires intensive medical/ventilation management rather than rehab diagnosis; inappropriate for Physiodle game context.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 58-year-old admitted to ICU 3 days ago with acute-onset severe breathlessness and low blood oxygen levels. Deteriorated rapidly over 24-48 hours despite starting antibiotics. Now requires mechanical ventilation.
C2 Activity: Even small positional changes (head of bed adjustments) or brief ventilator weaning attempts trigger rapid oxygen desaturation and respiratory distress. Oxygen requirements remain high despite maximum support settings.
C3 History: The patient developed sepsis secondary to community-acquired pneumonia 5 days prior to ICU admission. Medical history includes chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and diabetes. Inflammatory markers are markedly elevated with worsening clinical trajectory despite antibiotic therapy.
C4 Examination: Bilateral fine crackles throughout lung fields. Markedly reduced chest wall compliance requiring high ventilatory pressures. Refractory hypoxemia: PaO2 remains 60-70 mmHg despite FiO2 >0.6 and appropriate PEEP titration. No cardiac murmurs or signs of heart failure on examination.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray: bilateral diffuse infiltrates with ground-glass pattern and consolidation. CT confirms extensive bilateral opacities sparing dorsal-dependent zones. Cardiac silhouette normal; BNP negative. Imaging findings consistent with acute diffuse alveolar damage.
#225 Proximal Tibiofibular Joint Instability Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Good progression from lateral knee instability with activity to specific fibular head subluxation on examination to imaging confirmation of ligament disruption; fair difficulty and coherent story.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old recreational athlete presents with episodes of lateral knee instability and giving way during activity. Pain is localized below the lateral knee line and occurs unpredictably with certain movements, causing apprehension.
C2 Activity: The patient reports symptoms with pivoting movements, lateral bounding, and activities requiring rapid direction changes. Pain and instability are worse with running on uneven surfaces. Single-leg standing and jumping activities trigger episodes of giving way or shifting sensation.
C3 History: The patient sustained a direct blow to the lateral knee during contact sport 2 years ago. Initial swelling resolved, but persistent lateral instability began after return to activity. No prior knee injuries or ligamentous laxity in other joints. Athlete reports lack of confidence when changing direction during play.
C4 Examination: On examination, the anterior drawer test of the fibula is positive with subluxation noted. Palpation reveals prominence of the fibular head with reproduction of symptoms. The fibular head glides anteriorly with passive knee flexion. Lateral collateral ligament testing is intact; anterolateral rotatory instability is not demonstrated.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates disruption of the anterior and posterior tibiofibular ligaments consistent with chronic instability. Ultrasound shows fibular head translation of 8mm compared to the contralateral side.
#226 Achilles Tendon Rupture Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent progression from sudden push-off injury to complete functional loss to pathognomonic Thompson test to imaging confirmation; straightforward but educationally valuable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old recreational badminton player reports sudden sharp pain in the posterior ankle during a push-off movement. Patient immediately fell and was unable to bear weight. Rapid swelling developed over the following hours.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot walk without assistance. Plantarflexion against gravity is completely absent. Attempts to stand on toes are impossible. Passive dorsiflexion stretch produces sharp pain in posterior ankle.
C3 History: Patient had increased training volume over 4 weeks prior to injury. No prior ankle injuries, chronic ankle pain, or Achilles complaints. No medical comorbidities.
C4 Examination: Palpable gap in Achilles tendon approximately 5 cm proximal to insertion. Thompson test positive: manual calf squeeze produces no ankle plantarflexion. Swelling and ecchymosis present posterior ankle.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows complete discontinuity of the Achilles tendon with 2 cm separation between tendon ends. MRI demonstrates full-thickness tear at the musculotendinous junction with edema and hemorrhage in surrounding tissues. Sagittal imaging clearly delineates the gap between ruptured segments.
#227 Cancer Rehabilitation General
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C2 essentially repeats C1, C3 lists activity limitations that belong in C2, C4 and C5 mix examination findings with history/imaging, and the clinical narrative is disorganized making it difficult to follow the diagnostic progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 56-year-old woman 2 months post-chemotherapy for breast cancer presents with generalized fatigue, weakness, and difficulty with functional mobility. She reports persistent pain in the surgical scar region and the ipsilateral upper extremity. Functional capacity has declined significantly since treatment onset.
C2 Activity: A 56-year-old woman presents 2 months after completing cancer treatment with progressive fatigue, generalised weakness, and reduced functional capacity. She reports pain in a surgical scar region and the ipsilateral upper extremity.
C3 History: Patient cannot perform overhead activities or lifting with affected arm. Walking tolerance has declined markedly to 10 minutes. Stairs precipitate significant fatigue and she requires frequent rest breaks throughout the day.
C4 Examination: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy completed 8 weeks ago, followed by surgical reconstruction. Post-operative radiation therapy completed 4 weeks prior. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy developed in bilateral lower legs.
C5 Imaging: Shoulder abduction limited to 85 degrees due to pain and tissue tightness. Axillary cording evident with palpable restriction and pain. Grip strength asymmetry: 18 kg affected vs 28 kg unaffected side. Ankle dorsiflexion strength 4/5 bilaterally with glove-like sensory loss in feet.
#228 Concussion Brain
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from acute head impact with amnesia to cognitive/vestibular symptoms to objective balance/oculomotor deficits to normal imaging; appropriate for sports physio context.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An 18-year-old soccer player was struck in the head by another player's elbow during a match 5 days ago. The athlete reports headache, dizziness, and sensitivity to light and noise. Memory of the incident and the preceding 10 minutes is absent.
C2 Activity: The patient experiences worsening of symptoms with cognitive tasks and physical exertion. Reading, concentrating on schoolwork, and typing increase headache severity. Nausea occurs with rapid head movements. Balance appears impaired when eyes are closed or on uneven surfaces.
C3 History: No prior head injuries. The incident occurred during competitive play; witness noted 20–30 seconds of apparent confusion and disorientation immediately after impact, but no documented unconsciousness. Symptoms have persisted unchanged over 5 days despite rest; athlete was not permitted to continue playing. No vomiting, seizure, or loss of bowel/bladder control reported.
C4 Examination: On examination, the King-Devick test shows slowed reading speed and tracking difficulties. Tandem stance test demonstrates mild balance impairment with 1-2 foot touches required. Rapid alternating movements of hands are slightly uncoordinated bilaterally. Pupil reactivity is normal; cranial nerve examination is intact.
C5 Imaging: CT imaging of the head is normal with no acute intracranial abnormality, hemorrhage, or fracture identified. Baseline neuroimaging prior to injury is not available for comparison. Routine imaging does not show diffuse axonal injury on conventional sequences.
#229 Spondylolysis Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong progression from unilateral lower back pain with hyperextension in young athlete to positive single-leg hyperextension test to imaging demonstrating pars defect; well-sequenced with appropriate clinical context.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 16-year-old competitive swimmer presents with insidious-onset lower back pain localized to one side of the midline. Pain worsens with backward arching or hyperextension movements. The pain is sharp and increases with high-intensity training or sprinting activities.
C2 Activity: The patient experiences pain with butterfly stroke and backstroke more than freestyle. Pain intensifies with training progression and return to competitive intensity. Prolonged sitting and forward bending are tolerated without significant symptoms. Pain is worst at end-of-day after training.
C3 History: The patient has trained competitively for 5 years with high training volume and repetitive spinal extension loading. Pain onset occurred 8 weeks ago coinciding with increased training intensity. No traumatic event or acute injury. Family history is notable for low back pain in both parents.
C4 Examination: On examination, unilateral lumbar pain is reproduced with single-leg hyperextension test on the affected side. Lumbar extension combined with rotation toward the painful side increases symptoms. Palpation reveals paraspinal muscle tenderness at the level of pain. Lower extremity neurological examination is normal.
C5 Imaging: CT scan demonstrates a unilateral pars interarticularis defect at the L5 level with intact vertebral body and disc. SPECT imaging shows increased uptake at the symptomatic level, indicating active bone stress. Plain radiographs may appear normal; advanced imaging is required for definitive visualization.

Verdict: C2 repeats C1 nearly verbatim, C4 and C5 both contain identical palpation findings and plantarflexion reproduction information with significant overlap; severely redundant with poor clue progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old runner presents with plantar forefoot pain localized under the great toe ball of the foot. Pain has developed gradually over 6 weeks and is worsened by running and prolonged standing. The patient describes the discomfort as aching and tenderness in the area.
C2 Activity: A 28-year-old runner presents with gradual-onset pain in the forefoot that has worsened over 6 weeks. Pain is described as aching discomfort under the ball of the foot and is triggered by running and prolonged standing.
C3 History: Pain is reproducible with activities requiring forefoot propulsion: push-off during running acceleration and sprinting provoke symptoms. Single-leg plantarflexion (rising on toes) causes significant discomfort. Walking in supportive footwear is tolerated.
C4 Examination: On examination, palpation over the medial sesamoid bone reproduces pain and tenderness. Pain is specifically localized to the sesamoid region with surrounding plantar tissue examination unremarkable. Plantarflexion strength of the first metatarsophalangeal joint is reduced due to pain. Single-leg plantarflexion or rising on toes reproduces symptoms.
C5 Imaging: Palpation directly over the medial sesamoid elicits sharp tenderness and pain reproduction. Pain is strictly localized to the sesamoid region with no surrounding plantar tissue involvement. First metatarsophalangeal plantarflexion strength is diminished due to pain inhibition.
#231 Cervical Spinal Cord Injury Cervical Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is far too specific and descriptive (traumatic injury, bilateral weakness, surgical intervention)—only cervical spinal cord injury fits, leaving no room for meaningful guessing across 5 clues.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old construction worker sustained a traumatic injury 2 weeks ago with immediate onset of bilateral weakness and numbness. The patient reports loss of sensation and motor control affecting both arms and legs. Emergency hospitalization and surgical intervention were required.
C2 Activity: The patient cannot walk, stand, or sit without external support. Hand grip is absent bilaterally. Respiratory function varies with body position and trunk stability.
C3 History: Imaging at time of injury revealed spinal cord contusion and edema without complete transection. The patient was previously employed in heavy manual labor with normal neurological function prior to the incident.
C4 Examination: Motor testing reveals paralysis in bilateral hands and lower extremities, with preserved but weak shoulder and elbow function (2-3/5). Sensory loss is complete below C6 level. Lower extremity reflexes are brisk and hyperreflexic, suggesting spasticity.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows spinal cord contusion at C5-C6 levels with associated edema and hemorrhage within the cord. CT imaging demonstrates fracture-dislocation of the cervical vertebrae with posterior ligamentous disruption. Post-stabilization imaging confirms appropriate alignment and decompression of spinal canal.
#232 Autism Spectrum Disorder Whole Body
✗ REWORK
Verdict: ASD is a neurodevelopmental/psychiatric diagnosis, not primarily a physiotherapy condition; the puzzle conflates motor coordination issues with autism itself, making it pedagogically misaligned for a physio game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An 8-year-old child with parents reporting clumsiness in everyday activities and difficulty keeping up with peers during movement-based games. Onset in early childhood. Parents also note the child becomes distressed in busy, loud environments.
C2 Activity: Cannot tolerate unstructured group activities or busy social settings. Avoids participation in team sports and group games. Strength and basic motor control are intact, but coordination during complex or bilateral tasks is visibly impaired.
C3 History: Symptoms began in early childhood and persist across home and school. Parents report difficulty with transitions and changes to routine. Some difficulty with understanding social cues and nonverbal communication, though formal language development was age-appropriate.
C4 Examination: Gross motor coordination testing reveals mild dyspraxia particularly with bilateral coordinated movements and motor sequencing tasks. Fine motor strength is intact. Gait pattern is within normal limits.
C5 Imaging: Standardized neuropsychological assessment demonstrates deficits consistent with neurodevelopmental disorder affecting motor planning and social-communicative function. Brain MRI structurally normal without white matter abnormalities. EEG unremarkable.
#233 Vertigo - Age-Related Vestibular System
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (vertigo/dizziness has many causes), Dix-Hallpike in C4 is classic BPPV, and imaging rules out serious pathology while confirming benign etiology—solid progression for a physio student.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old retired teacher reports recent onset of spinning sensation when changing positions in bed or bending to pick up objects from the floor. Episodes last seconds to minutes and are accompanied by mild nausea. No hearing loss or tinnitus is noted.
C2 Activity: The patient avoids rapid head movements and reports difficulty with balance confidence when turning the head quickly. Morning routines are affected as dizziness occurs when lying down or rolling over. Walking is cautious but functional without assistive device.
C3 History: Recent head trauma is denied. The patient reports mild diabetes and hypertension managed with medications. Multiple falls in the past year have increased safety concerns. No prior episodes of vertigo are documented.
C4 Examination: Dix-Hallpike maneuver reproduces brief rotatory nystagmus with 2-3 second latency. Head impulse test normal bilaterally. Gait base is narrow with mild weight transfer asymmetry. Romberg test elicits mild sway.
C5 Imaging: CT temporal bones shows normal semicircular canal anatomy without canalith debris or hydrops. MRI excludes retrocochlear mass, demyelination, and central pathology. Findings consistent with age-related otolith dysfunction.
#234 Lymphoedema Limbs
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (lower leg swelling fits DVT, venous insufficiency, lymphedema), each clue adds specificity (pitting vs. non-pitting, cellulitis history, lymphoscintigraphy findings), and the progression is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old with long-standing lower leg swelling affecting the left lower extremity. Reports progressive discomfort and functional decline with difficulty walking and standing for prolonged periods.
C2 Activity: Avoids prolonged standing or walking; symptoms worsen throughout the day. Difficulty with stair climbing and footwear selection due to ankle swelling. Night-time elevation partially relieves symptoms. Recent episode of severe swelling after long airplane flight.
C3 History: History of left lower limb deep vein thrombosis 8 years prior, treated with anticoagulation. Persistent leg swelling despite treatment completion. Multiple episodes of cellulitis requiring antibiotics over the past 3 years. Obesity and sedentary lifestyle. No previous limb trauma or cancer history.
C4 Examination: Left lower leg pitting oedema from midthigh to ankle. Skin appears thickened and dimpled in places; subcutaneous tissues feel firm and non-compressible on deep palpation. No acute erythema or warmth. Mild restriction in ankle dorsiflexion. Calf circumference asymmetry 4 cm compared to right side.
C5 Imaging: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates severely delayed radiolabelled tracer clearance from the left lower limb with evidence of collateral lymphatic drainage pathways and dermal backflow. No significant deep venous obstruction on repeat compression ultrasound.
#235 Deconditioning Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 covers multiple cardiopulmonary/systemic conditions, C2-C4 progressively narrow via activity tolerance and fitness metrics, and C5 definitively confirms deconditioning via VO₂ max and muscle atrophy—fair and clinically realistic.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old office worker with a sedentary occupation reports generalized fatigue and difficulty with sustained activity. Breathing becomes labored after climbing a single flight of stairs. No acute illness precedes these symptoms, which developed gradually over several months.
C2 Activity: Previously independent with household tasks, the patient now requires frequent rest periods. Walking distance has progressively decreased to approximately two blocks. Cardiac symptoms are denied, but dyspnea is proportional to exertion intensity.
C3 History: The patient experienced prolonged illness requiring hospitalization and bed rest six months ago. Since discharge, activity levels have remained low despite medical clearance to resume normal function. No new cardiac or pulmonary diagnoses have been established.
C4 Examination: Resting heart rate is elevated at 92 bpm, with blood pressure 118/76 mmHg. Six-minute walk test demonstrates distance of 280 meters with dyspnea rating of 4/10. Lower extremity strength is 4/5 throughout major muscle groups. Cardiopulmonary auscultation is normal.
C5 Imaging: Resting energy expenditure by indirect calorimetry is 15% below predicted for age and body composition. Maximal oxygen uptake (VO₂ max) estimated at 18 mL/kg/min, consistent with 'poor' cardiovascular fitness category for age.
