Skeletal muscle cross-sectional area reduced by 12% compared to age-matched normative data on thigh MRI.
#236 Accessory Nerve Palsy Neck/Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad for nerve palsies, but C4 mentioning upper trapezius atrophy plus 'medial scapular winging' is somewhat redundant; otherwise progression is solid and C5 with EMG/MRI is appropriately definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old manual laborer reports progressive shoulder weakness and drooping appearance of the shoulder over four weeks. Shoulder elevation is effortful and shoulder shrugging is noticeably asymmetrical. No acute trauma or neck pain is reported.
C2 Activity: The patient reports difficulty with overhead work tasks and carrying loads on the affected shoulder. Shrugging against resistance feels weak compared to the contralateral side. Neck lateral flexion and rotation are unaffected functionally.
C3 History: Symptom onset was insidious over four weeks without preceding neck trauma, surgery, or focal nerve injury event. No family history of neuromuscular disease. Patient works as manual laborer with repetitive overhead demands.
C4 Examination: On examination, shoulder shrug strength is 2/5 bilaterally asymmetrical with evident atrophy of the upper trapezius on the affected side. Scapular positioning demonstrates medial winging. Neck flexion and lateral flexion strength are 5/5 bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Electromyography shows denervation potentials in the trapezius muscle with reduced motor unit recruitment. MRI of the neck demonstrates normal spinal canal and nerve root foramina. No mass lesions are identified along the accessory nerve course.
#237 Upper Crossed Syndrome Upper Body
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits postural neck pain broadly, C2-C3 narrow via desk work and forward head posture, C4 provides clinical muscle weakness pattern, and C5 with imaging of lordosis loss and scapular dyskinesis clinches diagnosis—coherent and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old office manager presents with insidious onset of posterior neck and upper back pain that worsens during the workday. Headaches originating from the base of the skull are frequent. Symptoms are not associated with trauma or recent illness.
C2 Activity: The patient reports significant limitation with sustained computer work and avoids overhead activities. Forward head posture is evident during daily tasks and contributes to symptom exacerbation. Postural correction temporarily eases discomfort.
C3 History: The patient has a ten-year history of desk-based work without ergonomic modifications. Recent job transition to new workspace increased postural demands. No prior neurological symptoms or imaging are documented. Sleep quality has diminished due to nighttime neck stiffness.
C4 Examination: On examination, cervical extension is limited to 35 degrees (normal 50 degrees) with reproduction of upper back pain. Scapular positioning demonstrates bilateral elevation and protraction relative to the thorax. Manual muscle testing of lower trapezius and serratus anterior shows weakness (4/5). Upper limb neurological testing is normal.
C5 Imaging: Cervical spine X-rays demonstrate loss of cervical lordosis with anterior translation of the head relative to C7. MRI shows no neural compromise. Soft tissue imaging reveals marked bilateral upper trapezius hypertrophy with corresponding atrophy of lower trapezius and serratus anterior, consistent with scapular dyskinesis.
#238 Radial Tunnel Syndrome Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 could fit multiple nerve compression syndromes, C2-C4 progressively isolate posterior interosseous nerve via resisted supination and radial tunnel tenderness, and C5 with nerve compression on ultrasound is clinically appropriate—solid guess progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old carpenter reports progressive lateral forearm pain that is worse with gripping and sustained pinching activities. Pain is localized to the proximal forearm and does not extend into the hand. Symptom onset was gradual without documented injury.
C2 Activity: The patient reports difficulty with sustained tool use and reports pain increases with repetitive supination and pronation movements. Gripping strength is significantly diminished compared to the contralateral side. Light activity such as writing causes mild discomfort.
C3 History: Occupational repetitive strain history is significant with decades of carpentry work requiring pronation and gripping. Symptoms began insidiously two months ago. No cervical radiculopathy history or neurological dysfunction is reported. No previous forearm injuries have occurred.
C4 Examination: Resisted supination reproduces lateral forearm pain. Resisted wrist extension is weak at 4/5 with lateral forearm reproduction. Palpation over the radial tunnel elicits point tenderness. Two-point discrimination and sensation are normal in the dorsal first web space.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the forearm demonstrates mild edema in the extensor carpi radialis muscle near the radial tunnel. High-resolution ultrasound shows compression of the posterior interosseous nerve with soft tissue impingement. No fractures or significant mass lesions are identified.
#239 Latissimus Dorsi Strain Back/Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple shoulder/scapular injuries, C2-C3 narrow via swimming mechanics and training load, C4 isolates latissimus via resisted adduction/internal rotation and posterior tenderness, and C5 confirms muscle edema/microtearing—fair and well-structured.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old recreational swimmer reports acute onset of sharp posterior shoulder pain during a training session. Pain is located in the lower scapular region and radiates laterally toward the mid-arm. No arm numbness or tingling is experienced.
C2 Activity: The patient reports pain increases significantly with arm pulling movements and rowing-like activities. Overhead activities provoke sharp symptoms, whereas resting the arm in the lap is comfortable. Swimming strokes that emphasize pull-through phase are most symptomatic.
C3 History: Injury occurred during sprint set in week 6 of periodized training block after progressive volume build. Patient denies prior shoulder injuries. No relevant comorbidities or inflammatory history. Training load increased 40% over preceding 4 weeks.
C4 Examination: Resisted shoulder adduction and internal rotation provoke sharp pain. Palpation over posterolateral shoulder at mid-scapular border is exquisitely tender. Scapular dyskinesis noted during arm elevation. Forward flexion limited to 120° due to muscle guarding; extension restricted to 20°.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates hyperintense signal within muscle belly at the junction of middle and lower thirds, consistent with edema and microtearing. No complete disruption of muscle-tendon continuity. Supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and labral structures are intact. No subacromial impingement.
#240 Cystic Fibrosis Lungs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 essentially names cystic fibrosis (genetic disorder, chronic productive cough, early-childhood onset with recurrent infections)—too specific; CF is also primarily a medical/genetic condition, not a physio diagnosis like the others.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 22-year-old with chronic productive cough and progressive dyspnea on minimal exertion. Symptoms began in early childhood. Reports recurrent chest infections and persistent sputum production despite frequent clearance attempts.
C2 Activity: Severely limited walking distance (100–200 m before requiring rest). Unable to sustain any aerobic activity. Requires multiple daily airway clearance sessions with percussion/oscillatory devices to manage sputum. Functional capacity declining year-on-year.
C3 History: Known genetic disorder diagnosed via newborn screening. Two or more respiratory hospitalizations in past 2 years. History of recurrent sinusitis and nasal polyps requiring surgical intervention. Concurrent glucose intolerance managed with dietary modification. No smoking exposure.
C4 Examination: Lungs demonstrate bilateral crackles and decreased air entry at lung bases. Six-minute walk test completed 280 meters with desaturation to 84%. Thoracic kyphosis with barrel chest appearance. Productive cough with thick, greenish sputum.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with increased bronchovascular markings and focal areas of bronchiectasis. CT confirms diffuse bronchial wall thickening and bronchus-to-artery ratio exceeding 1.5.
#241 Developmental Coordination Disorder Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (covers multiple developmental/motor conditions), clues progress logically from complaint to standardized assessment, and Clue 5 clinches with percentile scores and normal imaging ruling out structural pathology.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 6-year-old child presents with parental concerns about apparent clumsiness and difficulty with age-appropriate motor tasks. Teachers report the child drops objects frequently and moves awkwardly during class activities. Gross motor skills appear noticeably behind peers.
C2 Activity: The child demonstrates difficulty with bilateral coordinated movement tasks such as skipping or riding a bicycle. Handwriting is immature for age with poor pencil grip and inconsistent letter formation. Ball-catching and throwing demonstrate poor timing and coordination.
C3 History: Developmental milestones were met but with noted awkwardness in acquisition of motor skills. Early childhood gross motor development appeared slower than siblings. No history of neurological disease, cerebral palsy, or intellectual disability is documented. Symptoms have been lifelong and persistent.
C4 Examination: On examination, rapid alternating movements are slow and irregular bilaterally. Tandem stance reveals significant loss of balance. Gait assessment shows asymmetrical arm swing with trunk instability. Strength and deep tendon reflexes are normal throughout.
C5 Imaging: Movement ABC-2 testing shows motor proficiency score at the 3rd percentile. Standardized assessment of coordination, fine motor control, and balance all fall below expected range for age. Brain MRI is structurally unremarkable.
#242 Clavicle Fracture Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from acute shoulder girdle pain to visible deformity, point tenderness, and definitive imaging; the clinical story is coherent and a physio student would find it straightforward and fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old cyclist reports acute onset of severe pain in the shoulder girdle region following a fall. Visible deformity is present over the anterior shoulder. Shoulder movement is severely limited and guarded.
C2 Activity: The patient cannot bear weight through the upper extremity or support the arm without severe pain. Horizontal adduction across the chest reproduces sharp pain at the injury site.
C3 History: The injury occurred from direct impact to the anterior shoulder striking a curb. Symptom onset was immediate with rapid swelling. No prior shoulder girdle injuries or surgery.
C4 Examination: Palpation elicits sharp point tenderness over the injury site with a palpable step deformity. The clavicle appears shortened. Neurovascular examination is intact with normal distal pulses and sensation.
C5 Imaging: AP chest X-ray demonstrates a fracture line through the lateral clavicle with superior displacement and angulation of the fragments. Associated soft tissue swelling is present.
#243 Vertebral Compression Fracture Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong clinical narrative linking osteoporosis risk to acute thoracic pain with specific examination findings and imaging confirmation; clues build logically without redundancy.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old woman with a known history of osteoporosis presents with acute onset of severe midline thoracic back pain. Pain began without clear precipitant and is constant in nature, not relieved by positional changes.
C2 Activity: Forward bending sharply exacerbates pain at the injury site. The patient can tolerate standing and walking for only brief periods before pain escalates significantly.
C3 History: The patient has a 15-year history of diagnosed osteoporosis with multiple medication-treated risk factors. Recent history includes a minor fall from standing height that may have triggered onset. No direct significant trauma is reported, yet symptoms began acutely.
C4 Examination: On examination, palpation over the mid-thoracic vertebrae elicits severe point tenderness. Thoracic flexion is markedly limited to approximately 20 degrees. Neurological examination is intact with normal lower extremity strength and sensation throughout.
C5 Imaging: Thoracic spine X-rays demonstrate acute wedge-shaped compression deformity of T7 vertebral body with 30 percent height loss. MRI shows acute signal change on STIR imaging consistent with recent compression fracture. Posterior vertebral body line remains intact without retropulsion.
#244 Sacral Stress Fracture Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple running-related injuries, subsequent clues introduce female athlete triad context and stress fracture specificity, and Clue 5 with MRI/CT definitively confirms diagnosis via imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old distance runner reports progressive deep buttock pain that worsens with running and sitting. Pain is located in the sacral region and does not radiate into the lower extremity. Symptoms developed insidiously over eight weeks of increased training volume.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot tolerate any high-impact activity. Running and jumping are completely prohibited. Interestingly, pain does not ease with typical rest and NSAIDs despite reduced activity, suggesting a slower-healing pathology.
C3 History: Training volume increased by 40 percent over the past three months to prepare for a marathon. The patient reports a female athlete triad history with irregular menses and low bone density screening concerns. Previous stress fractures in the femoral neck occurred two years ago.
C4 Examination: On examination, palpation over the sacral ala reproduces localized pain. Single-leg stance on the contralateral leg reproduces symptoms on the affected side. Hip strength testing reveals weakness in hip abductors at 4/5. Neurological examination is completely normal.
C5 Imaging: Initial X-rays show no obvious fracture line. MRI demonstrates a linear fracture line through the sacral ala with surrounding bone marrow edema extending into the sacral body. CT imaging confirms fracture orientation consistent with stress mechanism. Imaging shows no displacement.
#245 Sprengel's Deformity Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 5 is somewhat confusing—it blends examination findings (palpation, scapular winging) with normal strength, when imaging should clarify the congenital scapular malposition more directly.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by parents who report noting asymmetry in shoulder positioning. The right shoulder appears elevated and displaced medially compared to the left. No complaints of pain are reported by the child.
C2 Activity: The child participates in sports with minimal limitation but demonstrates restricted overhead shoulder mobility on the affected side. Scapular positioning is visibly asymmetrical during functional tasks. Swimming and throwing activities demonstrate compensatory patterns.
C3 History: Despite minimal functional limitation, the child reports difficulty reaching overhead on the affected side and avoids butterfly strokes in swimming due to restricted shoulder abduction.
C4 Examination: On examination, the affected scapula is positioned medially and superiorly relative to the contralateral side. The inferior angle is displaced upward and medially. ROM testing demonstrates limited shoulder abduction and external rotation. Strength testing is normal throughout.
C5 Imaging: Palpation reveals the scapula is abnormally small and lacks the normal inferior angle prominence. Scapular winging is absent. Clavicle and upper trapezius are tethered in an elevated position. Strength testing is intact throughout, including serratus anterior and lower trapezius.
#246 Rheumatoid Arthritis Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately broad (multiple symmetrical joint conditions), progressive narrowing through symmetrical findings and morning stiffness, culminating in inflammatory imaging findings that confirm diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old female presents with insidious onset of symmetrical hand and wrist pain, worse in the mornings. She reports significant stiffness affecting grip strength and daily activities, with swelling in multiple joints affecting function.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning clothes and opening jars due to joint pain and reduced range of motion. Morning stiffness lasts over 60 minutes, improving only after activity and warm showers. Fatigue is a limiting factor in her functional capacity.
C3 History: Six-month progressive course with no recent trauma or infection preceding symptoms. Family history significant for autoimmune disease. Mild systemic symptoms including malaise and low-grade fevers accompanying the articular complaints.
C4 Examination: On examination, symmetrical swelling and warmth noted in metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Grip strength reduced at 15 kg bilaterally (normal >25 kg). Positive squeeze test of metacarpal heads elicits pain. Range of motion limited in wrist extension and flexion.
C5 Imaging: Hand X-rays reveal periarticular soft tissue swelling, juxta-articular osteopenia, and early marginal erosions at metacarpophalangeal joints. Ultrasound confirms synovial hypertrophy with increased vascularity on power Doppler, consistent with active inflammatory synovitis.
#247 Bronchiectasis Lungs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Bronchiectasis is outside the scope of a physiotherapy-focused diagnosis game; while respiratory physiotherapy exists, this condition is primarily a respiratory medicine diagnosis and lacks physiotherapy-specific clinical reasoning content.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old male smoker presents with chronic productive cough and dyspnea, worse over the past 2 years. He reports recurrent respiratory infections requiring multiple courses of antibiotics and describes expectoration of purulent sputum daily.
C2 Activity: Limited exercise tolerance; becomes breathless with moderate activity such as climbing stairs or brisk walking. Activities requiring sustained breathing lead to increased cough and sputum production. He tires easily during conversation and requires frequent rest periods.
C3 History: Significant 30-year smoking history. Recurrent lower respiratory infections since childhood with slow recovery after pneumonia episodes. Currently managed with bronchodilators and mucolytics but no significant hemoptysis reported. History of structural lung changes not yet evident clinically.
C4 Examination: On auscultation, bilateral coarse crackles heard predominantly in basilar lung fields that do not clear with coughing. Prolonged expiration noted. Respiratory rate elevated at 24 breaths/min at rest. Oxygen saturation 92% on room air, improving only slightly with exertion tolerance.
C5 Imaging: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates bronchial wall thickening with bronchus-to-artery ratio >1, indicating bronchial dilation. Bronchial pooling of contrast and traction bronchiectasis visible in lower lobes bilaterally. Bronchial walls remain thick without normal bronchial tapering.
#248 Antenatal Pelvic Girdle Pain Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits broader pregnancy-related pain, clues logically introduce trimester timing and movement provocations, and Clue 5 excludes structural pathology; clinically sound and educationally fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old woman in her third trimester presents with unilateral pain in the lower back and buttock region. Pain onset occurred approximately 8 weeks ago and has progressively worsened with advancing pregnancy. Patient describes the discomfort as sharp and aching, affecting activities and sleep quality.
C2 Activity: The patient reports pain with walking, climbing stairs, and rolling over in bed. Certain positions such as standing on one leg or crossing legs exacerbate symptoms significantly. Pain is notably worse at the end of the day and after prolonged standing or walking activities.
C3 History: This is the patient's second pregnancy; she had a complication-free first pregnancy 6 years prior. Current pregnancy is progressing normally without complications. Hormonal factors and weight gain associated with pregnancy are contributing factors. No prior history of low back or pelvic pain.
C4 Examination: On examination, the long dorsal sacroiliac ligament test reproduces pain on the affected side. Patrick's test and FABER positioning elicit discomfort in the sacroiliac region. Palpation over the sacroiliac joint produces tenderness; midline sacral tenderness is absent. Hip abductor strength is reduced due to pain inhibition.
C5 Imaging: Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates normal fetal development with appropriate amniotic fluid volume. No structural abnormalities are noted in pelvic anatomy. MRI, if indicated, would show no degenerative changes or inflammatory findings in sacroiliac joints or supporting structures.
#249 Quadriceps Contusion Thigh
✓ READY
Verdict: Direct impact mechanism in Clue 1 fits multiple thigh injuries, subsequent clues add temporal progression and compartment syndrome considerations, and ultrasound imaging in Clue 5 definitively confirms haematoma.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 19-year-old collegiate soccer player reports sudden-onset anterior thigh pain, swelling, and bruising following a direct impact during play. Immediate functional impairment noted.
C2 Activity: Unable to bear weight on the affected limb; pain is reproducible with passive knee flexion and restricts walking distance to a few metres. Limb feels swollen and tense.
C3 History: Single high-energy impact mechanism from opponent's knee strike. No previous thigh injuries or compartment syndrome history. Injury occurred 5 days ago; progressive swelling and bruising noted despite ice and compression application. No anticoagulation therapy.
C4 Examination: Firm, tender swelling palpable over anteromedial quadriceps extending proximally. Passive knee flexion markedly painful at end-range (60°). Quadriceps strength 3/5 with pain-guarding pattern. Compartments firm but no clinical signs of compartment syndrome.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound reveals well-defined hypoechoic collection within quadriceps musculature consistent with intramuscular haematoma, measuring 5 cm x 3 cm. Muscle fibers partially disrupted with surrounding edema. No evidence of compartment syndrome on clinical correlation, though compartments appear tense.
#250 Postnatal Pelvic Floor Dysfunction Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately fits multiple pelvic conditions, clues narrow through obstetric history and stress incontinence specificity, and Clue 5 confirms with dynamic ultrasound showing urethral insufficiency.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old female reports involuntary urinary leakage triggered by coughing, sneezing, and exertion. Symptoms began 6 months ago and have persisted despite conservative management.
C2 Activity: Patient avoids high-impact activities and reports difficulty with running, jumping, and childcare activities involving movement. Reports pain during intercourse and reduced sexual enjoyment secondary to symptoms and self-consciousness.
C3 History: Uncomplicated vaginal delivery with episiotomy; labour duration 8 hours with instrumental assistance. No significant perineal trauma documented at delivery. Previous pregnancies without pelvic floor symptoms. Reports no pelvic pain but significant functional and psychological impact from symptoms.
C4 Examination: On pelvic floor assessment, palpable weakness and reduced tone noted in pelvic floor musculature. Voluntary contraction strength graded at 3/5 on manual assessment. Positive cough stress test with demonstrable urine loss. Pelvic organ support appears normal on visual inspection without prolapse.
C5 Imaging: Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates normal bladder neck position at rest; however, dynamic ultrasound during cough shows insufficient urethral mobility and descent, with inadequate urethrovesical angle closure. No significant prolapse noted. Pelvic floor muscles show reduced echogenicity consistent with neuromuscular dysfunction.
#251 Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease Cardiopulmonary
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C2 and C4 are nearly identical (both repeat smoking history, infections, weight loss), creating no progression; clues should advance the diagnostic reasoning, not recycle information.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old male smoker with 40-year pack history reports progressive dyspnea and chronic cough productive of clear to whitish sputum. Symptoms have worsened over the past year, limiting his daily activities.
C2 Activity: A 68-year-old male with a significant smoking history reports gradually worsening shortness of breath and a persistent productive cough over the past 12 months. He describes a regular pattern of coughing, particularly in the mornings. Daily activities are becoming increasingly difficult.
C3 History: Forty-year smoking history continues despite multiple cessation attempts. Recurrent respiratory infections managed with courses of antibiotics. History of occupational exposure to dust. No significant cardiac history. Recently noted weight loss and reduced appetite.
C4 Examination: Long smoking history with continued active smoking despite cessation attempts. Recurrent chest infections managed with antibiotics over recent years. Previous occupational dust exposure. No known cardiac disease. Recent unintentional weight loss noted.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms and increased anteroposterior diameter. Peripheral bronchial wall thickening visible. HRCT demonstrates centrilobular emphysema predominantly in upper lobes with reduced lung parenchyma. Small airways disease pattern evident.
#252 Cervicogenic Headache Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is reasonably broad (office-based headaches fit multiple conditions), each clue adds specific information (posture→ROM restriction→imaging findings), and C5 clinches with multilevel spondylotic changes correlating to referred pain pattern.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old female reports unilateral headaches that began after starting a new office-based role. Pain worsens with certain neck movements and sustained postures. Analgesic medications provide minimal relief.
C2 Activity: Symptoms worsen during prolonged computer work and morning stiffness. Patient reports difficulty with work tasks requiring sustained posture or repeated neck turns to the right. Symptoms partially resolve with periodic breaks and postural changes.
C3 History: Six-month history of recurrent episodes following increased screen time and poor ergonomics. No history of head trauma, though she notes chronic forward head posture. No photophobia or phonophobia. Pain began after sustained office work transitions.
C4 Examination: On cervical examination, restricted right cervical rotation (60 degrees; normal >80 degrees) and extension provokes familiar headache. Positive cervical flexion-rotation test reproducing unilateral frontal pain. Palpation reveals asymmetrical muscle tension in right upper trapezius and cervical paraspinals.
C5 Imaging: Cervical spine MRI reveals mild degenerative changes at C3-C4 and C4-C5 levels with facet joint hypertrophy. No significant nerve root compression. Straightening of normal cervical lordosis noted. Multilevel spondylotic changes most prominent at C3-C5 corresponding to referred pain distribution.
#253 First Rib Dysfunction Thoracic
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 mentions whiplash and neurological symptoms which almost immediately narrow to TOS/first rib pathology; clue 1 should be broader (upper chest pain fits many conditions), and the clinical story conflates first rib dysfunction with classic TOS presentation unclearly.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 41-year-old female with recent whiplash injury reports upper chest and shoulder pain with associated upper extremity neurological symptoms. Pain is worse with certain neck postures and limits functional activities.
C2 Activity: Overhead activities and reaching aggravate symptoms dramatically. Sustained neck extension and contralateral side-bending reproducibly worsen pain and tingling in the arm. Symptoms improve with arm elevation and neck flexion positions. Sleeping on the affected side increases nocturnal symptoms.
C3 History: Motor vehicle accident 3 months ago with hyperextension-hyperflexion mechanism. Symptoms began gradually over weeks and have worsened despite ongoing conservative management. Recent flare correlates with increased work activity and positional demands.
C4 Examination: Elevated first rib palpable on affected side. Bilateral scalene muscle tension and tenderness. Positive Roos test: upper extremity claudication and colour change within 2 minutes, consistent with vascular compression.
C5 Imaging: Cervical imaging excludes spine pathology. CT demonstrates narrowed interscalene space with hypertrophic scalenes contributing to neurovascular bundle compression at first rib level.
#254 Thoracic Outlet Syndrome Shoulder & Thoracic
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is appropriately broad (bilateral upper extremity pain/numbness fits many conditions), progressive clues add positional/vascular detail, C4 distinguishes from other causes with Roos/Adson bilaterally, and C5 with MR angiography clinches TOS from compression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old female presents with progressive bilateral upper extremity pain, numbness, and a sensation of heaviness in the hands. Symptoms began insidiously over several months without clear trauma or precipitant.
C2 Activity: Cannot sustain overhead activities or maintain elevated arm positions for more than a few minutes. Symptoms worsen with tasks requiring arms held away from body, such as carrying or reaching. Sleep is disrupted by symptom flares with certain sleeping positions.
C3 History: Six-month progressive course without clear precipitant, though patient reports history of thoracic outlet symptoms with exercise. Recent workplace ergonomic changes and increased computer use. No prior surgical intervention. Symptoms initially postural but now present at rest in some positions.
C4 Examination: Adson test and Roos test reproducibly recreate symptoms bilaterally. Hand intrinsic weakness noted; thenar and hypothenar muscle bulk diminished. Supraclavicular palpation elicits tenderness and Tinel sign bilaterally. Pulses are equal and symmetric at rest.
C5 Imaging: MR angiography demonstrates reduced arterial caliber through thoracic outlet with compression of subclavian artery between anterior and middle scalene muscles. Color Doppler ultrasound confirms flow abnormality with positional changes. Cervical ribs absent but muscular hypertrophy contributes to narrowed interscalene space.
#255 Shoulder Labral Tear Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is reasonably broad for overhead athletes (impingement, instability, labral pathology all fit), each clue adds specificity (activity→mechanical clicking→special tests→imaging), and C5 definitively shows posterior-superior labral tear.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old overhead athlete reports insidious onset of deep shoulder pain with clicking and catching sensations over the past 8 months. Pain is greatest with throwing activities and worsens progressively with continued participation.
C2 Activity: Throwing reproduces sharp deep pain with a catching sensation that forces cessation of activity. Late-cocking phase of throwing causes particular symptom exacerbation. Nighttime symptoms limit sleep comfort in certain positions.
C3 History: Eight-month history following increased training volume in throwing sport. No specific traumatic event preceding onset. Previous right shoulder impingement syndrome. Progressive worsening despite rest periods and anti-inflammatory medication management.
C4 Examination: On examination, positive O'Brien's test producing deep shoulder pain consistent with patient's complaint. Anterior slide test and crank test in abduction/external rotation reproduce pain. Apprehension test positive in 90-90 position, suggesting anterior-superior instability pattern.
C5 Imaging: MR arthrography reveals posterior superior labral tear extending from 10 o'clock to 2 o'clock position with associated irregular articular surface. Fluid tracking into the tear confirms disruption. Subtle associated undersurface rotator cuff fraying noted. Anteroinferior labrum appears intact.
#256 Down Syndrome Systemic
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 essentially names the diagnosis with 'developmental delay affecting gross motor and cognitive function'—too specific for a guessing game; additionally C5 describes examination findings not imaging, violating the puzzle structure.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 4-year-old child presents with developmental delay affecting gross motor milestones and cognitive function. Parents report hypotonia and reduced strength limiting activities compared to age-matched peers.
C2 Activity: A 4-year-old child presents with global developmental delay. Parents report the child is slower than peers in reaching motor milestones, appears weaker, and tires easily with activity.
C3 History: Child demonstrates a wide-based gait and cannot hop or jump. Stair climbing requires upper limb support and rail-holding. Fine motor tasks are notably clumsy; pencil grasp is immature and feeding is messy.
C4 Examination: Condition identified in early infancy. Cardiac and orthopedic complications are known to be associated. No significant neurological events since birth. Attends early intervention services.
C5 Imaging: Positive prone head lift test (unable to clear forehead from mat). Severe ligamentous laxity noted: thumb-to-forearm touchable, ankle dorsiflexion 40°+. Knee valgus noted bilaterally even at rest. Vertical sagittal plane control poor during supported standing.
#257 Tibial Stress Fracture Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is broad (shin pain fits multiple lower leg conditions), clues progressively narrow through activity pattern→training load history→focal tenderness→MRI showing bone marrow edema, creating fair diagnostic progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 19-year-old runner reports insidious onset of deep anterior shin pain that began during training progression. Pain is activity-related and localized to the lower anterolateral leg, worse with running and relieving with rest.
C2 Activity: Onset of pain occurs during running, classically after 20 minutes of sustained activity. Sharp, deep pain in the shin prevents continued running and requires activity modification. Pain does not occur with walking at normal pace. Symptoms worsening with increased training volume despite attempts to rest.
C3 History: Twelve-week progressive history correlating with increased distance running and addition of high-impact drills. Recent changes to training surface from track to asphalt. No previous lower extremity injuries. Training load increased 40% over 4 weeks preceding symptom onset.
C4 Examination: On examination, focal tenderness elicited over anterior-medial tibia at the junction of proximal and middle thirds. Mild swelling noted over the painful region. Positive hop test reproducing familiar pain. Pain reproduced with resisted dorsiflexion. Percussion over tibia elicits focal pain.
C5 Imaging: Tibial radiographs appear radiographically normal in early stages. MRI reveals characteristic findings including bone marrow edema on T2 sequence localized to the anterior tibia in the symptomatic region. STIR imaging shows hyperintense signal reflecting inflammatory response. No complete fracture line identified.
#258 Baker's Cyst Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is appropriately broad (posterior knee bulge after effusion could be many pathologies), clues progressively add cyst-specific features (position-dependent→osteoarthritis association→transillumination→ultrasound confirmation), and C5 definitively clinches.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old female reports a painless bulge in the posterior knee that became noticeable after knee swelling and pain. The mass does not limit function significantly but causes subjective awareness and occasional discomfort with certain knee positions.
C2 Activity: Bulge becomes more apparent with knee extension and activity; the mass reduces somewhat with knee flexion and rest. Prolonged walking increases prominence. Certain knee positions occasionally provoke mild aching in the popliteal region. No mechanical symptoms or true locking noted.
C3 History: Two-year history of gradual enlargement following prior knee effusion related to osteoarthritis. No acute traumatic event preceding cyst appearance. Bilateral mild knee osteoarthritis evident on imaging. Intermittent knee swelling management with activity modification and NSAIDs.
C4 Examination: On examination, a palpable firm mass noted in the popliteal fossa, best appreciated with knee extended. No warmth or erythema overlying the mass. Popliteal mass transilluminates, suggesting cystic fluid-filled nature. Knee effusion palpable. Active flexion and extension pain-free without mechanical restriction.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound confirms anechoic fluid collection in popliteal fossa measuring 3.5 cm with no internal echoes. Direct communication demonstrated between cyst and knee joint capsule, confirming synovial origin. MRI shows T2 hyperintense cyst with intact walls, no hemorrhage. Associated knee joint effusion and mild degenerative changes noted.
#259 Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head Hip
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C5 repeats examination findings from C4 (internal rotation severely limited, flexion/abduction limited, FABER positive) rather than providing imaging findings; clue 5 should be imaging-based and definitive, not duplicate examination data.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old presents with progressive hip pain localized to the groin and lateral hip region. Pain is worse with weight-bearing and certain hip positions, with no recalled traumatic injury. The insidious onset over several weeks suggests multiple possible hip pathologies including osteoarthritis, labral pathology, or inflammatory conditions.
C2 Activity: The patient reports difficulty with weight-bearing activities and ambulation, particularly ascending stairs and transitioning from sitting to standing. Pain is aggravated by hip flexion beyond 90 degrees and internal rotation, while pain is eased with rest and hip abduction positioning. Walking distance has progressively decreased over the past month.
C3 History: The patient has a significant history of corticosteroid use for 2 years secondary to chronic respiratory disease. Additionally, there is past history of excessive alcohol consumption. These risk factors combined with the progressive nature of symptoms over 3 months increase suspicion for a vascular compromise pathology.
C4 Examination: On examination, hip flexion is limited to 90 degrees with pain onset, and internal rotation is significantly restricted to 15 degrees. The FABER test reproduces groin pain, and the log roll test elicits lateral hip and groin pain. Hip strength is 4/5 in hip abductors and flexors bilaterally, with pain-limited range of motion.
C5 Imaging: On examination, internal rotation is severely limited to 10–15 degrees with guarding and pain. Hip flexion and abduction are limited to 85–90 degrees. Hip strength is 4/5 in abductors and flexors, though motor deficit appears pain-limited rather than truly neurogenic. FABER test reproduces groin pain. Notably, external rotation and extension remain relatively preserved compared to internal rotation.
#260 Myofascial Pain Syndrome Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Puzzle is largely sound but C1 is slightly generic (3-month dull aching in trapezius/levator could fit cervicalgia, early degenerative changes); minor issue only—otherwise good progression through trigger points and normal imaging to clinch myofascial cause.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old office worker presents with a 3-month history of dull, aching pain localised to the upper trapezius and levator scapulae bilaterally. No history of trauma or significant injury. Pain is constant but fluctuates throughout the day.
C2 Activity: The patient reports pain is aggravated by prolonged sitting at a desk with poor posture, particularly with forward head positioning. Neck rotation and lateral flexion toward the affected side reproduce pain. Symptoms ease with heat application, massage, and neck stretching. The patient notes morning stiffness lasting 15-20 minutes.
C3 History: Patient attributes symptom onset to recent intensive project work with increased stress. Reports previous episodes of neck pain that resolved with rest and therapy 2-3 years ago. No significant medical history, no rheumatological or autoimmune conditions. Current sleep disrupted; wakes with stiffness that improves with movement.
C4 Examination: On palpation, firm myofascial trigger points are identified in the upper trapezius that reproduce referred pain to the posterior head and temporal region when pressed. Active cervical range of motion is within normal limits but reproduces local muscle tenderness at end-ranges. The Levine test for thoracic outlet syndrome is negative, ruling out neural involvement.
C5 Imaging: MRI cervical spine unremarkable for degenerative changes, disc herniation, or neural compression. Ultrasound of trapezius: normal muscle echotexture, no focal lesion, normal vascularity. Normal findings support clinical diagnosis of primary muscle pain disorder without structural substrate.
#261 Medial Epicondylitis Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (elbow pain, gripping), each clue adds distinct clinical information (activity limitations, occupational history, specific examination findings, imaging confirmation), and progression flows logically to a near-definitive diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old carpenter presents with a 2-month history of inner elbow pain that developed insidiously without trauma. Pain worsens with gripping and repetitive hand activities. No prior similar episodes.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot sustain grip-based activities or perform forceful wrist flexion. Pain is particularly triggered by combined wrist flexion with pronation movements. Symptoms improve with activity modification and rest, but return with repetitive gripping tasks.
C3 History: The patient works as a carpenter and reports recent increase in overhead work and repetitive gripping tasks over the past 3 months. There is no history of direct trauma to the elbow. The patient has not sought prior treatment for musculoskeletal conditions. Symptoms began gradually after intensive project work in winter months.
C4 Examination: On palpation, tenderness is maximal directly over the medial epicondyle. The Golfer's elbow test, performed with wrist flexion against resistance, reproduces medial elbow pain. Wrist flexor strength is diminished at 4/5 compared to the contralateral side. Elbow flexion and extension are preserved, but pronation with wrist flexion is limited by pain.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound of the medial elbow demonstrates heterogeneous echotexture and thickening of the flexor carpi radialis and palmaris longus tendon origins. There is mild hypoechoic change within the tendons consistent with tendinopathy. Power Doppler imaging shows increased vascularity at the medial epicondyle consistent with chronic inflammatory response.
#262 Adhesive Capsulitis Shoulder
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 2 and C3 are severely redundant (both describe pain, stiffness, and movement limitation), and the clue ordering is broken—C3 (examination findings) should come before C4 (history of immobilization), making the clinical story incoherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old woman presents with shoulder pain and progressive stiffness affecting the glenohumeral joint. The pain is localized deeply in the shoulder joint and is constant in nature, worsening at night. The 4-month gradual onset could suggest adhesive capsulitis, rotator cuff tear, or glenohumeral osteoarthritis.
C2 Activity: A 58-year-old woman reports shoulder pain that started 4 months ago and has worsened gradually. The pain is deep and constant, worse at night. She notices her shoulder feels increasingly stiff and restricted in movement.
C3 History: The patient cannot reach overhead or behind her back to fasten a bra. Forward flexion is possible but abduction and external rotation are severely restricted. Passive stretching provides temporary relief, but active movement is markedly limited.
C4 Examination: Six months ago the patient sustained a mild rotator cuff strain and was advised to immobilize the shoulder for several weeks. After the immobilization was discontinued, progressive stiffness and pain developed over the following months despite no further injury. No history of trauma, inflammatory disease, or diabetes.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the shoulder demonstrates thickening and contracture of the glenohumeral joint capsule, particularly visible on axial and coronal sequences. T2-weighted imaging shows patchy edema within the rotator cuff muscles due to disuse. No rotator cuff tears or labral pathology is identified. MR arthrography confirms reduced joint volume secondary to capsular inflammation.
#263 Sarcopenia Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 2 and C3 repeat functional limitations nearly verbatim (difficulty rising, stairs, balance); consolidating these would strengthen progression, but the puzzle remains playable with clear escalation to definitive imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An 78-year-old woman presents with generalized muscle weakness and loss of functional strength affecting multiple muscle groups. The patient reports progressive difficulty with ambulation and basic activities of daily living over the past 2 years. The insidious onset and diffuse distribution could suggest sarcopenia, myositis, neurological disease, or systemic metabolic derangement.
C2 Activity: The patient reports difficulty rising from a chair without upper extremity support, climbing stairs, and walking long distances. Standing balance is compromised, and the patient has experienced several near-falls. Fine motor tasks such as opening jars and buttoning buttons are increasingly difficult. The patient describes feelings of fatigue that limit daily participation.
C3 History: Patient reports difficulty rising from a chair without armrest support, climbing stairs, and walking long distances. Standing balance is compromised with several near-falls. She reports generalized fatigue that limits participation in sustained activities.
C4 Examination: On examination, grip strength is measured at 18 kilograms bilaterally, which is below the 10th percentile for age and sex. Knee extension strength is 3+/5 bilaterally, and hip flexor strength is 3/5. Gait speed is reduced at 0.8 meters per second. The Short Physical Performance Battery score is 4, indicating high fall risk and poor functional mobility.
C5 Imaging: DEXA scan shows low muscle mass with appendicular skeletal muscle index of 5.8 kilograms per meter squared, below the threshold for sarcopenia diagnosis. Computed tomography of the thigh shows marked intramuscular fat infiltration and reduced cross-sectional muscle area. MRI confirms widespread myosteatosis and atrophy of bilateral quadriceps and hip flexor muscles.
#264 Lumbar Spinal Stenosis Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 casts a reasonable net (bilateral lower limb pain, multiple etiologies possible), C2 introduces the classic neurogenic claudication pattern (standing worse than walking, flexion relief), and subsequent clues build progressively to imaging confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 70-year-old woman with established osteoarthritis presents with chronic bilateral lower limb pain, heaviness, and progressive functional decline over 2 years. She reports walking has become increasingly difficult, and she experiences numbness in both feet that worsens with activity.
C2 Activity: Standing still is worse than walking, and walking backwards or with hip flexion (leaning on a walker) improves symptoms. The patient reports increased pain during extension-based movements and relief with flexion-based positioning. She has reduced daily activity significantly and requires assistive devices.
C3 History: Known history of lumbar spondylosis diagnosed 10 years ago. Patient reports no recent trauma, falls, or acute exacerbations. She denies incontinence, retention, or bowel changes. She is on treatment for hypertension but is otherwise systemically well.
C4 Examination: Bilateral lower extremity strength is 4+/5 in hip flexors and ankle dorsiflexors. Patellar reflexes are diminished bilaterally (1+). Sensory testing reveals decreased sensation in both feet in an L5-S1 distribution. The lumbar spine shows marked stiffness with minimal forward bending capability.
C5 Imaging: CT imaging of the lumbar spine demonstrates severe multilevel degenerative stenosis involving L3-L4, L4-L5, and L5-S1 levels. There is marked narrowing of the spinal canal, lateral recess stenosis, and facet-mediated stenosis. Disc bulging and osteophyte formation are prominent contributors.
#265 Brainstem Stroke Brain
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This puzzle is out of scope for Physiodle—it is a medical/neurological emergency requiring immediate physician diagnosis and imaging interpretation, not a physiotherapy diagnosis puzzle suitable for a guessing game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 71-year-old man with a 20-year history of poorly controlled hypertension (BP 160-180 mmHg) and diabetes presents with acute-onset dizziness and imbalance. He reports vertigo with nausea and one-sided facial numbness. Symptom onset was sudden this morning without warning.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot stand or walk without assistance. Double vision occurs intermittently. Right-sided coordinated movements are impaired, particularly fine motor tasks. Nausea is provoked by head movement.
C3 History: Prior transient ischemic attack 5 years ago. Currently on aspirin monotherapy without additional antiplatelet or anticoagulation cover. No recent illness or trauma.
C4 Examination: On examination, the patient demonstrates left-sided facial weakness with drooping of the left mouth corner and asymmetric smile. Dysdiadochokinesia is present on right-sided rapid alternating movements. Horizontal nystagmus is observed on rightward gaze. The Romberg test shows significant postural instability. Cranial nerve examination shows miosis and ptosis on the left, consistent with Horner's syndrome.
C5 Imaging: MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging shows acute infarction in the left brainstem at the level of the pons, measuring 8 millimeters by 6 millimeters. The lesion involves the dorsomedial pontine tegmentum. Fluid-attenuated inversion recovery sequences confirm acute ischemic stroke. Magnetic resonance angiography of the intracranial vasculature shows no significant stenosis or occlusion.
#266 Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction Grade 1 Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (medial ankle pain, activity-related), C2 and C3 introduce distinct information (activity triggers, running volume escalation, subtle arch changes), and imaging provides objective confirmation without being prematurely diagnostic.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 54-year-old recreational runner presents with a 3-month history of gradual-onset medial ankle pain. The dull ache worsens with activity and improves with rest. No recent ankle injury recalled.
C2 Activity: Walking, stair climbing, and running aggravate pain. Patient reports difficulty maintaining arch support during prolonged standing. Arch support and rest provide relief. Jumping provokes sharp medial ankle pain.
C3 History: The patient is a recreational runner who recently increased training volume by 30% over the past 2 months without adequate progressive loading. There is no history of ankle trauma or prior ankle instability. The patient notes that symptoms correlate with increased running mileage. Body mass index is 28, contributing to increased lower extremity loading during activity.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is elicited along the posterior tibial tendon approximately 4 centimeters proximal to the medial malleolus. The single-leg heel raise test reveals mild difficulty with heel elevation on the affected side, with ability to complete 10 repetitions compared to 15 on the unaffected side. The arch shows subtle flattening with weight-bearing compared to the contralateral foot.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound of the posterior tibial tendon demonstrates mild hypoechoic signal within the tendon substance, suggesting early tendinopathy without significant fiber disruption. The tendon measures 6.2 millimeters in anteroposterior diameter, mildly enlarged compared to contralateral side at 5.8 millimeters. Power Doppler shows mild increased vascularity at the tendon surface consistent with early inflammatory response.
#267 Iliotibial Band Syndrome Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately lists multiple differential diagnoses, C2 introduces the characteristic range-of-motion pain pattern (20–30° knee flexion), C3 adds training load context, and C4/C5 confirm with tight band and femoral condyle edema.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old runner presents with lateral knee pain that develops during running activities. The pain is localized to the lateral knee joint line and describes a sharp, stabbing quality. The 4-week onset during increased training volume could represent iliotibial band syndrome, lateral meniscal tear, lateral ligament sprain, or patellofemoral pain syndrome.
C2 Activity: Pain is reproducibly aggravated when running more than 3 kilometers, particularly with downhill running. The patient reports pain with knee flexion between 20-30 degrees during the running gait cycle. Pain is eased with rest, ice, and foam rolling of the lateral thigh. Walking at normal pace does not provoke symptoms, but stairs require modification.
C3 History: The patient increased running volume from 15 kilometers per week to 35 kilometers per week over 4 weeks while training for a marathon. A recent change in running surface to concrete from park trails preceded symptom onset. The patient reports running in worn-out shoes with poor cushioning. No history of prior knee injury or lower extremity biomechanical dysfunction.
C4 Examination: The Ober test reveals hip adduction limited to 15 degrees on the affected side compared to 25 degrees contralaterally, indicating iliotibial band tightness. Hip abductor strength is 4/5 on the affected side versus 5/5 on the contralateral side. Noble compression test is positive. Knee effusion and ligamentous laxity are absent.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the knee demonstrates thickening of the iliotibial band measuring 4 millimeters compared to normal 2-3 millimeters. T2-weighted imaging shows edema adjacent to the lateral femoral condyle at the region of band friction. No meniscal tears, ligamentous injuries, or cartilage defects are identified. The vastus lateralis muscle shows normal signal intensity without fatty changes.
#268 Intertrochanteric Fracture Hip
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is an acute medical/surgical emergency requiring immediate orthopedic intervention, not a physiotherapy diagnosis puzzle; Physiodle should focus on musculoskeletal conditions managed within scope of practice rather than fracture diagnostics.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An 82-year-old woman fell from standing height while getting out of bed 2 hours ago. She reports severe pain in the hip and groin region and is unable to place any weight on the affected leg.
C2 Activity: The affected limb is held in external rotation and slight flexion at rest. Any attempt to internally rotate or abduct the hip triggers severe pain. She cannot perform transfers or ambulate without maximum assistance.
C3 History: The patient has a history of osteoporosis diagnosed 15 years ago with poor medication compliance. She takes calcium and vitamin D supplementation but reports limited physical activity due to age. The fall occurred during a simple transfer at home with no direct trauma to the hip region. She lives alone and could not call for help for 2 hours.
C4 Examination: On examination, the affected limb is held in external rotation at approximately 45 degrees and slight hip flexion of 10-15 degrees. The limb appears 2 centimeters shorter than the contralateral side. Palpation reveals crepitus in the hip region and severe pain with any movement. Sensation and pulses in the lower extremity are intact. No skin tenting or open wound is present.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs of the pelvis show a comminuted intertrochanteric fracture with the fracture line extending from the greater trochanter to the lesser trochanter. The fracture extends into the medial cortex, classified as an unstable intertrochanteric fracture. Bone density appears osteoporotic on radiographs. CT imaging confirms no hip dislocation and assesses fracture displacement suitable for surgical planning.
#269 Vaginismus Pelvis
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This puzzle is outside the intended scope of Physiodle (a pelvic floor sexual dysfunction requiring psychology/sexual therapy input), and while pelvic floor physiotherapy is legitimate, the diagnosis itself is primarily gynecological/psychological rather than a movement or musculoskeletal condition suited to the game format.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 31-year-old woman presents with difficulty and pain during vaginal penetration, including both sexual intercourse and gynecological examination. The patient describes an involuntary muscular response that prevents penetration. The onset 2 years ago could suggest vaginismus, vulvodynia, endometriosis, or pelvic floor myofascial pain.
C2 Activity: A 31-year-old woman reports difficulty with vaginal penetration during sexual intercourse for the past 2 years. She describes an involuntary muscular tightening that prevents entry despite her desire to proceed. Pain accompanies any penetration attempt. No prior trauma or significant life events coincided with onset.
C3 History: The patient reports no history of sexual trauma or abuse. She grew up in a conservative religious environment with limited sex education. She denies use of hormonal contraceptives and reports no prior gynecological surgeries. Menarche was at age 13 with regular menstrual cycles. General health is good with no other medical conditions or chronic pain syndromes.
C4 Examination: On pelvic examination, visual inspection of the vulva and introitus appears normal with no visible lesions, redness, or atrophy. However, when attempting to insert a single finger at the introitus, involuntary circumvaginal muscle contraction is palpated, preventing insertion despite adequate lubrication. The pelvic floor muscles are notably tense and hypertonic. Internal examination cannot be completed due to protective muscle guarding.
C5 Imaging: Transperineal ultrasound demonstrates increased muscle thickness in the bulbocavernosus and superficial transverse perineal muscles, consistent with hypertonic pelvic floor musculature. Increased echogenicity in the pelvic floor muscles suggests chronically contracted state. Pelvic MRI rules out structural abnormalities including endometriosis, fibroids, or anatomical variants affecting the vaginal canal.
#270 Hip Pointer Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute anterolateral hip pain post-trauma), C2 adds functional limitations, C3 anchors the mechanism, C4 confirms soft tissue contusion at the iliac attachment, and C5 rules out fracture to arrive at the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old football player presents with acute-onset severe pain over the anterolateral hip following direct shoulder-to-hip contact during a tackle. Pain is localized and well-demarcated.
C2 Activity: The patient is unable to return to play and has severe limitation with hip flexion and trunk movements. Walking is difficult with a pronounced limp, and the patient favors the unaffected side. Pain is exacerbated by activities requiring hip flexion and lateral trunk flexion away from the injury. Running and kicking are impossible due to pain.
C3 History: Mechanism: direct shoulder-to-hip contact during tackle. No prior hip injuries. No history of bone fragility or medical conditions affecting bone quality.
C4 Examination: On examination, significant tenderness and swelling are present over the iliac crest, particularly at the attachment site of the abdominal musculature. Ecchymosis is developing over the area. Hip flexion produces pain at the iliac attachment and anterior hip region. The anterior superior iliac spine and anterior inferior iliac spine are palpated and found to be intact without step-off or displacement. Hip strength testing is limited by pain.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs of the pelvis show no evidence of fracture through the iliac crest or acetabulum. CT imaging of the pelvis reveals soft tissue swelling over the anterolateral hip with blood products within the muscles at the iliac attachment. No occult fracture is identified. Bone integrity is maintained with normal cortical thickness.
#271 Lateral Collateral Ligament Sprain Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from acute lateral knee pain with instability → activity limitations and swelling → mechanism of injury clarification → specific ligament tenderness and positive varus stress → definitive MRI findings; clue 1 fits multiple knee injuries.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old recreational soccer player presents with acute-onset lateral knee pain after a twisting injury to the lower limb. The patient felt the knee 'give way' during the mechanism and experienced immediate, severe pain. Onset is acute, mechanism is non-contact.
C2 Activity: The patient is severely limited with weight-bearing and has a sense of instability with any dynamic movement or single-leg stance. Immediate swelling suggests significant ligamentous or meniscal involvement. Pain is lateral and worsens with any rotational or cutting movement.
C3 History: The patient has a prior history of an ankle sprain on the contralateral side 6 months ago. There is no prior knee injury. The soccer injury occurred when the patient landed on the outside of the foot with the knee in slight flexion, forcing the knee into inversion and external rotation. The patient continued playing briefly before severe pain forced him to stop.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is elicited along the lateral collateral ligament from the fibular head distally. The varus stress test at 30 degrees of knee flexion shows increased laxity compared to the unaffected side, graded as 1+ opening. The anterior drawer test is negative, and Lachman's test is negative, making anterior cruciate ligament tear less likely. Mild joint effusion is present.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the knee demonstrates a partial tear of the lateral collateral ligament with interruption of approximately 50% of the fiber bundle at the level of the fibular head. T2-weighted imaging shows edema and hemorrhage within and around the ligament. The anterior cruciate ligament and menisci appear intact. Soft tissue swelling is present in the lateral knee compartment.
#272 Clubfoot Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Logical progression from bilateral neonatal foot deformity → functional concerns about weight-bearing → prenatal/birth history → clinical examination findings → imaging confirmation; clue 1 could theoretically include other congenital foot anomalies.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A neonate with bilateral foot deformity noted at birth. Parent reports inability to passively correct foot position to neutral. Family history significant for similar presentation in maternal grandfather.
C2 Activity: Feeding and sleeping unaffected. Able to move hips and knees freely. Parents concerned about future weight-bearing and whether foot position will prevent normal gait development.
C3 History: Full-term vaginal delivery at 39 weeks. No maternal infections, medications, or diabetes during pregnancy. No other congenital anomalies. Foot deformity noted immediately at birth by delivery team.
C4 Examination: On examination, bilateral feet demonstrate equinus deformity with inversion and adduction of the forefoot. Pirani score measures 6/6, indicating moderate deformity. Passive dorsiflexion limited to 5 degrees bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Anteroposterior and lateral foot radiographs demonstrate talar-navicular uncovering. Talocalcaneal angle measures 15 degrees (normal >20 degrees). Tibiocalcaneal angle shows equinus positioning of 45 degrees.
#273 Tibialis Posterior Tendinopathy Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Good clinical narrative from gradual medial foot/ankle pain → activity aggravation and gait dysfunction → training history and predisposing factors → specific tendon tenderness and weakness → imaging confirmation of tendon pathology.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 54-year-old recreational runner reports gradual onset of medial foot and ankle pain over 6 weeks. Pain worse with morning stiffness and gradually improves with activity. Denies prior significant ankle injury.
C2 Activity: Pain increases significantly after running, particularly when going uphill. Walking on uneven surfaces elicits discomfort. Foot appears to pronate excessively during gait, and patient reports difficulty with single-leg stance activities.
C3 History: Patient recently increased running frequency from 3 to 5 days weekly. History of mild pes planus bilaterally. Takes no anti-inflammatory medications. No history of systemic inflammatory conditions or previous ankle surgery.
C4 Examination: On examination, point tenderness elicited along the posterior tibial tendon just posterior and inferior to the medial malleolus. Single-leg heel raise test demonstrates weakness and inability to maintain inversion at midfoot. Mild swelling noted in the medial ankle region with positive tenosynovitis sign.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound imaging reveals hypoechoic thickening of the posterior tibial tendon measuring 8mm in cross-sectional diameter (normal <5mm). Fluid collection evident within the tendon sheath. Power Doppler demonstrates increased neovascularization within and around the tendon.
#274 Ataxia Whole Body
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is slightly specific (mentioning coordination issues and fine motor dysfunction strongly suggests cerebellar pathology); consider broadening to 'progressive neurological gait and balance disorder' to increase differential initially, though otherwise logical progression to cerebellar-specific findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old accountant reports gradual progressive imbalance over 2 years. Wife notes increased stumbling and difficulties with coordination. Patient reports trouble with fine motor tasks such as writing and buttoning clothes.
C2 Activity: Patient reports unsteady gait that worsens on uneven terrain or in dim lighting. Requires rail support for stairs. Reaches for objects but overshoots the target consistently.
C3 History: Symptoms began insidiously without preceding infection or trauma. No significant past medical history. Father had similar symptoms starting in late 40s. Patient denies alcohol use. No medications with neurotoxic potential. Denies sensory symptoms or weakness.
C4 Examination: Tandem stance maintained for only 2 seconds with eyes open, impossible with eyes closed. Finger-nose testing demonstrates consistent overshooting bilaterally. Rapid alternating hand pronation-supination becomes irregular and slows after 2-3 cycles.
C5 Imaging: Brain MRI shows selective atrophy of the cerebellum, particularly the midline vermis. Scattered white matter signal changes in the deep periventricular regions. Genetic testing reveals a pathogenic trinucleotide repeat expansion in the SCA region.
#275 Meniere's Disease Inner Ear
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent progression from episodic severe vertigo with hearing loss and tinnitus → unpredictable incapacitating attacks with autonomic symptoms → history of gradual onset over 18 months → examination findings of reduced caloric response and sensorineural hearing loss → imaging confirmation of endolymphatic hydrops.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old woman with recurrent episodes of severe vertigo lasting 20 minutes to 3 hours. Associated with left-sided hearing loss, tinnitus described as low-frequency roaring, and left ear fullness sensation.
C2 Activity: Episodes are completely unpredictable and incapacitating—patient must lie down during attacks. Severe nausea and vomiting accompany most episodes. Avoids driving due to fear of attacks. Between episodes, reports only mild imbalance.
C3 History: First episode 18 months ago. Frequency has remained stable at 2-3 attacks monthly despite rigorous sodium restriction and diuretic trial over 6 months. No recent head trauma, no ototoxic medications, no prior vertigo before this illness began.
C4 Examination: At rest, no spontaneous nystagmus. Dix-Hallpike and Romberg maneuvers normal. Caloric irrigation shows reduced ice water response on left (40% reduction compared to right). Weber test: sound lateralizes to left ear (bone conduction intact). Audiometry shows low-frequency air-bone gap on left side.
C5 Imaging: Gadolinium-enhanced MRI demonstrates enhancement of the left endolymphatic sac and duct, consistent with endolymphatic hydrops. High-resolution imaging shows increased endolymphatic volume occupying >50% of the cochlear and vestibular spaces. Pure-tone audiometry shows low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss pattern.
#276 Tennis Leg Calf
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear acute presentation with audible pop and immediate swelling → functional loss of plantarflexion and weight-bearing → history of no warning signs → palpable defect and positive Thompson test → imaging confirmation of muscle tear.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 56-year-old recreational athlete reports sudden-onset severe calf pain during activity, accompanied by an audible 'pop'. Rapid onset of swelling and inability to bear weight on the affected leg.
C2 Activity: Unable to plantar flex foot or heel walk. Attempted jogging elicits severe calf pain. Swelling progressed over 24 hours with prominent bruising visible on the medial ankle and foot sole. Calf tightness severely restricts ankle dorsiflexion range.
C3 History: Recreational athlete with no prior calf problems. No warning signs before injury. No relevant medical history or current medications. Relatively infrequent training schedule (twice weekly).
C4 Examination: On examination, palpable defect detected in the gastrocnemius-soleus complex at the musculotendinous junction. Positive Thompson test with absent plantar flexion response on calf squeeze. Severe pain on passive ankle dorsiflexion. Swelling and bruising extending to medial ankle with ecchymosis tracking down to sole.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates complete tear of the medial gastrocnemius muscle with muscle fiber retraction. Hemorrhagic collection noted within the muscular compartment measuring 4 x 6 cm. MRI confirms soleus muscle involvement with T2 hyperintense edema throughout the muscle belly and proximal musculotendinous junction.
#277 Ankle Impingement Syndrome Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Logical progression from chronic anterior ankle pain → activity-specific aggravation with plantarflexion-inversion → training volume history and prior sprains → anterior impingement test positive with palpable tenderness → MRI findings of osteophytes and synovitis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 31-year-old soccer player with chronic ankle pain that developed gradually over 8 months. Denies recent injury, though had minor ankle sprains several years ago. Pain is constant but varies with activity.
C2 Activity: Kicking activities consistently trigger sharp anterior ankle pain. Pain worsens with plantarflexion combined with inversion. Walking on uneven terrain elicits discomfort. Stiffness occurs after periods of inactivity, gradually improving with gentle movement. Running speed significantly affected.
C3 History: High-level athlete with progressive symptoms and no single injury event. Training volume is substantial (3-4 sessions weekly). Has not undergone ankle surgery previously. No systemic disease or inflammatory indicators reported.
C4 Examination: On examination, palpable tenderness at the anterior tibiofibular interval. Plantarflexion-inversion combined movement reproduces pain sharply. Anterior ankle drawer test negative. Anterior impingement test positive with pain reproduction. Ankle plantarflexion range 50 degrees with restriction felt anteriorly.
C5 Imaging: MRI reveals anterior osteophyte formation at the talonavicular joint margin measuring 4mm. Hypertrophic soft tissue nodule present anterior to the capsule with T2 hyperintense signal. Anterior joint capsule demonstrates thickening and folding consistent with synovitis. Plain radiographs confirm subtle talonavicular joint space osteophytes.
#278 Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome - Paediatric Knee
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 4 repeats clue 3 (history and growth spurt information) instead of adding new examination findings; clue 5 is actually examination findings rather than imaging/testing, breaking the intended progression structure.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 14-year-old female soccer player reports bilateral anterior knee pain present for 4 months. Pain worsens gradually throughout the day and after sports practice. Denies specific injury or trauma; pain developed insidiously.
C2 Activity: Pain aggravated significantly by running and jumping activities. Ascending and descending stairs elicits bilateral anterior knee pain. Prolonged sitting with knees bent increases discomfort. Patient reports knee stiffness after practice that resolves with activity. Reduced training tolerance.
C3 History: Symptoms coincided with increased training frequency and intensity at start of competitive season. Growth spurt noted over past 18 months. No prior knee injuries or surgeries. Denies morning pain or night symptoms. Currently active in competitive soccer and training 5 days weekly.
C4 Examination: Symptoms coincided with competitive season intensification and documented growth spurt over 18 months. No prior knee injuries. Notably, pain is activity-dependent with no morning symptoms or nocturnal awakening; resolves with rest.
C5 Imaging: Bilateral hip abductor weakness (4/5) with positive single-leg squat test showing dynamic knee valgus. Special testing reveals patellar tilt sign positive bilaterally. Q-angle 16° (elevated for paediatric age). Palpable tenderness over medial patellar facet bilaterally. VMO activation deficit on manual muscle testing.
#279 Cerebellar Ataxia Brain
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 2 simply restates clue 1 without new information; clue 3 contains examination findings; clue 4 and 5 are nearly identical examination findings—the progression collapses completely after clue 1 with no genuine advancement of clinical information.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 71-year-old gentleman with acute onset of severe dizziness and ataxia following sudden excruciating occipital headache. Unable to stand or walk independently since symptom onset 6 hours prior. Reports associated nausea and vomiting.
C2 Activity: A 71-year-old gentleman with acute onset severe dizziness and inability to stand or walk. Sudden occipital headache this morning, accompanied by nausea and vomiting.
C3 History: Severely limited mobility — cannot stand or walk without maximal assistance. Speech is slurred and difficult to understand. Rapid, coordinated movements of the hands are severely impaired.
C4 Examination: On examination, dysmetria profound on finger-nose testing with severe overshooting bilaterally. Severe gait ataxia evident requiring maximal assistance for any standing attempt. Positive Romberg test with inability to maintain stance even with eyes open. Hypotonia noted in upper extremities. Nystagmus present with lateral gaze, horizontally directed.
C5 Imaging: Profound dysmetria on finger-nose testing with severe bilateral overshooting. Marked gait ataxia requiring maximal assistance. Maintains abnormal balance even with eyes open (positive Romberg). Horizontal nystagmus evident on lateral gaze.
#280 Medial Collateral Ligament Sprain Knee
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 2 is essentially a restatement of clue 1; clues 4 and 5 are duplicated (both report valgus stress test and anterior drawer findings)—this puzzle lacks meaningful information progression and repeats itself substantially across clues.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old recreational skier reports medial knee pain that began during a fall while skiing and has persisted for 2 weeks. Pain is present during walking and worsens with side-to-side movements. Swelling has been consistent since the injury. The presentation could encompass various knee ligament or meniscal injuries.
C2 Activity: A 45-year-old recreational skier reports medial-sided knee pain that began during a fall 2 weeks ago. Pain worsens with side-to-side movements and walking. Swelling has been present since injury onset.
C3 History: Patient reports pain with weight-bearing activities when the foot is externally rotated. Downstairs negotiation is limited. Normal walking is managed but feels unstable.
C4 Examination: On examination, the valgus stress test is positive at 30 degrees knee flexion with increased medial joint opening. Palpation reveals tenderness along the medial joint capsule and ligament. Passive knee extension reproduces pain. Anterior drawer test is negative.
C5 Imaging: Valgus stress test at 30 degrees knee flexion demonstrates increased medial joint opening. Medial joint line tenderness is present. Anterior drawer test is negative.
#281 Atelectasis Lungs
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (post-op dyspnea fits many conditions), each clue adds clinically relevant information (activity → exam findings → imaging confirmation), and C5 clinches the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old post-operative patient (Day 2 following abdominal surgery) reports shallow breathing and reduced exercise tolerance. Complains of mild dyspnea on minimal exertion. Mild dry cough present but non-productive.
C2 Activity: Unable to ambulate more than 5 metres without dyspnea and desaturation. Splinting behavior evident during coughing. Baseline oxygen saturation 96% on room air at rest.
C3 History: Surgical procedure was open partial colectomy under general anesthesia with 90-minute operative time. Pre-operative history significant for 40 pack-year smoking history, though quit 5 years ago. Mild COPD with baseline FEV1/FVC ratio 65%. Pain adequately controlled with multimodal analgesia including epidural catheter.
C4 Examination: Reduced breath sounds throughout right lower lobe with stony dullness to percussion. Respiratory rate elevated at 22 breaths per minute. Oxygen saturation 92% on room air. No clubbing, cyanosis, or peripheral edema. Chest wall pain on deep palpation limits voluntary deep inspiration.
