C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray demonstrates wedge-shaped radiodensity at right lung base with obscuration of right heart border. No mediastinal shift or pleural effusion. Remainder of lung fields clear. Findings consistent with lobar collapse.
#282 Vestibular Migraine Vestibular
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C3 and C5 are somewhat redundant (both emphasize normal vestibular testing and imaging), weakening the progression; the puzzle would benefit from C5 focusing more on migraine-specific features or risk factors rather than repeating normal investigations.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 41-year-old woman reports episodic vertigo occurring 2-4 times monthly without clear triggers. Episodes last 30 minutes to 4 hours. Associated with frontal headache and motion sensitivity during attacks. Denies hearing loss or tinnitus.
C2 Activity: During episodes, lying still in quiet dark room is essential. Visual motion and head movement exacerbate dizziness severely. Bright lights or screens worsen symptoms. Nausea accompanies most attacks though vomiting occurs in only 30%. Complete symptom resolution between episodes with normal balance and coordination.
C3 History: Personal history of migraine headaches since age 18, typically responding to triptans. Mother and maternal grandmother both with migraines. Symptoms began 18 months ago, initially attributed to inner ear disease. Audiometric testing normal. Caloric irrigation normal bilaterally. No recent vestibular testing abnormalities.
C4 Examination: On examination during asymptomatic period, gait normal and balance unimpaired. Nystagmus not observed. Dix-Hallpike maneuver produces no vertigo or nystagmus. Weber and Rinne testing normal. Cranial nerves intact. Head impulse test normal. No spontaneous nystagmus on straight-ahead gaze.
C5 Imaging: Brain MRI with and without gadolinium demonstrates no acute abnormality or demyelinating lesions. No mass effect or structural pathology identified. White matter signal intensity normal. Cerebellopontine angle clear bilaterally. No evidence of temporal bone or inner ear abnormality. Caloric testing from vestibular laboratory confirms symmetrical responses.
#283 Tibialis Posterior Tendinopathy Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from activity-related complaint through functional limitations to physical signs (too-many-toes, weakness, swelling) and MRI confirmation; clue 1 fits multiple foot/ankle conditions appropriately.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old teacher with a 3-month history of progressive medial ankle and arch discomfort. Pain develops gradually throughout the day and is particularly bothersome at end of workday. Denies acute injury or trauma.
C2 Activity: Prolonged standing aggravates symptoms significantly. Stair climbing and walking downhill cause sharp pain along the medial foot. Patient notes arch feels weak and unstable, particularly on walking surfaces that lack support.
C3 History: Occupation requires standing 6+ hours daily. History of chronic ankle sprains (5+ episodes over 15 years) with subsequent lateral ligament laxity. Early signs of hypertension managed with lifestyle modification. No diabetes or systemic inflammatory disease.
C4 Examination: On examination, visible midfoot collapse and valgus heel positioning evident during standing. Positive too-many-toes sign observed on visual inspection. Posterior tibial strength graded 3+/5 with reproduction of pain during muscle testing. Medial ankle swelling present with tenderness along tendon course.
C5 Imaging: MRI imaging demonstrates edema and partial tearing within the mid-portion of the posterior tibial tendon. Tendon diameter measures 7.5mm with signal changes indicating intratendinous mucoid degeneration. Mild lateral ankle joint space narrowing suggesting chronic instability pattern.

Verdict: Clue 1 is too specific and essentially names the diagnosis ('congenital lower extremity weakness' + 'prenatal diagnosis' + 'continence management' in a 5-year-old with paraplegia narrows it to 1-2 conditions), and the condition is too severe/rare to work well as a guessing game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 5-year-old with congenital lower extremity weakness and sensory changes. Parents report difficulty with continence management.
C2 Activity: Non-ambulatory, uses wheelchair for all mobility. Requires contact guard for transfers.
C3 History: Prenatal diagnosis of spinal defect with early surgical repair. Known hydrocephalus managed with shunt. History of recurrent infections.
C4 Examination: Complete paraplegia with grade 0/5 motor strength bilaterally in lower extremities. Sensory level corresponds to L3. Significant hip and knee flexion contractures.
C5 Imaging: Spinal MRI demonstrates segmental spinal cord aplasia with lipoma extending from dural sac. Ventricular enlargement from hydrocephalus evident on brain MRI. Spinal X-ray shows kyphotic deformity at surgical site.
#285 Posterior Cerebral Artery Stroke Brain
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple acute neurological presentations, progressive clues build a coherent story (visual loss → confusion → field defect → imaging confirmation), and the clinical reasoning is sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 78-year-old gentleman with sudden onset difficulty seeing to one side. Reports words appear cut off when reading. Wife noticed he seems confused this morning. No eye pain. Onset was acute, while sitting at breakfast.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot safely navigate his home environment. Repeatedly walks into furniture and doorframes on the right side. Wife reports he seemed confused about which room he was in earlier. Denies awareness of any visual problem—attributes difficulty to 'not paying attention.'
C3 History: History of atrial fibrillation (anticoagulated with warfarin, INR 2.1). Hypertension managed with lisinopril. Prior stroke 3 years ago affecting right motor cortex (minor residual weakness). Denies recent falls or head trauma. Symptom onset this morning with acute awareness of vision loss.
C4 Examination: Visual field confrontation demonstrates right-sided field loss equally in both eyes. Pupils reactive, eye movements normal. Unable to recall examiner's name or the date. Mild increase in right knee reflexes. No focal motor weakness on standard strength testing.
C5 Imaging: Acute MRI with DWI sequences shows restricted diffusion in the left posterior circulation distribution. Left occipital cortex and medial temporal lobe involved. Calcarine fissure region includes acute signal changes. Mild involvement of left thalamic region on T2 imaging.

Verdict: Clue 1 explicitly states 'known genetic syndrome diagnosed prenatally,' which essentially reveals the diagnosis immediately and eliminates meaningful guessing.

C1 Complaint: A 6-year-old child with significant developmental delay across motor and cognitive domains. Parents report persistent weakness and difficulty with balance-dependent activities. Known genetic syndrome diagnosed prenatally.
C2 Activity: Child requires handrail support for stairs and adult assistance for mobility. Cannot tolerate sustained activity due to rapid fatigue. Unable to perform single-leg balance tasks or execute coordinated running.
C3 History: Developmental trajectory significantly delayed: did not walk until 20 months. Born with known chromosomal abnormality. Prior cardiac assessment documented. Currently enrolled in multimodal rehabilitation.
C4 Examination: Palpable generalized ligament laxity with passive ROM notably increased. Knee joints demonstrate hyperextension. Core stability severely compromised. Lower extremity strength graded 3/5. Postural control absent without external support.
C5 Imaging: Cervical spine imaging reveals evidence of cervical myelopathy risk with atlantoaxial subluxation noted on flexion-extension films. Spinal cord appears normal. Skeletal findings consistent with generalized connective tissue laxity. Scoliosis screening reveals mild curvature progression over time.
#287 Scaphoid Fracture Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately broad (wrist injury from FOOSH fits many fractures/sprains), each clue adds distinct clinical information (mechanism → functional loss → physical signs → imaging findings), and progression is logical.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 22-year-old university student reports wrist pain and swelling after a fall on outstretched hand while playing basketball 3 days ago. Pain is localized to the thumb-side of the wrist. Despite mild swelling, patient reports being able to use the hand for most daily tasks.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to grip or squeeze without discomfort. Gripping activities and return to basketball are not tolerated. Wrist extension range of motion mildly reduced but does not severely limit daily function. Radial-sided (thumb-side) swelling persists on examination.
C3 History: High-energy mechanism with fall from height of 6 feet. Wrist extended at impact. Initial swelling subsided somewhat but residual discomfort persists. Anxiety regarding healing timeline and return to sport. Denies prior wrist injuries. Right-hand dominant; injury on dominant side.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness elicited at the radial-sided scaphoid tubercle. Positive scaphoid compression test reproducing wrist pain. Radial-sided wrist swelling palpated. Pain with ulnar deviation combined with wrist extension. Range of motion slightly limited in extension (50 degrees) and radial deviation (15 degrees). Neurovascular status normal.
C5 Imaging: Initial plain radiographs (AP, lateral, and scaphoid views) appear normal without obvious fracture line. CT imaging (High-resolution thin-section) demonstrates non-displaced fracture of the scaphoid waist with minimal separation (<2mm). MRI confirms T2 hyperintense signal through the fracture line with surrounding bone marrow edema. No ligamentous injury visualized.
#288 Spondylolysis Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple causes of adolescent back pain, activity-specific aggravation (hyperextension, butterfly stroke) narrows progressively, examination findings (single-leg hyperextension test) and imaging (unilateral pars defect) confirm diagnosis elegantly.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 16-year-old competitive swimmer presents with insidious-onset lower back pain localized to one side of the midline. Pain worsens with backward arching or hyperextension movements. The pain is sharp and increases with high-intensity training or sprinting activities.
C2 Activity: The patient experiences pain with butterfly stroke and backstroke more than freestyle. Pain intensifies with training progression and return to competitive intensity. Prolonged sitting and forward bending are tolerated without significant symptoms. Pain is worst at end-of-day after training.
C3 History: The patient has trained competitively for 5 years with high training volume and repetitive spinal extension loading. Pain onset occurred 8 weeks ago coinciding with increased training intensity. No traumatic event or acute injury. Family history is notable for low back pain in both parents.
C4 Examination: On examination, unilateral lumbar pain is reproduced with single-leg hyperextension test on the affected side. Lumbar extension combined with rotation toward the painful side increases symptoms. Palpation reveals paraspinal muscle tenderness at the level of pain. Lower extremity neurological examination is normal.
C5 Imaging: CT scan demonstrates a unilateral pars interarticularis defect at the L5 level with intact vertebral body and disc. SPECT imaging shows increased uptake at the symptomatic level, indicating active bone stress. Plain radiographs may appear normal; advanced imaging is required for definitive visualization.
#289 Cauda Equina Syndrome Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 broad enough (bilateral leg pain/weakness fits multiple spinal conditions), C2 adds acuity and functional severity, C3-C4 build urgency with bladder loss and bilateral motor/sensory deficits, C5 provides imaging confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old male reports acute onset bilateral leg pain and progressive weakness over 48 hours. He has noticed recent loss of bladder control and numbness in the perineal area.
C2 Activity: Unable to walk more than short distances; stairs are impossible. Reports severe difficulty lifting feet off ground when walking. Symptoms worsen significantly within 10 minutes of lying flat.
C3 History: Long history of low back pain with known disc disease. Acute worsening triggered by occupational heavy lifting 2 days ago. Currently unable to voluntarily move feet or toes.
C4 Examination: Straight leg raise test reproduces radicular pain bilaterally. Lower extremity myotomes show grade 2-3 strength in ankle dorsiflexors and plantarflexors. Anal sphincter tone is diminished on rectal examination. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are absent bilaterally; perianal sensory testing shows loss of sensation in S2-S5 distribution.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows a large central disc herniation at L4-L5 level causing severe compression of multiple nerve roots in the spinal canal. There is abnormal signal change within the cord and loss of fluid around the compressed nerves.
#290 Peroneal Nerve Palsy Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately non-specific (foot drop has many causes), C3 adds crucial history (fibular head pressure/trauma), C4 provides classic pattern (dorsiflexion weakness, sensory loss in first web space), C5 confirms with MRI findings of nerve compression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old patient presents with progressive difficulty lifting the left foot during walking over the past 2 weeks. The foot feels heavy and unresponsive, with no pain. Gait has become increasingly awkward, and the patient has begun tripping.
C2 Activity: The patient reports tripping frequently and catching the left toe when walking, particularly on uneven surfaces. Climbing stairs is significantly impaired; the patient cannot dorsiflex the ankle against gravity. Running and sports are impossible. The patient describes walking with a high-stepping gait to compensate for the dorsiflexion weakness.
C3 History: Onset was acute with no preceding trauma or injury. The patient recalls a pressure point or direct blow to the lateral knee area approximately 10 days before symptom onset. Recent prolonged sitting with leg crossed at the knee and tight band-aid or wrap around the upper fibular region preceded the symptoms by several days.
C4 Examination: Ankle dorsiflexion strength is 0-1/5 on the left; plantarflexion and inversion are intact at 5/5. Great toe extension is severely weakened. The patient demonstrates sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and first web space between the great toe and second toe. Tapping over the fibular head reproduces symptoms, suggesting nerve entrapment.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the left knee and proximal tibia shows external compression of the common peroneal nerve at the fibular head level. T2-weighted imaging reveals increased signal within the nerve distal to the compression point, indicating axonal swelling and denervation changes. No structural lesion or tumor identified.
#291 Neck Tension - Desk Work Neck
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (covers tension headaches, postural neck pain), clues progress logically from activity triggers to examination findings to imaging confirmation, and final clue clinches diagnosis with imaging evidence of degenerative changes and myofascial involvement.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old office administrator reports bilateral neck and shoulder tension with occipital headaches occurring 3-4 times weekly. The discomfort is described as a dull ache with occasional sharp shooting sensations, worsening as the workday progresses.
C2 Activity: Symptoms triggered specifically by sustained computer work and forward head posturing. Patient avoids prolonged desk tasks but tolerates short activity bursts. Morning stiffness present but improves with movement. No neurological symptoms reported.
C3 History: 15-year history of sedentary desk work with recent job transition increasing daily screen time to 6+ hours. Recent workplace ergonomic changes (monitor repositioning downward). No prior neck injury, no radicular history. Stress levels elevated in past 3 months coinciding with symptom onset.
C4 Examination: Cervical rotation limited bilaterally with headache reproduction at end-range; flexion/extension restricted approximately 20 degrees. Upper trapezius demonstrates marked hypertonia with trigger points; levator scapulae tender on palpation. Scapular positioning: elevated and protracted bilaterally. Neurological testing: normal reflexes and sensation throughout upper limbs; no dermatomal deficit.
C5 Imaging: Cervical X-rays: reversal of normal lordosis with anterior head position (approximately 3 cm deviation from neutral alignment). MRI: mild degenerative disc bulging at C4-C5 and C5-C6 without neural foraminal stenosis or cord compression. No acute structural pathology. Soft tissue signal shows myofascial thickening in bilateral upper trapezius consistent with chronic muscular tension.
#292 Lower Crossed Syndrome Lower Body

Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old male with a desk-based job presents with insidious-onset lower back and hip discomfort that has gradually worsened over the past year. He reports difficulty maintaining an upright posture and finds that his symptoms worsen as the day progresses.
C2 Activity: Pain is triggered and amplified by prolonged static positions, particularly sitting at desks or in cars. Activities requiring floor-level work or rising from low surfaces provoke significant discomfort. Standing and walking provide relief. Patient notes postural 'corrections' ease symptoms within minutes.
C3 History: Onset was insidious without trauma. The patient has maintained a sedentary lifestyle for a decade with minimal structured exercise. No history of previous back injury, no progressive neurological symptoms, and no constitutional red flags. Current medications and comorbidities are unremarkable.
C4 Examination: On examination, bilateral hip flexor tightness is evident with positive Thomas test. Hip abductor weakness is noted at 4+/5 bilaterally. Lumbar erector spinae and rectus femoris are tight; abdominal core strength is poor. Pelvic tilt is anterior with excessive lumbar lordosis. Neurological examination is entirely normal.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the lumbar spine shows minimal degenerative changes without disc herniation, stenosis, or facet arthrosis of clinical significance. The spinal cord and nerve roots are not compressed. Imaging is essentially normal and does not identify structural pathology; findings support a muscular and postural etiology.
#293 Post-Spinal Fusion Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 reasonably fits multiple post-operative spine conditions, clues systematically narrow through activity limitation, surgical history, examination findings reproducing radicular symptoms, and imaging definitively identifies adjacent segment degeneration with foraminal stenosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old male reports chronic low back pain with intermittent pain radiating into the left lower leg. Symptoms began 5 months ago and have worsened despite initial post-operative improvement.
C2 Activity: The patient demonstrates severe limitations with bending forward; even slight flexion beyond 15 degrees triggers back pain. Sit-to-stand transfers are slow and guarded with pain reproduction. Walking tolerance is reduced to 10-15 minutes before requiring rest. The patient reports difficulty with rotation movements and cannot lift objects heavier than 10 pounds.
C3 History: The patient underwent fusion surgery for spondylolisthesis grade 2 with spinal stenosis. Prior to surgery, 3 years of conservative management had failed. Post-operatively, the patient had initial improvement for 3 months, followed by recurrent pain. Current medications include opioids; the patient is 4 weeks into physical therapy with minimal improvement.
C4 Examination: Lumbar flexion limited to 20 degrees; forward bending reproduction test triggers left lower extremity radicular pain at approximately L5 distribution. Straight leg raise positive on left at 60 degrees reproducing radicular symptoms. Hip flexor strength reduced to 4/5 bilaterally. Palpation over surgical scar elicits localized tenderness without warmth or swelling.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative CT imaging shows successful fusion with solid bony bridging across the L4-L5 segment. Hardware positioning is appropriate without subsidence. However, MRI reveals adjacent segment degeneration at L3-L4 with left-sided foraminal stenosis and nerve root contact. T2-weighted imaging shows disc desiccation and early stenotic changes at the level above the fusion.

C1 Complaint: A 4-year-old child presents with persistent head tilt to the right with marked cervical asymmetry. The parents report the child has always held the head in this position since infancy, though range has gradually decreased. No acute trauma or injury history.
C2 Activity: The child demonstrates difficulty looking directly forward or to the left; the head consistently returns to right lateral flexion. Forward head movement is severely limited, and the child compensates by rotating the entire trunk. Attempts to passively correct the head position cause the child to return immediately to the left-tilted posture.
C3 History: The abnormal head posture was first noted at 6 weeks of age and has remained static. There is no history of birth trauma, intrauterine constraint, or neuromuscular disorder. The child was not confined to any particular position during infancy. Recent evaluation rules out vestibular dysfunction and vision deficits.
C4 Examination: Abnormal posture first noted at 6 weeks of age and has remained unchanged. Parents deny birth trauma, intrauterine constraint, or confinement during infancy. No prior neuromuscular diagnosis or ophthalmologic problems identified.
C5 Imaging: Cervical spine ultrasound shows the right sternocleidomastoid muscle with thickened, echogenic appearance consistent with fibrosis. MRI of the neck rules out intraspinal pathology, vascular malformations, or structural abnormalities. The cervical vertebrae demonstrate normal alignment without dysplasia or asymmetry affecting cervical positioning.
#295 Popliteal Tendinopathy Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple posterior knee conditions, clues progressively narrow through activity-specific triggers (downhill running, deceleration), training history, specific examination tests, and MRI confirmation of tendon thickening and tenosynovitis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old distance runner reports posterior knee pain for 6 weeks. Pain is sharp and burning, worsens with running, and has progressively limited training volume. No acute injury event.
C2 Activity: Running downhill significantly aggravates the pain; the patient reports immediate discomfort when initiating downhill running on trails. High-speed activities and sudden deceleration reproducibly provoke sharp posterior knee pain. Walking is generally tolerated; however, descending stairs with full body weight loading triggers symptoms. Prolonged sitting with knees flexed beyond 90 degrees causes mild discomfort.
C3 History: Training error: 40% mileage increase over recent weeks with shift to hilly terrain and tempo running. Bilateral posterior knee pain noted, right > left. No prior knee pathology relevant to current complaint. Previous patellar tendinitis fully resolved 3 years ago.
C4 Examination: Resisted knee flexion with external rotation at 30° reproduces pain. Pain elicited with combined resisted ankle plantarflexion and knee flexion. Posterior knee joint line tender to palpation. Minimal effusion; soft tissue thickening palpable over popliteal tendon insertion.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the bilateral knees demonstrates thickening and signal hyperintensity within the popliteus tendon at its femoral origin, measuring approximately 7mm in cross-sectional diameter compared to normal 3-4mm. Fluid signal is noted in the popliteal sheath, indicating reactive tenosynovitis. T2-weighted imaging shows increased signal within the muscle belly compatible with myotendinous strain.
#296 Anterior Cruciate Ligament Tear Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (acute knee instability fits multiple ligamentous/structural injuries), clues methodically narrow through functional instability pattern, pivoting mechanism, clinical special tests demonstrating increased translation and pivot shift, and MRI definitively confirms complete ACL rupture with associated bone bruising.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 24-year-old soccer player experienced a non-contact pivoting injury during match play 3 weeks ago with immediate onset of acute knee pain. The player reports persistent swelling, functional instability, and apprehension during weight-bearing activities, preventing return to sport.
C2 Activity: The knee gives way unpredictably during lateral movements and pivoting maneuvers; the player demonstrates a guarded gait. The patient reports significant pain and instability when weight shifts from the injured leg or during rapid direction changes. Ascending and descending stairs are performed slowly with hand rail support. The player cannot tolerate running or cutting movements.
C3 History: The injury occurred during a sudden deceleration with planted foot and external rotation at the knee; the player felt a pop and immediate pain. The player was unable to continue playing and experienced rapid knee swelling within 2 hours. No prior knee injuries. The player reports difficulty with proprioception and describes feeling unable to trust the knee.
C4 Examination: Lachman test at 30 degrees of knee flexion demonstrates markedly increased anterior tibial translation with soft end-feel, graded as 2-3/3 for positive anterior cruciate ligament insufficiency. Anterior drawer test is positive at 90 degrees. Pivot shift test reproduces apprehension and dynamic instability with subluxation and reduction. Moderate knee effusion is palpable; pain-free range of motion is limited to 0-80 degrees.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the knee demonstrates disruption of the anterior cruciate ligament fibers with diffuse increased signal throughout the substance of the ligament, consistent with acute complete tear. T2-weighted imaging shows bone bruising at the lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral tibial plateau, confirming the mechanism of injury. No meniscal tear is identified.

Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 31-year-old athlete presents with acute severe pain at the base of the great toe and swelling following a sudden forced backward bending of the toe during sport. Weight-bearing is immediately compromised.
C2 Activity: The patient cannot participate in sport and ambulates with significant pain avoidance. Push-off activities and toe-off during gait are severely limited. Functional activities requiring toe dorsiflexion or plantarflexion force are impossible.
C3 History: The injury occurred with a forceful dorsiflexion load through the first ray when the player's planted foot was struck by an opponent, forcing the great toe into maximum extension. Immediate pain and swelling developed within minutes. The player reported a popping sensation at the time of injury. No prior first ray pathology or injury history documented.
C4 Examination: Swelling and ecchymosis present over the dorsal and medial first MTP joint. Passive dorsiflexion is restricted and painful. Plantarflexion strength is 3/5. Palpation reveals focal tenderness over the plantar plate insertion and medial ligamentous structures.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs show a small dorsal avulsion fracture at the base of the proximal phalanx with minor sesamoid malalignment. MRI demonstrates disruption of the plantar plate with surrounding edema and hemorrhage within the joint capsule.
#298 Ankylosing Spondylitis Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple chronic inflammatory spine conditions (bilateral back pain, worse on waking, systemic symptoms), clues narrow through inflammatory pattern (improve with activity, morning stiffness), progressive postural changes and systemic features, examination findings of restricted mobility and chest expansion, and imaging showing characteristic bony ankylosis and sacroiliac fusion.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old male of Northern European descent presents with an 8-year history of chronic bilateral lower back pain. The pain is insidious in onset and follows an unusual pattern: worst upon waking and improving as the day progresses. He reports accompanying systemic symptoms including persistent fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers.
C2 Activity: Marked morning stiffness lasting 90 minutes that resolves with movement and activity. Pain and stiffness paradoxically worsen with rest and overnight immobility, opposite to typical mechanical back pain patterns. Significant sleep disturbance with frequent nocturnal awakenings and night sweats.
C3 History: Progressive disease course with gradual postural changes: loss of lumbar spine flexion and increasing thoracic kyphosis. Recent episode of anterior uveitis. Positive family history of rheumatological disease in a first-degree relative. Recent rheumatological workup completed.
C4 Examination: Lumbar spine flexion is severely restricted with the patient unable to touch toes, demonstrating significant loss of forward bending (Schober test shows only 2cm expansion, normal 5cm). Spinal extension is also limited. Chest expansion is reduced to 2cm (normal greater than 5cm), indicating thoracic involvement. Occiput-to-wall distance is 12cm, indicating loss of cervical lordosis and increased thoracic kyphosis.
C5 Imaging: Lumbar spine MRI demonstrates multiple vertebral bodies bridged by bony ankylosis in a linear pattern, with complete ossification of the anterior longitudinal ligament and intervertebral discs. Sacroiliac joints show bilateral complete fusion. Active inflammatory changes visible in early disease areas on T1-weighted imaging.
#299 Thumb CMC Osteoarthritis Hand
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 2 essentially repeats Clue 1 verbatim rather than adding new information, creating redundancy; recommend restructuring Clue 2 to describe activity-specific aggravation patterns or functional limitations in detail.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old postmenopausal female presents with progressive basilar thumb pain and functional limitation in pinching and grasping activities. Pain is localized to the base of the thumb at the carpometacarpal joint and worsens with repetitive gripping.
C2 Activity: A 58-year-old postmenopausal female presents with progressive pain at the base of the thumb, worse with gripping and pinching activities.
C3 History: Morning stiffness that improves with warmth and gentle activity. Demonstrates 3/5 thumb opposition strength and reduced adduction strength on examination.
C4 Examination: The carpometacarpal joint demonstrates swelling and bony enlargement with palpable osteophytes. Active thumb opposition is weak and limited to 3/5 strength. Abduction and adduction strength are reduced. Grinding sensation is palpable during passive thumb motion. A positive grind test reproduces pain with axial loading and rotation of the first metacarpal. No overlying inflammation or swelling of soft tissues noted.
C5 Imaging: Hand radiographs demonstrate significant first carpometacarpal joint osteoarthritis with joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and subluxation of the metacarpal base. Characteristic degenerative changes include marginal osteophytes creating a squared-off appearance. Plain films also show degenerative changes in the scaphotrapezial joint, consistent with grade 3-4 osteoarthritis in Eaton-Littler classification.
#300 Calcific Tendinitis Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough for multiple rotator cuff conditions, clues progress through activity limitations (overhead loss), inflammatory pain pattern with preserved passive ROM, examination findings pointing to supraspinatus involvement, and imaging definitively showing intra-tendinous calcification with resorption phase characteristics.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old female presents with acute-onset sharp shoulder pain that began 1-2 weeks ago without any remembered injury. Pain is severe, poorly responsive to analgesics, and significantly limits arm use. She recalls some mild discomfort in the weeks prior.
C2 Activity: Active shoulder abduction and forward flexion are severely limited beyond 40°, with inability to perform overhead tasks. Significant night pain disrupts sleep. Passive range of motion, however, remains relatively preserved.
C3 History: No significant past medical history or shoulder surgery. Recent cervical imaging was normal, ruling out cervical radiculopathy as source. Patient is otherwise generally healthy.
C4 Examination: Passive range of motion of the shoulder is relatively preserved compared to severe limitation in active range. Abduction lag is marked, with inability to maintain arm in abduction against gravity. The 'empty can' test for supraspinatus is positive with pain reproduction. Infraspinatus strength testing reveals 3/5 strength. Palpation over the rotator cuff insertion demonstrates point tenderness. No joint instability appreciated.
C5 Imaging: Shoulder imaging reveals a discrete calcific deposit within the supraspinatus tendon with surrounding inflammatory changes and edema. The calcification is intra-tendinous and demonstrates early resorption phase characteristics on advanced imaging.
#301 Tibialis Anterior Tendinopathy Lower Leg
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C5 merely repeats C4 examination findings verbatim instead of providing imaging data, breaking the clue progression entirely.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 26-year-old runner presents with anterior lower leg pain on the medial tibial border, localized over the anterior tibialis muscle belly. Pain is activity-related, worsening with running and walking on hard surfaces, with gradual onset over 4 weeks.
C2 Activity: A 26-year-old runner presents with anterior lower leg pain that began gradually over 4 weeks. Pain worsens with running and walking on hard surfaces and improves with rest.
C3 History: Running and high-impact activities reproduce sharp anterior tibial pain within 2-3 minutes of onset. Dorsiflexion-loading maneuvers, such as uphill walking or stair climbing, consistently trigger discomfort. Pain is reduced by rest and elevation.
C4 Examination: Direct palpation over the anterior tibialis muscle belly elicits marked tenderness and palpable muscle edema. Resisted ankle dorsiflexion reproduces pain, testing at 4+/5 strength due to pain inhibition. Passive plantarflexion stretch of the anterior tibialis triggers discomfort. Lower leg compartment firmness is normal without compartment syndrome features. Crepitus is noted with muscle activation.
C5 Imaging: Marked tenderness and palpable swelling over the anterior tibialis muscle belly. Resisted ankle dorsiflexion reproduces pain with strength testing at 4+/5 due to pain inhibition. Passive plantarflexion elicits discomfort. No signs of acute compartment syndrome. Crepitus noted with muscle contraction.
#302 Metatarsalgia Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad, each subsequent clue adds distinct clinical information, and C5 imaging findings appropriately narrow the differential.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old recreational runner presents with forefoot pain that worsens toward the end of the day. The pain is localized to the plantar aspect beneath the metatarsal heads, with no recent trauma or swelling reported.
C2 Activity: Pain is significantly aggravated by prolonged standing, walking on hard surfaces, and activities requiring push-off (running, dancing). Relief occurs with rest, elevation, and wearing supportive footwear with cushioning.
C3 History: The patient recently increased running volume by 30% two months ago and has worn minimalist shoes for three years. They report high arches and a history of bunions in their family, with no previous lower limb injuries.
C4 Examination: Palpation directly over the metatarsal heads (particularly 2nd-4th) reproduces pain. Metatarsophalangeal joint extension is painless. Foot posture shows increased arch height with no callusing or visible deformity in the interdigital spaces.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs show no fracture lines or bony pathology. Ultrasound of the forefoot demonstrates thickening of the plantar fascia in the metatarsal region without discrete mass or bursal distension.
#303 Sacroiliac Joint Dysfunction Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from complaint through activity triggers, postural history, specific SIJ examination tests, and confirmatory imaging without premature narrowing.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old office worker presents with unilateral pain in the posterior pelvis and buttock region that radiates occasionally to the lateral thigh. The pain pattern is asymmetric and reproducible with certain movements.
C2 Activity: Pain is aggravated by prolonged sitting (particularly one side more than the other), single-leg stance, and climbing stairs. Relief is obtained by lying down, changing position frequently, and avoiding sustained forward bending.
C3 History: The patient has been working from home for six months with a suboptimal desk setup and sits with a crossed-leg posture regularly. No history of trauma, pregnancy-related symptoms, or previous low back pain episodes.
C4 Examination: Positive Patrick's test on the symptomatic side with pain localized to the posterior pelvis. Gaenslen's test reproduces pain. Single-leg stance on the painful side causes posterior pelvic pain. Palpation over the SIJ reveals tenderness. Hip ROM is preserved and painless.
C5 Imaging: Pelvic X-rays reveal normal joint alignment without signs of osteoarthritis. MRI of the sacroiliac joints shows mild inflammatory changes in the synovial portion with no erosive changes or ankylosis.
#304 Frozen Hip Hip
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C4 and C5 overlap significantly with both describing ROM restrictions and imaging findings; C5 should focus purely on imaging interpretation rather than repeating examination data.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old retired accountant reports progressive hip pain and stiffness over eight months. Pain is located deep in the hip and groin region.
C2 Activity: The patient reports difficulty with activities requiring hip flexion and rotation: putting on shoes, climbing stairs, and rising from a seated position are now significantly limited.
C3 History: The patient has no history of hip trauma, falls, or previous hip pathology. They are sedentary post-retirement and report gradual onset of stiffness without preceding acute pain or swelling. No fevers or systemic symptoms.
C4 Examination: Hip flexion limited to 70 degrees, internal rotation severely restricted (10 degrees), external rotation limited to 20 degrees. FABER test is restricted and uncomfortable. Scour test elicits deep hip pain. Hip strength testing is difficult due to pain-limited ROM.
C5 Imaging: Plain hip radiographs show joint space narrowing and marginal osteophytes consistent with osteoarthritis. MRI demonstrates cartilage loss particularly in the anterior weight-bearing surfaces with subtle synovial inflammation and capsular thickening.
#305 Lumbar Spinal Stenosis Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent clue progression with distinct complaint, activity pattern, red flag screening, neurological findings, and definitive imaging that clinches the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 65-year-old former manual worker presents with an insidious onset of lower back pain and progressive right lower extremity pain over 8 months. The patient describes radiating pain into the right calf and foot associated with sensory changes, limiting walking capacity to less than a block.
C2 Activity: The patient reports that prolonged standing and walking exacerbate symptoms, while sitting and lying down provide relief. Forward bending and activities requiring spinal extension are particularly troublesome. Symptoms are reproducible with positional changes and activity level, with a clear pattern of claudication-like presentation.
C3 History: Long history of intermittent lower back pain over 20+ years, with progressive worsening in the last 2 years. No acute injury precipitated the current symptoms. Patient denies bladder/bowel dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or sudden leg weakness. Sleep is disrupted by nighttime symptoms.
C4 Examination: Right ankle reflex is absent (0) and patellar reflex diminished (1+). Sensory testing reveals decreased sensation in the right L5-S1 dermatome. Right hip and knee extension strength is mildly reduced at 4/5. Femoral stretch test is negative, but SLR on the right reproduces radicular pain.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the lumbar spine shows moderate to severe stenosis at L4-L5 with right-sided lateral recess stenosis and intervertebral disc herniation. There is compression of the right L5 nerve root with associated edema. Degenerative changes and facet joint arthrosis are evident at this level.
#306 Scleroderma Whole Body
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Scleroderma is a systemic rheumatological condition far outside typical physiotherapy scope of practice; this puzzle is inappropriate for a physio diagnostic game regardless of technical quality.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 55-year-old female presents with progressive tightness and thickening of the skin diffusely across the body, particularly affecting the hands, face, and trunk. She reports difficulty with facial expression and hand dexterity that has worsened over two years.
C2 Activity: A 55-year-old female presents with progressive skin tightness and hardening that started in her hands two years ago and has spread proximally. She reports difficulty with fine motor tasks and facial expression. Associated with long-standing episodes of finger blanching in cold weather.
C3 History: Patient reports progressive dysphagia with solid foods and new-onset heartburn. Exertional fatigue is present even with light activities. Breathing becomes labored on climbing stairs.
C4 Examination: Skin shows notable thickening and tightening in a symmetric distribution from hands to proximal forearms, face, and trunk. Sclerodactyly is present bilaterally. Finger extension and hip/knee flexion ROM is significantly reduced bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Skin examination reveals marked thickening and induration with loss of normal wrinkles on dorsal hands bilaterally, and tapering of fingers (sclerodactyly). Tight mask-like facies noted. Significant ROM loss: finger extension limited to 30°, hip/knee flexion restricted. Peripheral pulses diminished; BP elevated in lower extremities.
#307 Pleural Effusion Lungs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Pleural effusion requires medical diagnosis and intervention beyond physiotherapy scope; additionally, C5 imaging findings (tracheal deviation, stony dullness) essentially diagnose the condition making it too narrow for a fair guessing game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old male with a 10-pack-year smoking history presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion and right-sided chest pain that worsens with deep breathing. He reports a two-month gradual decline in exercise tolerance without acute trauma.
C2 Activity: Walking flat ground and climbing stairs trigger shortness of breath. The patient has difficulty with deep breathing exercises due to pleuritic pain. Lying on the affected side increases discomfort; left side lying provides some relief.
C3 History: Patient experiences orthopnea and needs multiple pillows. Cough is non-productive. Unable to lie flat; must recline at 45 degrees or greater. Fatigue is disproportionate to exertion level.
C4 Examination: Decreased breath sounds and percussion dullness over the right lung base. No crackles or wheezes appreciated. Respiratory expansion asymmetric with right side reduced. Cardiovascular exam unremarkable with no peripheral edema.
C5 Imaging: Tracheal deviation to the left. Stony dullness to percussion over right lower 2/3 of lung field with absent tactile fremitus. Breath sounds completely absent below the right 6th intercostal space. No clubbing, no cyanosis. JVP normal.
#308 Pes Anserine Bursitis Knee
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C4 and C5 are nearly identical repetitions of examination findings with no distinction between physical examination and imaging, and C5 lacks actual imaging data (ultrasound/MRI findings).
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 41-year-old administrative assistant reports anteromedial knee pain and localized swelling just below the knee joint. Pain has gradually worsened over three months with an achy quality that is activity-dependent.
C2 Activity: Pain is aggravated by ascending and descending stairs, prolonged sitting with knee flexion, and direct pressure on the medial knee. Symptoms improve with rest, ice application, and straight-leg activities. Running and jumping significantly worsen pain.
C3 History: The patient recently started a gym membership three months ago focusing on leg press and squat exercises performed two to three times weekly. No history of knee injury, and they sit at a desk for eight hours daily. No prior knee pain or swelling.
C4 Examination: Tender swelling at the pes anserine insertion point (medial tibia below joint line). McMurray test is negative and Lachman test is negative. Resisted knee extension causes localized anteromedial tenderness. No effusion or ligamentous laxity appreciated.
C5 Imaging: Tender swelling at pes anserine insertion point on medial tibia below joint line. McMurray and Lachman tests negative. Pain reproduced with resisted knee flexion (hamstrings/gracilis contraction) and on direct palpation of bursal site. No effusion or ligamentous laxity.
#309 Runner's Nipple Chest
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough for multiple chest wall irritations, each clue adds specific activity/material triggers and examination findings, and C5 appropriately excludes serious pathology via imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 29-year-old male marathon runner reports progressive anterior chest wall pain localized to the nipple region, with associated skin irritation. Symptoms began during long training runs and have worsened with increased mileage.
C2 Activity: Pain is triggered by running longer distances (>8 miles) and is significantly worse when wearing cotton shirts. Symptom relief occurs with proper sports bra usage, petroleum jelly application, or running in cold weather. Pain-free walking and cycling occur.
C3 History: The patient has trained for marathons for five years without previous nipple issues but recently switched to lower-quality technical shirts. Increased mileage over the past four weeks correlates with symptom onset. No history of dermatologic conditions or trauma.
C4 Examination: Nipple area shows erythema and excoriation with no purulent drainage or systemic infection signs. Superficial skin ulceration visible at the nipple tip. No masses, discharge, or lymphadenopathy appreciated. Vital signs normal.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray unremarkable (normal cardiac silhouette, clear lung fields) — confirms mechanical/superficial etiology and excludes cardiac or pulmonary involvement. Clinical diagnosis based on physical examination findings.
#310 Stress Fracture Limbs
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong progression from complaint through training history, specific examination findings, and imaging progression that shows how early stress fractures may appear normal on plain films before CT/MRI clarification.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 22-year-old college cross-country runner reports localized, sharp pain in the lower leg (anterior tibia or fibula region) that worsens progressively over three weeks. Pain is worse with running and limiting athletic participation.
C2 Activity: Running, jumping, and high-impact activities trigger sharp pain that can be severe enough to cause limping. Walking and non-impact activities (swimming, cycling) are relatively pain-free. Pain increases with morning stiffness and improves minimally with rest.
C3 History: The athlete increased training intensity and volume by 50% over the past month preparing for conference championships. They run on hard surfaces (track and pavement) exclusively. No previous lower limb injuries or stress fractures. Training increased from 40 to 60 miles weekly.
C4 Examination: Palpable point tenderness over the anteromedial tibia or lateral fibula corresponding to pain location. Single-leg hop test elicits sharp pain. Muscular flexibility normal; no calf or anterior compartment swelling. Neurovascular status intact with brisk reflexes.
C5 Imaging: Initial plain radiographs may appear normal early in the injury. CT scan demonstrates focal cortical thickening and periosteal new bone formation at the fracture site without a complete fracture line. MRI shows marrow edema and endosteal thickening.
#311 Flat Feet in Children Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from nonspecific foot deformity to definitive flexible flatfoot diagnosis; each clue adds distinct clinical information without redundancy.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 4-year-old boy is referred by his parents who note that his feet appear to have collapsed arches when standing. The child reports occasional mild foot discomfort after prolonged activity but is not significantly symptomatic or limited functionally.
C2 Activity: The child is fully ambulatory and participates in all playground activities without limitation or observable gait deviation. No pain with normal play or walking for typical childhood durations.
C3 History: Deformity noted since infancy with no antecedent trauma, swelling, or progressive pain. Parents have flexible flatfeet and remain fully asymptomatic and functional into adulthood.
C4 Examination: In standing, the medial longitudinal arch is absent bilaterally with the entire foot contacting the ground. When sitting or suspended (not weight-bearing), the arch reappears normally. No rigidity, tenderness, or toe deformities are present.
C5 Imaging: Weight-bearing X-rays of the foot show increased talocalcaneal angle and decreased talonavicular angle consistent with flexible pes planus. No tarsal coalition, abnormal bone development, or tuberosity enlargement. Arch reconstitution evident on non-weight-bearing views.
#312 Cervical Arterial Dysfunction Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Well-structured escalation from vague neuro symptoms to specific vertebral artery compression; C4 provocation test and C5 dynamic imaging solidly clinch the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 51-year-old teacher reports intermittent episodes of dizziness, tinnitus, and blurred vision occurring during cervical rotation to one side. Episodes last 10-30 seconds and resolve quickly with position change.
C2 Activity: Patient reports difficulty with overhead tasks at work, particularly sustained looking upward during classroom instruction. Symptoms worse with driving (head checks) and confined spaces where rotation is frequent. Symptom-free when reclining or neck immobilized.
C3 History: Two-year history of neck pain and progressive bilateral stiffness from desk work. Motor vehicle accident one month ago with significant deceleration-type injury; initially treated conservatively. Symptoms of dizziness began insidiously 3 weeks post-accident and have progressively worsened with head movements. No prior episodes of vertigo or neurologic deficits.
C4 Examination: Cervical active ROM limited in rotation and extension bilaterally. Dizziness and blurred vision reliably reproduced with combined cervical extension and ipsilateral rotation (positive vertebral artery provocation test). Nystagmus present during maneuver. Upper extremity strength, reflexes, and sensation intact. No focal neurologic deficits. Postural stability normal in supine.
C5 Imaging: Cervical spine MRI demonstrates osteophytic changes and disc bulging at C5-C6 and C6-C7 with foraminal stenosis and lateral recess narrowing on the right side. Vertebral artery appears patent on angiography. However, dynamic imaging (MRA with head rotation) reveals significant compression of the right vertebral artery lumen during ipsilateral rotation, consistent with extrinsic vascular compromise from osteophytes.
#313 Motor Neurone Disease CNS
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 and C2 are somewhat redundant (both emphasize hand weakness and fine motor loss); could strengthen C2 by adding upper motor neuron signs or cognitive preservation earlier.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged adult presents with progressive weakness in the hands affecting fine motor tasks. The patient reports difficulty with activities including writing and buttoning clothes over the past several months.
C2 Activity: Patient reports progressive loss of grip strength and fine dexterity, now affecting ability to write and perform occupational tasks. Functional decline is functionally limiting but patient remains cognitively intact and independent in self-care.
C3 History: Symptoms began focally in one region and gradually spread to involve other body areas. The patient reports no sensory changes, preserved cognition, and no significant trauma or infection history. Progressive nature over 6-12 months noted.
C4 Examination: Examination reveals asymmetric weakness predominantly in intrinsic hand muscles with visible muscle fasciculations in forearms and shoulders. Muscle bulk is reduced in affected regions. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk. Sensory examination remains intact throughout.
C5 Imaging: Needle EMG demonstrates acute denervation potentials with fibrillations and positive sharp waves in weak muscles, alongside reduced motor unit recruitment. MRI of cervical spine and brain are structurally normal with no evidence of compression or signal abnormality.
#314 Retrocalcaneal Bursitis Ankle
✓ READY
Verdict: Good specificity progression from posterior heel pain to bursal palpation to imaging confirmation; mechanical trigger and typical presentation are well-integrated.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A recreational athlete presents with localised pain in the posterior heel region, particularly where the shoe heel contacts the heel. Pain has developed gradually over recent weeks.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot tolerate firm-heeled shoes and struggles with prolonged walking. Pain worsens significantly with activities requiring repetitive ankle plantarflexion or calf loading.
C3 History: Recent change in training volume or shoe type triggered symptoms. No history of acute injury. Athlete reports chronic irritation from heel counter pressure, suggesting gradual mechanical irritation rather than single insult.
C4 Examination: On examination, swelling is palpable in the space between the Achilles tendon and calcaneus. Tenderness is elicited with direct palpation of this region. Pain is reproduced with passive plantarflexion and when the bursa is compressed against underlying bone.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound imaging demonstrates anechoic fluid collection posterior to the calcaneus, between the Achilles tendon and bone. Bursitis is confirmed with bursal distension. MRI may show associated calcaneal bone prominence (Haglund exostosis) contributing to compression.
#315 Coccydynia Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clean narrative arc from sitting-related pain to coccygeal point tenderness to imaging abnormality; clues add distinct information without overlap.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports posterior lower back and buttock pain that worsens with sitting. Symptom onset was recent. The pain is described as localised and constant.
C2 Activity: Patient reports inability to sit for more than 15–20 minutes without severe discomfort. Difficulty rising from a chair is pronounced. Patient resorts to standing or lying down frequently throughout the day.
C3 History: Patient reports no recent fall or direct blow to the buttock area. Symptom onset coincided with a new job requiring 8+ hours daily sitting. No prior episodes of similar pain. Symptoms have steadily worsened over the past 4 weeks.
C4 Examination: Palpation of the coccyx elicits point tenderness. Coccygeal mobilisation (gentle anteroposterior movement) reproduces the patient's familiar pain. Hip flexion and rotation are full and painless. Straight leg raise is negative bilaterally. No lower limb neurological signs.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs reveal coccygeal deviation, angulation, or fracture of the coccygeal segments. Dynamic imaging in sitting and standing positions demonstrates abnormal coccygeal mobility. MRI may show associated soft tissue inflammation around the coccyx.
#316 Cardiac Rehabilitation Post-MI Heart
✓ READY
Verdict: Comprehensive clinical story with ECG/troponin confirmation, functional testing, and echocardiographic verification; appropriate for physio students managing post-cardiac cases.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged to older adult reports marked fatigue and breathlessness with minimal physical exertion, significantly worse than before a recent acute cardiac event.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot walk more than one flight of stairs without severe dyspnea and chest discomfort. Previously could climb two flights without symptoms.
C3 History: ECG at time of acute event showed ST elevation in anterior leads with peak troponin elevation confirming full-thickness MI. Revascularization was performed via PCI with stent placement. Patient completed basic cardiac rehab phase but remains severely deconditioned.
C4 Examination: Six-minute walk test: patient achieves 280 m with dyspnea at 4 minutes (distance well below predicted for age). At rest: heart rate 92 bpm, BP 138/88 mmHg. No peripheral edema. Lung bases clear.
C5 Imaging: Transthoracic echocardiogram: left ventricular ejection fraction 35% (severely reduced). Anterior wall akinesia with dyskinetic segment at apex consistent with anterior MI territory. Mild mitral regurgitation secondary to LV dilatation.
#317 Post-Polio Syndrome CNS
✓ READY
Verdict: Distinctive history of childhood polio with decades-long stability followed by late-onset decline gives strong diagnostic flavor; each clue refines suspicion logically.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An adult reports progressive muscle weakness and fatigue that has worsened over the past few years. The patient recalls a severe neurological illness in childhood requiring hospitalization and prolonged recovery.
C2 Activity: The patient can no longer perform tasks requiring sustained exertion. For example, they tire rapidly with walking or repetitive arm movements, though rest does not fully resolve fatigue between days. Previously able activities now become impossible within hours.
C3 History: The patient had achieved near-complete functional independence following the childhood illness, remaining stable for several decades. Worsening began insidiously 2-5 years ago without new infection, injury, or clear trigger. New joint pain and muscle cramps have also emerged.
C4 Examination: Weakness is confined to muscles originally affected by polio and is asymmetrically distributed. Fasciculations are visible in affected muscles. Sensation is intact. Cranial nerves and unaffected limbs are normal. Reflexes are brisk in healthy limbs but diminished or absent in weakened muscles.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging of affected limbs demonstrates muscle atrophy in the distribution of previous poliomyelitis. Electromyography reveals abnormal motor unit action potentials consistent with chronic motor neuron loss. EMG may show evidence of denervation in formerly affected muscles.
#318 Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo Vestibular
✓ READY
Verdict: Textbook presentation with position-triggered vertigo, Dix-Hallpike reproduction, and characteristic nystagmus pattern; imaging appropriately excludes dangerous differentials.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient, often older adult, presents with brief episodes of rotatory vertigo triggered specifically by changes in head position. Nausea accompanies the episodes, and onset may be sudden or gradual.
C2 Activity: Episodes are consistently triggered by particular head movements such as looking upward, rolling over in bed, or rapid position changes including bending down. The patient develops avoidance behaviour, limiting functional activities.
C3 History: Symptom onset is often acute, sometimes preceded by head trauma or upper respiratory viral illness, though no clear precipitant is identified in many cases. The condition may recur intermittently. Hearing changes or tinnitus may be absent.
C4 Examination: The Dix-Hallpike maneuver reproduces vertigo with a characteristic latency of 2-10 seconds. Nystagmus appears with similar delay and typically shows rotatory and vertical components. The positional nystagmus fatigues with repeated maneuvers. Supine roll test may be positive.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the brain and internal auditory canals is normal, excluding retrocochlear or central lesions. Caloric testing shows reduced vestibular function on the affected side, consistent with peripheral vestibular origin.
#319 Exercise-Induced Bronchoconstriction Lungs
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear trigger (high-intensity exercise in cold/dry air), normal resting exam contrasted with post-exercise findings, and diagnostic spirometry decline make this clinically coherent and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young athlete reports chest tightness and difficulty breathing that develops during or shortly after intense training sessions. Symptoms resolve quickly with rest.
C2 Activity: Symptoms are reliably triggered by sustained high-intensity aerobic exercise, particularly in cold or dry air. The patient notes wheezing during these episodes.
C3 History: Symptom onset correlates with competitive training intensity rather than general fitness level. The patient may have a background of allergic rhinitis, atopic dermatitis, or mild asthma. Baseline lung function is typically normal between episodes.
C4 Examination: Resting respiratory examination and lung auscultation are completely normal. Baseline spirometry is normal. However, examination performed immediately post-exercise reveals wheezing and audible breathing difficulty.
C5 Imaging: Post-exercise spirometry shows FEV1 decline of >12% from baseline, measured 5-10 minutes after standardised treadmill running. Methacholine bronchial challenge test is positive. Resting spirometry between episodes remains within normal limits.
#320 Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo Vestibular
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Duplicate of puzzle 317 (index 318); only minor variation in C5 imaging detail (VNG added) makes this redundant for a game rotation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient, often older adult, presents with brief episodes of rotatory vertigo triggered specifically by changes in head position. Nausea accompanies the episodes, and onset may be sudden or gradual.
C2 Activity: Episodes are triggered by head movements, particularly extension and rotation. The patient reports severe dizziness lasting seconds to minutes, with fear of falling leading to reduced head mobility and activity avoidance.
C3 History: Symptom onset is often acute, sometimes preceded by head trauma or upper respiratory viral illness, though no clear precipitant is identified in many cases. The condition may recur intermittently. Hearing changes or tinnitus may be absent.
C4 Examination: The Dix-Hallpike maneuver reproduces vertigo with a characteristic latency of 2-10 seconds. Nystagmus appears with similar delay and typically shows rotatory and vertical components. The positional nystagmus fatigues with repeated maneuvers. Supine roll test may be positive.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the brain and internal auditory meatus is normal, excluding central vertigo, structural lesions, or retrocochlear pathology. Videonystagmography (VNG) during Dix-Hallpike shows characteristic nystagmus with rotatory and vertical components, confirming peripheral origin.
#321 Anxiety-Related Hyperventilation Respiratory System
✓ READY
Verdict: Good progression from nonspecific dyspnea to anxiety triggers to psychological history to reproducible hyperventilation exam to confirmatory capnography; clue 1 broad enough for multiple respiratory/cardiac conditions.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young adult presents with recurrent episodes of acute dyspnoea and chest discomfort that occur without obvious physical exertion. The patient reports sudden onset of breathing difficulty.
C2 Activity: Episodes are often triggered by stress or anxiety-provoking situations. The patient demonstrates rapid, shallow breathing pattern. Symptoms include dizziness, perioral paresthesia, and chest tightness that resolve spontaneously.
C3 History: Symptoms recur unpredictably over weeks to months. Patient has been investigated for cardiac causes with normal results. Known history of generalised anxiety disorder diagnosed 2 years ago. No recent life changes or trauma; symptoms worse during work pressure.
C4 Examination: During observation of an acute episode: rapid respiratory rate (24-28 breaths/min), shallow breathing pattern. Manual hyperventilation for 2 minutes reproduces exact symptom complex including perioral tingling and lightheadedness. Chest auscultation clear bilaterally. Heart rate elevated but regular; blood pressure normal. No focal neurological deficits.
C5 Imaging: Capnography during a triggered episode shows ETCO2 of 28 mmHg (normal 35-45 mmHg), with rapid, shallow waveform. 12-lead ECG and transthoracic echocardiogram normal; troponin negative. Chest X-ray shows no acute cardiopulmonary pathology.
#322 Cauda Equina Syndrome Lumbar Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 2 explicitly mentions bowel/bladder dysfunction and urinary retention, which are nearly pathognomonic for CES and eliminate most other diagnoses far too early; clue 1 should be broader.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged adult presents with acute onset severe lower back pain and bilateral leg pain. Symptoms began suddenly without prior warning.
C2 Activity: Patient reports progressive difficulty with normal bowel and bladder function. Urinary retention and loss of normal continence control are emerging.
C3 History: Prior history of intermittent lower back complaints and spinal imaging showing disc bulges. Current event followed heavy lifting 12 hours ago with very rapid deterioration of symptoms.
C4 Examination: Severe bilateral lower extremity weakness affecting hip flexion and knee extension. Saddle region sensory loss (L4-S4 dermatomes). Reduced anal sphincter tone on digital examination.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging of lumbar spine demonstrates massive central disc herniation at a single or multiple levels, compressing the cauda equina. MRI shows displacement of neural elements and confirms level of compression requiring urgent decompression.
#323 Post-Thoracotomy Rehabilitation Chest
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 3 stating 'thoracotomy was performed weeks prior' is slightly too direct; framing as 'recent chest surgery' would preserve challenge while maintaining accuracy.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports chest wall pain with exertion and difficulty with upper limb tasks. Pain is worse with deep breathing and coughing. Recovery is in the early weeks post-operatively.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot reach overhead or behind back. Cannot perform bed mobility independently. Fatigue limits walking to <100 metres. Activities of daily living such as dressing and hygiene are severely restricted.
C3 History: Thoracotomy was performed weeks prior for lung pathology. The patient is progressing through post-operative rehabilitation.
C4 Examination: Chest wall scar is firm and adherent. Scapular dyskinesis present bilaterally. Breathing pattern shallow and predominantly upper-chest; diaphragmatic excursion reduced on palpation. Intercostal muscles tender and restricted. Shoulder external rotation markedly reduced compared to internal rotation.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray shows stable lung parenchyma with no pneumothorax or pleural effusion. Rib cage alignment normal. Healing of incision line is complete without evidence of dehiscence.
#324 Cervical Disc Herniation Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits many neck pain conditions, progression is logical (movement patterns → history → radiculopathy signs → imaging confirmation), and Spurling's test in clue 4 appropriately narrows without fully defining diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged adult presents with neck pain that radiates into one arm. The pain has developed gradually or acutely following minor trauma or activity.
C2 Activity: Pain is reproducibly triggered by certain neck movements, particularly extension and ipsilateral rotation. The patient reports functional limitation with arm activities and difficulty maintaining posture.
C3 History: Long hours at desk work. Symptoms worse with repeated neck extension during driving or overhead tasks. No significant prior cervical pathology, but occasional stiffness before onset.
C4 Examination: On examination, neck movement is limited, particularly extension and ipsilateral side-bending. Upper extremity weakness is demonstrated in a dermatomal distribution. Diminished reflexes may be present. Spurling's test reproduces radicular pain.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging of cervical spine demonstrates disc herniation at the affected level with nerve root compression and narrowing of the neural foramen. The herniated material displaces neural structures.
#325 Tibial Nerve Palsy Lower Limb
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 2 describes foot drop and dorsiflexion difficulty, but tibial nerve palsy causes plantarflexion weakness and inversion deficit, not foot drop; the clinical presentation is anatomically contradictory and would confuse learners.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old male presents with acute unilateral lower leg weakness and foot drop that developed over several days. He reports burning foot pain, worse on the affected side, and difficulty with foot control.
C2 Activity: Walking and standing significantly limited due to inability to dorsiflex the foot, requiring high-stepping gait pattern. Climbing stairs and walking on uneven surfaces are particularly difficult.
C3 History: Three weeks prior, the patient underwent knee arthroscopy for meniscal repair. No perineural anesthetic blocks were used. He reports no diabetes, peripheral vascular disease, or previous lower limb neuropathy. No preceding trauma or compression injury.
C4 Examination: Motor examination shows dorsiflexion strength reduced on affected side (1/5) with plantarflexion intact at 5/5. Sensory testing reveals diminished sensation in the sole and medial foot (posterior tibial nerve distribution) on the affected side only.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows signal intensity changes in the tibial nerve consistent with compression or ischemia. Ultrasound demonstrates focal nerve swelling at the site of injury. Electrodiagnostic studies confirm denervation pattern consistent with complete tibial nerve involvement.
#326 Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder - Physical Rehabilitation General
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This conflates PTSD (psychological diagnosis) with post-traumatic orthopedic rehabilitation; the puzzle structure does not clearly separate psychological from physical findings, making it unclear what diagnosis a physio student should guess.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports physical pain and stiffness following a significant injury event several months ago. Symptom severity fluctuates unpredictably.
C2 Activity: Patient avoids certain positions and movements that were present during the injury. Reports difficulty progressing rehabilitation exercises independently.
C3 History: Injury occurred following a motor vehicle accident 4 months ago. Patient underwent initial surgery and has completed basic wound healing. Reports interrupted sleep and heightened startle response since the incident.
C4 Examination: Visible muscle guarding and bracing during passive range of motion testing. Heart rate elevation noted when patient attempts previously injured region. Pain catastrophizing observed during functional tasks.
C5 Imaging: CT imaging reveals healed fracture of left tibia with mild post-traumatic osteoarthritis at fracture site. Psychological screening questionnaires (PCL-5, GAD-7) show elevated scores consistent with trauma-related anxiety disorder with somatic features.
#327 Legg-Calvé-Perthes Disease Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear age-appropriate presentation, logical progression from nonspecific hip pain to movement restrictions to imaging findings, clue 1 broad enough to include other pediatric hip conditions.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A school-aged child presents with hip pain or limp that has developed insidiously. The pain may radiate to the knee, and the child demonstrates functional limitation with walking.
C2 Activity: The child reports pain with hip abduction and internal rotation movements. Difficulty with climbing stairs, getting in/out of car seats, and sitting cross-legged. Antalgic gait is visible.
C3 History: Insidious onset over weeks to months. Male child. No preceding trauma, infection, or surgery. Parents report gradual onset of limp without clear cause. No systemic illness or relevant medical history.
C4 Examination: On examination, hip internal rotation and abduction are limited. The Trendelenburg sign is positive. Hip pain is elicited with internal rotation. Muscle weakness and atrophy may be present.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate femoral head flattening, fragmentation, or widening of the joint space. MRI shows femoral head necrosis and loss of epiphyseal blood supply. Sequential imaging documents progressive changes.
#328 Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Good differential breadth in clue 1, each clue adds specificity (activity triggers → gradual onset pattern → anatomical signs → imaging confirmation), and Tinel sign in clue 4 appropriately narrows without fully determining diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged adult presents with pain in the region behind the ankle and on the sole of the foot. The pain has developed gradually and is reproducible with activity.
C2 Activity: Pain is aggravated by prolonged standing or walking, particularly in shoes that compress the medial ankle. The patient experiences pain relief with rest and elevation.
C3 History: No significant trauma history. Symptom onset correlates with increased activity, standing tolerance, or recent ankle swelling from unrelated cause. No prior ankle surgery or chronic ankle instability.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is elicited with palpation posterior to the medial malleolus. The Tinel sign is positive at the tarsal tunnel. Pain is reproduced with passive foot eversion. Sensory changes may be present on the sole of the foot.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows flexor retinaculum thickening and posterior tibial nerve compression at tarsal tunnel level with associated nerve edema and T2 signal hyperintensity. Ultrasound may show reduced nerve mobility with ankle plantarflexion.
#329 Biceps Tendinopathy Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 covers multiple anterior shoulder pain conditions, progression through activity type and provocative tests is logical, imaging findings in clue 5 are definitive without being overly specific in earlier clues.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged adult presents with pain in the anterior shoulder region at the shoulder joint. The pain develops during shoulder movements or activities requiring arm lifting.
C2 Activity: Pain is triggered by overhead activities and activities involving repetitive elbow flexion. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with lifting or reaching activities.
C3 History: The patient has no significant trauma history. Pain has developed gradually with overhead activities. Recent increase in activity level may be contributory.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the bicipital groove. Speed's test reproduces anterior shoulder pain. Yergason's test is positive with pain in the bicipital groove. Shoulder range of motion is usually preserved.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes within the tendon, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation. Tendon calibre abnormalities are evident.
#330 Cervical Spondylosis Cervical Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 5 mentions spinal cord compression and myelopathy changes, which shift diagnosis slightly toward myelopathic vs. non-myelopathic spondylosis; consider whether this level of specificity is appropriate or if imaging should be more general (degenerative changes).
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An older adult presents with chronic neck pain and progressive stiffness. The patient reports difficulty with neck movement and occasional radiation into the arm.
C2 Activity: Patient struggles with overhead reaching and turning the head while driving. Prolonged reading or computer work triggers exacerbations. Symptoms are inconsistent day-to-day but generally worse with cooler weather or after poor sleep.
C3 History: Neck pain began in the patient's 50s after years of desk work. Symptoms have slowly worsened over the past decade. The patient has had 2–3 episodes of sudden sharp pain with arm tingling that resolved within weeks. No significant trauma or whiplash history.
C4 Examination: Cervical extension is severely limited (10–15° loss). Lateral rotation to both sides is restricted. Upper limb strength is 5/5 throughout. Grip strength is mildly reduced and asymmetrical. Walking shows no ataxia. Reflexes are brisk in the lower limbs.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates multilevel degenerative changes including osteophytes, disc space narrowing, and spinal canal stenosis. MRI may show spinal cord compression or T2 signal changes indicating myelopathy.
#331 Osteoarthritis Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (multi-joint chronic pain), progression is logical with clear discrimination (activity limitation → history of injury/genetics/sedentary lifestyle → specific exam findings → imaging confirmation), and final clue is definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old retired businessman reports chronic, progressively worsening pain in multiple joints. Onset was insidious over decades. He experiences stiffness that partially improves with activity and temporary relief with heat and anti-inflammatories.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot walk more than short distances or climb stairs without significant pain and fatigue. Overhead reaching (e.g., getting items from shelves) is severely restricted. Symptoms are worst at end of day after activity.
C3 History: The patient has a 30-year history of knee pain from an old soccer injury and reports that both parents developed arthritis in their 60s. He is sedentary post-retirement with minimal exercise. No systemic inflammatory symptoms, fever, or recent weight loss.
C4 Examination: Bilateral knee crepitus with palpable osteophytes at joint lines. Knee flexion limited to 100 degrees on one side. Hip internal rotation painful and limited (20 degrees). Glenohumeral abduction limited to 120 degrees bilaterally with scapular compensation.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs of knees, hips, and shoulders show classic osteoarthritis findings: joint space narrowing, marginal osteophytes, and subchondral sclerosis. No erosions or inflammatory changes. Serum inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) are normal.
#332 Navicular Stress Fracture Foot
~ MINOR FIX
