Verdict: Clue 1 is narrow and highly specific (distance runner + deep midfoot arch pain during running is quite diagnostic), reducing guessing challenge; could broaden to 'midfoot pain in athlete' to fit more stress injuries.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A distance runner presents with deep foot pain localised to the midfoot arch. Pain develops during or after running activity.
C2 Activity: Patient reports pain is worse with repetitive impact and high mileage. Able to walk slowly on flat ground but cannot jog or run. Pain increases with each subsequent day of training.
C3 History: Recent increase in training distance or intensity coincides with symptom onset. Female athletes with amenorrhea are at increased risk.
C4 Examination: On examination, midfoot is tender to palpation over the arch. Pain reproduced with resisted plantarflexion and inversion. Mild swelling noted over midfoot. Single-leg stance is painful but tolerated.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows bone marrow edema and periosteal reaction in the midfoot tarsal region. CT or radiographs reveal stress fracture line with early callus formation.
#333 Shoulder Labral Tear Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (shoulder pain with catching fits multiple pathologies), each subsequent clue adds distinct information (activity pattern → mechanism/history → specific test findings → imaging confirmation), progresses logically.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young to middle-aged adult with shoulder pain and a catching or clunking sensation during arm movement. Symptoms are intermittent and activity-related.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform overhead reaching or throwing without increased symptoms. Pain is reproducible with specific arm positions rather than constant throughout the day.
C3 History: Insidious onset over months with repetitive overhead loading, or acute onset following a prior shoulder dislocation or subluxation event. No recent direct trauma.
C4 Examination: On examination, the crank test, anterior slide test, or O'Brien's test reproduces symptoms. Apprehension and relocation signs may be present. Weakness in external rotation may be demonstrated.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging with intra-articular contrast demonstrates labral tear with characterisation of tear location and size. MRI confirms association with rotator cuff involvement or instability.
#334 Iliotibial Band Syndrome Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (lateral knee pain in runner fits multiple knee conditions), progression is clear with activity-specific features (downhill, distance), exam findings (Ober, Noble), and imaging confirmation; fair and playable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old runner reports lateral knee pain that begins during training sessions and persists for several hours afterward.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot complete long-distance running (>5km). Pain worsens with downhill running and does not occur during cycling or swimming.
C3 History: Recent increase in running distance or intensity has coincided with symptom onset. The patient may have underlying biomechanical abnormalities including hip weakness.
C4 Examination: On examination, the Ober test demonstrates hip flexor and IT band tightness. Tenderness is palpable over the lateral epicondyle of the femur. The Noble compression test reproduces pain.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates IT band thickening and bursal fluid collection. MRI shows bursal inflammation and IT band signal changes. Imaging confirms inflammation at the distal IT band.
#335 Plica Syndrome Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is slightly too detailed (anteromedial location + catching sensations + sitting/stair pain is relatively specific); Clue 4 essentially confirms diagnosis with medial plica test description, reducing mystery before Clue 5; consider simplifying Clue 1.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old office worker complains of progressive anterior knee pain over the past 3 months. The pain is dull and aching, localized to the anteromedial knee, and exacerbated by prolonged sitting and stair climbing.
C2 Activity: Activities involving repetitive knee flexion-extension—particularly running on inclined surfaces—trigger sharp catching sensations that force the patient to stop. The pain improves with rest and ice application, but intensifies when resuming the same activity pattern within 24 hours.
C3 History: The patient recalled a hyperextension mechanism during a hiking trip 4 months ago, though initial symptoms were mild. She reports a history of anterior knee pain in her early 20s that resolved with conservative management. Recent increased walking duration in her job has coincided with symptom escalation.
C4 Examination: On examination, point tenderness is elicited along the medial femoral condyle at the level of the joint line when the knee is flexed 30 degrees. The medial plica test—performed with passive knee extension from 90 degrees flexion while internally rotating the tibia—reproduces the sharp catching sensation and patient's pain. McMurray's test is negative and there is no effusion.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the knee demonstrates a thickened and fibrotic medial synovial plica measuring 4mm in thickness with adjacent signal changes in the articular cartilage of the medial femoral condyle. No meniscal pathology or intra-articular loose bodies are identified.
#336 Diabetic Foot Ulcer Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (non-healing foot wound in diabetic patient), each clue adds meaningful context (activity limitation → diabetes/neuropathy history → sensory loss on exam → imaging confirmation), progresses logically without redundancy.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An adult with type 2 diabetes presents with a foot wound that is not healing well. The patient reports the wound is painless.
C2 Activity: The patient reports difficulty walking on the affected foot and cannot tolerate normal shoes. Gait is antalgic or avoidant.
C3 History: Long-standing diabetes with suboptimal glycemic control. Patient reports numbness in the feet. The ulcer appeared insidiously without recalled trauma or injury.
C4 Examination: On examination, a full-thickness ulcer is present, often plantar surface. Loss of protective sensation is demonstrated with monofilament testing. Surrounding erythema may be absent.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs show no bony erosion or osteomyelitis. Soft tissue depth and extent of ulcer are confirmed on imaging.
#337 Scapular Dyskinesis Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 4 is quite specific and diagnostic (medial border winging + weakness pattern strongly suggests dyskinesis), potentially over-narrowing the puzzle before Clue 5; could be less specific to preserve challenge.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Overhead athlete with insidious-onset shoulder pain during throwing or pressing movements. Pain worsens with continued activity.
C2 Activity: Unable to sustain loaded overhead positions or throwing without compensatory shoulder hiking or trunk rotation. Cannot perform push-ups without scapular winging.
C3 History: Symptoms began after returning to sport following a prior rotator cuff strain 6 months ago. Pain initially resolved but recurred when resuming full training. No significant trauma.
C4 Examination: Scapula cannot maintain neutral position during arm elevation — medial border lifts away from ribcage. Serratus anterior and lower trapezius strength 4/5. Scapulohumeral rhythm abnormal.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows supraspinatus tendinopathy without full-thickness tear. No labral pathology. Plain films normal. Imaging findings support functional scapular origin of symptoms rather than primary rotator cuff tear.
#338 Wrist Tendinopathy Wrist & Hand
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too vague ('wrist pain related to movement') fitting dozens of conditions; Clue 3 jumps into occupational history that belongs earlier; Clue 4 introduces keyboard work suddenly which should inform Clue 2 or 3; information flow is disorganized.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with history of repetitive wrist activities presents with wrist pain. The pain is localised to the wrist region and related to movement.
C2 Activity: Patient reports wrist pain that worsens with certain daily activities. Onset was gradual, not sudden.
C3 History: Cannot grip or pinch with full strength. Fine motor tasks requiring sustained wrist control (writing, buttoning) are avoided due to pain.
C4 Examination: Keyboard worker, 8+ hours daily for 2 years. Symptoms began 6 months ago and have worsened. No prior injury or trauma. No systemic inflammatory history.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates tendon thickening and hypoechoic changes within the tendon. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation.
#339 Snapping Hip Syndrome Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (hip pain in athlete fits many hip conditions), progression builds logically (activity type → gradual onset/training → exam findings with snap reproduction → imaging mechanism), fair challenge throughout.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young athlete with hip pain during or after sport. Pain is intermittent and activity-dependent.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform high-intensity movements—running, kicking, or cutting activities provoke symptoms. Pain worsens with repetitive hip motion over time.
C3 History: Gradual onset over weeks of increased training. Patient reports tightness in hip flexors and lateral hip. No prior hip injury or surgery.
C4 Examination: Snapping elicited on hip flexion–adduction test or during single-leg stance. Pain reproduced consistently. Marked limitation in hip internal rotation; iliopsoas and TFL tightness on palpation.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound or MRI demonstrates the anatomical cause, including iliotibial band snapping, iliopsoas snapping, or intra-articular loose body. Dynamic ultrasound may demonstrate the snap.
#340 Tibial Stress Fracture Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (shin pain in runner fits ITBS, compartment syndrome, other stress injuries), progression is clear (sharp localized pain → recent training increase → focal tenderness/percussion → imaging confirmation), clinically coherent and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A runner reports progressive shin pain that worsens with activity. Pain began gradually over the past 2–3 weeks.
C2 Activity: Patient reports sharp, localized pain along the medial tibia that prevents completion of running workouts. Walking and non-impact activities are pain-free.
C3 History: Recent increase in running distance or intensity coincides with symptom onset. The patient may have underlying biomechanical abnormalities or training errors.
C4 Examination: Marked focal tenderness over anteromedial tibia at a discrete site. Percussion (tap test) over the area elicits sharp pain. Hip and ankle ROM normal. Single-leg hop test not performed due to pain.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs may be normal initially, or show callus formation at chronic stage. MRI demonstrates bone stress with periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema. MRI confirms diagnosis when radiographs are negative.
#341 Morton's Neuroma Ankle & Foot
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C2 is largely redundant with C1 (both describe burning pain in forefoot with no new information), and C5 repeats examination findings rather than adding imaging data.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 46-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of burning forefoot pain localized to the third web space that have occurred intermittently over 2 years and are now becoming more frequent. She describes sharp, shooting pain radiating into the adjacent toe surfaces.
C2 Activity: A 46-year-old woman with a 2-year history of intermittent burning foot pain that has become more frequent over recent months. She describes sharp, shooting sensations in the forefoot that come and go unpredictably.
C3 History: Pain is triggered by prolonged walking and constrictive footwear, particularly high heels. Symptoms resolve with rest and removing shoes. She has significantly curtailed walking distance and modified her shoe choices to manage symptoms.
C4 Examination: On examination, palpation of the third intermetatarsal space elicits sharp pain with reproduction of her typical symptoms. Mulder's click is readily demonstrated when dorsiplantar pressure is applied while squeezing the metatarsals transversely. A palpable click or nodule may be felt in the web space. Two-point discrimination is reduced in the third and fourth toe web space region compared to other forefoot areas.
C5 Imaging: Palpation over the third intermetatarsal space elicits sharp pain reproducing her complaint. Mulder's click is readily demonstrated. Reduced two-point discrimination in the third and fourth toe web space compared to other forefoot areas. No evidence of metatarsalgia, plantarflexion weakness, or other neuropathy.
#342 Exercise-Induced Bronchoconstriction Lungs
✓ READY
Verdict: Broad initial complaint, clear progression from activity tolerance to history to examination findings to confirmatory testing, and a fair diagnostic challenge for physio students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young athlete, typically involved in endurance sports, reports chest tightness and difficulty breathing that develops during or immediately after high-intensity exercise. Symptoms are exercise-dependent.
C2 Activity: Patient can maintain only light activity without symptoms; attempts at sustained running or competitive training consistently trigger respiratory distress within 5-10 minutes. Symptoms resolve completely within 15-30 minutes of rest.
C3 History: Symptom onset correlates with competitive training intensity rather than general fitness level. The patient may have a background of allergic rhinitis, atopic dermatitis, or mild asthma. Baseline lung function is typically normal between episodes.
C4 Examination: Resting physical examination unremarkable: normal auscultation, no baseline wheeze or crackles, normal peak flow at rest. Post-exercise clinical assessment reveals audible wheeze and apparent increased work of breathing within 5 minutes of stopping exercise.
C5 Imaging: Methacholine or exercise challenge testing confirms bronchial hyperresponsiveness. Repeat spirometry 10 minutes after exercise re-challenge shows no FEV1 recovery, ruling out simple exercise-induced dyspnea from deconditioning.
#343 Post-Total Knee Replacement Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits many post-operative knee conditions, progression is logical from functional deficits to timeline to examination to imaging, and reasonably playable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports knee pain, swelling, and difficulty with mobility. Onset was acute following a surgical procedure several weeks to months ago.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot climb stairs without rail support and has marked difficulty with walking on uneven surfaces. Unable to achieve full knee extension during gait cycle.
C3 History: Surgery was performed for end-stage joint disease secondary to osteoarthritis or old trauma. Patient is 4–8 weeks post-operatively and attending outpatient physiotherapy three times per week.
C4 Examination: On examination, knee extension deficit is present with measurable lag. Knee flexion is restricted compared to goal. Mild to moderate swelling is evident. Quadriceps strength is reduced.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs show prosthetic components in neutral alignment with intact bone-cement interface and no loose bodies. No evidence of component malpositioning or early loosening.
#344 Scapular Fracture Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is quite specific (acute post-trauma severe pain) and may limit differential; clue progression is sound otherwise but starting clue could be broader.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports acute shoulder pain following a fall or direct blow. Pain is constant and severe at rest, not reproducible with specific movements yet.
C2 Activity: Patient guards the shoulder and cannot use the arm for any functional task. Unable to reach across body or elevate arm even passively. Severe guarding suggests significant structural injury rather than simple muscle strain.
C3 History: Injury occurred from high-impact mechanism: motor vehicle collision, fall from height, or direct blow to posterior/lateral shoulder. No prior shoulder pathology or fracture history.
C4 Examination: Crepitus palpable over posterior shoulder. Scapular winging absent. Marked swelling and ecchymosis over scapular region. Neurovascular exam intact. Clavicle and acromion non-tender; pain localised to scapular body or borders.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate scapular fracture with characterisation of fragment location and displacement. CT may be performed for better characterisation of complex fractures.
#345 Turf Toe Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear mechanism-based complaint, logical activity and history progression, distinctive examination signs, and imaging confirms; appropriately challenging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with acute onset foot pain following a push-off injury during sport. Pain is worse with weight-bearing through the forefoot.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform plantarflexion activities. Difficulty with walking lunges, jumping, or any activity requiring forceful push-off with the forefoot.
C3 History: Injury occurred during a football match when player planted foot and fell forward, forcing the big toe into hyperextension. Immediate swelling and inability to bear weight.
C4 Examination: Plantarflexion of the first MTP joint reproduces sharp pain. Passive dorsiflexion is guarded. Turf sign present — hallux in plantarflexion at rest due to pain inhibition.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs exclude fracture or dislocation of the first MTP joint. MRI demonstrates soft tissue injury including ligament and cartilage involvement.
#346 Swimmer's Shoulder Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 strongly implies 'swimmer' and overhead activity, making the differential narrow; should be reworded to broaden initial complaint to any repetitive overhead athlete.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A competitive athlete reports shoulder pain that worsens with overhead and repetitive upper limb activity. Onset was gradual over several weeks.
C2 Activity: Unable to complete full training sessions; must reduce volume and intensity. Pain particularly severe in the late stages of extended training sets.
C3 History: High-volume swimming training is present. Symptom onset correlates with increased training volume or intensity.
C4 Examination: On examination, positive impingement signs are demonstrated. Rotator cuff weakness, particularly supraspinatus, is evident. Scapular dyskinesis may be present.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound or MRI demonstrates rotator cuff tendinopathy or partial tear, often affecting the supraspinatus. Associated subacromial inflammation is evident.
#347 Plantar Plate Tear Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent progression from acute forefoot pain to specific activity triggers to relevant history (inflammatory arthropathy) to distinctive drawer test findings to definitive imaging; engaging and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old woman with moderate heel pain history presents with acute forefoot pain beneath the second metatarsal head. The pain began suddenly 2 weeks ago after an awkward step forward during yoga, and is described as sharp and stabbing when weightbearing.
C2 Activity: Ambulation, particularly on hard surfaces, reproduces severe pain that worsens with each step. The patient notes that the second toe drifts into extension relative to the other toes during standing. Walking barefoot is markedly worse than when wearing supportive footwear with a rocker sole.
C3 History: The patient has a 5-year history of inflammatory arthropathy affecting the small joints of her feet and hands. She denies previous foot trauma but recalls 18 months of progressive swelling in the second metatarsophalangeal joint. Predisposing factors include pes planus and high training volume in recreational activities.
C4 Examination: On examination, palpable tenderness is localized to the plantar aspect of the second metatarsal head with pain reproduction during the vertical metatarsophalangeal stress test. The drawer test of the second metatarsophalangeal joint demonstrates dorsal translation greater than 4mm compared to the contralateral side, suggesting ligamentous insufficiency. Subtle dorsal swelling at the metatarsophalangeal joint is noted.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound of the second metatarsophalangeal joint reveals a full-thickness tear of the plantar plate with hypoechoic edema in the deep structures. MRI confirms disruption of the plantar plate ligament fibers with associated joint effusion and synovitis, and secondary changes of dorsal metatarsal head subluxation.
#348 Concussion Brain
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 names 'head impact' essentially ruling in concussion immediately; Clue 5 contradicts physical therapy scope (CT/neuroimaging); concussion diagnosis is clinical, not imaging-based, making this misaligned with the game premise.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient experienced acute head impact during contact sport or fall. Reports headache, dizziness, and feeling 'foggy' or confused since the incident.
C2 Activity: Unable to tolerate return to sport or vigorous exercise without symptom exacerbation. Difficulty with fast head movements and visual tracking during activity.
C3 History: No recall of events immediately surrounding impact. Teammate witnessed patient appearing dazed. Previous concussions two years and four years ago, both with similar presentation.
C4 Examination: On examination, balance and cognitive screening reveal deficits. Vestibulo-ocular reflex may show abnormality. Neck and neurological examination rule out more serious injury.
C5 Imaging: CT head obtained at emergency department to exclude acute bleed or fracture — normal. No imaging findings of focal injury. Clinical diagnosis of concussion established on symptom presentation and examination findings; advanced imaging not indicated for uncomplicated injury.
#349 SLAP Lesion Shoulder
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 and C2 are nearly identical (both describe posterior shoulder pain with overhead activities with no new information), C4 repeats history elements rather than adding exam findings, and progression is incoherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with history of overhead activities presents with shoulder pain, often posterior shoulder pain. Symptoms develop with overhead movements.
C2 Activity: A patient presents with shoulder pain that worsens with overhead activities. Pain is often felt in the posterior shoulder. Symptoms are intermittent and activity-dependent.
C3 History: Patient reports pain specifically during late cocking phase of throwing or overhead pressing. Describes instability sensation during these movements. Mechanical symptoms (clicking/catching) suggest intra-articular pathology.
C4 Examination: Long-standing history of overhead throwing or single traumatic event with arm behind body. Symptoms accelerated after return to sport or increased overhead volume. No prior shoulder surgery or significant conservative treatment response.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging with intra-articular contrast demonstrates superior labral tear with characterisation of tear pattern. MRI confirms involvement of the biceps anchor.
#350 Achilles Tendinopathy Foot/Ankle
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately broad (ankle pain, running), clear progression through activity patterns to training history to palpation findings to imaging confirmation; well-structured and fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A recreational runner reports insidious onset of pain at the back of the ankle that worsens with running. Pain is worst in the morning and improves slightly once warmed up.
C2 Activity: Pain is triggered specifically by push-off activities and stairs. The patient reports pain is worst at the start of activity, then eases slightly mid-run, only to worsen again at the end.
C3 History: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. The patient may have prior history of Achilles pain.
C4 Examination: Tenderness is palpable 2–6 cm proximal to the calcaneal insertion. Pain is reproduced on single-leg calf raise and resisted plantarflexion. No swelling or warmth noted. Ankle dorsiflexion range is within normal limits.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates Achilles tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation confirming tendinopathy.
#351 Lisfranc Injury Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (foot pain + swelling post-twisting covers many conditions), clues progress logically with new detail at each step, and Clue 5 clinches via imaging findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports foot pain and mild swelling after a twisting injury while playing sports. Pain is moderate and located in the middle of the foot.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot bear weight comfortably on the affected foot. Walking is severely limited; patient prefers to keep the midfoot immobilised.
C3 History: Injury occurred during a high-impact mechanism (fall from height, crush injury, or forceful inversion while planted). Patient reports immediate, severe pain. No prior foot or ankle pathology.
C4 Examination: Swelling localised to the medial midfoot. Tenderness elicited along the tarsometatarsal joint line. Abduction stress test of the forefoot demonstrates abnormal motion and reproduces sharp pain. Pain also elicited with passive plantar flexion of the first ray.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs may show widening of the Lisfranc joint space. Stress radiographs demonstrate increased widening. MRI characterises soft tissue injury.
#352 SLAP Lesion Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 targets overhead athletes with deep shoulder pain (fits rotator cuff, labral issues, instability), progression is clear through activity limitations and dead-arm sensation, and Clue 5 is definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Overhead athlete presents with shoulder pain that worsens with throwing or serving motions. Pain is poorly localised but feels deep within the joint.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot complete full overhead sequences without pain or fear of shoulder 'slipping'. Reports dead-arm sensation during throwing. Symptoms worse with late cocking phase of overhead activities.
C3 History: Rapid return to competition after prior shoulder injury, or sudden increase in throwing volume. No prior significant shoulder pathology. Failed conservative treatment after 6-8 weeks.
C4 Examination: On examination, the crank test and compression-rotation test reproduce symptoms. The anterior slide test is positive. Associated apprehension may be present.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging with intra-articular contrast demonstrates superior labral tear with characterisation of tear pattern. MRI confirms involvement of the biceps anchor.
#353 Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (gradual shoulder pain in middle-aged adult fits multiple conditions), each clue adds specificity without redundancy, and Clue 5 provides imaging confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged adult reports shoulder pain that develops gradually with activity. Onset over weeks to months.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform overhead tasks or sustained lifting. Activities like reaching to high shelves, throwing, or carrying cause sharp pain. Pain worsens with repetition throughout the day.
C3 History: History of repetitive overhead work or sport. Recent increase in training volume or activity level. No acute trauma or fall. Symptoms have persisted despite rest attempts. Previous episodes of shoulder discomfort.
C4 Examination: On examination, positive impingement signs including Neer's test and Hawkins' test are present. Pain with resisted shoulder abduction is demonstrated.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates rotator cuff tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation.
#354 Huntington's Disease Brain
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This condition is outside typical physiotherapy scope and Game 1 clue essentially narrows to movement disorder + cognitive decline, making it too specific for a guessing game; consider replacing with a musculoskeletal or neurological physiotherapy diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged adult presenting with involuntary movements and gradual cognitive changes over the past 1-2 years.
C2 Activity: Patient reports difficulty with fine motor tasks (writing, dressing) and concentration. Gait has become unsteady and jerky.
C3 History: Disease onset insidious and progressive over 2+ years. No recent head injury or toxin exposure. Taking no medications that might cause movement disorder.
C4 Examination: On examination, choreiform movements are evident and increase with distraction. Cognitive impairment is demonstrated. Personality changes may be noted.
C5 Imaging: Genetic testing demonstrates CAG repeat expansion confirming Huntington's disease. MRI may show putaminal atrophy in advanced disease.
#355 ICU-Acquired Weakness Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad, but Clues 2 and 4 overlap significantly on examination findings; Clue 4 should emphasize EMG/biopsy findings to avoid redundancy with Clue 2's clinical presentation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient recovering from a prolonged critical illness presents with generalized weakness and fatigue. They are having difficulty performing basic tasks.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot stand or walk without assistance and cannot lift head off pillow. Respiratory weakness is also evident with difficulty clearing secretions.
C3 History: Extended stay in ICU for critical illness is documented. The patient may have had prolonged mechanical ventilation or sepsis.
C4 Examination: All limb muscles grade 3 or less. Cranial nerves intact. Reflexes are brisk and symmetrical. Sensation is normal. No fasciculations. Mild proximal predominance noted in lower limbs.
C5 Imaging: EMG shows brief, small, polyphasic motor unit action potentials with early recruitment. Muscle biopsy demonstrates Type 1 and Type 2 fiber atrophy with occasional necrosis and minimal inflammation.
#356 Tibialis Posterior Tendinopathy Ankle & Foot
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clues 2 and 4 are nearly identical (both describe same patient history and pes planus), Clue 5 shifts to examination findings when it should be imaging, and the clinical progression is incoherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 67-year-old retired postal worker presents with 8-month progressive history of medial foot pain and arch discomfort. Symptoms began insidiously without injury recollection. Reports significant functional decline affecting independence with household activities.
C2 Activity: A 67-year-old retired postal worker presents with 8-month progressive history of foot pain and arch discomfort. Symptoms began insidiously without recalled injury. Reports significant functional decline affecting independence with daily activities.
C3 History: Lifelong pes planus bilaterally. Type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.8%) for 12 years, currently controlled on metformin. Mild peripheral neuropathy confirmed with monofilament testing. No prior foot surgery or acute injuries. History of obesity with BMI 31.
C4 Examination: Lifelong bilateral pes planus with progressive arch collapse over past year. Type 2 diabetes for 12 years (HbA1c 7.8%) with documented peripheral neuropathy. BMI 31. No prior foot surgery, acute injuries, or recent trauma. No response to conservative management over 4 months.
C5 Imaging: Severe bilateral heel valgus with absent single-leg heel raise and inability to perform single-leg stance. Point tenderness elicited along the posterior tibial tendon course from medial malleolus to navicular tuberosity. Arch completely flattened in standing. No acute swelling or erythema.
#357 Calf Strain Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute posterior leg pain fits DVT, compartment syndrome, tendinopathy), clues logically progress with mechanism and 'pop' sensation, and Clue 5 confirms via imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old runner reports acute posterior lower leg pain that started mid-training session yesterday. Pain is sharp and localised to one side.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot achieve full stride length or push off explosively. Walking is possible but slow and cautious. Unable to walk on tiptoes.
C3 History: Pain began suddenly without warning during sprint intervals. No prior calf injury or significant lower leg history. Patient reports feeling a sudden 'pop' or sharp grab in the muscle at onset.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the calf muscles. Resisted plantarflexion or knee flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption, fluid collection, or hematoma within the calf. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.
#358 Posterolateral Corner Injury Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (acute knee pain post-twist fits ACL, PCL, meniscal tears), clues add genuine new information through instability and specific tests, and Clue 5 definitively identifies posterolateral structures.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient presents with acute knee pain following a twisting injury during sports. Pain is significant and onset was immediate.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot bear weight on the affected leg. Walking and stair negotiation are impossible. Feels like the knee is unstable or 'giving way'.
C3 History: Injury occurred during a sport requiring rapid deceleration and pivoting. No prior knee injuries. Patient reports immediate swelling and inability to continue activity.
C4 Examination: On examination, varus instability is demonstrated with varus stress test. Positive posterolateral drawer sign and external rotation recurvatum test indicate posterolateral corner injury.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates injury to posterolateral corner structures including fibular collateral ligament, popliteus tendon, and biceps femoris. MRI characterises injury severity.
#359 Distal Biceps Tendon Rupture Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute elbow weakness after forceful activity fits nerve injury, triceps issues, or tendon rupture), clues progress logically, and Clue 5 is definitive via imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Sudden onset loss of elbow strength after forceful arm activity. Patient reports acute pain in the anterior elbow.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform resisted elbow flexion or carry objects with the affected arm. Simple overhead reach is preserved but resisted activity is impossible.
C3 History: Injury occurred during eccentric loading—e.g., resisting arm extension while holding a heavy object. Patient is middle-aged. No prior elbow surgery or chronic tendinopathy.
C4 Examination: Palpable gap in the distal antecubital fossa where tendon normally inserts. Supination strength preserved but flexion strength severely reduced. Positive biceps squeeze test.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates absence of the distal biceps tendon insertion and proximal retraction of the muscle-tendon unit. MRI shows complete rupture at the radial tuberosity.
#360 Torticollis Neck
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 4 essentially names the diagnosis by describing a palpable sternocleidomastoid mass (fibromatosis), leaving little room for guessing by Clue 5; consider making Clue 4 more general (e.g., 'palpable muscle abnormality') to preserve guessing challenge.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 3-year-old child presents with persistent leftward head tilt and neck rotation limitation noted by parents over the past 6 months. The head tilt is constant, and the child avoids turning the head to the right.
C2 Activity: The child has difficulty following objects that require rightward head rotation and shows reluctance to eat food presented on the right side. Attempts to manually straighten the neck cause significant distress. The abnormal posture does not improve with rest and is present throughout sleep and waking hours.
C3 History: Parents recall a difficult breach delivery with forceps application; however, the child had normal head positioning at 1 month postpartum. The tilt gradually worsened over 4-6 months without clear precipitant. No history of trauma, fever, or recent upper respiratory infection. Developmental milestones were met appropriately.
C4 Examination: On examination, palpation of the left sternocleidomastoid reveals a firm 1.5cm mass within the muscle belly, consistent with a fibromatosis. Passive cervical rotation to the right is limited to 35 degrees compared to 70 degrees to the left. No cervical lymphadenopathy, facial asymmetry, or neurological deficits are present.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates an echogenic nodule within the left sternocleidomastoid muscle measuring 1.2 by 0.8cm with minimal vascularity. MRI of the cervical spine and soft tissues confirms a well-defined mass consistent with fibromatosis within the sternocleidomastoid with no intracranial abnormality or spinal cord compression.

C3 History: Patient unable to bear weight on affected limb and reports significant instability when attempting to stand or walk without support.
C4 Examination: Injury occurred 2-3 weeks ago and was managed surgically. Patient is now cleared to begin early rehabilitation.
C5 Imaging: Ankle dorsiflexion limited to 5 degrees, plantarflexion to 10 degrees. Marked ankle swelling. Single-leg stance impossible; proprioception severely impaired.

Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (progressive thoracic pain in adolescent fits multiple conditions), each clue adds specific clinical detail, and Clue 5 clinches diagnosis with characteristic imaging findings.

C3 History: Onset during growth spurt in early adolescence. Family history of spinal issues. No trauma. Progressive course over 12–24 months without prior intervention.
C4 Examination: On examination, fixed thoracic kyphosis is evident that does not correct with forward bending. Back pain is elicited with extension.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate thoracic vertebral body wedging and Schmorl's nodes characteristic of Scheuermann's disease. Kyphosis is measured.

Verdict: Clue 1 broadly describes chronic joint pain affecting many conditions, subsequent clues narrow progressively with functional limitation and imaging findings typical of advanced OA.

C2 Activity: Patient reports difficulty with prolonged walking and stair negotiation. Unable to rise from a chair without arm support. Gait is slow and antalgic with lateral trunk shift.
C3 History: Symptoms have progressed over 10+ years despite physiotherapy, analgesia, and weight management. Patient previously attended multiple treatment courses without sustained relief.
C4 Examination: Palpable bony enlargement at joint margins. Active range of motion severely restricted in flexion and internal rotation. End-feel hard. Quadriceps and hip abductors markedly wasted bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate severe degenerative changes with joint space obliteration, osteophytes, and bone sclerosis indicating advanced arthritis.

Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (giving way occurs in multiple ankle pathologies), clues build logically from mechanism to laxity tests to imaging confirmation.

C2 Activity: Patient unable to walk confidently on uneven ground or grass. Avoids sport participation due to fear of ankle going over. Normal walking on flat surfaces manageable.
C3 History: Ankle sprain sustained 18 months ago playing football; initial swelling resolved but patient never regained full confidence. No episode of complete ligament tear. No prior physiotherapy or rehabilitation.
C4 Examination: On examination, the anterior drawer test demonstrates anterior translation of the talus. The talar tilt test shows inversion laxity. Proprioceptive deficit is evident.
C5 Imaging: Stress radiographs demonstrate increased talar tilt and anterior translation compared to normal ankle. MRI may show ligament signal changes and chronic inflammation.
#365 Deep Vein Thrombosis Lower Limb

Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old male presents 8 days after hip surgery with acute-onset right calf swelling and pain that developed over a few hours. The calf feels warm, and discomfort worsens with walking and when the leg hangs down.
C2 Activity: Leg elevation provides minimal relief to the swelling, which is unusual for typical soft tissue swelling. The patient is unable to bear weight due to pain severity.
C3 History: He is immediately postoperative from total hip arthroplasty with general anesthesia lasting 2 hours; immobilization time was prolonged during recovery. He has no personal or family history of blood clots but takes lisinopril for hypertension. He was not prescribed mechanical or pharmacological thromboprophylaxis.
C4 Examination: On examination, the right calf circumference exceeds the left by 3cm measured 10cm below the tibial tuberosity. Homan's sign (passive ankle dorsiflexion) reproduces sharp calf pain. The right leg is visibly erythematous and warmer than the left; palpable cord-like resistance is present in the posterior calf, and slight distal foot edema is noted.
C5 Imaging: Bilateral lower extremity compression ultrasound demonstrates an echogenic thrombus within the right popliteal vein extending proximally into the superficial femoral vein; no compressibility is achieved with probe pressure. Doppler shows absent spontaneous flow; the contralateral left popliteal vein is fully compressible with normal augmentation.
#366 Meniscal Tear Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 describes acute knee pain with popping (fits ligament and meniscal pathology), each subsequent clue adds mechanistic and examination specificity, Clue 5 provides definitive imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old recreational soccer player reports sudden onset medial knee pain with a popping sensation during a game 1 week ago. The pain is localized to the medial joint line and worsens with weightbearing and twisting movements.
C2 Activity: The patient cannot return to sport due to intermittent episodes of knee locking where the knee becomes stuck in slight flexion for several seconds before suddenly extending with a pop. Squatting and cutting movements reliably trigger both locking episodes and sharp joint line pain.
C3 History: The injury occurred during a rapid deceleration while pivoting on the planted foot. He has no prior knee injuries, previous meniscal pathology, or osteoarthritis. His current pain is out of proportion to prior minor knee sprains and has not improved with standard rest and ice protocols over 7 days.
C4 Examination: On examination, point tenderness is elicited directly over the medial joint line. The McMurray test—performed with the knee flexed 90 degrees, internally rotating the tibia while extending—reproduces the patient's locking sensation and sharp pain. The Thessaly test at 20 degrees flexion reproduces medial joint line discomfort. No effusion is appreciated, and Lachman and anterior drawer tests are negative.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the knee reveals a vertical longitudinal tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus extending to the free edge. The tear pattern is consistent with degenerative changes at the tear interface. No signal abnormality in the anterior cruciate ligament, posterior cruciate ligament, or medial collateral ligament is identified.

C1 Complaint: Older adult reports increased caution with movement and reluctance to walk or change position. No acute injury reported, but patient seems tense and hesitant.
C2 Activity: Patient is now declining stairs, uneven surfaces, and crowded environments. Spends most time seated or holding onto furniture. Previously independent with community ambulation.
C3 History: Fall occurred 3 months ago after a trip; patient struck hip but imaging was normal. Since then, has become progressively withdrawn. No other medical or psychological history of note.
C4 Examination: Gait shows shortened stride, reduced arm swing, and frequent pauses. Patient grips rails or therapist's arm for reassurance. Berg Balance Scale 18/56 (high fall risk), but strength and ROM are intact. Timed Up and Go: hesitant, slow, cautious movement.
C5 Imaging: MRI and CT head are unremarkable. Cognitive screening normal. Geriatric Depression Scale and Falls Efficacy Scale confirm high anxiety and low fall confidence disproportionate to objective balance impairment.

C3 History: No recent head trauma, infection, or ototoxic medications. Hypertension controlled on current medication. Mild hearing loss noted. No previous episodes of vertigo.
C4 Examination: On examination, balance abnormalities including tandem stance deficit are evident. Vestibular and proprioceptive deficits may be present.
C5 Imaging: MRI head shows no acute lesion or posterior fossa mass. Videonystagmography reveals reduced caloric response bilaterally consistent with vestibular involution. Saccular function impaired.

C3 History: Surgical intervention to the chest wall and underlying structures occurred approximately 4–6 weeks ago. Indication was for treatment of intrathoracic pathology. Patient has received standard post-operative care.
C4 Examination: Chest wall tenderness localised to surgical scar. Shoulder abduction and external rotation are restricted (ROM 80° and 60° respectively). Inspiratory capacity reduced; accessory muscle recruitment noted. Scapular dyskinesis present.

#370 Gamekeeper's Thumb Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 describes insidious thumb base pain in older adult (fits multiple hand pathologies), clues progressively reveal specific mechanism (abduction injury), laxity findings, and imaging of avulsion fracture with ligament disruption.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 67-year-old retired teacher reports insidious onset of pain at the base of the right thumb over the past 8 months, worsening gradually with increased hand use. The pain is described as aching with occasional sharp stabbing when grasping or pinching.
C2 Activity: Fine motor activities—particularly opening jars, using tweezers, and writing—are significantly limited. The patient demonstrates thumb opposition weakness and avoids pinch grip; he substitutes lateral pinch for precision pinch. Pain intensifies progressively throughout the day with repeated pinching motions.
C3 History: The patient sustained a fall onto an abducted right thumb 14 months ago while hiking, causing acute pain and swelling that partially resolved over 6 weeks. He did not seek medical attention at the time. Subsequent attempts at recreational knitting and gardening triggered progressive worsening of baseline symptoms over the past year.
C4 Examination: On examination, the radial collateral ligament stress test of the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint at 30 degrees flexion demonstrates excessive radial deviation (>30 degrees laxity compared to the left side). Pinch strength testing shows 40% reduction compared to the contralateral thumb. Mild thenar eminence atrophy is appreciated. No acute swelling or warmth is present.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate a small avulsion fracture fragment from the medial base of the proximal phalanx of the thumb with mild degenerative changes at the metacarpophalangeal joint. MRI shows disruption of the radial collateral ligament with chronic fibrosis and healing response; the ligament appears attenuated but intact.
#371 Lumbar Instability Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (chronic mechanical LBP fits many conditions), each clue adds specific information (movement patterns → functional limitations → history → examination findings → imaging confirmation), and Clue 5 clinches it with hypermobility and ligamentous insufficiency.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old warehouse worker reports chronic low back pain of 4 years' duration that fluctuates in severity. Pain is mechanical, reproducible, and worsens with certain movements and prolonged postures, particularly forward bending and sustained standing.
C2 Activity: Functional limitations include reduced tolerance for lifting (now limited to 15 pounds versus 50 pounds prior to symptom onset) and difficulty with sustained postures during work. The patient demonstrates guarding and splinting behavior during movement; shifting weight side-to-side improves tolerance. Pain is least with supported sitting and walking.
C3 History: Symptoms began 4 years ago following a work-related lifting incident; imaging at that time was unremarkable. He has tried multiple conservative interventions with temporary benefits. Pain intensity ranges from 2/10 at best to 7/10 at worst, with exacerbations lasting 1-2 weeks. He works in a physically demanding job and is overweight (BMI 31).
C4 Examination: On examination, active lumbar flexion triggers pain reproduction and is accompanied by deviation (Schober test: 4cm excursion versus normal 5cm). Prone instability test produces sharp midline lumbar pain. Lumbar multifidus muscle tone is asymmetrically diminished on the left. Abdominal bracing partially relieves pain. Strength testing and straight leg raise are normal.
C5 Imaging: Flexion-extension lumbar radiographs demonstrate 4mm of anterior translation at L4-L5 on flexion compared to neutral, indicating segmental hypermobility. MRI shows loss of disc height at L4-L5 with facet hypertrophy; however, no significant canal stenosis or spondylolisthesis is present. Ligamentous insufficiency with facet effusions is noted.

Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with high-impact activities presents with progressive hip or thigh pain. Pain has developed insidiously during training.
C2 Activity: Patient reports pain with running and jumping, but can walk on flat ground without exacerbation. Pain worsens with prolonged standing or stairs.
C3 History: Recent increase in training volume or intensity coincides with symptom onset. Risk factors including female athlete triad or osteoporosis may be present.
C4 Examination: On examination, pain is elicited with single-leg stance. Focal tenderness may be present over the femoral neck. Pain is reproduced with hip flexion and internal rotation.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs may show stress fracture line or callus formation. MRI demonstrates bone stress with periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema indicating stress fracture.
#373 Musculocutaneous Nerve Palsy Upper Limb
✓ READY

Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old male presents with acute onset weakness and altered sensation in the right upper limb following a fall during sport 72 hours ago. He reports inability to bend his elbow and numbness in the forearm.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform elbow flexion or pulling motions. Hand strength and dexterity are normal. Sensory changes are confined to the lateral forearm and thumb dorsum.
C3 History: The mechanism involved forced shoulder abduction and external rotation combined with axial loading on the outstretched arm. He sustained a visible dislocation at the shoulder which was reduced in the emergency department. Radiographs excluded fractures. Prior shoulder history is unremarkable.
C4 Examination: Right elbow flexion strength is 0/5 and cannot be initiated. The biceps reflex is absent. Shoulder external/internal rotation strength normal. All other limb reflexes intact bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Electromyography performed 5 days post-injury demonstrates acute denervation changes (positive sharp waves and fibrillations) in the biceps brachii and brachialis muscles; the brachioradialis is spared. MRI of the brachial plexus reveals stretching injury of the musculocutaneous nerve at the level of the shoulder with surrounding edema and partial discontinuity.
#374 Fibromyalgia Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits many systemic conditions (generalized pain, no clear cause), Clues 2-4 progressively eliminate organic/inflammatory pathology while establishing widespread tender points and normal investigations, and Clue 5 seals diagnosis via normal labs and imaging ruling out structural disease.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 51-year-old woman with a 3-year history of generalised musculoskeletal pain. Burning and aching quality affecting multiple body regions. No clear injury or precipitant that she can identify.
C2 Activity: Light physical activity—including walking and household tasks—triggers significant pain exacerbation that persists for 1-2 days. Patient reports profound fatigue, cognitive difficulties affecting work, and prolonged morning stiffness.
C3 History: Pain onset followed a severe respiratory viral infection 3 years ago. She reports a personal history of major depressive disorder (treated) and anxiety. Multiple investigations including rheumatologic workup (negative ANA, RF, and inflammatory markers), imaging, and specialist consultations have failed to identify structural pathology. Previous treatments with NSAIDs and corticosteroids provided minimal benefit.
C4 Examination: Widespread tender points reproducible at multiple anatomic locations (cervical, trapezius, supraspinatus, costochondral junctions, lateral epicondyles, gluteal region, greater trochanters, knees). Pain evoked at reduced pressure thresholds. Strength, range of motion, reflexes, and joint examination are entirely normal. No signs of inflammation or structural abnormality on physical exam.
C5 Imaging: Laboratory investigations show all inflammatory markers and autoimmune serology within normal limits. MRI of the spine and pelvis reveals normal disc architecture with no degenerative disease, inflammatory changes, or myelopathy.
#375 Pilon Fracture Ankle
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too specific ('high-energy trauma' + 'severe pain + rapid swelling' essentially names a fracture), Clue 2 immediately reveals non-weightbearing with deformity (still screams fracture), and there is minimal diagnostic progression before Clue 5; this puzzle lacks sufficient ambiguity for fair guessing.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient sustained acute ankle injury from high-energy trauma. Presents with severe pain and rapid onset swelling.
C2 Activity: Patient is non-weightbearing with visible deformity and gross instability. Unable to perform any ankle plantarflexion or dorsiflexion actively.
C3 History: Axial loading injury (fall from height >2 metres or high-speed MVA with foot compression). Patient reports hearing a 'crack' at impact. No significant past ankle history.
C4 Examination: Severe swelling obscures bony landmarks. Ankle held in slight plantarflexion and inversion. Palpable crepitus over medial malleolus and anterior ankle. Neurovascular status intact but compartment firmness increasing.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs show comminuted fracture of the distal tibia with involvement of the articular surface and metaphysis. Fibula intact. CT shows complex fracture pattern with multiple fragments and degree of displacement.
#376 Gamekeeper's Thumb Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 presents thumb base pain post-abduction injury (fits multiple thumb pathologies), Clue 2 specifies thumb opposition/pinch loss, Clue 3 provides mechanism detail, Clue 4 narrows to ligamentous laxity with positive RCL stress test, and Clue 5 confirms RCL disruption on MRI.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old amateur skier presents with acute onset sharp pain at the base of the left thumb following a fall 3 days ago. The thumb is swollen and tender, and any gripping aggravates the pain significantly.
C2 Activity: The patient cannot perform fine pinch grip or opposition movements; attempts to hold a pen or grasp objects trigger sharp pain at the thumb base. Functional loss is primarily in thumb-to-finger pinch and opposition.
C3 History: The mechanism involved forced abduction of the thumb during the fall when the patient tried to break the fall with an outstretched hand. He applied ice and kept the thumb immobilized at home but symptoms have not improved. No prior thumb injury, and he denies any sensation of instability before the fall.
C4 Examination: On examination, marked swelling and ecchymosis are evident over the radial aspect of the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint. The radial collateral ligament stress test performed with the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint in 30 degrees flexion demonstrates excessive radial deviation (>20 degrees) compared to the contralateral side. Thumbs-up test shows pain with resistance.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs of the thumb in anteroposterior and lateral views are normal with no fractures or avulsion fragments visible. MRI demonstrates disruption of the radial collateral ligament with complete separation of the ligament from its proximal insertion; associated edema and effusion within the metacarpophalangeal joint capsule are present.
#377 Costochondritis Thoracic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (anterior chest wall pain fits cardiac, pulmonary, and musculoskeletal causes), Clues 2-3 anchor to mechanical/postural triggers and preceding cough, Clue 4 localizes to costochondral junction with positive palpation/Tietze test, and Clue 5 excludes cardiac/pulmonary pathology while showing soft tissue edema.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 46-year-old woman presents with anterior chest wall pain that started 2 weeks ago. The pain is sharp, positional, and worsens with certain movements and breathing.
C2 Activity: Pain is reproducibly triggered by deep inspiration, coughing, laughing, and reaching movements. Pressure over the painful area during activity intensifies symptoms. Anti-inflammatory medication and heat provide partial relief.
C3 History: Symptom onset followed 3 weeks of a persistent dry cough from a recent viral upper respiratory infection. The patient reports working at a desk with suboptimal ergonomics and has been doing garden renovation involving repetitive overhead activities. She denies trauma, fever, or constitutional symptoms.
C4 Examination: On examination, well-localized tenderness is reproduced with direct palpation of the costochondral junction at the 4th rib on the left side. The Tietze test (reproduction of pain with passive vertical flexion of the thorax) produces sharp left parasternal pain. No joint swelling, warmth, or erythema is appreciated; no heart or lung findings on auscultation.
C5 Imaging: Chest radiographs are normal with no infiltrates, pleural effusion, or mediastinal widening. MRI of the chest wall demonstrates mild soft tissue edema at the left 4th costochondral junction with subtle enhancement on post-gadolinium imaging. No cardiac abnormality, pulmonary pathology, or mediastinal masses are identified.

C1 Complaint: Patient reports inability to move or feel legs following a high-impact accident one week ago.
C2 Activity: Patient is unable to ambulate independently and requires wheelchair mobility. Requires assistance with all lower extremity-dependent activities of daily living.
C3 History: Motor vehicle collision with ejection. Loss of consciousness at scene. Currently 2 weeks post-injury. No prior neurological history.
C4 Examination: Lower extremity strength 0/5 bilaterally at L1 level. Sensory level sharply demarcated at T12 dermatome. Preserved motor/sensation above level; absent reflexes and tone below. Positive Babinski bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates spinal cord transection or severe contusion. MRI shows extent of spinal cord injury.

C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old man presents with acute polyarticular joint pain and swelling involving the knees, ankles, and wrists that developed 2 weeks after a diarrheal illness. The joints are painful, warm, and progressively more swollen over the past 10 days.
C2 Activity: Functional limitations are significant; the patient cannot walk without assistance due to bilateral knee and ankle pain, and gripping with both hands is severely limited. Morning stiffness lasts 45 minutes and improves slightly with movement. Symptoms are worst at rest and early morning.
C3 History: Patient reports significant morning stiffness (45 minutes) that improves with movement, and symptoms are disproportionately worse at rest. Pain is worse in the early morning and evenings.
C4 Examination: On examination, symmetric swelling, erythema, and warmth are present over the bilateral knee joints and ankle joints; the right knee demonstrates an effusion (ballotable patella). Wrist joints are swollen with limited flexion-extension range. Systemic examination reveals no rash, mucosal ulcers, or conjunctivitis. Inflammatory markers are elevated, and the patient demonstrates no cardiac findings.
C5 Imaging: On examination: symmetric swelling, erythema, and warmth over bilateral knees and ankles. Right knee demonstrates effusion with ballotable patella. Wrist joints swollen with restricted flexion-extension. Inflammatory markers significantly elevated (CRP/ESR). No rash, oral ulceration, or conjunctivitis present.

Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old man reports progressive abnormal head posture with involuntary leftward tilt developing over 18 months. Symptoms worsen throughout the day and with emotional stress.
C2 Activity: Patient reports neck pain and muscle fatigue worsening with sustained positioning, particularly during reading or computer work. Symptoms paradoxically worsen in crowded environments but completely resolve when recumbent.
C3 History: Onset was insidious without preceding trauma or infection. A family history of essential tremor is noted in his mother. He has no psychiatric history, prior movement disorders, or medication exposure. Symptoms have not responded to NSAIDs, muscle relaxants, or physical therapy over 12 months.
C4 Examination: Examination reveals involuntary head positioning can be transiently suppressed with conscious effort. Hypertonic sternocleidomastoid and trapezius bilaterally, predominant on left. Sensory examination, motor strength (5/5 throughout), and reflexes normal. No focal neurological deficit.
C5 Imaging: EMG demonstrates burst-like muscle activity in sternocleidomastoid and trapezius characteristic of dystonic muscle contraction. Cervical spine MRI and brain MRI are structurally normal.
#381 Calf Strain Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute lower leg pain fits many conditions), each subsequent clue adds specific information (mechanism, functional loss, palpation findings, imaging confirmation), and progression is logical and educational.

C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old recreational athlete reports sudden-onset sharp pain in the lower leg during a training session. Pain is severe enough to stop activity immediately.

C3 History: Injury occurred without direct trauma—patient felt a sudden 'pop' or tearing sensation in the midcalf while accelerating. No significant prior leg injury, but returned to running after 2 weeks off. No calf cramps or DVT symptoms.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the calf muscles. Resisted plantarflexion or knee flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption, fluid collection, or hematoma within the calf. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.
#382 Idiopathic Adolescent Scoliosis Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (asymptomatic adolescent screening), clues build logically from no functional complaints → clinical signs → imaging confirmation, and the puzzle teaches recognition of a common screening presentation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young adolescent presents for routine screening. No back pain or functional complaints reported.
C2 Activity: Patient reports no difficulty with activities of daily living or sports. No complaints with posture or endurance.
C3 History: The curve has developed during growth and typically progresses during adolescent growth spurt. Family history of scoliosis may be present.
C4 Examination: On forward bend test, asymmetric rib prominence noted. Shoulder and hip heights are uneven. Thoracic curve suspected.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate the scoliotic curve with measurement of Cobb angle. Spine maturity is assessed. MRI may be obtained if atypical features present.
#383 Osteoporosis Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clues are mostly strong but C2 jumps to fracture history slightly abruptly; reordering to emphasize bone quality/risk factors earlier would improve progression, though the puzzle remains playable as-is.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Older adult presenting for routine health screening. Reports no specific complaints, though some generalized aching in the spine and hips has been noted recently.
C2 Activity: Recent fall from standing height resulted in wrist fracture. Patient reports reduced confidence with stairs and walking on uneven surfaces due to fear of falling.
C3 History: Postmenopausal female, 15 years since menopause. Long-term corticosteroid use for COPD. Mother had hip fracture at age 72.
C4 Examination: Height loss of 4 cm compared to documented height 20 years ago. Thoracic kyphosis present. Able to walk independently but with cautious gait pattern.
C5 Imaging: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) demonstrates bone mineral density in the osteoporotic range. Radiographs may show compression fractures.
#384 Rectus Femoris Strain Thigh
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute anterior thigh pain in athlete), clues progressively narrow via mechanism → activity-specific pain → examination findings → imaging, and the distinction from other thigh strains is educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An athlete presents with acute pain in the anterior thigh that began during a training session. Pain is sharp and well-localised to one area.
C2 Activity: Activities combining hip flexion with knee extension—sprinting, kicking, jumping—consistently provoke pain. Patient avoids rapid hip/knee movement but tolerates walking and static stretching.
C3 History: Patient is a competitive sprinter with no significant prior lower-limb injury. Onset was sudden mid-sprint; he felt a sharp pop or stabbing sensation. No preceding stiffness or warning signs.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the rectus femoris. Resisted knee extension or hip flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption or hematoma within the rectus femoris. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.
#385 Calf Strain Lower Leg
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This puzzle is nearly identical to Index 380 (same diagnosis, same clue structure, overlapping wording); it should be removed as a duplicate or substantially rewritten with a different clinical presentation (e.g., different mechanism, patient age, or complication).
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old recreational athlete presents with acute onset pain in the lower leg that developed mid-run. Pain is sharp and localised to one leg.
C2 Activity: Unable to continue running or perform single-leg activities. Walking is possible but painful; limp is present. Pain worsens with push-off demands.
C3 History: Sudden onset without trauma. No prior lower leg injury. Patient was returning to running after 2 weeks away from sport. Had skipped warm-up that session.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the calf muscles. Resisted plantarflexion or knee flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption, fluid collection, or hematoma within the calf. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.
#386 Antenatal Pelvic Girdle Pain Pelvis
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is overly specific ('pelvic girdle pain' + 'during pregnancy' essentially names the condition), C4 redundantly restates pregnancy history, and there is minimal clue progression; the puzzle collapses after one guess.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A pregnant woman presents with pelvic girdle pain localised to the pelvic region. Pain develops during pregnancy.
C2 Activity: Pregnant woman in second trimester with progressive deep pelvic pain, worse with weight-bearing activities.
C3 History: Pain develops during pregnancy, typically in second or third trimester. Prior pelvic injury or multiple pregnancies increase risk.
C4 Examination: Symptoms began 6 weeks ago. Patient has history of previous pelvic trauma and is expecting third child.
C5 Imaging: Imaging is typically avoided during pregnancy. Assessment is clinical with pelvic stability examination.
#387 Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is very broad and somewhat generic; adding specificity (e.g., age range, night pain, or mechanism) would sharpen the opening, though the puzzle is still playable and clues do add information logically.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged to older adult reports shoulder pain that worsens with activity.
C2 Activity: Pain is triggered by overhead movements, lifting activities, or activities requiring arm elevation. The patient demonstrates functional limitation.
C3 History: Symptoms have developed gradually with repetitive shoulder activities or overuse. Recent increase in activity may be contributory.
C4 Examination: On examination, positive impingement signs including Neer's test and Hawkins' test are present. Pain with resisted shoulder abduction is demonstrated.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates rotator cuff tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation.
#388 Proximal Humerus Fracture Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (older adult with acute shoulder pain post-fall fits many fractures/soft tissue injuries), clues logically narrow via mechanism → functional loss → clinical signs → imaging confirmation, and the clinical presentation is coherent throughout.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Older adult presents after a fall with acute shoulder pain and inability to use the arm.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot initiate any active movement of the affected shoulder. Passive handling also provokes severe pain.
C3 History: Fall from standing height approximately 2 hours ago. Known history of osteoporosis. Symptoms began immediately post-fall.
C4 Examination: Swelling and ecchymosis visible over lateral shoulder. Painful arc present throughout all planes of passive motion. No sensation loss or motor weakness in upper limb distal to shoulder. Radial pulse intact.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate proximal humerus fracture with characterisation of displacement and pattern.
#389 Sacroiliac Joint Dysfunction Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (bilateral pelvic pain with asymmetry fits many conditions), clues systematically add information via activity triggers → detailed history → specific examination tests → imaging localization, and the progression is educationally sound and fair for a physio student.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old female yoga instructor reports insidious-onset bilateral pelvic pain that is asymmetric. Pain is worse on one side and occasionally refers to the groin and medial hip.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with asymmetric loading tasks (single-leg stance, unilateral hip extension). Relieved by symmetrical positions and activities that distribute load evenly across both sides.
C3 History: The patient completed a full marathon four weeks prior and has recently begun practicing more advanced yoga postures with hip rotation emphasis. They have a history of pelvic instability post-pregnancy (age 28) but have remained asymptomatic until now.
C4 Examination: Gaenslen's test positive bilaterally but asymmetric. Pain provoked with single-leg hip extension (ipsilateral posterior pelvic pain). FABER and FADIR asymmetric. Hip internal/external rotation ROM symmetrical. Sacroiliac joint palpation tenderness on symptomatic side only.
C5 Imaging: CT scan of the pelvis demonstrates unilateral sacroiliac joint involvement with mild capsular inflammation and small osteophytes along the joint margins. No evidence of fusion or significant degenerative changes.
#390 Ganglion Cyst Wrist & Hand
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is quite specific ('visible firm mass over dorsal wrist, gradually enlarged') and essentially points to ganglion cyst almost immediately; broadening C1 to focus on wrist pain/dysfunction without mentioning visible mass would improve the puzzle's guessing challenge.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 31-year-old graphic designer presents with a visible firm mass over the dorsal right wrist that has gradually enlarged over 6 months. The mass is generally painless but causes mild aching with repetitive wrist movements.
C2 Activity: Gripping and forceful wrist extension trigger mild discomfort at the mass site. The patient reports that the mass becomes more prominent during active wrist extension and less noticeable after rest. Prolonged keyboard use causes intermittent soreness in the wrist region that may relate to mass compression.
C3 History: The mass interferes with gripping activities and prolonged keyboard work. Wrist extension maneuvers exacerbate mild discomfort, though symptoms resolve quickly with rest.
C4 Examination: Mass appeared insidiously without antecedent wrist injury. She recalls a transient dull ache in the wrist several months before the mass became visible. No history of wrist trauma, arthritis, or prior hand/wrist surgery.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates a well-defined, homogeneous, hypoechoic fluid collection measuring 1.6 by 1.2cm at the dorsal scapholunate region with thin echogenic walls; the mass does not show internal vascularity on Doppler. MRI confirms a simple cyst containing fluid signal intensity with no internal hemorrhage, and demonstrates its direct communication with the dorsal wrist joint capsule.
#391 Post-Carpal Tunnel Release Wrist
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (post-operative hand symptoms), each clue progressively narrows toward incomplete nerve recovery, and C5 clinches with EMG/NCS findings; fair and educational for physio students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports persistent hand paresthesias and weakness several weeks after wrist surgery. Symptoms are not fully resolved.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to return to grip-dependent tasks (e.g., gripping tools, writing for prolonged periods). Fine motor control remains compromised.
C3 History: Surgery performed 6-12 weeks ago. Initial post-operative swelling resolved, but functional improvement plateaued. Patient reports recurrent night symptoms and occasional sharp pains at surgical site.
C4 Examination: Scar tissue thickened and tender over carpal tunnel site. Intrinsic hand muscle strength 4/5 (abductor pollicis brevis, lumbricals). Sensation in median nerve distribution demonstrates recovery but remains duller than contralateral side. Phalen's test negative; Tinel's sign positive at incision site.
C5 Imaging: Repeat EMG/NCS shows residual median nerve conduction slowing across the wrist with reduced amplitude compared to pre-operative baseline, consistent with incomplete axonal recovery or early recurrent compression.
#392 Flat Feet in Children Foot
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is overly broad and vague ('may or may not complain'); specificity and clinical context should be strengthened to distinguish from other pediatric foot complaints without making it too obvious.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Parent concerned about child's foot appearance and gait. Child may or may not complain of foot discomfort.
C2 Activity: Child able to walk and run normally but may avoid prolonged standing or long-distance walking. Symptoms worse at end of day.
C3 History: Onset gradual over years. Family history of flat feet or foot problems. Child was able to walk and run since normal developmental milestones.
C4 Examination: On examination, medial arch is absent or minimal when standing. Weight-bearing foot posture shows eversion and valgus alignment.
C5 Imaging: Weight-bearing radiographs show talonavicular uncovering and increased talar-first metatarsal angle. Medial arch collapses on standing but may reconstitute on toe-standing (flexible vs rigid differentiation).
#393 Anterior Cruciate Ligament Tear Knee
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 essentially names ACL injury ('acute knee injury, loss of stability, deceleration/pivoting'), leaving little room for differential diagnosis; clues lack meaningful progression and are heavily repetitive across C1–C3.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An athlete presents with acute knee injury and loss of knee stability. The injury occurred with a deceleration or pivoting mechanism.
C2 Activity: An athlete sustained an acute knee injury during sport and reports the knee feels unstable or 'gives way'.
C3 History: Patient cannot perform cutting, pivoting, or deceleration movements without the knee buckling. Cannot walk on uneven ground confidently.
C4 Examination: Incident occurred during attempted jump landing with foot planted and body rotating. No prior knee problems. Immediate swelling within 2 hours.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates complete anterior cruciate ligament tear. MRI characterises associated injuries including meniscal tears.
#394 Parkinson's Disease Neurological
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too specific and diagnostic ('tremor and slowness in middle-aged/older adult') and fits very few conditions; this reads as a medical diagnosis rather than a physiotherapy clinical puzzle, and C4–C5 confirm cardinal features with little ambiguity.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged to older adult reports gradual onset of tremor and slowness with everyday tasks over several months.
C2 Activity: Patient reports shuffling gait, difficulty rising from chairs, and reduced ability to perform fine motor tasks like buttoning. Walking speed has slowed noticeably.
C3 History: Symptom onset is insidious with gradual progression. The patient reports tremor at rest that improves with movement.
C4 Examination: On examination, resting tremor is evident. Rigidity with cogwheel quality is demonstrated. Bradykinesia affects movement speed.
C5 Imaging: Imaging of the brain (MRI or CT) excludes other causes of parkinsonism. Diagnosis is clinical based on motor features.
#395 Post-Total Hip Replacement Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately broad (post-operative hip pain/mobility loss), clues logically progress from general dysfunction to specific weakness pattern and imaging confirmation; realistic clinical scenario with fair guessing difficulty.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports hip pain and significant difficulty with lower limb weight-bearing and mobility. Onset was acute, following a surgical intervention several weeks ago.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot abduct the hip against gravity and demonstrates a compensatory limp. Lying flat and rolling in bed are particularly difficult. Sitting is limited to 30–45 minutes before pain worsens.
C3 History: Patient underwent a major surgical procedure on the hip joint for advanced osteoarthritis. Recovery timeline is 6–12 weeks into rehabilitation. No prior hip pathology or surgery on the contralateral side. Comorbidities include mild hypertension, well controlled.
C4 Examination: Hip abduction strength is 2/5 (poor, gravity-eliminated). Hip flexion ROM is 60°, abduction 20°. Pain is sharp and anterior with internal rotation. Glute med inhibition noted on palpation. No fever, wound healing normal. SLR positive for pain inhibition.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate appropriate prosthesis positioning and seating. Radiographs exclude mechanical complications.
#396 Anterior Cruciate Ligament Tear Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Very similar to Puzzle 392 with nearly identical clues; while C1 is slightly better refined, the puzzle still lacks sufficient specificity in early clues and has excessive overlap with the other ACL puzzle.
Show clues
