C1 Complaint: An athlete experiences sudden knee pain and immediate sense of the knee 'giving way' during a rapid direction change. The injury occurred mid-sport.
C2 Activity: Patient reports inability to plant and cut movements. Single-leg stance is unstable. Fear-avoidance behaviour limits weight-bearing activities, particularly on stairs.
C3 History: Acute injury during sport with specific mechanism of injury documented. The patient heard or felt a pop.
C4 Examination: On examination, positive Lachman test and anterior drawer test demonstrate anterior knee instability. Pivot shift test may be positive.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates complete anterior cruciate ligament tear. MRI characterises associated injuries including meniscal tears.
#397 Peripheral Neuropathy Peripheral Nerves
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (progressive numbness/tingling), context about diabetes and systemic features adds meaningful progression, and EMG/NCS findings in C5 confirm axonal neuropathy; educational and fair for physio students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports progressive numbness and tingling in the feet, gradually worsening over months. Some difficulty with fine motor control in the hands noted.
C2 Activity: Patient reports difficulty walking on uneven surfaces and balance impairment, particularly when eyes are closed. Trips on stairs.
C3 History: Diabetes diagnosed 15 years ago, poorly controlled. Recent fatigue and weight loss. No prior neurological symptoms. Family history of autoimmune disease.
C4 Examination: On examination, sensory loss in stocking-glove distribution is demonstrated. Reflexes are diminished or absent. Weakness may be present.
C5 Imaging: Electromyography demonstrates reduced amplitudes of compound muscle action potentials with normal conduction velocities, consistent with axonal degeneration. Nerve conduction study shows length-dependent pattern.
#398 Cubital Tunnel Syndrome Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is specific but reasonable (ulnar nerve symptoms, night worsening), each clue adds distinct clinical detail (activity triggers, occupational history, physical signs, imaging confirmation), and the progression is logical and engaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old warehouse worker presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the hand that worsens at night and affects sleep quality. The symptoms began insidiously 3 months ago without preceding trauma. Pain radiates from the elbow down to the ring and little fingers.
C2 Activity: The patient reports difficulty gripping tools at work and has dropped several items in the past month. Prolonged elbow flexion during computer work exacerbates symptoms, while extending the arm and shaking the hand provides temporary relief.
C3 History: The patient works assembly lines requiring repetitive elbow flexion. Past medical history includes rheumatoid arthritis controlled on medication. The patient denies neck pain or shoulder involvement. Symptoms are worse in the morning after sleeping with the elbow bent.
C4 Examination: Tinel's sign is positive at the medial elbow. Froment's sign demonstrates weakness of thumb adduction. Intrinsic hand muscle atrophy is visible between thumb and index finger. Two-point discrimination is diminished in the ulnar distribution compared to the contralateral side.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound imaging shows enlargement and hypoechoic appearance of the ulnar nerve at the medial elbow, consistent with nerve swelling. The nerve measures 0.7 cm in cross-sectional area at the compression site, well above normal limits of 0.4 cm.
#399 Osgood-Schlatter Disease Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately broad (adolescent anterior knee pain with activity), age and growth context refine the picture, physical findings and imaging confirm diagnosis; realistic and fair for physio education.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An adolescent athlete reports anterior knee pain that worsens with physical activity, particularly jumping and running sports.
C2 Activity: Cannot kneel without discomfort, and pain is reproducibly worse with activities involving forceful knee extension or explosive movements. Sitting with knee bent for prolonged periods is tolerated.
C3 History: The adolescent is typically 11-18 years old during growth spurt. Symptoms correlate with periods of rapid growth. Sport involvement is common.
C4 Examination: On examination, swelling and tenderness are present at the tibial tuberosity. Pain is reproduced with knee extension against resistance.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs may show tibial tuberosity enlargement and fragmentation. MRI demonstrates tibial tuberosity inflammation and soft tissue involvement.
#400 Exertional Rhabdomyolysis Muscle
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is quite specific with 'dark-colored urine' essentially naming rhabdomyolysis; remove or soften this detail to Clue 2/3 to broaden the initial differential and allow better progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports severe muscle pain and weakness developing during or immediately after an intense workout. Dark-colored urine is noted.
C2 Activity: Unable to continue or repeat the activity that triggered symptoms. Severe global malaise and fatigue limit all physical activity.
C3 History: Triggered by unaccustomed intense exercise (e.g., new training program, military bootcamp, extreme heat event). Patient has no prior history of similar episodes or known muscle disorders.
C4 Examination: Severe muscle tenderness and induration palpable in exercised muscle groups. Calf or quad swelling noted. Neurological examination intact. Urine dipstick positive for blood/myoglobin with absence of RBCs on microscopy.
C5 Imaging: Serum creatine kinase >5000 IU/L (often >10,000). Urine myoglobin present. Serum creatinine elevated with oliguria, consistent with acute tubular necrosis from myoglobin precipitation.
#401 Vestibular Migraine Vestibular
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (vertigo + migraines fit multiple conditions), each clue adds meaningful information, and Clue 5 clinches diagnosis with normal MRI excluding structural causes.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with migraine headache history presents with episodes of vertigo that occur with or separate from headache. Vertigo episodes are recurrent.
C2 Activity: Patient reports significant balance impairment during episodes, particularly difficulty with gait and head movements. Nausea and photophobia are prominent accompanying features.
C3 History: Migraines began in early adulthood. Vertigo episodes last minutes to hours. Triggers include stress, certain foods, and hormonal changes. No prior head trauma or hearing loss. Patient has tried migraine prophylaxis with variable response.
C4 Examination: On examination, positional nystagmus or gaze-evoked nystagmus may be present. Balance testing shows abnormalities. Neurological examination is otherwise normal.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain excludes other causes of vertigo. MRI is normal in vestibular migraine.
#402 Athletic Pubalgia Hip & Groin
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from groin pain in athletes through activity triggers to specific examination findings and imaging, with good educational value for sports physios.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An athlete presents with pain in the groin region or lower abdominal region. Pain develops with athletic activity and resolves with rest.
C2 Activity: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or twisting movements. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with sport.
C3 History: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. Sport involving rapid acceleration and deceleration is typical.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is elicited in the groin region and lower abdomen. Pain is reproduced with resisted hip adduction and internal rotation.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates adductor muscle strain or rectus femoris tear near the pubic symphysis. MRI characterises muscle injury and inflammation.
#403 Post-Meniscectomy Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is overly specific with 'twisting injury 3 weeks ago' and 'post-operative' language; should be reworded to describe post-surgical knee pain more broadly without revealing the surgical history upfront.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old recreational athlete presents with medial-sided knee pain and mechanical symptoms. Onset was 3 weeks ago following a twisting injury during sport. Pain is moderate intensity and worsened by certain movements.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with stairs, squatting, and kneeling due to joint line discomfort. Walking with an antalgic gait. Pain worsens with deep knee flexion and rotational activities.
C3 History: Twisting injury while playing football 3 weeks ago. Post-operative quadriceps inhibition and weakness noted. No significant prior knee injuries. Moderate pain interfering with sport and daily activities.
C4 Examination: Mild joint effusion on examination. Quadriceps strength 4/5, VMO atrophy evident. Joint line tenderness medially reproduced. Knee flexion range 95 degrees with terminal extension lag 5 degrees.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows meniscal resection with intact ACL, PCL, and collateral ligaments. No acute chondral lesions identified. Joint space preserved. No loose body.
#404 Facet Joint Syndrome Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 4 and C5 are swapped—examination findings (extension/rotation reproduction, facet tenderness) belong in C4, while Clue 4 currently contains history information; progression is incoherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged to older adult presents with unilateral low back pain and possible radiation into the leg. The pain is mechanical and activity-related.
C2 Activity: Pain is aggravated by extension and ipsilateral rotation activities. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with certain movements.
C3 History: Symptoms are chronic or recurrent with mechanical characteristics. Recent increase in activity may trigger exacerbation.
C4 Examination: Symptoms are longstanding with episodic flares triggered by sustained or repetitive end-range spinal extension. No significant trauma; gradual onset. Pain does not follow a specific dermatomal distribution and neurological signs are absent.
C5 Imaging: Lumbar extension and ipsilateral rotation reproduce pain. Unilateral tenderness elicited on palpation over the facet joint at affected level. Lumbar flexion and contralateral rotation do not reproduce pain. Straight leg raise and neurological examination are normal.
#405 Scheuermann's Disease Thoracic Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Adolescent with progressive thoracic kyphosis and mechanical back pain is reasonably broad in Clue 1, builds logically through fixed kyphosis and imaging findings that clinch the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An adolescent reports progressive upper back pain that worsens with activity and prolonged sitting. Pain is mechanical in nature.
C2 Activity: Patient reports difficulty maintaining upright posture for prolonged periods. Appearance shows exaggerated rounding of the upper back.
C3 History: Symptoms began in early adolescence with gradual worsening over 1–2 years. Family history of similar spinal deformity is noted. No prior significant back injury or trauma.
C4 Examination: On examination, fixed thoracic kyphosis is evident that does not correct with forward bending. Back pain is elicited with extension.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate thoracic vertebral body wedging and Schmorl's nodes characteristic of Scheuermann's disease. Kyphosis is measured.
#406 Pulmonary Fibrosis Lungs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is a respiratory/medical condition outside the scope of physiotherapy practice; while physios may manage patients with PF, the condition itself is not a physiotherapy diagnosis puzzle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with chronic lung disease presents with progressive dyspnoea on exertion and dry cough. Symptoms have developed insidiously over years.
C2 Activity: Progressive dyspnoea on exertion severely limits activities of daily living; patient becomes breathless with minimal effort, even at rest in advanced stages.
C3 History: History reveals significant occupational dust exposure (silica, asbestos) or chronic medication use (chemotherapy, amiodarone). Disease has progressed over 5+ years despite cessation of exposure.
C4 Examination: On examination, fine inspiratory crackles are heard on chest auscultation. Finger clubbing may be present. Cyanosis may develop in advanced disease.
C5 Imaging: High-resolution CT of the lungs demonstrates pulmonary fibrosis with ground glass infiltrates and reticular opacities. Pulmonary function tests show restrictive pattern.
#407 Pelvic Floor Dysfunction Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately broad (incontinence, dyspareunia, urgency fit multiple pelvic conditions), each clue adds distinct information, and C5 imaging findings are confirmatory without naming the diagnosis early.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old female presents with difficulty controlling bladder and bowel function that has progressed over 8 months. She reports urgency and occasional incontinence during exercise, coughing, or sneezing. There is also pain with sexual intercourse.
C2 Activity: The patient has modified her routine to avoid jumping or running due to urine leakage. She performs Kegel exercises but reports poor muscle control. Prolonged sitting worsens pelvic discomfort, while lying down relieves symptoms. She avoids tampon use due to discomfort.
C3 History: She had two vaginal deliveries 15 and 10 years ago with episiotomy noted during second delivery. She reports a history of chronic constipation. Menstrual cycles are regular without hormonal contraceptive use. She denies previous pelvic surgery beyond childbirth.
C4 Examination: Internal palpation reveals weak muscle contraction during requested pelvic floor squeeze, graded 2/5. A cough stress test produces leakage before pelvic floor contraction occurs. Tender trigger points are noted in the pelvic floor muscles bilaterally during assessment.
C5 Imaging: Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates descent of the bladder base beyond 5 cm on Valsalva maneuver, indicating hypermobility. Pelvic floor thickness measures 0.6 cm on endovaginal ultrasound, below the normal range of 0.8-1.2 cm, confirming atrophy and dysfunction.
#408 Burns Rehabilitation Various
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is quite vague ('heat exposure'); should specify burn severity or depth earlier, and 'weeks ago' in C1 contradicts the 6-week timeline in C3.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports pain, stiffness, and difficulty with movement after a recent incident involving heat exposure. Onset was acute, weeks ago.
C2 Activity: Cannot reach across midline or overhead. Difficulty with fine hand movements and walking due to lower limb tightness. Functional independence in ADLs is severely compromised.
C3 History: Sustained deep partial thickness and full thickness burns to upper and lower limbs 6 weeks ago. Initial treatment completed; now progressing through scar maturation phase. No prior burns or significant medical history.
C4 Examination: Visible hypertrophic scarring over dorsal hands and anterior shins with blanching erythema. Elbow flexion limited to 100° and ankle dorsiflexion to 0° due to soft tissue tightness. No sensory loss; strength intact but guarded by pain.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows thickened dermis with heterogeneous echogenicity consistent with hypertrophic scar. No evidence of underlying tissue compromise or active infection.
#409 Clavicle Fracture Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Acute shoulder trauma with visible swelling and pain is broad enough, step deformity and imaging findings provide clear progression, and diagnosis is solidly clinched by radiographs.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient presents with acute onset shoulder pain and visible swelling following a fall. Pain is reproduced with shoulder movement.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to abduct or elevate the arm. Arm splinting by patient. Unable to perform overhead activities or carry objects.
C3 History: Fall directly onto the point of the shoulder during contact sport. Heard/felt a crack at time of injury. No prior shoulder pathology.
C4 Examination: Palpable step deformity or prominence at the clavicle. Horizontal adduction (cross-body) test elicits focal pain. Scapular dyskinesis evident. No neurological deficit distally.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate clavicle fracture with characterisation of displacement and comminution. Imaging excludes neurovascular injury.
#410 Lumbar Disc Herniation Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately detailed for a complex case, each clue systematically adds neurological findings and imaging confirmation, and the L4-L5 posterolateral herniation with L5 nerve root compression clearly distinguishes it from other lumbar presentations.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old construction supervisor presents with severe lower back pain that began suddenly while lifting a heavy load 2 weeks ago. The pain radiates down the left leg into the foot with associated numbness in the foot. Sitting and bending forward intensely worsen the pain.
C2 Activity: The patient cannot sit for more than 15 minutes without severe radiating pain. Lying flat with the left leg elevated provides mild relief. Bending forward to pick up objects triggers sharp shooting pain down the left leg. Light walking is tolerated better than sitting or standing still.
C3 History: The patient has a 10-year history of intermittent lower back pain managed with NSAIDs and physical therapy. He reports smoking one pack daily and spends 8 hours per day in a vehicle for work. No prior episodes of leg pain. Family history includes degenerative disc disease in his father.
C4 Examination: Straight leg raise test is positive on the left at 35 degrees with reproduction of radiating pain. Ankle dorsiflexion weakness is demonstrated as 4/5 on the left. Sensory testing shows decreased sensation in the L5 dermatome on the left foot. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are intact bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the lumbar spine reveals a large posterolateral disc herniation at L4-L5 with significant compression of the left L5 nerve root. The herniated disc material is noted to be sequestered, and there is associated left-sided foraminal stenosis at the level of involvement.
#411 TFCC Tear Wrist
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (ulnar wrist pain from gradual onset in athlete), each clue adds specific clinical progression (activity limitations → history of overuse → specialized tests → imaging confirmation), and Clue 5 is definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old racquetball player presents with wrist pain on the ulnar side that began gradually during a tournament 6 weeks ago. Pain is located on the medial aspect of the wrist just above the wrist crease and worsens with gripping activities. There is associated clicking sensation in the wrist.
C2 Activity: Racquet sports and activities requiring power grip aggravate the pain significantly. Twisting motions of the wrist, particularly combined with ulnar deviation, provoke sharp pain. The patient reports weakness when attempting to lift objects with the affected hand. Rest and ice provide temporary relief.
C3 History: The patient has played racquetball competitively for 15 years with no prior wrist injuries. He reports recent increase in training intensity. No systemic inflammatory history. The patient is right-hand dominant with left wrist affected. He denies previous wrist fractures or dislocations.
C4 Examination: The ulnar fovea sign is positive with palpable laxity of the ulnocarpal ligaments. Positive ulnar fovea sign test reproduces the patient's clicking. The ulnar meniscus load test provokes pain with axial compression and ulnar deviation combined. Grip strength is reduced 25% on the affected side.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the wrist shows a tear of the triangular fibrocartilage with associated fluid signal extending into the tear. There is mild signal change in the lunate bone consistent with early chondral injury. The ulnar styloid process is intact without fracture or edema.
#412 Radial Head Fracture Elbow
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too vague and generic (could apply to any elbow fracture/injury), clues repeat the same information without progression, and the clinical story lacks detail and individuality.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with acute elbow trauma presents with elbow pain and swelling. The injury involved a fall on an outstretched hand.
C2 Activity: Functional limitation is present with difficulty with elbow movement and gripping. The patient demonstrates pain with pronation and supination.
C3 History: Acute trauma mechanism documented with fall on outstretched hand and elbow compression.
C4 Examination: On examination, elbow swelling and tenderness over the radial head are present. Pain with pronation and supination indicates radial head involvement.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate radial head fracture. CT imaging characterises fracture pattern and displacement important for treatment planning.
#413 Colles Fracture Wrist & Hand
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 explicitly names the mechanism and deformity (FOOSH + dorsal deformity), Clue 3 mentions elderly demographic, and multiple clues redundantly repeat the dinner fork deformity without meaningful progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with fall on outstretched hand (FOOSH) injury presents with acute wrist pain and visible deformity. The injury is dorsal wrist deformity.
C2 Activity: Severe functional limitation is present with inability to use the hand for functional activities.
C3 History: Fall on outstretched hand mechanism documented. The elderly are most commonly affected.
C4 Examination: On examination, "dinner fork" deformity is visible over the dorsal wrist. Significant swelling and tenderness are present.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate distal radius fracture with dorsal angulation and displacement characteristic of Colles fracture.
#414 Meralgia Paraesthetica Thigh
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (lateral thigh burning/numbness fits multiple neuropathies), each clue adds genuine information (positional triggers → weight gain/tight clothing history → specific physical exam finding with Tinel's → imaging excludes alternative diagnoses), and progression is logical and engaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old male reports burning pain and numbness on the outer thigh that began 3 months ago and is progressively worsening. The symptoms are localized to the lateral thigh and do not extend below the knee. He describes a constant burning sensation with occasional electric shock sensations.
C2 Activity: Prolonged standing and walking exacerbate the burning pain and numbness significantly. Wearing tight waistbands, belts, or sitting with legs crossed worsens the symptoms dramatically. Lying down with the hip flexed provides relief. The patient has reduced his walking distance from 30 minutes to 5 minutes.
C3 History: The patient has a 40-pound weight gain over the past 18 months and recently switched to wearing tighter fitting work pants due to his occupation. He denies lumbar pain or lower extremity radiculopathy. Recent history includes a fall onto his hip, though skin over the affected area is intact.
C4 Examination: Sensation is diminished to light touch and pinprick over the lateral thigh in the L2-L3 dermatome distribution. Straight leg raise test is negative. Hip flexion and extension strength are normal 5/5. Tapping over the inguinal ligament just medial to the anterior superior iliac spine reproduces the burning sensation.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the pelvis and hip shows no mass or structural lesion at the inguinal ligament. The appearance is normal without focal nerve enlargement. Imaging confirms the absence of lumbar radiculopathy or hip joint pathology, supporting a diagnosis of nerve entrapment rather than central or referred pain.
#415 Rib Fracture Thoracic
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is overly generic (applies to any chest wall injury), clues lack specificity and individuality, and the progression is flat with minimal new information added at each step.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with acute chest trauma presents with chest wall pain that is sharp and localised to the rib region. Pain is worse with breathing.
C2 Activity: Functional limitation is present with difficulty with breathing due to pain. The patient demonstrates guarding and shallow breathing.
C3 History: Acute trauma with fall or direct blow to the chest. Pain onset is immediate.
C4 Examination: On examination, focal tenderness over the rib is elicited. Pain is reproduced with chest compression or rib palpation.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs may demonstrate fracture line, though many rib fractures are not visualized initially. Chest CT demonstrates fracture and excludes pulmonary injury.
#416 Intersection Syndrome Forearm
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Generally sound progression with good activity and exam clues, but Clue 1 is slightly vague ('dorsal forearm pain' could fit several conditions); adding reference to specific occupations or 'proximal dorsal forearm' in Clue 1 would strengthen it.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with repetitive occupational activity presents with pain in the dorsal forearm. Pain is worse with gripping and wrist motion.
C2 Activity: Pain is triggered by repetitive wrist extension and resisted finger extension. Patient reports visible swelling in the proximal forearm.
C3 History: Symptoms onset insidious over weeks. Patient is a rower/rock climber [or specific activity]. No prior forearm injury. No systemic disease or inflammatory history.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the proximal dorsal forearm where extensors carpi radialis longus and brevis cross over extensor carpi radialis and extensor digitorum communis. Crepitus may be appreciated with movement.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates tendon sheath inflammation and tenosynovitis at the intersection area. MRI shows inflammation and swelling.
#417 Erb's Palsy Upper Limb
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (infant arm weakness/flaccidity), each clue adds specific information (functional deficits → delivery trauma history → characteristic waiter's tip posture → EMG/MRI confirmation), and the clinical story is coherent and educationally valuable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Infant presents with weakness and flaccidity of one arm noticed immediately after delivery. Caregiver reports the arm hangs limp at the side.
C2 Activity: Infant unable to lift the affected arm against gravity. No protective movement or grasp response on affected side. Cries asymmetrically during distress.
C3 History: Delivery complicated by shoulder dystocia requiring significant traction. Weakness noted in the delivery room. Parents report arm has remained weak over first 2 weeks with no improvement.
C4 Examination: Arm held in adduction and internal rotation (waiter's tip posture). Weakness predominantly in shoulder abduction and external rotation. Biceps and deltoid strength severely reduced. Wrist and finger movements relatively preserved.
C5 Imaging: Electromyography documents denervation changes in affected muscles. MRI may show nerve root avulsion if severe.
#418 Anterior Cerebral Artery Stroke Brain
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue structure is severely broken—C4 contains history/risk factors (hypertension, smoking) that should appear in C3, and C5 contains examination findings (strength grades, reflexes, cognitive score) rather than imaging, fundamentally violating the game's clue progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with acute stroke involving anterior cerebral artery territory presents with acute neurological deficit. The patient has unilateral lower extremity weakness.
C2 Activity: Patient presents with acute onset of leg weakness and difficulty moving one side of the body.
C3 History: Unable to walk independently. Severe difficulty with lower extremity control on one side affecting balance and mobility.
C4 Examination: Sudden onset during rest. History of hypertension and smoking. Symptoms began this morning with no prior similar episodes.
C5 Imaging: Lower extremity strength 3/5 on affected side with preserved upper extremity strength. Hyperreflexia and extensor plantar response noted. Mini-cog score 18/30 with mild confusion.
#419 Kienbock's Disease Wrist & Hand
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Solid progression overall, but Clue 4 mentions scaphoid shift test which is typically associated with scaphoid injuries rather than lunate pathology, and this could confuse or mislead the learner; should replace with lunate-specific findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged patient presents with central wrist pain and progressive loss of grip strength. Pain develops insidiously in the wrist.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform sustained gripping (e.g., opening jars, holding tools). Fine motor tasks like writing become progressively difficult. Symptoms worsen with repeated wrist loading.
C3 History: Gradual, progressive onset over weeks to months. No acute injury, though patient reports past wrist trauma (fracture or ligament injury) years prior. Symptoms do not improve with rest alone.
C4 Examination: Tenderness elicited over scaphoid-lunate joint. Wrist extension and radial deviation are restricted. Positive scaphoid shift test. Grip strength markedly reduced compared to contralateral side.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate lunate flattening and fragmentation. MRI shows lunate necrosis and signal changes. Sequential imaging documents disease progression.
#420 Pronator Syndrome Forearm
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (forearm/hand numbness from desk work fits multiple nerve compression/overuse conditions), each clue adds distinct information (activity triggers → occupational history and risk factors → specific pronation weakness and Tinel's sign → MRI confirms nerve compression at pronator teres), and progression is logical and clinically sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old software developer presents with numbness and tingling in the hand that worsened over 6 weeks. Symptoms are triggered by prolonged desk work. Pain is present in the forearm and hand.
C2 Activity: Patient reports difficulty with fine motor tasks and weakness in hand pinching. Resisted forearm movement in one direction reproduces forearm pain. Rest provides temporary relief.
C3 History: The patient works 8-10 hours daily at a desk with forearm resting on a padded armrest in pronation. He denies neck pain or shoulder involvement. Symptoms began insidiously without trauma. No history of elbow fracture or subluxation. The patient is right-hand dominant with symptoms on the right forearm.
C4 Examination: Weakness demonstrated in thumb-down pronation grip (4/5 strength). Tinel's sign is positive in the proximal forearm between two muscle bodies. Median nerve distribution sensory testing shows mild diminishment.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the forearm demonstrates hypoechoic swelling of the median nerve as it passes between the two heads of the pronator teres muscle. Ultrasound shows nerve compression without mass effect or fracture. Cross-sectional area of the median nerve at this level is increased compared to the contralateral side.
#421 Cervicogenic Headache Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (unilateral headache with neck stiffness), each clue adds genuine new information (activity triggers, whiplash history, specific ROM deficits, imaging findings), and Clue 5 is near-definitive; fair and engaging for physio students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old male office worker presents with episodic unilateral headaches located in the temporal and occipital regions bilaterally asymmetrical. Symptoms correlate temporally with neck stiffness and worsen throughout the day.
C2 Activity: Headache consistently provokes with prolonged sitting and neck extension movements. Relief noted when lying supine or with ice application. Certain neck postures reproducibly trigger pain within minutes. Neck stretching and movement paradoxically worsens the headache initially.
C3 History: Three-year history of intermittent symptoms, initially triggered by a motor vehicle accident with whiplash mechanism. Progressive worsening associated with sedentary work environment. Reports no visual changes, hearing loss, or vestibular symptoms. Stress management minimally affects pain pattern.
C4 Examination: Cervical range of motion significantly restricted in extension (40 degrees; normal >50 degrees) and right rotation (55 degrees; normal >80 degrees). Extension immediately reproduces familiar occipital-temporal headache. Positive flexion-rotation test with pain reproduction. Cervical paraspinal muscles palpably tight and tender.
C5 Imaging: Cervical spine X-rays demonstrate loss of cervical lordosis with subtle C4-C5 disc bulging. MRI shows C4-C5 and C5-C6 uncovertebral joint arthrosis with mild cervical spondylosis. Facet joint changes at C3-C4 level on the right correlate with restricted ipsilateral rotation and headache reproduction.
#422 Post-Carpal Tunnel Release Wrist & Hand
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clues are vague and non-specific (e.g., 'ongoing hand symptoms or recurrent symptoms', 'variable symptom improvement'), lack clinical detail, and fail to narrow the diagnosis progressively—a student could guess dozens of post-operative hand conditions.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient recovering from carpal tunnel release surgery reports ongoing hand symptoms or recurrent symptoms. The patient is in the post-operative phase.
C2 Activity: Functional limitation may persist with ongoing hand dysfunction. The patient may demonstrate improved or persistent symptoms.
C3 History: Carpal tunnel release surgery was performed weeks to months prior. Recovery is ongoing with variable symptom improvement.
C4 Examination: On examination, hand sensation and strength are assessed to document improvement from pre-operative baseline. Scar tenderness may be present.
C5 Imaging: Imaging may include electrophysiology studies to document improvement in median nerve conduction if symptoms persist.
#423 Rectus Femoris Strain Thigh
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (anterior thigh pain in athlete), each clue adds specificity (activity triggers, acute onset, resisted hip flexion/knee extension, imaging confirmation), and progression is logical; engaging and fair for physio students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An athlete presents with acute anterior thigh pain that developed during activity. Pain is localised to the anterior thigh.
C2 Activity: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or activities requiring hip flexion and knee extension. The patient demonstrates functional limitation.
C3 History: Acute onset during or immediately after athletic activity. The patient may have inadequate warm-up or prior thigh injury.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the rectus femoris. Resisted knee extension or hip flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption or hematoma within the rectus femoris. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.
#424 Bell's Palsy Cranial Nerves
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Strong puzzle overall, but Clue 1 ('acute unilateral facial weakness') is quite specific and may narrow options too early; otherwise clues progress well and Clue 5 is definitive with EMG and imaging correlation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young to middle-aged adult presents with acute unilateral facial weakness. The patient reports facial droop and asymmetry that developed over the course of hours to days.
C2 Activity: Functional impairment includes inability to close the eye completely on the affected side, difficulty with oral hygiene, and loss of facial symmetry during smiling or facial movements. Water escapes from the mouth during drinking due to lip weakness.
C3 History: Symptom onset was acute and unilateral with no prior warning. Recent upper respiratory tract infection or possible exposure history may be present. The patient denies prior facial weakness episodes and has no significant neurological history.
C4 Examination: On examination, unilateral lower facial weakness is more pronounced than upper facial involvement, with incomplete eye closure on the affected side. Tear production may be reduced. Hyperacusis and altered taste are possible. Facial grimace reproduces characteristic asymmetry.
C5 Imaging: Electromyography demonstrates denervation changes consistent with acute peripheral nerve injury. Imaging of the head (CT or MRI) is unremarkable, ruling out central causes, space-occupying lesions, or brainstem pathology affecting the facial nucleus.
#425 Whiplash Associated Disorder Cervical Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clues are extremely generic and repetitive (acceleration-deceleration mentioned twice, 'neck pain and stiffness' appears in both C1 and C4), lack distinguishing clinical features, and provide minimal progression from first to last clue.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with acute neck trauma presents with neck pain and stiffness. The injury resulted from acceleration-deceleration mechanism.
C2 Activity: Functional limitation is present with difficulty with neck movement. The patient demonstrates guarding and protective postures.
C3 History: Motor vehicle accident or similar mechanism causing rapid neck acceleration-deceleration. Immediate onset of symptoms.
C4 Examination: On examination, neck range of motion is limited by pain. Tenderness is present in cervical musculature. Neurological examination is normal.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs of cervical spine may be normal or show straightening. MRI may demonstrate soft tissue injury or disc herniation.
#426 Little League Elbow Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (young baseball pitcher with medial elbow pain), each subsequent clue adds specificity (throwing activity, valgus stress test, medial epicondyle pathology on imaging), and progression is clear and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young baseball pitcher presents with medial elbow pain. The pain develops during throwing activity.
C2 Activity: Pain is triggered by throwing, particularly with high-velocity throwing. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with throwing sport.
C3 History: Young athlete engaged in overhead throwing sports. Symptoms develop with increased throwing volume or intensity.
C4 Examination: On examination, medial elbow tenderness is present over the medial epicondyle. Valgus stress test reproduces pain.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs may demonstrate medial epicondyle avulsion or fragmentation. MRI characterises soft tissue involvement and ligamentous injury.
#427 Post-Amputation Rehabilitation Limbs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is a rehabilitation phase rather than a discrete diagnosis, clues are vague and could apply to any early post-operative rehabilitation, and there is minimal clinical differentiation—not suitable for a diagnosis-guessing game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient recovering from amputation presents with significant functional loss requiring rehabilitation. The patient requires prosthetic fitting and training.
C2 Activity: Severe functional limitation is present with loss of limb function. The patient is learning to ambulate with prosthesis.
C3 History: Amputation performed for limb loss from trauma, infection, or vascular disease. The patient is in early post-operative rehabilitation.
C4 Examination: On examination, residual limb healing and shaping is assessed. Phantom pain may be present. Balance and gait training documents progress.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs of residual limb document healing. Advanced imaging monitors soft tissue changes with prosthetic adaptation.
#428 Cervical Disc Herniation Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (neck pain radiating to arm), each clue adds discriminating detail (extension and rotation triggers, radicular weakness, Spurling's test, MRI confirmation with neural compression), and progression is logical and clinically coherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged adult presents with neck pain that radiates into one arm. The pain has developed gradually or acutely following minor trauma or activity.
C2 Activity: Pain is reproducibly triggered by certain neck movements, particularly extension and ipsilateral rotation. The patient reports functional limitation with arm activities and difficulty maintaining posture.
C3 History: History of neck pain may be present. Symptoms began spontaneously or after minor trauma. The patient may have prior history of cervical spine problems. Symptoms are activity-dependent.
C4 Examination: On examination, neck movement is limited, particularly extension and ipsilateral side-bending. Upper extremity weakness is demonstrated in a dermatomal distribution. Diminished reflexes may be present. Spurling's test reproduces radicular pain.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging of cervical spine demonstrates disc herniation at the affected level with nerve root compression and narrowing of the neural foramen. The herniated material displaces neural structures.
#429 Motor Neurone Disease CNS
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Strong clinical narrative with good clue progression, but Clue 4's mention of fasciculations and asymmetric weakness pattern combined with EMG in Clue 5 may be slightly redundant; otherwise the puzzle is educational and appropriately challenging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged adult presents with progressive weakness in the hands affecting fine motor tasks. The patient reports difficulty with activities including writing and buttoning clothes over the past several months.
C2 Activity: Functional decline is evident with impaired grip strength, difficulty with coordinated movements, and progressive inability to perform occupational tasks. The patient demonstrates a characteristic pattern of weakness that spreads to involve multiple limb regions over time.
C3 History: Symptoms began focally in one region and gradually spread to involve other body areas. The patient reports no sensory changes, preserved cognition, and no significant trauma or infection history. Progressive nature over 6-12 months noted.
C4 Examination: On examination, visible muscle fasciculations are present in affected regions, with measurable atrophy. Motor strength demonstrates asymmetric weakness pattern. Hyperreflexia is noted in hyperexcitable muscle groups. Cranial nerve examination may reveal mild tongue atrophy or fasciculations.
C5 Imaging: Electromyography (EMG) demonstrates acute denervation with fibrillations and positive sharp waves in affected muscles, consistent with anterior horn cell loss. Needle EMG confirms motor unit action potential changes. MRI of brain and spine excludes structural lesions or spinal cord compression.
#430 Lumbar Spondylolisthesis Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 establishes broad context (progressive lower back pain with leg weakness and relief with forward bending), clues progress logically through activity triggers (claudication-like pattern), examination findings (extension loading, palpation tenderness), and imaging (anterior slipping at L4-L5 with stenosis); clinically rich and fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 56-year-old accountant presents with progressive lower back pain and leg weakness that worsens with extended periods of standing or walking. The patient reports a forward-leaning posture is more comfortable than standing upright. Symptoms began 18 months ago and have gradually deteriorated.
C2 Activity: Walking forward uphill is tolerated better than walking backward or on flat surfaces. Prolonged standing without support causes severe pain and cramping in both legs. Sitting down or leaning forward significantly relieves symptoms within minutes. The patient can walk further when bent forward, using a shopping cart for support.
C3 History: The patient has had a 20-year history of intermittent lower back pain managed conservatively. No history of prior spinal surgery. Recent fall from a ladder with direct blow to the lumbar region occurred 18 months ago, coinciding with symptom onset. He denies bowel or bladder changes. Family history of osteoporosis is noted.
C4 Examination: Forward spinal flexion reproduces pain, but lumbar extension is limited and painful. Straight leg raise testing is negative. Neurological examination reveals normal strength and reflexes bilaterally. Palpation over the spinous process at L4-L5 elicits tenderness. Standing extension loading test increases symptoms.
C5 Imaging: Plain film X-rays show anterior slipping of L4 on L5 measuring approximately 15-20% of the vertebral body width, with associated degenerative changes. MRI confirms anterior spondylolisthesis at L4-L5 with canal narrowing and bilateral foraminal stenosis. No instability on flexion-extension radiographs. Disc signal is mildly decreased at the slipped level.
#431 Lateral Collateral Ligament Sprain Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 mentions both varus stress AND inversion mechanism (inversion is ankle-specific), creating slight anatomical confusion; the progression is otherwise solid but this detail weakens the opening clue's clarity.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with knee injury presents with pain on the lateral knee. The injury occurred with varus stress or inversion mechanism.
C2 Activity: Functional limitation is present with difficulty with walking and activities causing knee pain.
C3 History: Acute injury mechanism documented. The patient may have prior ankle or knee injury history.
C4 Examination: On examination, lateral knee tenderness is present. Varus stress test demonstrates excessive varus motion indicating ligamentous injury.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates lateral collateral ligament tear with characterisation of tear severity and associated injuries.
#432 Osteoarthritis Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Broad C1 (hand pain and swelling in older adult), strong clinical progression with occupational history, detailed examination findings (Heberden nodes, reduced grip), and definitive imaging; excellent educational puzzle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old woman reports progressive pain and swelling in her hand joints, particularly affecting the distal interphalangeal joints, with morning stiffness lasting 20 minutes. Pain is worse in the dominant hand and has progressed over several years.
C2 Activity: Fine motor activities such as writing, sewing, and opening jars are significantly limited by hand pain and weakness. Carrying objects with a firm grip worsens symptoms. Hand elevation and ice provide temporary relief; prolonged use causes pain flare-ups.
C3 History: The patient has had repetitive hand use occupationally (seamstress) for 40 years and experienced a distal interphalangeal joint injury at age 35. She has no systemic rheumatologic symptoms, fevers, or rashes. Family history significant for osteoarthritis in the paternal line.
C4 Examination: Palpable osteophytes (Heberden nodes) at the distal interphalangeal joints of fingers 2-5 bilaterally. Hand grip strength reduced to 18 kg on right (age-expected 25-30 kg). Finger ROM limited at distal joints, with crepitus appreciated. No synovitis or joint warmth.
C5 Imaging: Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing primarily at distal interphalangeal joints with prominent marginal osteophytes and subchondral bone sclerosis. No evidence of erosions, periostitis, or soft tissue calcifications. Metacarpophalangeal joints spared.
#433 Pilon Fracture Ankle
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 is quite specific ('high-energy trauma', 'axial load', 'ankle deformity') and essentially narrows diagnosis early; clues are somewhat repetitive (deformity and swelling appear multiple times) with limited new information progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with high-energy ankle trauma presents with acute ankle pain and swelling. The injury is often from a fall from height or motor vehicle accident.
C2 Activity: Severe functional limitation is present with inability to weightbear. The patient demonstrates ankle deformity and significant swelling.
C3 History: High-energy trauma is documented with direct axial load to the ankle. Associated injuries may be present.
C4 Examination: On examination, ankle deformity is evident with severe swelling. The ankle is extremely painful with any movement.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate pilon fracture with comminution of the tibial plafond. CT imaging characterises the fracture pattern and is essential for surgical planning.
#434 Functional Neurological Disorder CNS
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 appropriately broad (acute paralysis in young woman under stress), excellent clue progression revealing inconsistency, give-way weakness, normal investigations, and positive functional signs; pedagogically valuable for physios learning differential diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old woman presents with acute-onset paralysis of the right leg that began suddenly during an emotionally stressful period at work 4 weeks ago. She is unable to walk independently and uses a wheelchair for mobility. She reports no sensory symptoms below the waist.
C2 Activity: She demonstrates inability to bear weight on the right lower extremity, yet exhibits inconsistent functional use of the limb during natural activities and when unaware of observation. Her gait pattern when attempting to walk does not fit typical neurological disease patterns. She shows dramatic relief of symptoms during emotionally supportive interactions but sudden worsening during stressful encounters.
C3 History: She experienced significant workplace conflict and interpersonal stress coinciding with symptom onset. She has a history of anxiety symptoms and perfectionist personality traits. She has seen multiple neurologists without definitive diagnosis of organic neurological disease. Prior psychiatric history includes adjustment disorder 3 years ago treated with psychotherapy.
C4 Examination: On examination, she demonstrates give-way weakness of right hip and knee with sudden collapse of effort when testing lower extremity strength. Hoover sign is positive indicating functional deficit rather than true paralysis. Gait when mobilizing without attention shows relatively preserved motor function compared to formal strength testing. Sensory examination is completely normal throughout. Reflexes are symmetric and normal.
C5 Imaging: Spine MRI including cervical, thoracic, and lumbar regions is entirely normal without spinal cord lesions, compression, or structural abnormality. Brain MRI is normal with no white matter abnormalities, acute stroke, or demyelinating lesions. Routine laboratory studies including CBC and comprehensive metabolic panel are normal. Comprehensive neurophysiological testing is entirely normal without evidence of neuromuscular disease.
#435 Stress Fracture - Metatarsal Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is appropriately broad (progressive metatarsal pain in runners), each clue adds genuine new information (mechanism → functional impact → training history → localized signs → imaging confirmation), and puzzle is concise and engaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with running activity presents with progressive metatarsal pain. Pain develops in the ball of the foot.
C2 Activity: Pain is triggered by running and impact activities. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with weightbearing.
C3 History: Recent increase in training volume or intensity coincides with symptom onset. Female athletes with amenorrhea are at increased risk.
C4 Examination: On examination, focal tenderness is present over the affected metatarsal. Pain is reproduced with single-leg hopping.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs may show callus formation at chronic stage. MRI demonstrates bone stress with periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema.
#436 Temporomandibular Joint Disorder Jaw
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent progression from jaw pain and clicking (broad) through functional limitations and stress history to specific examination findings (disc displacement, myofascial involvement) and MRI confirmation; rich clinical detail supports fair guessing.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old female reports jaw pain localized to the joint in front of the ear that has progressively worsened over 4 months. She describes clicking or popping sounds with opening the mouth and occasional jaw locking that interferes with eating. Pain is dull and achy, worsening toward the end of the day.
C2 Activity: Chewing tough foods and prolonged talking exacerbate pain and clicking significantly. The patient reports difficulty eating harder foods and now avoids them. Yawning reproduces the locking sensation. Resting the jaw and applying ice provide moderate relief. She clenches her teeth during stressful situations.
C3 History: The patient reports a high-stress occupation with recent significant life changes. She works in a fast-paced environment and admits to daytime jaw clenching and nighttime teeth grinding. Dental history includes recent orthodontic treatment completed 2 years ago. No history of jaw trauma or prior TMJ disorders.
C4 Examination: Palpation over the temporomandibular joint bilaterally reveals tenderness on the affected side. Mouth opening measures 38 mm with deviation toward the right side. Clicking is audible during opening and closing of the mouth. Palpation of the masseter and temporalis muscles reveals significant myofascial trigger points with referred pain pattern.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows displacement of the articular disc anteriorly with possible reduction during opening movements, indicating disc displacement with reduction. Mild joint space narrowing is present. Signal intensity changes in the joint fluid suggest mild inflammatory changes. No evidence of osteoarthritis or significant degenerative changes on imaging.
#437 Smith's Fracture Wrist
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 essentially names the diagnosis by specifying 'FOOSH with wrist flexion' and 'volar deformity'—only Smith's fracture fits these details; clues are repetitive (deformity mentioned in C1, C4, C5) with minimal new information and puzzle is too narrow for a guessing game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with fall on outstretched hand (FOOSH) with wrist flexion presents with acute wrist pain and visible deformity. The deformity is volar (palmar).
C2 Activity: Severe functional limitation is present with inability to use the hand.
C3 History: Fall on outstretched hand mechanism with wrist in flexion documented.
C4 Examination: On examination, volar wrist deformity is visible. Significant swelling and tenderness are present.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate distal radius fracture with volar (palmar) angulation and displacement characteristic of Smith's fracture.
#438 Achilles Tendinopathy Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is appropriately broad (heel/calf pain in runners), logical progression from activity triggers through training history to focal tenderness and imaging findings, and clues build effectively without redundancy.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A recreational runner presents with pain and stiffness at the back of the lower leg. Pain is localised to the Achilles region and develops with activity.
C2 Activity: Pain is specifically triggered by running and activities involving plantarflexion. The patient experiences morning stiffness that improves with activity.
C3 History: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. The patient may have prior history of Achilles pain.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is palpable along the Achilles tendon. Crepitus may be appreciated with tendon movement. Resisted plantarflexion reproduces pain.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates Achilles tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation confirming tendinopathy.
#439 Supraspinatous Tendon Calcification Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong opening (shoulder pain with overhead activity in older athlete), excellent progression through functional limitations and trauma history, specific examination findings (painful arc, impingement signs), and definitive imaging with detailed calcification characteristics; highly educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old former tennis player presents with progressively worsening shoulder pain for 8 months, particularly when lifting the arm overhead. Pain is located over the top of the shoulder and radiates down the lateral arm. Sudden acute flare-ups occur with minor overhead movements.
C2 Activity: Overhead reaching activities, particularly reaching behind the head, provoke sharp pain and weakness. Carrying groceries with the affected arm is no longer possible due to pain and weakness. Lying on the affected shoulder is uncomfortable. Rest and ice provide temporary relief, but symptoms return quickly with activity resumption.
C3 History: The patient played competitive tennis for 30 years, retiring 10 years ago. She reports a fall on the outstretched hand 8 months ago that preceded current symptoms. Past medical history includes hypertension controlled with medication and mild osteoarthritis in other joints. She denies previous shoulder surgery or dislocations.
C4 Examination: Abduction of the arm reproduces sharp pain, particularly between 60-120 degrees. Supraspinatus strength testing shows weakness with painful arc. Positive Hawkins test and Neer's impingement sign are elicited. Infraspinatus strength is 5/5. Palpation reveals tenderness over the supraspinatus insertion on the humeral head.
C5 Imaging: Plain film X-ray of the shoulder demonstrates a focal rounded density over the supraspinatus tendon insertion area. CT imaging shows a calcification measuring 12 mm with a heterogeneous appearance located within the supraspinatus tendon. The calcification is located 8 mm lateral to the humeral head articular surface margin.
#440 Femoral Nerve Palsy Lower Limb
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 appropriately broad (acute post-operative leg weakness), excellent clinical progression from motor/sensory deficits through intraoperative mechanism to imaging showing hematoma and nerve compression; complex but fair and educationally rich.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old male presents with acute onset inability to move his affected leg after undergoing hip replacement surgery. He cannot walk and cannot lift his leg off the bed. The anterior thigh is noticeably swollen and tender.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform active hip flexion or knee extension. Sensory loss noted over the anterior thigh and medial lower leg in dermatomal pattern. Non-weight bearing and requires assistance with all mobility.
C3 History: The injury occurred intraoperatively during total hip arthroplasty when the femoral nerve was stretched or contused during retraction for surgical access. Post-operative imaging revealed significant hematoma in the psoas and iliacus region. No prior neurological deficits. The patient underwent urgent hematoma evacuation 24 hours after surgery.
C4 Examination: Hip flexion strength 0/5 with no visible iliopsoas contraction. Knee extension absent. Patellar reflex absent on affected side. Sensory loss to light touch in L2-L4 distribution. Complete paralysis of quadriceps and hip adductors.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the hip and proximal femur shows large hematoma in the iliopsoas and psoas major extending along the femoral nerve course. Nerve signal is interrupted at the level of the hematoma. Post-hematoma evacuation imaging shows partial reduction in swelling but persistent nerve compression. Nerve edema is visible extending distally.
#441 Plantar Fasciitis Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (chronic heel/arch pain fits multiple conditions), each subsequent clue adds specific findings (activity triggers, history of recurrence, windlass test, imaging), and progression is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old retired educator experiences chronic heel and arch discomfort extending across the sole of the foot. Morning pain is severe, necessitating 15-20 minutes of slow movement before tolerance improves. This chronic presentation affecting weight-bearing structures requires differentiation from multiple structural and systemic conditions.
C2 Activity: New or increased walking beyond 2 kilometers triggers escalation of pain for the following 24-48 hours. Elevated heels provide some relief. Running is not tolerated; even brisk walking produces discomfort within 10 minutes.
C3 History: Symptoms began 18 months ago following increased daily walking activity post-retirement. Patient has type 2 diabetes controlled by metformin with BMI 29. Previously experienced plantar fasciitis 10 years ago that resolved with conservative treatment within 6 weeks.
C4 Examination: On examination, pain is reproduced with palpation along the medial longitudinal arch. Windlass test produces marked pain with dorsiflexion of the hallux. Ankle plantarflexion range is normal; however, dorsiflexion is limited to 8 degrees bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: MRI reveals chronic thickening of the plantar fascia measuring 5.1 mm at the insertion point with heterogeneous signal intensity. Small calcaneal heel spur is visible. Marrow signal intensity at the calcaneal tuberosity is mildly altered, consistent with chronic inflammation.
#442 Femoral Nerve Palsy Lower Limb
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is sufficiently non-specific (progressive leg weakness fits many conditions), clues progressively narrow through functional loss, diabetes context, dermatomal sensory findings, and definitive MRI showing denervation/inflammation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old diabetic woman reports gradual progressive weakness in her right leg over the past 3 months without preceding injury. She describes the leg feeling heavy and numb.
C2 Activity: She reports severe difficulty with stairs and standing from a chair. Her walking tolerance has dropped significantly. She notes night-time thigh pain.
C3 History: The patient has a 15-year history of poorly controlled Type 2 diabetes with recent HbA1c of 9.2%. She reports unintentional weight loss of 12 pounds over the past 3 months. No history of leg trauma or prior neurological disorders. Recent imaging ruled out lumbar pathology. Vascular history is significant for peripheral vascular disease.
C4 Examination: Hip flexion strength is 2/5 with minimal antigravity power. Knee extension is similarly weak. Patellar reflex is diminished on the affected side. Sensory loss follows L2-L4 distribution.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the lumbar plexus and proximal femur shows signal abnormality within the psoas and iliacus muscles consistent with denervation edema. The femoral nerve appears enlarged proximally with T2 hyperintense signal consistent with inflammation. No focal mass or compressive lesion. Findings are consistent with diabetic femoral neuropathy.
#443 Osgood-Schlatter Disease Knee
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too specific (tibial tuberosity pain below kneecap in adolescent athlete nearly names the condition), and Clues 2-4 are highly redundant, repeating tibial tuberosity location and activity-related anterior knee pain without meaningful progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An adolescent athlete presents with pain at the tibial tuberosity below the kneecap. Pain develops during or after physical activity.
C2 Activity: An adolescent athlete reports anterior knee pain with activity. Pain is localised below the kneecap and worsens with running and jumping.
C3 History: Pain is reproduced with resisted knee extension and explosive movements. Functional impact includes difficulty with stairs, squatting, and returning to full sport participation.
C4 Examination: On examination, swelling and tenderness are present at the tibial tuberosity. Pain is reproduced with knee extension against resistance.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs may show tibial tuberosity enlargement and fragmentation. MRI demonstrates tibial tuberosity inflammation and soft tissue involvement.
#444 Deconditioning Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (fatigue/dyspnea post-illness fits multiple conditions), clues logically progress through functional limitation, immobility history, cardiac/pulmonary response patterns, and definitive exercise testing excluding structural pathology.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports persistent fatigue and shortness of breath with minimal exertion. Symptoms began after a prolonged period off work due to acute illness.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot climb stairs without stopping for breath and becomes exhausted after light household tasks. Walking >100m triggers symptoms.
C3 History: Prior illness, hospitalization, or prolonged immobility is documented. The patient has been inactive for weeks to months.
C4 Examination: Heart rate and blood pressure rise disproportionately with minimal activity. Resting heart rate elevated at 95 bpm. Six-minute walk test shows marked reduction in distance with dyspnoea at low workload.
C5 Imaging: Cardiopulmonary exercise testing shows VO2 max 40% below age-predicted values. Echocardiography and pulmonary function tests are normal; no structural cardiac or lung disease detected.
#445 Asthma Lungs
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 explicitly mentions 'history of allergies or asthma,' which partially names the diagnosis and over-narrows the differential; Clues 2-3 are somewhat redundant regarding variability and activity-related symptoms; restructuring C1 to focus solely on variable dyspnea/chest tightness would improve progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient with history of allergies or asthma presents with recurrent episodes of wheezing, dyspnoea, and chest tightness. Symptoms are variable.
C2 Activity: Patient reports recurrent episodes of breathlessness, chest tightness, and audible breathing sounds. Symptoms are intermittent and variable in frequency and severity.
C3 History: Unable to sustain aerobic activities without symptom onset. Symptoms typically resolve with rest or cessation of activity, though some persist for hours post-exertion.
C4 Examination: Symptoms worse during high pollen seasons and after exposure to cold air or specific allergens. Nocturnal coughing occasionally reported. No fever or recent infection preceding onset.
C5 Imaging: Spirometry demonstrates obstructive pattern with bronchial hyperresponsiveness. Response to bronchodilators documents reversibility.
#446 Athletic Pubalgia Hip & Groin
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad, but Clue 4's exclusionary statements (does NOT reproduce with hip flexion/knee extension, tenderness NOT further lateral) feel awkwardly constructed; simpler positive findings would improve clarity without changing diagnostic value.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An athlete presents with pain in the groin region or lower abdominal region. Pain develops with athletic activity and resolves with rest.
C2 Activity: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or twisting movements. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with sport.
C3 History: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. Sport involving rapid acceleration and deceleration is typical.
C4 Examination: Resisted hip adduction reproduces pain. Palpation over adductor insertion at pubis is tender. Pain does NOT reproduce with hip flexion or knee extension against resistance, and pubic palpation does not elicit tenderness further laterally toward the ASIS.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates adductor muscle strain or rectus femoris tear near the pubic symphysis. MRI characterises muscle injury and inflammation.
#447 Multidirectional Instability Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (shoulder instability/pain fits several conditions), clues progressively narrow through multidirectional symptoms, generalized laxity history, classic examination signs (apprehension, sulcus, relocation), and MRI-confirmed bidirectional labral/capsular pathology.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 25-year-old patient presents with recurrent shoulder pain and a sense of the shoulder 'slipping' or feeling unstable. Symptoms are triggered by overhead activities and throwing movements.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform overhead press or throwing activities without fear of shoulder giving way. Symptoms occur in multiple arm positions, not just one.
C3 History: The patient often has generalized ligamentous laxity. Symptoms develop with activity or may occur spontaneously.
C4 Examination: Apprehension sign positive in multiple planes. Sulcus sign present (inferior instability). Relocation test positive. Laxity testing reveals increased humeral head translation in anteroinferior and posteroinferior directions.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates anteroinferior and posteroinferior labral tears with enlarged capsule. Humeral head subluxation evident in multiple positions on dynamic sequences.
#448 Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction Grade 2 Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (medial ankle pain/swelling with arch collapse fits multiple foot pathologies), clues progressively add activity intolerance, progressive history, specific examination findings (navicular drop, inversion weakness), and definitive MRI showing partial-thickness tear with valgus deformity.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 56-year-old recreational jogger presents with progressive pain and swelling on the medial ankle that began insidiously 8 months ago without preceding acute injury. The foot is progressively losing its arch and turning outward. Walking on uneven surfaces has become increasingly difficult, and the patient describes a sensation of instability.
C2 Activity: Running is no longer tolerated; the patient can only walk short distances before ankle pain and swelling worsen. Walking on uneven terrain aggravates symptoms more than flat surfaces. Prolonged standing increases pain and swelling significantly. The patient reports the foot 'giving way' occasionally with walking. Rest, ice, and elevation provide only temporary relief.
C3 History: The patient has a 20-year history of sporadic ankle sprains, with no specific traumatic event preceding current symptoms. He runs 4 times per week on concrete surfaces. No surgical history. Past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He reports progressive loss of endurance over the past year.
C4 Examination: Significant foot pronation is observed during gait, with increased medial longitudinal arch collapse. A positive single-leg stance test shows loss of balance on the affected foot. Resisted inversion strength is 4/5 on the affected side. Palpation reveals swelling and tenderness along the posterior tibial tendon insertion. Navicular drop test measures 15 mm compared to 5 mm on the unaffected side.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the ankle shows thickening and partial-thickness tear of the posterior tibial tendon at the medial malleolar region, measuring 8 mm in width. Surrounding soft tissue edema is present. The spring ligament shows partial disruption. Hindfoot alignment demonstrates 8-degree valgus deformity with talonavicular uncoverage on coronal imaging.
#449 Cystic Fibrosis Lungs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 3 mentions 'pancreatic insufficiency with fat malabsorption,' which is pathognomonic for CF and essentially names the diagnosis; this clue appears too early and breaks the progression by jumping to systemic manifestations before pulmonary findings are fully developed.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports progressive shortness of breath with recurrent respiratory infections since childhood. Chronic productive cough is present.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot walk distances without stopping for breath and has difficulty climbing stairs. Daily activities are increasingly limited by fatigue.
C3 History: Long-standing pancreatic insufficiency with fat malabsorption. Family history of early-onset lung disease. Recent increase in sputum volume and colour change.
C4 Examination: On examination, decreased oxygen saturation with exertion. Fine crackles may be heard. Finger clubbing is present.
C5 Imaging: High-resolution CT demonstrates bronchiectasis and bronchial wall thickening. Pulmonary function tests show restrictive and obstructive patterns.
#450 Cerebral Palsy CNS
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is vague and generic (stiffness after rest could fit multiple CNS conditions), but Clues 2-4 rapidly converge on spastic presentation with specific bilateral patterns, and Clue 5 with perinatal hypoxic-ischemic injury is near-pathognomonic; the progression stumbles by being too broad early then too specific mid-puzzle without smooth information flow.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Child with progressive difficulty keeping up with peers during play and self-care tasks. Parents report stiffness that seems worse after rest.
C2 Activity: Cannot walk independently or maintain sitting without support. Gait is asymmetrical with one leg stiffer than the other.
C3 History: Difficult labour with low Apgar scores. Developmental milestones delayed from infancy. Symptoms have remained stable over the past 3 years with no deterioration.
C4 Examination: Modified Ashworth Scale 2+ in bilateral hip adductors and knee extensors. Clonus present at ankles bilaterally. Babinski sign positive. Weakness in antigravity positions.
C5 Imaging: MRI brain shows periventricular white matter loss with ventriculomegaly, consistent with hypoxic-ischaemic injury in the perinatal period.
#451 Hip Flexor Strain Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from acute hip pain to specific muscle tear; C1 fits multiple hip injuries, each subsequent clue adds meaningful diagnostic information, and C5 clinches the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 29-year-old competitive soccer player reports acute onset anterior hip pain that started 10 days ago during a match. Pain is sharp and localized to the anterior hip region. The patient felt something give while sprinting and was unable to continue playing.
C2 Activity: Hip flexion against resistance reproduces sharp pain at the injury site. Activities requiring hip flexion with extension of the knee, such as kicking or sprinting, are particularly painful. The patient walks with a stiff gait and leans backward to minimize hip flexion. Ice, rest, and anti-inflammatory medications provide moderate relief.
C3 History: Two-week period of increased training intensity in preparation for playoffs. Adequate warm-up performed before the injury match. No prior hip injuries, surgery, or inflammatory history. Competes at high frequency (4-6 times weekly).
C4 Examination: Hip flexion strength reduced to 4/5 with pain on resistance. Palpation tender just below the inguinal ligament. Visible bruising in anterior hip region. Hip extension range of motion appears slightly limited on the affected side.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the hip demonstrates acute partial-thickness tear of the iliopsoas muscle at the myotendinous junction approximately 2 cm distal to the inguinal ligament. Muscle edema and hemorrhage surround the tear site. No muscle retraction. Underlying hip joint and bony anatomy appear normal. Findings consistent with acute muscle strain grade 2.
#452 Non-Specific Low Back Pain Lumbar Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 is too vague and essentially names the diagnosis; the condition lacks specificity needed for a guessing game and the clinical picture (unremarkable imaging with non-specific pain) doesn't drive toward a single answerable diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old with low back discomfort that developed gradually over 3 months. Pain is dull and aching, worse with certain activities and better with rest.
C2 Activity: Symptoms are reproducibly aggravated by prolonged sitting and forward bending, but improved with lying down and gentle movement. The patient has difficulty maintaining work-related postures but can walk without limitation.
C3 History: No clear precipitating injury. Patient reports occupational stress and has been sedentary for the past 18 months. This is the first episode of significant back pain.
C4 Examination: Active lumbar flexion and extension achieved with mild discomfort; no restriction in ROM. Palpation reveals bilateral paraspinal muscle tightness, most prominent in lower lumbar segments. No neurological deficits detected.
C5 Imaging: Lumbar spine MRI is unremarkable: no structural pathology, no disc herniation, no facet hypertrophy, no evidence of spondylosis or stenosis.
#453 Oblique Strain Trunk
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C4 repeats injury mechanism and history already covered in C3 rather than adding new examination findings; otherwise solid progression from lateral trunk pain to specific muscle tear.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old recreational athlete presents with acute lateral trunk pain following an overhead throwing activity. The pain is sharp and well-localized to one side of the trunk, onset immediate during sport.
C2 Activity: The patient experiences severe pain with trunk rotation toward the affected side and with lateral flexion away from the side. Activities requiring core stability such as throwing, twisting, and lunging reproduce the pain sharply. Walking and rest positions provide relief.
C3 History: The injury occurred during competitive recreational baseball without prior warning. The patient had not warmed up adequately before the throwing activity. No previous history of similar injuries; overall good health with regular exercise routine.
C4 Examination: The injury occurred during overhead throwing in cold, without preceding warm-up. No prior episodes of trunk strain or neurological history. Patient denies midline/spinal symptoms and reports no radiation of pain.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound of the lateral trunk musculature shows focal hypoechoic changes within the external oblique muscle consistent with acute muscle strain. No fluid collection or significant hemorrhage noted. The muscle fibers appear partially disrupted in longitudinal view.
#454 Adhesive Capsulitis Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong progression from insidious shoulder pain to severe ROM restriction to imaging showing capsular thickening; C1 fits multiple shoulder conditions, each clue adds distinct information, and the diabetes risk factor plus post-cardiac recovery context solidifies the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old man presents with deep, aching shoulder pain that developed insidiously 5 months ago. Pain is present at rest and notably worse at night, disrupting sleep. The discomfort is localized to the shoulder joint itself rather than radiating distally.
C2 Activity: The patient reports marked limitation in shoulder external rotation and overhead elevation, making it impossible to reach behind the back or reach high shelves. Internal rotation is relatively preserved. Throwing and overhead sporting activities are no longer possible. Pain is partially relieved with heat and gentle therapeutic exercise within available range.
C3 History: Seven months prior to symptom onset, the patient suffered a myocardial infarction and was advised to minimize shoulder activity during cardiac recovery. Stiffness and pain gradually developed in the weeks following resumption of normal activity. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed 8 years ago.
C4 Examination: On examination, shoulder abduction is limited to 85 degrees and external rotation is severely restricted to 15 degrees with hard end-feel. Forward flexion is limited to 120 degrees. Passive range of motion is similarly restricted in all planes. Rotator cuff strength testing shows weakness attributable to pain inhibition rather than true muscle loss.
