Scapular mechanics are intact.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates capsular thickening and synovial inflammation with reduced joint space volume. Coronal and axial sequences show circumferential capsular fibrosis particularly affecting the anterior and posterior capsule. Rotator cuff muscles appear normal without fatty infiltration. No full-thickness tears are present. Findings are consistent with chronic adhesive capsulitis.
#455 Shoulder Impingement Syndrome Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Good clinical flow from office worker with overhead pain through positive impingement tests to MRI showing tendinopathy and subacromial narrowing; C1 is appropriately broad, clues progress logically, and C5 confirms the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old office worker presents with 8 weeks of chronic shoulder pain. Pain is described as a deep ache with occasional sharp catching sensations. Overhead activities aggravate symptoms.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot tolerate sustained arm elevation or reaching overhead. Activities requiring repetitive mouse use and awkward shoulder posturing at work significantly worsen pain. Symptoms resolve completely when arm is at rest.
C3 History: Pain developed insidiously over 8 weeks without specific injury. The patient reports increased shoulder demands from recent work changes requiring frequent computer mouse use and awkward posturing. History includes poor shoulder muscle conditioning and rounded shoulder posture.
C4 Examination: On examination, Neer impingement test and Hawkins test are both positive, reproducing the patient's pain. Supraspinatus strength is 4/5 with painful weakness noted. Posterior shoulder tightness is evident with cross-body adduction stretch. Scapular dyskinesis is observed during active arm elevation.
C5 Imaging: Shoulder MRI demonstrates thickening of the supraspinatus tendon with mild signal hyperintensity consistent with early tendinopathy. Subacromial space is narrowed to 7mm. Acromial morphology shows a Type II hooked acromion contributing to space compromise.
#456 Cervical Facet Joint Syndrome Cervical Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 is somewhat vague (unilateral posterior neck pain fits many conditions), but the whiplash history and specific facet loading signs in C3-C4 provide good progression; imaging in C5 could be slightly more specific to facet changes alone rather than general degenerative findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged to older adult presents with unilateral neck pain that is mechanical in nature. Pain is localised to one side of the posterior neck region.
C2 Activity: Pain is aggravated by neck extension and ipsilateral rotation. The patient demonstrates asymmetric movement restriction. Symptoms are activity-dependent and relieved by rest.
C3 History: History of motor vehicle accident or whiplash injury 2–5 years prior, with intermittent recurrent episodes of unilateral posterior neck pain. No radicular symptoms or neurological complaints reported. Pain pattern is episodic; flare-ups triggered by sustained postures or repetitive rotation.
C4 Examination: Neck extension combined with ipsilateral side-bending and rotation reproduces pain at the affected level. Unilateral posterior neck tenderness localised to facet region (just lateral to midline). Cervical rotation ROM restricted ipsilaterally by 20–30%. Upper extremity strength, reflexes, and sensation intact; no dermatomal deficit or lower limb signs. Negative neural tension tests.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates facet joint hypertrophy and joint space narrowing at the affected level. MRI may show degenerative changes including osteophytes and capsular inflammation.
#457 Heart Failure Heart
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is a cardiac condition inappropriate for a physiotherapy diagnosis game; clinical presentation is non-specific (dyspnea, fatigue fit many conditions including pulmonary disease, anemia, deconditioning) and the game assumes musculoskeletal/orthopaedic scope.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old with a history of hypertension presents with progressive shortness of breath and fatigue over the past 3 months. Symptoms worsen with activity.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot climb stairs without stopping. Cannot walk more than 50 metres on flat ground before becoming severely breathless. Nocturnal symptoms affecting sleep.
C3 History: History of cardiac disease including prior MI, hypertension, or cardiomyopathy is present. Progressive functional decline is documented.
C4 Examination: On examination, signs of heart failure including peripheral oedema, elevated JVP, or orthopnoea are evident. Vital sign abnormalities may be present.
C5 Imaging: Echocardiography demonstrates reduced ejection fraction or diastolic dysfunction. Imaging characterises cardiac systolic and diastolic function.
#458 Intertrochanteric Fracture Hip
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 naming 'acute severe hip pain following a fall' is overly specific and could only fit a handful of conditions; the progression is sound from there, but C1 should be broader (e.g., 'acute hip pain with inability to move leg').
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An older adult presents with acute severe hip pain following a fall. Unable to move the affected leg.
C2 Activity: Patient held leg rigidly in a fixed posture and resists any movement of the hip. Severe pain with any attempt at mobility.
C3 History: History of osteoporosis. Fall occurred from standing height. No prior hip pain or dysfunction.
C4 Examination: On examination, the lower extremity is held in external rotation and abduction. Significant swelling is evident over the hip.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs show a fracture line between the greater and lesser trochanters, extending from the lateral femoral cortex medially across the femoral neck base.
#459 Medial Collateral Ligament Sprain Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from acute knee swelling with twisting mechanism through localized findings to MRI confirmation; C1 broadly fits knee injuries, clues add specific information about injury type and location, and C5 definitively names the structure and severity.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old footballer experienced sudden onset knee pain during a match. Describes a 'popping' sensation and immediate swelling on the inner knee after a twisting tackle.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot weight-bear fully on the affected leg. Pain is worse with pivoting movements and side-stepping, but forward walking is tolerated.
C3 History: Injury occurred during contact sport when another player's shoulder drove into the patient's lateral knee while the foot was planted. No prior knee surgery, but patient reports previous ankle sprains.
C4 Examination: Grade 2 medial joint line tenderness. Anterior drawer test is negative. Lachman test is negative. Mild effusion present.
C5 Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates medial collateral ligament tear with characterisation of tear severity and associated injuries.
#460 Degenerative Disc Disease Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong clinical narrative from progressive mechanical low back pain in an older patient with occupational history through morning stiffness and facet signs to imaging showing disc degeneration; C1 fits multiple chronic spine conditions, progression is logical, and C5 clinches it.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old retiree presents with progressive mechanical low back pain that has developed gradually over several years. The pain is typically localized to the low lumbar region and is worsened in the morning and by end-of-day fatigue.
C2 Activity: Symptoms are exacerbated by sustained postures, particularly prolonged sitting and standing, with the patient reporting need for frequent position changes. Walking and gentle movement provide temporary relief. Bending activities are limited due to pain rather than mechanical restriction.
C3 History: The patient reports a 40-year history of occupational heavy lifting as a construction worker. Previous episodes of acute low back pain have occurred over the past 15 years with increasing frequency. Multiple risk factors include smoking history, obesity, and sedentary lifestyle post-retirement.
C4 Examination: On examination, lumbar flexion is limited to 40 degrees due to pain, with loss of normal lumbar lordosis. Palpation reveals paraspinal muscle spasm. Facet joint loading tests elicit localized pain. Straight leg raise is normal to 75 degrees bilaterally. Lower extremity strength remains 5/5 throughout.
C5 Imaging: Lumbar spine MRI shows multiple levels of disc degeneration with decreased disc height, particularly at L4-L5 and L5-S1. Signal loss within discs is noted on T2-weighted images. Osteophytes are present at degenerated segments without significant central canal stenosis.
#461 Whiplash Associated Disorder Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute neck pain post-MVA fits many cervical conditions), clues progress logically from symptom pattern to examination to imaging confirmation, and the clinical narrative is coherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient presents with acute onset of neck pain and upper back discomfort following a motor vehicle accident. Onset was immediate.
C2 Activity: Patient reports pain radiating toward the shoulder and upper limb. Symptoms worsen with sustained postures and improve with rest and support.
C3 History: Injury occurred 2 weeks ago. Patient reports no loss of consciousness. No prior neck problems. Symptoms have plateaued; initial severe pain has subsided partially but stiffness persists.
C4 Examination: Cervical flexion limited to 20°; rotation and lateral flexion also restricted symmetrically. Palpable muscle spasm in upper trapezius and paraspinal muscles. Cervical myelopathy signs absent. Upper limb strength and reflexes intact bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Plain cervical radiographs show loss of normal lordosis and no acute fracture or instability. MRI demonstrates mild muscle edema in paraspinal muscles without disc herniation, cord compression, or canal stenosis.
#462 Gout Systemic
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Gout is a rheumatological/metabolic condition, not typically a physiotherapy diagnosis for first-line management; puzzle is outside core scope of Physiodle and may confuse physio students about when to refer vs. treat.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old male presents with acute-onset severe pain in the right foot that woke him overnight. The affected joint is swollen, red, and warm. Pain is throbbing and intense, significantly limiting mobility.
C2 Activity: The patient cannot tolerate any weight-bearing and is confined to bed rest. Even light pressure or passive movement of the foot causes severe pain. No other joints are affected.
C3 History: The patient has a 10-year history of hyperuricemia and had been non-compliant with urate-lowering therapy. Recent dietary indulgences included a heavy seafood dinner and alcohol consumption. He reports prior similar episodes that resolved over 1-2 weeks without treatment.
C4 Examination: Joint examination shows marked swelling with severe restriction of dorsiflexion. Passive motion triggers acute pain. Exquisite tenderness at the first MTP joint line. No other joints show signs of inflammation. Core temperature is normal.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound of the first MTP joint demonstrates hypoechoic joint effusion with synovial thickening and mild hyperemia on Doppler imaging. X-ray imaging shows no acute fracture or significant degenerative changes. Serum uric acid level is elevated at 8.2 mg/dL.
#463 Hallux Valgus Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (progressive forefoot pain with bony prominence), clues provide progressive detail from activity pattern to imaging angles, and the story flows logically for a physio-relevant musculoskeletal condition.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old woman presents with progressive pain and swelling in her forefoot, particularly affecting the great toe joint region. She first noticed a bony prominence on the medial side of her foot in her early 30s, which has steadily worsened over the past decade.
C2 Activity: Pain is aggravated by walking in fitted shoes and high heels, with symptoms improving when shoes are removed. The patient has difficulty fitting into standard shoe sizes and has compensated by wearing increasingly wide shoes. Running and prolonged standing significantly worsen symptoms.
C3 History: Strong maternal family history of progressive foot deformities. Patient wore high heels occupationally for 15+ years. No preceding trauma or acute injury. Progressive worsening despite activity modification attempts.
C4 Examination: Weight-bearing assessment reveals lateral deviation of the great toe with a prominent bony bump on the medial forefoot. The first metatarsophalangeal joint shows tenderness to palpation, mild effusion, and restricted extension range. Intermetatarsal spacing appears widened on visual inspection.
C5 Imaging: Weight-bearing anteroposterior foot radiographs demonstrate lateral deviation of the hallux with intermetatarsal angle of 12 degrees and hallux valgus angle of 28 degrees. The medial eminence is prominent. Early degenerative changes are noted at the first MTP joint.
#464 Congenital Torticollis Neck
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is slightly too specific ('neck asymmetry' and 'positional preference' essentially describe the condition); broadening to 'infant with restricted neck motion and head preference' would improve the guessing challenge.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An infant presents with neck asymmetry and limited neck motion. The head demonstrates positional preference.
C2 Activity: Infant unable to turn head fully to one side and prefers to look over the same shoulder. Difficulty tracking objects across midline.
C3 History: The condition is congenital, often with history of difficult delivery or positioning. Early intervention is important for outcomes.
C4 Examination: On examination, unilateral sternocleidomastoid tightness and restriction of neck rotation are demonstrated. A mass may be palpable.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates focal thickening and fibrosis of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, confirming muscle-based pathology rather than vertebral or neurological cause.
#465 Patellar Tendinopathy Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 2 ('worsens with resisted knee extension') and Clue 4 (tenderness at tendon insertion with resisted extension) are somewhat redundant; Clue 2 could better emphasize eccentric loading specificity or landing mechanics.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young athlete presents with anterior knee pain triggered by jumping and running. Pain is activity-dependent and reproducible.
C2 Activity: Pain is specifically localised to the patellar region and worsens with resisted knee extension movements. Aggravated by eccentric loading during landing.
C3 History: Symptom onset correlates with increased training volume or intensity. Recent changes in training are often contributory.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is palpable at the patellar tendon insertion. Pain is reproduced with resisted knee extension.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates patellar tendon thickening and hypoechoic changes. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation.
#466 Generalised Joint Hypermobility Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (multi-joint pain and instability), clues progress meaningfully from symptoms to family history to Beighton scoring to genetic confirmation, creating fair difficulty and good educational value.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports progressive pain across multiple joints with episodes of instability and swelling. Symptoms have been lifelong with variable severity.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to participate in contact sports or high-impact activities. Experiences recurrent sprains and dislocations, particularly at ankles, shoulders, and fingers.
C3 History: Patient notes mother and two siblings with similar lifelong joint problems. Early childhood marked by unusual flexibility; no discrete injury triggering onset. Prior physiotherapy and orthopaedic assessment without definitive diagnosis.
C4 Examination: Beighton score 7/9 (hyperextension of knees and elbows, thumb opposition, forward trunk flexion). Generalised ligamentous laxity with no focal instability pattern. Strength and neurological examination intact.
C5 Imaging: MRI of shoulder: no structural labral tear or rotator cuff pathology despite recurrent instability episodes. Spine imaging normal. Genetic testing confirms haploinsufficiency affecting collagen architecture, consistent with benign joint hypermobility syndrome.
#467 Whiplash Associated Disorder Cervical Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is nearly identical to Puzzle 460 (same diagnosis, same mechanism, same clue structure); having two near-duplicate WAD puzzles defeats the daily game format and wastes educational opportunity.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient presents with acute onset neck pain and stiffness following a motor vehicle collision. Injury occurred several hours ago.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to tolerate sustained postures. Pain worsens with combined movements (rotation with extension). Has not returned to driving or desk work.
C3 History: No loss of consciousness at time of injury. No significant head trauma or facial injury. Patient reports feeling 'whipped' backward then forward. Symptoms have persisted and worsened over 48 hours despite initial apparent minimal injury.
C4 Examination: Cervical ROM limited in all planes by pain and muscle guarding. Tenderness diffuse across upper trapezius, levator scapulae, and paraspinals. Headache elicited with neck extension. Upper limb reflexes normal; no dermatomal sensory loss. No signs of radiculopathy.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs of cervical spine: normal alignment, no fracture or dislocation. MRI cervical spine: signal changes and edema in cervical musculature and ligaments consistent with soft tissue injury; no cord signal change or significant nerve root compression.
#468 Necrotising Fasciitis Post-Rehabilitation Various
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Necrotising fasciitis is a medical emergency requiring urgent surgical intervention; it is outside physio scope of practice and inappropriate for a physiotherapy diagnosis game—physiotherapists would recognize red flags and immediately refer, not diagnose.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient recovering from recent surgery or traumatic injury develops severe pain and swelling in the affected area, with fever and feeling systemically unwell.
C2 Activity: Rapid progression of local swelling prevents normal movement and use of affected limb. Systemic symptoms (fever, malaise, tachycardia) develop within hours to days.
C3 History: Recent surgery or penetrating trauma 24–72 hours prior. Patient reports pain and swelling progressing faster than expected for normal post-operative healing or wound infection.
C4 Examination: On examination, rapidly spreading erythema and swelling are evident with severe pain out of proportion. Skin changes including bullae may develop.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates extensive fascial inflammation and fluid tracking along tissue planes. Imaging helps define surgical extent.
#469 Subdeltoid Bursitis Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (shoulder pain with overhead aggravation fits rotator cuff, AC joint, and bursal pathology), clues progress from activity pattern to focal examination findings to ultrasound confirmation, and the narrative is coherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old presents with shoulder pain and swelling, worse with overhead activities. Multiple shoulder pathologies should be considered.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform overhead reaching or repetitive arm activities without pain. Pain is worst during abduction movements; rests arm in protective position.
C3 History: Recent increase in overhead work over past 2–3 weeks. History of chronic shoulder strain and possible rotator cuff involvement. Onset acute rather than insidious.
C4 Examination: On examination, focal swelling is evident over the deltoid region. Shoulder abduction reproduces pain. Neer's test is positive, and direct palpation elicits focal tenderness. Shoulder range of motion is restricted in abduction and external rotation.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows fluid collection within the subdeltoid bursal space. Bursa is distended; no rotator cuff tear visible. Findings consistent with bursal inflammation.
#470 Postural Hypotension Cardiovascular
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Postural hypotension is a cardiovascular/autonomic medical condition requiring physician management and medication adjustment; it is outside physio scope and inappropriate for a physiotherapy diagnosis game—not a condition physios diagnose or treat independently.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 71-year-old woman on multiple antihypertensive medications experiences lightheadedness and near-syncope upon standing from bed in the morning. Frequent episodes of vertigo sensation.
C2 Activity: Cannot tolerate prolonged standing; symptoms worsen as day progresses. Able to perform seated tasks without limitation.
C3 History: Hypertension controlled with amlodipine and lisinopril. Recently diagnosed diabetes on metformin. Previous episode of acute dehydration from gastroenteritis 1 week ago.
C4 Examination: Blood pressure supine 148/90 versus standing 118/74. Postural drop of 30 mmHg systolic documented. Carotid pulses intact bilaterally. No orthostatic tachycardia noted.
C5 Imaging: Continuous cardiac monitoring during position change shows absence of reflex tachycardia despite systolic pressure decline. Suggests autonomic dysfunction or medication-induced orthostasis rather than hypovolemia.
#471 Occupational Overuse Syndrome Limbs
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (fits multiple upper limb overuse conditions), progression is logical, and Clue 5 effectively rules out nerve compression and structural pathology to confirm the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old administrative professional working 8 hours daily at a computer workstation presents with bilateral forearm and wrist aching. Symptoms began insidiously 8 weeks ago with gradual onset.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with prolonged typing and mouse use, easing with rest breaks. Functional impact includes reduced work tolerance and awkward posture adoption to minimize discomfort.
C3 History: No history of acute traumatic injury. Desk height reduction 8 weeks ago coincided with symptom onset. No prior similar episodes. Patient denies evening swelling or night symptoms.
C4 Examination: Multiple tender points throughout flexor and extensor forearm musculature bilaterally. No positive Phalen or Tinel sign. Grip strength reduced but symmetrical. Forearm pronation/supination mildly limited.
C5 Imaging: EMG and nerve conduction studies normal bilaterally. Ultrasound imaging shows no tendon inflammation, nerve thickening, or structural abnormality. MRI of cervical spine unremarkable—no root compression or myelopathy.
#472 Biceps Femoris Tendinopathy Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong clinical progression from non-specific posterolateral knee pain through activity patterns, load history, and focal tenderness to definitive imaging confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old competitive distance runner reports posterolateral knee pain that began 3 weeks ago. Sharp, localized discomfort near the knee joint line on the outside.
C2 Activity: Worsens significantly downhill running and high-speed sprinting. Subsides with walking and rest. Unable to complete training sessions. Pain on eccentric hamstring loading.
C3 History: Increased weekly running distance by 40% four weeks prior to symptom onset. No history of acute hamstring tear. Had lateral knee pain from IT band syndrome two years ago, now resolved.
C4 Examination: Tenderness over biceps femoris insertion at fibular head, exacerbated with knee flexion against resistance. Palpable tendon thickening at insertion site. Strength testing normal. Pain reproduced with resisted knee flexion.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps femoris tendon thickening and hypoechogenicity at fibular insertion. MRI confirms tendon inflammation without full-thickness tear. Bursa involvement possible.
#473 Hammer Toe Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple forefoot deformities; subsequent clues progressively narrow to the fixed PIP plantarflexion deformity, culminating in diagnostic imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 64-year-old woman with a 15-year history of progressive foot deformity. Now presenting with pain in the forefoot, particularly over a bony prominence on one toe. Discomfort worsened by footwear.
C2 Activity: Unable to wear narrow shoes or heels. Reports altered gait pattern and avoidance of prolonged walking. Pain prevents normal shoe selection.
C3 History: Lifelong hallux valgus and crowding in foot structure contributed to hammer toe development. Inflammatory arthropathy absent. Daughters have similar foot structure. No acute trauma.
C4 Examination: Fixed deformity at proximal interphalangeal joint (plantarflexion position non-reducible). Metatarsophalangeal joint shows flexion contracture with dorsal prominence. Prominent callus and corn formation overlying the bony prominence.
C5 Imaging: Weight-bearing radiographs confirm fixed plantarflexion deformity at the proximal interphalangeal joint with metatarsophalangeal dorsal subluxation. Early degenerative changes visible at the proximal interphalangeal joint articulation.
#474 Iliopsoas Bursitis Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Anterior hip complaint with activity specificity, clear overuse trigger, examination findings that differentiate from labral pathology, and imaging confirmation form a coherent diagnostic sequence.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old ballet dancer reports anterior hip discomfort with groin tenderness onset 2 weeks ago. Pain worse with activities involving repeated hip flexion and turning movements. Some radiation into the upper anterior thigh.
C2 Activity: Cannot tolerate sustained hip flexion or repetitive flexion-based movements. Stair climbing and prolonged sitting provoke symptoms. Activities requiring hip extension after flexion are particularly uncomfortable.
C3 History: Intensive ballet training 4 hours daily with recent choreography changes requiring greater hip flexibility demands. No prior hip pathology. Pain began 2 weeks after increasing flexibility training intensity.
C4 Examination: Hip flexion ROM limited to 110 degrees (pain-limited). Thomas test positive suggesting hip flexor tightness. Palpation of anterior hip/groin elicits focal tenderness. Pain reproduced with combined hip extension and internal rotation (FABER modification).
C5 Imaging: Hip ultrasound demonstrates bursa effusion anterior to hip joint. MRI confirms iliopsoas bursitis without labral pathology. Iliopsoas tendon visualized normally on imaging.
#475 Cervical Spondylosis Cervical Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 4 prematurely introduces myelopathic signs (Babinski, hyperreflexia) that are quite specific to cord compromise; Clue 5 then essentially repeats 'myelopathy,' creating redundancy in the final clues.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 60-year-old with several years of progressive neck pain and intermittent tingling in both arms. Pain worsens with certain neck movements. Recently noticed increasing clumsiness with fine motor tasks.
C2 Activity: Patient struggles with writing and buttoning clothes. Difficulty gripping objects with consistent force. Gait has become slightly unsteady over past 6 months.
C3 History: Decades of desk work and poor posture. Previous imaging 10 years ago showed disc bulging. No major trauma. Symptoms stable until 18 months ago when progression accelerated. No previous neck surgery.
C4 Examination: On examination, neck range of motion is limited in multiple planes. Upper extremity strength testing reveals deficits consistent with myelopathy. Pathological reflexes (hyperreflexia, Babinski sign) may be elicited.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates multiple level disc degeneration with osteophytes and spinal cord compression. The spinal canal is narrowed. Cord signal changes may indicate myelopathic compromise. Imaging confirms cervical spondylosis with myelopathy.
#476 Diastasis Rectus Abdominis Abdomen
✓ READY
Verdict: Postpartum context broadens initially, activity pattern and response to bracing narrow the field, and imaging precisely confirms the diagnosis without redundancy.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 3-month postpartum woman reports difficulty with functional activities and core stability. Notices her abdomen looks different and bulges when performing certain movements.
C2 Activity: Unable to return to pre-pregnancy exercise routines. Bulging worsens with carrying baby and stair climbing. Notably improves with external abdominal bracing/splinting.
C3 History: Normal pregnancy and vaginal delivery 3 months ago. Pre-pregnancy fitness level good with strong core baseline. Postpartum exercise initiated without clearance or modification for diastasis.
C4 Examination: Midline separation palpable, approximately 2.5 fingers width at umbilicus. Tissue laxity evident. Abdominal contraction causes lateral bulging of rectus muscles. Fascia integrity intact.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound imaging shows diastasis rectus abdominis with interrectal separation 2.8 cm. Linea alba appears attenuated. No hernia. Dynamic assessment shows separation persists with contraction.
#477 Anxiety-Related Hyperventilation Respiratory System
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 2 strongly signals hyperventilation (paresthesia, perioral tingling, rapid resolution with breathing control) making the diagnosis nearly obvious before Clues 3–5 arrive; progression could be more subtle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 26-year-old healthcare worker reports recurrent episodes of chest tightness and breathlessness occurring several times weekly. Episodes are accompanied by anxiety and sense of panic.
C2 Activity: During episodes, patient experiences dizziness, tingling in hands and around mouth, and a feeling of losing control. Symptoms rapidly resolve when patient slows breathing deliberately. Avoidance behavior is developing—patient limits public outings due to fear of episodes occurring.
C3 History: Known anxiety disorder for 3 years, previously managed with CBT alone. Recently started sertraline 6 weeks ago following increase in workplace demands and workload. No prior respiratory or cardiac diagnoses. Patient reports episodes are triggered reliably by stress or crowded environments.
C4 Examination: During controlled hyperventilation challenge, patient reproduces symptoms of paresthesia and lightheadedness. Arterial blood gas shows respiratory alkalosis (pH 7.48, low pCO2). Cardiac exam normal.
C5 Imaging: Capnography shows end-tidal CO2 below 30 mmHg during symptoms, confirming hyperventilation-induced hypocapnia. ECG normal excluding cardiac cause. Spirometry normal pulmonary function.
#478 Non-Specific Low Back Pain Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately fits many causes of chronic back pain; history of sedentary strain and imaging showing mild degenerative changes without neural compromise support the non-specific diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 41-year-old office worker with sedentary job presents with chronic lower back pain of 6 months duration. Mild to moderate pain (4/10 at baseline) with periodic exacerbations.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting, improves with movement and standing. Functional limitations include difficulty with prolonged work postures and household activities.
C3 History: Gradual onset without specific traumatic event. Repetitive strain from workstation setup. Mild obesity (BMI 28). Previous episode of low back pain 3 years ago, self-resolved.
C4 Examination: Lumbar segmental mobility unremarkable. Hip flexor tightness bilaterally. Core strength 4/5. Straight leg raise negative. No neurological deficits or radicular signs. Palpation of lumbar paraspinals reveals mild muscular guarding without acute tenderness.
C5 Imaging: Lumbar spine X-rays show mild degenerative disc disease at L4-L5 and L5-S1. MRI demonstrates small disc bulging without neural compromise. No significant pathology explaining symptoms.
#479 Middle Cerebral Artery Stroke Neurological
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 explicitly states 'sudden onset' and acute setting, which severely narrows the differential to acute stroke; the puzzle would benefit from a slightly vaguer opening complaint.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 58-year-old man with hypertension and heavy smoking history. Sudden onset right-sided weakness and facial asymmetry while at work. Symptoms began 2 hours ago. Now in acute care.
C2 Activity: Cannot walk independently. Speech difficult to understand. Trouble understanding spoken words. Cognitive changes noted.
C3 History: Hypertension poorly controlled at admission (BP 180/110). Smokes 20 cigarettes daily. No prior neurological events. Symptoms reached maximum severity within minutes of onset.
C4 Examination: Left-sided facial droop noted. Right pronator drift present. Increased reflexes (3+) in right arm and leg. Babinski sign elicited on right. Visual field defect on right side bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Acute CT brain shows hypodensity in left MCA territory involving motor cortex and corona radiata. Diffusion-weighted MRI confirms acute ischemic stroke. Angiography shows MCA occlusion.
#480 Venous Leg Ulcer Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: Initial presentation fits multiple ulcer types, activity response (worse with dependency, improved with elevation) and history of DVT progressively point to venous etiology, confirmed by duplex imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old woman presents with a painful ulcer on the lower leg that has been present for 4 weeks. She reports long-standing swelling and gradual skin discolouration in the same area.
C2 Activity: Pain is significantly worse when the leg hangs down and improves with elevation. The ulcer produces moderate exudate and requires frequent dressing changes, limiting her walking tolerance.
C3 History: She reports a prior episode of deep vein thrombosis in the right leg 15 years ago. Her current lower leg edema has been progressive and unresponsive to compression therapy attempts over the past several years.
C4 Examination: Medial malleolar region ulcer with depth 0.5 cm, irregular borders, beefy red base. Surrounding skin with lipodermatosclerosis and hemosiderin deposition. Pitting edema bilateral legs.
C5 Imaging: Duplex ultrasound shows superficial and deep venous insufficiency with valve incompetence in great saphenous vein. No acute DVT. Ankle-brachial index 0.95, confirming adequate arterial perfusion.
#481 Post-Total Hip Replacement Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue progression is clear and logical: C1 establishes post-surgical context, C2-C4 narrow to hip dysfunction, C5 confirms prosthetic positioning; fair challenge for students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old patient presents with hip pain and difficulty with lower limb mobility that began 6 weeks ago following a surgical procedure. Pain is exacerbated by certain movement directions. Patient is currently progressing through rehabilitation.
C2 Activity: Patient demonstrates guarded movement in specific hip directions (notably combined flexion with adduction and internal rotation). Weight-bearing is limited; ambulation only achievable with walking aid. Stairs and transfers are significantly restricted.
C3 History: Patient underwent unilateral hip surgery 6 weeks ago for chronic degenerative joint disease. No prior hip pathology or revision. Comorbidities include age-related bone quality concerns. No neurological history or contralateral symptoms.
C4 Examination: Hip abductor strength is 3/5 throughout available range. Hip flexion to 80°, abduction to 25°, internal rotation severely limited. Trendelenburg sign present. No groin pain on palpation. No signs of swelling or warmth.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs confirm well-positioned prosthetic components with satisfactory bone ingrowth pattern. Cup and stem angles within normal parameters. Minimal periosteal reaction. No evidence of loosening, subsidence, or periprosthetic fracture.
#482 Supraspinatous Tendon Calcification Shoulder
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C2 and C3 are almost identical (both describe recurrent episodes and functional loss), and C4 repeats examination findings already in C2, breaking progression; clues need restructuring.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old man with shoulder calcification presents with recurrent episodes of acute shoulder pain. Previous episode 2 years ago, now recurring. Sudden exacerbation of symptoms.
C2 Activity: 58-year-old man with recurrent episodes of acute shoulder pain. Previous similar episode 2 years ago resolved spontaneously. Now experiencing sudden return of severe symptoms without obvious trigger.
C3 History: Overhead activities severely limited. Nighttime pain preventing sleep. Unable to perform shoulder abduction and forward elevation beyond 90 degrees. Functional losses similar to previous episode.
C4 Examination: Tender supraspinous region on palpation. Pain reproduction with painful arc maneuver. Guarding with shoulder motion. Scapular dyskinesis evident on movement.
C5 Imaging: Supraspinous tenderness on palpation. Painful arc reproduction between 60-120 degrees abduction. Scapular dyskinesis noted with active elevation. Strength testing normal. Neurovascular examination unremarkable.
#483 Cuboid Syndrome Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Logical progression from lateral midfoot pain post-inversion through activity limitations, cuboid-specific palpation findings, and imaging confirmation; well-scaffolded for physio students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 19-year-old soccer player presents with lateral midfoot pain after an inversion ankle sprain 2 weeks ago. Pain localized to lateral foot, not at ankle joint.
C2 Activity: Difficulty walking and running. Pain with weight-bearing on lateral foot edge. Unable to participate in training and competition. Worsens with plantarflexion and inversion.
C3 History: Inversion injury 2 weeks ago. Initial ankle swelling resolved within days, but isolated midfoot pain has persisted and worsened. No previous foot or ankle injuries. Patient is otherwise healthy with no relevant comorbidities.
C4 Examination: Tenderness over cuboid bone palpated from lateral foot. Pain with pronation and plantarflexion loading. Resisted plantarflexion and inversion reproduces pain. Positive cuboid squeeze test.
C5 Imaging: Foot X-rays show normal ankle and calcaneous alignment, but cuboid appears slightly subluxed relative to navicular. Talonavicular joint alignment assessed. No fractures identified.
#484 Middle Cerebral Artery Stroke Brain
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 is quite specific (sudden speech difficulty + left weakness in older patient with AFib) and may narrow the field too quickly; consider broadening to general acute neurological event.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 72-year-old woman with history of atrial fibrillation presents with sudden onset of speech difficulty and left-sided weakness. Symptoms began during morning routine. Immediate hospital admission.
C2 Activity: Unable to maintain balance or walk independently. Requires assistance with all mobility tasks and grooming. Left-sided limbs felt 'heavy' and uncoordinated.
C3 History: Known atrial fibrillation for 8 years, not on anticoagulation due to patient non-adherence. Hypertension managed. Previous TIA with full recovery 2 years ago. No recent head trauma or seizures.
C4 Examination: Left facial droop with asymmetric smile. Left upper extremity weakness 2/5, lower extremity 3/5. Pronator drift present. Hyperreflexia on left side.
C5 Imaging: MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging shows acute infarction in left middle cerebral artery territory, spanning cortical and subcortical regions.
#485 Cardiac Rehabilitation Post-MI Heart
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue sequence progresses logically from post-event rehabilitation context through exercise tolerance, cardiac history, vital signs, and stress testing; engaging and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old woman, 4 weeks post-acute cardiac event, referred for supervised exercise program. Reports dyspnea, chest discomfort with exertion, fatigue, and anxiety about physical activity.
C2 Activity: Exercise tolerance 2 METs at baseline. Dyspnea and chest discomfort with greater exertion. Anxiety about cardiac status limiting activity participation. Fatigue prominent.
C3 History: Inferior STEMI 4 weeks ago treated with primary percutaneous intervention and stent placement. Currently on guideline-directed medical therapy. Hypertension and hyperlipidemia managed. Quit smoking since event.
C4 Examination: Resting heart rate elevated at 78 bpm. Blood pressure normal at current status. No arrhythmias on telemetry monitoring. Cardiac murmurs absent. Peripheral edema absent.
C5 Imaging: Stress echocardiography demonstrates wall motion improvement in previously infarcted territory compared to acute phase. No new wall motion abnormalities. No ischemia provoked at achieved workload. LV ejection fraction unchanged at 40%.
#486 Trigger Finger Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from catching sensation through functional restrictions, occupational history, pulley-specific examination findings, and ultrasound confirmation; straightforward and fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 55-year-old typist reports catching sensation in right index finger with frequent triggering during work. Clicking sensation during finger flexion and extension. Progressive symptoms over 2 months.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform sustained gripping and typing activities. Morning stiffness in the right index finger that resolves within 30 minutes with activity. Most limited in data entry work.
C3 History: Occupational repetitive strain from data entry work 8 hours daily. Gradual progressive onset. No trauma. Previous history of mild wrist pain from RSI.
C4 Examination: Nodule palpable at MCP level of index finger. A1 pulley tenderness on palpation. Triggering demonstrated with active finger motion. Mild swelling over pulley.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows flexor tendon nodule and pulley thickening at A1 level index finger. Mild effusion around tendon. No partial tear. Dynamic ultrasound confirms tendon nodule catching.
#487 Peroneal Tendinopathy Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Solid progression from chronic lateral ankle pain through cycling-related activity exacerbation, prior ankle instability history, peroneal-specific palpation and resisted testing, and imaging confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 44-year-old cyclist with chronic lateral ankle pain and lower leg aching. Pain present over 4 weeks, worsening with lateral foot-loading activities.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with prolonged cycling and hill training; also reports giving way sensation on uneven ground or lateral stepping movements. Significantly impacting training frequency.
C3 History: Previous inversion ankle sprain 18 months prior that resulted in chronic lateral ankle instability. Cycling training frequency increased 2 months ago. Weak ankle stabilizers noted.
C4 Examination: Peroneal tendon palpable thickening and tenderness behind lateral malleolus. Pain reproduction with resisted foot eversion in plantarflexion. Normal anterior drawer test and talar tilt; ankle ROM preserved.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows peroneal tendon thickening with increased echo texture heterogeneity. Mild subcutaneous fluid. No tendon subluxation. MRI demonstrates tendinopathy without tear.
#488 Anterior Interosseous Syndrome Forearm
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent diagnostic progression: sudden weakness without sensory change narrows quickly, but thumb/index IP flexion loss is pathognomonic at C4, and nerve conduction confirms at C5.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old mechanic reports sudden onset of weakness in the forearm and hand 3 days ago. Notices difficulty with fine motor tasks and pinching activities. No pain, numbness, or trauma reported.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform precision gripping tasks required at work (holding small tools, fasteners). Weakness predominantly affecting thumb and index finger — other hand strength appears normal.
C3 History: Acute onset of weakness with overhead reaching activity at work 3 days ago. No trauma but possible nerve compression from muscle hypertrophy or pronator teres tightness.
C4 Examination: Thumb IP flexion weak/absent. Index finger DIP flexion weak/absent. Grip and wrist flexion strength intact. Sensation normal in median nerve distribution including first web space. Pronation strength normal bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction studies show slowed motor conduction in anterior interosseous branch of median nerve. EMG demonstrates acute denervation changes in flexor pollicis longus and flexor digitorum profundus.
#489 Post-Fall Anxiety Syndrome General
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is very specific (76-year-old with minor injuries but severe anxiety 3 weeks post-fall) and telegraphs the psychological diagnosis early; broaden C1 to focus on mobility reluctance without anxiety language.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 76-year-old woman presents 3 weeks after a fall from standing height. She reports significant anxiety, hypervigilance, and reluctance to move. Physical examination reveals only minor bruising.
C2 Activity: Patient refuses to walk unassisted and has become housebound. She reports waking at night with panic and catastrophic thoughts about another fall, despite no residual pain.
C3 History: Fall occurred while reaching for an object on a shelf. Prior to fall, patient was fully independent, lived alone, and walked regularly. Now refuses to leave home; family visits have become infrequent.
C4 Examination: Gait cautious and slower, avoids turning. Functional reach test limited due to fear rather than physical limitation. No demonstrable neurological deficit. Cognitive assessment normal.
C5 Imaging: Functional mobility assessment (Timed Up and Go) shows marked slowing primarily during turning phase. DEXA scan normal for age. Further psychological screening (GAD-7, PCL-5) shows elevated anxiety and intrusive thoughts specific to falling.
#490 Acute Wry Neck Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Classic presentation with clear progression from acute rotational injury through severe rigidity, preserved neurology, and normal imaging; playable and educationally relevant for physiotherapy students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 44-year-old woman develops acute onset severe neck pain after turning to reach something while seated. Neck rigidity and inability to move head. Pain sudden and severe.
C2 Activity: Unable to lift head from pillow without assistance. Arm and hand functional (no neurological deficit). Pain radiates across shoulders. Significant functional impairment.
C3 History: Sudden neck movement during reaching; no preceding trauma or chronic neck complaints. Symptoms began immediately without gradual progression.
C4 Examination: Significant cervical muscle spasm and rigidity palpable. Neck rotation severely limited in both directions. Cervical flexion-extension significantly restricted. No neurological signs.
C5 Imaging: Cervical X-rays normal; no fracture, subluxation, or significant degenerative change. Findings consistent with acute mechanical neck dysfunction without nerve root involvement.
#491 Fibula Fracture Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from nonspecific lateral leg trauma through functional loss to specific fracture localization; clue 1 fits common acute leg injuries.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 24-year-old soccer player reports acute onset severe pain to the lateral leg after direct contact injury during match. Immediate swelling noticed. Pain worsens with weight-bearing.
C2 Activity: Significant swelling developed within minutes. Unable to weight-bear or walk normally. Ankle movements painful. Could not return to play.
C3 History: Direct blow from another player's knee to lateral leg. Pain and swelling were instantaneous. No prior history of leg or ankle injury. No chronic conditions.
C4 Examination: Significant swelling and ecchymosis over lateral leg, mid-fibular region. Fibular head palpable bone tenderness. Pain with ankle plantarflexion and eversion loading. Ankle dorsiflexion intact.
C5 Imaging: Leg X-rays show simple transverse fracture of fibula at mid-shaft level. No associated tibia fracture. No proximal fibula injury. No ankle syndesmotic involvement.
#492 Guillain-Barré Syndrome Neurological
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too specific (progressive bilateral weakness post-viral illness moving upward is nearly pathognomonic); clue 5 contains EMG/CSF findings that clinicians would use for definitive diagnosis before physio assessment.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old man presents with progressive bilateral leg weakness beginning 3 days after viral illness. Weakness started in the feet and is moving upward. Now unable to stand without support.
C2 Activity: Progressing difficulty with fine motor tasks in hands. Facial weakness now apparent. Difficulty chewing and swallowing noted today.
C3 History: Upper respiratory tract infection 1 week prior with cough and congestion. Complete recovery from respiratory symptoms. 3 days post-infection, leg weakness began acutely.
C4 Examination: Bilateral lower extremity weakness 4/5 proximally, 3/5 distally. Patellar and Achilles reflexes absent bilaterally. Plantar responses downgoing. Sensation intact to light touch and pinprick throughout. Cranial nerves II-XII intact.
C5 Imaging: Electromyography shows acute demyelinating pattern with prolonged latencies and conduction blocks. Cerebrospinal fluid albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein, normal cells). Nerve conduction slowing distally.
#493 Muscle Contusion Various
✓ READY
Verdict: Broad clue 1 (direct contact anterior thigh pain) fits multiple leg injuries, progressive functional limitation, and ultrasound confirms hematoma; fair play for physio students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old recreational athlete presents with acute onset anterior thigh pain following direct contact during sport. Immediate swelling noted.
C2 Activity: Knee cannot flex beyond 90 degrees. Walking is severely limited. Unable to climb stairs or bear full weight comfortably.
C3 History: Injury sustained 2 hours ago. No prior injury to this thigh. No anticoagulant use or bleeding disorder history. Swelling rapidly progressive.
C4 Examination: Significant ecchymosis and swelling over quadriceps region. Knee flexion limited to 75 degrees due to pain and swelling. Quadriceps strength 4/5 due to pain inhibition. Palpable muscle firmness.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows muscle hematoma within quadriceps. Muscle fiber disruption visible with heterogeneous echotexture. No fracture seen on adjacent X-ray imaging.
#494 Long Thoracic Nerve Palsy Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 mentions 'pushing weakness' and clue 2 repeats pushing difficulty nearly verbatim; consider consolidating or making clue 2 add new functional context (e.g., specific ADL impact).
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 41-year-old woman reports progressive shoulder weakness and difficulty with pushing activities over the past 3 weeks. No clear injury event. Pain is minimal; the main complaint is loss of strength in the upper extremity.
C2 Activity: Patient reports difficulty with wall push-ups and bench press-type movements. Able to perform overhead lifting with the arm raised, but pushing motions cause significant weakness. Everyday activities like door closing and pushing off furniture are affected.
C3 History: No acute trauma identified. Possible nerve injury from heavy lifting or overhead activity. Gradual progressive onset suggesting nerve inflammation or injury.
C4 Examination: Scapular winging present at rest and exacerbated with arm protraction. Serratus anterior strength 3/5 on manual muscle testing. No sensory deficits in upper extremity. Shoulder flexion and abduction ROM full and pain-free. Trapezius and rhomboid strength intact. No cervical radiculopathy signs.
C5 Imaging: Electromyography shows denervation of serratus anterior muscle. Motor unit action potentials reduced and reinnervation changes variable. Nerve conduction normal for other upper extremity nerves.
#495 Lateral Epicondylitis Elbow
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 and 2 are nearly identical (both describe gradual lateral elbow pain from increased training); clues 3 and 4 overlap significantly with symptom progression and training history, creating poor information separation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old racquet sport player presents with lateral elbow pain and functional limitation. The patient reports pain with gripping and upper extremity use. Onset followed an increase in training intensity.
C2 Activity: A 45-year-old recreational athlete reports gradual onset of lateral-sided elbow pain over the past 6 weeks, coinciding with increased training frequency. Pain is activity-dependent and worse with repetitive arm use.
C3 History: Patient cannot comfortably perform overhead activities or sustained gripping tasks. Pain worsens progressively through the day with continued use. Rest relieves symptoms within hours.
C4 Examination: Symptoms developed gradually over 8 weeks without a specific traumatic event. Patient recently changed racquet and doubled weekly training sessions. No prior elbow injuries. No constitutional symptoms or night pain.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasonography demonstrates tendon thickening and hyperechoic changes in the extensor carpi radialis brevis tendon. Fluid is not typically seen. Imaging findings confirm lateral epicondylitis.
#496 Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (groin pain + limp in adolescent), each subsequent clue adds age/obesity context, movement restriction, and definitive imaging; engaging for physio students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 13-year-old obese boy presents with groin pain and limping gait. Pain began 2 weeks ago with insidious onset. Hip pain worse with internal rotation.
C2 Activity: Cannot participate in sports or climb stairs. Limping gait with externally rotated position preference. Pain worsens significantly with weight-bearing over the past 2 weeks.
C3 History: Rapid growth during puberty. Obesity (BMI 32 at age 13). No preceding trauma. Pain with physical activity but worsens with continued loading.
C4 Examination: Hip internal rotation severely restricted (near 0°) and painful throughout range. Hip external rotation preserved. Positive Patrick's test reproduction of groin pain. Knee examination and neurovascular status normal.
C5 Imaging: Hip X-rays (frog-leg view) show widened and irregular epiphyseal plate with femoral head slippage relative to neck. Posterior displacement of capital femoral epiphysis visible. MRI confirms SCFE.
#497 ICU-Acquired Weakness Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (post-critical illness fatigue and inability to move), progressive functional loss through clues 2–4, and EMG/biopsy findings clinch myopathy; fair clinical puzzle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 62-year-old man admitted 3 weeks ago with severe sepsis requiring mechanical ventilation. Now extubated but reports extreme fatigue and inability to perform basic movements.
C2 Activity: Cannot lift legs against gravity or roll in bed. Requires maximal assist to sit upright. No respiratory distress or pain with attempted movement.
C3 History: ARDS from severe sepsis requiring intubation 2 weeks. Prolonged sedation with paralytic agents. Minimal mobilization during acute illness. Nutritional support inadequate.
C4 Examination: Marked generalized weakness in all muscle groups. Hip strength 2/5, knee extensors 1+/5. Hand grip minimal bilaterally. Tone decreased. Reflexes intact.
C5 Imaging: EMG shows diffuse myopathic pattern without denervation. CK normal. Muscle biopsy demonstrates Type 2 fiber-selective atrophy with minimal inflammatory infiltrate.
#498 Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately broad (infant hip asymmetry on routine exam), each clue layers risk factors and classic clinical signs (Ortolani/Barlow), and ultrasound confirms dysplasia; suitable difficulty.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 6-week-old female infant. Parents noticed asymmetry in thigh appearance during diaper changes. No reported pain or fever. Detected during routine newborn screening.
C2 Activity: Asymmetric skin folds noted. Asymmetric hip abduction when hips flexed and externally rotated. No pain noted as infant non-verbal but shows discomfort with maneuvers.
C3 History: Born full-term vaginally, no complications. Female sex, first-born, positive family history of hip dysplasia in paternal grandmother. Breech presentation during labor.
C4 Examination: Ortolani test produces palpable clunk with hip reduction. Barlow test demonstrates hip instability and redislocation. Asymmetric abduction limited to ~60 degrees bilaterally. Bilateral gluteal fold asymmetry present.
C5 Imaging: Hip ultrasound shows dysplastic acetabulum with inadequate coverage. Femoral head position lateralized relative to acetabulum. Graf classification system used for assessment.
#499 Pelvic Floor Dysfunction Pelvis
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonable but slightly specific (exertional incontinence is a hallmark finding); clues 2 and 3 add good context but could better differentiate urgency vs. stress; otherwise solid progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old woman reports involuntary urine leakage triggered by physical exertion. Symptoms began approximately 1 year ago and have progressed gradually.
C2 Activity: Avoids exercise, impact activities, and public settings due to fear of incontinence episodes. Particularly problematic with jumping, running, and coughing.
C3 History: Multiple vaginal deliveries with instrumentation. Menopause 8 years prior. No prior pelvic floor rehabilitation or pessary use. Denies urgency incontinence or nocturia.
C4 Examination: Weak pelvic floor muscles on examination. Positive cough stress test. Normal detrusor function on preliminary assessment. Sphincter laxity evident.
C5 Imaging: Urodynamics documents urethral sphincter incompetence and bladder neck hypermobility on cough stress test. Detrusor function normal. Leak point pressure reduced at 45 cm H2O.
#500 Pulmonary Embolism Lungs
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately nonspecific (post-op dyspnea/chest pain fits multiple conditions), clues layer risk factors and exam findings without naming PE, and CTPA clinches diagnosis; engaging and fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old man, hospitalized 5 days post-abdominal surgery, develops acute onset dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain.
C2 Activity: Hypoxemia present (SpO2 89-90% on room air). Severe dyspnea with minimal exertion; cannot lie flat or mobilize.
C3 History: Post-operative day 5 from abdominal surgery for small bowel obstruction. Immobility during recovery period. Hypercoagulable post-operative state. No anticoagulation prophylaxis given.
C4 Examination: Tachycardia 115, tachypnea 28. Unilateral leg swelling and calf tenderness absent. JVD not prominent. Lung auscultation: scattered crackles bilateral bases. Lower limb DVT signs absent.
C5 Imaging: CT pulmonary angiography shows filling defect in right lower lobar pulmonary artery confirming embolus. Bilateral subsegmental emboli visible. Echocardiography shows right ventricular strain.
#501 Frailty Syndrome Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (covers multiple conditions causing progressive weakness), each clue adds new clinical information, and Clue 5 with albumin/hemoglobin/metabolic panel effectively narrows the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An 82-year-old man reports progressive weakness and slowing down over the past year. Has lost weight unintentionally.
C2 Activity: Unable to climb stairs without stopping. Walking distance reduced to approximately 50 metres before fatigue forces rest. Lives independently but leaving home has become difficult.
C3 History: No recent illness or injury to explain onset. Lives alone with minimal social contact. Hypertension and type 2 diabetes managed with medications. No recent hospitalisation.
C4 Examination: Hip extension strength 3+/5 bilaterally. Gait velocity 0.6 m/s, unsteady with reduced stride length. Current weight 10% below his usual baseline. Hand grip strength bilaterally reduced (unable to quantify precise values with standard dynamometry).
C5 Imaging: Serum albumin 3.2 g/dL (low). Haemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (mild anaemia). Serum B12 and folate normal. TSH normal. No acute infection or metabolic derangement on basic metabolic panel.
#502 De Quervain's Tenosynovitis Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple wrist/hand conditions, Clue 2 adds activity-specific pain pattern, Clue 4's Finkelstein test is pathognomonic, and Clue 5 confirms with imaging; good progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 44-year-old assembly line worker reports radial-sided wrist pain that began gradually 2 months ago. Pain worse with gripping and pinching activities. No acute injury recalled.
C2 Activity: Swelling visible over the radial wrist. Cannot perform repetitive gripping work. Fine pinching tasks are severely limited.
C3 History: Repetitive assembly line work for 20 years with poor workstation ergonomics. Symptoms progressed insidiously over 2 months without triggering incident. No prior similar episodes.
C4 Examination: Finkelstein test positive reproducing pain. Tenderness at radial styloid process. Thumb abduction strength reduced secondary to pain. First dorsal compartment swelling palpable.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows APL and EPB tendon thickening with synovial effusion. First dorsal compartment tendinitis evident. No tendon subluxation. Mild surrounding edema.
#503 Lumbar Radiculopathy Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (back pain + radiation), each clue progressively narrows with L5-specific neurological findings, and Clue 5 with MRI imaging confirms L5 nerve root compression definitively.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old manual laborer reports acute onset lower back pain with radiation into one leg. Numbness and weakness have developed over the past week, affecting ability to work.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with forward bending and prolonged sitting. Relief occurs with lying down and leg elevation. Weakness specifically affects ankle dorsiflexion and great toe extension.
C3 History: Onset followed a heavy lifting episode with acute low back strain. Chronic degenerative disc disease predisposes to nerve root compression. Progressive neurological symptoms indicate ongoing nerve compression.
C4 Examination: Straight leg raise reproduces ipsilateral leg pain at 45 degrees. L5 dermatomal sensory loss (dorsum of foot) present. Hip abduction and ankle dorsiflexion weakness (grade 4/5). Ankle reflex normal; knee reflex intact.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows L5 disc herniation with compression of the right L5 nerve root in the lateral recess. Signal changes in the L5 myotome on fat-suppressed sequences confirm active nerve compression.
#504 Age-Related Sarcopenia Whole Body
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 could be slightly more generic (sarcopenia vs. frailty overlap significantly), and Clue 3's sedentary lifestyle + reduced protein intake almost names sarcopenia; otherwise sound progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 76-year-old man presents with progressive loss of muscle mass and strength over 2 years. Difficulty with stairs and arising from chair. Generalized weakness noted.
C2 Activity: Significant difficulty rising from low chairs without armrests, climbing stairs, and carrying groceries. Walks slowly indoors. Requires rail support for safety.
C3 History: Sedentary lifestyle due to knee osteoarthritis. Retirement and reduced physical activity. Poor nutritional intake with reduced protein consumption. Progressive age-related changes.
C4 Examination: Generalized muscle atrophy visible in thighs and upper arms. Leg strength testing shows hip flexors 3+/5, knee extensors 3+/5. Slow gait velocity. Poor sit-stand performance.
C5 Imaging: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry shows low appendicular lean mass index (ALM/height² = 5.8 kg/m²), meeting sarcopenia criteria. Serum biomarkers show elevated creatinine-to-cystatin C ratio, reflecting reduced muscle mass.
#505 Groin Strain Groin
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple groin/hip conditions, Clue 2 adds adduction-specific limitation, Clue 4 with resisted adduction pain is highly suggestive, and Clue 5 confirms partial adductor tear with imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 35-year-old male with acute onset medial thigh pain during hockey game. Unable to continue playing. Pain began suddenly with no prior groin problems.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform hip adduction maneuvers. Pain significantly worse with skating and rapid direction changes. Antalgic gait limiting walking distance.
C3 History: Previous groin strains treated conservatively 2 years ago, fully resolved. Current injury occurred mid-stride during rapid deceleration. No neurological symptoms, no night pain, no radiation.
C4 Examination: Adductor tenderness at insertion. Pain with resisted hip adduction testing. Muscle guarding present. Swelling developing at acute phase. Antalgic gait evident.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows adductor muscle fiber disruption with hematoma formation. Fluid collection at injury site. Partial muscle tear confirmed. No complete rupture.
#506 Medial Tibial Stress Syndrome Lower Leg
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 explicitly names 'medial tibial stress syndrome' making the puzzle unsolvable; clues 2–3 are largely redundant (both describe shin pain with running); structure is fundamentally broken.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 26-year-old distance runner with previous history of medial tibial stress syndrome develops recurrent shin pain. Pain along medial tibia worse with running. Returning to training after recovery.
C2 Activity: 26-year-old distance runner with recurrent shin pain along the inner leg. Pain worsens with running and has happened before.
C3 History: Unable to tolerate tempo runs or high-mileage training weeks. Pain improves with rest but returns when volume increases.
C4 Examination: Pain began acutely 4 weeks ago after doubling weekly mileage. No recent trauma. Patient is female, no systemic disease. Previously treated successfully with load management and targeted strengthening.
C5 Imaging: X-rays normal without stress fracture. Bone scan shows uptake along medial tibia consistent with stress response. Similar pattern to previous episode of MTSS.
#507 Tibial Shaft Fracture Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (severe leg pain + swelling after trauma), Clue 2 adds deformity, Clue 4 confirms fracture findings on exam, and Clue 5 definitively establishes tibial shaft fracture with imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old with acute onset severe pain in the lower leg following a fall. Immediate inability to bear weight and rapid swelling. No prior leg injury history.
C2 Activity: Visible angular deformity of the lower leg. Gross swelling and bruising noted. Unable to tolerate any weight or passive movement.
C3 History: Mechanism occurred 6 hours ago. No relevant past medical history. No medication use. No prior lower limb injuries. Impact to anterior tibia.
C4 Examination: Visible deformity of lower leg with angulation at fracture site. Significant swelling and ecchymosis. Pain with palpation over fractured area. Distal neurovascular status intact.
C5 Imaging: Leg X-rays show transverse fracture of mid-tibial shaft with displacement. Fibula fracture also present (associated injury). No vascular compromise. Open fracture assessment performed.
#508 Sinding-Larsen-Johansson Disease Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple knee conditions in adolescents, Clue 2 adds jump-specific pain pattern, Clue 3 includes growth spurt context, and Clue 5 with inferior patellar pole MRI findings clinches the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 14-year-old male basketball player presents with acute-onset knee pain below the kneecap. Pain started 2 weeks ago during jump training.
C2 Activity: Pain is activity-dependent: reliably triggered by jumping and explosive movements during basketball. Pain-free with walking. Cannot tolerate prolonged kneeling or stairs.
C3 History: Adolescent growth spurt with rapid skeletal maturation. Intensive basketball training with frequent jumping. No prior knee pathology.
C4 Examination: Tenderness localised to inferior patellar pole on direct palpation. Pain reproduced with resisted knee extension and single-leg hop. No knee effusion. Full passive ROM. Normal patellar tracking.
C5 Imaging: Knee X-rays show small ossification center irregularity at patellar ligament insertion. MRI demonstrates inflammation at patellar ligament enthesis. Growth plate changes evident.

C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old woman presents with diffuse anterior knee pain with functional limitation. Pain with squatting, climbing stairs, and prolonged sitting. Gradual onset.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform single-leg stance or hop. Knee gives way with changes in direction. Pain worse with pivoting movements and prolonged standing.
C3 History: Sedentary occupation with prolonged sitting. Recent home exercise program initiation. Possible hip weakness and flexibility issues contributing.
C4 Examination: Positive Clarks test reproduces anterior knee pain. Vastus medialis strength reduced. Patellar tracking questionable. Pain with single-leg squat. Tight hip flexors and IT band.
C5 Imaging: Imaging normal without structural pathology. Patellofemoral cartilage intact on MRI. Q-angle and patellar tilt measured normal. Diagnosis based on clinical examination.
#510 Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome Cardiovascular
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 2 strongly hints at orthostatic intolerance (classic POTS presentation), and Clue 4's standing HR spike to 118 is near-diagnostic; progression is slightly front-loaded but Clue 5 with tilt table and norepinephrine still confirms it.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 28-year-old woman reports dizziness, palpitations, and fatigue that worsen when standing or exercising.
C2 Activity: Unable to tolerate prolonged upright posture (meetings, shopping). Symptoms improve when supine or sitting. Previously active but now mostly sedentary.
C3 History: Symptoms began abruptly 3 months after viral illness. No prior cardiovascular or autonomic symptoms. Lives in warm climate. Family history of syncope.
C4 Examination: Supine HR 68, BP 110/70. Standing 2 minutes: HR 118, BP 108/68. No presyncope relief with squatting. Normal cardiac auscultation.
C5 Imaging: Tilt table test: HR rises from 68 to 102 bpm within 3 minutes upright, sustained throughout 10-minute test. Systolic BP drops <10 mmHg. Plasma norepinephrine abnormally elevated supine (>600 pg/mL). Echocardiogram and Holter monitor normal.
#511 Peroneal Tendon Subluxation Ankle
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is appropriately broad (lateral ankle instability with catching sensation fits multiple conditions), clues progress logically with each adding specificity, and C5 is definitive for the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 28-year-old athlete with recurrent episodes of lateral ankle instability and a catching or popping sensation with activity over the past 8 months.
C2 Activity: Unable to tolerate cutting and pivoting movements in sport. Pain and instability worse with rapid ankle plantarflexion and inversion, such as during change of direction drills.
C3 History: Injury followed an inversion ankle sprain 8 months ago. At the time of the sprain, imaging suggested peroneal tendon involvement. Episodes of instability have been intermittent but recurring with specific movements.
C4 Examination: Palpable subluxation of peroneal tendons posterior to lateral malleolus with ankle position changes. Positive peroneal tendon subluxation test. Pain with resisted eversion.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound and dynamic MRI show peroneal tendons subluxing posterior to lateral malleolus with ankle plantarflexion and inversion. Superior peroneal retinaculum laxity noted.

✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 explicitly names 'COPD' and describes severe advanced disease, making this far too specific; a physio student guessing this would effectively solve it after the first clue alone.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 75-year-old man with severe COPD presents with progressively worsening dyspnea despite therapy. Continuous cough with minimal sputum. Severe functional decline. Minimal exercise tolerance.
C2 Activity: A 75-year-old man with a lifelong smoking history presents with progressive shortness of breath on exertion. He reports a persistent cough productive of minimal sputum. Over the past year, his exercise tolerance has declined significantly.
C3 History: Dyspnea occurs at rest and with minimal exertion. He requires continuous supplemental oxygen to maintain adequate saturation and has become largely housebound.
C4 Examination: Severe cough. Minimal air movement throughout lungs. Prolonged expiration. Accessory muscle use prominent. SpO2 low even on oxygen. Barrel chest prominent.
C5 Imaging: Spirometry shows severe obstruction, FEV1 25% predicted. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation and emphysema. ABG shows chronic hypoxemia and hypercapnia. Cor pulmonale suspected.

C4 Examination: Weak pelvic floor on assessment. Stress test positive. Bladder neck hypermobile. No urgency incontinence component.
C5 Imaging: Urodynamics confirms stress incontinence. Sphincter incompetence documented. Normal detrusor function without overactivity.

C1 Complaint: 19-year-old presents 2 weeks post-incident with persistent headaches, difficulty concentrating, and feeling 'off balance'. Reports memory lapses for recent events.
C2 Activity: Cognitive difficulties including memory problems and concentration issues. Headaches daily with light and sound sensitivity. Dizziness and balance difficulty. Functional limitation with school work.
C3 History: Witnessed loss of consciousness at scene lasting several hours. Post-traumatic amnesia extends beyond the period of unconsciousness. ICU monitoring initiated due to concern for intracranial injury.
C4 Examination: Mild cognitive deficits noted on basic testing. Photophobia and phonophobia present. Balance testing shows mild deficit. Headache reproducible with movement. Mood irritability noted.
C5 Imaging: Initial CT brain showed small epidural hematoma (managed conservatively). Follow-up CT normal. MRI shows diffuse axonal injury. Neuropsych testing shows mild cognitive deficits.

C2 Activity: 58-year-old man presents with shoulder pain and severely restricted arm use. Symptoms began 4 weeks ago following a surgical procedure. Currently wearing a protective sling and managing pain with medication.
C3 History: Cannot elevate arm overhead or use arm for any functional tasks. Pain increases sharply beyond approximately 70 degrees of flexion or 30 degrees of external rotation.
C4 Examination: Tear was large and involved two muscles (supraspinatus and infraspinatus). Repaired with open approach and anchor fixation. Patient is compliant with gradual ROM progression per surgeon's protocol.
C5 Imaging: Passive ROM: abduction 65°, external rotation 30°, flexion 70°. Significant scapulohumeral rhythm guarding and pain-inhibited movement. No neurovascular deficits. Pain 3/10 at rest, 5/10 with passive ROM attempts.
#516 Patellar Dislocation Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is appropriately broad (acute knee instability/giving way fits multiple diagnoses), clues progressively narrow through mechanism, apprehension testing, and imaging confirmation without redundancy.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young athlete reports sudden-onset knee pain and instability during a pivot-and-plant movement. The knee 'gave way' during activity and has not been the same since.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform directional changes or cutting movements without fear of knee buckling. Weight-bearing in slight knee flexion with trunk rotation reproduces apprehension.
C3 History: Patient is a contact sport athlete with a family history of knee instability. This is the first episode of acute instability; no prior dislocations or subluxations reported.
C4 Examination: On examination, positive apprehension test indicates fear of redislocation. Medial restraint testing reveals ligamentous insufficiency. Quadriceps strength is diminished. Swelling and joint effusion are present.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate a patella that is reduced in anatomical position with possible osteochondral findings. MRI shows acute ligamentous disruption of the medial patellofemoral ligament. Imaging confirms acute patellar dislocation.
#517 Intertrochanteric Fracture Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 describes fall-related hip pain in elderly patient (fits multiple hip injuries), each clue adds relevant clinical detail, and C5 with imaging is definitive; strong progression for a fracture diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An 82-year-old woman living independently presents to the emergency department following a fall from standing height at home, reporting severe right hip and groin pain with inability to weight-bear.
C2 Activity: She was unable to stand or take any steps after the fall and required an ambulance. Prior to the fall she was ambulatory with a walking stick indoors, and her mobility had been declining over the preceding months.
C3 History: History of osteoporosis with declining bone density despite supplementation. Two previous falls in the last year without fracture, but this fall occurred from standing height only. On warfarin for atrial fibrillation, creating perioperative management complexity.
C4 Examination: The right lower limb is shortened and externally rotated at rest. There is marked tenderness over the greater trochanteric and proximal thigh region. Any attempt at active hip movement produces severe pain, and a positive log-roll test is noted with axial loading.
C5 Imaging: Pelvic AP X-ray demonstrates a fracture line below the femoral neck in the region between the greater and lesser trochanters. The fracture is classified as an unstable 4-part intertrochanteric pattern, and orthopaedic referral for dynamic hip screw fixation is arranged.

C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old man reports persistent morning stiffness and aching in the shoulders and hips over the past 6 months. Symptoms gradually worsened before seeking care.
C2 Activity: Unable to overhead reach or comb hair due to shoulder pain and stiffness. Hip stiffness limits rising from chair and walking distance. Symptoms worst in early morning.
C3 History: Recent-onset bilateral shoulder and hip pain in a patient with elevated inflammatory markers. Minimal prior joint disease history. Rapid symptom progression over 2–3 months before presentation.
C4 Examination: Bilateral shoulder abduction and external rotation limited to 120° and 45° respectively. Hip flexion and internal rotation restricted. Proximal strength 4+/5 but pain-limited. No focal joint swelling or warmth. Grip strength normal.
C5 Imaging: ESR 52 mm/hr, CRP elevated at 8.2 mg/dL. No evidence of temporal artery tenderness or jaw claudication. Temporal artery ultrasound normal, excluding concurrent giant cell arteritis.

C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates posterior ankle impingement with flexor hallucis longus involvement. Soft tissue inflammation in posterior compartment. No bony pathology.

Verdict: Clue 1 naming 'Stage 1 pressure injury' and C5 stating 'no imaging required' with explicit NPUAP classification essentially gives away the diagnosis, removing any genuine guessing challenge.
