Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old woman in a nursing home presents with localized redness over the sacrum that she noticed after her recent hip surgery. She reports minimal discomfort in this area.
C2 Activity: Patient bedbound post-operatively with minimal independent repositioning. Requires full assistance from care staff to change position. Spends extended periods in same position during recovery.
C3 History: Hip fracture ORIF 1 week ago. Patient post-operative, immobilized during healing phase. Nursing home resident receiving turning assistance. No prior pressure injuries documented.
C4 Examination: Non-blanchable erythema over sacrum. Blanching test negative. Skin surface intact with no ulceration or tissue breakdown. Subcutaneous induration palpable beneath erythema. No purulent drainage. Sensation intact in area.
C5 Imaging: No imaging required for diagnosis. Clinical assessment confirms non-blanching erythema without tissue breakdown, consistent with Stage 1 pressure injury per NPUAP classification.
#523 Patellar Dislocation Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 broadly describes acute knee instability (fits multiple conditions), each clue adds specificity from mechanism to apprehension test to MPFL tear on imaging, engaging progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young athlete experienced acute knee pain and instability during a twisting pivot-and-plant maneuver in sport. The knee gave way suddenly, and they felt something shift in the knee before it felt 'normal' again moments later.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform cutting or pivoting movements in sport. Apprehension and fear of the knee giving way limit weight-bearing during lateral movements and weight shifts. Patient reports needing to modify or avoid multi-directional agility tasks.
C3 History: Acute patellar dislocation occurred during athletic activity with a pivot-and-plant mechanism. Underlying anatomical predisposition such as increased Q-angle or ligamentous laxity may be present. This is the initial episode of dislocation.
C4 Examination: Apprehension test reproduces fear of redislocation. Lachman and anterior drawer tests are normal, ruling out ACL injury. Patellar tracking is abnormal with lateral glide preference. Moderate joint effusion present. VMO strength noticeably diminished compared to VL.
C5 Imaging: MRI reveals acute tear of the medial patellofemoral ligament with bone marrow edema. Small osteochondral fragment visible on lateral femoral condyle. No evidence of other knee ligament injuries or meniscal pathology. Patella is concentrically reduced.
#524 Snapping Hip Syndrome Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 describes reproducible clicking (nonspecific), clues 2–4 narrow to hip flexion-extension pattern with positive Ober's test, and dynamic ultrasound confirms ITB snapping, solid logical flow.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 22-year-old female ballet dancer presents with a clicking sensation around the hip that started 12 months ago. Initially painless, it has recently become painful with activity and is affecting her training.
C2 Activity: The snap occurs reproducibly during hip flexion-extension and lateral movement patterns typical of her dance routine, with sharp brief pain that worsens with rehearsal intensity.
C3 History: She has no history of hip trauma or prior hip pathology. The onset coincided with a significant increase in her dance training hours twelve months ago. She notes the sound is audible to others in the room during warm-up exercises.
C4 Examination: Passive hip flexion-extension reproduces the snap palpable over the anterior-lateral hip. Ober's test is positive. Hip ROM is full with no restrictions. Pain is elicited with resisted hip flexion.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs of the hip are normal with no bony abnormality. Dynamic ultrasound imaging confirms snapping of the iliotibial band over the greater trochanter during the provocative movement, with no underlying bursitis or labral pathology identified.
#525 Sjögren's Syndrome Whole Body
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 essentially names the diagnosis ('progressive ocular and oral dryness'), and C5 jumps straight to antibody serology and biopsy that are definitive but belong much later in a guessing game; C2–C4 feel redundant.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old woman with an 8-month history of progressive ocular and oral dryness, with constant dry eye sensation affecting daily comfort.
C2 Activity: Significant difficulty with sustained near vision tasks (reading, screen work). Also reports difficulty eating dry foods and persistent thirst despite fluid intake.
C3 History: Insidious progressive onset over several months. Associated fatigue and joint aching. No prior autoimmune history. Symptoms progressive and worsening.
C4 Examination: Reduced tear production on Schirmer test (<5 mm in 5 minutes bilaterally). Xerostomia evident on examination. No focal parotid swelling. Ocular surface drying evident on slit lamp.
C5 Imaging: Anti-SSA/Ro and anti-SSB/La antibodies positive confirming Sjögren syndrome. Labial salivary gland biopsy shows lymphocytic infiltration. Rheumatoid factor positive.
#526 Lateral Epicondylitis Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (lateral elbow pain, work-related), clues 2–4 build logically through activity limitation and classic examination findings, ultrasound findings confirm, standard physio puzzle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old painter reports lateral elbow pain that started 6 weeks ago. Pain is worse with work activities but he cannot pinpoint a specific injury.
C2 Activity: Unable to work at full capacity. Pain limits ability to perform sustained gripping and any forceful wrist movements. Symptoms worsen as the day progresses with continued activity.
C3 History: Symptoms started without a specific injury event. Patient has continued working despite pain. No prior elbow injuries or treatment. No systemic illness or medications relevant to joint pain. Pain does not wake him at night.
C4 Examination: Lateral elbow tenderness at epicondyle. Painful resisted wrist extension. Mill's test positive with pain reproduction. Grip strength reduced bilaterally more on affected side.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound common extensor origin shows tendinopathic changes with heterogeneity. Mild surrounding edema. No complete rupture. Lateral collateral ligament intact on assessment.
#527 Elbow Dislocation Elbow
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 already states 'acute severe pain and deformity' which essentially names the condition; C2 repeats C1 verbatim; C3–C4 provide mechanism detail rather than new clinical information; structural breakdown.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old construction worker sustains blow to arm during work accident causing elbow injury. Acute severe pain and deformity. Swelling developing rapidly. Emergency care indicated.
C2 Activity: 42-year-old construction worker. Acute severe arm pain after direct blow to elbow during work accident. Visible deformity and rapid swelling. Severe pain limiting all movement.
C3 History: Arm held in fixed, abnormal position at elbow. Unable to passively extend or flex. Severe pain prevents any weight-bearing or functional use of arm.
C4 Examination: No prior elbow problems. First major injury. Mechanism: caught between machinery at high force. Immediate onset; patient did not attempt self-reduction. Brought to ED within 30 minutes.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs show elbow dislocation with possible associated fracture. CT performed for further characterization. Neurovascular assessment. Post-reduction imaging planned.
#528 Age-Related Sarcopenia Whole Body
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 describes functional decline in elderly (nonspecific), clues 2–4 progressively reveal widespread weakness and marked muscle wasting, and C5 confirms with objective lean mass measurement; fair progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An 81-year-old woman reports progressive difficulty with stairs and rising from a chair over the past 18 months.
C2 Activity: Unable to carry groceries or perform household tasks. Requires assistance with dressing and bathing. Lives with family due to safety concerns.
C3 History: Lifelong relatively low physical activity. Post-menopausal with inadequate nutrition. Recent decline in function over 1-2 years. Multiple fall history from weakness.
C4 Examination: Marked generalized weakness bilaterally. Slow gait with small steps. Hip and knee strength severely reduced 2-3/5. Grip strength markedly diminished. Visible wasting.
C5 Imaging: DEXA and bioelectrical impedance analysis show appendicular lean mass index <5.4 kg/m² (female threshold for sarcopenia). Muscle quality severely diminished.
#529 Obturator Nerve Entrapment Hip
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is specific enough (groin/medial thigh pain with numbness suggests nerve pathology), but C4 already names adductor weakness and medial thigh sensory loss which heavily narrow diagnosis; slight redundancy between C4 and C5.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old woman with progressive groin and medial thigh pain reports numbness and weakness. Pain radiates along medial thigh. Progressive symptoms over 2 months.
C2 Activity: Patient reports functional limitation with hip flexion past 90 degrees and internal rotation. Pain worsens with prolonged walking. No difficulty with abduction or external rotation. Able to perform most daily activities but avoids stairs.
C3 History: No preceding trauma or injury. Patient denies recent hip surgery or groin procedures. No history of pelvic mass or malignancy. Symptoms began insidiously while doing yoga with deep hip flexion and adduction positions. No improvement with rest or NSAIDs over 2 months.
C4 Examination: Hip adductor (gracilis, adductor longus) strength 4/5 bilaterally, weaker on right. Hip flexion strength intact. Sensory testing shows patch of diminished sensation on medial thigh below knee. Palpable tenderness at groin with hip flexion and internal rotation. Hip abduction strength normal. No gluteal involvement. Femoral nerve examination normal.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows possible nerve compression at obturator foramen. Obturator nerve pathway visualized with compression noted. No obvious mass. Possible anatomic variation.
#530 Wrist Sprain Wrist & Hand
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute wrist pain post-fall), but C4 specifically mentions 'scaphoid region' tenderness which narrows suspicion to scaphoid injury rather than generic sprain; C5 confirms soft tissue injury but clue 4 was overly specific.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 31-year-old with acute wrist pain and swelling after fall. Wrist held protectively. Unable to bear weight through hand or perform fine motor tasks.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with radial/ulnar deviation and gripping. Swelling visible within 30 minutes. Bruising emerging over dorsal wrist.
C3 History: Fall occurred during sport. Patient recalled immediate sharp pain but continued activity briefly before realising severity. No prior wrist injury. Wearing standard shoes, no medical comorbidities.
C4 Examination: Wrist swelling and ecchymosis present. Tenderness over scaphoid region and throughout wrist. Wrist motion limited by pain. Neurovascular intact distally.
C5 Imaging: X-rays negative for fracture. MRI shows soft tissue injury between scaphoid and lunate with surrounding edema and fluid. No skeletal involvement.
#531 Adductor Strain Hip & Groin
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from acute groin pain → specific movement limitations → training history → localized examination findings → confirmatory imaging; clue 1 broad enough for multiple groin pathologies.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 32-year-old athlete reports sudden onset groin pain during sport. Sharp quality, acute functional loss.
C2 Activity: Pain provoked specifically with hip adduction and hip flexion/external rotation activities. Difficulty with side-stepping and diagonal movements.
C3 History: No prior groin injuries. Recent increase in training volume over past 3 weeks. Flexible hips but limited adductor flexibility. Warm-up completed before injury.
C4 Examination: Tenderness along medial thigh in longitudinal distribution. Pain on resisted hip adduction (30° hip flexion). Adductor bulk palpable but no obvious discontinuity. Antalgic gait with hip adduction avoidance.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows muscle fiber disruption with surrounding inflammation. Hematoma forming. Adductor muscle involvement confirmed. No complete rupture.
#532 Persistent Postural-Perceptual Dizziness Vestibular
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 5 is overly definitive and essentially names the diagnosis via exclusion criteria and anxiety anchoring; C4 could better emphasize psychological features earlier in progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old man with chronic dizziness for 10 months reports constant swaying sensation. Balance difficulty with movement. Anxiety about symptoms prominent.
C2 Activity: Avoids crowded environments and driving. Symptoms worse when focusing on visual targets or when walking in open spaces. Social withdrawal due to fear of symptom onset in public.
C3 History: Mild head injury with brief dizziness 10 months prior. Dizziness persisted after physical recovery. Progressive anxiety worsened symptoms. Multiple specialist evaluations unrevealing.
C4 Examination: Postural sway present but mild on standing. Dizziness reproducible with rapid head turns and gaze shifts. Romberg test normal; tandem stance mild instability. Hyperventilation provokes dizziness and lightheadedness. Nystagmus absent. Dix–Hallpike and head roll tests negative.
C5 Imaging: Caloric testing normal, head impulse test normal. videonystagmography within normal limits. MRI head and internal auditory canals normal. No evidence of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, vestibular neuritis, or central pathology. Diagnosis anchored to persistent symptoms and anxiety profile rather than objective abnormality.
#533 Growth Plate Injury General
✓ READY
Verdict: Good progression from gradual knee pain in young athlete → activity limitations → growth/training history → localized tenderness → specific imaging findings; clue 1 fits osteochondritis, meniscal injury, and other youth sports injuries.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 15-year-old basketball player with knee pain during intensive training. Onset gradual over past 4-6 weeks, worse with jumping and running.
C2 Activity: Cannot tolerate plyometric training or high-impact sports. Symptoms worsen with repetitive loading. Some relief with rest.
C3 History: Intensive training program with frequent jumping and running. Rapid growth during adolescent spurt. Inadequate recovery between training sessions.
C4 Examination: Localized tenderness over medial aspect of proximal tibia. Pain reproduction on single-leg hop test. No effusion. Knee ligament stability tests normal. Full ROM.
C5 Imaging: Widened growth plate with increased T2 signal on MRI at proximal tibial physis. No metaphyseal fracture line or avulsion. Physis remains open and intact.
#534 High Ankle Sprain Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Logical flow from pain above ankle joint → external rotation sensitivity → syndesmotic examination findings → confirmatory imaging; clue 1 broad enough to include ankle sprains and syndesmotic injuries.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 29-year-old rugby player injured their ankle during a tackle. Reports pain above the ankle joint, worse with pivoting and weight-bearing movements.
C2 Activity: Unable to bear weight comfortably or change direction quickly. Pain reproduced with external rotation movements of the ankle.
C3 History: Injury occurred 3 days ago. Patient reports immediate swelling and severe pain, preventing return to sport. No prior ankle injuries. No physiotherapy yet attempted.
C4 Examination: Syndesmotic tenderness above ankle joint, between tibia and fibula. Pain with external rotation stress test positive. Squeeze test positive (pain with compression of tibia/fibula).
C5 Imaging: Ankle X-rays with weight-bearing show widened tibiofibular space suggesting syndesmotic disruption. Stress radiographs demonstrate increased talar rotation. CT further characterizes injury.
#535 Boxer's Fracture Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear clinical narrative from acute punch injury → inability to fist → obvious deformity → classic radiographic appearance; clue 1 fits multiple hand injuries but subsequent clues narrow appropriately.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 22-year-old male boxer presents with acute hand pain and swelling after a punch during a match.
C2 Activity: Cannot make a closed fist. Inability to clench hand without sharp pain over the knuckle area.
C3 History: Injury occurred 2 hours ago during training. Player felt immediate sharp pain and heard/felt a crack. No prior hand injuries or fracture history.
C4 Examination: Fifth metacarpal head swelling, ecchymosis, and angulation. Pain with palpation over fracture site. Prominence of knuckle reduced (depressed fracture appearance).
C5 Imaging: Hand X-rays show fifth metacarpal neck fracture with apex dorsal angulation (classic 'boxer's fracture'). Displacement and angulation measured on X-rays.
#536 Triceps Tendinopathy Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Good differentiation through posterior elbow pain → specific overhead/pressing activities → training overload history → insertion tenderness with weakness → confirmatory ultrasound; progression is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old recreational tennis player presents with posterior elbow pain that worsens with forceful arm movements. Pain began 4 weeks ago and has been gradually worsening.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform overhead throwing or serving motions. Pushing and pressing movements (like bench press or wall push-ups) reproduce pain. Training intensity significantly reduced.
C3 History: Increased tennis training volume focusing on serving technique. Repetitive overhead serving causing triceps overload. Progressive symptoms over 4 weeks.
C4 Examination: Marked tenderness over triceps insertion at olecranon. Resisted elbow extension reproduces sharp pain. Triceps strength 4/5 due to pain inhibition. Possible palpable swelling or thickening at insertion site.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows triceps tendon thickening at insertion with heterogeneous echotexture. Possible small tear or chronic inflammation. Olecrannon bursa may show fluid.
#537 Lumbar Spinal Fusion Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 essentially describes post-operative rehabilitation rather than a diagnostic puzzle, making it more of a management case; consider refocusing C1 on pre-operative presentation or reframing as post-operative recovery.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 45-year-old woman with low back pain and reduced spinal mobility 6 weeks after lumbar surgery. Pain controlled with analgesics. Incision healing well.
C2 Activity: Cannot bend forward beyond 20 degrees or extend backward beyond 10 degrees without pain or apprehension. Difficulty with stairs and prolonged sitting (>15 minutes). Unable to lift more than 5 kg.
C3 History: Prior non-operative management failed (6 months physiotherapy and analgesia). Imaging pre-operatively showed L4-L5 disc degeneration with facet arthropathy and intermittent neurogenic claudication. Symptoms progressively worsened over 2 years.
C4 Examination: Lumbar ROM: flexion 20°, extension 10°, lateral flexion 15° bilaterally. Straight leg raise 70° bilaterally, pain-free. Hip and knee ROM full and pain-free. Neurological examination normal. Incision well-healed, no signs of infection. Pain 2-3/10 at rest, 4-5/10 with movement.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative X-ray (6 weeks): Fusion hardware properly positioned across L4-L5. Lateral view shows appropriate restoration of disc height and sagittal alignment. No evidence of hardware loosening, malposition, or breakage. Early bony bridging between vertebral bodies visible on CT.
#538 Occupational Asthma Respiratory System
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Occupational asthma is outside typical physiotherapy scope of practice and diagnosis; this reads as a respiratory medicine puzzle rather than a condition physiotherapists independently diagnose or manage.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old assembly line worker presents with onset of dry cough and shortness of breath that worsens during work shifts. Symptoms began 2 months ago and have progressively increased in frequency. History of allergic rhinitis but no prior asthma diagnosis.
C2 Activity: Cough and dyspnea are significantly worse during work shifts and improve markedly during weekends and vacations. Patient notes associated wheezing and chest tightness but denies orthopnea, PND, or symptoms at rest during non-work periods.
C3 History: 3-year cumulative exposure to workplace chemicals without consistent respiratory protection. Symptom onset temporal relationship to recent workplace environmental change. No prior asthma, but baseline allergic rhinitis. No previous occupational lung disease screening.
C4 Examination: Spirometry shows obstructive pattern with FEV1/FVC ratio 65% and reversibility with bronchodilators. Peak flow variability exceeds 20% during work weeks versus non-work days. Bronchial hyperresponsiveness confirmed on methacholine challenge test.
C5 Imaging: High-resolution CT chest unremarkable, excluding alternative parenchymal or structural pathology. Serological testing for specific occupational sensitization markers pending.
#539 Vestibular Neuritis Vestibular
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong clinical story from sudden severe vertigo post-viral → constant disabling symptoms with head movement → unilateral deficit on HIT/caloric testing → normal structural imaging; differentiates well from BPPV and central causes.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.
C2 Activity: Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.
C3 History: No prior episodes of vertigo. Patient reports gradual return to upright posture tolerance over 48 hours. No medication changes. Works in office environment with minimal physical demands.
C4 Examination: Head impulse test shows positive catch-up saccade indicating unilateral vestibular deficit. Dix-Hallpike maneuver negative excluding BPPV. Caloric testing shows reduced response in affected ear (50% unilateral paresis). Romberg test positive with falls toward affected side.
C5 Imaging: MRI inner ear structures normal, no T2/FLAIR signal abnormality in IAC. No enhancement of CN VIII. Auditory evoked responses normal. Videonystagmography shows reduced caloric response ipsilateral to positive head impulse test.
#540 Adductor Strain Hip & Groin
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Very similar to Puzzle 530 (duplicate condition); while this version is slightly more acute/sprint-focused, having two near-identical adductor strain puzzles in one batch reduces educational variety and engagement.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old athlete reports acute onset groin pain during a sprint. Sharp medial thigh discomfort with sudden functional loss.
C2 Activity: Pain reproduced with hip adduction and resisted adduction. Unable to perform explosive multi-directional movements; weight-bearing in weight-shift activities is painful.
C3 History: Sudden onset during rapid acceleration/deceleration movement in sport. No prior groin injury. No preceding symptoms. Immediate pain and loss of load tolerance.
C4 Examination: Adductor tenderness at insertion near pubis. Pain with resisted adduction. Possible small myofascial defect palpable. Adductor weakness secondary to pain (4-4+/5).
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows partial muscle fiber disruption of adductor longus with surrounding edema and fluid. No complete muscle rupture. Strain injury with inflammation.
#541 Brachial Plexus Injury Shoulder & Neck
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (post-traumatic shoulder weakness), each clue adds specific findings (weakness pattern → neurological signs → imaging findings), and Clue 5 clinches diagnosis with EMG/NCS confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old motorcyclist presents 3 weeks after a fall with shoulder dislocation. Reports progressive weakness in upper arm and shoulder with associated pain.
C2 Activity: Weakness predominantly affects shoulder abduction and external rotation with inability to lift arm against gravity. Patient reports altered sensation in lateral shoulder and thumb region.
C3 History: Post-injury imaging demonstrated edema and hemorrhage in brachial plexus region. No prior neurological history. Patient reports symptom progression over 3 weeks with no improvement.
C4 Examination: Physical examination shows Erb's palsy pattern: waiter's tip position of arm with internal rotation, adduction, and elbow extension. Supraspinatus/infraspinatus atrophy visible. Upper limb reflexes (biceps, brachioradialis) diminished or absent on affected side. Horner syndrome absent (excluding lower trunk).
C5 Imaging: EMG/NCS shows acute denervation potentials in biceps, supraspinatus, and deltoid muscles with slowed conduction velocities. Motor amplitudes reduced in axillary and suprascapular nerves. MRI demonstrates brachial plexus edema and hemorrhage at level of upper trunk with partial continuity.
#542 Cervical Myelopathy Cervical Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 4 (Examination) repeats Clue 3 (History) almost verbatim—no new clinical examination findings are presented, breaking the progression model.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old office worker presents with progressive hand clumsiness and unsteady gait over 6 months. Reports difficulty with fine motor tasks like buttoning. Wife noted increased stumbling and careful walking pattern.
C2 Activity: Dexterity impaired more than strength in hands. Gait described as wide-based and cautious with reduced arm swing. Patient reports difficulty with stairs and uneven surfaces. Symptom progression noted with activity.
C3 History: Long history of cervical spine stiffness. Previous minor car accident 5 years ago. Gradual symptom progression over months suggesting chronic cord compression. No acute trauma. Smoking and age as risk factors.
C4 Examination: Long-standing cervical stiffness and neck pain. Motor vehicle accident 5 years prior. Progressive bilateral symptoms over 6 months. Age 58, current smoker. No acute trauma or recent exacerbation.
C5 Imaging: MRI cervical spine shows multilevel cervical spondylosis with cord compression at C5-6 and C6-7 levels. T2-weighted imaging demonstrates myelopathic changes with central cord signal hyperintensity. Sagittal diameter reduced to 10mm at most compressed level. No acute fracture.
#543 Guillain-Barré Syndrome Peripheral Nerves
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (progressive weakness post-GI illness), each clue adds distinctive information (ascending pattern → reflex loss → CSF/EMG findings), and diagnostic criteria are appropriately distributed across clues.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 34-year-old woman with 4 days of progressive weakness starting in the legs. Reports gastroenteritis with diarrhea about 10 days ago, which seemed to resolve. Now weakness is worsening and spreading upward.
C2 Activity: Cannot walk independently. Difficulty speaking clearly and chewing. Has noticed mild shortness of breath with exertion. Weakness symmetric on both sides of body.
C3 History: No prior neurological or autoimmune disease. Vaccination history unremarkable. Symptoms appeared suddenly 4 days after GI illness resolved, and have progressed daily without improvement. No similar presentations in family.
C4 Examination: Bilateral lower extremity weakness symmetric. Absent reflexes throughout. Ascending pattern with hip and knee involvement. Upper extremity strength testing begins to show weakness.
C5 Imaging: CSF shows elevated protein with minimal cells (albuminocytologic dissociation). EMG shows demyelination pattern. Nerve conduction studies show conduction blocks. Respiratory function monitored.
#544 Dupuytren's Contracture Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (progressive finger extension difficulty with palmar lump), subsequent clues narrow progressively through family history and examination findings, and ultrasound confirms diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 65-year-old man of Northern European ancestry presents with progressive difficulty extending his hand. He first noticed a firm lump in his palm 2 years ago and has increasingly struggled to straighten his fingers, particularly the ring and little fingers.
C2 Activity: Cannot easily perform activities requiring open palm positioning—difficulty with writing, typing, and personal hygiene. Grip strength subjectively reduced but pain minimal.
C3 History: Family history of similar hand contractures in father and uncle. Northern European ancestry (Swedish descent). Age-related presentation typical for Dupuytren disease. No significant trauma or repetitive hand use occupation.
C4 Examination: Physical examination reveals firm palmar nodules along flexor surface. Palpable longitudinal cords extending from palm into ring and little finger. Ring finger MCP joint flexion contracture 45 degrees, PIP involvement minimal. Table top test fails with inability to place palm flat on surface. Skin dimpling and pit formation noted.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates hypoechoic fascial cords extending from palm into ring and little finger rays with MCP joint involvement. No evidence of nerve or vessel entrapment. Severity graded moderate.
#545 Biceps Tendinopathy Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 3 (History) largely repeats information from Clue 2 (Activity) regarding activity-related pain; History should focus more on baseline risk factors or onset context rather than symptom reproduction.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old recreational tennis player presents with anterior shoulder pain for 3 weeks. Pain began during overhead serve motion. Reports occasional clicking sensation in shoulder during reaching.
C2 Activity: Sharp anterior shoulder pain with overhead activities, serving, and throwing motions. Pain exacerbated by resisted elbow flexion and supination. Discomfort improves with rest and ice. Patient experiences pain at night when lying on affected shoulder.
C3 History: Unable to serve effectively or throw overhead without sharp anterior shoulder pain. Pain worsens with repetitive arm elevation and persists through the night when sleeping on the affected side. Improves temporarily with ice and relative rest.
C4 Examination: Intensive tennis participation (4 times weekly) over the past 12 months. Gradual symptom onset rather than traumatic incident. No previous shoulder surgery. Screening examination revealed weakness and poor control of rotator cuff musculature.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps tendon thickening (5mm) with hypoechoic signal and increased vascularity on Doppler imaging consistent with tendinopathy. Subacromial space narrowed suggesting associated subacromial impingement. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration of proximal biceps tendon without full thickness tear.
#546 Carpal Tunnel Syndrome Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits many hand conditions (paresthesias/numbness), each subsequent clue adds distinct information (nocturnal pattern → work correlation → specific examination signs → electrophysiological confirmation), and progression is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged office worker presents with hand paresthesias and numbness. Symptoms are worse at night and disturb sleep. Fine motor tasks and gripping activities feel more difficult than usual.
C2 Activity: Symptom pattern is distinctly nocturnal—worst at night and upon waking. Shaking or flicking the hand provides transient relief. Daytime symptoms correlate with repetitive hand-intensive work but are far less bothersome than night symptoms.
C3 History: Symptoms began insidiously 3–4 months ago during a period of increased keyboard and administrative work. Patient denies preceding trauma or acute injury. Similar symptoms reported in contralateral hand but milder. No prior episodes of paresthesias or neurological symptoms elsewhere.
C4 Examination: On examination, Tinel's sign is positive over the median nerve at the wrist. Phalen's test reproduces symptoms after 60 seconds. Two-point discrimination is reduced in median-innervated fingers. Grip strength is diminished.
C5 Imaging: Electromyographic testing demonstrates median nerve conduction slowing across the wrist with fibrillation potentials. Nerve conduction studies confirm focal demyelination at the carpal tunnel. The diagnosis is established by neurophysiological testing.
#547 Pleural Effusion Lungs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Pleural effusion is primarily a pulmonology/internal medicine diagnosis, not a core physiotherapy diagnosis; while physiotherapists manage post-effusion complications, the underlying condition sits outside typical physio scope and educational focus.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old man with significant smoking history presents with progressive shortness of breath and chest discomfort over the past 2 weeks. Exercise tolerance markedly reduced.
C2 Activity: Shortness of breath worse with exertion and when lying flat on left side. Dull chest pain that increases with deep breathing. Patient reports mild orthopnea. No fever or productive cough noted. Symptoms progressively worsening.
C3 History: Recent chemotherapy 4 weeks ago. Oncology notes mention increased pleural thickening on prior imaging. No prior pleural procedures or drainage attempts. Progressive course over 2 weeks despite ongoing treatment.
C4 Examination: Dullness to percussion at left lung base with decreased tactile fremitus. Breath sounds markedly reduced or absent at bases bilaterally. Egophony positive over area of effusion. Hepatomegaly noted on abdominal examination suggesting liver involvement. Oxygen saturation 88% on room air.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray shows blunting of left costophrenic angle with meniscus sign consistent with moderate effusion. CT chest confirms left-sided pleural effusion measuring 4cm at widest point with associated right-sided smaller effusion. Pleural fluid analysis shows exudative properties with adenocarcinoma cells present on cytology.
#548 Bell's Palsy Cranial Nerves
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 5 (Imaging) contains examination findings (forehead wrinkles, eye closure, sensation, hearing, gag reflex) that belong in Clue 4; imaging section should focus on imaging modality results rather than clinical exam redundancy.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old woman awakens with sudden onset facial droop on right side and cannot close right eye properly. No prior neurological history. Onset occurred overnight without prodrome.
C2 Activity: Right lower facial weakness greater than upper face. Complete eye closure impossible on right. Water drips from affected side during drinking. Difficulty with oral intake and speech distortion. Pain in right ear preceding facial weakness by 24 hours.
C3 History: Right-sided facial weakness affecting ability to smile, close eye, and maintain oral competence. Water dribbles from mouth during drinking. Difficulty articulating consonants. Note: ear pain preceded weakness onset.
C4 Examination: Onset within 72 hours of waking. Hypertension and diabetes as background comorbidities. No recent head/neck trauma, surgery, or significant infection. No vesicular rash noted by patient at presentation. Similar presentation once before, 8 years ago (resolved completely).
C5 Imaging: Complete inability to wrinkle right forehead and close right eye. Asymmetric smile with right lip droop. Light touch sensation normal across all trigeminal divisions. Hearing normal bilaterally. No visible vesicles in external auditory canal, post-auricular area, or palate. Gag reflex intact.
#549 Anterior Cruciate Ligament Tear Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute knee injury with functional loss), each clue adds specific information (rotational instability → mechanism → clinical tests → imaging confirmation), and progression is sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old recreational athlete presents following acute knee injury during sports. The patient reports sudden-onset knee pain and immediate functional loss. The knee gave way and could not bear weight.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to return to pivoting/cutting sports. Attempts to change direction cause sudden instability and knee collapse. Straight-line walking is tolerated but rotational control is absent.
C3 History: Acute injury occurred during a deceleration-plant-and-cut maneuver in sports. Mechanism involved combined flexion, external tibial rotation, and valgus stress. This acute ACL injury requires surgical consultation and management.
C4 Examination: Positive Lachman test indicates anterior tibial translation. Positive pivot shift test demonstrates rotational instability. Mild effusion is present. Pain limits examination, but clinical signs are consistent with ACL rupture.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates disruption of the anterior cruciate ligament with discontinuity of fibers. Bone bruising is visualized at the expected locations. Associated meniscal or collateral ligament injuries may be present. Imaging confirms complete ACL tear.
#550 Osteoporosis Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (height loss, back pain, fragility fracture fit many conditions), subsequent clues progressively narrow through risk factors and objective findings, and DEXA/fracture assessment confirm diagnosis appropriately.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old woman presents with progressive height loss over the past year and chronic mid-back pain. Recent fall onto outstretched wrist resulted in fracture.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform overhead activities or carry loads due to thoracic pain and kyphosis. Unable to tolerate prolonged standing. Reports increasing fear of falling and declining balance confidence.
C3 History: Menopause 15 years ago without hormone replacement therapy. History of poor calcium intake and limited outdoor activity. Mother had vertebral fractures in her 70s. Recent corticosteroid use for respiratory condition (6 months, moderate dose).
C4 Examination: Thoracic kyphosis 45 degrees measured via digital inclinometry. Spinal palpation tenderness at T7-8 with palpable step-off. Grip strength 18 kg bilaterally. Functional reach test 6 inches. No focal neurological deficit. Pain with spinal extension worse than flexion.
C5 Imaging: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan shows T-score of -2.8 at lumbar spine and -2.5 at femoral neck (osteoporotic range). Vertebral fracture assessment shows compression fracture at T7 (15% height loss). Biochemical markers show elevated P1NP and CTX-1 indicating high bone turnover.
#551 Sciatic Nerve Injury Hip
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 is too specific (laceration + foot drop + sensory loss essentially names sciatic nerve injury); C5 contradicts the diagnosis by stating 'more than one peripheral nerve' involvement when sciatic injury should be isolated.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old man sustains buttock laceration from glass during fall. Acute severe pain noted with immediate weakness in lower leg. Wound bleeding controlled but patient unable to dorsiflex foot.
C2 Activity: A 35-year-old man sustained a laceration to the buttock from broken glass during a fall. He reports immediate severe pain in the buttock and lower leg, with acute onset weakness affecting foot movement. Unable to lift his foot off the ground.
C3 History: Sensory loss noted in lateral lower leg and dorsum of foot. Pain radiates down posterior and lateral aspect of leg.
C4 Examination: Injury occurred at the level of the proximal posterior thigh where major neural structures course superficially. Immediate neurological deficit present from time of injury. No prior history of neuropathy or nerve dysfunction.
C5 Imaging: High-stepping gait observed. Tinel sign elicited at gluteal wound site. Patellar and Achilles reflexes diminished bilaterally. Distribution of weakness and sensory loss corresponds to more than one peripheral nerve.
#552 Dupuytren's Disease Post-Fasciectomy Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (post-surgical contracture recurrence), each clue adds genuine information (activity → history of surgical procedure → examination findings → imaging confirmation of recurrence), and progression is logical.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old man presents 6 weeks after hand surgery for a finger contracture. Reports excellent initial progress with restored movement, but over the past week has noticed increasing tightness returning and range of motion declining.
C2 Activity: Unable to fully flatten hand and extend fingers completely. PIP joint of ring finger particularly stiff despite good recovery in early weeks. Recent increased hand use with therapy and work contributing to symptom progression.
C3 History: Original contracture measured 50 degrees pre-operatively. Surgical plan included complete fascial excision. Increased activity with physical therapy and return to work commenced 4-5 weeks post-op, coinciding with symptom return.
C4 Examination: Range of motion measurement shows MCP extension improved to 10 degrees flexion contracture (from pre-op 45 degrees) but PIP extension limitation developing. Scar tissue palpable along surgical site with firmness increasing. Minimal swelling and erythema. No signs of infection or wound complications.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound at 6 weeks post-op shows intact fasciectomy with resected tissue planes visualized. No fluid collection or hematoma. However, early recurrent disease process evident with hypoechoic nodules reforming at operative margins and PIP joint region. Scar tissue shows intermediate echogenicity consistent with early proliferative phase.
#553 Calcific Tendinitis Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple shoulder conditions, subsequent clues progressively narrow focus through mechanism (non-traumatic), examination pattern (severe pain with positive Hawkins), and finally imaging definitively confirms calcium deposit.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old office worker presents with acute onset severe shoulder pain yesterday during routine desk work. Sharp anterior shoulder pain unrelated to specific trauma. Pain awakens patient at night with inability to rest arm comfortably.
C2 Activity: Severe limitation with shoulder abduction and internal rotation; reaching across body markedly painful. Pain prevents active motion through guarding; passive range of motion not yet assessed. Discomfort worse with lying on affected side.
C3 History: No preceding trauma or overuse history. Office work involves minimal overhead demands. Pain intensity markedly disproportionate to mechanism (routine desk task). Similar acute presentations often self-resolve over weeks despite severity.
C4 Examination: Severe tenderness over supraspinatus tendon insertion. Passive range of motion better preserved than active motion. Supraspinatus strength testing limited by pain rather than true weakness. Dugas test negative (shoulder can still adduct). Hawkins test markedly positive reproducing sharp pain.
C5 Imaging: Shoulder X-ray (AP and scapular Y views) demonstrates dense calcium deposit within supraspinatus tendon measuring 8x6mm. Calcium appears radiodense with well-defined borders. Associated soft tissue swelling visible around deposit. MRI shows T2 hypointense deposit with surrounding fluid signal indicating active inflammation and resorption phase.
#554 Peripheral Neuropathy Peripheral Nerves
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 is quite specific to diabetic neuropathy already (12-year diabetes + progressive symmetrical numbness in feet); could broaden to 'progressive symmetrical distal sensory symptoms' to allow non-diabetic peripheral neuropathies.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 58-year-old man with 12-year diabetes history presents with progressive symmetrical numbness and burning pain in the feet over the past 2 years, affecting sleep quality.
C2 Activity: Patient reports loss of protective sensation in feet — recently slipped in shower because unable to sense hot water temperature. Symptoms worse at night, interfering with sleep. No upper limb involvement noted.
C3 History: Poor glycemic control with HbA1c 8.5% throughout disease course. No prior neuropathy screening or intervention. Sedentary lifestyle and obesity (BMI 32). Recent addition of statin medication 6 months ago. Multiple vascular risk factors including hypertension and smoking history.
C4 Examination: Diminished vibratory sensation at great toe (tuning fork 128Hz lost after 3 seconds versus normal 10+ seconds). Monofilament testing shows loss of protective sensation in both feet using 10g monofilament. Ankle reflexes absent bilaterally. Mild weakness in ankle dorsiflexion 4/5. Proprioception decreased in lower extremities.
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction study shows reduced amplitudes in sural sensory response bilaterally with prolonged distal latencies. Motor conduction of peroneal nerve shows reduced velocities (38 m/s). EMG demonstrates chronic denervation changes in foot intrinsic muscles. MRI excludes other spinal causes. Fasting glucose 185 mg/dL.
#555 Diastasis Rectus Abdominis Abdomen
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately identifies postpartum abdominal wall issue without naming the condition, clues 2-4 add functional and examination detail, and C5 imaging confirms with measurements.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 5-month postpartum woman reports persistent midline abdominal wall separation and weakness. Functional limitation with daily activities. Concerned about long-term cosmetic outcome.
C2 Activity: A 5-month postpartum woman reports persistent abdominal weakness and difficulty with functional activities. Notices visible bulging along midline when exerting herself.
C3 History: Cannot return to pre-pregnancy exercise intensity. Reports pain and bulging with coughing, sneezing, and straining. Difficulty maintaining upright posture for prolonged periods.
C4 Examination: Visible abdominal bulging with contraction. Palpable separation 3.5 fingers wide at umbilicus. Reduced abdominal wall tension. Rectus contraction shows lateral bulging. No signs of hernia.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates diastasis rectus measuring 4.2 cm interrectal distance with thin linea alba. No fascial dehiscence. Cross-sectional imaging confirms bilateral muscle separation.
#556 Proximal Hamstring Tendinopathy Thigh
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (buttock pain in runner), each subsequent clue adds distinct information (activity pattern → training history → specific clinical tests → imaging confirmation), with good clinical progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old recreational runner presents with buttock pain for 8 weeks that worsens during runs over 5km. Pain localized to lower buttock region near ischial tuberosity. Recent increase in training volume and intensity.
C2 Activity: Pain worse during running, especially hills and sprinting. Sitting on hard surfaces increases discomfort (describing difficulty with office chair use). Pain radiates to posterior thigh but does not extend below knee. Morning stiffness in buttock lasting 10-15 minutes.
C3 History: Gradual onset over 8 weeks correlating with increased running mileage (40 to 55 miles per week). No acute traumatic event. Prior history of lower back tightness and hip flexor tightness. Weak gluteal activation noted. Running on cambered surfaces recently.
C4 Examination: Palpable tenderness over ischial tuberosity region with pain on hamstring contraction against resistance. Positive modified Puranen-Orava test (buttock pain with hip flexion and knee extension). Pain with straight leg raise 70 degrees. Slump test negative (radicular component excluded). Hip internal rotation limited compared to external rotation.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows increased T2 signal in proximal hamstring tendons at ischial attachment with tendon thickening (8mm versus normal 5mm). No full-thickness tear visualized. Associated inflammation around ischial tuberosity. Ultrasound confirms hypoechoic tendon with loss of normal fibrillar pattern and increased vascularity on Doppler imaging at attachment point.
#557 Heart Failure Heart
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This condition falls outside physiotherapy scope of practice for a physiotherapy education game; while rehabilitation is involved, the diagnosis and management are primarily medical/cardiac, making it inappropriate for Physiodle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old man with a cardiac history presents with progressive shortness of breath on exertion and increasing fatigue over recent weeks. Exercise tolerance has declined noticeably. Reports difficulty keeping up with daily activities.
C2 Activity: Cannot lie flat at night without significant dyspnea. Bilateral lower limb swelling noted. Nocturnal cough present. Weight gain of 5 pounds over 2 weeks reported.
C3 History: Previous anterolateral myocardial infarction treated with percutaneous coronary intervention 3 years ago. Multiple cardiac risk factors present: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and smoking history. Recent non-compliance with medications noted. No recent chest pain.
C4 Examination: Elevated blood pressure (150/95) and heart rate (102 bpm). Bilateral crackles at lung bases. S3 gallop audible. Raised jugular venous pressure. Hepatomegaly present.
C5 Imaging: Echocardiography demonstrates ejection fraction 35% (HFrEF). Left ventricular end-diastolic dimension 60mm (dilated). Anterior wall motion abnormality consistent with prior MI. Mitral regurgitation moderate degree. Diastolic dysfunction also evident with restrictive filling pattern. BNP elevated at 850 pg/mL.
#558 Proximal Humerus Fracture Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 and C2 are nearly identical and redundant; C2 should focus on acute presentation/imaging rather than repeating the fall mechanism, though the overall diagnostic progression remains sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 75-year-old osteoporotic woman falls from standing height onto outstretched arm. Immediate severe shoulder pain and inability to move arm. Right shoulder swelling and deformity noted at scene.
C2 Activity: A 75-year-old woman with known osteoporosis falls from standing height onto her outstretched right arm. She reports immediate severe shoulder pain and is unable to move the arm. Staff at the scene note visible swelling and altered shoulder contour.
C3 History: Patient cannot attempt any active shoulder movement. Rapid swelling extends into the upper arm over minutes. Pain severity out of proportion to typical soft tissue injury alone.
C4 Examination: Shoulder deformity with loss of normal contour. Crepitus palpable at fracture site. Axillary nerve testing shows sensory loss to lateral shoulder and difficulty with deltoid contraction (0/5). Radial pulse intact and capillary refill normal. Sensation intact in radial and ulnar distributions but diminished axillary.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows 4-part proximal humerus fracture with displacement. Greater tuberosity fragment displaced 1.2cm. Humeral head retropulsion present. CT scan confirms comminution with axillary nerve involvement. Angiography normal excluding vascular injury but confirms soft tissue trauma with hematoma.
#559 Central Sensitisation Syndrome Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 is somewhat specific with 'widespread musculoskeletal pain' and explicit mention of findings disproportionate to pathology; could be broadened to 'diffuse pain with atypical features' to allow other differential diagnoses initially.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old woman presents with widespread musculoskeletal pain for 5 years. Pain described as burning and aching throughout the body, affecting multiple regions. Patient reports pain seems out of proportion to what physical examination and imaging would explain.
C2 Activity: Patient reports light touch causes significant pain (allodynia). Sleep severely disrupted with non-restorative pattern. Symptoms worsen with stress and weather changes. Concurrent memory and concentration difficulties reported.
C3 History: Gradual insidious onset 5 years ago following minor neck strain. No significant trauma or incident. Multiple negative investigations over years. History of anxiety and depression. Recent major life stressors and significant emotional distress. Pain catastrophizing evident in interview.
C4 Examination: Physical examination shows widespread tender points (11+ of 18 tender points positive). Allodynia confirmed to light touch across multiple body areas. No neurological deficits, no synovitis, no muscle atrophy. Objective findings notably disproportionate to severity of pain complaints.
C5 Imaging: Laboratory studies all normal including inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, and thyroid function. MRI cervical and lumbar spine normal without disc herniation or stenosis. Sleep study shows non-restorative sleep pattern (reduced deep sleep stages). Quantitative sensory testing shows lowered pain thresholds throughout body (wind-up amplification confirmed).
#560 Post-Amputation Rehabilitation Limbs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 does not clearly state 'amputation' but the clue is so specific to post-amputation presentation (missing limb, phantom sensations, 8 weeks post-op) that only amputation fits; this violates the principle of broad Clue 1.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 52-year-old man presenting 8 weeks post-operative from lower limb surgery. Reports pain at surgical site, cramping sensations in the missing limb, and difficulty with early mobilization. Expresses frustration and denial about functional changes.
C2 Activity: Cannot independently transfer or mobilize. Requires contact guard or moderate assistance for all positional changes. Trunk balance severely compromised without upper limb support. Demonstrates reluctance to engage in rehabilitation activities and expresses uncertainty about recovery timeline.
C3 History: Type 2 diabetes with HbA1c 9.2% and long-standing peripheral neuropathy. Infection required aggressive sepsis management. Pre-morbidly sedentary with limited family support. Recent social disruption affecting engagement in rehabilitation program.
C4 Examination: Stump edema present requiring compression wrapping. Incision healing well without significant drainage or erythema. Hip abductor and flexor strength 3+/5 bilaterally. Trunk balance fair requiring upper extremity support for stability. Transfer ability requires moderate assistance. Psychological assessment shows depression (PHQ-9 score 18).
C5 Imaging: Stump radiograph shows consolidated bone at amputation level without prominent bony protrusions. Soft tissue imaging shows mature scar formation with appropriate tissue planes. Vascular ultrasound confirms adequate blood flow to stump tissues (ankle-brachial index 0.7 in residual limb). Prosthetic prescription ready: SACH foot with microprocessor knee recommended for safety.
#561 Tuberosity Avulsion Fracture Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from acute knee pain to loss of active extension to localized tenderness to imaging confirmation; clue 1 appropriately broad (fits acute knee injuries), each subsequent clue adds genuine new information.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 22-year-old collegiate soccer player reports acute anterior knee pain during game when planting foot during pivot maneuver. Severe pain with sensation of something tearing in anterior knee. Immediate swelling and loss of function.
C2 Activity: Severe anterior knee pain with any attempt at active knee extension. Unable to perform straight leg raise. Rapid swelling developed within 30 minutes of injury. Guarding of entire knee joint.
C3 History: Young athletic male with high-demand sport (soccer). Acute traumatic mechanism during cutting maneuver. No prior knee surgery or injury. Recent growth spurt (18 years old) with some residual cartilage sensitivity. Full participation in sport up to injury.
C4 Examination: Complete loss of active knee extension strength (0/5 against gravity). Gross anterior knee swelling with visible hematoma localised to tibial tubercle region. Severe point tenderness over tuberosity. Knee effusion present. Quadriceps muscle palpable but unable to generate contraction.
C5 Imaging: X-ray (AP, lateral, and Merchant views) shows avulsion of tibial tuberosity fragment with 1.5cm displacement. CT confirms fracture extends into articular surface of tibial plateau. MRI shows complete disruption of patellar tendon at bone insertion with severe edema. Associated bone marrow edema in proximal tibia. Soft tissue trauma with hematoma extensive.
#562 Ischiofemoral Impingement Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Good clinical progression from deep buttock pain to activity-related worsening to occupational history to specific impingement findings to MRI confirmation; clue 1 is appropriately broad and clues build logically.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old man presents with deep buttock pain with walking for 3 months. Pain described as sharp and localized deep in buttock. Difficulty with prolonged ambulation (limited walking distance to 500 meters).
C2 Activity: Deep buttock pain worse with walking, particularly during hip adduction phase of gait. Pain relieved by sitting and lying down. Difficulty transitioning from sitting to standing. Pain not radiating down leg (no sciatica pattern). Groin pain sometimes accompanying buttock discomfort.
C3 History: Gradual insidious onset over 3 months. No significant trauma reported. Occupational sitting 8 hours daily. Hip tightness with limitation in internal rotation. History of groin strain 2 years prior to current symptoms.
C4 Examination: Pain with hip adduction across midline against resistance. Ischiofemoral stretch test reproduces deep buttock discomfort. Limited hip internal rotation (25 degrees versus 45 degrees opposite side). Tenderness with deep palpation in infrapiriformis region. Hamstring muscle tenderness present. Gait antalgic with reduced weight bearing phase.
C5 Imaging: MRI pelvis shows narrowing of ischiofemoral space measuring 18mm (normal >24mm) with adjacent quadratus femoris muscle edema. Hamstring origin tendinopathy visualized at ischial tuberosity. Gadolinium-enhanced imaging confirms inflammation in deep hip musculature. No femoroacetabular impingement or labral pathology identified.
#563 Developmental Coordination Disorder Systemic
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is overly specific (naming DCD with 'poor coordination concerns' since preschool is essentially diagnostic); condition also falls outside typical physiotherapy scope of practice and is more developmental/neuropsychological than biomechanical.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 7-year-old boy referred for poor school performance and motor coordination concerns. Mother reports difficulty with pencil grip, unable to ride bicycle, and poor balance. Clumsiness noted since preschool years.
C2 Activity: Struggles with fine motor tasks including writing, drawing, and buttoning clothes. Poor gross motor coordination with difficulty running smoothly and jumping. Falls frequently and appears clumsy compared to peers. Writing illegible with poor letter formation and spacing.
C3 History: Difficulty with playground skills: cannot skip or hop, trips over own feet during play. Also struggles with self-care tasks like tying shoes and fastening clothing. Noticeably slower at sports and climbing compared to peers.
C4 Examination: Physical examination shows poor pencil grasp and weak finger dexterity. Nine-hole peg test significantly slower than age norms (taking 2.5 minutes versus 1 minute expected). Difficulty with tandem stance (falls after 5 seconds). Hopping on single leg shows poor balance and coordination. Gait analysis reveals asymmetric arm swing and poor dynamic balance.
C5 Imaging: Standardized motor assessment (Movement Assessment Battery for Children) shows scores below 5th percentile for age across gross and fine motor domains. Neurological examination normal with normal strength, reflexes, and sensation. Brain MRI normal excluding structural pathology. Audiological testing normal. Cognitive testing normal.
#564 Subtrochanteric Fracture Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear traumatic progression with appropriate clue sequencing; clue 1 fits multiple hip injuries, each clue adds new detail (mechanism → positioning → risk factors → specific exam findings → imaging confirmation).
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old woman on long-term corticosteroid therapy for rheumatoid arthritis falls from standing height onto her left side. Acute severe left hip pain with inability to bear weight. Left leg held in external rotation.
C2 Activity: Severe pain with any hip movement. Cannot transfer bed-to-chair or ambulate. Left leg adopts external rotation and flexion posture. Marked swelling in proximal thigh with visible deformity.
C3 History: Prolonged corticosteroid use (15 years) for autoimmune disease causing osteoporosis. Low-energy mechanism (standing fall) concerning for pathological fracture. History of previous right hip fracture 8 years ago. Female postmenopausal age 62. History of insufficient vitamin D.
C4 Examination: Hip shortened 2cm compared to contralateral side. Leg externally rotated and flexed. Severe tenderness over greater trochanteric region and proximal femur. Hip abduction strength impossible to test due to pain. Groin pain with passive internal rotation. Neurovascular examination intact.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows transverse subtrochanteric fracture with lateral comminution. Fracture line extends from medial aspect at junction of femoral neck to lateral cortex. Medial cortex disruption with 30-degree valgus angulation. CT confirms comminuted fracture pattern with significant soft tissue trauma. DEXA T-score -3.5 confirming osteoporosis.
#565 Cervical Spondylosis Cervical Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 4 contains findings (sustained clonus, brisk reflexes 3+, ataxic gait, lower limb weakness) suggestive of myelopathy that somewhat anticipates the advanced imaging finding; consider pulling some neurological severity back to clue 5 or toning clue 4 slightly.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 60-year-old patient with a 10-year history of intermittent neck pain now reports progressive tingling and weakness in both arms over the past 6 months.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot work at a desk for more than 30 minutes without symptom escalation. Activities requiring overhead reaching and downward gaze (reading, cooking) trigger acute worsening. Symptoms do not improve with rest alone.
C3 History: Patient reports morning stiffness lasting 45 minutes. No prior trauma or neck injury. No imaging performed previously. Symptoms gradually worsened despite two courses of physiotherapy (6 months apart). Intermittent episodes of grip weakness and difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning, writing).
C4 Examination: Neck flexion limited to 30°. Bilateral hand intrinsic weakness (grip strength 3+/5 bilaterally). Brisk reflexes in upper extremities (3+) with sustained clonus at ankles. Positive Lhermitte sign (electric shock down spine with neck flexion). Lower limb weakness present (hip flexion 4/5 bilaterally). Gait ataxic.
C5 Imaging: MRI cervical spine shows osteophyte-mediated stenosis at C4–C6 with canal diameter reduced to 8 mm (normal >13 mm). T2 hyperintense signal within spinal cord at C5 level. Cord compression ratio >60% with flattening evident.
#566 Vestibular Neuritis Vestibular
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent diagnostic progression from acute vertigo with viral history to constant disabling symptoms to unilateral vestibular deficit findings to exclusionary imaging; clue 1 is appropriately broad and each clue adds specificity without overlap.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.
C2 Activity: Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.
C3 History: No prior episodes of vertigo. Patient denies hearing changes or tinnitus. No recent head trauma, otologic procedures, or autoimmune history.
C4 Examination: Head impulse test shows positive catch-up saccade indicating unilateral vestibular deficit. Dix-Hallpike maneuver negative excluding BPPV. Caloric testing shows reduced response in affected ear (50% unilateral paresis). Romberg test positive with falls toward affected side.
C5 Imaging: MRI inner ear structures and cerebellopontine angle normal, excluding vestibular schwannoma or brainstem lesion. No abnormal cranial nerve enhancement. Differential diagnosis now narrows to viral-inflammatory vestibular nerve pathology.
#567 Mallet Finger Wrist & Hand
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 2 and Clue 3 are nearly redundant (both describe inability to extend DIP joint from slightly different angles); clue 4 says 'Flexor digitorum superficialis intact' but mallet finger is an extensor injury, creating confusion; clue 5 lacks imaging (should include X-ray for avulsion confirmation).
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old man catches ring finger in door resulting in hyperflexion injury. Immediate pain over distal phalanx with inability to extend distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint. Minimal swelling and no gross deformity.
C2 Activity: 34-year-old man with acute injury to ring finger sustained 1 day ago when caught in door. Reports immediate pain over tip of finger and inability to straighten the end joint. Minimal swelling, no visible deformity.
C3 History: Unable to actively straighten the distal interphalangeal joint — finger held in slight flexion at rest. Passive extension is possible but painful. No weakness or numbness elsewhere in hand.
C4 Examination: Physical examination shows DIP joint in 30-degree flexion with inability to actively extend. Passive extension possible with resistance and pain. Isolated DIP flexor tendon (flexor digitorum superficialis) intact. No lacerations to extensor surface. Sensation intact to light touch and two-point discrimination.
C5 Imaging: DIP joint held in approximately 30-degree flexion deformity. No laceration over dorsal aspect of finger. Flexor tendons (FDP and FDS) intact and functional. Sensation and circulation intact. No evidence of compartment syndrome or vascular compromise.
#568 Reactive Arthritis Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong progression from polyarthritis post-infection to systemic features (urethritis, conjunctivitis) to pathognomonic findings (keratoderma, mucosal ulcers) to definitive serology and HLA-B27; clue 1 fits multiple arthritides appropriately.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old man presents with acute-onset painful swelling in knees and ankles 2 weeks after an acute diarrheal illness. Reports fever (101°F), fatigue, and malaise. Morning stiffness lasts approximately 2 hours.
C2 Activity: Unable to walk comfortably due to knee and ankle pain. Also experiencing dysuria and penile discharge. Complains of eye redness and discomfort bilaterally.
C3 History: Acute diarrhea 2 weeks prior to joint symptoms (self-limited 3-day illness with bloody diarrhea). Recent unprotected sexual contact 4 weeks ago with new partner. Family history negative for autoimmune disease. No prior joint problems. Previously healthy.
C4 Examination: On examination: warmth and effusion in knees and ankles; asymmetric polyarthritis noted. Urethral discharge visualized. Bilateral conjunctivitis with mild mucoid discharge. Distinctive psoriasiform papules (keratoderma blennorrhagicum) present on palms and soles. Shallow oral ulcers noted on palate.
C5 Imaging: ESR elevated at 68 mm/hr, CRP 12 mg/dL (elevated). Rheumatoid factor negative. Anti-CCP negative. HLA-B27 positive. Stool culture positive for Shigella flexneri. Serology positive for chlamydial antibodies (microimmunofluorescence). Synovial fluid shows inflammatory non-septic pattern (WBC 8,000, PMN-predominant).
#569 Trigger Finger Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear clinical progression from triggering phenomenon to functional impairment to diabetes risk association to palpable nodule to ultrasound confirmation; clue 1 is suitably broad (fits multiple hand conditions) and clues build logically.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old man with Type 2 diabetes reports sudden-onset triggering sensation in fingers bilaterally, with difficulty extending the digits in the morning.
C2 Activity: Functional impact worsens with repetitive gripping and activities requiring fine motor control. Significant functional limitation by evening, though symptoms partially resolve overnight.
C3 History: Diabetes diagnosed 8 years ago, poorly controlled (HbA1c 8.5%). No prior hand pathology. Symptoms began 1 month ago with rapid bilateral involvement.
C4 Examination: Nodular thickening palpable at A1 pulley level bilaterally. Triggering phenomenon reproducible with grasping. Pain with triggering event. Reduced grip strength secondary to pain.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound bilateral index and middle fingers shows flexor tendon nodular thickening and pulley hypertrophy. Inflammatory changes around tendon sheath. No tendon tearing.
#570 Jersey Finger Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent traumatic mechanism with clear progression from inability to flex DIP to specific exam findings (intact PIP but absent DIP flexion) to imaging confirmation of tendon rupture; clue 1 fits multiple acute finger injuries appropriately.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 26-year-old rugby player sustained an acute injury to the ring finger during a tackle. Sharp pain and sudden inability to flex the ring finger. Swelling developed rapidly in the forearm within minutes of injury.
C2 Activity: Ring finger held in fixed extension. Unable to make a fist with the ring finger. PIP joint can bend slightly but with significant pain and weakness. Pain worse with any attempt to grip or squeeze.
C3 History: Acute traumatic mechanism with finger caught in jersey with sudden traction injury. Injury occurred 2 hours ago. Sport involves high-contact mechanism. Young athletic male with full mobility demands. No prior finger trauma.
C4 Examination: Ring finger held in extension at DIP joint with inability to actively flex (0/5 DIP flexion). PIP flexion intact at 4/5 strength. Mild swelling noted in volar forearm proximal to wrist. Tender palm with fullness along flexor digitorum profundus tendon region. No sensory loss or vascular compromise. No wound or skin disruption.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows no bone avulsion at distal phalanx base (distinguishes from bony mallet). MRI demonstrates complete rupture of flexor digitorum profundus tendon at level of wrist crease. Tendon retracted proximally with gap of 3cm. Associated edema in forearm soft tissues. Ultrasound confirms tendon discontinuity with fluid collection (hematoma) tracking proximally in forearm.
#571 Cervical Radiculopathy Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute neck pain with arm symptoms fits multiple conditions), each clue adds progressively specific information, and Clue 5 clinches with imaging confirmation of nerve root compression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old reports acute onset of neck pain with numbness and weakness in one arm following a position change during lifting. Symptoms began 2 weeks ago and are affecting work capacity.
C2 Activity: Pain radiates distally in a linear pattern down the arm. Neck movements in certain directions provoke symptoms; brief rest and gentle traction provide temporary relief.
C3 History: Patient has chronic neck stiffness from prior years of desk work. Current acute flare coincided with moving furniture. No prior imaging of the neck. Similar but milder episodes resolved within weeks previously.
C4 Examination: On examination, neck range of motion is limited in flexion and rotation. Dermatome-specific sensory changes are present. Motor weakness corresponds to the affected nerve root. Reflexes may be diminished.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates intervertebral disc herniation or spondylotic changes compressing the nerve root at the appropriate spinal level. The compression level corresponds to clinical findings. Imaging confirms mechanical nerve root compression.
#572 Cervical Spine Surgery Neck
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too specific (essentially names 'post-surgical cervical condition') and the puzzle conflates post-op recovery trajectory with diagnosis; not suitable for a guessing game format.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 54-year-old woman presents 6 weeks after elective neck surgery. Reports persistent neck stiffness and mild arm numbness that is gradually improving. Pain is well-controlled.
C2 Activity: Hoarseness of voice present since procedure. Neck movement remains restricted but progressing. Patient reports gradual recovery of arm sensation and overall functional improvements with therapy.
C3 History: Pre-operative presentation included progressive hand weakness and gait disturbance concerning for myelopathy. Anterior surgical approach selected for direct decompression and fusion. Patient is engaged with rehabilitation and has realistic recovery expectations.
C4 Examination: Neck active range of motion limited: flexion 30 degrees (normal 45), extension 20 degrees (normal 45), lateral flexion 20 degrees bilaterally (normal 40). Incision healed without complications. Upper extremity strength 4+/5 (improved from 3+/5 pre-operatively). Dysphonia present but voice improving. Gait improved with better balance and coordination.
C5 Imaging: Cervical spine X-ray shows adequate positioning of ACDF allograft with normal alignment. Anterior plate and screw fixation appropriately positioned. Early fusion incorporation visible on lateral radiograph. MRI shows decompression of cord at C5-6 with resolution of cord signal abnormality. No recurrent disc herniation or new compression.
#573 Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (lateral hip pain in middle-aged woman fits multiple conditions), clues progress logically through activity patterns, examination findings, and imaging confirmation without significant redundancy.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old woman presents with lateral hip pain for 4 weeks exacerbated by walking and lying on affected side. Pain localized to outer hip region. Affects sleep and daily walking tolerance limited to 1 block.
C2 Activity: Sharp lateral hip pain worse with walking, especially on stairs and uneven surfaces. Pain severe when lying on affected side at night. Difficulty crossing legs and internal rotation of hip increases discomfort. Pain improves with rest and ice.
C3 History: Gradual onset 6 weeks ago coinciding with new exercise program focused on walking/hill work. No traumatic event. Sedentary desk job. Prior episodes of lower back pain (resolved). Mild bilateral hip abductor weakness noted, worse on affected side. No previous hip injections or formal physio.
C4 Examination: Palpable tenderness directly over greater trochanter. Positive Trendelenburg test showing gluteal dysfunction. Limited hip abduction strength 3+/5 on affected side. Noble compression test reproduces lateral hip pain. Single-leg stance balance reduced. Lumbar spine examination normal (no radiation).
C5 Imaging: X-ray hip shows no osteoarthritis or structural abnormality. MRI reveals gluteus medius tendinopathy with intrasubstance signal changes. Modest edema in greater trochanteric bursa. No labral pathology. Ultrasound demonstrates tendon thickening and hypoechoic degeneration with increased Doppler vascularity. Bursal thickening confirmed.
#574 Hearing Loss Ear
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Hearing loss is fundamentally an audiological/medical diagnosis outside physiotherapy scope of practice; not appropriate for a physio-focused guessing game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old man reports progressive difficulty hearing over the past 5 years. Wife notes he frequently asks for repetition and keeps television volume loud. Denies ear pain, drainage, or balance problems.
C2 Activity: Struggles most in noisy settings (restaurants, social gatherings). Tinnitus present as high-pitched ringing in both ears. No vertigo or balance loss. Declining participation in group activities.
C3 History: Age 68 with expected age-related hearing changes. 45-year occupational noise exposure as machinist without ear protection. No ototoxic medications. No prior ear surgery or trauma. Family history of hearing loss in father. Smoking history 40 pack-years.
C4 Examination: Otoscopy: clear bilateral tympanic membranes, no cerumen impaction. Weber test: no lateralization. Rinne test: air conduction > bone conduction bilaterally (normal pattern). Speech discrimination score 68% at 65 dB. No otologic signs of trauma, infection, or mass.
C5 Imaging: Audiometry: bilateral symmetrical sensorineural hearing loss, worse at frequencies >2000 Hz. Air-bone gaps absent bilaterally. Bone conduction thresholds elevated 50–60 dB at high frequencies; air conduction similarly affected. Tympanometry type A bilaterally; acoustic reflexes present. Speech discrimination 68%.
#575 Multiple Sclerosis Neurological
✗ REWORK
Verdict: MS is a neurological disease diagnosis that physiotherapists manage as part of team care but do not diagnose; Clue 3 (multiple episodes over years) and Clue 5 (CSF oligoclonal bands, VEPs) are too medical/specialist for a physio game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 34-year-old woman presenting with sudden vision loss in right eye with pain on eye movement, and progressive lower limb weakness over 4 weeks. Reports fatigue and mild cognitive difficulties affecting work. Recent viral illness-like symptoms 2 months prior.
C2 Activity: Unable to climb stairs independently due to leg weakness and imbalance. Frequent falls secondary to coordination loss. Bladder symptoms (urgency, frequency) interfering with social activities and sleep. Stocking-glove sensory loss in feet.
C3 History: Patient reports 3-4 episodes of unexplained neurological symptoms over past 2 years, each lasting weeks to months then partially resolving. Current episode began 4 weeks ago following recent upper respiratory infection. No significant past medical history. Strong family history of rheumatoid arthritis and lupus.
C4 Examination: Eye examination shows reduced visual acuity 20/40 right eye with afferent pupillary defect. Visual field testing shows central scotoma on right. Lower extremity strength 4/5 with increased tone and hyperreflexia. Positive Babinski signs bilaterally. Gait shows spasticity and ataxia. Finger-to-nose dysmetria present.
C5 Imaging: MRI brain shows multiple periventricular T2/FLAIR hyperintensities consistent with demyelination. Spinal cord MRI reveals T2 hyperintense lesion at C4-5 level. Gadolinium enhancement noted in multiple lesions indicating acute inflammation. CSF analysis shows oligoclonal bands positive. Visual evoked potentials show prolonged latency in right eye (optic nerve demyelination).
#576 Cervical Myelopathy Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (progressive hand clumsiness and gait issues fit multiple conditions), each clue adds meaningful clinical detail, and Clue 5 confirms with MRI findings; fair progression for physio students.
Show clues
