C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old office worker presents with progressive hand clumsiness and unsteady gait over 6 months. Reports difficulty with fine motor tasks like buttoning. Wife noted increased stumbling and careful walking pattern.
C2 Activity: Dexterity impaired more than strength in hands. Gait described as wide-based and cautious with reduced arm swing. Patient reports difficulty with stairs and uneven surfaces. Symptom progression noted with activity.
C3 History: Long history of cervical spine stiffness. Previous minor car accident 5 years ago. Gradual symptom progression over months suggesting chronic cord compression. No acute trauma. Smoking and age as risk factors.
C4 Examination: Lhermitte sign positive on forward neck flexion. Bilateral hyperreflexia with Hoffman's reflex present and pathologic. Lower extremity hyperreflexia with spasticity. Gait shows bilateral stiffness pattern. Weakness mild to moderate in hand intrinsics with loss of fine coordination.
C5 Imaging: MRI cervical spine shows multilevel cervical spondylosis with cord compression at C5-6 and C6-7 levels. T2-weighted imaging demonstrates myelopathic changes with central cord signal hyperintensity. Sagittal diameter reduced to 10mm at most compressed level. No acute fracture.
#577 Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is slightly too broad/vague (anterior knee pain limiting running is minimal detail), and Clue 5 essentially says 'normal imaging rules out everything else'—consider adding more specific activity detail to Clue 2 or making Clue 1 slightly more descriptive of patient profile.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old female with anterior knee pain limiting running activities. Pain worsens with running volume.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform single-leg squat or step down without anterior knee pain. Pain worse with prolonged sitting (movie sign).
C3 History: Increased running frequency without adequate recovery. Previous episode of knee pain 2 years prior resolved with training modification. Recurrent with current training increase.
C4 Examination: VMO weakness confirmed on manual test. Knee valgus noted during single-leg squat. Pain with patellar compression during weightbearing activities. Q-angle elevated. No focal tendon tenderness; patellofemoral joint line tender to palpation.
C5 Imaging: X-rays normal; no OA. MRI normal; no cartilage damage, no tendinopathy, no ligamentous pathology. Diagnosis confirmed clinically — structural imaging excludes alternative causes.
#578 Femoroacetabular Impingement Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 establishes reasonable breadth (hip pain post-activity with longstanding tightness), Clue 2 adds specific movement provocation pattern, Clue 4 reinforces ROM restriction, and Clue 5 confirms with imaging; good progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A middle-aged woman presents with intermittent hip pain following return to recreational dancing. The patient reports functional limitations with lower extremity mobility. Lifelong history of hip tightness and restricted mobility predates current symptom onset.
C2 Activity: Patient reports pain worsens with hip flexion combined with internal rotation and adduction. Struggles with lunging, pivoting movements, and crossing legs. Pain is predictable and reproducible with these combined movement planes.
C3 History: Patient has had long-standing hip stiffness since adolescence but was previously asymptomatic. Returned to dancing 3 months ago after 2-year break. No previous imaging. No hip trauma or surgery history. Symptoms gradually worsened over first 4 weeks of renewed activity.
C4 Examination: Hip flexion ROM 110°, internal rotation 15° (restricted bilaterally, more so on symptomatic side). Pain reproduced at end-range when combining flexion with adduction and internal rotation. Hip flexor tightness noted in prone lying. Pain anterolateral hip region, not groin. No catch or giving way. Neurology intact.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates cam-type morphology at femoral head-neck junction with associated labral tear. No hip dysplasia. Mild synovitis present.
#579 Quadriceps Tendinopathy Thigh
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Title says 'Quadriceps Tendinopathy' but most clinical clues and imaging point to patellar tendon involvement; either clarify the diagnosis as 'Patellar Tendinopathy' or adjust clues to focus on quadriceps insertion.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old recreational volleyball player reports anterior knee pain for 6 weeks that worsens with jumping and explosive movements.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform repetitive jumping or spiking without pain. Eccentric loading during deceleration significantly aggravates symptoms. Pain settles quickly with rest but reliably returns when returning to sport participation.
C3 History: 6-week history correlating with increased training frequency (4x weekly volleyball). No acute injury event. Prior similar patellar tendon discomfort 2 years ago (self-resolved). Recent decrease in hamstring and quadriceps flexibility noted.
C4 Examination: Palpable tenderness directly over proximal patellar tendon at superior patellar pole. Quadriceps contraction against resistance elicits pain. Single-leg squat shows valgus knee position (femoral internal rotation) suggesting biomechanical dysfunction. Knee flexion 0-120 degrees pain-free but 120-140 degrees aggravating. Palpable swelling at insertion.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic thickening of proximal quadriceps tendon (8mm versus normal 5-6mm) with loss of fibrillar echotexture. Increased Doppler signal indicating inflammation and neovascularity. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration without full-thickness tear. T2 signal abnormality within proximal third of tendon.
#580 Serratus Anterior Injury Shoulder
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 5 (EMG with denervation, reduced MNCV amplitude) diagnoses a nerve injury that is essentially pathognomonic and overly specific; Clues 1–4 don't provide sufficient breadth to justify a guessing game, and real nerve injury requires medical/neurological diagnosis, not physio clinical reasoning alone.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 26-year-old female volleyball player with 4-week gradual onset of shoulder weakness and dysfunction during overhead activities. Reports difficulty with throwing mechanics and general shoulder fatigue with training.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform overhead pressing without fatigue. Difficulty maintaining control during push-ups. Weakness worsens with repeated reaching forward or upward. Bench press and throwing activities provoke discomfort.
C3 History: Overhead athlete with progressive training load increase over 4 weeks without adequate deload. No acute trauma or nerve injury event. Previous shoulder issues in past season. Trains 5–6 days per week with minimal rest days.
C4 Examination: Visibly prominent medial scapular border evident during push-up test (scapular winging present). Positive serratus wall push-up test with inability to maintain scapular control. Weakness with forward reaching and arm elevation. Shoulder abduction and internal rotation strength 3+/5. Thoracic kyphosis noted.
C5 Imaging: EMG shows active denervation in serratus anterior muscle with fibrillations and positive sharp waves. Motor conduction of long thoracic nerve demonstrates reduced amplitude. Nerve conduction velocity normal but amplitude reduced 50% compared to contralateral side. Chest X-ray normal excluding bony pathology. MRI shoulder shows mild scapular dyskinesis pattern.
#581 Thoracic Spinal Cord Injury Thoracic Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 already contains the diagnosis (thoracic spine injury, T6-7, spinal cord contusion) and is far too specific for a guessing game; no student could reasonably narrow from 10+ conditions.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old man sustains thoracic spine injury from motor vehicle collision with loss of consciousness. Acute spinal cord contusion at T6-7 level. Acute paralysis below level of injury with sensory loss.
C2 Activity: 34-year-old male admitted following high-speed motor vehicle collision. Reports inability to move lower extremities and loss of sensation below mid-torso. Acute onset with loss of consciousness at time of injury.
C3 History: Cannot ambulate or move lower extremities. Unable to maintain continence; requires catheterization. Loss of normal bowel/bladder sensation and control.
C4 Examination: High-velocity motor vehicle collision with significant blunt thoracic trauma. Loss of consciousness at scene. Intubated for airway protection. Critical care admission.
C5 Imaging: Flaccid lower extremities with 0/5 strength bilaterally. Deep tendon reflexes absent throughout lower extremities (areflexic phase). Upper extremity strength intact 5/5 throughout. Bilateral pinprick loss with clear demarcation at T8. Perianal sensation absent; anal wink absent.
#582 Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple joint conditions (JIA, reactive arthritis, Lyme disease); each subsequent clue adds distinct information (insidious course, labs, imaging) and Clue 5 clinches with positive ANA and elevated inflammatory markers.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 6-year-old girl presents with knee pain and morning stiffness in both knees lasting 2 hours daily. Mother notes decreased activity compared to peers. Swelling in knees apparent after child wakes.
C2 Activity: Unable to participate in normal play activities; limping gait that improves with activity and warms up. Difficulty with stairs and prolonged standing. Swelling noticeable to parents in both knees after rest periods.
C3 History: Onset of symptoms 2 months ago insidious without trauma. Negative for recent infections or illness triggers. Family history negative for autoimmune disease. Otherwise healthy child with normal development. No rash or systemic symptoms reported.
C4 Examination: Physical examination shows bilateral knee effusions with warmth and swelling. Knee flexion contractures bilateral measuring 15 degrees. Decreased knee active range of motion. Mild pain with passive range of motion. No joint asymmetry. Gait shows antalgic pattern with shortened step. Absence of rash and hepatosplenomegaly.
C5 Imaging: ESR elevated at 32 mm/hr, CRP 8 mg/dL. Antinuclear antibody positive (homogeneous pattern). Rheumatoid factor negative. Anti-CCP antibody negative. Knee X-rays show no structural damage early on. Ultrasound demonstrates synovial hypertrophy with joint effusions. Complete blood count shows mild anemia (Hb 10.2).
#583 Radial Nerve Palsy Upper Limb
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately broad (nerve palsy, motor loss); sleep compression history is good differentiator; progression through motor pattern, sensory sparing, and imaging is logical and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old man woke this morning with inability to lift his wrist and weakness extending his fingers. Some tingling reported in the hand. No recent trauma or heavy lifting.
C2 Activity: Cannot lift wrist against gravity. Completely unable to extend thumb at interphalangeal joint. Fingers cannot be extended at metacarpophalangeal joints to make a fist properly. No sensory deficit noted on self-examination.
C3 History: Symptoms began immediately upon waking. Patient recalls arm was draped over chair edge during sleep with pressure on the lateral upper arm. No prior episodes of nerve injury or arm weakness. No numbness or tingling before sleep.
C4 Examination: Wrist held in flexion with inability to dorsiflex (0/5 wrist extension). Thumb interphalangeal joint extension weak (1/5). Finger metacarpophalangeal extension weak (2/5) for all digits. Sensory loss in first dorsal web space only (posterior interosseous nerve sparing). Triceps and elbow extension strength intact. Sensation in remaining hand normal.
C5 Imaging: EMG/NCS shows reduced motor amplitude in posterior interosseous nerve branch with slowed conduction velocity. Sensory conduction normal in superficial radial sensory branch. Denervation pattern in extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis muscles. MRI shows nerve compression at level of spiral groove with surrounding edema. No fracture or other structural lesion.
#584 Iliotibial Band Syndrome Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple running injuries; activity pattern, mileage increase, and hip weakness build logically; physical findings and imaging confirm cleanly.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old distance runner reports lateral knee pain that develops during running. Symptoms onset gradually over recent weeks and resolve with rest days.
C2 Activity: Sharp lateral knee pain elicited during running, particularly with downhill running and repetitive knee flexion-extension cycles. Pain increases as running distances increase. Resolves quickly with rest. Pain not present with walking or static stretching.
C3 History: Gradual onset over 3 weeks coinciding with increase in weekly mileage (30 to 50 miles) and switch to hillier running routes with cambered surfaces. Patient notes recent history of hip weakness.
C4 Examination: Positive Ober test indicating ITB tightness and hip abduction contracture. Noble compression test reproduces lateral knee pain over ITB insertion. Iliotibial band palpable tenderness at knee level. Hip abduction strength weak (3/5). Glute med weakness with positive Trendelenburg sign. Gait shows excessive knee valgus on single leg stance.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows thickened ITB with edema and signal changes at distal insertion point at knee. Inflammation at ITB-knee lateral compartment interface. No meniscal or ligamentous injury. Associated hip abductor muscle edema (gluteus medius and minimus). Ultrasound confirms ITB thickening with hypoechoic signal change and Doppler hyperemia.

Verdict: Clue 3 explicitly mentions 'golfer' and swing mechanics, which essentially names the condition; should be more generic (e.g., activity-related rather than golf-specific) to preserve mystery.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old male with 8 weeks of gradual-onset groin pain that worsens with activity and improves with rest. Pain began coinciding with increased physical demands at work. Describes anterior hip discomfort affecting functional mobility.
C2 Activity: Pain predominantly with hip adduction (bringing legs together) and combined hip flexion-internal rotation. Worse with activities requiring forceful hip squeezing. Pain resolves with activity modification but patient cannot sustain prolonged standing or walking.
C3 History: Gradual onset over 8 weeks with increased practice frequency (3 rounds per week). Swing change 2 months ago changing weight transfer mechanics. Long-term golfer without prior hip issues. Age-related tissue changes contributing. Limited hip flexibility particularly adductors.
C4 Examination: Focal tenderness at pubic bone near medial thigh. Hip adduction strength grossly reduced; hip flexion and internal rotation limited compared to contralateral. Abdominal strength normal. Antalgic gait with reduced hip extension on stance phase. Single-leg stance balance maintained. No neurovascular signs.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows adductor longus tendinopathy at proximal insertion with T2 signal hyperintensity. Pubic bone edema present at adductor origin. Associated labral fraying at anteroinferior labrum. Iliopsoas myotendinopathy present as secondary finding. Ultrasound confirms thickened adductor tendon with mixed echotexture and Doppler neovascularization.

Show clues
C1 Complaint: 58-year-old retired man with 3 years of chronic low back pain. Pain 4-5/10 at baseline, intermittently worse. Reports frustration with multiple failed treatment attempts.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting beyond 20 minutes and prolonged standing beyond 15 minutes. Walking for 30+ minutes provides relief. Difficulty with activities of daily living has worsened over past year.
C3 History: Onset gradual without preceding injury or event. Multiple prior imaging studies and specialist consultations completed. Tried manual therapy, injections, and medications with minimal sustained relief. Reports pain behavior out of proportion to functional limitation; significant mood and sleep disturbance.
C4 Examination: Hip flexors tight bilaterally. Lumbar extension ROM reduced by 20%; lateral flexion and rotation largely preserved. Core stability testing reveals weak motor control, particularly in transversus abdominis (3+/5). Straight leg raise normal bilaterally. No dermatomal sensory loss or motor weakness. Palpation reveals generalized paraspinal muscle guarding.
C5 Imaging: Lumbar MRI: mild-to-moderate disc bulging at L4-L5 and L5-S1 without central canal stenosis or nerve root compression. Bilateral facet joint arthrosis. Mild endplate changes and osteophyte formation at multiple levels. No acute findings. Imaging findings do not explain severity of reported pain or functional limitation.

C2 Activity: Pain worsens with trail running and inversion activities. Subsides with rest. Unable to continue training volume. Tightness along lateral ankle and foot.
C3 History: Recent increase in running mileage by 30% over 3 weeks. Previous history of inversion ankle sprain 6 months ago with incomplete rehabilitation. Peroneal tendon subluxation briefly noted post-sprain.
C4 Examination: Tenderness over peroneal groove behind lateral malleolus. Pain with resisted foot eversion. Swelling along lateral ankle. Pain reproduced with single-leg stance on uneven surface.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates peroneal tendon thickening and hypoechogenicity over 1.5 cm length behind lateral malleolus. No subluxation noted. Mild fluid surrounding tendon.

C3 History: Difficulty with uneven terrain and rapid directional changes. Swelling worsens toward end of day. Unable to tolerate sport-specific cutting movements.
C4 Examination: Injury occurred 48 hours ago during rapid deceleration and pivot maneuver. No prior ankle injuries or chronic instability reported. Immediate onset of pain and rapid swelling.
C5 Imaging: Palpable tenderness over anterior talofibular ligament region. Anterior drawer test shows increased tibiotalar translation. Inversion stress reproduces sharp pain. Ability to perform single-leg stance significantly compromised.
#589 Stress Urinary Incontinence Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple incontinence types and pelvic floor conditions; activity pattern, parity/hysterectomy history, and exam findings progress logically; Clue 5 confirms with urodynamics.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old woman post-hysterectomy reports urine loss with coughing, exercise, and straining. Small volume leakage with physical activities. Significant social limitation.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with high-impact activities (running, jumping, aerobics). Leakage during exercise predictable and reproducible. No nighttime symptoms or constant dribbling.
C3 History: Multiple vaginal deliveries prior. Hysterectomy 5 years prior. Menopause 3 years ago. Progressive incontinence worsening with activity. Previous bladder symptoms.
C4 Examination: Weak pelvic floor on assessment. Positive cough stress test. Bladder neck hypermobile on straining. No involuntary contractions. Positive stress incontinence sign.
C5 Imaging: Urodynamics shows stress incontinence with sphincter incompetence. Normal detrusor function. Bladder neck descent on cough. Urethral pressure profile weak.
#590 Meralgia Paraesthetica Thigh
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple causes of lateral thigh dysesthesia; occupational/mechanical history and obesity are good differentiators; LFCN distribution, sensory findings, and imaging progression are clear and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old man presents with burning pain and numbness on lateral thigh surface for 6 months. Symptoms unilateral on right side. Burning dysesthesia constant but variably intensified. No lower extremity weakness reported.
C2 Activity: Burning pain and paresthesias (pins-and-needles) affecting lateral thigh from hip to knee region. Pain worse with standing and walking, improved by sitting and lying down. Sensation described as burning and hypersensitive to touch. No pain in lower leg or foot.
C3 History: Gradual insidious onset over 6 months without clear inciting event. Occupational sitting with tight waistband pressure (construction supervisor). Obesity present with BMI 31. Recent weight gain of 15 pounds over past year. No recent trauma or nerve compression episode.
C4 Examination: Sensory loss to light touch in lateral femoral cutaneous nerve distribution (lateral thigh). Dysesthesia and allodynia present in same distribution. No motor weakness noted. Lower extremity strength intact (5/5 throughout). Straight leg raise normal and painless. Palpable tenderness at inguinal ligament where nerve may be compressed.
C5 Imaging: EMG/NCS shows reduced sensory amplitude in lateral femoral cutaneous sensory response. Motor conduction studies normal (femoral, peroneal, tibial). MRI shows no structural mass or pathology compressing nerve at groin level. Ultrasound demonstrates nerve thickening at inguinal ligament with hypoechoic changes. CT confirms mild nerve compression at inguinal canal.
#591 Sarcopenia Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (age + weakness fits many conditions), each clue adds distinct information (activity reveals symmetrical weakness, history adds nutritional/lifestyle context, examination confirms muscle wasting pattern, imaging clinches with DEXA and MRI findings), and progression is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 76-year-old woman presents with progressive weakness and functional decline over 2 years. Difficulty rising from chair without armrest support. Walking slower and shorter distances than previously. Recent fall from trip on uneven pavement.
C2 Activity: Reduced grip strength (12 kg, versus 22 kg in right hand). Symmetrical weakness affecting both upper and lower extremities. Difficulty with isolated fine motor tasks and sustained gripping. No focal weakness or sensory loss noted. Weakness not attributable to pain or joint limitation.
C3 History: Age 76 with sedentary lifestyle and reduced physical activity. Inadequate protein intake (40g daily versus recommended 1.0-1.2 g/kg body weight). Loss of appetite with reduced food intake. Multiple comorbidities including osteoarthritis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease limiting activity.
C4 Examination: Visible muscle wasting in bilateral upper extremities and thighs. Hip flexor and knee extensor strength 4/5 bilaterally; shoulder and elbow strength 4+/5. Tight hip flexors, otherwise no contractures. Normal tone. No tremor, fasciculations, or sensory loss. Mild difficulty with single-leg stance.
C5 Imaging: DEXA scan: fat-free mass index 5.2 kg/m² (below sarcopenia cutoff <5.75). MRI thigh: marked reduction in muscle cross-sectional area with diffuse intramuscular fat infiltration; no focal muscle edema or signal change to suggest inflammatory myopathy. Prealbumin 17 mg/dL (low, <20). Albumin 3.1 g/dL (low). CRP 2.1 mg/L (normal). TSH, creatine kinase normal. Vitamin D 18 ng/mL (deficient).
#592 Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder - Physical Rehabilitation General
✗ REWORK
Verdict: PTSD is a psychological diagnosis that belongs in mental health, not physiology; even framed as 'physical rehabilitation,' the core puzzle is about psychiatric diagnosis rather than a musculoskeletal or physiological condition a physio would independently diagnose, making it conceptually misaligned with the game's scope.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 35-year-old woman presenting with chronic widespread pain and stiffness 8 months post-motor vehicle collision. Reports sleep disturbance and significant anxiety. Avoids driving and has reduced exercise tolerance.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with unexpected loud noises and situations resembling the accident environment. Demonstrates guarded, protective movement patterns. Displays heightened vigilance and exaggerated startle response during examination.
C3 History: Severe motor vehicle collision with loss of consciousness and hospitalization. Reported whiplash mechanism with extensive soft tissue injuries. Persistent intrusive memories of event and active avoidance of vehicles and driving. Elevated mood symptoms.
C4 Examination: Upper cervical and thoracic ROM restricted in all planes with protective guarding. Cervical paraspinals and upper trapezius demonstrate elevated resting tone and multiple trigger points. Forward head posture present. Movement reproduction fails to identify structural pain source. Neurological examination unremarkable.
C5 Imaging: MRI cervical spine normal — no fracture, disc herniation, or cord pathology. Mild paraspinal muscle edema noted. Pain catastrophizing and fear-avoidance beliefs scale scores in upper range, indicating significant pain-related anxiety and cognitive amplification of symptoms.
#593 Patellar Dislocation Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute knee instability fits several conditions), clues 2–4 progressively narrow via activity limitations and clinical signs, and Clue 5 provides definitive imaging confirmation; the progression is clear and fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young athlete experiences acute knee pain and instability following a twisting injury during sport. The knee felt like it 'gave out' during activity.
C2 Activity: The patient cannot perform lateral movements or cutting maneuvers without fear of instability. Weight-bearing on a bent knee triggers apprehension. Cannot return to pivoting sports.
C3 History: The injury occurred during a sudden deceleration with foot planted. Patient reports this is the first time this has happened. Physiotherapist notes family history of knee instability and patient's naturally loose joints.
C4 Examination: Apprehension sign present with lateral patellar pressure. Medial patellofemoral ligament tenderness on palpation. Significant knee effusion restricts active extension in final 10 degrees.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate a patella that is reduced in anatomical position with possible osteochondral findings. MRI shows acute ligamentous disruption of the medial patellofemoral ligament. Imaging confirms acute patellar dislocation.
#594 Postural Hypotension Cardiovascular
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 naming 'dizzy spells upon standing' is reasonably broad, but Clue 4 essentially confirms the diagnosis by measuring the BP drop (25 mmHg) that defines orthostatic hypotension—Clue 5 becomes somewhat redundant by confirming the same measurement pattern over time rather than adding new clinical insight.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old retired teacher reports dizzy spells upon standing from a seated or supine position, occurring several times daily. Experiencing presyncope without loss of consciousness.
C2 Activity: Symptoms resolve within seconds to minutes of remaining still. Patient restricts activities due to fall risk concern. Slower positional changes help reduce episodes.
C3 History: History of hypertension managed with antihypertensive medications including ACE inhibitor and calcium channel blocker. Recent dose increase 2 weeks prior. No cardiac arrhythmias noted.
C4 Examination: Systolic blood pressure drops 25 mmHg when rising from supine to standing (sitting 142/88, standing 117/82). Heart rate appropriately increases from 62 to 80 bpm. No orthostatic tachycardia or paradoxical bradycardia. Gait steady, no balance deficits. No focal neurological signs.
C5 Imaging: Home blood pressure log over 7 days shows reproducible 20–30 mmHg systolic drops in morning hours and early evening. Orthostatic vital signs measured at multiple times of day confirm consistent pattern. No postural tachycardia or compensatory HR elevation variance.
#595 Post-Cardiac Surgery Rehabilitation Heart
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple post-surgical presentations, clues 2–3 add specific timeline and cardiac history, Clue 4 reveals musculoskeletal guarding and deconditioning pattern, and Clue 5 elegantly confirms cardiac parameters are normal—ruling out ischemia and anchoring the diagnosis in rehabilitation physiology.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 61-year-old man 6 weeks post-major cardiac surgery presents with progressive dyspnea and chest discomfort triggered by minimal activity. Exercise tolerance severely limited. Reports fatigue affecting daily function.
C2 Activity: Dyspnea limiting walking to 100 metres on flat ground. Chest wall pain restricts overhead reaching and shoulder mobility. Marked tachycardia response: HR 110 at rest, 140 with minimal walking. Disproportionate heart rate response compared to activity level.
C3 History: Six weeks post-CABG x3 with saphenous vein grafts. Ejection fraction improved from 35% to 50% post-operatively. Pre-operative sedentary lifestyle. Comorbidities: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes. No recent complications reported.
C4 Examination: Sternotomy incision well-healed without dehiscence. Shoulder abduction limited to 100 degrees due to guarding and incisional pain. Paradoxical breathing pattern with chest wall splinting evident. Orthostatic vital signs normal. Gait slow and cautious. No signs of acute cardiac compromise at rest (HR 85, BP 138/82).
C5 Imaging: Post-operative echocardiography: ejection fraction 50% with anterior wall motion normalising. ECG normal sinus rhythm, no ischemic changes. Cardiac biomarkers (troponin, BNP) normalized. Stress testing at 6 weeks: no ischemic changes, target heart rate achieved. Conclusion: exercise limitation attributable to deconditioning and musculoskeletal guarding rather than cardiac ischaemia or graft compromise.

C1 Complaint: 24-year-old hockey player presents with acute onset anterior chest wall pain after collision during play. Reports sudden swelling and visible deformity over medial shoulder region.
C2 Activity: Unable to adduct arm across body without sharp anterior chest pain. Cross-body reach test highly restricted. Overhead reaching also limited.
C3 History: Injury occurred during high-impact collision with direct anterior force. No prior shoulder or clavicular pathology. No neurovascular symptoms reported at time of injury.
C4 Examination: Marked tenderness over sternoclavicular joint. Medial clavicle palpably prominent and mobile superiorly. Pain reproduction with cross-body adduction. Upper extremity strength 5/5 throughout. Pulses and sensation intact distally. Deep palpation suggests soft tissue involvement extending posteriorly toward sternum.
C5 Imaging: X-ray (AP and 40-degree cephalic tilt views) shows anterior displacement of medial clavicle with joint space widening. CT chest demonstrates clavicle positioned anterior to sternum. No vascular compromise on imaging. Soft tissue swelling around joint evident on advanced imaging.

Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old man reports progressive anterior shoulder pain over 6 months. He struggles with tasks like putting on shirts and reaching behind his back. Pain is worse at night and disrupts sleep. History of overhead work activities.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform the lift-off test (hand locked against back). Weakness with internal rotation strength testing. Sharp anterior pain on resisted internal rotation. Scapular winging observed during arm elevation.
C3 History: Chronic rotator cuff pathology with degenerative changes. Progressive symptoms over 6 months despite physical therapy. Age-related tissue degeneration contributing. History of overhead work stress. Poor proprioceptive control and scapular dyskinesis noted.
C4 Examination: Lift-off test impossible (0/5 power, hand cannot lift from back). Bear-hug test shows 3/5 weakness. Positive anterior slide test. Tenderness over subscapularis insertion. Full passive internal rotation preserved; weakness only with resistance.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows subscapularis tendon tear with full-thickness component at musculotendinous junction. Associated rotator cuff pathology with supraspinatus and infraspinatus involvement. Subscapularis muscle atrophy evident. Bone marrow edema at lesser tuberosity insertion site. Ultrasound confirms tear with loss of normal tendon architecture.
#598 Falls in the Elderly Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad but somewhat generic (two falls + dizziness could fit many syndromes), and while clues 2–5 build a coherent multifactorial picture, the puzzle lacks a single unifying diagnosis—it reads more as a geriatric fall syndrome than a specific condition, which may frustrate players seeking a discrete answer.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 82-year-old woman reports two falls in the past month. Describes dizziness preceding each event. Expresses reduced confidence with mobility.
C2 Activity: Requires contact guard during walking. Complains of lightheadedness when changing position from sitting to standing. Demonstrates hesitant, slow movement pattern. Avoiding previously tolerated activities due to fear of recurrence.
C3 History: Age 82 with multiple fall risk factors: osteoporosis, hypertension on multiple medications causing orthostatic hypotension, mild cognitive impairment, vision reduced from cataracts. Lives alone in cluttered home environment. History of vitamin D insufficiency.
C4 Examination: Timed up and go test 28 seconds (abnormal >12 seconds). Single leg stance 3 seconds each leg (abnormal <10 seconds). Berg Balance Scale score 38/56 (high fall risk <45). Orthostatic vital signs positive (systolic BP drop 20 mmHg on standing). Gait assessment shows reduced stride length, shuffle pattern, and reduced arm swing.
C5 Imaging: DEXA scan confirms osteoporosis (T-score -2.9). Brain MRI normal. Vestibular assessment normal. Medication review confirms orthostatic-inducing antihypertensive regimen contributing to BP drops on standing.
#599 Mallet Finger Wrist & Hand
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clues 1 and 2 are nearly identical (both describe acute finger injury with inability to extend DIP joint), and Clues 3–4 repeat the same information without adding new detail—the progression stalls and fails to build suspense or reveal new information, making it unengaging as a guessing game.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 34-year-old man catches ring finger in door resulting in hyperflexion injury. Immediate pain over distal phalanx with inability to extend distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint. Minimal swelling and no gross deformity.
C2 Activity: 34-year-old man sustained acute finger injury when hand caught in door 1 day ago. Reports pain at tip of finger with inability to straighten the finger. Minimal visible swelling.
C3 History: Cannot actively straighten the distal joint of affected finger. Swelling mild and localised to fingertip. Sensory function and grip strength normal.
C4 Examination: Injury occurred 1 day ago with no prior finger problems or systemic disease. Patient has low occupational trauma risk but reports immediate functional loss after incident.
C5 Imaging: DIP joint held in 30-degree fixed flexion. Cannot actively extend against gravity or resistance. Passive extension elicits pain. Extensor mechanism appears intact proximally. No skin lacerations or bruising on dorsal surface. Neurological examination normal.
#600 Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome Cardiovascular
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (palpitations + lightheadedness on standing fit several cardiovascular conditions), clues 2–3 add timeline and triggering factors, Clue 4 reveals the key tachycardic response with minimal BP drop (distinguishing from orthostatic hypotension), and Clue 5 confirms with tilt-table testing and elevated norepinephrine—progression is sound and clinically educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 24-year-old woman reports palpitations, lightheadedness, and tremor that consistently occur when standing up from sitting or lying down.
C2 Activity: Unable to tolerate standing for >10-15 minutes without symptom worsening. Exercise causes disproportionate fatigue and palpitations. Lying down provides rapid symptom relief.
C3 History: Symptoms present for 3 years with gradual onset post-viral illness. No cardiac history. Symptoms worsen with heat, dehydration, and menstrual cycle.
C4 Examination: Resting supine vitals normal (HR 62 bpm, BP 118/76). On standing, heart rate rises to 115 bpm within 5 minutes with systolic BP drop <10 mmHg. Patient reports palpitations and lightheadedness with standing.
C5 Imaging: Tilt table testing (70° for 10 minutes): heart rate increases from 64 to 118 bpm while systolic BP remains stable at 116 mmHg. Plasma norepinephrine elevated when upright (500 pg/mL). Echocardiography and ECG normal.
#601 Glenohumeral Instability Shoulder
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C5 is labeled 'Imaging' but contains physical examination findings (apprehension test, relocation test, sulcus sign, load-and-shift test) rather than actual imaging; the clue progression collapses when examination findings repeat across C4 and C5.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young athlete presents with recurrent shoulder instability during sports activities. The patient reports sensation of shoulder slipping or apprehension with certain movement patterns. Functional limitation affects upper extremity performance.
C2 Activity: Young athlete reports intermittent shoulder symptoms affecting throwing and overhead activities. Onset occurred during sports participation. Patient describes episodes of fear or hesitation with certain arm positions.
C3 History: Patient unable to perform throwing activities or rapid overhead arm movements. Loss of confidence in shoulder control during sports-specific positions. Difficulty returning to pre-injury athletic performance.
C4 Examination: On examination, positive apprehension test indicates instability fear. Relocation test (Jobe's) relieves apprehension. Shoulder range of motion in external rotation is increased. Generalized capsular laxity is evident on examination.
C5 Imaging: Apprehension test positive at 90° abduction and external rotation. Relocation (Jobe's) test reproduces confidence. Sulcus sign present. Compared to contralateral side, significant asymmetry in external rotation range. Positive load-and-shift test indicating humeral head translation.
#602 Psoriatic Arthritis Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong progression from polyarthritis complaint through activity/history building systemic context, examination revealing skin/joint/nail findings, and C5 with serology/imaging confirming PsA diagnosis; fair difficulty for physio student.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old man with a 20-year history of skin disease presents with 6 months of progressive joint pain affecting fingers, wrists, and knees. Morning stiffness lasting 1.5 hours. Insidious onset.
C2 Activity: Unable to grip effectively (reduced functional grasp). Difficulty with fine finger movements and wrist flexion/extension. Morning stiffness prevents normal morning routine for first 1-2 hours.
C3 History: Skin condition flared 2 months before joint symptoms began. Strong family history of both skin disease and inflammatory arthritis. No previous joint problems. No recent infections or trauma.
C4 Examination: Asymmetric polyarthritis with dactylitis (sausage appearance of fingers). Nail dystrophy with pitting and onycholysis. Psoriatic plaques on elbows, knees, and scalp. Tenderness at Achilles insertions bilaterally. Wrist and finger joint swelling with warmth. Reduced grip strength (28 kg). Joint flexion contractures developing.
C5 Imaging: ESR elevated at 42 mm/hr, CRP 8.2 mg/dL. Rheumatoid factor negative, anti-CCP negative (seronegative pattern). HLA-B27 positive (positive in 70% of PsA). Hand and wrist X-rays show periosteal new bone formation (fluffy periostitis) and pencil-in-cup deformity early stages. Ultrasound demonstrates synovial hypertrophy with power Doppler activity indicating active inflammation.
#603 Hamstring Strain Thigh
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C5 is labeled 'Imaging' but repeats physical examination findings (palpation, SLR, hip extension, gait) already present in C4 with no actual imaging data; structural contradiction.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old sprinter presents with acute posterior thigh pain during 400-meter race. Pain onset abrupt during acceleration phase. Immediate functional loss with difficulty continuing sprint.
C2 Activity: A 28-year-old athlete presents with acute onset posterior thigh pain during high-intensity training. Pain occurred suddenly during an explosive movement. Unable to continue activity immediately.
C3 History: Pain triggered by hip flexion and knee extension in combination. Cannot weight-bear without protective antalgic gait. Swelling and bruising visible within 24 hours of onset.
C4 Examination: Palpable tenderness at mid-belly of hamstring. Swelling and warmth at mid-thigh location. Positive straight leg raise test with pain (limited to 40 degrees). Weak hip extension against resistance (3/5) due to pain. Gait antalgic with reduced stride length. Visible ecchymosis beginning to appear.
C5 Imaging: Palpable tenderness at mid-musculotendinous region with localized warmth. Straight leg raise limited to 40 degrees due to pain. Hip extension weakness (3/5) with preserved proprioception. Gait markedly antalgic with reduced stance phase on affected limb.
#604 Trigger Finger Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from catching/locking symptom through functional limitation and gradual history, to specific pulley nodule examination, and ultrasound confirmation of stenosing tenosynovitis; each clue adds genuine information.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old woman presents with catching and locking sensation in the right ring finger at the MCP joint. Clicking noise with finger flexion and extension. Worsens in morning.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with fine motor tasks including writing and typing. Painful with full finger extension. Functional limitation in hand dexterity and grip activities.
C3 History: Gradual onset over 3 months. No acute trauma. Repetitive gripping activities in workplace (packing work). Symptoms progressive with worsening locking episodes.
C4 Examination: Nodular thickening palpable over A1 pulley at MCP joint. Triggering reproduced with active finger flexion and extension. Triggering relieved with passive extension by opposite hand. Swelling at pulley level.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates nodular thickening of flexor tendon at A1 pulley with increased fluid signal around tendon. Swelling is localised to pulley, with no evidence of osteoarthritic changes or joint pathology. Findings consistent with stenosing tenosynovitis.
#605 Hamstring Strain Thigh
✓ READY
Verdict: Well-structured progression from acute posterior thigh pain in sprinter through mechanism/timeline and clinical findings, to MRI/ultrasound grade and location confirmation; clinically coherent and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old sprinter presents with acute onset posterior thigh pain. Pain began mid-competition during a high-speed running effort. Unable to continue activity immediately following onset.
C2 Activity: Sharp pain localizes to posterior mid-thigh during running and kicking motions. Visible swelling and bruising developed within 24 hours. Antalgic gait with markedly reduced stride length.
C3 History: Acute traumatic mechanism during sprinting with forceful hip flexion and knee extension (acceleration phase of running). Young athletic male with high-demand sport. No prior hamstring injury. Pre-existing hip flexor tightness noted. Inadequate warm-up prior to race.
C4 Examination: Palpable tender mass at mid-thigh with localized warmth. Straight leg raise limited to 40° due to pain. Hip extension strength 3/5 against resistance. Pain on resisted knee flexion.
C5 Imaging: MRI confirms Grade II partial thickness hamstring strain at musculotendinous junction of biceps femoris. Hematoma present at injury site (4cm x 3cm). Surrounding muscle edema. Intact fascial integrity (not retracted). Ultrasound shows hypoechoic fluid collection at strain site with intact muscle architecture proximally and distally.
#606 Lumbar Spondylolisthesis Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Logical progression from mechanical back/leg pain pattern through activity provocation (extension/standing worse, flexion relief) to neurological findings suggesting stenosis, culminating in imaging confirmation of slip and canal narrowing.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 54-year-old man with 5-year history of progressive lower back pain and bilateral leg pain. Symptoms worsening over past 6 months with increasing heaviness and fatigue in legs with walking.
C2 Activity: Back pain and leg symptoms worse with standing and lumbar extension; significantly relieved by sitting and forward bending. Walking tolerance severely limited; symptoms do not improve when standing still, only with rest in flexion.
C3 History: Gradual progressive course. Prior imaging noted degenerative disc changes in lower lumbar spine. Occupational history: 20 years heavy lifting and repetitive twisting. Limited core strength and sedentary lifestyle outside work.
C4 Examination: Lumbar extension reproduces back pain and bilateral leg heaviness. Forward flexion limited (hands to knees). Right ankle dorsiflexion weakness 4/5 (L5 myotome). Right ankle reflex diminished. Bilateral straight leg raise limited to 45° by pain in posterior leg.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows L4-5 spondylolisthesis with L4 vertebra slipped anteriorly 3mm relative to L5. Degenerative disc changes at L4-5 and L5-S1. MRI confirms spondylolisthesis with significant central spinal stenosis at L4-5 level (50% canal narrowing). Ligamentum flavum hypertrophy and disc bulging contributing. Foraminal stenosis on left side.
#607 Heterotopic Ossification Various
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Solid progression and clinical coherence, but C1 is quite specific (hip dislocation + fracture 12 weeks prior) which may constrain guessing too early; could broaden C1 to 'progressive motion loss post-major hip injury' for better guessing challenge.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old man presents 12 weeks after a significant hip injury with progressive loss of motion, pain, and swelling in the hip region. Activities of daily living increasingly difficult.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot dress or perform hip-intensive ADLs due to motion loss. Hip flexion contracture present; internal and external rotation markedly restricted. Pain limits all hip movements.
C3 History: High-energy posterior hip dislocation with complex femoral head fracture 12 weeks prior. Significant soft tissue trauma and prolonged immobilization. Male, age 42.
C4 Examination: Hip flexion contracture present (unable to achieve full extension). External and internal rotation severely limited (<15 degrees each direction). Abduction limited to 10 degrees. Palpable stiffness with firm end-feel on passive motion. Tenderness over hip region. Functional limitation with gait and transfers.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows extensive bony bridging across hip joint with mature ossification. CT demonstrates mature bone formation in soft tissues surrounding hip capsule. Associated joint space narrowing noted.
#608 Lower Back Pain - Work-Related Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C5 is labeled 'Imaging' but contains only physical examination findings (forward flexion, SLR, ROM, neuro exam, palpation) with no actual imaging data; clue 5 provides no new diagnostic information beyond C4 and fails as imaging-level confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old warehouse worker presents with chronic lower back pain for 3 years exacerbated by work activities. Pain worse at end of work week. Associated work-related absence and functional limitations.
C2 Activity: 38-year-old warehouse worker with 3 years of intermittent lower back pain. Notices pain builds throughout the work week and improves slightly with weekends off. Has taken several weeks off work in the past year due to flare-ups.
C3 History: Cannot sit comfortably for more than 30 minutes. Standing tolerance improving as day progresses. Symptoms improve with gentle movement but worsen sharply with repetitive lifting. No radiation below the knee.
C4 Examination: Limited forward flexion to fingertips 15cm from toes due to pain. Straight leg raise limited to 60 degrees by hamstring tightness and pain. One-legged stance provocative on both sides with pain reproduction. Lumbar paraspinal tenderness with guarding. Gait antalgic. Neurologically intact.
C5 Imaging: Forward flexion limited to 15cm from toes with pain at end-range. Bilateral SLR 60° limited by pain (not neurological irritation). Hip and knee ROM full and pain-free. Lumbar rotation worse than flexion. Neurologically intact with normal lower limb reflexes and strength. Palpable spasm in lumbar paraspinals bilateral; pain reproduced with resisted Valsalva.
#609 Ankylosing Spondylitis Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C4 is labeled 'Examination' but contains only history/timeline details (onset, prior imaging, family history) rather than physical findings; clue order is fundamentally misaligned with the framework.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old man presents with progressive morning stiffness in lumbar spine lasting 2 hours daily for 8 months. Pain improves with activity and worsens with rest. Nocturnal pain awakening him from sleep.
C2 Activity: Spinal stiffness particularly in morning and with inactivity. Buttock and lower back pain described as deep ache. Pain improves with activity and exercise. Progressive loss of spinal mobility with difficulty bending forward. Associated fatigue and malaise.
C3 History: Morning stiffness and spinal rigidity limiting forward bending. Buttock and lower back pain described as deep ache. Associated fatigue and malaise. Pain improves significantly with activity and exercise, worsening with prolonged rest.
C4 Examination: Insidious onset over 8 months without preceding infection, trauma, or occupational exposure. No significant medical history. Family history of autoimmune or rheumatologic disease unknown. Morning stiffness pattern and improvement with activity suggest inflammatory rather than mechanical aetiology. No prior imaging or rheumatologic workup performed.
C5 Imaging: MRI sacroiliac joints shows bilateral sacroiliitis with erosions and inflammatory signal changes. Lumbar spine shows syndesmophyte formation connecting vertebral bodies (bamboo spine early phase). HLA-B27 positive. ESR elevated at 48 mm/hr, CRP 9.2 mg/dL. Complete blood count and metabolic panel normal.
#610 Atelectasis Lungs
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from post-op respiratory distress through pain-limited breathing mechanics, to physical findings of reduced breath sounds and lobar changes, to imaging confirmation of collapse; educationally relevant for respiratory physio.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 54-year-old man, post-operative day 3 from major abdominal surgery. Worsening shortness of breath and oxygen saturation drop to 90% on room air. Fever 38.5°C. Significant pain at surgical site limiting ability to breathe deeply.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform incentive spirometry or effective coughing due to pain. Prefers shallow, rapid breathing pattern. Reluctant to mobilise or change position due to discomfort.
C3 History: Anesthesia 3 hours. Standard post-operative analgesia regimen (opioid-based). No prior lung disease or smoking history. Mobilisation deferred due to pain severity. Patient reports feeling 'unable to take a deep breath' and avoiding coughing.
C4 Examination: RR 24/min. Breath sounds diminished at both lower lobes, particularly left. Dullness to percussion over left lower lobe field. Coarse crackles in lower zones on inspiration. SpO₂ 90% RA, improves to 95% with supplemental oxygen. Fever 38.5°C. Chest wall splinting evident.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray shows opacification and collapse of left lower lobe fields consistent with atelectasis. Right lower lobe shows similar but less severe changes. Elevated hemidiaphragm on left. CT chest confirms left lower lobe consolidation and volume loss. No pneumonia (no peripheral consolidation pattern initially).
#611 Degenerative Disc Disease Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (chronic mechanical back pain fits many conditions), each clue adds distinct information (activity tolerance→history of occupational/lifestyle factors→normal neuro exam→imaging confirmation), and progression is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old woman with 8 years of chronic lower back pain. Gradual onset without inciting event. Pain mechanical in nature, worse with activity and sitting.
C2 Activity: Morning stiffness lasting 30 minutes that improves with movement. Difficulty with forward bending. Declining standing tolerance by end of day. Pain relief with specific position changes and recumbency.
C3 History: Age 62 with degenerative changes expected with aging. Occupational prolonged standing and repetitive bending for 35 years. History of heavy lifting in younger years. Sedentary lifestyle contributing to deconditioning. Smoking history 30 pack-years with known effects on disc health.
C4 Examination: Lumbar forward flexion limited to fingertips 20cm from toes. Straight leg raise normal (70 degrees) without radiculopathy. Lumbar paraspinal muscles tight with guarding. One-legged stance normal without pain. Neurologically completely intact. Gait normal without antalgic pattern.
C5 Imaging: Lumbar spine X-rays show multilevel degenerative disc disease with loss of disc height at L4-5 and L5-S1. Osteophyte formation present (bone spurs). Facet joint osteoarthritis. MRI shows disc desiccation with decreased T2 signal in multiple levels. Modest disc bulging at L4-5 without stenosis. No neural compromise.
#612 Overtraining Syndrome Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 captures fatigue + declining performance (fits multiple systemic conditions), subsequent clues narrow systematically through recovery patterns→training history→physiological markers→blood work ruling out alternatives, creating fair progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 24-year-old elite endurance athlete presents with persistent fatigue and declining athletic performance despite increased training volume over 3 months. Resting heart rate elevated 15 beats above baseline. Multiple minor illnesses over past 2 months.
C2 Activity: Unable to sustain previous training paces. Completes sessions but lacks usual power and speed. Feels 'heavy' during running. Takes 48-72 hours to feel recovered between hard sessions versus previous 24-hour recovery. Irritable and unmotivated about training despite previous enthusiasm.
C3 History: Intensive endurance training program with progressively increased volume without adequate recovery. Mileage increased 50% over 3 months. Inadequate sleep averaging 6 hours nightly. Poor nutritional recovery with insufficient caloric intake. Psychological stress from competition and team dynamics.
C4 Examination: Resting heart rate elevated at 75 bpm (personal baseline 60 bpm). Heart rate recovery after exercise test shows delayed normalization. VO2 max testing shows 10% reduction compared to previous testing. Psychological assessment shows mood disturbance and depression symptoms. Musculoskeletal examination normal.
C5 Imaging: Blood work shows normal thyroid function excluding thyroiditis. Normal complete blood count excluding anemia. Serum cortisol elevated with abnormal circadian rhythm. Ferritin at lower limit of normal suggesting iron depletion. CPK mildly elevated at 380 IU/L suggesting muscle stress. No viral serology positive.
#613 Frailty Syndrome Systemic
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clues 1 and 2 are nearly identical (both describe weakness/fatigue in a 79-year-old), Clue 3 belongs in examination (functional decline should be tested not just reported), and clue ordering is chaotic—makes puzzle incoherent rather than progressive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 79-year-old woman with progressive functional decline and multiple comorbidities presents with weakness and fatigue.
C2 Activity: A 79-year-old woman reports progressive tiredness and difficulty keeping up with daily tasks over the past 12 months.
C3 History: Cannot bathe or dress independently. Falls twice in past month without clear trauma. Increasingly confined to home. Family reports she's sleeping more and attending fewer social activities.
C4 Examination: Diabetes and heart disease managed suboptimally. Unintentional 8 kg weight loss over 8 months. Poor appetite. Lives alone, family provides twice-weekly support. Denies recent hospitalization or acute illness. Medications often missed.
C5 Imaging: Hand grip strength 12 kg (very low for age/sex). Knee extension 3/5 bilaterally. Timed up and go 35 seconds. Cannot rise from chair without arm support. Mini-Cog score 2/3 (cognitive impairment). Heart rate 92, BP 138/82 at rest.
#614 Dementia-Related Mobility Decline Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Overall structure works (shuffling gait→cognitive limits→severe imaging findings), but Clue 1 is quite specific (shuffling + weekly falls + cognitive context narrows diagnosis significantly) and could be broader; otherwise playable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An 82-year-old woman reports progressive difficulty walking over the past 18 months. She is increasingly unsteady, has multiple falls weekly despite supervision, and can no longer manage stairs independently.
C2 Activity: Patient demonstrates short, shuffling steps (approximately 15cm stride). Arm swing is absent and posture is stiff. Balance is severely impaired even with contact guard. Walking becomes noticeably worse when she tries to perform another task simultaneously (e.g., talking while ambulating).
C3 History: Patient has severe cognitive impairment limiting her ability to follow movement instructions or adapt to corrections. She has been sedentary in a care facility for several years and has lost 8 kg over the past year. Known hypertension and osteoporosis present.
C4 Examination: Gait speed measured at 0.3 m/sec. Hip and knee flexion markedly reduced during swing phase. Patient cannot lift feet properly, dragging them forward. Stance transitions reveal significant postural instability and risk of collapse. Forward head posture and stooped trunk evident. Patient cannot correct gait errors even when instructed verbally.
C5 Imaging: Brain MRI shows severe cortical atrophy with enlargement of lateral ventricles and multiple white matter changes. The pattern of atrophy involves frontal and parietal regions including motor and executive control areas. No acute focal lesions, stroke, or intracranial hemorrhage.
#615 Knee Osteoarthritis Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad, but Clue 5 merely repeats Clue 4 information (both describe OA on imaging without adding new discriminatory detail), and the McMurray's note as 'ambiguous' is vague rather than educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old presents with insidious-onset knee pain and stiffness over several years. Pain worsens with activity and is present first thing in the morning.
C2 Activity: Patient reports pain peaks mid-to-late afternoon after activity. Swelling appears after prolonged standing. Walking on flat surfaces is less symptomatic than walking downhill or descending stairs.
C3 History: No significant acute trauma. Patient reports previous knee ligament injury 15 years ago. Progressive worsening over past 3 years despite activity modification. One prior intra-articular steroid injection 6 months ago with temporary relief.
C4 Examination: Joint line tenderness on palpation. Crepitus on active ROM throughout range. Flexion limited to 100°, extension lag of 10°. Mild palpable effusion. Positive McMurray's test (ambiguous). Quadriceps strength 4/5 bilaterally, hip abductors 4+/5.
C5 Imaging: Weight-bearing radiographs demonstrate joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis. Cartilage loss is evident. Imaging confirms osteoarthritic changes.
#616 Thoracolumbar Fascia Pain Lumbar Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (lateral lower back pain + twisting), each clue adds genuine specificity (activity triggers→occupational history→fascial imaging changes), and MRI/ultrasound findings clinch the diagnosis distinctly.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old construction worker reports lateral lower back pain that developed gradually over 6 weeks. Pain worsens with twisting and bending movements typical of his job.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform sustained rotational work or reach across his body. Simple bed rest relieves symptoms within 24 hours. Functional impact prevents normal construction work duties.
C3 History: Onset correlates with a particularly demanding construction project involving repetitive lifting and twisting in awkward postures. Prior lumbar strain 2 years prior resolved with conservative care. No acute trauma or fall. Patient admits to minimal exercise outside of work.
C4 Examination: Tenderness along thoracolumbar fascia region bilaterally (flanks at L4-5 level). Pain with trunk rotation against resistance. Reduced lumbar rotation in both directions (limited by pain). Palpable muscle guarding in quadratus lumborum region. Single leg stance provokes lateral back pain. Neurologically intact.
C5 Imaging: Lumbar spine X-rays normal excluding fracture or structural abnormality. MRI shows fascial thickening and edema in thoracolumbar fascia region. Quadratus lumborum muscle shows edema and signal changes consistent with myofascial injury. No nerve compression or disc pathology. Ultrasound confirms fascial thickening with echo changes.
#617 Rheumatoid Arthritis Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple arthropathies (bilateral symmetric joint swelling), subsequent clues progressively narrow through morning stiffness pattern→family autoimmune history→symmetric hand exam findings→serology and inflammatory markers definitively confirming RA.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 42-year-old woman with 6-week history of gradual-onset joint pain and swelling affecting both hands. Symptoms worse in the morning, improving somewhat with activity. Reports fatigue and general malaise.
C2 Activity: Difficulty gripping objects, dressing, and fine motor tasks. Pain and swelling predominantly in hands bilaterally. Functional impact worse after prolonged rest or overnight inactivity.
C3 History: No preceding injury or trauma. Family history significant for autoimmune thyroid disease and lupus. Recent mild weight loss. No improvement with rest or non-steroidal anti-inflammatories. Patient previously well with no medical comorbidities.
C4 Examination: Symmetric swelling and warmth of metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Grip strength bilaterally reduced (14 kg versus baseline 30 kg). Rheumatoid nodules palpable on elbows. Positive squeeze test of metacarpophalangeal joints reproducing pain. Reduced hand function with difficulty making fist.
C5 Imaging: Rheumatoid factor positive (titer 1:1280). Anti-CCP positive (256 IU/mL). ESR elevated at 52 mm/hr, CRP 18 mg/dL. Hand X-rays show early periarticular osteopenia with marginal erosions. Ultrasound demonstrates synovial hypertrophy with power Doppler activity in affected joints. Complete blood count shows mild anemia (Hb 10.8).
#618 Dyspareunia Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (postmenopausal pain with multiple causes possible), clues 2–4 build logically from symptom triggers→postmenopausal context→pelvic floor hypertonia examination, and imaging confirms muscular/connective tissue pathology rather than structural lesion.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 38-year-old woman with 8 months of pain during sexual activity. Postmenopausal for 1 year. Significant relationship impact and mood disturbance.
C2 Activity: Pain occurs only with penetration attempt, preventing intercourse. No pain with non-penetrative contact or daily activities. Pain severity has worsened progressively over 8 months.
C3 History: Gradual onset 8 months ago without clear inciting event. Post-menopausal status 1 year prior contributing to tissue changes. No prior pelvic trauma or surgery. Stress and relationship factors contributing. History of anxiety and depression. Recent hormonal changes.
C4 Examination: Pelvic floor examination shows hypertonic pelvic floor muscles with trigger points palpable. Tenderness on introital palpation. Pelvic floor muscle spasm evident during examination. Levator ani muscles tight bilaterally. Vulvar erythema present in introital region. Speculum examination normal excluding other causes.
C5 Imaging: Transvaginal ultrasound shows increased echogenicity and thickening of pelvic floor muscles. No endometriosis, adenomyosis, or structural lesions. Findings consistent with myofascial hypertrophy and spasm.
#619 Chronic Pain Syndrome Whole Body
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clues 1 and 2 are virtually identical (both describe 4-year widespread pain post-injury with normal imaging), redundancy kills progression, and the clinical story relies heavily on psychological labels (non-organic findings, pain catastrophizing) rather than building a fair diagnostic mystery.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 55-year-old woman with 4-year history of persistent widespread pain following low back injury. Extensive prior investigations normal without clear structural cause. Functional capacity significantly reduced with mood disturbance and social withdrawal.
C2 Activity: 55-year-old woman with 4-year history of persistent widespread pain that began after a low back strain. Extensive imaging and investigations have been normal. Reports significant disability and mood changes, but functional loss seems disproportionate to any structural findings on imaging.
C3 History: Pain reported in back, neck, shoulders, and hips. Constant baseline pain with variable intensity throughout day. Sleep severely disrupted with unrefreshing sleep pattern affecting daytime alertness and concentration.
C4 Examination: Physical examination shows non-anatomic pain patterns and inconsistent findings. Pain reproducible with non-organic examination maneuvers. Widespread tender points throughout body. Psychological distress evident with mood changes during examination. Functional guarding with inconsistent effort during testing.
C5 Imaging: Multiple prior imaging studies normal including MRI spine, pelvis, and brain. Extensive lab work unrevealing (normal inflammatory markers, metabolic panel, thyroid). Psychological evaluation shows depression (PHQ-9 score 22), anxiety (GAD-7 score 18), and pain catastrophizing. Pain amplification scale elevated suggesting central sensitization component.
#620 Freiberg's Disease Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (forefoot pain in young athlete), each clue adds specificity (weight-bearing impact→no trauma history→localized metatarsal head tenderness→imaging of osteonecrosis), and progression is clear and engaging for physio students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 16-year-old female athlete presents with insidious-onset pain in the forefoot. Pain worsens with weight-bearing activities over the past 2 months.
C2 Activity: Unable to tolerate impact activities and sports. Swelling visible over the forefoot. Antalgic gait with avoidance of push-off phase during walking.
C3 History: Athlete in repetitive jumping/running sport. No acute injury. Symptoms gradually worsening despite activity modification and rest. No prior treatment sought yet.
C4 Examination: Palpable tenderness directly over second metatarsal head (dorsal aspect). Swelling and prominence of metatarsal head region. Pain with passive dorsiflexion of second metatarsophalangeal joint. Limited metatarsophalangeal joint motion secondary to pain. Gait antalgic with reduced stance phase on affected foot.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows flattening and fragmentation of second metatarsal head consistent with Freiberg's infraction (osteonecrosis). Dorsal hypertrophic changes present. MRI shows osteochondral lesion with bone marrow edema. T1 signal hypointensity within metatarsal head indicating necrosis. Associated cartilage irregularity.
#621 Shoulder Dislocation Shoulder
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is excessively specific (mentions 'anterior dislocation,' 'forced external rotation,' 'immediate severe pain,' 'protective position') making it nearly diagnostic; clues 2-4 largely repeat the same clinical presentation without progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 22-year-old male athlete sustains anterior shoulder dislocation during contact sport. Violent arm positioning with forced external rotation and abduction. Immediate severe pain with arm held in protective position.
C2 Activity: A 22-year-old male athlete presents with acute severe shoulder pain and inability to move the arm following a tackle during contact sport. Arm held protectively at side. Onset was immediate.
C3 History: Cannot abduct or externally rotate shoulder. Arm guarded against any movement away from torso. Visible prominence of anterior shoulder contour.
C4 Examination: Mechanism involved a tackle with arm driven into forced abduction and external rotation. No history of prior shoulder instability, generalized hypermobility, or connective tissue disorder. No prior dislocations or sprains.
C5 Imaging: X-ray (AP, scapular Y, and axillary views) confirms anterior humeral head dislocation. Associated Bankart lesion (glenoid rim fracture) visible on specialized imaging. CT scan shows intra-articular loose body and osseous Bankart fragment. MRI post-reduction shows rotator cuff integrity with possible labral involvement.
#622 Multidirectional Instability Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (vague shoulder instability in overhead athlete), clues progress logically from activity-specific symptoms to examination findings (sulcus sign, apprehension) to imaging (enlarged capsular volume), creating fair diagnostic progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 28-year-old overhead athlete reports intermittent shoulder pain with a sensation of the shoulder 'giving way' during throwing. Symptoms are vague but reproducible with certain movements in sport.
C2 Activity: Cannot tolerate cocking phase of throw without apprehension. Unable to perform weighted activities (e.g., push-ups, bench press) without feeling shoulder is unstable. Symptoms worse in positions of shoulder abduction and external rotation.
C3 History: Overhead athlete with progressive symptoms over past 2 seasons correlating with increased throwing volume. No discrete traumatic injury. Denies prior shoulder surgery or known hypermobility syndrome. Reports sense of fatigue in shoulder during extended practice.
C4 Examination: Positive sulcus sign (inferior shoulder subluxation with traction). Positive apprehension and relocation tests. Laxity demonstrated with inferior translation in dependent position (drawer sign). Scapular dyskinesis evident during active motion. Reduced internal rotation relative to external rotation (reverse position). Strength 4+/5 indicating dynamic stabilizer weakness.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows enlarged rotator cuff interval with increased capsular volume. Posterior labral fraying (SLAP pathology) associated lesion. Excessive glenohumeral translation documented on imaging. No discrete labral detachment (rules out Bankart). Ultrasound demonstrates increased capsular volume and laxity on dynamic maneuvers.
#623 Erb's Palsy Upper Limb
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is slightly broad but mentions 'neonatal' context which narrows possibilities; clues 2-3 repeat the protective positioning, but the progression to preganglionic injury on imaging is reasonable—could improve clue overlap but remains playable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Neonate presents with upper limb weakness and loss of function noted shortly after birth. Parents report decreased movement and apparent pain or discomfort with arm handling during the first few days of life.
C2 Activity: Infant demonstrates severe limitation in shoulder movement. The arm is held in a characteristic resting posture with reduced abduction and external rotation. Upper limb protective positioning is evident; infant resists passive shoulder movement.
C3 History: Delivery was complicated by shoulder dystocia requiring manual traction to deliver the infant. Neonatal exam performed within 24 hours of birth noted the motor deficit. No prior upper limb trauma or congenital anomalies.
C4 Examination: On examination: biceps and deltoid strength grossly weak (0-1/5). Moro reflex is absent on affected side; lower limb Moro intact. Passive shoulder abduction and external rotation severely limited. No claw hand or wrist drop observed.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the brachial plexus reveals avulsion of the C5-C6 nerve roots from the spinal cord. The upper trunk demonstrates signal abnormality and discontinuity. Nerve root avulsion is confirmed with imaging findings consistent with preganglionic injury.
#624 Chronic Ankle Instability Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is sufficiently broad (recurrent ankle giving way post-injury fits multiple conditions), clues progress logically from activity limitations to mechanical testing to imaging, with good information distribution.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 30-year-old athlete reports recurrent episodes of ankle giving way, particularly during cutting or pivoting movements. The instability started after a previous ankle injury several months ago.
C2 Activity: Unable to walk confidently on uneven terrain without fear of ankle giving way. Struggles with quick directional changes and single-leg activities. Normal weight-bearing walking is mostly stable, but sudden movements trigger instability.
C3 History: Chronic ligamentous laxity follows previous ankle sprains and incomplete rehabilitation. Proprioceptive deficits persist despite symptom resolution. Multiple recurrent inversion episodes have established chronic instability.
C4 Examination: On examination, positive anterior drawer test indicates significant tibiotalar translation. Inversion stress testing demonstrates hypermobility. Proprioceptive testing reveals impaired balance. Ankle proprioception is diminished.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates chronic lateral ligament attenuation with scar tissue. Anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL) shows partial thickening and signal changes consistent with healed but weakened ligament. No osteochondral lesion or syndesmotic involvement.
#625 Cerebral Palsy CNS
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad, but clues 2-3 are somewhat redundant (both emphasize developmental delay and motor patterns); clue 4 shifts confusingly to birth history when examination findings should dominate—minor sequencing issue.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young child presents with developmental delay in motor milestones and abnormal movement patterns. Muscle tone abnormalities and stiffness restrict normal motor function. The condition is nonprogressive but becomes more apparent with age and development.
C2 Activity: A young child with delayed motor development and increased muscle stiffness. Parents report abnormal movement patterns emerging over the first months of life. The condition has remained stable but becomes more functionally limiting as the child grows.
C3 History: Child walks on toes with knees bent and legs crossing in front of each other. Hip adduction is pronounced bilaterally. Unable to achieve normal heel-strike during ambulation.
C4 Examination: Birth complicated by prolonged labour and low Apgar scores. Motor difficulties were apparent within first weeks of life. Parents note the pattern of movement dysfunction has not worsened or improved significantly over years.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the brain demonstrates periventricular leukomalacia with signal changes in white matter around the ventricles. Ventricular dilation may be present secondary to white matter loss. The imaging findings correlate with the pattern of spastic diplegia observed clinically.
#626 Exercise-Induced Mood Improvement General
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is not a pathological diagnosis suitable for Physiodle; it's a treatment outcome or normal physiological response, not a condition to be 'diagnosed'—fundamentally misaligned with the game's purpose.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports feeling low in mood and anxious. Describes lack of motivation and mental fatigue impacting daily function.
C2 Activity: Patient finds it difficult to engage in work and social activities. Reports feeling most functional and engaged during and immediately after structured physical activity.
C3 History: Six-month history of low mood and anxiety. Previously tried antidepressant medications with modest response. Noticed spontaneous improvement when started attending gym 3× weekly. No other recent life changes or medical events.
C4 Examination: Cardiovascular fitness testing shows improved VO₂ max and resting heart rate compared to baseline 3 months ago. Mood questionnaire (PHQ-9) score improved from 16 to 8. Patient demonstrates consistent attendance and progressive training intensity.
C5 Imaging: Baseline psychiatric evaluation ruled out bipolar disorder and primary mood disorder requiring pharmacotherapy. Sleep study normal. No abnormalities on basic metabolic or thyroid screening. Exercise-based intervention has produced sustained mood improvement without additional medication or psychotherapy changes.
#627 Adhesive Capsulitis Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (middle-aged woman with shoulder pain), clues progress logically through activity loss, risk factors, characteristic capsular pattern examination, and confirmatory imaging findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Middle-aged woman with diabetes presenting with shoulder pain. Onset gradual over recent weeks. Pain present at rest and with activity, disrupting sleep and daily routines.
C2 Activity: Unable to reach overhead or behind back. All shoulder movements restricted. Patient reports difficulty with dressing, grooming, and sleeping on affected side.
C3 History: Risk factors include middle age, female gender, and poorly controlled diabetes mellitus. The condition often follows minor shoulder trauma or immobilization. Onset is typically gradual with progressive loss of motion over weeks to months.
C4 Examination: Passive external rotation severely limited (near 0 degrees). Scapulohumeral rhythm abnormal with early scapular elevation. Discomfort at end-range in capsular pattern. No weakness detected.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs of the shoulder are typically normal without bony abnormality. MR arthrography demonstrates thickening and enhancement of the joint capsule. The rotator cuff interval is tightened with loss of the normal axillary pouch distensibility.
#628 Overactive Bladder Pelvis
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clues 1-3 are somewhat repetitive (all emphasize frequency and urgency), and clues 1-2 together essentially describe the diagnosis; clues 4-5 add value but earlier redundancy reduces progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 55-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of urinary urgency and frequency that significantly impacts her quality of life. She experiences a persistent urge to urinate 12-14 times daily and 3-4 times nightly, even when the bladder contains minimal urine.
C2 Activity: The patient has modified her activities extensively to stay near bathroom facilities and has experienced occasional urgency incontinence when unable to reach the toilet immediately. Social activities and travel are limited by her symptoms, and sleep is disrupted by nocturia.
C3 History: Patient has restricted activities to remain near bathrooms and experiences occasional leakage when unable to toilet immediately. Sleep is disrupted by frequent nighttime voids. Social and travel plans are significantly limited.
