C4 Examination: On examination, vital signs are normal and abdominal examination reveals no masses or tenderness. Pelvic floor muscle assessment shows normal strength but evidence of increased tone. Neurological examination of lower extremities and perineal sensation are intact.
C5 Imaging: Bladder ultrasound performed post-void demonstrates normal post-void residual volume of 25 mL. Renal ultrasound is unremarkable with no hydronephrosis. Urinalysis is completely normal with negative culture. Cystoscopy was performed and revealed no structural abnormalities or malignancy.
#629 Patellar Tendinopathy Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (anterior knee pain in athletes fits multiple conditions), clues progress logically from activity-specific reproduction to localized tenderness to imaging confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young athlete reports anterior knee pain that worsens with jumping and cutting activities. Pain is activity-dependent, onset gradual over weeks to months.
C2 Activity: Pain during landing from jumps and deceleration activities. Single-leg stance and stairs provoke symptoms. Pain often resolves during activity but returns severely after sport, with stiffness the next morning.
C3 History: Onset follows increased training volume or intensity, particularly jumping-based sports. Weakness in hip or core muscles may contribute to abnormal loading patterns. Previous patellar or knee pathology may predispose to tendinopathy.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is localized to the patellar tendon insertion at the tibial tubercle. Single leg squats demonstrate poor motor control and knee valgus. Pain occurs with resisted knee extension testing.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound imaging reveals thickened patellar tendon with mixed echogenicity and increased vascularity on Doppler. MRI demonstrates signal changes within the tendon substance. Imaging findings correlate with symptom duration and severity.
#630 Axillary Nerve Palsy Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (acute shoulder weakness post-trauma could fit several conditions), clues progress logically through inability to abduct, specific neurological findings (regimental badge distribution), and confirmatory imaging/EMG.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old male presents with acute-onset weakness and numbness in the shoulder region following a severe fall with direct shoulder impact.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot initiate any movements requiring shoulder abduction. Passive range of motion is preserved. Weakness is the primary functional limitation rather than pain.
C3 History: Direct trauma from fall onto shoulder from standing height. No prior neurological symptoms, shoulder pathology, or recurrent dislocations. First neurological symptoms noted immediately post-injury.
C4 Examination: On examination, deltoid muscle is atrophic and significantly weakened with shoulder abduction strength 2/5. Sensation is diminished over the lateral shoulder in the regimental badge distribution. Axillary nerve traction test is positive. Rotator cuff strength is intact.
C5 Imaging: Shoulder X-rays rule out fracture or dislocation. MRI shows no rotator cuff tear but reveals signal changes in the axillary nerve course with surrounding soft tissue edema. Electromyography demonstrates denervation potentials in deltoid and axillary nerve conduction slowing.
#631 Post-Total Shoulder Replacement Shoulder
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 explicitly names the diagnosis ('following a surgical procedure'), making it impossible to guess; the entire puzzle becomes trivial once revealed.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports shoulder pain and difficulty moving the arm. Pain is worse with attempted active motion. Onset was acute, following a surgical procedure several weeks ago.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform overhead activities or reaching tasks. Cannot lift arm away from body or sleep on affected side. Requires assistance with dressing and grooming. Functional limitation is global rather than movement-specific.
C3 History: Patient underwent major shoulder surgery approximately 6 weeks ago for chronic degenerative joint disease. Pre-operative ROM was severely restricted. Recovery has been slower than anticipated; passive stretching causes apprehension.
C4 Examination: Passive external rotation is limited to 20°; forward flexion 60° with pain. Apprehension noted at end-range passive motion. No strength testing performed. Rotator cuff integrity not formally assessed. Shoulder girdle musculature appears atrophic.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative X-rays show secure component fixation with no signs of loosening or subsidence. No heterotopic ossification noted. Soft tissue shadow consistent with post-operative edema.
#632 Cystocele Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (pelvic heaviness fits multiple conditions), each clue adds specific information, and Clue 5 clinches with imaging confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A woman in her 60s reports sensation of heaviness and pressure in the pelvis that worsens as the day progresses. She also notices increased urinary frequency and occasional urgency.
C2 Activity: Patient reports she must avoid prolonged standing and cannot perform heavy lifting without significant symptom exacerbation. Symptoms improve within minutes of lying flat.
C3 History: Parity and vaginal deliveries are significant risk factors for pelvic floor dysfunction. Chronic straining from constipation or chronic cough may contribute. Advancing age and menopause worsen pelvic floor laxity.
C4 Examination: On pelvic examination, anterior vaginal wall bulging is evident with Valsalva maneuver. The bladder prolapse may be visualized or palpated during straining. Grade of prolapse is classified from 1-4 based on extent of descent.
C5 Imaging: Dynamic pelvic MRI shows anterior compartment prolapse with bladder descent of >2 cm below pubic symphysis during straining, consistent with grade 2-3 cystocele. No posterior compartment involvement.
#633 Post-Total Hip Replacement Hip
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 2 explicitly states 'following a surgical procedure' making diagnosis obvious; also Clue 5 repeats ROM and examination findings rather than adding new information.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient in the immediate post-operative period following total hip arthroplasty reports pain at the surgical site. Hip motion is restricted by protective positioning and precautions. Functional mobility is severely limited in early post-operative phase.
C2 Activity: 64-year-old presents with acute hip pain and severe functional limitation. Onset was 5 days ago following a surgical procedure. Pain is worst with hip movement, particularly flexion and internal rotation. Patient is non-weight bearing and using assistive devices.
C3 History: Patient cannot weight bear independently and requires walker for mobility. Hip abduction is markedly limited. Dressing, toileting, and bed transfers are severely restricted. Pain increases sharply with hip flexion beyond 60 degrees and adduction past midline.
C4 Examination: On examination, surgical site shows appropriate wound healing without excessive drainage. Hip motion is limited per post-operative precautions. Muscle guarding is present with pain on end-range motion testing.
C5 Imaging: Surgical wound healing well with minimal erythema or effusion. Hip ROM: flexion 45 degrees, abduction 15 degrees, internal rotation severely restricted. Straight leg raise weak and painful. Trendelenburg sign present. No calf swelling or warmth. Hip flexor and abductor muscle guarding evident.
#634 Diastasis Rectus Abdominis Abdomen
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (postpartum abdominal bulging fits multiple conditions), progression is logical with palpable gap then ultrasound confirmation, and the story is coherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 6-week postpartum woman reports visible abdominal bulging at the midline and generalized abdominal weakness. Concerned about functional recovery.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform sit-ups or roll bed-side without support. Difficulty carrying infant. Symptoms worsen with straining or Valsalva maneuver but improve with abdominal bracing.
C3 History: Uncomplicated vaginal delivery 6 weeks prior. Weight gained 16 kg during pregnancy. Return to exercise initiated without postpartum rehabilitation guidance. No diastasis history.
C4 Examination: Palpable gap of 3 fingers width between rectus muscles at midline above umbilicus, reducing to 2 fingers below umbilicus. Rectus muscles separate further with abdominal contraction. Intact fascia, no hernia.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound confirms diastasis rectus abdominis with interrectal distance 3.5 cm. Linea alba thickness normal. No ventral hernia present. Dynamic ultrasound shows muscle separation increase with contraction.
#635 Pectoralis Major Rupture Chest
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (sudden chest pain during weightlifting fits multiple conditions), each clue adds distinct information, and Clue 5 provides definitive imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A weightlifter reports sudden sharp pain in the anterior chest during a heavy bench press. Immediate weakness and inability to continue lifting. Notices visible change in chest appearance.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot initiate or perform any pushing movements. Unable to perform horizontal adduction against gravity. Pain increases with any attempt to adduct the arm across the body.
C3 History: Rupture typically occurs during heavy weightlifting with eccentric loading during bench press. The distal tendon attachment at the humerus is the most common rupture site. Steroids or chronic tendon pathology may predispose to rupture.
C4 Examination: Strength testing confirms profound weakness in horizontal adduction and internal rotation. Resisted adduction produces no contraction. Palpation along the anterior chest reveals a palpable gap or indentation where muscle continuity is lost. Asymmetry with the contralateral side is striking.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the chest reveals discontinuity of the pectoralis major tendon near its humeral insertion. Muscle retraction and edema are evident at the rupture site. Imaging confirms the site and extent of rupture for surgical planning.
#636 Meniscal Tear Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough to fit multiple knee pathologies, progression builds logically from symptoms through special tests to MRI, and the puzzle is fair and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports knee pain with intermittent mechanical symptoms. Pain is localized around the knee joint. Swelling develops gradually, particularly worse after prolonged weight-bearing activities.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to perform rotational movements of the knee under load. Squatting and deep flexion with rotation are significantly limited. Occasional episodes of knee 'giving way' or sudden inability to extend fully.
C3 History: Tear may follow a specific injury with twisting mechanism or develop insidiously in degenerative knees. Previous meniscal pathology or knee injuries increase risk. Degenerative tears are common in middle-aged and older patients.
C4 Examination: Joint line palpation reproduces localized tenderness. Knee effusion present. Range of motion limited in flexion, particularly with rotation. Special tests for meniscal irritation are positive.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the knee demonstrates meniscal tear in the medial or lateral meniscus with classification of tear pattern. T2 signal intensity within the meniscal substance indicates tear. Imaging localizes the tear and guides treatment decisions.
#637 Hearing Loss Ear
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Hearing loss is outside the scope of a physiotherapy diagnosis game and its clues describe audiological/medical examination rather than physiotherapy assessment.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old man reports progressive hearing difficulty for past 5 years affecting both ears symmetrically. Difficulty hearing high-pitched sounds and female voices. Wife notes television volume excessively loud.
C2 Activity: Patient reports difficulty understanding speech in background noise, described as 'cocktail party problem'. Words sound muffled and distorted. Tinnitus present. Withdrawn from social gatherings and group meetings due to communication difficulty.
C3 History: Age 68 with expected age-related hearing changes. 45-year occupational noise exposure as machinist without ear protection. No ototoxic medications. No prior ear surgery or trauma. Family history of hearing loss in father. Smoking history 40 pack-years.
C4 Examination: Weber test lateralizes to right ear (better hearing). Rinne test shows air conduction > bone conduction bilaterally but air-bone gap present. Otoscopy reveals cerumen impaction bilaterally; post-removal, gap persists. Speech discrimination score 68%.
C5 Imaging: Audiometry shows bilateral sensorineural hearing loss predominantly at frequencies >2000 Hz, with air-bone gaps of 15-25 dB across all frequencies. Bone conduction thresholds elevated at 4000-8000 Hz. Tympanometry normal (type A). Acoustic reflexes intact bilaterally.
#638 Osteitis Pubis Hip & Groin
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (groin pain in athletes fits multiple conditions), each clue adds specific information about location and mechanism, and Clue 5 provides imaging confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An athlete reports insidious onset of pain in the lower abdomen and groin region over several weeks. Pain is activity-related and worsens with sport participation. No acute injury or trauma.
C2 Activity: Pain is provoked by hip adduction and flexion under load. Crossing legs, rapid directional changes, and forceful kicking reproduce symptoms. Symptoms settle with activity modification but recur with return to sport.
C3 History: Condition occurs in athletes with repetitive hip adduction and flexion activities. Asymmetric hip strength and flexibility contribute to excessive shear at the symphysis. Previous hip or groin injuries increase risk.
C4 Examination: Tenderness is elicited over the pubic symphysis, worse with direct palpation. Pain is reproduced with resisted hip adduction in supine, and with combined flexion-adduction against resistance. Pain is NOT reproduced with hip abduction or external rotation.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate symphyseal joint changes including widening, sclerosis, and bone resorption. Bone scintigraphy shows increased uptake at the pubic symphysis. MRI reveals edema and inflammatory changes at the symphyseal joint.
#639 Sarcopenia Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 5 essentially repeats examination findings instead of providing new imaging or confirmatory data; consider adding DEXA or body composition imaging for true progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 76-year-old woman presents with progressive weakness and functional decline over 2 years. Difficulty rising from chair without armrest support. Walking slower and shorter distances than previously. Recent fall from trip on uneven pavement.
C2 Activity: Reduced grip strength (12 kg versus 22 kg in contralateral hand). Difficulty with stairs and walking long distances. Loss of independence with heavy household tasks. Slow gait velocity (0.7 m/sec versus normal 1.4 m/sec). Difficulty getting out of bed and toilet independently.
C3 History: Age 76 with sedentary lifestyle and reduced physical activity. Inadequate protein intake (40g daily versus recommended 1.0-1.2 g/kg body weight). Loss of appetite with reduced food intake. Multiple comorbidities including osteoarthritis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease limiting activity.
C4 Examination: Visible muscle wasting in upper extremities and thighs. Reduced grip strength 12 kg (abnormal cutoff <16 kg for women). Slow gait speed 0.7 m/sec (abnormal <1.0 m/sec). Reduced lower extremity strength particularly hip flexors and knee extensors (4/5). Difficulty rising from chair in <12 seconds (requires 18 seconds).
C5 Imaging: Visible muscle wasting in upper extremities and thighs. Reduced hip flexor and knee extensor strength (4/5 bilaterally). Calf muscle bulk reduced. No focal neurological deficits. No joint swelling or active inflammatory signs. Normal proprioception and balance on tandem stance; difficulty initiating movement rather than coordination loss.
#640 Pulmonary Fibrosis Lungs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Pulmonary fibrosis is a medical/respiratory diagnosis outside the core scope of physiotherapy diagnostics, and the clues describe medical rather than physiotherapy-relevant assessment.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports progressive dyspnea on exertion with gradual loss of exercise capacity. Dry cough that does not improve persists for months. Fatigue and general weakness accompany respiratory symptoms.
C2 Activity: Patient reports significant exertional dyspnea with limited exercise tolerance. Breathlessness is disproportionate to activity—walking short distances causes substantial respiratory distress.
C3 History: Progressive symptoms over 1–2 years. Occupational dust exposure (silica, asbestos, or organic antigens) or significant smoking history present. No acute infectious trigger identified.
C4 Examination: On auscultation, fine end-inspiratory crackles are heard bilaterally at lung bases. Digital clubbing may be evident with nail bed changes. Cyanosis may appear with advanced disease and hypoxemia.
C5 Imaging: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates reticular opacities with traction bronchiectasis in a usual interstitial pneumonia pattern. Pulmonary function testing shows restrictive ventilatory defect with significantly reduced DLCO.
#641 Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (anterior knee pain), each clue adds distinct information (activity limitations → history → biomechanical findings → imaging), and progression is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old female cyclist with chronic anterior knee pain reports increasing difficulty with cycling distances. Pain worsens with climbing hills and longer rides. Progressive functional decline.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with squatting and descending stairs. Pain on deep knee bend. Unable to perform single-leg activities. Activity limitations more pronounced during eccentric loading tasks.
C3 History: Cycling hobby with recent increase in distance and intensity. No acute injury. Gradual progressive onset. Multiple attempted conservative interventions with limited benefit.
C4 Examination: Positive J-sign patellar tracking. VMO strength weak. Patellar irritation with movement. Pain with loaded knee extension. Tight hip muscles evident.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows normal articular cartilage and no structural pathology. No meniscal involvement. Mild synovial effusion present. Femoral anteversion noted on imaging. No evidence of ligamentous injury or osteochondral lesion.
#642 Intercostal Muscle Strain Thorax
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple chest wall conditions, clues progress logically from activity limitations through palpation findings to imaging confirmation, and the clinical story is coherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old reports acute sharp chest wall pain that worsens with deep breathing. Onset was sudden, 2 days ago. Pain is focal and constant but exacerbated by movement.
C2 Activity: Unable to lie on affected side. Reaching across body and bending forward are particularly limited. Difficulty with overhead activities and sustained postures.
C3 History: Strain typically follows direct trauma, coughing, or sudden twisting movement. Heavy lifting or forceful physical exertion may precipitate the injury. Previous chest wall strain increases recurrence risk.
C4 Examination: On palpation, tenderness is elicited along the intercostal muscles. Reproducing pain with intercostal space compression confirms muscular origin. Restricted breathing pattern is evident with protective guarding.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows localized edema and small hematoma within the 5th–6th intercostal muscles. Rib imaging is normal; no fracture or pneumothorax.
#643 Atlantoaxial Instability Cervical Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Down syndrome presentation in C1 is quite specific and may overly narrow guessing; consider broadening C1 to 'connective tissue disorder with neck pain' or similar to preserve broader differential initially.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Adult with Down syndrome presents with intermittent neck pain and episodes of stiffness. Symptoms worse with certain head movements. No prior trauma reported.
C2 Activity: Cannot turn head fully to either side without discomfort. Difficulty looking down when flexing neck. Avoids rapid or large cervical movements.
C3 History: Known connective tissue laxity throughout the body. No recent trauma or falls. Two episodes of transient arm weakness over past 6 months. Never had rheumatoid arthritis screening.
C4 Examination: Sharp-Purser test reproduces transient tingling in fingers. Bilateral upper limb hyperreflexia (3+) and positive Babinski bilaterally. Lower limb strength intact 5/5. Sensation intact.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the cervical spine demonstrates increased atlantodental interval measuring greater than 5mm. Cervical myelopathy is evident with T2 signal changes in the spinal cord. The odontoid process position and cord compression severity are critical findings.
#644 Ganglion Cyst Wrist & Hand
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C4 description (transillumination, firm non-compressible mass) is somewhat contradictory (transilluminating fluids are typically compressible); clarify or simplify physical exam findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports a palpable lump on the wrist or hand that may be painless. The cyst may enlarge and shrink over time. Some patients report aching pain or functional limitation depending on cyst location and size.
C2 Activity: Patient reports difficulty with sustained gripping or repetitive wrist extension. Pain worsens when bearing weight through the hand (e.g., leaning on palm).
C3 History: Patient recalls gradual onset without clear injury, though minor wrist trauma occurred 2-3 months prior. No constitutional symptoms. No prior treatment attempted. Similar lesions have not appeared elsewhere.
C4 Examination: Palpation reveals a firm, smooth, non-compressible mass fixed to underlying structures. The mass transilluminates brightly. Wrist extension ROM is restricted compared to unaffected side. Scaphoid tenderness is absent.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates a unilocular, anechoic lesion arising directly from the scapholunate joint capsule, measuring 1.2 cm with clear cystic boundaries and no internal septations.
#645 Muscle Contusion Various
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately specific to acute trauma without naming the diagnosis, clues build logically through temporal progression and imaging confirmation, and the clinical narrative is coherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old female rugby player sustains direct blow to left thigh during match. Acute severe pain and rapid swelling over lateral thigh. Unable to continue playing.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to weight-bear on left leg. Rapid onset of extensive bruising visible within minutes. Unable to perform kicking or change of direction movements.
C3 History: Tackle occurred 2 hours ago. No previous thigh injuries or bleeding disorders. Patient takes no anticoagulants. Swelling has continued to progress since impact.
C4 Examination: Significant swelling and bruising visible over lateral thigh. Hip abduction painful. Knee ROM limited by thigh pain. Strength testing limited by guarding. Visible muscle deformation.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows muscle hematoma with fiber disruption. Vastus lateralis and biceps femoris involvement noted. Hematoma measures 6x8 cm. No fracture or compartment syndrome signs.
#646 Post-Total Hip Replacement Hip
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 essentially names the condition ('following a surgical procedure...two weeks ago' combined with severe post-operative pain), eliminating meaningful guessing and making this a recognition task rather than a diagnostic puzzle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old patient presents with severe hip pain and inability to walk independently. Onset was acute following a surgical procedure performed two weeks ago. Pain is worsened by movement and the patient requires assistive devices for mobility.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot tolerate hip flexion beyond 60–80 degrees and actively avoids adduction across the body. Attempts to cross legs or sit in low chairs trigger sharp pain and muscle guarding. Lying supine is poorly tolerated; side-lying is only comfortable on the operated side with a pillow between knees.
C3 History: Patient underwent surgery 14 days ago. Surgeon issued strict precautions: no hip flexion beyond 90 degrees, no adduction past midline, no internal rotation. Patient is currently weight-bearing as tolerated on both legs. No complications reported (no excessive swelling, no calf pain, no fever). Physiotherapy commenced 48 hours post-operatively.
C4 Examination: Hip abduction and external rotation are preserved; flexion and adduction are markedly limited and guarded. Palpation elicits tenderness over anterior hip and proximal thigh. Trendelenburg sign is present. No signs of infection (temperature normal, wound well-approximated). No evidence of hip instability or apprehension on gentle passive motion testing within precaution limits.
C5 Imaging: Anterior-posterior and lateral radiographs show femoral and acetabular components in neutral alignment with no evidence of malpositioning. Acetabular anteversion is 15–20 degrees. Femoral stem is well-fixed with no early subsidence. Hip-to-knee distance and soft tissue contours are symmetric. No periprosthetic radiolucencies or osteolytic lesions.
#647 Post-Rotator Cuff Repair Shoulder
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 explicitly states 'following a surgical procedure', which is overly directive; C2–C4 also heavily emphasize post-operative status, eliminating diagnostic uncertainty and making it guessing a procedure type rather than a diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 62-year-old woman presenting with shoulder pain and limited mobility. Symptoms began 8 weeks ago following a surgical procedure. Currently progressing through structured rehabilitation with improving function but ongoing activity limitations.
C2 Activity: Unable to initiate active shoulder movements independently. Requires assistance for arm elevation. Cannot perform overhead reaching or loaded tasks. Scapular control insufficient for functional activities.
C3 History: Mechanism of injury not clearly recalled. Gradual onset of shoulder dysfunction several months prior to surgery. No prior shoulder interventions. Comorbidities well-controlled. Patient motivated for rehabilitation.
C4 Examination: Passive ROM: forward flexion 110°, abduction 95°, external rotation 35° at side. Active ROM: forward flexion 45°, abduction 20°. Empty-can test: severe weakness, guarding. Scapular dyskinesis present. Neurovascular examination intact. Palpation: mild tenderness over surgical site.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound: rotator cuff repair in anatomical position. Supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons visualised without gap or fluid collection. Bursa normal. No signs of acute retear or significant inflammation.
#648 Compartment Syndrome Limbs
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 describes severe pain out of proportion (a classic teaching point), each clue adds critical new information (pain with passive stretch → sensory changes → pressures), and progression is pedagogically sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old athlete presents with severe leg pain following a crush injury to the lower leg during contact sport. Pain severity seems excessive relative to the visible soft tissue damage.
C2 Activity: Pain dramatically worsens with passive stretching of the ankle and foot. Active ankle movements are increasingly difficult. Pain continues to escalate despite standard pain relief measures.
C3 History: Injury occurred 4-6 hours ago from direct blunt crush during sport. Visible swelling began immediately and has worsened steadily. No fracture was identified on initial assessment. Patient reports the pain trajectory is unusually rapid for a simple contusion.
C4 Examination: Anterior leg is visibly swollen and firm/tense. Passive ankle plantarflexion elicits severe pain. Foot sensation is diminished in a stocking-like pattern. Pulses are palpable bilaterally, but the skin appears pale. No obvious fracture deformity.
C5 Imaging: Compartment pressure measurement via needle manometer shows anterior compartment pressure of 65 mmHg at rest, exceeding the critical threshold of 60 mmHg. CT imaging of the leg demonstrates swollen muscles with no fracture. MRI shows edema within the anterior compartment muscles.
#649 Meniscal Tear Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 is slightly broad but acceptable; however, C3 and C5 both mention 'medial or lateral' without specificity, making the final clue less clinching—consider specifying which meniscus or tear pattern to improve diagnostic precision.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports knee pain that comes and goes unpredictably. Sometimes the knee feels like it's catching or getting stuck, requiring a shift in position to feel normal again. Swelling develops, particularly after activities like walking or standing for extended periods.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform rapid directional changes or pivoting movements without fear of the knee giving way. Deep squatting and kneeling are intolerable. Stair descent is cautious and controlled.
C3 History: Tear may follow a specific injury with twisting mechanism or develop insidiously in degenerative knees. Previous meniscal pathology or knee injuries increase risk. Degenerative tears are common in middle-aged and older patients.
C4 Examination: Positive McMurray test with reproduction of clicking at joint line. Thessaly test positive at 20 degrees knee flexion. Significant joint effusion with positive ballottement sign. Medial or lateral joint line tenderness present depending on meniscal location.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the knee demonstrates meniscal tear in the medial or lateral meniscus with classification of tear pattern. T2 signal intensity within the meniscal substance indicates tear. Imaging localizes the tear and guides treatment decisions.
#650 Wrist Tendinopathy Wrist & Hand
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too generic (wrist pain from gripping fits dozens of conditions including carpal tunnel, arthritis, ligament sprains), and C2–C5 remain generic without specifying which tendon (ECRB, FCR, etc.), offering little progression and no clear diagnostic endpoint.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports wrist pain provoked by gripping or twisting activities. Pain is activity-related and varies depending on movement direction.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot sustain repetitive fine motor tasks (typing, assembly work, racquet sports). Pain worsens as duration increases; morning stiffness or pain-free warm-up period is absent.
C3 History: Tendinopathy develops from repetitive wrist motion or overuse activities. Poor ergonomics during work or sports activities contribute to injury. Degenerative changes may predispose middle-aged patients to tendinopathy.
C4 Examination: On examination, pain is elicited with resisted wrist movements in the plane of the affected tendon. Palpation over the tendon reproduces pain. Crepitus may be palpated with tendon movement if chronic inflammation is present.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound reveals thickened tendon with mixed echogenicity and increased vascularity on Doppler. MRI demonstrates signal changes within the tendon substance. Imaging confirms the presence and extent of tendon pathology.
#651 Radial Tunnel Syndrome Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (forearm pain + weakness in gripping), each clue adds specific anatomical/functional detail, and C5 clinches with nerve imaging findings; progression is logical and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports progressive weakness and pain in the forearm during gripping tasks. Symptoms began insidiously over weeks. Age 45, plays recreational tennis twice weekly.
C2 Activity: Activities involving sustained grip or repetitive pronation/supination significantly worsen symptoms. Patient reports pain when opening jars or using a screwdriver, worse by end of day.
C3 History: Symptoms followed a period of intensive racquet sports training 3 months ago. No prior trauma or surgery. Patient reports similar symptoms did not improve with rest alone over 6 weeks.
C4 Examination: Tenderness localized to a point 3–4 cm distal to the lateral epicondyle along the dorsal forearm. Resisted supination with elbow extended reproduces pain more reliably than resisted pronation. Finger extensor strength 4/5 on manual testing.
C5 Imaging: High-resolution ultrasound shows focal enlargement of the radial nerve at the proximal third of the forearm, consistent with compression. Nerve conduction studies demonstrate slowing of motor conduction across the proximal forearm with preserved distal segments.
#652 Acromioclavicular Joint Sprain Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C2 and C3 overlap significantly (both focus on specific movement restrictions and direct trauma), reducing the information gain between clues; C1 is also somewhat specific ('direct blow to upper shoulder') which narrows differential early.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports sharp shoulder pain following a direct blow to the upper shoulder. Pain is worse with overhead reaching and when crossing the arm across the body.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform pressing movements (e.g., push-ups, bench press) and struggles with activities requiring arm adduction across midline. Overhead reaching is severely limited.
C3 History: Injury occurred following a direct shoulder impact approximately 1 week ago. Patient reports mild visible deformity at the top of the shoulder. No significant prior shoulder history.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is elicited directly over the AC joint. Crossover adduction test reproduces pain at the AC joint. The O'Brien test may also elicit AC joint pain with positive result.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate clavicular displacement and AC joint space abnormalities based on sprain grade. Weighted views show degree of clavicular elevation if AC ligament is disrupted. MRI reveals ligament injury and associated findings.
#653 Venous Leg Ulcer Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is appropriately broad (non-healing leg wound), each subsequent clue adds chronicity, venous signs, and finally diagnostic imaging confirming venous origin; clinical progression is coherent and fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 68-year-old woman with a non-healing wound on the lower leg for several months. History of leg swelling. Denies recent trauma to the area.
C2 Activity: Swelling worsens with prolonged standing. Patient reports heaviness and aching in the leg by end of day. Compression clothing provides temporary relief.
C3 History: Swelling began gradually over 10 years. Prior leg ulcer healed 2 years ago. Current wound opened in the same medial lower leg location 3 months ago despite initial healing. No significant trauma or surgery.
C4 Examination: Medial leg ulceration with surrounding hemosiderin staining and lipodermatosclerosis. Edema pitting on pressure. Calf circumference increased bilaterally. Venous stasis signs present.
C5 Imaging: Venous duplex ultrasound demonstrates valvular incompetence in superficial and deep veins. Reflux pattern documented. Ankle-brachial index normal excluding arterial disease.
#654 Congenital Torticollis Neck
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 captures the presenting sign broadly (infant head tilt), C2–C4 progressively refine with functional limitation, birth/early infancy timing, and palpable muscle pathology, and C5 confirms with ultrasound findings; well-structured progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Infant presents with persistent head tilt to one side. Parents report the child prefers to keep head in this position and seems resistant to turning the other way. Age of presentation varies from birth to early infancy.
C2 Activity: Child demonstrates difficulty tracking objects or following caregivers' faces when turning head toward the restricted side. Passive attempts to correct the head position meet resistance, and the child returns to the preferred tilt.
C3 History: Birth history unremarkable or notable for difficult/instrumented delivery or breech presentation. Condition present from birth or noticed within first 2–3 months of life. No recent trauma. Family history not contributory.
C4 Examination: On examination, a tight mass or rope-like sternocleidomastoid muscle is palpable on the affected side. Head tilt toward the affected side with chin rotation away is evident. Facial asymmetry may be present with flattening on the affected side.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound of the sternocleidomastoid muscle demonstrates focal enlargement and echogenic appearance consistent with fibrosis. MRI may show muscle pathology and structural changes. Imaging rules out spinal or skeletal abnormalities.
#655 Olecranon Fracture Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is sufficiently broad (acute elbow pain + swelling post-trauma), C2–C4 add functional loss and clinical signs (step-off, ecchymosis), and C5 confirms with radiographic findings; clear and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports acute onset severe elbow pain and rapid swelling following a direct blow or fall. The arm is held protectively in a flexed position and any attempt to straighten it causes intense pain.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform any functional activities requiring the arm — cannot push off a chair, cannot use the arm for self-care, cannot support weight through the affected arm.
C3 History: Fracture typically follows direct blow to the olecranon or fall on flexed elbow. High energy trauma results in displaced fractures. Low energy injuries may result in undisplaced fractures.
C4 Examination: On examination, significant swelling and ecchymosis are present over the olecranon. A palpable step-off may be felt at the fracture site. Crepitus may be elicited with movement of fracture fragments.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate olecranon fracture with classification of displacement and comminution. Fracture line is visible with relationship to the joint surface assessed. Imaging guides treatment decisions regarding operative intervention.
#656 Vestibular Neuritis Vestibular
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 establishes sudden vertigo with viral prodrome, C2–C4 systematically differentiate from BPPV and Menière's via head impulse test and caloric findings, and C5 rules out CNS causes; excellent diagnostic progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.
C2 Activity: Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.
C3 History: No trauma, otalgia, or recent air travel. Patient reports mild imbalance persisting even at rest, but no positional component and no fluctuating symptoms suggestive of Menière disease.
C4 Examination: Head impulse test shows positive catch-up saccade indicating unilateral vestibular deficit. Dix-Hallpike maneuver negative excluding BPPV. Caloric testing shows reduced response in affected ear (50% unilateral paresis). Romberg test positive with falls toward affected side.
C5 Imaging: MRI brain and internal auditory canal normal with no T2 signal abnormality or enhancement of vestibulocochlear nerve. Excludes demyelinating disease and structural lesions. Findings consistent with peripheral vestibular origin.
#657 Post-Spinal Fusion Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: The diagnosis is essentially a post-operative state rather than a specific pathology, making it too broad and contextual; a physio student guessing 'spinal fusion recovery' or 'post-operative spinal patient' would be equally valid, undermining the puzzle's clinical precision.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports persistent back or neck pain with severe restriction in spinal movement. Onset was acute, following a recent surgical procedure. Neurological symptoms (if present pre-operatively) are being monitored for change.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot independently perform activities involving spinal flexion, extension, or rotation. Sitting tolerance is limited to short periods. Transfers from bed and ambulation require assistance or close supervision due to instability concerns.
C3 History: Spinal fusion was performed 2-4 weeks ago for a degenerative or structural pathology. Pre-operative symptoms included radiculopathy or claudication-type pain. Patient was immobilized post-operatively with strict motion precautions. No prior spinal surgery.
C4 Examination: Surgical incision is clean, dry, and well-approximated. Spinal ROM is severely limited in all planes (<10° in most directions). Neurological examination shows persistent lower extremity weakness or sensory changes consistent with nerve root distribution; deep tendon reflexes may be hypoactive.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative X-rays show appropriate instrumentation alignment with no obvious hardware failure or migration. Early signs of callus formation are visible at fusion levels. MRI (if performed) confirms adequate neural decompression and absence of epidural hematoma or infection.
#658 Parkinson's Disease Brain
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is appropriately broad (tremor + movement difficulty + falls), C2–C4 add functional loss and cardinal motor signs (resting tremor, rigidity, gait), and C5 confirms with imaging; solid progression for a complex neurological condition.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Older adult reports gradual onset of involuntary shaking at rest and difficulty with fine motor tasks. Movement feels slower and stiffer overall. Recent falls despite no acute injury.
C2 Activity: Cannot button clothing or use cutlery with usual dexterity. Walking requires concentration and has become slow and shuffling. Difficulty rising from chairs and turning in bed.
C3 History: Symptoms began gradually 18 months ago. No acute injury or recent medication change. One relative diagnosed with similar movement disorder in their 70s. Symptoms have progressively worsened without remission.
C4 Examination: On examination, resting tremor is evident at 4-6 Hz frequency. Rigidity is present with cogwheel quality on passive motion testing. Gait demonstrates shuffling pattern with reduced arm swing.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the brain may show midbrain atrophy. PET imaging demonstrates reduced dopamine uptake in striatum confirming diagnosis. DaTscan shows reduced dopaminergic activity in the putamen.
#659 Growing Pains Lower Limb
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C2 and C3 are largely redundant—both emphasize evening/night timing and benign nature—reducing information gain; C1 is also quite specific ('school-age child, evening/night pain') which may narrow the differential too early.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A school-age child reports intermittent pain in the lower legs. Pain typically emerges in the evening or night and resolves by morning. No history of injury or specific trauma.
C2 Activity: Pain occurs predominantly at rest in the evenings and nights. Activity during the day does not typically provoke symptoms. Child is symptom-free by morning.
C3 History: Growing pains occur during growth spurts in children typically between ages 3-10. Symptoms are benign and self-limited. Family history of growing pains is common.
C4 Examination: On examination, no objective findings are present. Lower extremity range of motion is normal. Gait is normal during examination with no limp observed.
C5 Imaging: X-rays are normal with no evidence of fracture, physeal injury, or bony lesion. Diagnosis is clinical and based on characteristic presentation; imaging is typically unnecessary unless red flags present.
#660 Trigeminal Neuralgia Face
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 captures the pain character broadly (electric, facial, brief episodes), C2–C4 add trigger specificity and examination findings ruling out sensory loss, and C5 confirms with imaging of nerve-vessel relationships; coherent and fair progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports sudden brief episodes of severe facial pain. Pain is electric shock-like in quality affecting one side of the face. Pain episodes are unpredictable but may be triggered by touch or chewing.
C2 Activity: Patient reports functional avoidance of eating, speaking, and facial grooming due to pain episodes. Difficulty maintaining oral hygiene and sustaining conversations. Quality of life significantly impacted by unpredictability of triggers.
C3 History: Trigeminal neuralgia typically presents in older adults with increased prevalence after age 50. Multiple sclerosis increases risk particularly in younger patients. Vascular compression of the trigeminal nerve root is proposed mechanism.
C4 Examination: Facial sensation intact in most cases with no dermatomal loss. Pain reproducibly triggered by light touch to perioral region, cheek, or chin. Trigeminal motor function (masseter strength, jaw deviation) normal. No other cranial nerve deficits elicited.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the brain and brainstem assesses for vascular loop compressing the trigeminal nerve. T1 weighted imaging demonstrates anatomical relationships. High-resolution CISS sequences show nerve-vessel interface.
#661 Little League Elbow Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (medial elbow pain in young athletes fits multiple overuse conditions), clues progress logically from complaint to definitive imaging, and the clinical picture is coherent and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young athlete reports progressive medial-sided elbow pain that worsens with repetitive overhead activity over several weeks. Pain is activity-dependent and improves with rest.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform throwing activities without exacerbating symptoms. Pain onset correlates with throwing frequency and intensity. Complete cessation of throwing resolves symptoms within days to weeks.
C3 History: Little League elbow occurs in young pitchers typically before skeletal maturity. Excessive throwing volume and poor mechanics increase risk. Rapid growth during adolescence predisposes to apophyseal injury.
C4 Examination: On examination, tenderness is elicited over the medial epicondyle. Valgus stress testing reproduces medial-sided elbow pain. Forearm pronation and wrist flexion against resistance may reproduce symptoms.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs show medial epicondyle apophysitis with fragmentation or widening of the apophysis. MRI demonstrates edema and inflammatory changes at the medial epicondyle. Advanced imaging confirms extent of injury.
#662 Hip Fracture Hip
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is somewhat specific (elderly patient + fall + acute severe pain + unable to weight bear narrows to fracture/dislocation significantly); consider broadening to 'acute hip pain after fall' without the weight-bearing detail, or accept as slightly tight but still playable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An elderly patient reports acute severe pain in the hip region following a fall from standing height. Unable to weight bear.
C2 Activity: Inability to internally rotate the hip. Pain is severely reproduced by any attempt to move the leg, particularly flexion and external rotation.
C3 History: Osteoporosis documented on prior DEXA scan. Patient lives alone and has had multiple falls over the past 18 months. No significant trauma history otherwise.
C4 Examination: On examination, the hip is often held in external rotation and flexion. Tenderness over the hip and groin is severe. Crepitus may be palpated with leg movement.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs confirm femoral neck fracture with posterior displacement. CT shows comminution of the intertrochanteric region. No associated pelvic injury.
#663 Endometriosis Pelvis
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Endometriosis is outside the scope of physiotherapy diagnosis games—it is a medical/gynecological condition requiring surgical confirmation and pharmaceutical management; Physiodle should focus on musculoskeletal, neurological, or rehabilitation diagnoses that physiotherapists independently assess and treat.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A woman in her 30s reports severe pelvic pain during menstruation that has worsened over several years. Pain severity is disproportionate to findings on routine examination.
C2 Activity: Patient reports functional impairment is primarily cyclical; symptoms ease between menses. Dyspareunia is a significant functional complaint affecting partner relationships.
C3 History: Reproductive-age woman with progressive symptoms over years. Multiple prior treatments (oral contraceptives, NSAIDs) provided incomplete relief. Subfertility noted.
C4 Examination: On pelvic examination, tenderness is elicited with palpation of posterior cul-de-sac and uterine ligaments. Pelvic floor muscle tension and hypertonicity are common. Fixed uterine retroversion may be noted.
C5 Imaging: Transvaginal ultrasound demonstrates cystic ovarian lesions with internal debris and nodular thickening of posterior pelvic peritoneum. MRI confirms multifocal pelvic involvement. Diagnostic laparoscopy visualizes and biopsies lesions, confirming diagnosis.
#664 Quadriceps Tendinopathy Thigh
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (anterior knee pain in athlete with training volume change fits multiple conditions), clues add progressive specificity without repetition, and Clue 5 imaging clinches the diagnosis clearly.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old recreational athlete reports anterior knee pain for 6 weeks that worsens with explosive movements and is aggravated by training volume increases. Pain is localized around the knee but character and specific location not yet defined.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform jumping or deceleration-based movements without sharp anterior knee pain. Pain-free with walking and static activities. Symptoms reproducible and consistent with sport participation but resolve completely with 48-hour rest. No night pain or swelling that persists overnight.
C3 History: Gradual onset correlating with increased training volume (volleyball 4x per week). Repetitive jumping and landing mechanics in sport. No acute traumatic event. History of patellar tendon soreness 2 years ago. Adequate flexibility initially but recently decreased.
C4 Examination: Palpable tenderness directly over proximal patellar tendon at superior patellar pole. Quadriceps contraction against resistance elicits pain. Single-leg squat shows valgus knee position (femoral internal rotation) suggesting biomechanical dysfunction. Knee flexion 0-120 degrees pain-free but 120-140 degrees aggravating. Palpable swelling at insertion.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic thickening of proximal quadriceps tendon (8mm versus normal 5-6mm) with loss of fibrillar echotexture. Increased Doppler signal indicating inflammation and neovascularity. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration without full-thickness tear. T2 signal abnormality within proximal third of tendon.
#665 Cancer Rehabilitation General
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clues are vague and non-specific—fatigue, dyspnea, deconditioning, and cardiac/pulmonary changes fit dozens of post-illness conditions without narrowing meaningfully; Clue 3 hides the diagnosis ('major medical event' and 'medical intervention') rather than revealing it progressively, and 'cancer rehabilitation' is too broad a category, not a diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports persistent fatigue and difficulty with sustained physical activities. Reports feeling significantly weaker than before, with reduced tolerance for exertion.
C2 Activity: Patient reports dyspnea and palpitations on minimal exertion. Cardiac monitoring recommended during activity. Able to walk only short distances before breathlessness forces cessation.
C3 History: Deconditioning began 3-6 months ago following a major medical event. Patient reports prior good baseline fitness. Symptom onset coincided with period of reduced activity and medical intervention.
C4 Examination: Resting heart rate elevated (>100 bpm). Significant effort dyspnea on minimal activity. Peripheral muscle wasting evident on inspection. Breath sounds diminished at lung bases bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray shows baseline changes consistent with prior radiation (if thoracic field involved); may show ground-glass opacities suggesting pulmonary toxicity. Echocardiogram may reveal mild ventricular dysfunction or reduced ejection fraction post-cardiotoxic chemotherapy.
#666 Jones Fracture Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately fits lateral midfoot pain in runners (stress fracture, cuboid syndrome, lateral ankle sprain all possible), clues add detail progressively without overlap, and Clue 5 imaging is definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A runner reports lateral midfoot pain with acute onset during a pushing-off movement. Pain is sharp and localized to the lateral foot, worsened by weight bearing and running.
C2 Activity: Running and rapid directional changes provoke pain. Single-leg stance and explosive push-off activities are not tolerated. Swelling develops in the lateral midfoot with prolonged activity.
C3 History: Six-week history of progressive lateral midfoot pain in a distance runner. Recent increase in training volume. Alternatively, acute onset after inversion injury during sport. Area notably slow to improve with conservative rest and ice.
C4 Examination: Pinpoint tenderness at the lateral aspect of the midfoot, just proximal to the fifth metatarsal base. Pain reproduced by single-leg stance and hopping. Lateral foot swelling present. Inability to bear weight on lateral border of foot.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate transverse fracture at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal. CT imaging assesses fracture displacement and healing. Advanced imaging guides timing of weight bearing progression.
#667 Pectoralis Minor Strain Chest
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (anterior chest/shoulder pain fits multiple upper extremity and chest wall conditions), subsequent clues add specificity through activity and palpation findings, and imaging confirms the diagnosis clearly.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports anterior chest and shoulder pain. Onset gradual over 2-3 weeks. Aggravated by upper extremity activities.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to perform overhead pressing at gym. Throwing activities impossible. Horizontal adduction movements consistently reproduce pain. Symptoms worse with repetitive scapular protraction-based activities.
C3 History: Patient is competitive swimmer training 5 days per week with recent increase in volume. Acute on chronic presentation — underlying tight pectoralis minor noted on previous physio assessment 6 months ago. No direct trauma. Pain started during training block.
C4 Examination: Palpable tenderness and spasm in pectoralis minor in anterior axilla. Resisted horizontal adduction and scapular protraction reproduce sharp pain. Scapular positioning asymmetrical — anteriorly tilted on affected side. No neurological signs. Shoulder ROM otherwise preserved.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates hyperechoic edema within pectoralis minor muscle belly with disruption of normal fibre echotexture. No full-thickness tear. MRI T2 signal changes localised to medial pectoralis minor, consistent with acute muscle strain. Anterior shoulder structures otherwise unremarkable.
#668 Pneumonia Lungs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Pneumonia is a medical diagnosis outside physiotherapy scope; Clue 1 essentially names the diagnosis (productive cough, fever, dyspnea in elderly COPD patient is highly specific to pneumonia/respiratory infection), and Clue 2 repeats Clue 1 nearly verbatim, breaking the progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease presents with a 5-day history of productive cough with purulent sputum, fever, and dyspnea. The patient reports worsening respiratory symptoms with difficulty sleeping and fatigue.
C2 Activity: A 72-year-old with a chronic lung condition presents with a 5-day history of productive cough, fever, and worsening dyspnea. Difficulty sleeping and fatigue reported.
C3 History: Dyspnea is now limiting even minimal exertion such as walking short distances or standing. Orthopnea is present, affecting sleep quality. Overall functional decline is rapid.
C4 Examination: On examination, respiratory rate is 28 breaths per minute with oxygen saturation 88% on room air. Auscultation reveals consolidative crackles and bronchial breath sounds in the right lower lobe. Fremitus is increased over affected area. Vital signs show fever of 38.5°C and tachycardia.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray demonstrates a segmental consolidation in the right lower lobe with air bronchograms, consistent with bacterial pneumonia. Blood cultures are positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae. Sputum culture confirms the same organism.
#669 Vibration White Finger Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (episodic finger blanching and color changes fit Raynaud's, other vascular conditions), clues add occupational and examination detail progressively, and Clue 5 imaging narrows to the occupational vascular disorder clearly.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Worker reports episodic finger blanching and color changes triggered by cold or vibration exposure. Fingers turn white, then red and painful as they rewarm. Numbness and tingling accompany episodes.
C2 Activity: Symptoms worsen with prolonged grip or repetitive hand tasks. Patient avoids cold environments due to severe finger reactions. Occupational exposure is unavoidable given job demands.
C3 History: Vibration white finger develops from chronic exposure to vibrating power tools. Jackhammers, chainsaws, and pneumatic tools are common culprits. Duration and intensity of tool exposure correlate with symptom severity.
C4 Examination: Affected fingers show poor color return after cold provocation test. Grip strength reduced in affected hand. Two-point discrimination diminished in fingertips. Pulse oximetry shows reduced oxygen saturation in affected digits compared to unaffected fingers.
C5 Imaging: Nailfold capillaroscopy demonstrates abnormal capillary patterns characteristic of vascular dysfunction. Thermography shows abnormal temperature distribution in affected fingers. Doppler ultrasound assesses digital artery flow and perfusion.
#670 Multiple Sclerosis CNS
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is somewhat specific (progressive bilateral leg weakness + blurred vision + recent viral trigger + fatigue in young woman strongly suggests MS or CNS demyelination rather than broader musculoskeletal/neuro conditions), though still playable; consider softening the combination or accepting this as a slightly tighter opening clue.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 34-year-old woman with 4 weeks of progressive bilateral leg weakness and recent blurred vision in right eye. Reports fatigue and difficulty concentrating at work. Symptoms began 2 months after a viral illness-like episode.
C2 Activity: Cannot climb stairs due to leg weakness. Difficulty with balance and coordination. Numbness in feet and lower legs. Increased bladder urgency. Pain noted when moving eyes.
C3 History: Multiple prior episodes of unexplained neurological symptoms that resolved and recurred. Family history of autoimmune conditions. Current presentation began 2 weeks after upper respiratory infection. Symptoms have been waxing and waning since onset.
C4 Examination: Eye examination shows reduced visual acuity 20/40 right eye with afferent pupillary defect. Visual field testing shows central scotoma on right. Lower extremity strength 4/5 with increased tone and hyperreflexia. Positive Babinski signs bilaterally. Gait shows spasticity and ataxia. Finger-to-nose dysmetria present.
C5 Imaging: MRI brain shows multiple T2 hyperintensities in periventricular distribution with some showing gadolinium enhancement. Spinal cord MRI reveals focal T2 hyperintense lesion at cervical level. CSF oligoclonal bands present. Visual evoked potentials prolonged bilaterally.
#671 Deep Vein Thrombosis Lower Limb
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (sudden leg swelling/pain fits many conditions), each subsequent clue adds genuine clinical progression, and Clue 5 is definitive; a fair physio puzzle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports sudden-onset unilateral leg swelling and calf pain. Onset occurred over hours to 1–2 days. Pain is constant and aching in character.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot walk normally due to pain and swelling. Lying flat with leg elevated temporarily reduces discomfort. No relief with rest alone; swelling persists.
C3 History: Risk factors include recent surgery, immobilization, or prolonged travel. Malignancy, hypercoagulable states, and previous DVT increase risk. Oral contraceptive use and pregnancy are important risk factors in women.
C4 Examination: Calf circumference on affected side is 3+ cm greater than opposite side. Calf is warm and mildly erythematous. Passive dorsiflexion of ankle reproduces calf pain. No significant asymmetry in ankle or foot pulses bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Venous duplex ultrasound demonstrates absence of venous compressibility and thrombus visualization. Compression ultrasound shows no flow in the involved vein. CT or MR venography confirms diagnosis and extent of thrombosis.
#672 Runner's Nipple Chest
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (chest wall pain in runners), clues progress logically from symptom to friction mechanism to clinical findings, and the diagnosis is clinched by imaging/clinical findings.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A runner reports localized chest wall pain that occurs during or after running sessions. Pain is described as superficial burning or irritation.
C2 Activity: Pain is specifically aggravated by prolonged running distance and improves with rest. Symptoms worsen in warm weather and with worn or poorly fitting athletic tops.
C3 History: Runner's nipple occurs from repetitive friction between clothing and chest wall. Male runners are more commonly affected than females. Increased running volume and intensity increase symptom risk.
C4 Examination: On examination, localized erythema and skin breakdown at the nipple are evident. Range of motion and shoulder function are normal. No systemic signs of infection. Reproduction of symptoms occurs when clothing is moved across the affected area.
C5 Imaging: Imaging is not routinely performed. If obtained, plain radiographs and ultrasound are normal with no underlying bony or soft tissue pathology. Diagnosis is confirmed clinically by history of friction exposure, visible signs of superficial skin damage at the nipple, and resolution with protective measures.
#673 Posterior Ankle Impingement Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits multiple ankle conditions, each clue reveals new information (activity limitation → training context → specific movement reproduction → imaging confirmation), and progression is logical.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 26-year-old ballet dancer presents with gradually worsening posterior ankle pain over recent weeks. Pain is activity-related and limits training.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform pointe work or sustained plantarflexion. Pain worsens with intensity of rehearsal and is preventing return to full training load.
C3 History: Recent increase in training volume over the past 6 weeks. No prior ankle injury or imaging. Previous episodes of ankle discomfort resolved with rest.
C4 Examination: Palpable tenderness in posterior ankle compartment. Pain with plantarflexion and inversion combined movement. Resisted plantarflexion reproduces pain. Full ankle ROM available.
C5 Imaging: Ankle MRI shows impingement of posterior structures (flexor hallucis longus and soft tissues) with plantarflexion. Possible posterior talus talar chondral lesion. Achilles intact.
#674 Lumbar Radiculopathy Lumbar Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 3 is somewhat redundant with Clue 1 (both describe the radiating pain nature); Clues 4-5 are strong but earlier clues could better distinguish radiculopathy from other mechanical back pain.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports pain radiating from the low back down one leg. Numbness and tingling accompany the pain in a dermatomal distribution. Functional limitation occurs with bending and walking activities.
C2 Activity: Patient reports symptoms are worse in the morning and after prolonged sitting. Standing and walking initially worsen pain but symptoms may ease with continued walking. Certain postures like slumped sitting dramatically increase leg pain.
C3 History: Radiculopathy may follow a specific lifting injury or develop insidiously from degenerative disc disease. Disc herniation is the most common mechanical cause. Spinal stenosis and spondylolisthesis contribute in older patients.
C4 Examination: Straight leg raise test is positive with pain reproduction below the knee at 45 degrees. Calf and foot dorsiflexion weakness noted on manual muscle testing. Ankle reflex diminished compared to opposite side. Palpation over the affected nerve root pathway reproduces radiating symptoms.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates posterolateral disc herniation at L5-S1 with compression and displacement of the traversing nerve root. T2-weighted images show high signal intensity in the affected dermatomal distribution on DTI, consistent with nerve root edema.
#675 Ankle Impingement Syndrome Ankle & Foot
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clues are poorly sequenced: Clue 5 contains examination findings (tenderness, squeeze test, ROM changes) that should be in Clue 4, while Clue 4 lists history; the logical flow is broken and information placement is incoherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports anterior ankle pain with plantarflexion and inversion movements. Pain is localized to the anterior ankle. Functional limitation occurs with movements that pinch structures in the anterior ankle.
C2 Activity: A 45-year-old recreational footballer reports anterior ankle pain that worsens with certain movements. Onset was gradual over 6 months. Pain is sharp and localized to the front of the ankle.
C3 History: Patient cannot walk comfortably on uneven terrain or descend stairs without pain. Activities requiring sustained ankle plantarflexion (e.g., climbing stairs, prolonged standing on toes) are particularly problematic.
C4 Examination: Patient has a history of ankle sprains over the past 5 years. Symptoms began after a significant inversion injury 18 months ago that was poorly rehabilitated. No significant improvement with conservative care. Clinical suspicion for anterior ankle impingement based on presentation.
C5 Imaging: Anterior ankle tenderness localised to anterolateral gutter. Plantarflexion-inversion combined movement (squeeze test) reproduces exact pain. Passive dorsiflexion and eversion relieve discomfort. Anterior ankle swelling present. No significant ligamentous laxity on stress testing.
#676 Guyon's Canal Syndrome Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (hand weakness/weakness), clues progress from symptoms to mechanism to exam findings to imaging confirmation, and each adds new diagnostic information.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports progressive weakness in hand grip and pinch strength. Fine motor control is affected. Pain or tingling may occur in the medial (ulnar) side of the hand.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with sustained gripping, pinching, and repetitive hand use. Weakness becomes more apparent with fine motor tasks.
C3 History: History of repetitive hand use, vibration exposure, or occupational trauma. Recent onset. No prior wrist or hand surgery. No systemic neurological disease.
C4 Examination: On examination, hypothenar muscle weakness and atrophy are evident. Froment sign is positive indicating thumb interphalangeal flexion with key pinch. Sensation is intact in the medial palm as the dorsal sensory branch is spared.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the wrist demonstrates mass lesion or cyst compressing the ulnar nerve in Guyon's canal. Ultrasound visualizes nerve compression and causative lesion. Nerve conduction studies confirm ulnar nerve motor involvement distal to the wrist.
#677 Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction Grade 2 Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (foot pain/deformity), clues logically progress through functional limitation, timeline, clinical examination (arch collapse/single-leg rise), and imaging confirmation; fair difficulty for physios.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports progressive foot deformity with pain on the inner side of the ankle. Symptoms worsen with prolonged weight-bearing activities. Onset was gradual over months.
C2 Activity: Patient reports difficulty with high-impact activities and single-leg stance. Cannot run or jump. Notices progressive difficulty pushing off with the inner forefoot.
C3 History: Gradual onset over 6-12 months in a patient with a history of pes planus or prior ankle injury. No acute trauma. Progressive nature despite conservative treatment. Associated with activities involving prolonged standing or walking on uneven surfaces.
C4 Examination: On examination, visible flatfoot deformity is evident with loss of medial arch height. Medial ankle tenderness and swelling are present. The single leg heel rise test demonstrates weakness or inability to rise on the medial forefoot.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates thickened and abnormal tibialis posterior tendon. Tendon signal changes indicate inflammation and partial tears. The degree of arch collapse and talonavicular subluxation is assessed on imaging.
#678 Facet Joint Syndrome Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (localized back pain with radicular features fits multiple conditions), clues progress through movement patterns and examination findings (extension-biased symptoms, negative SLR), and imaging clinches facet pathology.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old patient reports localized lower back pain that worsens with certain movements and prolonged positions. Pain occasionally radiates into the buttock or upper thigh. Symptoms have been present intermittently for several months, with recent worsening.
C2 Activity: Patient reports significant difficulty with prolonged standing and walking; symptoms improve when lying down. Bending forward provides temporary relief, but the patient cannot maintain this position for extended periods.
C3 History: Patient has a 10-year history of intermittent back pain with gradual progression. Works in manual labor involving frequent bending and lifting. Recent imaging noted degenerative changes in the lumbar spine. No significant trauma or acute injury recalled.
C4 Examination: On examination: full lumbar flexion is pain-free with good ROM; extension is limited and reproduces pain. Ipsilateral side-bending and rotation toward the affected side increase symptoms. Neurological examination is intact; no lower limb weakness or sensory changes. Straight leg raise is negative.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates facet joint hypertrophy and osteoarthritic changes. Signal changes indicate inflammation within the joint. Degree of facet-mediated stenosis is assessed if neurological symptoms are present.
#679 Vasculitis Blood Vessels
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This puzzle is not appropriate for Physiodle—vasculitis is a systemic medical/rheumatological condition outside physiotherapy scope of practice and diagnosis; it requires blood work and specialist involvement, not clinical reasoning within physiotherapy expertise.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports fever, joint and muscle pain, and a rash. Symptoms began insidiously 2 weeks ago and have worsened. Some fatigue and malaise noted.
C2 Activity: Patient is unable to walk long distances due to joint and leg pain. Unable to perform upper limb overhead tasks. Generalized weakness limits participation in daily activities and work.
C3 History: Recent respiratory illness 3–4 weeks prior to onset. No significant past medical history or autoimmune disease. No recent travel or sick contacts. No new medications. Symptoms progressing despite rest and analgesia.
C4 Examination: Palpable, non-blanching purpura on lower legs and buttocks. Ankle and knee swelling with tenderness. Mild abdominal tenderness on palpation. No neurological deficits. HR elevated at 96 bpm; temperature 37.8°C.
C5 Imaging: Laboratory findings including elevated ESR and CRP indicate systemic inflammation. Antigen testing for ANCA (ANCA-associated vasculitis) or ANA may be positive. Imaging and tissue biopsy confirm the diagnosis and extent of organ involvement.
#680 Hip Labral Tear Hip
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 3 is somewhat redundant with Clue 1 (both describe insidious onset and mechanism); the history/mechanism clue could be more specific to earlier differentiate from other hip pathologies like OA or bursitis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports deep groin pain that started insidiously several months ago. Pain is worse with certain movements and activities but improves with rest. Occasionally feels a catching sensation in the hip.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform cutting and pivoting movements required for sports. Pain is reproducible when hip is positioned in deep flexion combined with internal rotation. Single-leg weight-bearing activities cause discomfort.
C3 History: Labral tear may follow a specific trauma or develop insidiously from repetitive microtrauma. Hip impingement and femoroacetabular impingement increase tear risk. Athletes in rotation-based sports have increased incidence.
C4 Examination: FABER test, anterior impingement test (90/90 position), and passive internal rotation in hip flexion reliably reproduce anterior groin pain. Hip abduction and external rotation ROM notably reduced. Log roll test and FADIR test exacerbate symptoms.
C5 Imaging: MRI arthrography demonstrates labral tear with visualization of contrast leaking through the tear. MRI shows the tear morphology and associated findings. Imaging guides treatment decisions regarding surgical intervention.
#681 De Quervain's Tenosynovitis Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (radial wrist pain in athlete), each subsequent clue adds specific progressive detail, and Clue 5 is definitive; fair and engaging for a physio student.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old squash player presents with radial wrist pain affecting athletic performance. Pain on thumb side of wrist with gripping racquet. Symptoms over 3 weeks.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with gripping and racquet handling in sport. Pain worsens with forceful thumb pinch and wrist deviation. Functional limitation in sport and work activities.
C3 History: Recent increase in squash training frequency with 4 matches per week. Repetitive gripping and dorsiflexion movements during sport.
C4 Examination: Radial-sided wrist pain reproduced with Finkelstein test. Thumb abduction strength testing painful. Swelling visible over radial styloid region. Pain with thumb opposition against resistance.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates first dorsal compartment tenosynovitis with fluid around APL and EPB tendons. Tendons thickened with hypoechoic appearance. No subluxation evident.
#682 Cervical Radiculopathy Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 covers multiple cervical conditions, progression is logical from general complaint through postural triggers to dermatomal findings to imaging correlation, making it pedagogically sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports neck pain with radiating pain down the arm. Numbness and tingling in the hand. Difficulty gripping and fine motor tasks.
C2 Activity: Patient reports symptoms are worse with neck extension and turning the head away from the affected side. Lying flat with neck support provides relief. Symptoms are intermittent, triggered by prolonged desk work and poor posture.
C3 History: Patient is a 52-year-old with a 4-week history of gradual symptom onset. Symptoms began after increased computer work and prolonged overhead activities. Prior episodes of neck stiffness, now worsening. No significant trauma or acute onset.
C4 Examination: On examination, upper limb strength testing reveals dermatomal weakness corresponding to nerve root involved. Sensory testing demonstrates dermatomal sensory loss. Spurling test reproduces the radiating arm pain.
C5 Imaging: MRI cervical spine shows left-sided disc herniation at C6-C7 with compression of the C7 nerve root in the neural foramen. No evidence of myelopathy. Imaging correlates with dermatomal weakness and sensory loss on examination.
#683 Polymyalgia Rheumatica Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 4 mentions temporal artery tenderness which is giant cell arteritis overlap, not pure PMR; softening this clue or separating the two conditions would improve clarity.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old woman presents with symmetrical muscle pain and stiffness in shoulders and hips. Pain and morning stiffness worse upon waking, gradually improving with activity.
C2 Activity: Significant functional limitation with shoulder and hip mobility. Difficulty getting out of bed, dressing, and lifting arms. Morning stiffness lasting 1+ hours.
C3 History: Sudden onset 2 weeks ago with symmetrical pattern. No prior rheumatologic history. Associated low-grade fever and malaise. Rapid functional decline.
C4 Examination: Painful shoulder abduction and hip flexion bilaterally. Generalized tenderness over shoulder girdle and hip musculature. Temporal arteries prominent and tender to palpation bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: ESR markedly elevated at 92 mm/hr with CRP 8.5 mg/dL. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies negative. No evidence of structural damage on shoulder/hip ultrasound.
#684 Chronic Exertional Compartment Syndrome Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (exercise-induced shin pain), clues build logically from complaint to activity reproducibility to normal resting exam to post-exercise compartment pressures; strong educational progression.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A runner reports bilateral anterior shin pain that occurs predictably during running activity. Pain develops after consistent running distance and resolves completely with rest. The condition recurs with return to running at similar intensity.
C2 Activity: Pain is reproducible and occurs during running or intense lower leg activity. The pain is bilateral and symmetric in the anterior tibial compartments. Complete pain relief occurs within 15-30 minutes of ceasing activity.
C3 History: Patient unable to sustain running beyond 20-30 minutes despite normal walking tolerance. Pain is reproducible and symmetric bilaterally. Able to perform daily activities without symptoms.
C4 Examination: Pre-exercise examination may be normal with no resting compartment tenderness. Post-exercise examination reveals tight and tender anterior compartments. Compartment pressure measurement at rest is normal but elevated post-exercise.
C5 Imaging: Compartment pressure measurement obtained immediately post-exercise (within 1-5 minutes) demonstrates elevated pressures in anterior compartment. Post-exercise pressure exceeds 30 mmHg with slow return to normal. Serial measurements during standardized exercise protocol establish diagnosis.
#685 Pressure Injury Stage 1 Skin
✗ REWORK
