Verdict: Clue 1 essentially names the diagnosis ('discolored skin that doesn't blanch') which is the defining feature of Stage 1 pressure injury, leaving only 1-2 possible guesses rather than a realistic differential.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 72-year-old man hospitalized following stroke, now confined to bed due to weakness. Reports caregiver noticed discolored skin over lower back that doesn't blanch when pressed.
C2 Activity: Cannot independently change positions or weight-shift. Spends extended periods in supine and side-lying positions without repositioning due to hemiplegia.
C3 History: Right-sided hemiplegia since stroke 3 weeks ago. Currently in acute care hospital. Nursing staff notes skin changes appeared approximately 5 days ago over area of frequent contact with bed.
C4 Examination: On palpation: area is warm and slightly indurated. Surrounding skin normal temperature and appearance. Capillary refill of erythematous area does not blanch with fingertip pressure. No maceration, blistering, or open wounds.
C5 Imaging: Wound care assessment documents Stage 1 pressure injury by NPUAP staging. No subcutaneous involvement or tissue necrosis evident on clinical assessment. High-risk anatomical location with ongoing pressure exposure.
#686 Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (young female with arthralgia and fatigue), clues progress from symptom pattern through photosensitivity and rash to serology, and Clue 5 is definitive; playable and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Young female with insidious onset of joint pain and significant fatigue over several weeks. Symptoms wax and wane unpredictably.
C2 Activity: Photosensitive skin rash develops or worsens with sun exposure. Morning joint stiffness exceeds one hour.
C3 History: Condition characterized by unpredictable flares and remissions. Recent initiation of hormonal contraception preceded symptom onset.
C4 Examination: On examination, the characteristic malar or butterfly rash is visible across the cheeks and nose. Symmetric polyarthritis is evident with swelling of multiple joints. Oral ulcers may be present on palate or buccal mucosa.
C5 Imaging: Serology testing reveals positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) with high titers. Anti-double stranded DNA antibody and anti-Smith antibody are positive indicating specific SLE diagnosis. Complement levels (C3, C4) are low reflecting disease activity.
#687 Work-Related Upper Limb Disorder Upper Limb
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 4 is very specific (medial epicondyle tenderness, Phalen positive) which narrows the differential prematurely; clues 2–4 could be more generic to maintain uncertainty through to Clue 5.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A worker reports progressive pain in the upper extremity that worsens throughout the workday. Symptoms interfere with job performance and daily tasks.
C2 Activity: Symptoms are reproducible with specific repetitive hand and wrist motions required by the job. Sustained gripping or typing postures particularly provoke pain. Overnight rest provides temporary relief but symptoms recur within hours of returning to work.
C3 History: Symptoms began insidiously 6–8 weeks ago after increased workload. Patient has no prior history of upper limb injury. Works in assembly line role involving repetitive forceful pinching and gripping for 8 hours daily without job rotation. No recent change in technique or equipment.
C4 Examination: Palpation reveals point tenderness over the flexor carpi radialis origin at the medial epicondyle and along the flexor digitorum tendons in the forearm. Grip strength reduced by 30% compared to non-dominant side. Phalen test reproduction of pain; Tinel test negative.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates diffuse hypoechoic thickening of the flexor carpi radialis tendon at the elbow with peritendinous edema. No nerve compression or structural tear. Findings consistent with insertional tendinopathy.
#688 Tibial Plateau Fracture Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute severe knee pain post-trauma), clues build logically from mechanism through clinical signs to imaging confirmation, and progression is clear and fair.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports acute severe knee pain and rapid swelling after a high-impact injury. Unable to bear weight on the affected leg.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot stand or walk. Knee is held in slight flexion and resists any attempt at straightening. Swelling is tense and effusion is clinically apparent.
C3 History: The injury occurred during a motor vehicle accident at highway speeds. Patient recalls immediate giving way and inability to weight bear. No prior knee injury or surgery.
C4 Examination: Severe effusion present with ballottement sign. Anterior drawer and Lachman tests are positive suggesting ACL involvement. Varus/valgus stress testing elicits pain but laxity is difficult to assess due to guarding. Crepitus palpable over anterolateral tibial plateau.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate tibial plateau fracture with depression and comminution severity. CT imaging shows three-dimensional fracture anatomy guiding surgical planning. Associated ligamentous and meniscal injuries are identified.
#689 Marfan Syndrome Whole Body
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 mentions tall stature and family history of sudden cardiac death which is suggestive but not definitive, and subsequent clues add objective skeletal and cardiovascular findings that clinch the diagnosis fairly.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A young adult reports progressive tall stature, joint pain with instability, and chest discomfort with exertion. Family members have similar features and cardiac concerns.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform overhead activities or heavy lifting without chest pain. Avoids contact sports and running due to medical guidance. Progressive limitation in endurance over past 2 years.
C3 History: Mother and maternal grandmother both have tall stature and joint flexibility issues. Maternal grandfather died suddenly in his 40s from cardiac event. Patient's symptoms began in early adolescence, progressing through teenage years.
C4 Examination: Positive thumb sign and wrist sign. Arm span-to-height ratio >1.05. Pectus carinatum with narrowed anterior-posterior chest diameter. Generalized hypermobility on Beighton score (≥6/9). Cardiac auscultation reveals early diastolic murmur.
C5 Imaging: Echocardiography shows aortic root diameter of 5.2 cm (z-score >3) with evidence of aortic regurgitation. Pectus deformity confirmed on chest X-ray. Lens subluxation visualised on slit-lamp examination. Genetic testing pending.
#690 Bronchiectasis Lungs
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 5 mentions 'bronchus to artery ratio >1' which is a highly specific radiological sign; rewording to describe general bronchial dilation and wall thickening would preserve diagnostic uncertainty slightly longer.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Chronic productive cough with fever and recurrent chest infections. Progressive shortness of breath on exertion over months to years.
C2 Activity: Patient requires frequent postural drainage and manual therapy to mobilise secretions. Lying flat worsens cough; benefits from semi-upright positioning.
C3 History: History of severe respiratory infection (e.g., measles, whooping cough, tuberculosis) or recurrent childhood pneumonias. Alternatively, immunodeficiency disorder or congenital ciliary dysfunction suspected. Symptoms worsened over years despite antibiotic courses.
C4 Examination: Coarse bibasal crackles that clear partially with coughing. Clubbing of fingers and toes. Hyperinflation visible on chest wall inspection. Accessory muscle use during conversation.
C5 Imaging: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates bronchial dilation with bronchus to artery ratio greater than 1. Bronchial wall thickening and traction bronchiectasis are visible. Extent and distribution of bronchiectasis are documented.
#691 Knee Osteoarthritis Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Solid progression from broad mechanical knee pain to specific imaging findings; C1 fits multiple conditions, each clue adds distinct information, and C5 clinches the diagnosis clearly.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports chronic knee pain with morning stiffness that improves with activity. Pain worsens with prolonged standing and walking. Functional limitation occurs with stairs and uneven ground.
C2 Activity: Difficulty with single-leg stance and step-downs. Pain described as mechanical, worse mid-day after repetitive loading. Morning pain resolves within 20-30 minutes of movement.
C3 History: Age 55+. History of ACL injury 10 years prior, managed conservatively. BMI 28. Works in construction with 20+ years of repetitive squatting. Symptoms began insidiously 2 years ago, progressive. Prior physiotherapy provided temporary relief only.
C4 Examination: On examination, knee effusion may be present with positive ballottement test. Crepitus is palpated with knee motion. Range of motion is limited with pain at end-range flexion and extension.
C5 Imaging: Weight-bearing radiographs (AP, lateral, patellofemoral views) show focal joint space narrowing medial compartment, marginal osteophytes at femoral condyles, and subchondral sclerosis. Severity Kellgren-Lawrence grade 2-3. No loose bodies or cruciate laxity.
#692 Breast Cancer Lymphedema Upper Limb
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C3 explicitly states 'lymphedema develops from lymph node removal or radiation during breast cancer treatment,' essentially naming the diagnosis before imaging, collapsing diagnostic uncertainty.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports progressive arm swelling and sensation of heaviness over several months. The affected limb feels tight and uncomfortable, limiting functional use.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot tolerate prolonged arm dependency or sustained gripping activities. Symptoms worsen with heat exposure and repetitive upper limb tasks.
C3 History: Lymphedema develops from lymph node removal or radiation during breast cancer treatment. Onset may be immediate or develop years post-treatment. Progressive swelling occurs without appropriate management.
C4 Examination: Significant limb circumference asymmetry documented. Stemmer sign positive. Tissue consistency is firm and non-pitting with absence of normal skin folds.
C5 Imaging: Lymphoscintigraphy shows dermal backflow pattern with delayed transit time and asymmetric lymph node uptake, confirming lymphatic obstruction.
#693 Falls in the Elderly Systemic
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Overly broad and non-specific (falls have hundreds of causes); C1–C4 list generic fall risk factors without narrowing to a single diagnosis, making this a syndrome summary rather than a diagnostic puzzle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: An 82-year-old woman presents with 2 falls from standing over the past month. Reports dizziness before each fall. Significantly reduced confidence with walking affecting daily activities and independence.
C2 Activity: Unsteady gait with reduced walking speed (0.5 m/sec). Balance impaired requiring contact guard for safety. Reports lightheadedness with position changes. Fear of falling evident with cautious movement patterns and activity avoidance.
C3 History: Multiple medical comorbidities: treated hypertension, mild cognitive impairment, cataracts affecting vision. Lives alone in cluttered home environment. Recent medication changes started 6 weeks prior. Vitamin D levels low.
C4 Examination: Timed up and go test 28 seconds (abnormal >12 seconds). Single leg stance 3 seconds each leg (abnormal <10 seconds). Berg Balance Scale score 38/56 (high fall risk <45). Orthostatic vital signs positive (systolic BP drop 20 mmHg on standing). Gait assessment shows reduced stride length, shuffle pattern, and reduced arm swing.
C5 Imaging: Brain MRI normal excluding acute stroke or structural lesion. Vestibular assessment normal. DEXA scan shows T-score -2.9 (osteoporosis). Comprehensive medication reconciliation reveals antihypertensive regimen contributing to orthostatic hypotension. Environmental hazards identified: throw rugs, inadequate lighting, poor handrails.
#694 Pes Anserine Bursitis Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 presents medial knee pain (fits multiple conditions), C2–C4 progressively refine location and mechanism, and C5 MRI findings definitively confirm the diagnosis with excellent clinical-imaging correlation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old distance runner presents with medial knee pain located just below the joint line, accompanied by mild swelling in the same region. Pain is reproducible with weight-bearing activities and has developed over the past six weeks.
C2 Activity: Running, particularly on cambered surfaces or hills, triggers sharp anteromedial knee pain within 10-15 minutes. Walking on level ground is minimally symptomatic. Pain relief occurs with ice, elevation, and anti-inflammatory medications.
C3 History: The patient increased training volume by 40% over the past two months in preparation for a marathon. They recently switched running shoes and report tightness in the hamstring muscles bilaterally. No prior knee pathology or previous running-related injuries.
C4 Examination: Localized tenderness over the pes anserine insertion with palpable swelling. Noble test is negative. Single-leg squat triggers medial knee discomfort. Hamstring tightness confirmed with straight-leg raise limited to 60 degrees bilaterally.
C5 Imaging: Knee MRI reveals fluid collection within the pes anserine bursa (12mm in transverse diameter) with surrounding soft tissue edema. No meniscal tears, ligamentous injuries, or articular cartilage damage identified. Tibia and femur intact.
#695 Post-Carpal Tunnel Release Wrist
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Mostly solid but C3 mentioning 'surgical release performed 3 weeks ago' narrows it significantly early; reframing as post-operative complications without stating the procedure would maintain better diagnostic flow.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports hand paresthesias and grip weakness that began insidiously over months prior to presentation. Symptoms are worst at night and with repetitive gripping activities.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to perform fine pinch tasks and reports dropping objects. Grip strength significantly reduced compared to contralateral side. Activities requiring sustained wrist extension or forearm supination exacerbate symptoms.
C3 History: Surgical release performed 3 weeks ago via open palmar approach. Pre-operative symptoms refractory to conservative management lasting 8+ months. No history of diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, or pregnancy.
C4 Examination: Incision well-healed without erythema or drainage. Two-point discrimination reduced in median nerve distribution compared to ulnar nerve. Phalen's test negative; Tinel's sign mildly positive at thenar eminence. Abductor pollicis brevis strength 4/5.
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction studies show slowed median motor latency (7.2 ms, normal <4.2 ms) and prolonged distal motor latency with reduced amplitude. Sensory conduction severely reduced. Findings confirm focal demyelination across carpal tunnel with axonal loss.
#696 Phantom Limb Pain Limbs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C3 explicitly states 'phantom limb pain develops post-amputation from altered sensory input,' naming the condition and mechanism outright, leaving no diagnostic mystery for subsequent clues.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports pain in a limb that feels present despite visible absence. Quality varies from cramping to burning to sharp stabbing. Symptoms are distressing and persistent.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens with emotional stress and weather changes. Prosthetic use and residual limb activity modulate intensity. Symptoms peak at night and during prolonged inactivity.
C3 History: Phantom limb pain develops post-amputation from altered sensory input to the brain. Pre-operative pain in the limb increases risk of phantom pain post-amputation. The pain typically decreases over time but may persist chronically.
C4 Examination: Residual limb inspection reveals no acute inflammation or skin changes. Pain cannot be reproduced by palpation of the absent limb. Careful history distinguishes phantom sensations from local stump tenderness.
C5 Imaging: Neuroimaging studies have shown abnormal cortical reorganization in individuals with phantom limb pain. fMRI demonstrates altered sensory cortex representation. PET imaging shows metabolic changes in pain processing regions.
#697 Boutonniere Deformity Wrist & Hand
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C3 states 'Boutonniere deformity develops from disruption of the central slip,' essentially naming the diagnosis too early; reframing to describe the mechanism without the eponym would improve the puzzle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports progressive finger deformity affecting hand function. Gripping and fine motor control have deteriorated over recent weeks.
C2 Activity: The patient cannot actively straighten the affected finger at the middle joint, even with assistance. Attempts to extend the PIP joint fail despite intact sensation and strength in the intrinsic muscles.
C3 History: Boutonniere deformity develops from disruption of the central slip of the extensor tendon mechanism. Traumatic injury from laceration or blunt trauma causes the initial injury. Progressive deformity occurs if the deformity is not corrected early.
C4 Examination: On examination, the PIP joint is held in flexion and resists passive extension. The DIP joint lies in hyperextension. Palpation reveals a palpable gap or defect over the dorsal PIP joint. The Fowler test (passive flexion at MCP with extended DIP) shows loss of DIP extension when PIP is relaxed.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows disruption or attenuation of the central slip of the extensor tendon at the level of the PIP joint. Associated soft tissue swelling and possible avulsion fracture at the dorsal base of the middle phalanx confirm the diagnosis.
#698 Swimmer's Shoulder Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 fits multiple overhead athlete shoulder conditions, C2–C4 narrow progressively with activity pattern and examination findings, and C5 ultrasound/MRI confirms rotator cuff tendinopathy in the overhead context.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Overhead athlete reports shoulder pain during training sessions. Onset was gradual over recent weeks with increasing training intensity. Pain is diffuse around the shoulder with overhead movements.
C2 Activity: Patient unable to complete full training sets due to pain. Symptoms worsen proportionally with session duration and frequency. Rest days provide temporary relief but symptoms recur with return to activity.
C3 History: Recent addition to competitive squad led to doubled training volume 3 weeks ago. No prior shoulder injuries. Previous physiotherapy for similar complaints in other overhead athletes suggests predisposition. No neurological symptoms or night pain.
C4 Examination: On examination, shoulder weakness is evident particularly with external rotation. Scapular dyskinesis is noted with altered scapulohumeral rhythm. Positive impingement signs are elicited on examination.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates rotator cuff tendinopathy with thickening and signal changes. MRI shows supraspinatus tendon involvement and inflammatory changes. Imaging correlates with clinical examination findings.
#699 Gout Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 presents sudden joint pain (fits multiple acute arthropathies), C2–C4 add risk factors and classic examination findings, and C5 serum uric acid and crystal analysis provide definitive confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports sudden-onset severe joint pain in the foot, worse with weight bearing. Pain began overnight and is limiting mobility significantly.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot tolerate footwear or pressure on the affected joint. Symptoms are worst in early morning and evening; daytime rest provides temporary relief.
C3 History: Middle-aged male with history of similar acute episodes. Recent heavy alcohol use and consumption of red meat. Takes a diuretic for hypertension. Episodes resolve spontaneously over 1-2 weeks. No prior diagnosis established.
C4 Examination: Marked erythema and swelling over the first MTP joint with warmth to palpation. Exquisite point tenderness at the joint line. Pain reproduced with passive dorsiflexion and plantarflexion. No signs of instability. Strength and sensation intact distally.
C5 Imaging: Serum uric acid level is elevated confirming hyperuricemia. Joint aspiration demonstrates monosodium urate crystals confirming diagnosis. Radiographs may show tophaceous changes in chronic gout.
#700 Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction Grade 1 Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 describes medial foot pain (fits multiple foot conditions), C2–C4 progressively narrow with single-leg testing and tendon-specific findings, and C5 MRI confirms early inflammatory changes with preserved arch.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports mild pain on the medial side of the foot during weight-bearing activities. Pain is vague in onset and worsens progressively through the day with standing or walking. Rest provides relief.
C2 Activity: Patient reports difficulty maintaining prolonged walking or standing. Single-leg activities and dynamic weight shifts increase symptoms. Running is not tolerated well.
C3 History: Insidious onset over weeks to months. No clear traumatic event. Patient reports gradual worsening with increased activity demand or change in footwear. No prior foot injuries or significant comorbidities.
C4 Examination: On examination, mild tenderness is present behind the medial ankle. Single leg heel rise is possible but may produce mild discomfort. Arch height is maintained with intact support mechanism.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates tibialis posterior tendon inflammation with mild thickening. Signal changes within the tendon indicate inflammation. Arch alignment remains normal without structural changes at this stage.
#701 Kinesiophobia General
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (persistent pain with normal healing), each subsequent clue adds meaningful information (psychological factors, inconsistent exam findings, normal imaging), and Clue 5 clinches the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old reports persistent moderate low back pain six months after a work-related strain. Pain has not resolved despite reassurance and normal healing timelines for the initial injury.
C2 Activity: Patient actively avoids bending, lifting, and prolonged sitting. Family reports progressive restriction of activities, including avoidance of movements previously tolerated. Patient expresses catastrophic thoughts about movement causing harm.
C3 History: Significant anxiety history predates the initial injury by several years. The injury occurred in a high-stress work environment with ongoing job dissatisfaction. Previous pain episodes resolved fully with activity resumption.
C4 Examination: Physical examination reveals pain responses and guarding that exceed findings on imaging. Strength testing is inconsistent and variable. Patient demonstrates difficulty tolerating repeated movement trials. Mood and anxiety screening indicates elevated distress.
C5 Imaging: MRI demonstrates resolution of inflammatory changes from the initial injury with normal spinal cord and nerve root anatomy. Imaging findings do not explain the extent of functional limitation and pain reported by the patient.
#702 Sever's Disease Ankle & Foot
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 explicitly mentions 'growth plate pathology should be considered' which narrows the field prematurely; Clue 2 is also partially redundant with Clue 1, both describing the same posterior heel pain presentation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 10-year-old competitive soccer player presents with heel pain that worsens during and after training sessions. Pain is localized to the posterior heel with occasional radiation to the plantar surface. Growth plate pathology should be considered in this age group.
C2 Activity: A 10-year-old competitive athlete presents with posterior heel pain that worsens during and after training sessions. Pain is localized to the back of the heel. Onset was gradual over the past 4-5 weeks.
C3 History: Patient experiences morning stiffness and pain in the heel upon weight-bearing. Pain resolves quickly with rest but returns with any running or jumping activity. Symptoms are worse after high-volume training days.
C4 Examination: Midline calcaneal tenderness is elicited with direct palpation and medial-lateral compression of the calcaneus reproduces pain. Ankle dorsiflexion is limited, and gastrocnemius tightness is evident on assessment. The heel appears mildly swollen relative to the contralateral side.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs show fragmentation and sclerosis of the calcaneal apophysis consistent with apophysitis. No frank fracture or epiphyseal displacement is present. Imaging findings, combined with age, activity level, and clinical presentation, confirm the diagnosis.
#703 Osteoporosis Systemic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (height loss and fragility fracture fit multiple conditions), clues progressively add risk factors and clinical findings, and Clue 5 with DEXA and fracture confirmation is near-definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old woman presents with progressive height loss over several years and chronic mid-back pain. She sustained a wrist fracture from a minor fall 2 weeks ago. Reports increasing stooped posture.
C2 Activity: Unable to bear weight through wrist for 3 weeks post-injury. Thoracic pain prevents lying flat and limits walking distance to <10 minutes. Difficulty rising from low chairs and bed.
C3 History: Menopause at age 57 without hormone replacement. Long-standing low calcium and vitamin D intake. Sedentary lifestyle with minimal weight-bearing exercise. Family history of osteoporosis in mother. Limited sunlight exposure and dietary restrictions.
C4 Examination: Height loss 2 inches confirmed. Thoracic kyphosis ~50 degrees on measurement. Grip strength 3/5 bilateral. Percussion tenderness over T7 spinous process. Timed Up and Go 18 seconds (prolonged). No lower limb neurological deficit.
C5 Imaging: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan shows T-score of -2.8 at lumbar spine and -2.5 at femoral neck (osteoporotic range). Vertebral fracture assessment shows compression fracture at T7 (15% height loss). Biochemical markers show elevated P1NP and CTX-1 indicating high bone turnover.
#704 Rib Fracture Thoracic
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (chest wall pain post-fall), Clue 2 adds mechanical pattern (worse with breathing/movement), Clue 3 provides risk context, and Clue 5 with CT imaging confirms the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old with a history of mild osteoporosis presents with sudden-onset sharp chest wall pain following a minor fall from standing height. Pain is strictly localized to one side.
C2 Activity: Pain is markedly aggravated by deep inspiration, coughing, and any trunk flexion or rotation movements. The patient splints the injured side when coughing. Lying on the affected side is intolerable.
C3 History: The patient has been on corticosteroids for 2 years due to polymyalgia rheumatica, increasing fracture risk. No previous rib injuries are reported. Smoking history is significant with 30 pack-years.
C4 Examination: Precise point tenderness elicited over the posterolateral chest wall at one rib level. Direct palpation reproduces sharp pain. Intercostal muscle guarding is marked.
C5 Imaging: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates a linear cortical break through one rib with minimal displacement. No associated flail segment or pulmonary compromise identified.
#705 Knee Osteoarthritis Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is sufficiently broad (gradual knee pain in middle-aged patient), each clue adds distinct information (mechanical pattern, effusion, imaging changes), and the progression is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old patient reports gradual-onset knee pain over 2 years. Pain is worse in the morning but eases with light activity. Pain returns with prolonged walking or standing. Patient reports difficulty with stairs.
C2 Activity: Patient reports knee swelling by end of day after activity. Pain is mechanical: worse with weight-bearing but improves with rest overnight. Morning stiffness typically lasts 15–20 minutes.
C3 History: Patient sustained a meniscal tear in right knee 5 years ago; mild surgery was avoided. BMI is 31. Works in construction with repetitive squatting. Progressive onset; no acute injury precipitated current worsening.
C4 Examination: On examination, knee effusion may be present with positive ballottement test. Crepitus is palpated with knee motion. Range of motion is limited with pain at end-range flexion and extension.
C5 Imaging: Weight-bearing AP radiographs show medial compartment joint space narrowing with marginal osteophytes and subchondral sclerosis. Lateral and skyline views show mild patellofemoral changes.
#706 Upper Crossed Syndrome Upper Body
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 uses the phrase 'multiple musculoskeletal patterns may contribute' which is vague and undermines the diagnostic clarity; otherwise the progression is sound and Clue 5 appropriately confirms postural dysfunction.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old desk worker presents with chronic upper back and neck tension with associated cervical headaches. Symptoms have developed gradually over 3 years of sedentary work. Multiple musculoskeletal patterns may contribute to this presentation.
C2 Activity: Symptoms worsen significantly with prolonged computer work and improve with movement breaks and stretching. Forward shoulder posture increases pain within 15-20 minutes. Thoracic extension and scapular retraction relieve symptoms temporarily.
C3 History: The patient works 9-10 hours daily at a desk with a poorly positioned monitor and keyboard. Previous physiotherapy provided only temporary relief. No significant trauma history, though postural habits have been poor since adolescence.
C4 Examination: Postural assessment reveals forward head posture and rounded shoulders. Palpation confirms significant upper trapezius and pectoralis tightness. Scapular dyskinesis noted on movement screening. Lower trapezius and serratus anterior strength testing shows grade 3-4/5 bilaterally. Cervical extension ROM limited to 35 degrees. No neural signs on upper limb neurodynamic testing.
C5 Imaging: Cervical and thoracic spine X-rays negative for disc herniation, spondylosis, or subluxation. Postural analysis using plumb-line assessment shows head projection 3 cm anterior to acromion. Scapulohumeral rhythm is asymmetrical with restricted upward rotation bilaterally. Findings confirm postural dysfunction without underlying structural disease.
#707 Neck Tension - Desk Work Neck
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (bilateral neck stiffness with many possible causes), Clue 2 adds activity correlation, Clue 3 provides ergonomic trigger, and Clue 5 with normal imaging and resolution with ergonomic correction clinches a functional diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old office worker presents with bilateral posterior neck stiffness that emerged 4 weeks ago. Symptoms are worse at end of day and improve with rest on weekends.
C2 Activity: Neck tension worsens progressively through the workday and is worst by late afternoon. Taking frequent breaks and performing neck stretches provides 30-45 minutes of relief. Symptoms are minimal on weekends and during vacations.
C3 History: The patient transitioned to a home office setup with suboptimal ergonomics (laptop screen positioned too low). No history of neck trauma, whiplash, or previous neck symptoms. Stress levels have increased due to workload.
C4 Examination: Bilateral upper trapezius muscle tightness with palpable trigger points. Cervical ROM is full but restricted by muscle guarding. Forward head posture noted. No neurological signs, nerve tension, or radicular pattern.
C5 Imaging: Cervical X-rays normal—no degenerative changes, fracture, or alignment abnormality. Imaging ruled out structural disease; diagnosis confirmed by symptom resolution with ergonomic correction over 2 weeks.
#708 Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease Lungs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is a respiratory/medical emergency diagnosis poorly suited to a physiotherapy game; the puzzle describes an acute exacerbation requiring medical management (antibiotics, steroids, ABG) rather than a condition physios independently treat as a primary diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 71-year-old with long-standing cigarette smoking history presents with acute worsening of chronic dyspnea, productive cough, and wheezing. Yellow-tinged sputum noted.
C2 Activity: Unable to walk more than 20 metres without severe breathlessness. Previously could manage light household tasks; now struggling with self-care activities.
C3 History: URI symptoms began 1 week ago with fever and malaise. Recent hospitalisation 2 months ago for similar exacerbation treated with antibiotics and steroids.
C4 Examination: Tachypnea (RR 26), accessory muscle use marked. Diminished breath sounds with wheezes. Productive cough. Cyanosis noted in nail beds. Mental status alert.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with possible infiltrate suggesting infection. Sputum culture growing bacteria. Spirometry consistent with obstructive pattern. ABG shows hypoxemia and hypercapnia.
#709 Post-Total Knee Replacement Knee
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (post-operative knee pain and stiffness), clues progressively reveal functional deficits and examination findings, and Clue 5 excludes structural failure, confirming a rehabilitation-responsive complication.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old presents 6 weeks post-operatively with persistent knee pain and stiffness that has not improved as expected. Pain is present at rest and with activity.
C2 Activity: Patient is unable to fully extend the knee during walking and struggles with stair descent. Knee swelling increases with activity lasting longer than 30 minutes. Quadriceps control is noticeably weak.
C3 History: The patient had severe pre-operative knee flexion contracture of 15 degrees. A continuous passive motion machine was used post-operatively for 10 days. Compliance with home exercises has been suboptimal.
C4 Examination: Knee extension is -10 degrees (extension lag present during straight leg raise). Knee flexion reaches 85 degrees actively. Quadriceps strength is 4/5. Significant anterior knee swelling is noted with a positive joint effusion sign. No signs of infection or deep vein thrombosis are present.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative radiographs show correct component alignment and positioning. No evidence of hardware failure, loosening, or fracture. Mechanical integrity of the reconstruction is sound.
#710 Post-Mastectomy Rehabilitation Chest
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clues 2, 3, and 4 are largely redundant, all restating surgical history and timing without adding new diagnostic information; the puzzle lacks adequate progression and clinical detail to make guessing engaging or educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 54-year-old female presents 8 weeks post-operative from left total mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection for invasive ductal carcinoma. Restricted shoulder function and chest wall tightness are limiting daily activities. Post-surgical complications including seroma and lymphedema must be assessed.
C2 Activity: A 54-year-old female presents with shoulder and upper chest wall symptoms 8 weeks following left-sided surgical intervention. Functional limitations with daily activities are worsening. No acute pain or fever.
C3 History: The surgical incision healed well without dehiscence. No chemotherapy or radiation therapy has been initiated yet. Lymph nodes were removed from levels 1 and 2. The patient is right-hand dominant.
C4 Examination: Left axillary lymph node dissection (levels 1–2) performed 8 weeks ago. Surgical wound has healed without breakdown. No chemotherapy or radiation initiated. Right-hand dominant.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound of the axilla shows no evidence of seroma recurrence or lymph collection. Soft tissue imaging confirms scar tissue adhesions in the axillary region. Imaging does not identify mechanical complications requiring surgical intervention.
#711 Scapular Dyskinesis Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (overhead athlete shoulder pain), each clue adds distinct information (activity pattern → training load/postural history → physical findings → imaging confirmation), and the progression is logical and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 26-year-old overhead athlete presents with progressive anterolateral shoulder pain during explosive overhead activities. Onset was 8 weeks ago and has worsened despite activity modification. Pain is reproducible with sport-specific movements.
C2 Activity: Pain is specific to overhead activities performed explosively and is worst during the late cocking and acceleration phases of throwing. Rest provides partial relief but symptoms recur immediately upon return to sport. Repetitive activities reproduce pain reliably.
C3 History: Training volume increased significantly 2 months prior to onset. No traumatic event. History of postural dysfunction (lower crossed syndrome). Previous shoulder episodes responded to rest but this presentation is more persistent.
C4 Examination: Scapular medial border winging noted during active arm elevation. Serratus anterior and lower trapezius weakness on strength testing. Tightness in posterior shoulder and pectoralis. Supraspinatus sign positive (pain 80–120 degrees abduction).
C5 Imaging: MRI: mild supraspinatus tendinopathy without full-thickness tear. Rotator cuff otherwise intact. No labral pathology or glenohumeral joint abnormality. Subacromial space narrowing consistent with impingement secondary to scapular positioning.
#712 Postnatal Pelvic Floor Dysfunction Pelvis
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 captures multiple postnatal pelvic symptoms without naming the condition, subsequent clues layer in timing, delivery specifics, examination findings, and imaging confirmation with good clinical coherence.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old presents 12 weeks post-partum with pain during intercourse and occasional loss of control of intestinal gas. She reports these symptoms began after delivery but have not fully resolved.
C2 Activity: Stress urinary incontinence occurs with coughing, sneezing, and running. The patient has avoided exercise and high-impact activities for 12 weeks due to fear of leakage and pelvic discomfort.
C3 History: The labor lasted 16 hours with instrumental assistance in the second stage. Perineal tears occurred but were repaired immediately post-delivery. The patient is breastfeeding exclusively, which affects hormonal status. No episiotomy was performed.
C4 Examination: Pelvic floor muscle strength is reduced (2/5 on manual assessment). Voluntary contraction fatigue occurs rapidly within 2–3 repetitions. Palpation reveals tender scar tissue and fascial restrictions in the perineal body. Anal sphincter tone is intact and symmetric.
C5 Imaging: Transperineal ultrasound shows intact external anal sphincter and no defects in the perineal body. Pelvic floor muscle thickness is reduced compared to age-matched controls, consistent with muscular atrophy rather than structural trauma.
#713 Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad but C3 adds little new information (tennis-specific, no trauma) that wasn't already implied in C1–C2; C4 is somewhat redundant with C2 findings, though C5 clinches it with imaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old recreational athlete presents with insidious-onset anterolateral shoulder pain over 3 months, worsened by activity and overhead movements.
C2 Activity: Pain is worse with overhead reaching and cross-body adduction. Sleeping on the affected side aggravates symptoms. Rest provides gradual relief over days to weeks.
C3 History: The patient plays competitive tennis 3 times weekly and has recently increased intensity. No acute trauma or fall occurred. The dominant (right) shoulder is affected. Previous shoulder problems are denied.
C4 Examination: Positive Hawkins and Neer impingement tests. Supraspinatus weakness present but no complete loss of strength. Scapular kinematics normal.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows thickening and heterogeneity of the supraspinatus tendon with intact fiber continuity confirming tendinopathy. No full-thickness tear is present. Subacromial subdeltoid bursa shows mild fluid consistent with secondary bursitis.
#714 Ankle Osteoarthritis Ankle
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (chronic ankle pain with history of injury), each clue progressively narrows the differential (progressive limitation, cumulative trauma history, ROM loss, imaging confirmation), and the story is coherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old with chronic ankle pain and stiffness progressing over 5 years, particularly with recent acceleration. Long history of recurrent ankle injuries in the same ankle.
C2 Activity: Pain worsens significantly with ambulation on uneven ground and with pivoting movements. Walking tolerance has decreased from 45 minutes to 20 minutes due to progressive ankle pain. Morning stiffness lasting 30 minutes is reported upon waking.
C3 History: Multiple ankle sprains over 30 years have progressively damaged the joint surfaces and ligamentous support. No significant trauma in the past year, but cumulative joint loading has been high from walking/hiking activities. Previous physiotherapy for ankle stability was completed 10 years ago.
C4 Examination: Ankle dorsiflexion is limited to 5 degrees (normal 15 degrees) and plantarflexion to 35 degrees (normal 50 degrees). Significant anteromedial ankle joint line tenderness is elicited. The gait demonstrates a stiff ankle pattern with circumduction. Weight-bearing calf raise produces pain.
C5 Imaging: Weight-bearing radiographs show marked joint space narrowing at the tibiotalar joint with marginal osteophytes and subchondral sclerosis. No significant subluxation or instability is evident. Imaging confirms advanced ankle osteoarthritis with degenerative changes consistent with post-traumatic disease.
#715 Hip Labral Tear Hip
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C4 and C5 are swapped or merged confusingly—C5 repeats examination findings (internal rotation, FABER, strength testing) rather than providing imaging confirmation, making the clue progression incoherent and the game unplayable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A patient reports deep groin pain with activities involving hip flexion and internal rotation. Mechanical symptoms including catching or clicking may occur. Functional limitation occurs with running and pivoting activities.
C2 Activity: A patient reports deep groin or anterior hip pain during athletic activities. Onset was gradual over several weeks. Pain is intermittent but reproducible with certain movements. Mechanical symptoms may accompany pain.
C3 History: Patient cannot tolerate combined movements of hip flexion and adduction (e.g., bringing knee to opposite shoulder). Pain worsens with prolonged sitting. Walking is tolerated but high-impact activities are limited.
C4 Examination: On examination, the FABER test and anterior impingement test may reproduce symptoms. Internal rotation of the hip in flexion triggers pain. Hip flexibility deficits are often noted.
C5 Imaging: On examination, the hip shows restricted internal rotation in flexion (less than 10 degrees). Pain is reproduced with combined flexion, adduction, and internal rotation. No significant capsular end-feel. Palpable psoas and adductor tenderness may be present. Hip abductor strength is intact.
#716 Tennis Leg Calf
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is well-scoped (acute calf tear), clues layer in temporal swelling/bruising, history of prior episode, examination findings, and imaging confirmation; the clinical narrative is clear and the progression is sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 44-year-old recreational athlete reports sudden-onset severe pain in the posterior lower leg during a tennis match. A sharp, tearing sensation was felt, followed by immediate difficulty with weight-bearing. No prior calf symptoms.
C2 Activity: Patient reports severe swelling has developed over the first 24-48 hours post-injury. Bruising is becoming visible extending from mid-calf toward the ankle. Morning stiffness and pain with any weight-bearing are significant.
C3 History: Patient reports an episode of acute calf pain 10 years prior that self-resolved without intervention. Current injury occurred during explosive movement in tennis. No chronic calf problems or previous Achilles issues. No recent corticosteroid use or systemic illness.
C4 Examination: Significant calf swelling is noted with bruising extending toward the ankle over days post-injury. Plantarflexion strength is severely weakened (1-2/5) with pain limiting effort. The Thompson test is negative (indicating intact Achilles tendon). A midcalf muscle defect may be palpable on careful examination. Considerable ecchymosis is present.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows a hypoechoic disruption in the gastrocnemius-soleus muscle complex with edema and hemorrhage consistent with a partial muscle tear (grade 2-3 strain). The tear is localized to the medial gastrocnemius. No Achilles tendon discontinuity is identified.
#717 Cubital Tunnel Syndrome Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (progressive numbness in ulnar distribution without naming the condition), each clue adds activity pattern, ergonomic context, specific signs, and confirmatory electrophysiology with good logical flow.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old desk worker presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the medial hand (ring and little fingers) over 4 months. Symptoms are worse at night and have begun affecting grip strength.
C2 Activity: Prolonged typing exacerbates symptoms significantly. Sleeping with the elbow flexed triggers symptoms that wake the patient during the night. Activities requiring sustained elbow flexion (holding phone to ear) reproduce paresthesias within 5-10 minutes.
C3 History: Patient works with poor ergonomic setup: armrest too low, keyboard too high, elbow flexed for prolonged periods. No recent trauma to the elbow.
C4 Examination: Positive Tinel's sign is elicited by percussing the ulnar nerve at the elbow (just posterior to the medial epicondyle). The Froment test is positive with inability to adduct the thumb without flexing the interphalangeal joint. Intrinsic hand muscle atrophy is subtle but present in the interossei. Two-point discrimination is diminished in the little finger.
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction studies show slowing of conduction velocity across the elbow segment consistent with cubital tunnel syndrome. EMG demonstrates denervation activity in intrinsic hand muscles and mild motor involvement. MRI shows compression of the ulnar nerve at the level of the cubital tunnel.
#718 Jersey Finger Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately specific to the injury mechanism but not the diagnosis, subsequent clues layer in loss of DIP flexion, tendon palpation findings, and imaging confirmation without redundancy.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 26-year-old rugby player. Ring finger caught forcefully during tackle. Sharp pain and immediate inability to bend finger at one joint. Swelling developed quickly in the forearm.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot actively straighten the ring finger at rest; finger held in bent position. Pain worsens when attempting to bend the finger. Foregrip and gripping activities severely limited.
C3 History: Injury occurred 2 hours ago during competitive contact sport. No prior hand or finger injuries. Patient is fully active athlete with high functional demands.
C4 Examination: Ring finger held in extension at DIP joint with inability to actively flex (0/5 DIP flexion). PIP flexion intact at 4/5 strength. Mild swelling noted in volar forearm proximal to wrist. Tender palm with fullness along flexor digitorum profundus tendon region. No sensory loss or vascular compromise. No wound or skin disruption.
C5 Imaging: X-ray shows no bone avulsion at distal phalanx base (distinguishes from bony mallet). MRI demonstrates complete rupture of flexor digitorum profundus tendon at level of wrist crease. Tendon retracted proximally with gap of 3cm. Associated edema in forearm soft tissues. Ultrasound confirms tendon discontinuity with fluid collection (hematoma) tracking proximally in forearm.
#719 Pelvic Floor Dysfunction Pelvis
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 is overly broad (mixed incontinence symptoms fit multiple conditions including overactive bladder, stress incontinence, neurogenic dysfunction), C3 repeats functional limitations without new clinical detail, C4 and C5 lack specificity and essentially describe the same weak pelvic floor assessment twice with minimal imaging correlation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 67-year-old woman with pelvic floor dysfunction reports mixed symptoms of urgency and stress incontinence. Frequency significant with 12+ voidings daily. Nocturnal symptoms with multiple awakenings.
C2 Activity: Functional limitation from incontinence and frequency. Difficulty with social activities and travel. Sleep disruption from nocturia. Significant quality of life impact.
C3 History: Unable to engage in social outings, avoid travel. Sleep severely disrupted by nocturia (wakes 4+ times nightly). Anxiety about public situations due to incontinence episodes.
C4 Examination: Weak pelvic floor on assessment. Pelvic heaviness sensation. Palpable pelvic floor laxity. Possible mild pelvic organ prolapse. Guarding from pain possible.
C5 Imaging: Weak voluntary pelvic floor contraction (grade 2/5 strength, poor endurance). Pelvic heaviness on Valsalva. Palpable anterior vaginal wall laxity. Unable to isolate deep core muscles. No significant pain-related guarding noted.
#720 Morton's Neuroma Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (forefoot burning pain), each clue adds activity specifics, relevant history (prior contralateral neuroma), characteristic examination findings (Mulder's test, tenderness), and imaging confirmation without major redundancy.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old woman presents with 6 months of burning pain in the ball of the foot. Pain radiates into the toes and is notably aggravated by tight footwear and prolonged standing. Symptoms are intermittent.
C2 Activity: Walking on hard surfaces and ascending stairs exacerbate symptoms. Patient reports a sharp shooting sensation into specific toes during gait, particularly in the push-off phase. Activities requiring forefoot gripping (e.g., standing on tiptoes) are avoided.
C3 History: Patient has a documented history of the same condition on the contralateral foot 8 years ago, resolved with conservative measures. Symptoms are recurrent in the same occupation. No history of foot trauma or inflammatory arthropathy. No sensory disturbance elsewhere in the foot.
C4 Examination: Direct compression between the metatarsal heads reproduces sharp pain into the 3rd and 4th toes (Mulder's test positive). A palpable click or 'pop' may be felt with this compression. Plantar palpation elicits exquisite tenderness in the interspace. Sensation to light touch is normal in the affected toes.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows a hypoechoic nodular mass between the 3rd and 4th metatarsal heads consistent with a neuroma (typically 3-5mm). MRI confirms a focal mass in the plantar intermetatarsal space with T2 signal intensity consistent with nerve tissue edema. Imaging rules out other forefoot pathology.
#721 Delirium - Rehabilitation Phase General
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 appropriately broad (acute confusion post-surgery fits many conditions), progression is logical from behavioral presentation → cognitive deficits → medical workup → imaging, and Clue 5 clinches diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old admitted 4 days ago following hip fracture repair. Family reports sudden onset of confusion and disorientation that seems to come and go. Patient alternates between agitation and lethargy. Baseline cognition was borderline—family notes he was becoming more forgetful before admission.
C2 Activity: During the day, patient is drowsy and difficult to engage in therapy. At night, he becomes agitated and tries to get out of bed unsupervised. Staff report he doesn't seem to know where he is or what time of day it is. Mood and alertness shift unpredictably hour to hour.
C3 History: Pre-admission cognition: early dementia with slow decline. Post-operatively: opioids introduced for pain, two sleep aids prescribed. Medical team has investigated and ruled out infection, electrolyte abnormality, and dehydration. Patient has no prior delirium episodes. This is his first time in hospital.
C4 Examination: Bedside cognition testing: cannot name the date or hospital location. Digit span: struggles to repeat 4 digits forward. Attention test (months backwards): stops after 3 months. No focal weakness, sensation intact, cranial nerves normal. Heart rate 78, BP 138/82, temperature 37.1°C.
C5 Imaging: CT head performed to exclude acute intracranial pathology. No acute stroke, no subdural hematoma, no bleed. Only finding: chronic small vessel ischemic changes consistent with age. Structural causes ruled out. Clinical diagnosis of delirium confirmed.
#722 Heterotopic Ossification Various
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonably broad (progressive hip stiffness has multiple causes), each subsequent clue adds genuine information (functional loss → trauma history → examination findings → imaging confirmation), and progression is coherent.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old man presents with progressive stiffness and loss of motion in the hip, developing over the past 3 months. Pain and swelling in the hip region limiting daily activities like dressing and walking.
C2 Activity: Progressive loss of hip internal and external rotation. Hip flexion contracture developing (unable to fully extend hip). Abduction and adduction limited. Pain with attempted motion. Functional decline with difficulty dressing and toileting.
C3 History: Hip posterior dislocation with femoral head fracture sustained 12 weeks ago from high-energy trauma. Significant soft tissue injury. Prolonged immobilization in acute phase followed by delayed mobilization.
C4 Examination: Hip flexion contracture (unable to achieve neutral extension). Severe restriction of internal rotation and external rotation (<15° each direction). Firm, non-painful end-feel on passive range. Palpable stiffness in periarticular tissues. No neurological deficit. Antalgic gait.
C5 Imaging: X-ray hip demonstrates extensive bone formation in soft tissues surrounding the hip joint with bridging across the articulation, graded as severe. CT imaging shows mature ossified bridges in capsular and periarticular tissues. MRI confirms ossification signal consistent with mature bone.
#723 Neuropathic Pain Whole Body
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 fits 10+ conditions (distal burning pain in lower extremities), clues progress logically through symptom character → examination findings → investigations, and Clue 5 definitively establishes neuropathic origin.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes for 12 years presents with progressive pain in the feet and lower legs over the past 8 months. Pain is described as burning, electrical, or 'pins and needles' with a distal-to-proximal pattern.
C2 Activity: Light touch or clothing contact triggers sharp pain out of proportion to stimulus severity. Symptoms are notably worse at night and wake the patient from sleep. Prolonged standing or walking significantly exacerbates discomfort.
C3 History: Glycemic control has been inconsistent (HbA1c 7.8–8.5% over 2 years). Patient is also hypertensive. No preceding injury or trauma to the feet. Symptoms began insidiously without clear trigger.
C4 Examination: Sharp-dull discrimination is diminished in the feet bilaterally with a stocking distribution. Vibration sense is reduced at the ankles. Monofilament testing (10g) shows loss of sensation in the plantar surface. Temperature sensation is mildly altered. Reflexes are diminished at the ankles. Motor strength is normal.
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction studies show reduced motor and sensory conduction velocities in bilateral lower extremities consistent with demyelination. EMG demonstrates mild denervation in distal foot muscles. DN4 neuropathic pain questionnaire score is elevated, supporting neuropathic origin.
#724 Cognitive Impairment Brain
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is overly broad and could indicate dementia, depression, or delirium equally; Clue 5 deliberately hedges with 'does not exclude neurodegenerative process' which undermines specificity, though the vascular history thread is present.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 78-year-old reports increasing difficulty remembering recent events and managing complex everyday activities over the past 18 months. Spouse notices the patient often forgets recent conversations and becomes confused with familiar tasks.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot manage new learning or complex sequential tasks without written support. Medication management with multiple prescriptions is now unreliable; requires supervision for unfamiliar activities.
C3 History: History of transient ischemic attack 5 years prior. Currently managed for hypertension and high cholesterol but medication adherence is inconsistent. Depression screening unremarkable. No acute neurological events in recent months.
C4 Examination: Formal cognitive screening using Montreal Cognitive Assessment shows score of 22/30 (below age-adjusted norms). Memory is impaired for recent events but long-term memory is preserved. Attention and executive function are mildly affected. Speech is normal and no focal neurological deficits are present.
C5 Imaging: MRI reveals small vessel ischemic disease and mild age-appropriate brain atrophy. No acute infarction, hemorrhage, or mass. Imaging pattern consistent with vascular contributions to cognitive decline but does not exclude underlying neurodegenerative process.
#725 Colles Fracture Wrist & Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (FOOSH wrist injury), classic progression from mechanism → functional loss → deformity → imaging confirmation, and 'dinner fork' deformity in Clue 4 is a fair teaching moment.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old woman fell onto an outstretched hand from standing height and reports immediate wrist pain with visible swelling.
C2 Activity: The patient cannot grip or lift objects. Wrist movement in all directions is severely limited by pain. She is unable to perform self-care tasks involving hand use.
C3 History: Known osteoporosis with progressive balance impairment contributing to falls. No significant prior wrist injuries. Fall occurred at standing height without additional trauma context.
C4 Examination: The wrist demonstrates a 'dinner fork' deformity with dorsal displacement of the distal fragment. Marked swelling of the dorsal wrist is evident. Tenderness is greatest over the distal radius. Radial and ulnar pulses are intact. Hand color and temperature appear normal, and finger sensation is preserved.
C5 Imaging: Plain wrist radiographs (posteroanterior and lateral views) show a dorsally displaced metaphyseal fracture of the distal radius with comminution of the dorsal margin. Ulnar styloid fracture is present in 50% of cases. CT may be used to assess fracture complexity for surgical planning.
#726 Osteogenesis Imperfecta Whole Body
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (recurrent fractures from minimal trauma fit several conditions), each clue adds distinctive information (family history → developmental delay → connective tissue signs → genetic confirmation), and progression is coherent and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 7-year-old presents with a fourth fracture in 18 months after falling from a low height during play. Parents report the child has always bruised easily and been unusually fragile compared to siblings.
C2 Activity: Child is unable to weight-bear on the fractured limb and refuses to participate in running or contact play. Motor milestones have been delayed; walking began at 20 months.
C3 History: Mother and maternal grandmother have history of recurrent fractures and hearing loss. Child's growth has trailed below 10th percentile since infancy. Audiometry at school screening shows conductive hearing loss. Dental clinician noted enamel hypoplasia with unusually early decay despite good hygiene.
C4 Examination: Generalized ligamentous laxity evident; passive range of motion at all major joints exceeds normal limits. Muscle bulk and tone are diminished throughout. Respiratory effort appears increased at rest. Skin is translucent with visible veining. Palate is high-arched.
C5 Imaging: X-rays of the fractured limb show the acute fracture with osteopenic bone (reduced bone density) and thin cortices. Generalized skeletal survey reveals healed fractures in various stages of healing and evidence of previous long bone deformities. Bone density assessment (DEXA scan) shows significantly reduced bone density. Genetic testing confirms collagen I abnormality.
#727 Infraspinatus Tear Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (posterior shoulder pain and weakness fit supraspinatus, infraspinatus, axillary nerve, or other conditions), external rotation weakness in Clue 4 adds specificity, and MRI confirmation is definitive.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 54-year-old presents with 2 months of progressive deep shoulder pain and weakness. Pain is localised posteriorly but non-radiating. Weakness is noted with certain arm movements.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform overhead activities without compensation and reports sharp pain with throwing. Reaching behind the back is markedly limited. Interestingly, passive range of motion appears relatively preserved.
C3 History: A fall on an outstretched arm with external rotation occurred 3 months ago, initially attributed to a minor strain. Symptoms have progressively worsened rather than improving. No significant prior shoulder history. The patient is right-hand dominant.
C4 Examination: Marked weakness of external rotation (1-2/5) with reproduction of pain. Supraspinatus strength is preserved. Posterior shoulder atrophy is evident. Infraspinatus tenderness elicited on palpation beneath posterior deltoid.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows a full-thickness tear of the infraspinatus with mild retraction of the muscle belly. The supraspinatus is normal. Fatty infiltration is absent, indicating acute injury. Ultrasound confirms infraspinatus tear with gap at the musculotendinous junction. The tear size and patient age determine management approach.
#728 Pneumonia Lungs
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 and Clue 2 are nearly identical (both describe fever, cough, productive sputum, dyspnea), creating a progression failure; the game would reveal minimal new information between first and second guesses.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old previously healthy teacher presents with acute-onset fever, cough, and chest discomfort of 3 days duration. The cough is productive of greenish-yellow sputum and is associated with dyspnea with minimal exertion.
C2 Activity: A 42-year-old previously healthy teacher presents with a 3-day history of fever, persistent cough, and chest discomfort. The cough is productive of discolored sputum. Shortness of breath occurs with minimal exertion.
C3 History: Exertional dyspnea is limiting daily activities; patient unable to return to work. Lying flat worsens cough and breathing; patient sleeps propped up. General fatigue prevents non-work activities.
C4 Examination: On examination, temperature is 38.8°C, respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 94% on room air. Auscultation reveals crackles in the left lower lobe with egophony present. Dullness to percussion noted over affected area. Tachycardia present at 102 bpm.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray demonstrates lobar consolidation in the left lower lobe with air bronchograms, consistent with community-acquired pneumonia. Sputum culture is pending. White blood cell count is elevated at 14,500 cells/mL. Procalcitonin level is elevated supporting bacterial etiology.
#729 Olecranon Bursitis Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (posterior elbow swelling fits trauma, infection, rheumatoid disease), progression logically moves from complaint → activity pattern → history → examination findings → ultrasound confirmation, with good distinction between inflammatory and septic bursitis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old desk worker presents with swelling over the posterior aspect of the elbow that developed insidiously over 2 weeks. Pain is mild and localised to direct pressure on the swollen area; elbow movement remains largely pain-free.
C2 Activity: Swelling is significantly worse after resting or leaning on the elbow for extended periods. Activities requiring active elbow movement are tolerated well; however, passive end-range elbow extension and direct contact with the swollen area provoke discomfort.
C3 History: The patient recalls no specific trauma but notes increased time leaning on elbows while working at a desk over the past 3 months. No fever, chills, or systemic symptoms are reported. No intravenous drug use. Rheumatoid arthritis screening has been negative.
C4 Examination: On examination: prominent soft, fluctuant swelling localised to the olecranon bursa. Overlying skin is normal in appearance without erythema or warmth. Palpation of the bursal fluid elicits tenderness, but the olecranon bone itself is non-tender. Elbow range of motion is full and pain-free. No systemic signs: no fever, no local erythema, lymph nodes normal.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows a fluid collection in the olecranon bursa without internal echoes (simple fluid) or thick septations, consistent with bursitis. Bursal wall is thickened. No osteophytes or bone erosion is evident. Imaging rules out septic bursitis (which would show echogenic debris or thick loculations).
#730 Jones Fracture Foot
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 ('sudden-onset midfoot pain on outer border') is quite specific for fifth metatarsal injury; broadening to 'lateral midfoot pain during push-off' would allow more differential diagnoses (stress fracture, syndesmotic injury, cuboid syndrome) and make Clue 1 more genuinely broad.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A runner reports sudden-onset midfoot pain on the outer border of the foot. Pain began during or immediately after a push-off movement or twisting incident. Weight bearing is significantly limited.
C2 Activity: Lateral foot pain is reproducible with single-leg hop and push-off. Swelling develops within hours. Pain does not resolve with rest and anti-inflammatory management over several days.
C3 History: Injury occurred 2 weeks ago during running. Initial swelling and bruising were pronounced. Patient attempted to continue activity. Pain has not improved despite relative rest. No prior foot injuries.
C4 Examination: Tenderness is sharply localized to a small area at the lateral midfoot base; palpation reproduces exact pain location. Weight bearing on the affected foot is severely limited. Ankle inversion and eversion are pain-free. Resisted plantarflexion provokes sharp lateral foot pain.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate transverse fracture at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal. CT imaging assesses fracture displacement and healing. Advanced imaging guides timing of weight bearing progression.
#731 Temporomandibular Joint Disorder Jaw
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C5 repeats examination findings (palpation, clicking, mouth opening, trigger points) rather than providing imaging data, breaking the clue progression entirely.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old presents with pain localized to the anterior ear region bilaterally, clicking in both temporomandibular joints, and difficulty with wide mouth opening. Symptoms have developed over 2 months. Myofascial and intra-articular disorders must be differentiated.
C2 Activity: A 45-year-old presents with pain around both ears and clicking in the jaw joints. Mouth opening has become more difficult over the past 2 months.
C3 History: Chewing tough foods and prolonged talking aggravate the pain. The patient notices the jaw deviates to one side when opening and experiences morning stiffness of the jaw.
C4 Examination: The jaw deviates to the left on opening (10mm at maximum). Bilateral temporomandibular joint palpation elicits mild tenderness and is felt clicking during opening and closing. Masseter and temporalis muscle palpation is tender with trigger points. Maximum mouth opening is limited to 35mm (normal 40-50mm).
C5 Imaging: Palpation of both temporomandibular joints elicits clicking during opening and mild tenderness. Maximum mouth opening is 35mm. Masseter and temporalis muscles are tender with palpable trigger points bilaterally. Jaw deviates 10mm to the left at maximum opening.
#732 Costochondritis Thoracic
✓ READY
Verdict: Broad C1 (chest pain differential), good progression through activity/history/exam, and C5 appropriately rules out cardiac causes; fair and playable.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old presents with sharp anterior chest wall pain on the left side for 3 weeks, worse with deep breathing and coughing. The pain is localized and reproducible. Cardiac causes are being considered in the differential.
C2 Activity: Movements that increase chest wall tension or rib cage excursion aggravate symptoms, particularly heavy lifting and repetitive pushing. Sleeping on the left side is uncomfortable. Symptoms improve with arm support and reduced upper body activity.
C3 History: The patient had an upper respiratory infection with persistent cough 2 months ago; however, the cough has resolved, but pain persists. No trauma to the chest wall or weight lifting occurred. Previous episode of similar pain occurred 3 years ago and resolved in 6 weeks.
C4 Examination: Tenderness is elicited on direct palpation of specific points on the anterior rib cage. Chest wall ROM is unrestricted. Cardiac auscultation is normal. Respiratory examination reveals no abnormalities.
C5 Imaging: Chest X-ray shows no acute findings. ECG is normal, excluding cardiac causes. Clinical examination alone is sufficient to establish the diagnosis.
#733 Generalised Joint Hypermobility Systemic
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C1 is slightly too specific (mentions 'double-jointed' and recurrent dislocations narrowing the field considerably), but the progression is logical and C5 clinches well with Beighton score.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 22-year-old presents with a lifetime history of joint instability, frequent dislocations (particularly shoulders and fingers), and diffuse joint pain that worsens with activity. The patient reports being 'double-jointed' since childhood. Systemic connective tissue disorder must be assessed.
C2 Activity: A 22-year-old reports a lifelong pattern of joint instability and recurrent dislocations (particularly shoulders and fingers), alongside diffuse joint pain that worsens with activity. Friends have always remarked on unusual flexibility. No single injury explains the pattern.
C3 History: Patient cannot sustain repetitive fine motor activities (typing, playing instruments) without pain and apprehension. General exercise tolerance is preserved, but dynamic multi-joint movements (jumping, pivoting, contact sports) consistently provoke instability episodes.
C4 Examination: Family history: mother has documented joint hypermobility. Patient reports easy bruising, delayed wound healing, and a childhood able to perform extreme flexibility feats (thumb-to-wrist bending, full splits). No connective tissue diagnosis yet established in the family.
C5 Imaging: Beighton score 6/9 (thumbs touch forearm, fingers hyperextend >90°, palms flat on floor with knees straight, elbow hyperextension, knee hyperextension). Shoulder girdle strength reduced symmetrically. Proprioceptive testing shows subtle deficits; postural stability during single-leg stance is reduced.
#734 Biceps Tendinopathy Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is appropriately broad (anterior shoulder pain in overhead athlete), each clue adds specific information (activity, repetitive history, pathognomonic tests, then imaging confirmation), and progression is logical.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old recreational tennis player presents with anterior shoulder pain for 3 weeks. Pain began during overhead serve motion. Reports occasional clicking sensation in shoulder during reaching.
C2 Activity: Cannot tolerate overhead throwing or serving. Pain worsens with sustained reaching above shoulder height. Symptoms improve partially with rest and ice, but night pain disturbs sleep, particularly when lying on the affected side.
C3 History: Repetitive overhead sports participation (tennis 4x/week) for past year. Gradual onset suggesting tendinopathy rather than acute tear. No prior shoulder surgery. Underlying rotator cuff dysfunction noted on screening examination.
C4 Examination: Speed's test (supinated elbow flexion against resistance) reproduces anterior shoulder pain. Yergason test (supinated forearm with elbow flexion) also positive. Palpable tenderness over bicipital groove. Apprehension test negative. Rotator cuff strength 4+/5 suggesting dynamic instability.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps tendon thickening (5mm) with hypoechoic echotexture and increased Doppler signal indicating active inflammation. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration of the proximal biceps tendon with intact fibres and no full-thickness tear.
#735 Myositis Ossificans Whole Body
✓ READY
Verdict: Strong clinical progression from post-operative trauma history through worsening motion loss and swelling to imaging evidence of heterotopic ossification; diagnosis fairly earned by C5.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old presents 4 weeks post-operatively from a high-energy elbow injury requiring surgery. Progressive pain and severe loss of motion have developed despite appropriate post-operative management. Swelling persists.
C2 Activity: Elbow extension severely limited to approximately 60 degrees of flexion; passive attempts provoke significant pain and guarding. Basic functional activities like reaching overhead and grooming are now impossible.
C3 History: Sustained the original injury 4 weeks ago from a fall from height. Required open surgical reduction and internal fixation due to fracture-dislocation. Significant soft tissue trauma at time of injury. Post-operative wound healing and rehabilitation have progressed normally otherwise, but motion loss and pain are worsening despite therapy.
C4 Examination: Severe soft tissue swelling persists over the antecubital fossa and dorsal elbow. Elbow flexion is approximately 90 degrees. Attempts at passive extension reproduce significant pain and apprehension. Skin temperature is slightly elevated. Radial and ulnar pulses remain intact. Fingers are warm with normal color.
C5 Imaging: Plain radiographs reveal cloud-like opacities within soft tissues around the elbow with early demarcation—consistent with heterotopic bone formation. CT demonstrates extent of ossification in relation to the surgical fixation. Bone scan shows marked uptake in the affected region.
#736 Acromioclavicular Joint Sprain Shoulder
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: C3 reads like a reference text (explaining grading system) rather than patient history, and C1 mentions 'direct blow' which is fairly specific; reframe C3 as actual history and generalize C1 slightly.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Patient reports sharp shoulder pain following a direct blow to the top of the shoulder during a contact sport. Pain worsens with arm movements that cross the midline or reach overhead.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform bench press or push-ups. Symptoms flare when throwing or reaching across the body, such as fastening a seat belt. Overhead lifting is markedly painful.
C3 History: Sprain typically follows direct blow to the shoulder or fall onto the outstretched arm. The injury grades from 1-6 based on ligament disruption and clavicular displacement. Higher grade sprains demonstrate clavicular subluxation or complete dislocation.
C4 Examination: Marked tenderness directly over the AC joint with joint line palpation. Crossover arm adduction test reproduces sharp pain at the joint. Shoulder abduction strength is intact but adduction under load causes discomfort. No signs of glenohumeral instability or rotator cuff pathology.
C5 Imaging: Radiographs demonstrate clavicular displacement and AC joint space abnormalities based on sprain grade. Weighted views show degree of clavicular elevation if AC ligament is disrupted. MRI reveals ligament injury and associated findings.
#737 Wound Dehiscence Post-Surgical General
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from acute post-op pain/drainage through activity limitation and examination findings to imaging confirmation; diagnosis is specific but fairly revealed by C5.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 64-year-old presents 7 days post-operatively from abdominal surgery with acute pain at the surgical site and visible leakage of fluid from the incision. Patient reports sudden onset during the recovery period.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform normal post-operative mobilisation. Straining, coughing, or bending triggers sharp pain and increased fluid drainage from the site. Ambulation is severely limited.
C3 History: Initial post-operative course was unremarkable until day 6, when sudden pain and drainage developed. Patient has significant post-operative cough and BMI of 32. No prior wound complications or immunosuppression.
C4 Examination: Visual inspection of the incision reveals partial separation of the wound edges with visible subcutaneous tissue in the area of separation. Serosanguineous drainage is present. The surrounding skin is erythematous. No systemic signs of infection are present (normal temperature, vital signs stable). Some wound edges remain intact.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound confirms separation of fascial and subcutaneous layers with a fluid collection measuring 3 cm. No evidence of loculated abscess. Intact deep fascia noted on imaging.
#738 Acute Wry Neck Cervical Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: C1 is appropriately broad (sudden neck pain could be multiple conditions), good activity/history/exam specificity, and C5 appropriately rules out fracture/subluxation; playable and educational.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old man wakes with sudden severe unilateral neck pain and inability to move his head normally. Onset during sleep with no recall of trauma.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot turn head to the affected side. Rotation in opposite direction is possible but painful. All neck movements exacerbate symptoms.
C3 History: No prior neck problems. No trauma recalled. Symptoms started immediately upon waking; patient had slept on an unfamiliar pillow.
C4 Examination: Significant muscle spasm in ipsilateral sternocleidomastoid and upper trapezius visible. Neck rotation severely restricted in direction of pain, approximately 20 degrees. Palpable muscle tightness.
C5 Imaging: Cervical spine X-rays show normal alignment and no acute bony pathology. No evidence of subluxation or fracture.
#739 Shoulder Arthroscopy Shoulder
✗ REWORK
