Verdict: C1 names the procedure itself ('surgical procedure') making it essentially a spoiler; the puzzle should describe impingement syndrome or post-operative pain generically, not the intervention.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old presents with persistent shoulder pain and stiffness 2 weeks after a surgical procedure. Despite the intervention, overhead movements remain difficult and sleep is disrupted. Pain is present with most arm activities.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform overhead tasks or reach behind the back for dressing. Pain escalates sharply with any arm elevation beyond 90 degrees of abduction. Night pain prevents comfortable sleeping positions.
C3 History: Patient reports pre-operative symptoms were consistent with classic impingement: pain worse with overhead activity, night pain waking them from sleep. Imaging prior to surgery showed degenerative changes and inflammation. Physiotherapy compliance post-op has been good.
C4 Examination: Special tests reveal positive supraspinatus sign and apprehension with external rotation. Scapular dyskinesis is noted during arm elevation. Palpation elicits pain over the anterior shoulder and acromioclavicular joint. Strength testing intact but guarded.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative ultrasound shows complete osteophyte removal and enlarged subacromial space. Rotator cuff is intact with no evidence of iatrogenic tear. Bursa is clear. However, there is persistent mild subdeltoid edema suggesting ongoing inflammation.
#740 Thoracic Disc Herniation Thoracic Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: C1 mentions 'myelopathic features' and C2–C4 repeat the same history/activity information with minimal progression; clue overlap and educational redundancy make this structurally weak.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old presents with progressive mid-back pain (thoracic spine) and bilateral lower extremity pain with a girdle-like distribution around the chest at the level of the thoracic spine. Pain is localized to the thoracic region with radiation bilaterally. Myelopathic features must be assessed.
C2 Activity: A 52-year-old with progressive mid-back pain and bilateral lower limb pain. Pain has a banding quality around the trunk. Symptoms developed insidiously over 3 months without preceding injury.
C3 History: Symptoms developed insidiously over 3 months without trauma. The patient has a sedentary desk job. Previous episodes of mid-back pain have been minimal. No significant trauma or falls. Progressive nature of symptoms is concerning.
C4 Examination: Sedentary desk worker. Symptoms began 3 months ago without trauma or clear inciting event. Minimal prior back history. Progressive worsening despite conservative measures (rest, activity modification).
C5 Imaging: MRI shows central or paracentral disc herniation at the thoracic level compressing the spinal cord. The cord shows signal change (T2 hyperintensity) consistent with myelomalacia. CT can clarify bone anatomy if surgical intervention is contemplated. Imaging demonstrates cord compression with edema indicating significant pathology.
#741 Labyrinthitis Inner Ear
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (sudden vertigo + nausea post-URI fits multiple conditions), and each subsequent clue adds genuine specificity, with Clue 5 clinching the diagnosis via imaging and audiometry confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 42-year-old with sudden onset of severe vertigo and nausea, started 3 days after an upper respiratory infection resolved. Vertigo is worse with any head movement.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot walk without severe imbalance and falls risk. Cannot tolerate reading, screen use, or rapid eye movements. Has vomited multiple times since onset.
C3 History: Sudden hearing loss noted in left ear since vertigo onset. No prior episodes of dizziness or hearing problems. No head trauma, no medication changes. Lives alone, unable to care for self currently.
C4 Examination: Spontaneous nystagmus present, fast component beating toward left ear. Head impulse test abnormal on leftward rotation—unable to maintain target fixation. Romberg test positive. Nausea reproduced with rapid head turns. Weber test lateralizes to left (conductive pattern). Rinne shows air-bone gap on left.
C5 Imaging: MRI with contrast shows T2 hyperintensity in the labyrinth on the affected side consistent with labyrinthitis. The vestibular nerve may show enhancement. Audiometry documents sensorineural hearing loss in the affected ear. Imaging and audiometry confirm viral labyrinthitis with associated hearing loss.
#742 Persistent Postural-Perceptual Dizziness Vestibular
✓ READY
Verdict: Well-structured progression from chronic fluctuating dizziness to normal objective vestibular testing with preserved VOR and resolved asymmetry, making it a fair diagnostic puzzle for physio students.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old woman presents with chronic dizziness and vertigo sensation lasting 8 months. Symptoms fluctuate but persistently present. Not true vertigo but persistent dizziness.
C2 Activity: Avoids busy visual environments and crowded spaces. Difficulty walking straight lines. Symptoms worse with head movements and self-motion. Better when standing still or holding onto stable support.
C3 History: Dizziness onset post-vestibular neuritis 8 months prior. Acute vestibular symptoms resolved but dizziness persisted and worsened. Anxiety developed from symptoms.
C4 Examination: Head impulse test normal (preserved VOR gain). Tandem stance and Romberg stable. Positive visually-evoked vestibulo-ocular reflex sensitivity (symptoms provoked by visual motion). Gaze stability normal. No spontaneous nystagmus.
C5 Imaging: ENG: previously showed vestibular asymmetry now resolved. MRI brain: normal. Caloric testing: normal vestibular function bilaterally. Diagnosis of PPPD confirmed by chronic symptoms persisting 8+ months post-vestibular neuritis with normalization of objective vestibular testing and absence of CNS pathology.
#743 Carpal Tunnel Syndrome Wrist/Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear clinical progression from bilateral numbness/tingling to positive provocative tests (Tinel's, Phalen's) to confirmatory NCS/EMG findings, with appropriate educational detail for physio learners.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 44-year-old secretary reports progressive numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, and middle fingers bilaterally, worse on the right. Symptoms are particularly troublesome at night, frequently awakening her from sleep.
C2 Activity: Prolonged typing aggravates symptoms within 30 minutes of work. Gripping objects produces numbness and tingling. Driving worsens symptoms. Night symptoms interfere with sleep quality, requiring frequent position changes.
C3 History: Works 8 hours daily at a computer with poor ergonomic positioning. No prior wrist trauma or fracture history. Recent thyroid screening was normal.
C4 Examination: Positive Tinel's sign is elicited by percussing over the median nerve at the wrist crease. Phalen's test (wrist flexion held 30 seconds) reproduces numbness and tingling within 10 seconds bilaterally. Two-point discrimination is diminished on the thumb and index finger. Hand strength is normal in the intrinsic muscles tested.
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction studies show prolonged latency of median sensory and motor nerves across the wrist compared to the forearm, confirming conduction slowing at the carpal tunnel level. Electromyography shows mild denervation in the abductor pollicis brevis. MRI shows flattening of the median nerve at the wrist. Studies confirm carpal tunnel syndrome bilaterally.
#744 Tuberosity Avulsion Fracture Knee
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is somewhat specific (acute cutting mechanism + inability to extend knee strongly hints at patellar tendon/tibial tuberosity injury), though still defensible; Clue 5 is excellent but Clue 3 and 4 could be reordered for better flow.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 22-year-old collegiate soccer player with acute anterior knee pain during a cutting maneuver. Reports sensation of something tearing. Immediate inability to continue activity with rapid swelling onset.
C2 Activity: Cannot perform straight leg raise or active knee extension. Sharp pain with any attempt to straighten the knee or bear weight through extended leg. Functional guarding of the entire knee.
C3 History: Young athletic male with high-demand sport (soccer). Acute traumatic mechanism during cutting maneuver. No prior knee surgery or injury. Recent growth spurt (18 years old) with some residual cartilage sensitivity. Full participation in sport up to injury.
C4 Examination: Marked anterior knee swelling with visible hematoma over tibial tuberosity. Complete loss of active knee extension strength (0/5). Quadriceps musculature palpable but unable to contract. Large knee effusion. Patellar tendon tense and exquisitely tender.
C5 Imaging: X-ray (AP, lateral, and Merchant views) shows avulsion of tibial tuberosity fragment with 1.5cm displacement. CT confirms fracture extends into articular surface of tibial plateau. MRI shows complete disruption of patellar tendon at bone insertion with severe edema. Associated bone marrow edema in proximal tibia. Soft tissue trauma with hematoma extensive.
#745 Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Limbs
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent progression from disproportionate pain post-minor injury to allodynia and vasomotor/sudomotor changes in Clue 4, with three-phase bone scan and imaging findings in Clue 5 providing clinical confirmation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old presents 8 weeks after an ankle inversion injury. Initial injury seemed minor, but pain and swelling have progressively worsened and remain out of proportion to the original trauma. Pain is described as burning and constant.
C2 Activity: The patient cannot tolerate any weight-bearing and avoids contact with the affected foot. Even light touch provokes severe burning pain that is disproportionate to normal sensory stimulation.
C3 History: Initial ankle sprain from walking. Pain has escalated progressively over 8 weeks despite appropriate rehabilitation and ligamentous healing. Patient reports secondary depression and anxiety.
C4 Examination: Examination reveals bright red, warm, edematous ankle and foot with shiny skin appearance. Sudomotor changes are evident with increased sweating and skin moisture. Vasomotor changes include color variation from red to purple depending on dependency. Nails appear brittle. Pain assessment with gentle touch demonstrates allodynia. Strength testing is limited by pain, not by motor deficit.
C5 Imaging: Three-phase bone scan shows increased uptake in the delayed phase with blood pooling pattern. Radiographs demonstrate spotty, patchy osteoporosis. MRI reveals edema in bone and soft tissues. Combined clinical and imaging findings meet diagnostic criteria for the suspected condition.
#746 Cerebellar Stroke Brain
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clues 2, 3, and 4 are out of order (C2 repeats C1, C3 and C4 describe activity/history but lack clear sequencing) and Clue 5 contains examination findings instead of imaging, breaking the intended clue structure entirely.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old with hypertension and atrial fibrillation presents with acute onset of severe headache, dizziness, vomiting, and gait imbalance. The patient had a sudden fall and is unable to ambulate. Posterior circulation stroke must be rapidly assessed.
C2 Activity: A 68-year-old with hypertension and atrial fibrillation wakes with acute severe headache, dizziness, vomiting, and inability to walk. Patient fell immediately upon attempting to stand.
C3 History: Unable to walk or sit upright without severe dizziness and nausea. Any fine motor task or balance attempt triggers vertigo and loss of coordination.
C4 Examination: Symptoms began upon waking this morning with no warning. Multiple vascular risk factors: resistant hypertension, untreated atrial fibrillation, hyperlipidemia, and heavy smoking history. No prior cerebrovascular events.
C5 Imaging: Nystagmus present. Dysmetria on finger-to-nose testing bilaterally. Speech is ataxic and irregular. Cognition preserved. Gait wide-based and severely unsteady, unable to perform tandem or single-leg stance.
#747 Calf Strain Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: Solid diagnostic progression from acute plantarflex pain during sport to loss of plantarflexion power, negative Thompson test (excluding Achilles rupture), and ultrasound confirmation of muscle disruption with hematoma.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 52-year-old tennis player felt a sudden sharp sensation in the back of the lower leg during an explosive pushing movement in play. Immediate severe pain and inability to continue. No prior significant leg injuries.
C2 Activity: Cannot rise up on toes or push off the ground. Severe pain and instability with any attempt to plantarflex the ankle under load. Walks with foot held in slight plantarflexion. Stair climbing severely restricted.
C3 History: Injury occurred during explosive push-off movement. No warm-up prior to match. Otherwise sedentary between competitions; age 52. No prior significant calf problems.
C4 Examination: Significant calf swelling is immediately visible, with bruising developing within hours and advancing distally toward the ankle. Plantarflexion strength is 0-1/5 (severe weakness). The Thompson test is negative (calf squeeze does not produce plantarflexion, suggesting gastrocnemius-soleus tear rather than Achilles rupture). Calf tenderness is marked in the midsubstance region.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows a hypoechoic disruption in the gastrocnemius or soleus muscle with surrounding edema and hemorrhage (grade 2-3 muscle strain). The musculotendinous junction is intact. Achilles tendon is continuous. Imaging grade guides management (conservative for grade 1-2, possibly surgical for grade 3).
#748 Muscular Dystrophy Muscle
✓ READY
Verdict: Classic presentation builds effectively from proximal weakness and falls in a young boy to Gowers' sign and calf hypertrophy in Clue 2, with genetic/biochemical confirmation (elevated CK, dystrophin absence, DMD mutation) in Clue 5.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 5-year-old boy is referred by his parents because he is falling frequently and struggling to keep up with age-matched peers during play. Over the past year, he has noticed increasing difficulty with running and climbing stairs. His parents report he was reaching normal developmental milestones until recently.
C2 Activity: The child demonstrates a characteristic sign when rising from the floor: he uses his hands to 'climb up' his own legs. He walks with an exaggerated waddling gait and toe-walks when running. Stairs require bannister support; jumping is impossible. Generalized proximal weakness is evident.
C3 History: The child achieved normal early motor milestones. His maternal uncles had similar childhood-onset progressive weakness. No siblings or father are affected. Onset was insidious—no trauma or specific event triggered the decline. Progressive functional loss over one year.
C4 Examination: Strength testing: hip and shoulder girdle 2-3/5; ankle plantarflexion preserved at 5/5 (disproportionate to hip weakness). Calf muscles appear enlarged despite weakness. Waddling gait with marked lumbar lordosis. Scapular winging. No sensory loss. Reflexes preserved.
C5 Imaging: Serum creatine kinase markedly elevated (>10,000 IU/L). Muscle biopsy immunostaining shows complete absence of dystrophin protein. Genetic testing confirms mutation in the DMD gene. EMG demonstrates myopathic pattern with brief, small polyphasic motor units.
#749 Post-Concussion Syndrome Head
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clues 1 and 2 are highly redundant (both describe the same 6-week presentation with headache/concentration difficulties), and imaging should appear in Clue 5 but is mentioned in Clue 4; reordering and condensing would improve flow.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old presents 6 weeks after a motor vehicle accident with ongoing headache, difficulty concentrating, and dizziness. A concussion was diagnosed acutely after loss of consciousness. Persistent post-concussion symptoms are limiting return to work. Diagnostic criteria must be confirmed.
C2 Activity: A 28-year-old presents 6 weeks after a motor vehicle accident with loss of consciousness. Ongoing headache, difficulty concentrating, and dizziness are limiting daily function.
C3 History: The accident involved moderate-speed motor vehicle collision with loss of consciousness for 15-20 seconds. No focal neurological injury was identified acutely. The patient is slowly returning to activities but symptoms persist weeks beyond expected recovery. Previous concussion history is negative.
C4 Examination: Cognitive testing shows mild slowing on reaction time tasks and impaired working memory. Oculomotor testing (smooth pursuit) shows minor asymmetry. Balance testing (tandem stance) is mildly abnormal. Vestibulo-ocular reflex testing may show minor impairment. Headache provocation testing reproduces pain with cognitive exertion.
C5 Imaging: Brain MRI is structurally normal with no focal lesions, hemorrhage, or contusion. Diffusion tensor imaging may show subtle white matter microstructural changes but is not routinely indicated. The imaging is reassuring, and diagnosis is clinical using the SCAT5 (Sport Concussion Assessment Tool). Imaging rules out structural complications.
#750 Unilateral Vestibular Hypofunction Vestibular
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clues 2, 3, and 4 are severely out of order—Clue 4 repeats C3 history information, C3 contains exam-like details, and the clinical story is incoherent; puzzle needs complete restructuring to follow the complaint→activity→history→exam→imaging format.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 55-year-old presents with 3 weeks of intermittent vertigo, head movement intolerance, and oscillopsia (visual blur with head movement) following a viral upper respiratory infection. The patient tilts the head toward the unaffected side to reduce symptoms. Peripheral vestibular dysfunction is likely.
C2 Activity: A 55-year-old presents with 3 weeks of intermittent vertigo and oscillopsia (visual blur with head movement) that began shortly after a viral upper respiratory infection. Head movements trigger symptoms, and the patient has noticed tilting the head toward one side provides relief.
C3 History: Acute vertigo onset occurred 3 weeks prior following a viral URI with vertigo lasting several days. Residual dizziness and head movement intolerance persist. No hearing loss is reported (unlike labyrinthitis). No recent trauma.
C4 Examination: Vertigo onset was abrupt 3 weeks ago and lasted approximately 5 days, with residual dizziness and head movement intolerance persisting since. No hearing loss, ear fullness, or tinnitus has developed. No prior episodes of vertigo or head trauma.
C5 Imaging: MRI of the brain and internal auditory canal is normal, ruling out retrocochlear pathology. Videonystagmography (VNG) shows impaired vestibulo-ocular reflex gain on the affected side. Caloric testing shows reduced response on the affected side (unilateral hypofunction). Imaging rules out stroke or tumor.
#751 Cyclist's Palsy Hand
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (hand numbness in cyclist), progression is logical from activity trigger to nerve-specific findings, and Clue 5 clinches the diagnosis with NCS/EMG specificity.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 45-year-old recreational cyclist presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the ring and little fingers after recent increases in cycling volume (now 40+ miles weekly). Weakness in finger spreading movements is developing. Onset was gradual over the past 3–4 weeks.
C2 Activity: Gripping handlebars reliably triggers numbness in the affected fingers within 30 minutes of cycling. Fine motor tasks (typing, writing) are increasingly difficult. The patient reports finger clumsiness when attempting delicate hand tasks.
C3 History: Long-distance cycling is the main recreational activity with recent increase in cycling distance (from 20 to 40+ miles per week). Handlebar type and positioning have not changed. No previous nerve problems reported.
C4 Examination: Finger abduction and adduction strength are reduced (4/5) indicating intrinsic hand muscle weakness. Two-point discrimination is decreased in the ring and little fingers. Sensation to light touch is mildly reduced. The ulnar pulse is palpable at the wrist. No atrophy is evident yet (recent onset).
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction studies show slowing of ulnar nerve conduction across the wrist and palm, consistent with compression at the wrist level (Guyon canal region). EMG shows mild denervation activity in interossei muscles. Ultrasound may show compression of the ulnar nerve at the wrist from external pressure. Imaging confirms ulnar neuropathy from compression.
#752 Overactive Bladder Pelvis
✗ REWORK
Verdict: This is a urological diagnosis outside physiotherapy scope of practice; physios may manage pelvic floor muscle training as adjunct but cannot diagnose or primarily treat urodynamic bladder pathology.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old male presents with progressive urgency and urinary frequency of 6 months duration. He reports an urgent need to void 10-12 times per day and 4-5 times nightly, with a sensation of incomplete emptying persisting after each void.
C2 Activity: Patient has significantly restricted daily activities: avoids travel and public outings due to unpredictable urgent need to void. Sleep is severely fragmented, limiting daytime function. He denies symptoms related to specific movements or postures—urgency occurs regardless of activity level.
C3 History: Recent diuretic initiation for hypertension coincided with symptom escalation 6 months ago. Long-standing history of benign prostatic hyperplasia, stable on medical management. No history of urinary tract infection, trauma, or neurological disease. Lives independently; symptom impact is primarily on social participation and sleep.
C4 Examination: Pelvic floor assessment reveals normal voluntary contraction strength and tone; no dyssynergia on assessment. Lower limb neurology intact. Post-void residual by ultrasound is 60 mL (mild retention). Abdominal and digital rectal examination unremarkable except mild prostatic enlargement without suspicious nodules.
C5 Imaging: Cystometry or urodynamic testing shows detrusor overactivity during filling phase with involuntary contractions unrelated to voluntary void initiation. Normal bladder compliance. Renal function and imaging are normal; no hydronephrosis or structural abnormality.
#753 Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome Ankle & Foot
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad enough (foot pain with multiple causes), each clue adds specific neuro information, Tinel's sign at posterior tibial nerve is characteristic, and imaging confirms compression diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old presents with several months of burning pain in the sole of the foot and medial ankle region. Symptoms worsen with activity and are particularly bothersome at night. Mild ankle swelling is noted. Pain is described as sharp and burning rather than dull ache.
C2 Activity: Standing and walking aggravate the burning sensation in the foot. Tight shoes worsen symptoms. Sleeping position changes are frequent due to nighttime foot pain. Running is not attempted due to symptom aggravation.
C3 History: The patient has a history of ankle inversion sprains and currently has mild ankle instability. Mild varicose veins are present. No significant trauma precipitated current symptoms, which developed insidiously over months.
C4 Examination: Positive Tinel's sign at the posterior tibial nerve (behind medial malleolus) reproduces tingling into the sole of the foot. Pain is localized to the medial ankle and sole of foot (not lateral ankle as in peroneal neuropathy). Toe sensation is reduced, particularly medial toes (plantar surface).
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction studies show slowing of posterior tibial nerve conduction velocity specifically across the ankle region. EMG demonstrates denervation pattern in abductor hallucis. MRI reveals thickening of the flexor retinaculum compressing the posterior tibial nerve within the tarsal tunnel space.
#754 Scoliosis Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 captures common presentation (asymmetric posture) fitting many conditions, progressive clues narrow to structural spinal deformity, and Cobb angle measurement definitively diagnoses scoliosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 14-year-old girl presents after her school health check revealed uneven shoulder and hip height. Her mother has noticed this asymmetry has become more obvious over the past 2 years. The patient is otherwise healthy and has no pain.
C2 Activity: The patient reports occasional mid-back discomfort when sitting at her desk for long periods. Otherwise, she participates fully in sports and daily activities without restriction. Main concern is the visible asymmetry.
C3 History: Menarche occurred 18 months ago, indicating rapid skeletal growth. The patient is tall for age (85th percentile). No significant family history of scoliosis is reported. General health is good.
C4 Examination: Postural assessment reveals right shoulder higher than left and right hip higher than left. Adam's forward bend test shows rib hump on the right side, indicating structural scoliosis. Truncal asymmetry is evident. Neurological examination is normal with no deficits.
C5 Imaging: Posteroanterior standing spine radiographs show right thoracic curve measuring 28 degrees (Cobb angle measurement). No significant structural abnormalities or vertebral anomalies are noted. Skeletal maturity assessment using Risser sign is 2/5, indicating significant remaining growth. MRI is not indicated given the benign clinical presentation and normal neurological exam.
#755 Tension-Type Headache Head
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately non-specific (bilateral head pressure common to many headache types), clues progressively narrow via stress/postural triggers and muscle findings, and imaging exclusion of red flags supports tension-type diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 36-year-old reports bilateral head pressure that has worsened over 6 months, occurring on most days. Pain quality is described as tightening or pressing. Intensity is mild to moderate.
C2 Activity: Headaches worsen with stress and prolonged computer use. Fatigue and poor sleep quality exacerbate symptoms. Physical activity and rest provide only partial relief. Medication is required 3-4 times weekly for pain control.
C3 History: Patient works in high-stress environment with poor work-life balance and inconsistent sleep schedule. Caffeine consumption is 4-5 cups daily. Prior headache evaluation has excluded migraine with aura.
C4 Examination: Tenderness of bilateral upper trapezius and cervical paraspinal muscles is palpable. Neck range of motion is mildly limited in all directions with muscle tightness. Neurological examination is normal. No vision changes, sensory deficits, or focal neurological signs are present.
C5 Imaging: CT and MRI imaging are unremarkable when performed. Imaging adds no diagnostic value given absence of red flags (no focal neurological signs, normal vision, no acute change in headache character or severity).
#756 Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip Hip
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is too specific (asymmetric leg + delayed crawling in 6-month-old essentially names the condition) and multiple clues repeat examination findings (Barlow, Ortolani, abduction limitations) without progression; this is a medical diagnosis better suited to pediatric orthopedics than physiotherapy practice.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 6-month-old infant presents with asymmetric leg appearance and delayed crawling compared to peers. No significant trauma or acute event. Parents noted one leg seems shorter.
C2 Activity: Cannot abduct hips symmetrically when lying supine. One hip appears to "stick" or resist opening. Asymmetric thigh and buttock creases noted on visual inspection.
C3 History: Born at term without complications. First child. Maternal grandmother and paternal aunt both required hip surgery in infancy. No imaging performed in first months of life.
C4 Examination: Barlow maneuver positive with dislocation. Ortolani sign positive with reduction clunk. Hip abduction significantly limited bilaterally. Asymmetric skin folds marked.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound shows severe dysplasia bilaterally with subluxation. Femoral heads poorly covered. Graf classification shows severe dysplasia requiring intervention. Delayed diagnosis.
#757 Piriformis Syndrome Hip
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 explicitly states the diagnosis ("Piriformis muscle compression") when it should be broader, and Clues 2–4 are redundant (all describe sitting and buttock pain); Clue 5 adds diagnostic value but earlier clues need restructuring.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old develops progressive buttock pain and radiating pain down the posterior leg (pseudoradiculopathy) after prolonged sitting. Pain mimics sciatic nerve radiculopathy but without low back pain. Piriformis muscle compression of the sciatic nerve must be differentiated from true radiculopathy.
C2 Activity: A 48-year-old develops progressive buttock pain with radiation down the posterior leg after a period of increased sitting. Pain is constant but varies with position. No recent trauma or low back injury reported.
C3 History: The patient works at a desk with long sitting periods of 4-6 hours daily. No low back injury or trauma occurred. Previous similar episodes occurred but resolved with activity modification. The patient attributes pain to long work hours.
C4 Examination: Patient works at a desk with prolonged daily sitting (4–6 hours without breaks). No acute injury. Previous similar episodes resolved with activity modification and lighter workload. No imaging or formal diagnosis sought previously.
C5 Imaging: Lumbar spine imaging (CT or MRI) is normal with no disc herniation or nerve root compression, helping to rule out true radiculopathy. Diagnostic ultrasound or MRI may show piriformis muscle enlargement or trigger points. Imaging rules out lumbar pathology and supports piriformis syndrome diagnosis.
#758 Chronic Exertional Compartment Syndrome Lower Leg
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (exercise-induced shin pain), progression logically moves from activity pattern to exercise-specific pressure findings, and post-exercise compartment pressure measurement definitively confirms diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A runner reports bilateral anterior shin pain that occurs predictably during running activity. Pain develops after consistent running distance and resolves completely with rest. The condition recurs with return to running at similar intensity.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot sustain running beyond a specific distance threshold before anterior compartment tightness forces activity cessation. Pain does not occur with walking or cycling at moderate intensity. Symptoms do not occur with sport-specific drills performed at low speed, only with sustained endurance running.
C3 History: Onset correlates with increased running volume or intensity. The athlete may have history of shin splints or tibial stress. Repetitive high-impact activities such as running on hard surfaces exacerbate symptoms.
C4 Examination: Pre-exercise examination may be normal with no resting compartment tenderness. Post-exercise examination reveals tight and tender anterior compartments. Compartment pressure measurement at rest is normal but elevated post-exercise.
C5 Imaging: Compartment pressure measurement obtained immediately post-exercise (within 1-5 minutes) demonstrates elevated pressures in anterior compartment. Post-exercise pressure exceeds 30 mmHg with slow return to normal. Serial measurements during standardized exercise protocol establish diagnosis.
#759 Calcific Tendinitis Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is broad (acute shoulder pain from desk work could be many conditions), clues progressively narrow to supraspinatus-specific signs, and calcium deposit on imaging clinches the diagnosis.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old office worker reports acute onset severe shoulder pain that began yesterday during desk work. Sharp anterior pain with nocturnal symptoms disrupting sleep. No preceding trauma or specific movement recalled.
C2 Activity: Unable to perform overhead tasks. Pain markedly worsens with abduction and cross-body reaching. Avoids lying on affected side. Active range of motion severely limited by guarding; passive movements tolerated better.
C3 History: No prior shoulder injury or overhead/repetitive activities. Pain intensity appears excessive relative to desk-based occupation, suggesting acute inflammatory process rather than cumulative strain. Previously asymptomatic.
C4 Examination: Severe tenderness over supraspinatus tendon insertion. Passive range of motion better preserved than active motion. Supraspinatus strength testing limited by pain rather than true weakness. Dugas test negative (shoulder can still adduct). Hawkins test markedly positive reproducing sharp pain.
C5 Imaging: Shoulder X-ray (AP and scapular Y views) demonstrates dense calcium deposit within supraspinatus tendon measuring 8x6mm. Calcium appears radiodense with well-defined borders. Associated soft tissue swelling visible around deposit. MRI shows T2 hypointense deposit with surrounding fluid signal indicating active inflammation and resorption phase.
#760 Pelvic Organ Prolapse Pelvic Floor
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad, progression is logical, but this diagnosis typically requires gynecological assessment and imaging interpretation; physiotherapy scope is pelvic floor rehabilitation post-diagnosis rather than primary diagnostic workup.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with progressive sensation of heaviness and dragging discomfort in the lower pelvis that worsens as the day goes on. She reports feeling uncomfortable during sexual activity. Symptoms began gradually several years ago and have been slowly worsening.
C2 Activity: Heaviness sensation worsens with prolonged standing, lifting, or straining. Walking longer distances triggers worsening symptoms. Symptoms improve significantly when lying down. Exercise and physical activity are limited by perineal discomfort.
C3 History: The patient is gravida 4 para 4 (four pregnancies, four vaginal deliveries). Menopause occurred 8 years ago without hormone replacement therapy. Chronic straining from constipation has been a lifelong issue. Previous pelvic surgery has not been performed.
C4 Examination: Visual inspection at rest shows the vaginal tissues bulging slightly. Valsalva maneuver exacerbates the bulge significantly with visible prolapse of the vaginal vault. Palpation confirms laxity of the pelvic floor support structures. Pelvic floor contraction strength is reduced (2/5).
C5 Imaging: Pelvic ultrasound or MRI with Valsalva demonstrates significant descent of the vaginal vault and bladder neck below the pelvic floor plane, consistent with Stage III-IV prolapse. Imaging also reveals rectocele component, indicating concurrent posterior compartment involvement.
#761 Osteoporotic Vertebral Compression Fracture Spine
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progressive clues from acute back pain post-fall → postural limitation → osteoporosis history → focal tenderness with preserved forward flexion → imaging confirmation; clue 1 is appropriately broad (fracture/trauma) and each clue adds distinct clinical value.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 74-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with acute-onset mid-back pain following a minor fall from standing height. Pain is localized to the thoracic spine and began immediately after the incident.
C2 Activity: The patient walks with a flexed posture and is unable to extend the trunk without significant pain. Self-care tasks such as dressing and bathing require assistance.
C3 History: Osteoporosis was diagnosed 5 years ago (T-score -3.2) but treatment adherence has been poor. The patient is a long-time smoker with low body weight (BMI 19). No previous fracture history.
C4 Examination: Tenderness is elicited over the mid-thoracic spine. Trunk extension is severely limited by pain, whereas forward flexion is relatively preserved. Neurological examination is intact with no focal deficits or myelopathic signs.
C5 Imaging: Plain thoracic radiographs demonstrate anterior vertebral body wedging with loss of height at the thoracic level. MRI shows acute bone marrow edema (STIR signal increase) within the affected vertebra, confirming an acute fracture with no canal compromise.
#762 Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease Peripheral Nerves
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Strong puzzle overall, but clue 3 (family history of CMT1A) is arguably too specific and nearly diagnostic before exam findings; consider generalizing to 'inherited neuropathy' or moving genetic detail to clue 5 only.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 22-year-old reports progressive weakness in the feet and lower legs over the past 5 years, with increasing difficulty walking and running. Patient notes high-arched feet and curled toes. Symptoms began in late adolescence.
C2 Activity: Patient experiences recurrent inversion ankle sprains and foot instability. Ankle dorsiflexion weakness is evident during gait. Fine motor tasks involving foot intrinsic muscles are impaired.
C3 History: The father has similar symptoms and was diagnosed with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1A. The patient's symptoms began in late adolescence with gradual progression. No trauma or toxin exposure occurred. Hyperlipidemia and diabetes are not present.
C4 Examination: Lower leg and foot muscle atrophy is prominent with 'stork leg' appearance (wasting of calf muscles). Strength testing shows weakness of ankle dorsiflexors (2/5) and plantarflexors (3/5). Intrinsic foot muscle atrophy and weakness result in hammer toe deformities. Sensation to vibration and position sense is mildly reduced distally.
C5 Imaging: Nerve conduction studies show slow conduction velocity (demyelinating pattern) in all nerves tested, consistent with hereditary motor-sensory neuropathy. EMG shows denervation patterns in distal muscles. Genetic testing confirms PMP22 gene duplication (CMT1A). DNA sequencing identifies the specific mutation confirming diagnosis.
#763 Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Limbs
✓ READY
Verdict: Excellent progression from disproportionate post-operative pain → protective posturing with allodynia → post-operative timeline with anxiety context → striking trophic changes and edema → imaging findings of soft tissue edema and osteoporosis; clue 1 broadly fits multiple post-surgical complications.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 38-year-old presents 10 weeks post-operatively from wrist fracture fixation with severe hand pain and swelling that seem excessive for the surgical trauma. Pain is burning in quality and triggered by minimal stimuli.
C2 Activity: Protective posturing of the hand. Any light touch triggers severe pain; active movement attempts provoke intense pain and patient spontaneously avoids use. Patient holds hand immobilized and has withdrawn from rehabilitation participation.
C3 History: A Colles fracture was treated surgically with open reduction and internal fixation. Immediate post-operative period was unremarkable for the first 2-3 weeks. Subsequently, pain has progressively worsened rather than improved. The patient is anxious about hand recovery.
C4 Examination: Hand demonstrates marked edema, warmth, and erythema with cyanotic discoloration. Profuse sweating noted. Skin is glossy, thin, and fragile with visible trophic changes. Allodynia confirmed—even light pressure elicits severe pain disproportionate to stimulus.
C5 Imaging: Three-phase bone scan demonstrates increased blood pooling in delayed phase. Radiographs show patchy osteoporosis in hand and wrist bones. MRI reveals diffuse soft tissue and bone marrow edema throughout the hand.
#764 Glenohumeral Instability Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Good progressive structure from apprehension in overhead athlete → apprehension in provocative positions → prior dislocation history → positive apprehension and relocation tests with hypermobility → labral pathology on imaging; clue 1 is broad enough for multiple shoulder disorders.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: 28-year-old overhead athlete reports episodic shoulder apprehension during throwing. States fear of shoulder 'slipping out' but no confirmed dislocation history. Symptom onset insidious, worsening over past 6 months.
C2 Activity: Throwing is limited by apprehension when the shoulder reaches 90 degrees abduction and external rotation position. The patient modifies throwing mechanics to reduce apprehension. Reaching behind the head with arm abducted reproduces apprehension. Sleeping on the affected shoulder is avoided.
C3 History: A previous anterior shoulder dislocation occurred 2 years ago while pitching, which was reduced in the emergency department. Physical therapy was performed but return to competitive pitching resulted in symptom recurrence. The patient continues to play despite apprehension.
C4 Examination: Apprehension test positive in supine, 90° abduction/external rotation. Relocation test positive. External rotation ROM 110° (hypermobile compared to contralateral 80°). Anterior drawer test and load-and-shift test mildly positive.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows possible Bankart lesion (anteroinferior glenoid labrum detachment) or reverse Hill-Sachs lesion. 3D CT reconstruction may show glenoid bone loss if engaging Hill-Sachs lesion is present. Imaging identifies labral pathology and glenoid anatomy, guiding management (conservative versus surgical).
#765 Cerebral Palsy - Ataxic Whole Body
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear developmental trajectory from clumsiness and incoordination → wide-based unsteady gait with fine motor impairment → prematurity with IVH → intention tremor and cerebellar dysmetria on exam → cerebellar hypoplasia on MRI; clue 1 fits multiple developmental coordination disorders.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 6-year-old with a history of prematurity and early developmental delay presents with progressive difficulty with coordinated movement. Parents report the child is clumsy and unsteady, particularly when trying to pick up objects or walk quickly.
C2 Activity: Walking is unsteady with a wide-based gait. Running is impossible. Fine motor tasks (writing, eating utensil use, building with blocks) are significantly impaired. The child cannot jump or skip with typical age-appropriate coordination.
C3 History: Born at 34 weeks with documented intraventricular hemorrhage (grade 2). Developmental milestones were delayed. CP diagnosis established at 18 months. No history of spasticity, seizures, or progressive regression. Premature birth with IVH is the known etiology.
C4 Examination: Pronounced intention tremor is evident when the child reaches for objects with the arms, worsening as the hand approaches the target (cerebellar dysmetria). Wide-based gait with poor balance and trunk control is observed. Muscle tone is low-normal (hypotonia). Reflexes are normal. No spasticity is present.
C5 Imaging: Brain MRI shows cerebellar hypoplasia with reduced cerebellar volume and thin cerebellar folia. The cerebral cortex appears structurally normal. White matter appears appropriately myelinated. The imaging pattern is consistent with cerebellar hypodevelopment from perinatal injury.
#766 Post-Laminectomy Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Solid puzzle structure, but clue 3 and 4 overlap significantly (both discuss persistent leg weakness and unchanged ankle dorsiflexion strength); consider reframing clue 4 to add distinct information like segmental instability findings or imaging of adjacent-level changes.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 55-year-old presents with persistent leg pain and weakness 6 weeks after lumbar spinal surgery. Symptom improvement has plateaued despite expectations for continued recovery.
C2 Activity: Walking tolerance remains limited to 15 minutes despite expected improvement to 30+ minutes by this point in recovery. Standing from sitting is difficult. Running is not attempted. The patient remains on significant opioid pain medication.
C3 History: Severe lumbar stenosis at L3-L4 with left leg radiculopathy was treated surgically with left L4 laminectomy and L4-L5 decompression. Pre-operative imaging clearly showed severe stenosis and neural foraminal compression. The patient had significant pre-operative leg pain (7/10).
C4 Examination: Left ankle dorsiflexion strength remains 3+/5, unchanged from pre-operative. Hip flexion strength is intact at 5/5. Palpation reveals no surgical site tenderness or instability. Lumbar segmental mobility at L4-L5 is present but guarded. No lower limb reflexes are diminished.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative MRI shows adequate decompression with relief of stenosis and neural foraminal opening at L4-L5. No hematoma, recurrent stenosis, or missed pathology at other levels is identified. Imaging demonstrates successful decompression; persistent symptoms may suggest post-laminectomy syndrome or inadequate decompression.
#767 Ankylosing Spondylitis Spine
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Critical structural flaw: clue 4 contains examination findings (symptoms began insidiously, denies infections/GI symptoms, office worker, no past medical history) that reads as history/demographics rather than examination findings, breaking the clue progression logic.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 28-year-old man presents with progressive morning stiffness in lumbar spine lasting 2 hours daily for 8 months. Pain improves with activity and worsens with rest. Nocturnal pain awakening him from sleep.
C2 Activity: Spinal stiffness particularly in morning and with inactivity. Buttock and lower back pain described as deep ache. Pain improves with activity and exercise. Progressive loss of spinal mobility with difficulty bending forward. Associated fatigue and malaise.
C3 History: Patient reports difficulty with lower back and buttock pain when sitting >30 minutes. Pain radiates toward thighs but straight leg raise is painless. Early morning stiffness improves within 1-2 hours of movement. Associated with persistent low-grade fatigue despite adequate sleep.
C4 Examination: Symptoms began insidiously 8 months ago without preceding injury. Patient denies recent infection, GI symptoms, eye inflammation, or skin rash. Works in office setting with prolonged sitting. No significant past medical history or regular medications. First presentation of spinal symptoms.
C5 Imaging: MRI sacroiliac joints shows bilateral sacroiliitis with erosions and inflammatory signal changes. Lumbar spine shows syndesmophyte formation connecting vertebral bodies (bamboo spine early phase). HLA-B27 positive. ESR elevated at 48 mm/hr, CRP 9.2 mg/dL. Complete blood count and metabolic panel normal.
#768 Suprascapular Neuropathy Shoulder
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear progression from deep posterior shoulder pain in overhead athlete → overhead activity limitation → high-volume training history → supraspinous/infraspinous atrophy with weakness → EMG confirmation and nerve imaging showing compression; clue 1 is reasonably broad for shoulder pathologies.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 32-year-old overhead athlete presents with 3 months of progressive deep posterior shoulder pain and weakness. No prior trauma. Pain worsens with overhead activities.
C2 Activity: Overhead serving in volleyball triggered symptom onset during high-intensity training. Cannot perform overhead activities due to combined pain and weakness. Also carries heavy backpacks recreationally.
C3 History: Athlete trains 5 days per week with high-volume overhead work and rapid shoulder movements. Recently increased training intensity. No previous shoulder pathology.
C4 Examination: Deep posterior shoulder ache is elicited with palpation over the suprascapular notch. External rotation at 0 degrees abduction is significantly weak (2/5) as is abduction strength (3/5). Supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscle atrophy is evident on visual inspection and measurement. The patient demonstrates wasting of the supraspinous and infraspinous fossae.
C5 Imaging: EMG demonstrates fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves in supraspinatus and infraspinatus with normal motor conduction in proximal upper limb. Nerve conduction velocity shows slowing across suprascapular notch. MRI reveals ganglion cyst or ligamentous hypertrophy at suprascapular notch causing compression.
#769 Post-Ankle ORIF Ankle
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Good puzzle overall, but clue 1 ('persistent swelling and stiffness 8 weeks post-trauma') is somewhat specific to post-surgical recovery; reframe to broader 'ankle stiffness and swelling' to fit other ankle conditions (adhesive capsulitis analogue, complex regional pain syndrome, severe ankle sprain).
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 42-year-old presents with persistent ankle swelling and stiffness 8 weeks following high-energy trauma. Range of motion remains significantly restricted. Pain limits functional recovery.
C2 Activity: Still requires crutches for ambulation at 8 weeks. Unable to progress weight-bearing as anticipated. Walking causes significant ankle pain.
C3 History: Significant soft tissue trauma accompanied the initial fracture injury. Followed standard post-operative protocol: 6 weeks immobilization, then progressive weight-bearing over past 2 weeks.
C4 Examination: Significant ankle swelling persists with pitting edema extending into the foot. Ankle dorsiflexion is limited to 5 degrees (normal 15 degrees). Plantarflexion is limited to 25 degrees (normal 50 degrees). Medial and lateral malleoli are tender. Hardware prominence is palpable. No signs of infection (normal temperature, incision healed).
C5 Imaging: Post-operative radiographs confirm anatomical reduction of ankle mortise without articular step-off, hardware in correct position, and maintained syndesmotic reduction. No evidence of hardware loosening, migration, or fracture.
#770 Distal Humerus Fracture Elbow
✓ READY
Verdict: Clear clinical narrative from severe elbow pain with deformity post-fall → immobility and self-care loss → corticosteroid use and direct trauma → posterior prominence with severe limitation → comminuted intra-articular fracture; clue 1 is broad enough for multiple elbow injuries.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 72-year-old with osteoporosis presents with severe elbow pain and visible deformity after a fall on an outstretched arm. The elbow is swollen and bruised.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot perform any elbow motion and is unable to perform self-care activities such as dressing or eating. The arm is immobilized and severely guarded against any movement.
C3 History: Fall occurred while descending stairs, landing directly on the posterior elbow. Patient reports taking oral corticosteroids for 5 years (COPD). No history of prior elbow injury or arthropathy.
C4 Examination: Elbow held in slight flexion; marked posterior prominence of olecranon visible. Full thickness ecchymosis over posterior elbow. Radial and ulnar pulses normal and symmetric. Finger sensation and motor function intact. Pain severely limits any passive range testing beyond 20 degrees.
C5 Imaging: AP and lateral radiographs show a comminuted fracture at the distal humerus with displacement of both medial and lateral condyles. Fracture extends into the articular surface. No associated avulsion fractures. No neurovascular compromise evident.
#771 Hip Osteoarthritis Hip
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (progressive groin pain + stiffness fits hip OA, labral pathology, FAI, capsulitis), each clue adds genuine progressive specificity (activity limitation → ROM/weakness → imaging confirmation), and the clinical narrative is coherent and educationally sound.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 68-year-old presents with progressive right groin pain worsened by weight-bearing over 3 years. Morning stiffness in the hip lasting 30 minutes, improving somewhat with initial activity.
C2 Activity: Walking tolerance has decreased from 45 minutes to 15 minutes due to hip pain. Stairs (particularly descending) provoke sharp groin pain. Putting on shoes and socks requires modification. Standing from a seated position causes pain.
C3 History: No significant hip trauma or previous hip surgery. The patient has worked a labor-intensive job requiring prolonged standing for 40 years. Obesity is present with BMI 32. Morning pain is worst, improving with activity but worsening with prolonged exertion.
C4 Examination: Hip flexion limited to 100 degrees, internal rotation severely restricted (15 degrees). Positive Thomas test (15-degree flexion contracture). Hip abductor weakness (3+/5). Positive Trendelenburg sign. Pain reproduced at end-range flexion and internal rotation. Anterior groin palpation tender but non-specific.
C5 Imaging: Hip radiographs show joint space narrowing, osteophytes at the femoral head-neck junction and acetabular rim, and subchondral sclerosis of the femoral head and acetabular roof. Mild acetabular dysplasia may be noted. MRI confirms cartilage loss and bone marrow edema consistent with advanced osteoarthritis.
#772 Quadriceps Contusion Thigh
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is slightly too specific—'direct contact' + 'anterior thigh' + 'sudden onset' + 'immediate swelling' together nearly name a contusion, leaving few plausible alternatives; broadening to 'acute thigh injury with swelling' would improve the puzzle.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 24-year-old American football player reports sudden onset severe pain and immediate swelling in the anterior thigh following direct contact during play. Unable to continue activity.
C2 Activity: Knee flexion severely restricted; unable to walk without significant pain and limp. Weight-bearing tolerance markedly diminished. Complete functional loss for sport.
C3 History: Single direct blow to anterior thigh from player contact. First thigh injury; no prior episodes. Excellent premorbid conditioning and no medical comorbidities.
C4 Examination: Gross anterior thigh swelling visibly deforming the contour. Ecchymosis developing. Knee flexion severely restricted (pain and mechanical block). Quadriceps strength testing impossible due to pain inhibition. Neurovascular examination intact.
C5 Imaging: Ultrasound demonstrates intramuscular hematoma and muscle fiber disruption within quadriceps. Radiographs exclude fracture. Serial imaging in 3-4 weeks required to detect heterotopic bone formation, a known complication of high-impact muscular trauma.
#773 Calcaneal Fracture Foot
✗ REWORK
Verdict: Clue 1 is far too specific—'fell from 10-foot ladder landing on both heels' + 'severe heel pain' + 'inability to bear weight' essentially diagnoses the condition before any guessing begins, violating the fundamental principle of progressive revelation.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 35-year-old fell from a 10-foot ladder landing on both heels. Presents with severe heel pain, immediate marked swelling, and inability to bear weight on either foot.
C2 Activity: Severe foot edema developing rapidly. Skin over heels tense and dusky. Pain with any attempt at plantarflexion or inversion. Calf compartment appears tight; patient reports increasing foot numbness distal to the ankle.
C3 History: Fall occurred 4 hours ago. Patient also reports brief loss of consciousness and pain radiating into the calf. No significant past medical history. Similar mechanism injury in elderly relative 5 years ago required prolonged rehabilitation.
C4 Examination: Severe heel swelling is evident bilaterally with ecchymosis developing over the heels and soles. Palpation of the calcaneus elicits severe pain. Neutral ankle alignment may be lost with varus deformity developing. Compartment syndrome signs (excessive pain with passive stretch, numbness, cyanosis) must be monitored for urgently.
C5 Imaging: Heel radiographs show fracture of the calcaneus, typically intra-articular with loss of the subtalar joint space. CT of the foot shows the fracture pattern, number of fragments, degree of comminution, and extent of articular involvement. Imaging determines if conservative or surgical treatment is appropriate based on fracture severity and displacement.
#774 Post-Laminectomy Spine
~ MINOR FIX
Verdict: Clue 1 is reasonable, but Clue 3 reveals 'L4-L5 disc herniation confirmed pre-operatively with clear imaging' which discloses too much anatomical detail too early, and the distinction between post-op inflammation and true recurrence/syndrome is muddied across C4–C5.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: A 48-year-old presents with persistent leg pain 10 weeks after lumbar spine surgery. Expected recovery has not occurred. Pain radiates distally and remains severe.
C2 Activity: Patient cannot tolerate walking or prolonged sitting/standing. Pain is predominantly unilateral and follows a dermatomal pattern to the foot. No improvement in function or pain severity post-operatively.
C3 History: Left-sided L4-L5 disc herniation was confirmed pre-operatively with clear imaging evidence of neural compression. The surgical indication was straightforward, yet post-operative symptoms have not resolved as anticipated.
C4 Examination: Left ankle dorsiflexion remains weak at 3/5 with minimal recovery. Straight leg raise is markedly limited. Neurological examination suggests continued compression of the L5 nerve root rather than post-operative inflammation alone.
C5 Imaging: Post-operative MRI shows the laminectomy defect with adequate decompression. However, recurrent disc material at the same level (or different level) may be evident. Re-herniation occurs in 5-15% of cases. Imaging determines if revision surgery is indicated or if conservative pain management should continue.
#775 Work-Related Upper Limb Disorder Upper Limb
✓ READY
Verdict: Clue 1 is appropriately broad (progressive wrist/forearm pain with occupational and morning stiffness context fits CTS, tendinopathy, RSI, cervical radiculopathy), clues escalate logically from complaint → activity → occupational history → specific nerve signs → imaging confirmation, and the puzzle is fair and educationally engaging.
Show clues
C1 Complaint: Office worker reports progressive pain in the wrist and forearm that worsens toward the end of the workday. Notices stiffness in the morning that improves with activity.
C2 Activity: Unable to grip a pen or keyboard for more than 30 minutes without sharp pain in the thumb and index finger. Fine motor tasks deteriorate as the day progresses.
C3 History: Employed as a keyboard operator for 8 hours daily over the past 18 months. No prior arm injuries. Workstation lacks proper ergonomic support. Symptoms began 4 months ago and have progressed despite occasional days off.
C4 Examination: Positive Phalen's test and Tinel's sign over the median nerve at the wrist. Grip strength reduced on the affected side. Intrinsic hand muscle atrophy noted. Two-point discrimination mildly diminished in radial digits.
C5 Imaging: MRI shows median nerve compression at the carpal tunnel with mild signal changes. No tendon tear. Nerve conduction studies confirm slowed latency across the wrist consistent with carpal tunnel syndrome.
